title: "The Ukrainian scenario had been written in Georgia in 2008. All you had to do was want to read."
lead: Same methods, same strategy, same justifications: Russia is using the same logic against Ukraine as it did with Georgia, counting on the passivity of Western countries.
text_eng:
Chronicle. During a meeting of the Russian Security Council dedicated to the Donbass that will go down in history as a televised moment of autocratic power, former President Dmitry Medvedev made a devastating comparison for Western democracies on Monday, February 21, at the Kremlin. The comparison was with Georgia, where Russian troops have occupied 20% of the territory since Moscow recognized two small separatist republics in 2008 following a brief war. "I remember well 2008, when we decided to recognize Abkhazia and South Ossetia," said Mr. Medvedev in front of his successor, Vladimir Putin. "We saved hundreds of thousands of lives. Today, the risk is greater, there are more people. We know what will happen next. We know the sanctions. We know the pressure. But we also know how to respond. If we can be patient, they will tire and come back to us to talk about strategic security and stability. Russia must recognize the independence of the Donbass republics. Experience shows that tensions will ease." "They." Dmitry Medvedev did not even bother to name them, as the Kremlin has internalized the behavior of Europeans since that fateful precedent: in his mind, they express outrage, threaten, sanction, and then move on. The Europeans, particularly the then French President Nicolas Sarkozy, were indeed involved in the Georgian episode which, in many ways, foreshadowed the current Ukrainian drama. His American counterpart George W. Bush, nearing the end of his term, had delegated the crisis management to the European Union (EU), with France holding the rotating presidency at the time. Tried and tested strategy However, the most troubling aspect, when revisiting the Georgian affair, is Russia's behavior. In hindsight, all the elements of the crisis that Vladimir Putin has just provoked over Ukraine were already in place, the strategy tested, the geopolitical framework set. Everything was laid out. It just needed to be read. Succeeding Boris Yeltsin, Putin came to power in 2000. The September 11, 2001 attacks gave him hope for cooperation with the United States, but the Iraq war dashed that prospect. In 2003 and 2004, the first "color revolutions" occurred in two former Soviet republics: the "Rose Revolution" in Georgia, followed by the "Orange Revolution" in Ukraine, brought democrats to power. This was a turning point for the Russian leader, who saw these two countries leaning towards the West while NATO and the EU were already integrating former satellite countries in Central Europe. In 2007, he delivered a speech at the Munich Security Conference that was seen as aggressive regarding NATO expansion, sending a chill through the audience. In April 2008, at the NATO summit in Bucharest, George Bush wanted to announce the upcoming membership of Ukraine and Georgia. Concerned about not irritating Moscow, Nicolas Sarkozy and especially Chancellor Angela Merkel opposed it. A compromise was adopted: Ukraine and Georgia are destined to become NATO members, but no timeline was set. In Moscow, they only saw the first part of this equation. At the end of July 2008, pro-Russian separatists in a small region of Georgia, South Ossetia, bombed a village and Georgian soldiers. It was a provocation, but Georgian President Mikheil Saakashvili fell into the trap and sent reinforcements against the separatists. Russia intervened by deploying 40,000 soldiers - 20,000 in South Ossetia and the same number in another separatist region, Abkhazia. In five days, Georgian forces were crushed by Russian troops who advanced towards the capital, Tbilisi. 

The same fanciful accusation, this time against Ukraine. Accompanied by cyberattacks, the offensive began on the day of the opening of the Beijing Olympics. Nicolas Sarkozy was there, returned to France, called Medvedev, who, in 2008, held the position of president to allow Putin, who had become prime minister, to run for re-election in 2012. He proposed to go to Moscow. "Come," Medvedev replied, promising a ceasefire during their talks. At the Kremlin, Sarkozy negotiated with Medvedev and the indispensable Putin an imperfect agreement that allowed the Russian troops' advance to be stopped. In his book "The Time of Storms" (L'Observatoire, 2020), the former French president presents this event as a great diplomatic success for Europe. "Vladimir Putin kept his word," he writes. He forgets to mention that a few days later, Russia undermined him by recognizing the independence of Abkhazia and South Ossetia - which it still occupies to this day - under the pretext of ending the "genocide" of Russians perpetrated by Georgia. The same fanciful accusation is now being used against Ukraine in the Donbass. Medvedev also put forward a familiar justification: that of "privileged interests" spheres that Russia intended to preserve "in certain regions." In another book, "A Little War that Shook the World" (Palgrave Macmillan, 2010, untranslated), American Ronald Asmus (1957-2011), a former Clinton administration official, draws a less flattering assessment than Nicolas Sarkozy. Russia, he writes, "altered the borders of a sovereign state by force, allowed ethnic cleansing" in parts of the two republics "to create more ethnically homogeneous entities, only partially respected the terms of a ceasefire signed by its president, and was not held accountable by anyone." She wanted to provoke a regime change through economic suffocation. According to Mr. Asmus, "the real reason for this war was Tbilisi's aspiration to join the democratic West." Ukraine and Europe are now paying the price for having ignored this lesson.

title: War in Ukraine: Israel positions itself as a mediator in the face of Moscow
lead: The Prime Minister of the State of Israel, Naftali Bennett, met with Vladimir Putin on Saturday in Moscow and spoke on the phone with Emmanuel Macron. Nothing has been revealed about this mediation attempt led by this almost novice in international relations.
text_eng: Never before had an Israeli prime minister taken on such a responsibility. By going to the Kremlin on Saturday, March 5, to discuss for three hours with Vladimir Putin about the war in Ukraine, Naftali Bennett left diplomats and observers in Israel speechless. He holds a historic opportunity for his country, an undeclared nuclear power, a small ally of Washington in the Middle East, to speak to the world about something other than itself. Nothing has been revealed about the substance of this mediation, conducted during the Sabbath by this leader from the religious far-right, a former special forces member, ex-entrepreneur, and defense minister, in office for less than a year. Did Mr. Bennett convey or clarify messages, seek to keep a channel of discussion open, or obtain a ceasefire? In a cabinet meeting on Sunday, he gave himself "little chance of success," but mentioned a "moral duty." This near-novice in international relations simply described the Russian invasion of Ukraine as a divine catastrophe, a natural disaster without causes or culprits. His consistent and unique caution on the Western stage has recently drawn criticism from Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky, as well as in Israel. However, on Sunday, the Ukrainian ambassador in his country, Yevgen Korniychuk, said he was "grateful." Mr. Bennett followed "a friendly suggestion" from his country, he said, by acting as an intermediary after months of contacts. Both with Mr. Zelensky, to whom he had refused to provide weapons before the conflict, and with Mr. Putin, who had rejected a previous mediation proposal before the war. "Israel is the only democratic state with excellent relations with both parties. Minsk [the Belarusian capital where a diplomatic sequence was opened in 2015 to end the conflict in Donbass, buried by the Russian invasion] can no longer be a place for negotiations for us (...). We are looking for another location and Jerusalem is a possibility," believes this diplomat. The guarantors of the Minsk agreements, including France, now appear "burned" in Moscow. However, Mr. Bennett is trying to present his initiative as an action taken in concert with Western democracies. According to the Walla website, he informed the U.S. National Security Advisor, Jake Sullivan, of his departure as early as Friday. It is hard to imagine that Washington requested his attempt. The White House seems too focused on rallying broad support for sanctions against Moscow. But it did not dissuade him, and on Monday, U.S. Secretary of State Antony Blinken was to meet with his Israeli counterpart, Yair Lapid, in Lithuania. "The American representative at the UN criticized Israel for its lukewarm stance before we eventually voted on the resolution in the General Assembly condemning the Russian invasion [on March 2]. But the tune we hear in Washington is different: they understand Israel's unique situation, and they do not reproach us," said a former Israeli diplomat. The fact that Israel has not imposed significant sanctions against Russia does not seem to bother them. "However, it is possible that Washington is encouraging Israel to discreetly cut off Moscow's access to certain Israeli cyber weapons used by the military apparatus," noted a Western diplomat. This includes tools for computer surveillance. From Moscow, Mr. Bennett traveled to Germany to have dinner with Olaf Scholz, a few days after a visit by the chancellor to Jerusalem. He also spoke on the phone with Emmanuel Macron, who had informed him of his recent discussions with the Kremlin leader, according to the Elysée. On Sunday, he continued these contacts by phone, including with Vladimir Putin. Fragile balance Mr. Bennett is not there by chance. Israel is among the nations most affected by this war, due to its community from the former USSR (15% of the population). As early as 2011, Mr. Putin acknowledged a special status to this country, being "practically Russian-speaking." The government spares no effort to help those who wish, among the 200,000 eligible Ukrainian Jews to make aliyah (emigrate to Israel), to leave the country. The historical significance of this endeavor is not lost on any Israeli, especially as Odessa, the "Jerusalem of Europe," is threatened. Israel is also familiar with Russian war logic: it understands its use of force in international relations and takes its nuclear threats seriously. The military maintains a constant dialogue with Moscow, whose troops have been ensuring the survival of the Syrian regime at its border since 2015. These exchanges have never allowed Israel to curb Iran's influence in Damascus, its major regional enemy. However, by constantly highlighting Moscow's unfulfilled promises, the Israeli state has legitimized its own strikes against Iranian interests in the country. Without ever catching the Russian army off guard. Israel fears that this fragile balance may be upset by Moscow's plunge into the abyss. Becoming a pariah state, threatened with a sanctions regime similar to what Iran has been facing since 2018, Russia may be tempted to exert its disruptive power in Syria. Tehran may also hope to draw closer to Moscow and gain more room for maneuver. Finally, Israel closely monitors Mr. Putin's temptation to link the war in Ukraine to international negotiations on the Iranian nuclear issue, which were concluding in Vienna before the war. For now, Mr. Bennett is navigating these complex diplomatic waters with caution, aware of the potential implications for Israel's security and regional stability. Bennett is leading these efforts with a small "commando" team, consisting of two advisors for national security and diplomacy, in consultation with Yair Lapid, the powerful Minister of Foreign Affairs. A prominent figure in this team is Zeev Elkin, the Israeli official who has had the longest association with Mr. Putin. A native of Ukraine – his brother lived in Kharkiv before the war – Mr. Elkin has accompanied former Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu in most of his meetings with the Kremlin leader since 2009. As Minister of Construction, he played the role of Cassandra within the security cabinet as early as February, warning his skeptical colleagues about a possible Russian invasion of Ukraine, which he already deemed likely.

title: In eastern Ukraine, the worst-case scenario
lead: With the surge of violence in the pro-Russian republics of the Donbass region, Moscow and its proxies are suspected of manipulating refugees and staging tensions to have a pretext for a military intervention.
text_eng: Between provocations, manipulations, and violence, events have escalated since Friday, February 18 in the Donbass, raising fears of a Moscow offensive. Saturday morning, a Ukrainian soldier was killed by shrapnel, and the leaders of the two pro-Russian separatist republics in this eastern region of Ukraine declared a "general mobilization." In quick succession on Friday, the separatist authorities had announced explosions and staged the evacuation of the population to Russia. These acts of violence, portrayed as Ukrainian provocations, could serve as pretexts for a Russian military intervention. The images, filmed by the separatists and widely broadcast by Kremlin-controlled television stations, could allow Vladimir Putin to justify the operation to his own public opinion. The pro-Russian authorities had initially stated that a car bomb had exploded near an official building in Donetsk, a city under their control. Then, in the neighboring city of Luhansk, a section of gas pipeline had caught fire after a "powerful explosion," according to Russian news agencies citing correspondents on the ground, followed by a second detonation. None of these incidents resulted in casualties. In this area of simmering conflict since the annexation of Crimea and the beginning of the conflict in the Donbass in 2014, the ceasefire has suddenly collapsed over the past three days with a significant increase in shelling (411 violations in the Donetsk region and 1,050 in the Luhansk region, according to observers from the Organization for Security and Cooperation in Europe). These new acts of violence have accelerated calls for the evacuation of populations. On the separatists' social media and then in Russian state media, images have multiplied: children gathered in schoolyards and orphanages for a hasty departure, families leaving their homes, long lines of cars heading towards Russia. The local representative of the Ministry of Emergency Situations in Donetsk announced the planned evacuation of 500,000 to 700,000 people – a statement to be taken with extreme caution. This orchestrated climate of fear was heightened by the sound of gunfire in the distance. Panic spread among some residents, who rushed to supermarkets, banks, and gas stations to anticipate any shortages. Suspicions of a pre-organized operation arose. Throughout the day, the evacuations appeared well-prepared, with lists of gathering points disseminated by local authorities for residents to assemble in front of schools, hospitals, stores, etc. Notices were taped to the doors of buildings urging men aged 18 to 55 not to leave. "It is better for them to stay in the republic," declared Denis Pushilin, the separatist leader of the Donetsk region who, a few hours earlier, had officially announced the evacuation of civilians to Russia, "primarily women, children, and the elderly." His counterpart in the Luhansk region, Leonid Pasechnik, did the same before calling on "all able-bodied men to defend their homeland." Both accuse Kiev of preparing an operation following increased clashes to invade their two republics. However, according to some media analyzing the metadata of the videos, these calls were actually recorded two days earlier. This is one element among others fueling suspicions of a pre-organized operation and a disaster scenario that seems too obvious. Currently leading the operation, Denis Pushilin has been a key figure in well-orchestrated operations in Moscow since 2014. This Donetsk figure, aged 40, had taken the lead in pro-Russian and anti-Ukrainian protests after the "referendum" allowing the creation of the separatist "people's republic," which he has since sought to annex to Russia. President of the republic since November 2018, four months after the death of his predecessor, Alexander Zakharchenko, in a bombing, Denis Pushilin is now zealously organizing the evacuation of the Donbass population to Russia: buses have been made available, and a "green corridor" has been opened at the border to facilitate crossings. Involuntary departures On the Russian side, in Rostov, the reception of the first refugees is also efficiently arranged. Another transport is waiting for them thanks to the rapid deployment of buses ready to receive them. Moscow has sent emergency ministry teams to assist in food distribution and housing allocation. The Rostov region has received 5 billion rubles (nearly 60 million euros) from the federal budget to assist refugees. And the Kremlin has announced that each person will receive 10,000 rubles in emergency aid. Grandmothers have reportedly been taken from their village to be sent to Russia. Other residents have allegedly been taken by force. However, independent journalists in Donbass report that not all these "refugee" departures are voluntary. Grandmothers have reportedly been taken from their village to be sent to Russia. Other residents have allegedly been taken by force. "Residents are urgently called to work. They are being told how to obtain financial assistance. Two buses have already left for Rostov with women and children," a resident of Luhansk told a family member in Ukraine, in Kharkiv, in a message seen by Le Monde (they prefer to remain anonymous for security reasons). "She understood the scam all by herself," commented a member of her family. He is convinced that it is a staged event to show the Russian population Ukraine's intention to perpetrate a "genocide" in Donbass – an unfounded accusation reiterated on Tuesday by President Putin. Traffic is becoming scarce on the roads in the Luhansk region that run along the contact line with the separatist zone. The echoes of artillery fire from the front line can be heard, spaced out but incessant. Luhansk region, Ukraine, February 18, 2022. LAURENT VAN DER STOCKT FOR "LE MONDE" On the other hand, Ukrainian intelligence services urged residents of the separatist territories on Friday not to take the road because they have information "regarding the mining by Russian special services of a number of sites in Donetsk with the aim of later detonating them. These events are intended to destabilize the situation in the temporarily occupied territories of our country and to create a basis for accusing Ukraine of terrorism." Continued diplomacy These new developments come as, for several days now, Russian authorities have been sending a message widely echoed by state media: Moscow does not plan to invade Ukraine, but in case of provocations on the other side of the border, the army could go on the offensive. Especially since, in a well-orchestrated scenario, the Kremlin has had on its table since Wednesday the call from Duma deputies to recognize the independence of the two pro-Russian territories in Donbass. "[Ukrainian President] Zelensky is provoking the start of a major war. If there were a threat to the lives of Russian citizens and their compatriots [living in the two self-proclaimed republics], our country will stand in defense. Civilians are suffering and dying. We cannot close our eyes," warned Duma spokesperson Vyacheslav Volodin on Friday. The spokesperson for the U.S. State Department denounced Moscow's "cynicism," accusing it of "using human beings as pawns." This scenario follows a script that has already proven effective at the beginning of the conflict in Ukraine in 2014, as well as in 2008 during the war between Russia and Georgia. However, there is a major difference: Moscow is no longer relying on the element of surprise. "All of this corresponds to the strategy previously used by the Russians, which involves creating a false justification to intervene against Ukraine," warned U.S. President Joe Biden during a new remote meeting with his European and NATO allies. Shortly before, the spokesperson for the U.S. State Department had denounced Moscow's "cynicism," accusing it of "using human beings as pawns to divert the world's attention from the fact that Russia is strengthening its troops for an attack." French President Emmanuel Macron, who is scheduled to speak with Vladimir Putin on the phone on Sunday, following a discussion with his Ukrainian counterpart Volodymyr Zelensky, called for a "cessation of military actions," expressing regret that "Russian military pressure is not easing." While the situation on the ground is deteriorating, diplomacy continues, with a meeting planned for Thursday "in Europe" between US Secretary of State Antony Blinken and Russian Foreign Minister Sergey Lavrov. As for Vladimir Putin, he expressed regret over the "worsening situation" in eastern Ukraine. The Kremlin leader accused Kiev of refusing to implement the peace plan from the 2015 Minsk agreements. After ordering emergency measures to assist refugees from the Donbass region, he is set to oversee maneuvers of his army's strategic forces on Saturday, including ballistic and cruise missile launches. The clear message is to showcase Russian strength in the midst of a showdown with Western powers.

title: War in Ukraine: Emmanuel Macron, president of crises
lead: "Yellow vests", pandemic, Russo-Ukrainian conflict... so many crises that have shaken Emmanuel Macron's five-year term. The head of state returned yesterday to one of his favorite roles, that of protector of the nation.
text_eng: Editorial. With the Ukrainian flag in the background, alongside the tricolor banner and the European Union flag, President Macron set the stage for his speech on Wednesday, March 2. France is living through the war that Russia is waging in Ukraine, and the whole of Europe is being transformed by it, with the President dedicating the majority of his time to the issue. Solemn yet restrained, Emmanuel Macron put words to this Russian aggression at the heart of Europe, against a country whose only fault is wanting to be free and choose its own destiny. "President Putin has chosen war," he accused. He did so "alone," fueled by a "revisionist reading of European history" and a "spirit of revenge." While clearly denouncing the "lies" of the Kremlin leader, Macron did not backtrack on his own policy towards Vladimir Putin, a key aspect of his foreign policy during his presidency. He also did not mention the futile efforts made in recent weeks, including visiting Moscow, to try to convince the Russian president to abandon war. While these diplomatic efforts, justified, were conducted in perfect consultation with France's partners, it was different for Macron's initial Russian initiatives, which are now seen as doomed to fail against a Russian leader trapped in his resentment. This strategy will be reviewed. For now, Emmanuel Macron intends to stay in contact with Putin and takes responsibility for it, "to tirelessly seek to persuade him to give up arms." He aptly emphasized the distinction between Putin and the Russian people, stating that "France is not at war with them." Unity in the face of aggression The President also aimed to prepare the French for the consequences of the war and the massive European sanctions imposed on Russia, affecting their daily lives and the economy. Returning to one of his favorite roles as protector of the nation, he hinted at a new "whatever it takes" following the Covid-19 pandemic, to assist affected businesses. Finally, Macron addressed the impact of this war, a "sign of a changing era," on the future of Europe. In familiar territory, he sees in this conflict the validation of the ideas he has tirelessly promoted since his 2017 campaign: European sovereignty, the assertion of Europe as a power, and the strengthening of its defense capabilities. With Germany aligning with these goals following the shift announced by Chancellor Olaf Scholz on Sunday, the President announced that the European summit on March 10 and 11 would be dedicated to these issues. One could almost forget that a presidential election is taking place in five weeks and that the president who spoke on Wednesday evening to the viewers-voters had only two days left to formalize his candidacy. Mr. Macron only mentioned it at the end of his speech, advocating unity in the face of Russia's aggression: it was not the focus of his intervention, and he was right not to mix the two subjects. But it is clear, especially in the eyes of the other candidates, that this president, whose five-year term seems to have been a succession of crises, from the "yellow vests" to the pandemic and now to the war at the EU's doorstep, will draw on this for the momentum of a swift campaign.

title: Crisis in Ukraine: Strongly sanction the Russian government
lead: After Vladimir Putin's announcement on Monday, February 21, recognizing the self-proclaimed republics of Donetsk and Luhansk, Western countries must react firmly.
text_eng: Editorial from "Le Monde". There is a foundational principle in international law, agreed upon by all countries that are signatories of the United Nations Charter, including Russia, which is one of the five permanent members of the UN Security Council: that of territorial integrity and political independence of states. For the second time in eight years, Russia, under the orders of its president, Vladimir Putin, openly violated this principle on Monday, February 21, by sending its troops to occupy a part of Ukrainian territory. The first intervention, in March 2014, resulted in the outright annexation of Crimea. The second, on Monday, followed a different procedure: the leaders of two regions in Donbass, the self-proclaimed republics of Donetsk and Luhansk created by pro-Russian separatists in 2014, asked Russia to recognize their independence. This request was granted on Monday. Transported to Moscow, they signed an agreement "of friendship and support" with President Putin at the Kremlin. This agreement provided a legal pathway for the sending of Russian troops, decided immediately under the pretext of "peacekeeping." This decision was preceded on Monday by two highly revealing events about the functioning and motivations of the Kremlin. The first, televised in a cold and reminiscent of the darkest hours of Sovietism, was the Security Council meeting during which several high-ranking officials, intimidated and sometimes stuttering in front of President Putin's mocking smile, recommended that he recognize the two Donbass republics. It was claimed that Ukraine was preparing to become a nuclear power, committing "genocide" in Donbass, and had never had any intention of implementing the Minsk agreements, just like France and Germany, who sponsor them, to resolve the conflict in that region. Familiar rewriting of history As a logical consequence, Mr. Putin later announced his decision in a speech of nearly an hour, echoing the same arguments. Fifty-five minutes of cynicism and familiar rewriting of history, a litany of grievances, fantasies, and false accusations against Kiev and the West, expressed with barely contained violence, punctuated by angry sighs. Mr. Putin's behavior on Monday, as in his recent meetings with several Western leaders – including President Macron –, may raise doubts about the rationality of his reasoning. But the facts are there: Vladimir Putin is the absolute master of the Kremlin, and he has just decided to invade a sovereign country. This is the second time for Ukraine, the third for a former Soviet republic seeking to approach NATO and the European Union, Georgia, whose Russian army has occupied two regions, Abkhazia and South Ossetia, since 2008. This direct challenge to the European order in place since the end of the Cold War poses a major challenge to Western countries and the international community. Eager to avoid a more serious offensive from Moscow, the United States and European countries were leaning towards a "proportionate" response on Monday evening, with targeted sanctions. They are still counting on the deterrent effect of the threat of "massive" sanctions ready to be triggered in the event of an invasion of Ukraine. They want to believe that, since Russia has been de facto present in the Donbass since 2014, it is not strictly speaking an invasion. This response does not measure up to the aggression committed. It legitimizes the 2014 intervention. It does not take into account the deep ambition of the Russian president: to restore a division of the European continent into spheres of influence, based on his own criteria. Experience shows that, unless met with firmness from Western countries, he will not stop at the tenuous border of these two small republics, which claim the entirety of the Donbass. Stronger sanctions are needed, without delay.

title: "How to win the war in Ukraine without fighting it"
lead: In an article in "Le Monde", Julian Fernandez and Jean-Baptiste Jeangène Vilmer, specialists in international relations, identify measures to hasten Russia's defeat without engaging militarily in the conflict. They also emphasize the importance of finding a diplomatic solution to exit the conflict.
text_eng: Op-ed. By deciding to aggress Ukraine and bring the conflict to the heart of its territory, Vladimir Putin has put himself in a situation where he is left with no choice but to choose his defeat: either negotiate by revising down his ambitions to ultimately aim for territorial gain only in the southeast of the country, or persist by further brutalizing the population, even by escalating the conflict beyond Ukrainian borders. The more time passes, the more it becomes evident that Russia is getting bogged down - literally - and the risk of escalation is high as the Kremlin leader may be convinced that this is the only way out for him. In this context, how can we support Ukrainian resistance while avoiding direct confrontation that could lead to a third world war? How can we help Russia lose without giving it a pretext for escalation? How can we win the war without fighting it? In this gray area between war and peace, belligerence and indifference, the question is to maximize our support while staying below the threshold of direct engagement in armed conflict. How? By increasing its cost on one hand, while providing an exit strategy on the other. First, it is necessary to increase pressure not only economically but also militarily. Economically, European states should not wait to be united to individually decide to ban imports of Russian oil, gas, and energy as the United States and the United Kingdom have already done. Militarily, the no-fly zone demanded by some is not desirable, as it would concretely involve NATO planes shooting down Russian planes. But at least three things seem appropriate. Firstly, intensify arms deliveries, especially anti-aircraft weapons which are crucial in limiting Russia's ability to bomb cities. The past few weeks have shown the effectiveness of portable air defense systems (Manpads), including the formidable Stinger, and it is important to send more of them. However, they are only effective against low-altitude targets. In order to force Russian planes to fly lower, making them vulnerable to Manpads, Ukrainians also need medium and long-range surface-to-air missiles - of Soviet manufacture since they are familiar with and know how to use them. The two are complementary and this combination is much more effective than a hypothetical delivery of Mig-29s which would pose an escalation risk without ensuring a major operational gain. In addition to these anti-aircraft defenses, other essential equipment includes portable anti-tank missile launchers - such as the famous Javelin - and armed drones, particularly the Turkish Bayraktar TB2, already used by Ukrainian forces. Purchasing and delivering more of these systems would help slow down the advance of Russian troops. However, supplying weapons is not enough: Ukrainian forces must also know how to use them. These forces are made up of civilians who may not have received prior military training. This question mainly arises for the portable systems described earlier, which are shoulder-launched missiles that are now widely used. In these circumstances, supporting the Ukrainian resistance involves training some of their units. There are still many possibilities here, especially if Ukrainian operators are still able to travel abroad for a few days. On the other hand, it would be risky to send instructors to the area. To avoid becoming a belligerent, the state must also not appear to be "sending" armed bands or groups to fight in Ukraine or allowing them to do so "on its behalf," within the meaning of international law. Furthermore, sharing human and electronic intelligence and supporting civil society engaged in cyberattacks are other measures that are likely already being taken but could certainly be strengthened. Many hackers around the world are now targeting Russian administrations and infrastructure. The Anonymous collective, for example, has stolen and published several important databases that can then be used for more targeted operations. However, these non-state initiatives would be more effective if they were properly directed towards the most relevant targets. Additionally, in what is also an information war, third-party states can provide information and images to counter Russian disinformation, not only in Ukraine and Europe, but also and especially in Russia. The goal is to weaken the regime's "rear" support as much as possible, to reach the Russian population whose access to independent information has been drastically reduced. Various means can be considered to bypass censorship, including shortwave radios and hacking television channels and mobile operators. The Khrushchev precedent All of these measures, and others that may be more or less assumed, would strengthen the Ukrainian side while remaining below the threshold of co-belligerence. Increasing Ukrainian resistance, and thus the cost of the invasion for Putin, could push him to negotiate but also to escalate. Balance is therefore fragile and slippery, especially in a relationship where codes and rules need to be reinvented. The Russian president maintains a strategic ambiguity about the positioning of his red lines, as he himself acknowledged in his speech to the nation in April 2021. So, while taking risks is necessary today, it is not sufficient. Secondly and simultaneously, it is necessary to create the conditions for a way out of the crisis by keeping the diplomatic path open. Peace cannot and should not be abandoned, and the Franco-German duo, in particular, must ensure that communication channels remain open and that the proposed options are creative. It is hard to imagine Vladimir Putin resisting the war effort weighing on Russia until 2036. He may one day decide to save face, if not his head, and remember that Nikita Khrushchev was quickly ousted following the Cuban Missile Crisis. To this end, sanctions must remain targeted, gradual, and - at least for some of them - reversible. Vladimir Putin is beginning to understand the cost of his military adventurism in Ukraine. But if he sees no advantage in backing down, it will be a sure path to disaster, with catastrophic consequences for Europe.

title: Crisis in Ukraine: Westerners cautious in the face of Vladimir Putin's uncertain diplomacy.
lead: The Kremlin surprised by announcing the departure of some units from the Ukrainian border. At the same time, Russian deputies were calling for the recognition of the independence of Donbass.
text_eng: Faced with the contradictory signals sent by Moscow, Western countries are being extra cautious about the chances of de-escalation in Ukraine. "Words are good, but we are waiting for actions," insisted French Foreign Minister Jean-Yves Le Drian on Tuesday, February 15. By announcing the return of some units to their garrisons deployed along the Ukrainian border on Tuesday, the Kremlin caught Western countries by surprise and, through this initial action, wanted to send a message. While they feared a Russian offensive around February 16, according to intelligence provided by the United States, Russia has decided on what could be the beginning of a military withdrawal, signaling a relaxation. However, at the same time, in a well-orchestrated military-political choreography, the deputies of the State Duma (the lower house of the Russian Parliament) called on the Kremlin to recognize the independence of Donbass, the pro-Russian territories in eastern Ukraine. A lever for Moscow in its standoff with the West, the recognition of the Donetsk and Luhansk republics, at the heart of the clashes organized by Moscow between the Ukrainian army and separatists since 2014, would bury the Minsk agreements, the basis of the painstaking peace process in Ukraine. The initiative of the State Duma remains a mere resolution, without concrete implications. However, by calling for these two territories to become "autonomous, sovereign, and independent states," the deputies are threatening Paris' efforts to revive negotiations. The Minsk agreements, under Franco-German mediation, provide for the return of Donetsk and Luhansk under Kyiv's control. The revival of these discussions is one of the achievements that Emmanuel Macron praised after his visit to the Kremlin on Monday, February 7. The French president had then stated that he had received assurances that the State Duma's project would not succeed. "He asked Vladimir Putin not to recognize the independence of Donetsk and Luhansk. The Russian president said it was a parliamentary initiative that he did not approve," the Elysée Palace recalled on Tuesday. This version has not been confirmed by the Kremlin. Dmitry Peskov, Vladimir Putin's spokesperson, simply stated that there was "no official decision." By maintaining ambiguity, he emphasized that the State Duma "reflects the opinion of the population." Contradictory messages "This allows Moscow to play on two fronts and tell the West: 'We are ready for de-escalation, but if negotiations fail, we have the card of recognizing Donetsk and Luhansk in reserve.' This would be a real blow to the West," warns Andrei Kortunov, director of the Russian Council, a think tank on international issues in Moscow. No one knows what is going on in Putin's mind, but with these two new moves, he continues his game of chess. The Russian President, Vladimir Putin, during a press conference with the German Chancellor, Olaf Scholz, in Moscow on February 15, 2022. SERGEY GUNEEV / AP On the military front, the Kremlin also maintains ambiguity. The announcement of the withdrawal of some units from the Southern and Western districts "having completed their tasks" did not come from the presidency, but from a simple spokesperson of the Ministry of Defense. "A usual process," Dmitry Peskov temporized. "We have always said that after the exercises are completed, the troops will return to their garrisons," he explained, once again accusing the West of "hysteria." The situation remains tense as the deployment of the Russian army remains impressive around Ukraine: along the border but also to the north, with drills in Belarus, and to the south with maneuvers in the Black Sea. While the Russian Minister of Defense, Sergey Shoigu, announced that some exercises were "coming to an end," he did not specify the end date for all operations in Belarus. And, parallel to the deployment in the Black Sea, he launched maneuvers in the Arctic waters of the Barents Sea, mobilizing about twenty ships. This is to maintain pressure on the Western countries. Capture from a video published by the Russian Ministry of Defense showing Russian tanks after joint exercises with the Belarusian armed forces in Brest, Belarus, on February 15, 2022. AFP Faced with these conflicting messages, the Western countries prefer to remain cautious. "The fact that we now hear that some troops are being withdrawn is in any case a good sign. We hope there will be follow-ups," said German Chancellor Olaf Scholz after his meeting on Tuesday with Vladimir Putin at the Kremlin. His reaction was awaited, two months after taking office, as Berlin is often accused of being too conciliatory towards Moscow. Olaf Scholz's warning "For Europeans, it is clear that there can be no lasting security against Russia but only with Russia," insisted Olaf Scholz. "It is our damn duty to engage for peace," said the chancellor, not accustomed to strong statements. "We are ready to continue working together. We are ready to go down the path of negotiation," Vladimir Putin replied during the joint press conference, regretting the rejection by the Western countries of his main demands and lamenting the lack of a "constructive response" on their part. Eager to give diplomacy every chance "to avoid a war in Europe," Olaf Scholz warned Vladimir Putin, cautioning that an attack on Ukraine would not go unanswered. "We know what to do in such a case, and I have the feeling that others know it too," he assured. However, as usual, without providing details on the sanctions that could, in such a situation, target Russia. Regarding Nord Stream 2, the gas pipeline that is supposed to transport more Russian gas to Germany and then to the rest of Europe, a project at the heart of a geopolitical tangle between Moscow and Washington, Olaf Scholz used the same rhetoric. "As for the pipeline, we all know what it is about," he declared, eight days after US President Joe Biden stated by his side that Nord Stream 2 would not come into operation in the event of an invasion of Ukraine. Russian President Vladimir Putin (left) and German Chancellor Olaf Scholz during their meeting at the Kremlin in Moscow on February 15, 2022. MIKHAIIL KLIMENTYEV / AP In Washington, the strategy has not changed: above all, do not deviate from a line that appears effective. This is how the United States reacted to the hint of relaxation at the Ukrainian border. The White House greeted with infinite caution the information about a beginning of withdrawal. Joe Biden chose to make a solemn statement himself on Tuesday. He stated that this withdrawal "would be a good thing," but "not yet verified" on the ground. He also reminded that a Russian offensive "remained entirely possible," with Moscow having "150,000 soldiers" surrounding Ukraine. He appealed to history, so often evoked by Vladimir Putin, with a reference to the common fight against Nazism. "World War II was a war of necessity. If Russia attacked Ukraine, it would be a war of choice, a war without cause or reason," said the US President. Ukraine's skepticism Joe Biden reiterated all points of his strategy. A willingness to discuss certain topics such as arms control. But no compromise on principles, including the right of every people to decide their future. A firm commitment, as well, to defend "every inch" of territory of every NATO member, and the expressed willingness to respond to any form of Russian aggression, conventional or hybrid. Giving little credit to public statements made in Moscow, Joe Biden did not express any optimism. On the contrary, he seemed to be preparing the public opinion in his country for all options, including that of a military escalation, and the introduction of sanctions. "If Russia decided to invade, it would also have consequences here, with us. There could be an effect on energy prices," he warned. In order to avoid any divergence, Emmanuel Macron and Joe Biden spoke on the phone again on Tuesday for about an hour. "The shared feeling is that we must take note of these Russian measures and verify their scope and significance," explained the Elysee Palace after their conversation, the fourth in a few weeks. We must remain cautious: Given the mass deployment of military forces, many things are still possible," observes the French presidency. The two leaders also sought to prepare for the future in three different directions: continue the work of deterrence to discourage any Russian attack plans; prepare for a possible way out of the crisis; and initiate discussions on a new European security order. In the short term, the urgency is to strengthen Franco-German mediation between Russia and Ukraine regarding the conflict in Donbass. With a priority on the status of the two pro-Russian separatist republics. Ukraine, under pressure, must implement a bill to this effect and plan a reform of the Constitution. It has set a red line: no direct contact with the separatists, contrary to Moscow's demands. Kiev is particularly cautious given that the Russian announcements of a "partial withdrawal" have been met with great skepticism. "When we see troops withdrawing, we will believe in de-escalation," said Foreign Minister Dmytro Kuleba. New massive cyberattack "We must be very careful and not rejoice too quickly," warns Oleksiy Melnyk, co-director of the Razumkov think tank in Kiev. He reminds us that lying is a classic military strategy. The fact that Russian deputies subsequently voted to urge Vladimir Putin to recognize the independence of the two pro-Russian separatist territories is anything but a coincidence. According to him, it is a coordinated political maneuver aimed at pressuring Ukraine to accept the Minsk agreements on Russian terms. "The message from Moscow is: 'Accept our demand or we will continue the troop movements at the borders.' The escalation strategy continues," says Oleksiy Melnyk. Far from military maneuvers, pressure can take more hybrid forms. On Tuesday, Ukraine was the victim of a new massive cyberattack. The websites of the Ministry of Defense and the Armed Forces of the country were inaccessible in the evening, as were those of the private banks PrivatBank and Ochtchabank. In a statement posted on Facebook, the government center for strategic communications and information security pointed the finger at the "aggressor," a term usually used to refer to Russia.

title: War in Ukraine: Follow on a map the evolution of the Russian invasion day by day
