According to our information, "possible sanctions or suspensions" are part of the ongoing discussions, although these decisions could ultimately only be made by the Audiovisual and Digital Communication Regulatory Authority (Arcom, formerly CSA). The executive branch is not alone in its concerns. Centrist senator Laurent Lafon (Val-de-Marne) has requested Arcom to "immediately suspend the broadcast of Russia Today, which is the official Russian propaganda outlet in France." Subsequently, Arcom assured that it was "closely monitoring RT France's compliance with its legal and conventional obligations." This warning was issued while an investigation is already underway. At the end of 2021, the audiovisual regulator was approached by the association Media & Democracy Europe, which complained about RT France's coverage of the Donbass. In its range of tools, Arcom could also quickly suspend the broadcast by directly appealing to the president of the litigation section of the Council of State, a mechanism inserted in the 1986 law, known as the Léotard law, relating to freedom of communication. However, a solid case must be presented. RT France responded to these criticisms by condemning "the political pressure exerted on Arcom which goes against the legal framework aimed at protecting freedom of expression." The channel also claims to have covered the beginning of the Ukrainian crisis "comprehensively, by presenting all points of view." Balancing act Is this really the case? To maintain its television license, RT France is engaging in a delicate balancing act to avoid being accused of misinformation. On Thursday, its journalists reported all information regarding the Russian strikes, including international condemnations. However, the vocabulary chosen was carefully selected: there was no mention of "war" or "invasion" by the Russian army. If journalists tried to maintain a form of neutrality, the guests on set did not spare their efforts to convey the Kremlin's rhetoric. "People wonder if we are going to close, and what future they can give to their career: will they always be associated with RT France," wonders an SNJ official. "The Atlanticist world did not expect an operation of demilitarization and denazification of Ukraine," judged Karine Bechet-Golovko, a law professor at Moscow University who has her place at RT France, and who thus endorsed Mr. Putin's words. "When there are strategic events for Russia, suddenly there is a takeover of news production in Moscow and an alignment of RT subsidiaries on the same language elements," explains researcher Maxime Audinet, author of Russia Today (RT): a media of influence in the service of the Russian state (INA). Frédéric Taddeï's departure had the effect of an explosion. This heavy criticism against RT France plunged its editorial staff into worry. "People wonder if we are going to close, and what future they can give to their career: will they always be associated with the intensified criticisms towards RT France?" asks an SNJ official. Frédéric Taddeï's departure, who had been hosting "Interdit d'interdire" since 2018, had the effect of an explosion. The famous host justified the end of his collaboration by his "loyalty" to France. "Staying does not mean that we are disloyal to France. Because how do we pay our rent?" grumbles a journalist. Following the declaration of war on Ukraine, Jacques de Guillebon, editor-in-chief of the very right-wing magazine L'Incorrect, who had been appearing in a show on the presidential election since the beginning of the season, also ended his collaboration. "I always thought that I would regain my freedom in case of a problem. The very structure of RT means that there can be no debate on the conflict," he explains. The editorial staff of RT France is made up of journalists comfortable with the Kremlin's line, who justify the editorial treatment of the channel by the right to "information pluralism," and others who have found a permanent contract "rather better paid than elsewhere," says one of them, as the media industry recruits few. "We have received several calls to protest in front of the editorial office," continues the SNJ representative. "We are called 'traitors,' 'collaborators,' or 'enemies of the nation.' We now fear isolated acts against employees on site." Maxime Audinet analyzes: "The contradiction will increase between the requirement to comply with the rules of the Arcom and those of the Russian state, which will need all its media apparatus to advance its narrative." In the meantime, Xenia Fedorova, the head of RT France, sent an email to the teams offering to address their concerns. A general assembly, initiated by the management and the unions, and "dedicated to the situation" will be held on Wednesday, March 2. However, censorship of RT France could lead to retaliation. "RT France is not a threat to democracy, given its low viewership. In case of prohibition, the Russian state would immediately expel journalists from RFI or France 24," predicts the researcher. RT France has only 3.7 million visits per month, according to SimilarWeb. The Spanish and Arabic versions perform much better, with 22 million and 31.4 million visits respectively. RT's impact is not limited to France. In the UK, the Office of Communications (Ofcom) is currently examining its case. In Germany, the regulator has just banned it for legal reasons.

title: War in Ukraine: President Volodymyr Zelensky calls on citizens to fight and promises weapons
lead: In a progress report released on February 24, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky calls on citizens to fight, donate their blood, and promises to provide them with weapons against the Russian offensive.
text_eng: Following the military offensive led by Russia against Ukraine, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky called on citizens to fight and promised weapons. Mr. Zelensky also urged Ukrainians to donate their blood in hospitals for the wounded soldiers.

title: War in Ukraine: China is reluctant to take on the role of mediator
lead: The Chinese government, which does not want to take the spotlight, is concerned about the impact of arms deliveries to Kiev as well as the sanctions imposed on Moscow.
text_eng: China does not currently intend to play a leading role in resolving what it continues to call "the Ukrainian crisis." This is evidenced by the - lengthy - statement published by Beijing after the telephone conversation that President Xi Jinping had on Tuesday, March 8, with French President Emmanuel Macron and German Chancellor Olaf Scholz. Xi had only discussed the issue with one foreign leader before: Vladimir Putin, on February 25, the day after the start of the "special military operation," as Moscow refers to the war it is currently waging in Ukraine. Two phone calls in thirteen days and none for the Ukrainian president. Meanwhile, U.S. President Joe Biden has had seven phone conversations with European leaders and met with Finnish President Sauli Niinistö at the White House. According to the statement, Xi "deeply regrets the return of war to Europe." China has offered its assistance, but it largely relies on "humanitarian corridors" which are often, according to Europeans, a trap set by Vladimir Putin for civilian populations. Xi Jinping reiterated China's consistent position since the beginning of the conflict: "the sovereignty and territorial integrity of all countries must be respected" - without condemning the invasion of Ukraine - and "the legitimate security concerns of all countries must be taken seriously." While "China appreciates the mediation efforts made by France and Germany," it clearly does not intend to take the lead, as confirmed by the statement from the Elysée: "President Xi expressed his support for the actions of France and Germany for a ceasefire." China is even less inclined to take a prominent role as it disagrees on several points with the Europeans. For example, it believes it is "necessary to call for the utmost restraint to avoid a major humanitarian crisis," indirectly criticizing Western arms deliveries to Ukraine. More explicitly, Beijing is concerned about the consequences of Western sanctions, without questioning the war, as they "will have impacts on the stability of finance, energy, transportation, and supply chains worldwide and will weigh heavily on the global economy (...) which is not beneficial for anyone," the statement continues. "Too early" to commit For China, Europe must "pursue its strategic autonomy and promote a balanced, effective, and sustainable European security framework." How? "By conducting an equal dialogue between the European Union, Russia, the United States, and NATO," Xi believes. In Beijing, European diplomats are not surprised by China's reservations. "It is too early, China will only engage when it is sure of achieving a positive outcome and when it will be at the center of the photo," says an ambassador from Southern Europe. "At the moment, this conflict only produces losers: Russia, which is becoming a pariah state, Europe, which shows its fragility, and the United States, which are no longer definitively the world's policemen. In comparison, China loses much less. Maybe a little growth. So, it is the winner," judges another diplomat. "The Balkans, Georgia, Crimea, Syria... On all fronts, it sees the retreat of the West," laments a third. Mentioned a few days ago by Josep Borrell, the High Representative of the Union for Foreign Affairs, the idea of Chinese mediation is not unanimous in Europe. MEP Reinhard Bütikofer (Greens, under Chinese sanctions due to his actions against the repression of the Uyghur minority) considers Beijing to be too close to Moscow. For others, this is not really a disadvantage. "China precisely plays the role of mediator when it is close to one of the parties but judges that this party is going too far," explains researcher Andrew Small of the German Marshall Fund on Twitter. In this case, China "plays the role of the big brother, delivers difficult messages to its partners, conditions the support it provides them, and negotiates behind their backs." But there is no indication that China is ready to do the same towards Moscow, its strategic partner. On the contrary. Washington and Beijing only issued brief statements following the telephone conversation on March 5 between Secretary of State Anthony Blinken and Foreign Minister Wang Yi. "It went badly," decrypts a Western diplomat in Beijing. On Monday, March 7, during his annual press conference, Wang Yi even blamed the United States for almost all international tensions. In this context, Andrew Small even warns those who expect too much from China: "The worst thing would be if China conditions a mediation role on an improvement in relations (...) and obtains concessions without any real return." Is it a coincidence? The Chinese statement begins with a long paragraph on the "green and digital partnerships" that China and the European Union should pursue.

title: The suffering and determination of Viktoria Godzenko, mother of a Ukrainian soldier on the front line.
lead: At 58 years old, the war against Russia has already taken her husband, who fell on the front lines of Donbass in 2016. Today, it is her eldest son who is risking his life on the front lines.
text_eng: Viktoria Godzenko stands upright, as if on springs, in the living room of her modest three-room apartment not far from the center of Kiev, on Monday, February 14. At 58 years old, she has maintained the posture of a dancer, long black hair, and an unwavering determination to fight against the invader. The war against Russia has already taken her husband, who fell on the front lines of Donbass in 2016. Today, it is her eldest son who is risking his life on the front lines. "Of course, I suffer like any mother knowing her child is in danger. He doesn't tell me in detail what he does, he probably isn't allowed to, but I know he is on the front lines, facing the enemy. I only have real conversations with him when he comes home on leave." Viktoria Godzenko eagerly awaits October 2022, when her son Alexei, 30 years old, will complete his commitment. "He volunteered for three years and - thank God - has never been injured. So many of his friends have died... You know, I don't wring my hands thinking: how could I let him go to war? First of all, he is an adult man. Secondly, we raised our children this way. A tragedy has befallen our country. We must resist and defend ourselves, it is a duty. I cannot ask my son not to defend his country! It is a matter of honesty and justice." Viktoria Godzenko was born in western Ukraine, in Lviv, into a family of Russian-speaking military personnel. She is quick to point out that "language has never been a problem, contrary to the stereotypes [propagated by Moscow]. Russian speakers do not suffer from any discrimination. My mother tongue is Russian, and I speak Ukrainian perfectly." When the family moved to the capital, Kiev, in 2000, Russian was still spoken at home. "But from the 'Orange Revolution' [uprising against the fraudulent victory of a pro-Russian candidate in the 2004-2005 presidential election], Alexei, who has a revolutionary nature, decided to speak only Ukrainian, even at home. But I still respond to him in Russian," his mother jokes. "Defending democracy" In 2014, the second wave of Ukrainian patriotism, Maidan Square, deeply affected the Godzenko family. "We were shocked by the police violence against the young student protesters. We forbade Ivan [the younger son, then 15 years old] from joining Maidan, but my husband, Alexei, and I participated, like half of Kiev. We were convinced that we had to defend democracy and continue Ukraine's integration into Europe. I couldn't just sit and passively watch television." This was followed by the annexation of Crimea and the occupation of part of Donbass by military forces controlled from Moscow. "I remember that Alexei and his father debated at length about which of the two should enlist. In the end, my husband, Dmitro, decided that he would go. He wanted to spare his son from the war and fulfill his duty as a defender of the homeland," recalls Viktoria Godzenko. A senior engineer at the state-owned company Naftogaz [a Ukrainian equivalent of Engie], Dmitro Godzenko decided to put his flourishing professional career on hold to enlist as a simple soldier. He signed up for a year and a half in 2015 and was deployed north of the city of Gorlovka, in an area where fighting was intense. "At that time, our army was not equipped like it is today. My husband was a big man, over 2 meters tall and wearing size 46 shoes. No uniform fit him. He fought for a long time in sneakers before friends brought him firefighter boots from Germany in his size." Injured by shrapnel from shells, two of which remained lodged near his spine because they were inoperable, he returned to the front two months later despite his wife's pleas. "I did everything I could, but his determination was unshakable. Dmitro always wanted to follow through on his commitments because that was his character." The culprit? "Putin, of course!" Dmitro Godzenko was killed by a mortar shell at 6 a.m. on the very last day of his one-and-a-half-year contract. "I was waiting for him at home the next day. Everything was already prepared to welcome him, his favorite dishes. It was Alexei who first learned the news. A phone call woke him up in the morning. He dismissed the first bearer of bad news. Someone else called him back immediately to confirm. He came out of his room and passed by me saying, 'Dima [short for Dmitro] is dead,' without looking at me. I, not understanding, asked him what he wanted for breakfast. I was so happy about my husband's return, I didn't hear what he was saying, it didn't register. Then he repeated, 'Dima is dead.' I still didn't understand: 'Which Dima?' Because it wasn't possible, he was supposed to arrive the next day. At that moment, my son fell silent. And the news hit me." A stage manager in events, Viktoria Godzenko fell into psychiatric problems and anxiety disorders from which she only managed to fully recover after five years. Now retired, she helps widows or grieving mothers and volunteers at the archives of a military recruitment center. Who is responsible for her suffering? "Putin, of course!" Viktoria Godzenko exclaims without hesitation. "Not alone, of course, I simplify. It's Russia. But I am certain that there are still many normal, reasonable people in Russia who understand the situation. I also know that Russian propaganda works with great efficiency." Like the vast majority of the residents of Kiev, Viktoria Godzenko shows no signs of anxiety in the face of the possibility of a massive attack by the Russian army. "If they attack, it will be from all sides at the same time. But I am convinced that we can resist their offensive. It will not be a walk in the park for them. We are motivated because we are defending our territory, and we have been ready for this for a long time. Putin is ready to shed the blood of the civilian population. He has no qualms about massacring anyone, not just Ukrainians. Perhaps that is why we are finally receiving support from the United States and Europe. Arms deliveries are a different story from the empty words of concern from the West that we were served until now! I think this change has made Putin think." Certainly, but to think is not to yield.

title: War in Ukraine: In Mykolaiv, residents' anger under the bombs
lead: The Russian-speaking city in southern Ukraine is a strategic point for Russian troops on the road to Odessa. Its residents have repelled dozens of offensives since the beginning of the war.
text_eng: Fourteen days after the start of the war, Mykolaiv is still blocking the Russian offensive on the road to Odessa. Backed by the Southern Bug River in southern Ukraine, this industrial city of half a million inhabitants has repelled dozens of assaults since February 24 and endured just as many bombardments. Wednesday morning, residents woke up to a second relatively calm night, while rumors of an imminent assault from the north, east, and south are on everyone's lips. "I'm tired of sleeping fully dressed! All night, we are waiting for a Russian guest," complains Tatiana Zakharova, 58, referring to the projectiles fired from the Russian positions a few kilometers away. Modestly dressed, with a plastic bag of provisions in each hand, she chats with her neighbors at the foot of her building, 134 Cosmonauts Street. Monday morning, a "Russian guest" had already shown up in the square formed by the buildings, shattering most of the windows, breaking down walls, and riddling the parked vehicles on the street with holes. "It wasn't just one explosion, but at least a dozen," says a neighbor. "It's a Smerch," Vadim believes, inspecting his damaged van. "A cluster munition rocket, that's why we heard many explosions. Look at this hole in the ground [about a meter in diameter], if it had been a Grad [rockets fired in salvos, widely used by both sides], the hole would have been much bigger." According to residents, no one was killed in this bombing, which occurred at 5 a.m. But the incident traumatized Tatiana Zakharova and triggered a verbal outpouring. "Why? But why are they shooting at us? I don't understand. You know, I've always respected Vladimir Putin," she confides, a little apart from her neighbors. "He straightened out his country and led it with a strong hand. I had a good opinion of him. I don't understand why Putin is sending his army here to massacre us. Did I ask Putin for anything, for him to come save me? No. Did I ask if I wanted to go to Russia? No. Does the Russian people want me to come to Russia? No. And no one wants Russia to come here. This is our country, it's our choice, whether Putin likes it or not," she exclaims, without pausing for breath. A gaping hole. Tears well up in her eyes. "I have lived my whole life in Ukraine, while speaking Russian, I am Ukrainian myself," she continues. "But I speak Russian, like most people here in Mykolaiv. No one is attacking us because we speak the Russian language. My husband is Russian, my father is Belarusian, so what? Why do the Russians want to annihilate us? What have we done to deserve living like rats?" She nods toward her staircase. "The most frightening thing for me is these Russian army officers that I know and who live in Moscow. Their parents live here, in Mykolaiv, in this building, they are 80 years old. I have known them since they were little. These people, they shoot at us even though they have their own parents here, under the bombs. How can one understand such a thing? Why do they treat us like this? The Russians may hate us, fine. But to bomb their own parents! Putin is massacring us, but he is also sending thousands of young Russians to their deaths. I feel sorry for them too." Artillery fire can be heard in the distance. No one seems to pay attention to it. On Cosmonauts Street, like in Mykolaiv in general, you see mostly elderly people moving between grocery stores and their homes. Endless lines of cars and buses filled with children congest the road to Odessa, but those who remain, probably still the majority, take the same stance as Tatiana Zakharova: "I stay here because I have nowhere else to go. At my age, leaving on the roads with my diabetic husband... it's beyond my strength." Her apartment has been damaged by the explosions, two windows have come out of their frames. Plastic films have been hastily fixed to the broken windows awaiting repair. The temperature hovers around 0°C these days, and snowfalls are frequent in the region. In the communal kitchen of her building, a "obchejitié" (workers' hostel), Vitali Komar, 76, watches Cosmonauts Street. Through the gaping hole made by a Russian rocket. "Of course, it's Putin's fault," he explains in a dispassionate voice. "This guy has been in power for too long. At first, he was normal, but now, no one wants him anymore. I liked him at first, but I quickly changed my mind." He grimaces at the piercing sound of an electric saw. Municipal workers are busy on the facade of the building making emergency repairs. A former truck driver, Vitali has been to Russia many times. He gestures wearily with his arm: "They don't like us much there. I've been called all sorts of names because I'm Ukrainian. The Russians look down on us," he says with a shrug. Mischievous air. This thin man, who lives alone in a room in the hostel, is not very optimistic about the military prospects. "If you [the Westerners] don't close the sky, Putin will keep shooting at us until the last one." He refers to the debate on the aerial ban on Russian planes by NATO armies, which Kiev has been demanding for several days. "Putin will destroy the [Ukrainian] nuclear power plants. But you don't act because you're afraid he'll press the red button." The only consolation for Vitali: "It seems that the Russian soldiers are fleeing and crying when caught, claiming they were unaware of their mission to invade Ukraine," he sneers. The former driver rants against the Ukrainian president, Volodymyr Zelensky, "who spent all the money on building new roads instead of rearming the country." The same complaint is heard at Emergency Care Center No. 3 in Mykolaiv. "Close the sky, it's the only way to stop this fascist," exclaims 43-year-old Ioulia Krioukova, referring to the Russian president. "The Russians are bombing us with banned cluster bombs and the world is just watching," laments this employee of the Iounost concert hall, slightly injured the day before. "Our guard was killed during the bombing. The missile fell on a children's playground! They bombed at 5 in the morning, like fascists. They attack at dawn, that's their rule." She suddenly takes on a mischievous look: "In the evening, we go to bed in pajamas, because we trust the Ukrainian army! This phrase has become a saying here." Like all the people interviewed by Le Monde, Ioulia Krioukova speaks in Russian, but she adds: "We are mostly Russian speakers here, but now we all want to speak Ukrainian!" This combative attitude, widely shared, has come at a high cost for Viktor Sokol, a 22-year-old man of sturdy build lying in a bed. His mother, Lioudmila, recounts in a blank voice how her son was seriously injured in the head on March 1st. "He is part of the territorial defense [a branch of the Ukrainian army made up of volunteers]. He threw a Molotov cocktail at a Russian armored vehicle. He ran but couldn't get to safety in time. Something [likely shrapnel from a grenade, according to the doctor] hit him in the temple. Shrapnel entered his skull and is now in his brain," she says through tears. "He is in critical condition. Please make sure he doesn't fall into the hands of the Russians!"

title: War in Ukraine: Russian grip tightens around Kiev
lead: At the end of the first day of war, the residents went from astonishment to fear, now fearing the arrival of Russian tanks.
text_eng: As they discover the extent of the Russian military assault on their country and as columns of tanks inexorably approach Kiev, the inhabitants of the Ukrainian capital have gone from astonishment to fear. Even though they are not giving in to panic, more and more people are trying to leave the city as the hours pass. Even though the war was predicted and announced, it caught the people of Kiev by surprise. "I think that apart from those directly involved in this conflict, no one, during the first hours, believed it... Waking up and learning that Russian missiles were falling on Kiev, I was in disbelief. And I don't know a single friend, neighbor, or colleague who wasn't," says Vladimir, whose family, originally from the Donbass region in the East of the country, is accustomed to the sounds of war. While some residents made a point of moving around the city, others sought shelter in underground bunkers during each air raid alert, and many simply stayed at home waiting for news from the front, thousands of people left for the west. After the columns of cars heading towards the city of Lviv or Poland, the railway and bus stations were overwhelmed. For now, trains and buses are still running. Growing concern among the population In front of the ticket counters at the train station, the people of Kiev sometimes wait for hours before being able to buy a ticket. They are calm. They generally carry few belongings, just a bag or a suitcase, as if they planned to return home quickly once the conflict is over. Some only go to the countryside to a family home, wishing to avoid these days of bombings, while others take trains to Poland without knowing when they will see Kiev again. At the bus station, calm also prevails, except in front of the bus to Lviv. This city in the West, where families connected to the government and foreign embassies have already taken refuge, is the most sought after. It is from there that they will wait to see if Russia will halt its offensive or, on the contrary, if it will invade and occupy the country entirely. It is also from there that it will be easier for them to quickly reach Poland. Musician Ratmir Beloded waits in the parking lot, his guitar in hand. Before his eyes, groups of Ukrainians are grappling with foreign workers and migrants to board the bus. The composer and DJ recounts how his life has changed. "Last night, I finally finished an EP I had been working on for a year. It should be called Death Ambient. I finished my work around 4 a.m. and thought I would finally rest for a few days. An hour later, it was war." Seeking a less crowded bus, Ratmir Beloded says he has "no intention of waiting for the Russians, who may be here as soon as tomorrow." After Lviv, he will continue his journey to Frankfurt, Germany. Despite government statements about the strength of the armed resistance of Ukrainian forces, the population's concern has continued to grow. The speed of the Russian advance, however, leads residents to believe that tanks could appear at any moment. Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky did not hide the difficulties in a video message broadcast on the night of Thursday, February 24, to Friday, February 25, following the first day of war. Referring to the "heroism" of Ukraine's defenders and reporting a preliminary toll of victims (137 dead and 316 wounded civilians and military personnel), he warned that "sabotage groups" of Russian special forces may already be infiltrated in Kiev. While designating himself as "their number one target" to "destroy the head of state," he promised not to flee and to "stay in the capital." Arms distribution Mr. Zelensky also indicated that he had signed a decree ordering the general mobilization of conscripts and reservists aged 18 to 60. The government has reportedly already distributed ten thousand automatic weapons in anticipation of urban combat in Kiev, beyond the ranks of the regular army. The Ukrainian president, who is speaking truthfully to the population in this crisis, regretted that Ukraine is "alone" in this struggle against "one of the most powerful armies in the world." "A new Iron Curtain has fallen and separates Russia from the civilized world," added Volodymyr Zelensky. "This is a mistake. It will not be forgiven." As the curfew came into effect with the imposition of martial law, many residents went to sleep in underground shelters. Many are also seeking refuge in metro stations, meant to withstand the worst bombings. After a few hours of respite, the air raids resumed just before dawn on Friday at 4 a.m. While a plane or drone was shot down in the capital's sky and crashed into a building, explosions were heard in the outskirts. Between the armored columns advancing from the north and east and the missiles raining down on the city, the noose is tightening around Kiev.

title: Intelligence services monitoring the departure of French volunteers to Ukraine.
lead: According to the DGSI, between 60 and 70 people, mostly from the far right, are determined to join the ranks of the Ukrainian army.
text_eng: Former military personnel, idealists, students, adventurers: who are the French people eager to join the Ukrainian army? And most importantly, how many of them are there? Since the call for foreign volunteers launched on February 28 by Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky, thousands of them have subscribed to hastily created Facebook pages to connect potential candidates for departure. The most frequently cited motivations revolve around the desire to assist the Ukrainian people, whose unexpected level of resistance against an army presented as one of the most powerful in the world has inspired many. However, their exact number remains difficult to accurately assess for intelligence services, notably the General Directorate for Internal Security (DGSI), which primarily focuses on targeted profiles, such as political activists or individuals most determined to join the theater of operations. The latter are estimated to be between 60 and 70, with a majority being sympathizers of the far-right. Among them, a small group of about ten, identified, have already fought in the Donbass region. "They particularly catch our attention," says a source from the intelligence services, "given the involvement in the conflict of the Azov Battalion, which seems to exert a certain fascination on a fringe of the French far-right." Established in 2014 to combat separatist tendencies in the eastern part of the country, this openly neo-Nazi and white supremacist unit has eventually become an official force and is currently attached to the National Guard. This institutionalization fuels accusations made by Russian authorities against the alleged "neo-Nazism" of the Ukrainian regime. Post-war concerns Despite the Ministry of Foreign Affairs reminding that "it is formally advised against traveling to Ukraine until further notice," it is legally impossible to prevent citizens from freely moving, even when the desire to bear arms in a foreign conflict seems evident. Moreover, nothing prohibits these French nationals from participating in a conflict outside the national borders, except for active military personnel, who are subject to an obvious prohibition in principle. While the provisions of the April 14, 2003 law on the repression of mercenarism prohibit any individual who is not a member of a party to the conflict from participating in military operations, they do not apply to "members of the armed forces of that State." However, the Ukrainian authorities incorporate foreign volunteers into their own army, within an "international legion." This incorporation effectively turns the soldiers into members of a regular army and not militiamen, members of paramilitary groups without official status, or mercenaries motivated by financial gain, to whom the prohibition of the 2003 law would fully apply. Specialist in military law, Elodie Maumont, considers that the involvement of French citizens in the Ukrainian army "does not pose any immediate difficulties if it is done within the legal framework of the host country, with a contract similar to that signed by foreigners enlisting in the French Foreign Legion." The lawyer especially notes the "low probability of seeing a prosecutor, who has sole discretion over whether to prosecute, take action against French citizens solely for their departure to Ukraine, even within combat units, based on current laws." Beyond the risks of French citizens dying and the diplomatic implications of their involvement in combat between two third-party countries, intelligence services are particularly concerned about the post-war period: the return to France of veterans now experienced in handling weapons, individuals suffering from post-traumatic syndromes, or those who may have been involved in war crimes. A military source emphasizes that this possibility is always conceivable in times of conflict, especially concerning individuals who are inexperienced and completely unprepared to live under the physical and moral conditions imposed by the life of a combatant during wartime.

title: The war in Ukraine is dividing the world of cryptocurrencies.
lead: The main exchange platforms have refused to block access to Russian citizens, as requested by the Ukrainian government.
text_eng: It is a major peak, the most significant in a long time: the invasion of Ukraine and the economic sanctions imposed on Russia and Belarus have led to significant volumes of cryptocurrency purchases paid in rubles, as shown by the data compiled by the specialized company Kaiko, based in Paris. On February 24, bitcoin purchases reached a peak of 1.5 billion rubles (approximately 20 million euros, at the exchange rate of that day) on the two main exchange platforms accepting the Russian currency, just before its value collapsed. The data details suggest that these movements are mainly "the work of small investors, with some traders placing larger orders," explains Clara Medalie, an analyst for Kaiko. Russian buyers, "seeking a safe haven," have also invested more heavily in Tether, a "stablecoin," which is a cryptocurrency known to be more stable than "the highly volatile bitcoin," notes Clara Medalie. Russians are not the only ones buying more cryptocurrencies than usual. In Ukraine as well, bitcoin purchases are on the rise, and globally, the cryptocurrency price has exceeded 40,000 euros, well below its historical high of nearly 60,000 euros in November, but showing a clear increase. Cryptocurrencies are also being used by the Ukrainian government since February 26 to directly collect donations. The equivalent of just over 20 million euros was transferred on Tuesday, March 1, to various accounts generated by the Ukrainian government, which thanked the "crypto community as we fight for our freedom." Heated debates On the other hand, a request from the Ukrainian government has sparked heated debates - and divisions - among cryptocurrency advocates: on Sunday, Deputy Prime Minister Mykhailo Fedorov called on major exchange platforms to join the global economic sanctions movement against Russia and freeze the accounts of Russian nationals. These platforms allow the conversion of cryptocurrencies into traditional currencies and are a vital access point for being able to "spend" them. The main exchange platforms rejected the Ukrainian government's request on Monday. Most of them, like the global leader Binance, stated that they could not "unilaterally freeze the accounts of millions of innocent people." For now, there are no sanctions against Russia and Belarus concerning cryptocurrencies or exchange platforms, which emphasize that they comply with current laws. "Furthermore, if we were to freeze the accounts of nationals from countries that attack others and provoke violence in the world, the first step would be to block American accounts," writes Kraken CEO Jesse Powell in the midst of a series of lyrical and provocative messages. In the cryptocurrency community, whose followers predominantly share libertarian ideas emphasizing individual freedom, there are very few voices supporting Vladimir Putin and the invasion of Ukraine. However, many believe that the freedom to control one's own money takes precedence, in principle, over everything else; on social media, sometimes heated debates pit proponents of "freedom" against those who argue, with images of the conflict as evidence, that the "principles" of free flow of money count for little in the face of the suffering of Ukrainian civilians. On Reddit, which hosts one of the largest English-language cryptocurrency discussion spaces (4.5 million subscribers), Binance's decision is overwhelmingly welcomed. The main threads of discussion defend the refusal of exchange platforms to block Russian and Belarusian accounts. One argument recurs frequently: "An entire country cannot be held responsible for the actions of its government." "If you are in favor of a blockade, you have no damn clue what cryptos are. Cryptos = freedom. (...) P.S.: Screw Putin," argues one of Tuesday's most popular posts. The very nature of cryptocurrencies in question These off-the-cuff arguments – which sometimes seem to ignore all the other sanctions already in place against Russia and Belarus – are often contradicted by other enthusiasts who point out that cryptocurrencies and exchange platforms are not the same thing. "Cryptos are decentralized, exchanges are not," summarizes one of them. Faced with very libertarian stances emphasizing the "freedom" of cryptocurrency users, others remind that "cryptocurrencies are just a means to send payments from one person to another without going through financial institutions. That's literally what the first sentence of the Whitepaper [bitcoin's founding document] says. It doesn't talk about 'freedom,' 'sovereignty,' or 'war'." "Satoshi [Nakamoto, pseudonym of the bitcoin creator] never mentioned things like freedom or sovereignty in his text," adds another commentator. "People have simply read too much into what he was writing and make him say things that go far beyond his original text." Vitalik Buterin, on the other hand, made sure his message was very clear: The co-founder of Ethereum, the second most popular cryptocurrency after Bitcoin, was born in Moscow, lives in Canada, and has been very explicit about his stance on the invasion of Ukraine. "It is a crime against the Ukrainian and Russian peoples. I wish you to stay safe, but I know there will be no safety. Glory to Ukraine," he wrote on February 24th. Just a reminder: Ethereum is neutral. Not me.

title: "I feel so powerless": the echo of the war in Ukraine, on the Bosphorus
lead: The conflict triggered by Russian President Vladimir Putin has shocked the Ukrainian community in Turkey - 10,000 people living in Istanbul, Ankara, Antalya, Bursa, and other cities in the country.
text_eng: ISTANBUL LETTER Arriving in Istanbul a few days before the Russian invasion, Snejana, a young architect from Kharkiv, had planned to visit the Ottoman palaces, update her wardrobe, and visit friends who had been living in the city on the Bosphorus for a long time. The entry of Russian tanks into Ukraine on Thursday, February 24, disrupted her plans. Since then, she has spent most of her time trying to reach her mother who remained in Kharkiv, the second largest city in Ukraine, where the Kremlin's troops are attempting to conquer, so far without success. "On the phone, we hear the explosions. The poor woman is terrified. She says she saw a Russian missile land in the middle of Kharkiv without exploding. I am very afraid for her. What can I do? I feel so helpless!" explains the young woman with blond hair, her eyes red from crying. Oksana, in her fifties, who has been living in Istanbul for years, is worried about her family in Kherson, a city in southern Ukraine, "at the mercy of Putin, this bringer of death." In their defense, "Ukrainians can only rely on themselves," she repeats. At the mention of her father and brother, who are volunteers for the territorial defense of Kherson, currently under Russian fire, her face tightens. Glued to her phone from morning to night, the mother follows the bombings on her country live, thanks to the images broadcast by Russian online television channels, designated as "foreign agents" by the Kremlin. "Meduza, Dojd, Nastoiachtchee Vremia really report the situation on the ground in real time. The images broadcast remind me of how the Russian army failed in Grozny in 1996 [during the first Chechen war, between 1994 and 1999]: columns of burnt tanks, demotivated Russian soldiers, lost, hungry, taken prisoner... The fierce resistance of our people uplifts me," Oksana consoles herself, more comfortable in Russian than in Ukrainian. The conflict triggered by Russian President Vladimir Putin has shocked the Ukrainian community in Turkey - 10,000 people living in Istanbul, Ankara, Antalya, Bursa, and other cities in the country. Hence the call to protest on Thursday, February 24, in front of the Russian Federation consulate located on Istiklal pedestrian avenue, in the European part of Istanbul. Only about a hundred people were present. The deployed police outnumbered the protesters. Clearly, they had been instructed to prevent the small group from approaching the Russian consulate, protected by a cordon of riot police. After singing the Ukrainian national anthem, shouting some slogans, the protesters were asked to leave the area. Uncomfortable position of President Erdogan The scene illustrates the uncomfortable position of the Turkish President, Recep Tayyip Erdogan, caught between Russia and Ukraine, his two neighbors on the Black Sea, with whom he maintains cordial relations, buying gas and anti-aircraft missiles from Moscow, and selling Bayraktar TB2 drones to Kiev. Aligned with Ukraine since the Kremlin's annexation of Crimea in 2014, which he has never recognized, Mr. Erdogan is consistent. During a recent meeting of the Security Council, he condemned the Russian attack as "unacceptable." He conveyed this to his friend Vladimir Putin, with whom he spoke on the phone on Wednesday, stating that Turkey "would not recognize any measures directed against the sovereignty and territorial integrity of Ukraine." The Turkish President is walking on eggshells as he must also maintain his good relationship with his Russian counterpart. In this crisis, Turkey is at great risk. Its economic and security exposure to Russia makes it particularly vulnerable, especially at a time when Turkish households, impoverished by inflation (over 50%), are less inclined to support the ruling Islamic conservatives, who are declining in the polls. Three sectors are affected: tourism, with nearly 4 million Russian vacationers received in 2021, energy, with 34% of consumed gas coming from Russia, and finally security, under agreements concluded with Moscow on Syria and Ankara's acquisition of Russian S-400 anti-aircraft missiles delivered to Ankara in 2019. The position of the Turkish authorities has become even more uncomfortable since Kiev demanded the closure of the Bosphorus and Dardanelles straits to Russian warships. According to the 1936 Montreux Convention, Ankara can limit belligerents' access to the straits in case of war. Although allied with Kiev and a member of NATO, Turkey had not immediately responded to this request. So far, the Turkish government had avoided using the term "war," but its position changed on Sunday, February 27. "The situation in Ukraine has turned into a war. Turkey will implement, in full transparency, all the provisions of the Montreux Convention," said Turkish Foreign Minister Mevlüt Çavusoglu in an interview with CNN Türk on Sunday. However, Turkish authorities cannot deny Russian ships wishing to return to their bases in the Black Sea. No Russian ship has recently requested passage. The last to pass through the Bosphorus, six ships and a submarine, are now stationed along the coasts of Ukraine, besieged from all sides.

title: Mykhailo Fedorov, digital warfare chief in Ukraine
lead: The 31-year-old Ukrainian Deputy Prime Minister wants to use all digital tools to put pressure on Vladimir Putin. And the former entrepreneur is trying to rally a part of Silicon Valley to his cause: Elon Musk, Netflix, and Apple have already reached out to him.
text_eng: The public discussion between Mykhailo Fedorov and Elon Musk began on February 26, a little after 1 p.m. We are forty-eight hours after the start of the Russian invasion. The 31-year-old Ukrainian Vice Prime Minister and Minister of Digital Transformation addressed the star CEO of SpaceX and Tesla on Twitter: "While you are trying to colonize Mars, Russia is attempting to occupy Ukraine! While your rockets successfully land from space, Russian missiles are attacking Ukrainian civilians! We ask you to provide Ukraine with Starlink stations and to address the Russians who are still sane to oppose this." Ten hours later, this Internet access service, provided by 1,600 satellites in low Earth orbit, was made available throughout the country. The cooperation did not stop there. "New terminals are on the way," Musk concluded. Several hundred thousand likes and retweets later, the new equipment was delivered to Ukraine. It was then photographed and celebrated by the minister on the evening of February 28, still on the same social network, where he is followed by almost 230,000 people. "This tweet has already made history," explained the politician to M, in an email written from a secure location in Kiev, the capital. The discussion with Elon Musk continued on Twitter: updates on Starlink software, advice on how to deal with possible power outages, and thanks for the "many lives" saved thanks to the technology of the global tech mogul. The messages multiplied, and Mykhailo Fedorov was visibly proud. He emphasized: "Digital technology is our main tool in this unjust war." "The State in a Smartphone" The entry into politics of the youngest minister in the government is recent. The founder of SMM Studio, a digital services company, and unsuccessful candidate of the Ukrainian libertarian micro-party 5.10 in the 2014 legislative elections, joined the team of candidate Volodymyr Zelensky in 2019. During this presidential campaign, he was in charge of digital affairs: his mastery of viral videos allowed the former comedian to quickly gain popularity. After the victory, Fedorov was given a position created for him: Minister of Digital Transformation. In the aftermath, he conceived a project nicknamed "The State in a Smartphone," which would dematerialize most public services. The minister, trained at Zaporijia National University in the south of the country, reached out to Silicon Valley through a team dedicated to developing new relationships with tech giants. From late August to early September 2021, Mykhailo Fedorov and President Zelensky were in the United States. The agenda included meetings with around fifty Silicon Valley executives, including investors, entrepreneurs, and several major CEOs. "We even met with Tim Cook, the CEO of Apple," rejoices Oleksandr Bornyakov, deputy minister, 40 years old, also on the trip. Following this tour, the iconic brand updates Siri so that voice command can work in Ukrainian. Like a confirmation of their good relationship. An army of volunteers With Russia's declaration of war, Mykhailo Fedorov, almost unknown just a few weeks ago, has transformed into a minister of a new kind of war. Ready to fight, but on the digital front. Through the Telegram application, a little over 300,000 people have already joined the IT Army of Ukraine channel, the national digital army composed solely of volunteers. A few hours after the start of the conflict, a first call to wage a "cyberwar" is sent from a bunker in the Ukrainian capital by Yegor Aushev, 39 years old, CEO of Cyber Unit, a company specializing in digital data protection. Coordinated via the encrypted Signal application, the group recruits by invitation. "We do not want FSB agents, the Russian intelligence, in our machine," Aushev specifies. Supported by Mykhailo Fedorov, these code virtuosos seek to render Russian and Belarusian government or banking websites and browsers unusable. "We have already carried out about fifty attacks," the minister specifies, claiming the long-term blocking of online platforms of the Kremlin, the FSB (the Federal Security Service), and several state media outlets. On the second day of the conflict, Illia (the name has been changed), a former Ukrainian defense industry professional turned entrepreneur in the United States, embarks on a "special mission" against Russia. From U.S. territory, he is in contact with Anton Melnyk, an advisor to Mykhailo Fedorov. In addition to facilitating relations with certain American tech companies, he joins an "information influencers team," as he calls it. Composed of analysts, former Ukrainian intelligence officers, and artificial intelligence experts, this informal task force, whose existence is confirmed by the minister's office, fights through screens to show the reality of the conflict to Russian citizens inundated by Vladimir Putin's state propaganda. Their "influence operation" aims to prevent certain Russian families from sending their children to the front lines. To achieve this, the small group creates visuals intended to be disseminated on a large scale throughout Russia. Illia details the questions that this information war seeks to raise: "Which side are you on? The good or the evil?" Silicon Valley Diplomacy Alongside this digital war, Mykhailo Fedorov is pursuing a true "Silicon Valley diplomacy." "In this technological era, it is possible to learn anything at any time, and this approach inspires me a lot," details the thirty-year-old. From the early hours of the conflict, Mykhailo Fedorov and President Volodymyr Zelensky have leveraged their relationship established a year earlier and have written several letters, which were reviewed by Mr. They are addressed to Mark Zuckerberg, the head of Meta (formerly Facebook); Parag Agrawal, at Twitter; and Sundar Pichai, CEO of Google, within the parent company Alphabet. Each letter presents the same request: the complete cessation of their services in Russia to protect their country following a military attack that the Ukrainian president describes as "deceptive and absolutely scandalous." "These companies can harm Russia," explains Oleksandr Bornyakov, the deputy minister. "The war needs to affect the citizens so that they start asking questions." The response from the companies is generally positive. After Apple and Microsoft, the video game publisher Epic Games and the streaming platform Netflix have accepted the Ukrainian request. "It's very clever to hold them accountable like this," judges Cédric O, the Secretary of State for Digital Affairs, visibly impressed by the young Ukrainian minister, with whom he participated in a meeting on March 3. Facebook and Twitter had their access blocked or restricted on March 4 by Russian authorities to control information. Some business leaders still resist and prefer to continue operating in Russia, including Elon Musk, who cites a commitment to freedom of expression. This did not prevent President Zelensky from announcing, on Sunday, March 6, the receipt of new Starlink systems after a recent phone call with the billionaire. The two men also discussed possible space projects in Ukraine once the war is over.

title: The director of the viral images of a bombing in Paris claims it is "a work of science fiction, not propaganda."
lead: "Le Monde" spoke with the director of a video that has been widely shared on social media, depicting Paris under bombardment and calling for more support for Ukraine.
text_eng: A young girl stands in front of the Eiffel Tower. She has raised her arms as tourists sometimes do, and in the images filmed with a smartphone, it looks like she is holding the Eiffel Tower between her hands. Suddenly, an explosion, screams, and plumes of black smoke rise behind her. Another shot on the smartphone, in a typical Parisian street: a fighter jet drops a bomb on a building. The video continues with other images of Paris in flames, against a backdrop of sirens, children crying, and panicked adults. It ends with a message in English – "Imagine if this were to happen in another European capital" – and an appeal from the Ukrainian president, Volodymyr Zelensky: "Close the sky over Ukraine or give us more pilots. If we fall, you fall." The video appeared on Friday evening and quickly spread on social media, particularly on Ukrainian government accounts. Just on the country's Parliament account, the video has been viewed over two million times. Le Monde was able to speak with the director of the video. A French screenwriter, director, and film producer living in Kiev, Olias Barco woke up on Sunday morning to the sound of Russian missiles hitting the Ukrainian military base in Yavoriv. He had already sent his wife and children out of the country several days ago and had moved to western Ukraine. Although he initially chose not to reveal his identity to let the video speak for itself, he agreed to answer some questions once his name started circulating. "To Shock the West" "This was not commissioned by the Ukrainian government," he immediately clarifies, explaining with anger in his voice: "It's something I wanted to do myself, with producer Jean-Charles Lévy, to shock the West. In my opinion, we are in a third world war and this conflict will escalate. It's a work of speculative fiction, not propaganda." The video was filmed in Paris and produced last week from Ukraine. Olias Barco received assistance from a major French company specializing in special effects, which he prefers not to name, as well as the individuals he collaborated with. He wants to protect them from the threats he has started receiving on social media. "We were not paid, not funded," he emphasizes. The video is chillingly realistic. While it features familiar landscapes and professional special effects, its realism comes from its striking resemblance to the images that are shared daily from Ukraine by ordinary citizens now living under Russian bombardment. The framing is random, the images are a bit shaky, you can hear the fear of the person holding the phone when war suddenly appears on the screen. "I wanted this video to be realistic, because I wanted it to touch the world. I made it in Paris because I am French, it's my city," explains Olias Barco: "I wanted to showcase the Parisian monuments while keeping the filming style typical of social media, to show that it could and would happen in Paris. People I work with and live with are falling one after the other because the sky is filled with missiles and no one is doing anything. I made this film in anger. We need to intervene, we need to close that damn sky."

title: The use of an energy embargo divides Europeans.
lead: New sanctions against Russia are being discussed, but the Twenty-Seven are concerned about the countermeasures that Moscow could decide. The issue is expected to animate discussions at the European Council in Versailles on Thursday, March 10 and Friday, March 11.
text_eng: The United States has taken the lead alone, unable to bring the Europeans along with them. However, the debate on stopping imports of gas and oil is not yet closed within the Twenty-Seven. The issue is expected to animate discussions at the European Council in Versailles on Thursday, March 10, and Friday, March 11, without it being possible at this stage to know the outcome, as opinions within the Union diverge. Heads of state and government should at least commit to "breaking free from dependence on Russian gas, oil, and coal imports," but without specifying a timeline, according to a draft of conclusions circulating in Brussels. As the war in Ukraine enters a new phase, with the near-encirclement of Kiev and the prolonged bombing of several major cities, the Twenty-Seven are discussing additional sanctions beyond the massive measures already taken since the beginning of the conflict. A potential embargo on energy supplies from Russia was discussed on Tuesday at the Elysée Palace during talks between U.S. Secretary of State Antony Blinken, President Emmanuel Macron, and Foreign Minister Jean-Yves Le Drian. Highly sensitive issue, however, remains. Indeed, 45% of the gas and 20% of the oil imported by the EU come from Russian fields. A revealing detail is that, to date, Gazprom's bank has been spared from sanctions. "Let's not harm ourselves more than we harm Putin. We must not destabilize our societies at a time when we need to remain united," warned Frans Timmermans, the European Commission's Vice President for Energy and Climate Issues, on Tuesday. Once again, much will depend on Germany's stance. Economy Minister Robert Habeck (Greens) warned of the heavy consequences of stopping imports of hydrocarbons from Russia. "This would not be mere comfort restrictions but significant damage affecting the economy and society as a whole," he said on Tuesday. "The sanctions were imposed to hit the Russian economy and Putin's regime. But they were also chosen so that we could endure them in the long run. Reckless behavior could have the opposite effect," he warned, as 55% of natural gas and 42% of oil imported into Germany in 2020 came from Russia. While German Chancellor Olaf Scholz (SPD) also emphasized on Monday the "essential" nature of Russian hydrocarbon imports for Europe, some members of his majority are on a different line, especially among the Free Democrats (FDP). "The threat from Putin to shut down the Nord Stream 1 gas pipeline shows that our sanctions are hitting him hard," said Marie-Agnes Strack-Zimmermann, the president of the defense commission of the Bundestag (FDP), on Tuesday to the DPA news agency. "We must stay in control and decide to open a new chapter in our gas and oil supplies. Germany must immediately decide to stop importing these two raw materials from Russia." The energy weapon has the unique ability to be activated by both Europeans and Russia, if the latter decides to suspend its supplies in response to the massive sanctions imposed by the West - a retaliation that Russia has so far refrained from announcing. Europeans are aware that they may need to anticipate in order to avoid being subject to Kremlin's decisions. "We are reaching a point where the additional measures we could take are getting closer to the countermeasures that the Russians could take against us," said a French diplomat. "The closer we get to the peak of the crisis, the closer we get to systemic issues." Reflection on "a resilience plan" These considerations could go hand in hand with efforts to share the costs generated by the conflict through a "resilience plan" financed, like the recovery plan during the pandemic, by issuing new common debt. Quietly but surely, work in this direction has intensified in recent days within the Twenty-Seven. It is uncertain whether a final decision will be made at the European Council, but discussions are progressing. On the other hand, the United Kingdom announced, just moments before the United States, the end of oil and oil derivatives imports from Russia "by the end of 2022," as stated by Energy Minister Kwasi Kwarteng, who also mentioned "exploring options to end" gas imports from Russia. The country is much less dependent on Russian hydrocarbons than other European states: only 8% of its oil consumption and 4% of its gas consumption are imported from Russia. For its electricity consumption, it can now rely on wind power, especially offshore, which has seen significant development, on nuclear power, and on North Sea oil - both extracted off the British coast and Norwegian imports. The ban on Russian products will still have an impact, warned Mr. Kwarteng: industrialists "must take advantage of the current year to smoothly transition." Boris Johnson had warned the day before that he was considering the possibility of "increasing hydrocarbon extraction" in the North Sea, while ensuring that the country's carbon neutrality goals (by 2050) would be maintained.

title: War in Ukraine: Western countries demand "unconditional withdrawal" and prepare a response that will be "massive" and "costly" for Russia.
lead: The European Council is expected to decide on Thursday evening on the extensive sanctions plan that has been prepared for weeks by the Twenty-Seven against Russia.
text_eng: The war initiated by Vladimir Putin in Ukraine on Thursday, February 24, has sparked a wave of condemnations and consultations from Western capitals. They had been on high alert since Wednesday afternoon when new intelligence emerged about an imminent large-scale offensive. "President Putin has chosen to launch a premeditated war that will result in catastrophic human suffering and loss," said U.S. President Joe Biden in a statement. "Russia alone is responsible for the death and destruction that this attack will cause," he emphasized, stating that "the world will hold Russia accountable." The European Union strongly condemns the "unjustified invasion of Ukraine by Russian forces" as well as the "involvement of Belarus" in this aggression. The Twenty-Seven also call on the Russian president to "immediately cease military operations and unconditionally withdraw all military forces from the entire Ukrainian territory." Sending weapons, Emmanuel Macron was able to reach his Ukrainian counterpart Volodymyr Zelensky on his mobile phone early in the morning to express "full support and solidarity" from France at a time when Kiev was being targeted by bombings. Zelensky, according to the Elysée, "described the situation and requested multiple interventions to support Ukraine." Following this, the Elysée convened a defense council. Convened on Wednesday afternoon as the Russian offensive seemed imminent, the European Council is expected to decide on Thursday evening the extensive sanctions plan prepared by the Twenty-Seven in close consultation with the United States. Member state representatives met early in the morning to finalize the details of this response, described as "massive" and "costly" for Russia. This includes blocking exports in various sectors, particularly high-tech products. On Thursday afternoon, the G7 leaders were also scheduled to hold a video conference, initiated by German Chancellor Olaf Scholz, who currently chairs the group. The focus was on coordinating different sanction plans to respond quickly to Vladimir Putin's decision to go to war. With little optimism, the aim is also to encourage him to return to the negotiating table to try to restore security across the entire European continent, starting with Ukraine. NATO is also on high alert. "We stand with the Ukrainian people in this terrible moment. NATO will do whatever it takes to protect and defend all allies," said Secretary-General Jens Stoltenberg. A meeting of the North Atlantic Council (NAC), the main decision-making body of the organization, was immediately convened in Brussels. The consultation process with allies as outlined in Article 4 in case of external aggression was set to be activated on Thursday morning, at the request of several Central and Eastern European capitals, as well as Paris. The purpose is to review defense plans established for NATO countries bordering Russia or neighboring Ukraine, who may feel threatened by the Kremlin's offensive: Poland and the Baltic States on the eastern flank of the Alliance, as well as Romania and Bulgaria. Since Ukraine is not a member of the Alliance, the mutual assistance clause of the North Atlantic Treaty (Article 5) cannot be invoked to militarily come to its aid. However, some voices, including that of Tobias Ellwood, the influential chairman of the British House of Commons Defense Committee, have called on NATO to send weapons to Ukraine. Without delay, British Prime Minister Boris Johnson was also preparing to announce additional military aid to Kiev bilaterally later in the day. UN Vote Lastly, the United Nations Security Council serves as another channel for coordination. "President Putin, in the name of humanity, withdraw your troops to Russia!" exclaimed Antonio Guterres, the Secretary-General of the United Nations, right after the announcement of Russian military operations in the middle of a Security Council session. "This is the saddest moment of my tenure as Secretary-General of the United Nations," he added. A resolution project, led by the United States to condemn the offensive, was to be put to a vote later on Thursday. This text stands no chance of being adopted due to Russia's veto. "We call on Russia to respect international humanitarian law in all circumstances, we call for the protection and respect of all civilians, especially vulnerable individuals, women, children, and humanitarian personnel," declared the French Ambassador to the UN, Nicolas de Rivière. Western nations hoped, albeit with little belief, that China would abstain, even though it has recently aligned more with Russia. Emmanuel Bonne, President Macron's diplomatic advisor, was set to contact his Chinese counterpart in the morning to discuss the situation.

title: "Vladimir Putin has been waging a continuous assault against Ukraine for years."
lead: For the Kremlin leader, the fall of the USSR resulted in Russia being deprived of vast territories. Eager to reclaim them, he has continuously adjusted his strategy since coming to power in 2000.
text_eng: Chronicle. The military operation announced by Russian President Vladimir Putin in Ukraine on Thursday morning, February 24 at dawn, was denounced from Washington by his American counterpart Joe Biden as an "unjustified attack." This offensive will cause "catastrophic suffering and human losses," according to the White House. However, Russia's intervention in Ukraine is not a recent development, not even since 2014 when the war, already well underway, erupted in the Donbass region between pro-Russian separatists supported by Moscow and the Ukrainian army. Interference, pressure, cyberattacks, military intrusions, violations of borders and treaties, hostage-taking, annexation of the Sea of Azov, up to the recognition today of the independence of the self-proclaimed republics of Donetsk and Luhansk, it is a continuous assault that Vladimir Putin has been waging against the neighboring country of Russia "that does not exist" for years. Even before coming to power in 2000, the Kremlin chief had already expressed his views on the "gifts" that Russia, the self-proclaimed heir of the former USSR, had supposedly abandoned to the former Soviet republics. In 1994, while he was still the deputy mayor of Saint Petersburg, Anatoly Sobchak, he mentioned the loss of "gigantic territories" during an obscure symposium, citing, among other things, Crimea. Twenty years later, the Ukrainian peninsula was annexed by Russia. Putin does not improvise. He adapts his planned strategy to the circumstances. Perceived as a "paranoid complaint," his vision of Ukraine presented on Monday, February 21, before his Security Council, and then on television – as a "creation of Vladimir Ilyich Lenin," as he claimed – is part of a well-rehearsed discourse that has gradually materialized into a tragic reality, step by step. The first step took place in 2004 during the Ukrainian presidential election, which pitted Viktor Yushchenko against Viktor Yanukovych in a toxic atmosphere, amidst suspicions of poisoning of the former, while the anticipated victory of the latter, supported by Moscow, sparked massive protests. The "Orange Revolution," the color worn by Yushchenko's supporters, ultimately led to his victory after a new vote was organized. Yanukovych was defeated, but only temporarily. For Vladimir Putin, the idea of Ukraine, with its 603,000 square kilometers and 44 million inhabitants (18% of the population of the former USSR), moving closer to the West is inconceivable. The so-called Western-backed color revolution that unfolded then demanded revenge. A real war. The second major and brutal stage occurred ten years later in 2014, after thoroughly modernizing his army. From now on, it is not only the Ukrainian political scene that the Kremlin leader intends to control, but its identity and borders, which were confirmed by a referendum in 1991 and internationally guaranteed by the Budapest Memorandum. Signed in December 1994, the memorandum ensured "the independence, sovereignty, and existing borders of Ukraine." In exchange, Kiev committed to getting rid of the stockpile of nuclear weapons it had inherited from the Soviet era. It is precisely with this treaty violation that the "beginning of invasion" of Ukraine physically begins. Russian special forces are deployed in Crimea to take possession of the peninsula, while others infiltrate the Donbass region in eastern Ukrainian territory. At the time, American and Western intelligence agencies were aware of these movements, but it was not yet a matter of public knowledge. Since then, the clashes in this part of the territory have resulted in 14,000 Ukrainian deaths on both sides, while thousands of civilians have had to flee their homes. Yes, indeed, a real war. Just read the excellent novel by Andrei Kurkov that has just been released, "The Gray Bees" (Liana Levi, 400 pages, 23 euros), to grasp the full reality of it. With disarming simplicity, the Ukrainian writer describes the daily lives of retirees isolated in abandoned villages at the heart of a "gray zone" that separates pro-Russian separatists from the rest of the Ukrainians on the front line. Crimea, however, has been written off. After a wave of protest, Western powers have stopped seeing the word "war" in Ukraine and have instead opted for the post-Soviet terminology of a "frozen conflict." Despite the stalling of mediation attempts under the so-called "Minsk agreements" and its Normandy format appendix, European and American leaders have, more or less, come to accept the situation. Gradually, despite principled statements, the idea of relegating Crimea to the realm of losses and gains has even gained acceptance. The assault, however, continued. Less visible, indeed, but equally effective, this time through the distribution of thousands of passports to residents of the Donbass. A "technique" already tested in 2008, before Moscow's unilateral recognition of the independence of South Ossetia and Abkhazia, two separatist regions of Georgia, which thus lost 20% of its territory. Then, as a third step, Russian troops began to mass at the borders of Ukraine, 10,000, 40,000, 80,000... In April 2021, the Russian Ministry of Defense promised their withdrawal after "exercises." The same scenario repeated itself just eight months later, in December 2021: massive arrivals of soldiers and equipment, with yet another unfulfilled promise. Up to the encirclement, today, of Ukraine, with the support of Belarus, subservient to Moscow, where, here too, Russian troops "on exercise" have the unfortunate tendency not to disappear afterwards. For now, Crimea and the Donbass of separatists under Russian tutelage represent less than 10% of Ukrainian territory, but for how much longer? This is one of the major weaknesses of the Western powers: they do not take Vladimir Putin's words at face value. In his speech, the Kremlin leader makes no distinction between regions, he does not compartmentalize. He speaks of Ukraine. And he advances.

title: Gabriel Attal praises "the heroism of the Ukrainians who have chosen to resist."
lead: The government spokesperson was a guest on France Inter, France Télévisions, and "Le Monde" on Sunday, from noon to 1 p.m.
text_eng: Gabriel Attal, the government spokesperson, was a guest on "Political Questions" on Sunday, February 27 on France Inter. Interviewed by Thomas Snégaroff, along with Nathalie Saint-Cricq (France Télévisions), Françoise Fressoz (Le Monde), Laurence Peuron, and Alexandra Bensaïd (France Inter), he was extensively questioned about the war in Ukraine and the economic sanctions against Russia. "Russia has chosen total war, we proposed an alternative of discussion and dialogue, but it was swept aside," lamented Gabriel Attal. "The cost of the war must force Putin to reconsider his plans." "I want to salute the heroism of the Ukrainians who have chosen to resist. Things are not going as planned for Vladimir Putin," declared the government spokesperson. "He expected the Ukrainians to surrender, but they chose to resist. He expected the allies and Europe to be weak, but we are firm and united. We have decided on a historic package of sanctions, of an unprecedented strength," he rejoiced. "The cost of the war must be so significant that Vladimir Putin is forced to reconsider his plans and abandon them." Regarding the defensive weapons deliveries requested by Ukraine from France, Gabriel Attal confirmed them and announced that "we continue to deliver them and we will increase our deliveries." "President Zelensky has communicated a very clear list of equipment he needs, and we are responding to it," he stated. "We are calling for a ceasefire to resume negotiations," Mr. Attal reiterated. When asked if it could be said that France is at war, he believed that "our country is not at war," but that "it stands alongside a country, Ukraine, to which Russia is waging war." "France supports Ukraine in humanitarian matters, defense equipment, and is also mobilized to achieve a ceasefire and a resolution to this conflict," he explained. "There will be a democratic debate." The government spokesperson was then questioned about Europe's gas supply. "There is no risk of shortage - we have stocks and reserves ahead of us," but "there is a risk in terms of prices," said Gabriel Attal, highlighting that the gas price freeze extends until the end of 2022. As for the economic impact of the conflict on the French economy, there will be one, "we must not hide behind our little finger," but the government, which is working on a "resilience plan" for agriculture and food, promises to be "mobilized to limit it." While Emmanuel Macron is still not officially a candidate for re-election, "there will be a democratic debate, a clash of projects, we will find the modalities," assured Gabriel Attal, noting that this situation of war in Europe six weeks before the presidential election was "unprecedented."

title: Ukrainian crisis: The United States defends their strategy of gradual sanctions against Russia.
lead: Joe Biden announced on Tuesday a "first set" of economic measures directly targeting Moscow. For Washington, this is seen as the beginning of our response to the "start of an invasion."
text_eng: The United States and Russia are facing off, but each on its own turf. Moscow has put the world before a fait accompli by recognizing the two self-proclaimed republics in Ukrainian Donbass. Washington, on the other hand, has long made it known that it will not engage in a military conflict with unpredictable consequences. However, the Biden administration has decided to use another attribute of power and sovereignty to respond to Russia: the dollar. "Nearly 80% of Russia's daily currency transactions are in dollars and almost half of Russia's international trade is conducted in dollars," the White House reminds in a public note. Following the introduction of sanctions focused on investments and trade with Donbass, Joe Biden announced on Tuesday, February 22, a "first tranche" of economic measures directly targeting Russia. Beyond the freezing of the Nord Stream 2 gas pipeline, which Washington seems to claim credit for along with Germany, the United States carefully weighs each announcement. Some measures that have been discussed for months are still being held in reserve. For now, there is no talk of banning exports to Russia of equipment with American components, or disconnecting the country from the Swift financial information system, which would have dire consequences for Western companies. In this crisis, everything has a price: Russian military actions, as well as European and American responses. Each decision is a testament of determination and a message to the other party. "For heaven's sake, where does Putin think he has the right to recognize so-called new countries on territory belonging to his neighbors? This is a blatant violation of international law, requiring a firm response from the international community," declared the American president. On his part, Secretary of State Antony Blinken, who met with his Ukrainian counterpart Dmytro Kuleba, denounced "the most serious security threat in Europe since World War II." The Ukrainian minister did not criticize the American strategy, but dismissed considerations about the different levels of severity of Russian actions. "There is no such thing as a minor, moderate, or major invasion. An invasion is an invasion." Leaving a glimmer of hope for diplomacy, Washington insists that sanctions are not an end in themselves. It is about leaving a glimmer of hope for diplomacy. However, Vladimir Putin himself is making choices easier by tearing up the Minsk agreements and openly expressing his denial of Ukraine as a sovereign country. Therefore, Secretary of State Antony Blinken logically canceled his planned meeting with his counterpart Sergey Lavrov in Geneva on Thursday. No meeting between the American president and Vladimir Putin is being considered anymore. "This is the beginning of the invasion of Ukraine," acknowledged Joe Biden. Washington has announced sanctions equivalent to entries in a substantial meal. Two state-controlled Russian banks are targeted: Vnesheconombank – presented as serving the Kremlin – and Promsvyazbank – linked to the defense sector. Both banks represent $80 billion (€70 billion) in assets, which is much less than the main banks in the sector. Therefore, this is more of a warning than a powerful blow to the Russian banking sector. "We are ready to press a button to take further actions against the largest Russian financial institutions, including Sberbank and VTB, which collectively hold nearly $750 billion in assets," warned a senior administration official during a press briefing. He summarized the American approach in one sentence: "This is the beginning of an invasion and the beginning of our response." The speed of the response is remarkable compared to the sequence of events in 2014 after the annexation of Crimea.
