However, only more severe measures could strike a blow to the Russian elite and the Kremlin. This includes decisions to prevent the issuance of new Russian sovereign debt on the secondary market. In the medium term, Moscow should not suffer from this decision, especially since the Kremlin has nearly $620 billion in foreign exchange and gold reserves and has worked hard to make the country less dependent on foreign creditors. Deployment of additional American forces The same applies to individuals targeted by the sanctions. They include Alexander Bortnikov, the head of the Russian security services (FSB), and his son; Sergei Kirienko, deputy chief of the presidential administration, and his son; and finally, Pyotr Fradkov, the head of Promsvyazbank. However, Vladimir Putin himself is not on this list, which should not greatly concern those involved. "These individuals have benefited from Kremlin gains in corruption, and they will now share the pain," said the senior administration official to journalists. Other members of the Russian elites and their families are now warned that additional actions could affect them in the same way. Joe Biden also announced the redeployment of additional forces in the Baltic countries for purely defensive purposes, in an attempt to reassure the eastern flank of NATO. Nearly 800 soldiers from an infantry battalion based in Italy, eight fighter jets, and twenty Apache attack helicopters are involved. Twelve more helicopters of this type will be sent to Poland. In total, there are currently 90,000 American soldiers in Europe, according to the State Department. But Joe Biden emphasized on Tuesday that none of them will be ordered to fight Russia. The American president has another concern at the moment, the consequences of his own sanctions on the energy market, and the price of gasoline at the pump.

title: "The war in Ukraine dampens the euphoria of the Morozov exhibition."
lead: The event at the Louis Vuitton Foundation, which has already attracted over a million visitors, is part of Russian "soft power." Its continuation, following the Russian army's offensive in Ukraine, is sure to raise questions, notes Michel Guerrin, editor-in-chief at "Le Monde," in his column.
text_eng: Article. Can economic sanctions against Russia affect culture? The question is on the table at a time when the Louis Vuitton Foundation in Paris is hosting an exhibition of the collection assembled at the turn of the 20th century by the Russian brothers Mikhail and Ivan Morozov, consisting of 200 modern paintings, and not the least. This event, scheduled until April 3, is part of Russian "soft power," as it could only be organized with the blessing of Vladimir Putin himself. Who has now shifted to "hard power." "If one question must remain open, it is culture," declared the autocrat of Moscow in 2016, at a time when the war in Syria and the Ukrainian issue - already - chilled the friendship with France. The Louis Vuitton Foundation had just opened another Russian exhibition, the collection of Sergei Shchukin, as part of a Franco-Russian year, which also included the inauguration in Paris of the Russian Orthodox cathedral and cultural center, fully funded by Moscow. Not to mention the donation by the oligarch Vladimir Potanin of over 250 works from his collection to the Pompidou Center. This cultural barrage, not to mention other appetizers, prompted the catchy headline in L'Opinion: "France and Russia can only see each other through painting." How far can this openness be sustained? While Ukraine is being bombed and Putin intends to "denazify" this country, the foundation of Bernard Arnault, head of the luxury group LVMH, is at the very least embarrassed. Its management has informed us that, at this stage, they do not intend to suspend the exhibition. More than Russia, it is the visitors who would be penalized. But the foundation also adds that it is not turning a blind eye, and that it is waiting to see how the conflict evolves and what decision the Elysée Palace will make. Dazzling art Nevertheless, the war in Ukraine dampens the euphoria. The Morozov collection has already attracted over a million visitors in five months, making it the most visited exhibition in France by far. The time slots for visits are already well filled for the upcoming last month, to the extent that free evening openings are being considered. Undoubtedly, the goal is to surpass the Shchukin collection, which totaled 1.3 million entries in 2017, which would make Morozov the most visited exhibition of all time in France. Moreover, in the context of the Covid-19 pandemic and constrained capacities, the achievement would be significant. The Louis Vuitton Foundation is privately owned, giving the exhibition a lesser symbolic weight than if it were held in a state museum. Above all, the hanging is not "Putin-esque." It is solely about art - dazzling art. The paintings are by Matisse, Gauguin, Cézanne, Manet, Monet, Van Gogh, or Picasso, combined with Russian artists; enough to seal the friendship between the two countries. We are far from the Russia Today (RT) channel, which is increasingly contested in Europe, particularly in France, where journalist Frédéric Taddéi has just suspended his program "Interdit d'interdire" out of "loyalty to France." Let's not be naive. Diplomacy and economics also guide the Morozov event. The President of the Republic inaugurated it in front of a rich Russian delegation, recalling what binds the two countries and "all that separates us." And then the exhibition catalog is intriguing. It opens with a text by Emmanuel Macron, praising "the bridges that artists and art lovers have built" between Russia and France, and another by Vladimir Putin, addressing his "French friends." It is rare for two heads of state, especially significant ones, to sign texts in an exhibition catalog. The fact that the latter has just referred to the former as "paranoid" makes the reading strange. "Dirty" patronage Also strange, in hindsight, is the meeting between Bernard Arnault and Jean-Paul Claverie, his special advisor, with Vladimir Putin in November 2016 at the Kremlin. The French made the trip to Moscow to thank the new Russian tsar for the Shchukin gift, freshly hung on their Parisian walls. In the excitement, Jean-Paul Claverie suggested one day hosting the Morozov collection. Yes, Putin replied. When obstacles arose, it was again him, during his visit to Versailles in the summer of 2017, who unblocked an exhibition that France had included in the Trianon Dialogue project, aiming to increase economic and cultural initiatives between the two countries. The foundation, without being obliged to believe it, swears it did not provide any cash for this Morozov exhibition. It funded the restoration of paintings, often severely damaged, housed in the three lending Russian museums (Hermitage, Pushkin, Tretyakov), while also funding an expertise and restoration laboratory at the Pushkin Museum. Not to mention the insurance and transportation (40 trucks). In the end, the bill is high. Bernard Arnault and the Louis Vuitton Foundation have means that French museums do not. And the president of LVMH, who presented part of his own art collection at the Pushkin Museum in 2019, has a commercial activity in Russia (luxury clothing, cosmetics, alcohol...) that museums do not have either. At a time when museums are forced to give up patronage deemed "dirty," such as that of oil companies, it will be interesting to see if the Morozov exhibition is criticized and if attendance will decrease. In any case, the site's security will be reinforced. There are already voices, in a joking manner, suggesting that freezing the Morozov collection in France would be a way to punish Putin. However, a law from 1994 prohibits seizing works "loaned by a foreign power." Let's imagine instead that their repatriation will be delicate. And that the cultural and economic bubble, patiently woven by the luxury group, suddenly burst.

title: War in Ukraine: In Chambéry, Eric Zemmour tries to defend his U-turn on Russia
lead: In a meeting on Friday, February 25, the far-right candidate tried to justify himself after downplaying for months the threats posed by Moscow, by accusing the "propaganda" of the "American services."
text_eng: On the second day of the war in Ukraine, Eric Zemmour continued his campaign undeterred. "Obviously, the event is serious, and a war in Europe is not something trivial," acknowledged the candidate, passing through on Friday, February 25, at a farm in Haute-Savoie. "But we are in a presidential campaign, which is a democratic appointment that is also not trivial." He assured that people were waiting for him in the region and that he "did not want to disrespect them." Furthermore, "there is a war in Europe, but there is no war in France." The far-right candidate took advantage of the lunch to gather three endorsements, including the one, extensively staged, from Antoine Valentin, the Republican mayor of Saint-Jeoire, in Haute-Savoie. The mayor had organized a consultation of his 3,400 constituents a month ago to decide whom to endorse. The results are not available anywhere, but Eric Zemmour came out on top, according to the mayor, followed by Jean-Luc Mélenchon and Marine Le Pen. "Let's be clear, the endorsement is not a political support," he had specified in a video, but it was ultimately he who opened Eric Zemmour's evening rally and called for "reconquest." His endorsement was actually a half-surprise: he had already published articles in 2021 in Valeurs actuelles praising Bonaparte and the "real country," dear to Charles Maurras. On February 25, 2022, Eric Zemmour received the endorsement of the Republican mayor of Saint-Jeoire (Haute-Savoie), Antoine Valentin. Eric Zemmour, for his part, was mainly trying to overshadow in the high mountains his embarrassing statements about Russia and his strong admiration for Vladimir Putin. "The problem in Ukraine is not an invasion, I don't believe in it," he asserted on December 9, 2021. "I'm willing to bet that Russia will not invade Ukraine." "I think there is a lot of propaganda, agitation by American services to hype up this story," he said again on February 20. Twenty-four hours later, Vladimir Putin declared recognizing the independence of the pro-Russian separatist areas. "We will not be beholden to anyone." The candidate avoided revisiting the topic all day on Friday and assured that his speech in Chambéry would "address all questions" during a meeting "imbued with more gravity than the previous ones." He ultimately settled for two sentences. "Remember, not long ago, many of us thought that no side would let the situation escalate to war," he declared before an excited audience of nearly 3,500 people, "and our government was sure it had convinced the Russians not to intervene. And we had reasons to believe that we would avoid war." He attributes the responsibility to the United States, who intend to "push the boundaries of NATO further each time." Indeed, "we know how to be wary" of the Russians, who "can surprise, betray" and do not hesitate to go to war. "Putin's Russia lives in the 19th century, and sees war as a means to defend its strategic vision," assured the far-right polemicist. But "it is not by ostracizing Russia, it is not by sanctioning its nationals, it is not by ignoring its issues that we will succeed." And to estimate, like Talleyrand, that "diplomacy was about knowing how to put oneself in the place of one's interlocutor." In the stands of Eric Zemmour's meeting in Chambéry on February 25, 2022. AGNES DHERBEYS / MYOP FOR "LE MONDE" In substance, the candidate proposes to sign a peace treaty, to enshrine in stone the halt of NATO's eastward expansion, in exchange for a ceasefire and the withdrawal of Russian troops "from separatist areas," without even explaining what he would do with Crimea, annexed in 2014 by Moscow. He repeated, as at Mont-Saint-Michel on February 19, that France had "a duty of power and independence," and that he intended to increase the military budget from 41 billion to 70 billion euros per year by 2030. "We will not be beholden to anyone, not the Americans, not NATO, not the Russians, not the Chinese, not Europe," declared the candidate – "not even the UN." In the electric atmosphere of the meetings, the candidate reassures himself about his destiny, as the audience barely listens, sometimes covering his speech with "we love you!" and shouting "Zemmour for president!"

title: Presidential election 2022: candidates present their ideas without direct exchange
lead: Eight out of the twelve presidential candidates, including Emmanuel Macron, participated on Monday evening on TF1 in the first major televised electoral event, during a show where they never had the chance to meet or debate with each other.
text_eng: 26 days before the first round of the presidential election, eight of the twelve candidates, including Emmanuel Macron, presented their projects on Monday, March 14th evening on TF1, one after the other, for the first major electoral evening, largely devoted to the war in Ukraine. Relations with Vladimir Putin, the role of Europe, energy and fuel, purchasing power, pensions: in turn, the eight contenders for the Elysée Palace outlined, in a polite atmosphere, their proposals to address the current crisis, in a format where the candidates never crossed paths or directly exchanged. Mr. Macron's opponents, who had previously imposed their conditions on the TF1 channel by refusing an eight-way debate, much to the chagrin of his opponents, took the opportunity to criticize the actions of the incumbent president-candidate, who leads in the first-round voting intentions according to polls, far ahead of the far-right candidate Marine Le Pen. Ukraine at the center of discussions "What I am trying to do on behalf of France, but what we are trying to do as Europeans, is to stop this war without waging war," argued the head of state, who indicated that he was mobilized to try to obtain a ceasefire. When asked if he considered his Russian counterpart Vladimir Putin a "dictator," Mr. Macron replied: "It's not by insulting or labeling that it will be most effective," specifying that he intended to continue talking to him. Marine Le Pen, as well as the Insoumis Jean-Luc Mélenchon (LFI) and Eric Zemmour, all three suspected of past connections with the Russian president, also refused to comment, preferring the term "autocrat," unlike the socialist Anne Hidalgo, the ecologist Yannick Jadot, or the candidate from The Republicans (LR) Valérie Pécresse. She also regretted that Europe did not "see the threat coming (...), maybe a much more structured diplomacy could have prevented this threat." When asked if he was a "warmonger," as accused by Mr. Mélenchon, Yannick Jadot said: "It is not warmongering to want to support the heroic resistance of the Ukrainians." He said that it was necessary to "track down Russian billionaires everywhere" and called for an embargo on Russian gas and oil - several candidates are against it - also calling for "an extraordinary mobilization plan" on energy. The candidates extensively discussed the surge in fuel prices, the most immediate concrete consequence so far of the war in Ukraine for the French, and more generally on defending purchasing power, the number one concern of voters. But Emmanuel Macron did not take advantage of the arena to announce new measures to support the French, pointing out, with a smile on his face, that "the candidate was preceded by the president and that is normal." He recalled that the government had already allocated 20 billion euros to support the purchasing power of the French, citing for example the inflation check. A ring-shaped stage Several candidates criticized the government's announcement of a 15-cent per liter fuel price reduction starting from April 1, deeming it "too late," similar to Ms. Hidalgo who even joked about a possible "April Fools' joke." Mr. Mélenchon advocated for a price cap at "1.30 euros or 1.40 euros at the pump." The debate, polite and without new announcements, also revolved around the issue of pensions, with Mr. Macron confirming his intention to raise the retirement age to 65, as well as regarding energy savings and nuclear power where they displayed deep disagreements. Jean-Luc Mélenchon emphasized the "danger" of civilian nuclear power in case of war, while the ecologist Yannick Jadot stressed the need to phase out nuclear power, with Anne Hidalgo advocating for a "mix." The others, including the communist Fabien Roussel, defended nuclear power as a means of energy independence. For the occasion, TF1 had designed a stage all in blue in the form of a ring, with the candidates choosing either to stand like Valérie Pécresse, or to sit on a stool placed in the middle of the stage like Emmanuel Macron or Yannick Jadot. Jean-Luc Mélenchon alternated between the two. Several candidates with very low poll numbers were not invited, such as Nathalie Arthaud (LO) and Philippe Poutou (NPA) on the far left, or on the right the sovereigntist Nicolas Dupont-Aignan, and Jean Lassalle who denounced being treated as a "shitty and useless candidate."

title: War in Ukraine: In Germany, experts support the embargo on Russian energy imports
lead: Critiquing the government's opinion, several experts believe that an immediate halt to Russian hydrocarbon imports is imperative and "manageable."
text_eng: Will we soon shiver in German apartments due to a lack of gas to heat them? This option is being seriously discussed by economists and security experts in Germany, as Russia threatened on Monday, March 7, to cut off the Nord Stream 1 gas pipeline that supplies Germany through the Baltic Sea. While currently rejected by the government, an immediate voluntary embargo on Russian hydrocarbon imports would be the most severe sanction against Vladimir Putin after the invasion of Ukraine, and the most consequential for Germany, which relies on Russia for 55% of its gas supply. "Our options are limited. But what we can do, we should do. Germans have shown strong solidarity towards Ukrainians, they would be willing to endure a bit more cold at home," said Christoph Heusgen, president of the Munich Security Conference and former diplomatic advisor to Angela Merkel, in a television interview on Tuesday, March 8. For Norbert Röttgen, a Christian Democratic deputy and security expert, "the issue of oil and gas deliveries is crucial, as it concerns Putin's only vital economic artery," he wrote on Twitter. The day before, Chancellor Olaf Scholz had stated that the government deliberately avoided imposing sanctions on Moscow affecting the energy sector. "European energy supply for heating, mobility, electricity production, and industry cannot be ensured otherwise; it is crucial for the lives of our citizens," he declared. The Minister of Economy and Climate, Robert Habeck, an environmentalist, also fears that an energy embargo on Russian deliveries could jeopardize social peace in Germany. His ministry assured that gas and oil reserves are currently sufficient for the country to last until next winter. They stated that they are evaluating all possibilities to reduce dependence on Russian energy as quickly as possible. One of the options considered, extending the operation of nuclear power plants still in operation, was ultimately ruled out on Tuesday morning. After examination, the relevant ministries declared that "extending the lifespan of the plants could only contribute in a very limited way to solving the problem, at a very high economic cost and with risks to constitutional law and security." While the price to pay would be high, but tolerable, according to a report published by the Leopoldina Academy of Sciences. However, several economists believe that even without restarting nuclear power, Germany could withstand an immediate embargo on Russian hydrocarbon imports. According to economist Veronika Grimm, a member of the "Council of Economic Experts" of the federal government, "if this can stem the escalation and make the spread of war in Europe less likely, then we should take the step. Prolonged armed conflicts in Europe would have far more serious consequences than stopping energy supplies," she told Handelsblatt. Obviously, the price to pay would be high, but tolerable, according to a report published by the Leopoldina Academy, in which Veronika Grimm participated. She recommends acting quickly to soften the effects of rising prices for households... including by further postponing the date for returning to budget deficit limits, the "debt brake" imposed by the Constitution. A study conducted by two renowned economists, published on Tuesday, March 8, also estimates that Germany could withstand an embargo on imports of Russian hydrocarbons. "The lights would not go out," Moritz Schularick, a professor at Sciences Po and co-author of the study, told Le Monde. "We should expect a GDP decrease of 1% to 3%. Oil and coal can be replaced, the issue is gas. But about 20% of the gas used to generate electricity can be replaced in the short term by coal. Then, there is still about 30% of the previously available gas missing for households and industry. This is the adaptation shock that awaits us."

title: Vladimir Putin: "Ukraine was created by Bolshevik Russia."
lead: The Russian president on Monday, February 21, extensively justified his decision to recognize the independence of the separatist territories in Donbass, in eastern Ukraine. Here are excerpts from his speech.
text_eng: Excerpts. Main passages from the televised address that Vladimir Putin delivered from the Kremlin on February 21, in which he explained and justified his recognition of the independence of the separatist territories of Donbass. "Dear citizens of Russia, my dear friends, Today, I am speaking directly to you, not only to assess what is happening, but also to inform you of the decisions that have been made and the potential measures to come. I will emphasize once again: Ukraine, for us, is not just a neighboring country. It is an inseparable part of our history, our culture, our spiritual space. These are our comrades, our loved ones, among whom there are not only colleagues and friends, but also relatives, people to whom we are connected by family ties, blood ties. For a long time, the inhabitants of the historical lands of ancient Russia in the Southwest referred to themselves as 'Russians' and 'Orthodox'. They did so before the 17th century, when a part of these territories was united with the Russian state, and they continued to do so afterwards. It seems to us that we are not ignorant of this, that these are facts known to all. (...) Let's start with the fact that contemporary Ukraine was entirely and completely created by Russia, more precisely by communist, Bolshevik Russia. This process began almost immediately after the revolution of 1917, and Lenin and his comrades acted very undelicately towards Russia: they took from it, tore away from it, a part of its historical territories. Of course, nobody asked the millions of people who lived there. (...) 'Two years before the collapse of the USSR, its fate was practically decided. Today, radicals and nationalists, including and above all in Ukraine, claim credit for conquering independence.' I remind you that after the October 1917 coup and the ensuing civil war, the Bolsheviks set out to build a new form of state, and sharp disagreements emerged among them. Stalin, who in 1922 was both the General Secretary of the Bolshevik Party's Central Committee and the People's Commissar for Nationalities, proposed creating a country based on the principles of autonomy, offering the republics - the future administrative and territorial units - wide powers, even as they joined a unified state. Lenin criticized this plan and proposed making concessions to the nationalists, the nezavisimtsi ('independents'), as he called them at the time. (...) Why did they have to, like grand lords, satisfy all the growing nationalist ambitions on the margins of the former empire?" Why transfer vast territories to the federated republics - to these administrative entities, reconstituted and often formed arbitrarily - that sometimes had no connection whatsoever with these entities? I repeat: transferring them with the population of historical Russia. Furthermore, de facto, these administrative entities were given the status and appearance of national state formations. I ask again: why was it necessary to make such generous gifts, which even the most fervent nationalists had not dreamed of before, and why also give these republics the right to secede from the unified state without any conditions? At first glance, it is absolutely incomprehensible, a kind of madness. But it is only at first glance. There is an explanation. After the revolution, the Bolsheviks had as their main objective to stay in power at any cost, absolutely at any cost. That is why they accepted everything: the humiliating conditions of the Treaty of Brest-Litovsk [in March 1918], when the German Empire and its allies were, militarily and economically, in an extremely difficult situation, and the outcome of the First World War was almost assured; but also all the demands, all the wishes of the nationalists within the country. From the point of view of the historical destinies of Russia and its peoples, the Leninist principles of state construction were not just a mistake; it was, as they say, much worse than a mistake. And this became completely visible after the collapse of the USSR in 1991. (...) In a context of superficial and populist chatter about democracy and a bright future, based either on a market economy or a planned economy, while people were actually getting poorer and everything was lacking, no one, among those in power, thought about the inevitable and tragic consequences for the country. And then it was about satisfying, based on what had been created at the beginning of the USSR, the ambitions of nationalist elites, ambitions nurtured within the ranks of their national parties, but without considering that the CPSU [Communist Party of the Soviet Union] no longer had, and thank God, the instruments to hold power and the country, instruments such as state terror and Stalinist dictatorship. And even the proverbial leading role of the party was disappearing like morning mist. In September 1989, a document, in fact fatal, was adopted at the plenum of the Central Committee of the CPSU: the so-called party's national policy in contemporary conditions, the platform of the CPSU. It contained the following provisions, I quote: "The federated republics have all the rights corresponding to their status as sovereign socialist states." Another point: "The supreme representative organs, in federated republics, can challenge and suspend the resolutions and regulations of the federal government on their territory." And finally: "Each federated republic has its own citizenship, which extends to all inhabitants of that republic." Was it not obvious where such formulations and decisions would lead? (...) Why, in already difficult conditions, did it become necessary to further destabilize the country? (...) Two years before the collapse of the USSR, its fate was practically sealed. Today, radicals and nationalists, including and above all in Ukraine, claim credit for achieving independence. As we can see, this is not the case at all. It was the historical strategic errors of the Bolshevik leaders and the leadership of the CPSU (...) that led to the collapse of our united country. The collapse of historical Russia, then called the USSR, is on their conscience. (...)" Translated by Cécile Vaissié

title: War in Ukraine: Facing Russia, Joe Biden's bet on the long-term strength of retaliatory measures
lead: The package of sanctions presented on Thursday by the White House demonstrates both a desire to punish Moscow on an unprecedented scale and to protect the interests of the American consumer.
text_eng: The worst seemed inconceivable in Ukraine, and it happened. Adopting a new role as a Pythia, Washington had been denouncing Russia's military plans against Ukraine for three months, warning Moscow of the consequences. After the offensive on Thursday, February 24, the Biden administration was therefore forced to trigger the package of sanctions it had prepared, in close coordination with its European allies. For the third time this week, the American president delivered a speech on Thursday to raise the bar of these measures, according to a mysterious scale of proportion, supposed to correspond to Russian military actions on the ground. It is unknown what the American response would be to a potential removal of Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky to the sound of cannons. On ABC, Secretary of State Antony Blinken said he was "convinced" that this was Vladimir Putin's objective. In the meantime, the package of sanctions presented testifies both to a willingness to punish Russia on an unprecedented scale and to preserve the interests of the American consumer at the pump. "Putin's aggression against Ukraine will end up costing Russia very dearly, economically and strategically," Joe Biden assured. "We will make sure of that. Putin will be a pariah on the international stage. Any country that supports Russia's blatant aggression against Ukraine will be tainted by association." Putin and his family spared, for now. The first part of the sanctions concerns the financial sector. The ten largest Russian institutions, holding nearly 80% of the country's assets, are targeted. The goal is to deprive these institutions and state corporations of any foreign financing. Sberbank - Russia's largest bank - will no longer have access to the American financial system and will struggle to conduct transactions in dollars. VTB Bank and all its subsidiaries will have their assets frozen as soon as they engage in the American financial system, according to a White House statement. Similar sanctions target three other institutions, but the amounts involved are unknown. The second part involves numerous state-controlled companies in the banking, energy, or transportation sectors that will no longer be able to "raise funds through the American market." These entities represent a total of $1.4 trillion in assets. Washington has also expanded its list of targeted sanctions, adding known or unknown members of the Moscow elite, both political and economic. Vladimir Putin and his family are not among them. "It is being considered," the American president simply stated. He also announced that the "drum rolls" would continue "in the coming days" against "corrupt billionaires." The United States has decided not to initiate the expulsion of Russia from the Swift financial information system, as some members of Congress had requested. "Currently, this is not the position that the rest of Europe wants to adopt," summarized Joe Biden, while emphasizing that the package of sanctions presented on Thursday may have "more consequences" than Swift. "Some of the most powerful effects of our actions will only become apparent over time, as we will strangle Russia's access to financing and technology for its strategic sectors of the economy and degrade its industrial capabilities for years," explained the American president. Preventing a rise in gasoline prices, the United States has decided to block all exports of parts produced in the US, or containing American software, to the Russian defense sector. More broadly, high-tech activities will be targeted by the sanctions. "If we add our actions to those of our allies and partners, we estimate that we will block more than half of Russia's high-tech imports," Joe Biden specified. These measures target strategic sectors such as the military, navy, and aerospace industry. More lasers, sensors, navigation tools, and telecommunication devices, made with American parts. But the effects of this deprivation may only be seen in the medium or long term. The White House has ruled out any offensive posture against Russia. Nearly 7,000 soldiers, based in Germany, will be deployed, in addition, to Eastern NATO flank countries, for defensive purposes. Similarly, spokesperson Jen Psaki denied the information from NBC about options presented to the president to launch cyber attacks against Russia to disrupt its military deployment. America especially does not want to appear as a party to the conflict and is thinking about its own internal balances. Joe Biden also claimed to have left energy contracts with Russia outside the scope of sanctions, except for freezing the Nord Stream 2 project, the consequences of which the Germans are assuming. He warned American oil and gas companies not to "exploit this moment" by raising prices and assured that his administration was using "every tool" at its disposal to prevent a rise in gasoline prices, as inflation is already reaching unprecedented highs in the United States in forty years. Some American lawmakers are calling for more firmness. In the heat of the moment, it is impossible to predict the effect of these American sanctions, whether direct - on Russian companies and banks - or indirect - the shockwave in society and its elites. "It will take time," admitted Joe Biden. "[Putin] is not going to say, 'Oh my God, here come the sanctions! "I am withdrawing." He will test the West's resolve to see if we remain united. That will be the case." The American president believes - he said it - that the sanctions will be as devastating as the Russian army's missiles and bullets. "Let's have a conversation in about a month to see if they are working," he told the press. And maybe he will be right in the long term, as Washington has been right for the past two months in its warnings about a Russian military operation. But the current situation is not favorable to America. Russia is plunging a dagger into the heart of Europe, and Washington is putting on accountant glasses, identifying sectors, bank accounts to target as objectives. Perhaps this is a form of warfare more suited to the 21st century, but how do you explain it to the Ukrainians? Within the Democratic ranks, some lawmakers are demanding more firmness. This choice by the White House also reflects a renunciation, that of external adventures, and the deliberate choice to let a people sacrifice themselves in defense of their sovereignty. The U.S. Ambassador to the United Nations, Linda Thomas-Greenfield, mentioned the figure of five million potential Ukrainian refugees knocking on the door of the European Union; Ukraine has about 44 million inhabitants. "History has repeatedly shown how quick territorial gains eventually give way to overwhelming occupations, massive acts of civil disobedience, and strategic deadlocks," Joe Biden said. A relevant remark, perhaps influenced by the Iraqi experience, but one that is more expected from a commentator than from the American president.

title: Arpad Soltesz: "We Slovaks know exactly what it means to be liberated by our Russian Slavic brothers."
lead: The journalist and writer discusses the lies about Ukraine that the Russian regime feeds to the population, the actual state of Russia, and the dangers that the situation entails.
text_eng: Let's face it: Putin believes it is right to kill us. When I woke up on that Thursday morning [February 24], there was war in Ukraine and it was shocking but not surprising, more like the feeling when a loved one dies after a long illness. The path that the master of the Kremlin kleptocracy would one day take was as predictable as the fate of a patient with pancreatic cancer. We Slovaks know exactly what it means to be "liberated" by our Russian Slavic brothers. They came to save us from imperialism in 1968, when we naively believed we could decide our country's fate. This was followed by two decades of occupation. Some of us were killed even though we offered no resistance. It is our millennial national survival strategy - we do not fight, so you do not have to kill to enslave us. The Russians killed us, just on principle. What would an occupation be without killings, after all? If the Soviet regime had not collapsed, we would still be under Russian occupation today. We continue to live with its mental and cultural consequences. Slovak society is divided into "Russophobes" and "Russophiles," which has torn apart old friendships and families. Even thirty years after the last Russian soldier left our country, we have not managed to extract Soviet propaganda from the minds of a good portion of the population. And that is precisely what allows us to always decipher it. We know exactly what every representative of the Kremlin is thinking when he lies. We know he is lying because his lips are moving. Putin is only crazy if we try to measure him by our standards. Let's start with the fact that Ukraine is not Vladimir Putin's final goal. It is just his first step. If we do not stop him there, he will come after us too. It may take him a little longer to reach Paris than Bratislava, but rest assured, he also dreams of photos with his generals and the Eiffel Tower in the background. Sounds crazy? Sure, it's crazy. But one day, you could easily find in the dictionary this definition of suicidal stupidity: not taking a dangerous madman seriously because he has insane goals. Furthermore, Putin is only crazy if we try to measure him by our standards. Otherwise, he is just a particularly representative figure of Russia. This is evidenced by polls showing that his domestic popularity has not decreased since the start of the war, but has instead seen a clear rebound. And even before, it was at a level that the most popular Western leaders could never have dreamed of. The average Russian's brain is constantly simmering in a toxic state propaganda that tells them their historical responsibility is to save the world, protect traditional values against the decay of democratic alienation and the unrestrained tolerance of the West. Europe must be punished and brought to reason by force, the United States turned into a scorched desert. It's not Putin speaking like this, obviously. It's the ideologues and propagandists of the Kremlin who say it for him in the state-controlled media; in Russia, there are very few independent media outlets, and they have very little reach. An overwhelming majority of the population sees the world through the lens of aggressive messianic propaganda. The only real surprise is that even Putin himself has come to believe his own lies. Initially, he defended no ideology, only his own interests. Many mistook this for calculated, rational, and predictable pragmatism. Because unlike a fanatic terrorist, one can still come to terms with a criminal, right? "Behind every luxurious property on the French Riviera, owned by a Russian oligarch, there are people somewhere in Russia who died prematurely in poverty." The rulers of Russia have always treated even their own country as a conquered enemy territory. They looted it without scruples, ruling over the people through terror. From Ivan the Terrible to Putin, passing through Stalin, the mode of governance has not changed. Serfdom was abolished in the 19th century, but the servile mentality has not disappeared. Life in Russia is a game with a score of zero. If you don't oppress, you are oppressed. Any profit made by others constitutes your loss. Those who are not willing to take everything will have nothing. Putin's new kleptocratic elite has fabulously enriched themselves by plundering the state. Behind every luxurious property on the French Riviera, owned by a Russian oligarch, there are people somewhere in Russia who died prematurely in poverty, ignorance, without adequate healthcare services, and no real chance of improving their status. The meticulously maintained lawns of luxury villas around Cannes or Saint-Tropez are watered with tears, and sometimes even with blood. Vladimir Putin is the king of the thieves who make up his kleptocratic elite. The state has no resources left to develop. Schools, hospitals, roads, and human dwellings outside Moscow and Saint Petersburg are in a situation comparable to those in underdeveloped regions of Asia. Russia's ability to produce anything more sophisticated than a barrel of crude oil is particularly well illustrated by the story of Putin's presidential limousine. Its design required an investment of 400 million euros, the dictator even uses it sometimes, but its mass production has never been launched. When you see it, you will understand why. "That Thursday morning we all woke up to war. Potentially the last one our species will trigger, we should surely be aware of it." The Russian army is in the same state as the country. The generals fly in luxury, the sergeants in comfort, and the simple soldier, if he wants to eat, steals food wherever he can because the commanders steal food from the men by the truckload. It is practically impossible to conduct an effective war with such an army, unless you have nuclear weapons. Putin has them. We will come back to this point. Putin's mistake is to have believed in his own propaganda, whose original purpose was to provide Russians with a kind of substitute for prosperity. The dictator today has no idea what Russia looks like. Not only because it has been ten years since he last heard the word "no." It has been ten years since he has only heard information that does not affect his mood: Is Russia powerful? The most powerful. And the West? Rotten, weak, unable to survive! Can our mother Russia save the world? She can and must! How? Through the might of the weapons of her invincible army! Putin lives in a universe whose rules were written by a schizophrenic and paranoid J. R. R. Tolkien high on methamphetamine. He has come to believe that his duty is to destroy the West and establish a new world order. He has just begun. If we do not want him to succeed, we must stop him. Now. Slovaks welcome the first Ukrainian refugees in Vysne Nemecke (Slovakia), at the border with Ukraine, on February 25, 2022. RADOVAN STOKLASA / REUTERS People are just one of the resources that the political leader of Russia can spend as he pleases. Yes, it will be dangerous. Putin has nuclear weapons. He clearly states that he will use them if necessary. He is not threatening, he is not blackmailing. He informs us bluntly that if we oppose him, he will try to kill us. The use of nuclear weapons is just one of the possible means for him to achieve this. He does not prefer it because he is aware of the cost involved. He would have to lose a few more million Russian lives. It would be a relatively complicated and painful decision for him, just like paying 400 million euros for an ugly and poor-quality limousine. The term "human resources" ultimately has a different meaning in Russia than in Europe. People are just one of the resources that the political leader can spend as he pleases. Foreigners are not resources, but inconveniences to be disposed of. On that Thursday morning, we all woke up to war. Potentially the last one our species will trigger, we should surely be aware of it. We did not choose this war. We did not start it, we do not want it, but we have nowhere to escape it. It will come for us. Putin will also come after you, because he wants to and can, and he believes it is right. War is the essence of evil. We may die in it. Many of us may die in it. If we refuse to fight, then death is certain. Translated from Slovak by Barbora Faure.

title: Significant but targeted arms deliveries to Ukraine
lead: Many countries have announced sending weapons to Kiev, and discussions are ongoing to provide the aircraft requested by the Ukrainian leader.
text_eng: Ten days after the start of the war in Ukraine, arms deliveries to the regime of Volodymyr Zelensky, announced by many European countries since mid-January, are starting to be convoyed and reach their destination, according to converging testimonies and official statements. The details on the equipment sent and how governments politically assume them vary from one country to another. The United States are the ones that have most claimed, so far, their military support to Ukraine. According to the State Department, the total security assistance amounted to 1 billion dollars (920 million euros) in one year. This aid has further increased since the beginning of hostilities on February 24. President Joe Biden approved a package of 350 million dollars on February 26, and more than two-thirds of the equipment has been provided in five days. According to the New York Times, Washington has notably delivered more than 17,000 weapons, including Javelin anti-tank missiles and Stinger anti-aircraft missiles. The United States also seem ready to find ways to provide Ukraine with the aircraft requested by Mr. Zelensky. The idea, advocated in Congress, would be to transfer Russian-made aircraft (such as MIG or Sukhoi) to the Ukrainian air force, which are already possessed by countries in Eastern Europe, such as Poland, Bulgaria, or Slovakia. The Ukrainian military is already trained on these aircraft. The question remains on how these transfers could be organized. On Sunday, March 6, Secretary of State Antony Blinken stated that they are working with the Poles on the subject: "We strongly support them in providing aircraft to the Ukrainians." However, to ensure there is no "shortfall in Poland's own security," the United States would consider providing F-16s to Warsaw in exchange. These aircraft were already promised to Taiwan, according to the New York Times. A delicate situation for Berlin In Berlin, the situation is more delicate. Nine days after giving the green light to a first series of arms deliveries to Ukraine (500 Stinger anti-aircraft missiles, 1,400 anti-tank rocket launchers, and nine howitzers), the German government says it is ready to provide other equipment. "Everything that is possible is currently being studied," said Defense Minister Christine Lambrecht on Monday, March 7. Criticized for refusing, for weeks, to deliver weapons to Kyiv before finally relenting two days after the start of the Russian offensive, the German government does not want to be accused again of lacking solidarity. At the same time, the recent alarm raised by the chief of the army's general staff, who, on the first day of the conflict, stated that the Bundeswehr is "more or less out of ammunition," forces them to exercise some restraint: While a war rages just a two-hour flight from Berlin, the Germans may not admit that the army, already underequipped, is parting with some of its equipment. It remains to be seen what new equipment would be provided to Ukraine. On March 3, several German media outlets reported that the government was about to approve the delivery of 2,700 Soviet-made Strela anti-aircraft missiles from the stocks of the former East German army. However, according to Der Spiegel, these missiles have been considered obsolete and unusable since 2014. Furthermore, the army reportedly lacks the necessary parts for their launch. The Nordic countries united Encouraged by Chancellor Olaf Scholz's announcements, all the Nordic countries, after much hesitation, have decided to send military equipment to Ukraine. Sweden, which had not delivered weapons to a country at war since 1939, donated 5,000 84mm anti-tank rocket launchers, while Helsinki decided to deliver 2,500 assault rifles with 150,000 rounds of ammunition, and 1,500 anti-tank weapons. Denmark and Norway, on the other hand, each delivered 2,700 and 2,000 rocket launchers, respectively. While Finnish Prime Minister Sanna Marin stated that her country must maintain its military equipment reserves in case of necessity, Danish Defense Minister Morten Bodskov "definitely did not rule out" increasing their deliveries. "We are constantly considering it," he assured on March 1. According to a poll, 65% of Danes would be in favor. In Sweden, however, it is not currently being considered. As for other Western European countries, Belgium intends to deliver 2,000 light machine guns, 5,000 automatic rifles, 200 anti-tank weapons, and 3,800 tons of fuel. Some of this equipment has already been shipped. Since March 3, the Netherlands has decided not to disclose their deliveries anymore. Members of parliament will be informed in secret by the government. Previously, the country had provided non-lethal equipment and promised "dozens" of rocket launchers, "hundreds" of anti-tank weapons, 200 Stinger surface-to-air missiles, as well as an unspecified number of sniper rifles and ammunition. For the first time, Luxembourgish lawmakers have also approved deliveries, including 100 anti-tank weapons, as well as non-lethal equipment and military transport capabilities with an Airbus A400M. A sensitive issue in the East According to the Spanish Ministry of Defense, Madrid sent 1,370 C90 anti-tank grenade launchers with a range of 350 meters and nearly 700,000 cartridges for light weapons and submachine guns over the weekend. This delayed first shipment of equipment has divided the left-wing government led by socialist Pedro Sanchez, with the radical left party Podemos opposing it. The first tensions arose at the end of January, when the executive branch advanced the departure of a frigate and two mine sweepers to the Black Sea, as well as fighter jets to Bulgaria, as part of NATO. In the eastern flank of Europe and NATO, where fears towards Russia are particularly acute, the delivery of weapons or military equipment is an extremely sensitive issue. Following statements from the US administration about discussions regarding the transfer of fighter jets from the Polish army to the Ukrainians, the Prime Minister's office denied any such initiative. Slovakia, which also has Russian MiG aircraft in its military fleet, also ruled out sending planes, stating that they needed them for their own defense, while Bulgaria decided not to send any weapons. A concern for discretion that has also caught up with the United Kingdom. While in January the Boris Johnson government was among the first to admit to sending arms, including 2,000 anti-tank missiles, now the Ministry of Defense only speaks of fundraising and humanitarian aid. The latest announcement, on Monday, March 7th, stated that London would directly inject 100 million pounds (120 million euros) into the Ukrainian budget. France, on the other hand, has maintained the greatest reserve since the beginning of the conflict, out of concern, it is emphasized, not to disclose weaknesses of the Ukrainian army. Kiev's requests to France mainly concern surface-to-air missiles, anti-tank missiles, demining capabilities, and individual protections. Paris simply stated on February 26th that "additional defensive equipment will be delivered to the Ukrainian authorities, as well as support in fuel."

title: War in Ukraine: "Metals caught in a vice"
lead: Russia exerts its full weight on the markets of gas, oil, wheat, sunflower, as well as aluminum, nickel, palladium, or titanium. The planet measures its dependence and worries about disruptions in its supplies, notes Laurence Girard, an economic journalist at "Le Monde".
text_eng: Aluminum, nickel, zinc... all these metals are melting. The markets are in turmoil and prices are soaring. Since the invasion of Ukraine led by Vladimir Putin, the fever has risen a notch. The metals are surging under the gunfire of the Ukrainian war. Aluminum, a lightweight metal, carried away by an irresistible upward trend, is breaking record after record day by day. Again on Friday, March 4, with a price never before recorded in the annals, at $3,850 (approximately €3,527) per ton, on the London Metal Exchange (LME) in London. Nickel, on the other hand, has climbed to $27,976 per ton, the highest in eleven years. As for zinc, it has crossed the $4,000 per ton mark, an unprecedented value since 2007. The LME Index, an index that combines the prices of aluminum, copper, lead, nickel, tin, and zinc traded on the London market into one melting pot, reached a historic peak on Wednesday, March 2, at 5,046.7 points, representing an increase of over 30% in a year, illustrating the surge in industrial metal prices. Multiple industrial uses Aluminum was already trading at a premium price before the outbreak of the war. It is true that this metal with multiple industrial uses, from coffee capsules to cans to airplane fuselages, was benefiting from the post-Covid-19 recovery. By igniting the fuse of the Ukrainian conflict, Mr. Putin has further fueled the surge in prices. And not only that of aluminum. Russia exerts its full weight on the markets of gas, oil, wheat, corn, sunflower, aluminum, nickel, palladium, or titanium, not to mention the Ukrainian granaries rich in wheat, corn, and sunflower. The planet is assessing its dependence and worrying about disruptions in its supplies. While maritime traffic is at a standstill in Ukrainian ports and significantly reduced on Russian shores, as American and European sanctions fall on Russia, and military maneuvers continue, anxiety is rising. Investors are adding fuel to the fire. The price of gas is skyrocketing, having surpassed the 200 euros per megawatt-hour mark in Europe on Friday, a first, as well as the price of a barrel of oil. This is putting pressure on energy-intensive industries. The metals are caught in a vice.

title: With the war in Ukraine, Eric Zemmour's campaign undermined by doubt
lead: The far-right candidate in the presidential election has experienced a sharp decline in the polls. Torn between a stance close to the Kremlin and a political circle that is starting to worry, he is looking for a way out.
text_eng: "Air pocket," "rough patch," and even "nightmare": the war in Ukraine is causing concern within Eric Zemmour's campaign team. The nationalist candidate has dropped several points in the polls to land at 13% of voting intentions in the sixth wave of the Ipsos-Sopra Steria study for Le Monde, behind Marine Le Pen (14.5%) and Emmanuel Macron (30.5%). His inner circle is now divided between the "political" circle, seized by doubt, and the pro-Kremlin "geopoliticians." All his supporters agree that his remarks were risky. "I bet Russia will not invade Ukraine," he assured in December 2021. "Lost," says Jean-Marie Le Pen at the Montretout manor. "He made a serious mistake. It's a sign of questionable judgment that should not work in his favor." While he had supported Mr. Zemmour on the "obsession with inclusion" of the disabled, who had called to thank him, the founder of the National Front (FN) now refrains from coming to his aid. His friends have urged Eric Zemmour to keep a low profile. "Be quiet," Paul-Marie Coûteaux implored him on Thursday, February 24. "Don't fall into the trap of saying what's on your mind." Zemmour immediately "condemns" the Russian aggression but continues to hold NATO "responsible." Eager to "give something" to Vladimir Putin, as he puts it, he opposes sanctions and the delivery of weapons to Ukrainians, proposes to withdraw from NATO's integrated command, and to sign a treaty excluding any entry of Ukraine into the Alliance. Meeting with a close associate of Putin, he stands alone in opposing the reception of Ukrainian refugees, despite the favorable public opinion and France's asylum commitments. "The Ukrainians don't want to come, they want to stay close to their own," he maintains on CNews on Wednesday, March 2, contrary to the opinion of his campaign team and even his identitarian faction, advocating for a Christian Europe and alarmed by Ramzan Kadyrov's Chechens. These hesitations are linked to his geopolitically oriented vision towards the "ally" Russia, but also result from an obsessive campaign strategy: denouncing a "great replacement" and celebrating traditional values. How to erase in a day a line patiently built over the years? His book "The French Suicide" (Albin Michel, 2014) was on the bedside table of the former Russian ambassador to France, Alexandre Orlov, who saw in it "the truth of France today," as recounted by Nicolas Hénin in "La France russe. Enquête sur les réseaux Poutine" (Fayard, 2016). According to our information, in January 2016, in Saint Petersburg, Eric Zemmour was a guest at a meeting at the Central Naval Museum, before a restricted lunch with Vladimir Yakunin, a close associate of Putin, former KGB officer and former powerful head of the Russian railways, and Aymeric Chauprade, who was a Member of the European Parliament close to the Kremlin elected in 2014 under the FN label. A key figure in Russian "soft power" in Europe, Mr. Yakunin is the honorary president of the Franco-Russian Dialogue association, co-directed by the National Rally MEP Thierry Mariani. Friends of Eric Zemmour, from Jean-Frédéric Poisson to Patrick Buisson, as well as Caroline Galactéros, his diplomatic advisor with a pro-Kremlin leaning, gather there to criticize the "absolutely patent failure of human rights-ism" and praise states that "defend their identity," "do not swallow the potion and do not intend to enter this great 'glop' of 'humanistic values'." On February 24, during a conference on a barge in Paris, reported by Mediapart, she advocated for Reconquête! a "renegotiation of security balances in Europe" that would in fact benefit Moscow. The same anti-NATO stance is held by Jean-Bernard Pinatel, former head of army communications, with whom she founded the Geopragma think tank, a contributor to Zemmour's defense project. Jean-Luc Schaffhauser, the man behind the Russian loan to Marine Le Pen, says he maintains a connection with "Eric." On February 23, he wrote to him that the situation is such that Vladimir Putin will act. "Anyone you attack and humiliate will react," he says, judging that "waiting strengthens the position of the West, confused with the US 'deep state' in Ukraine." Torn, Eric Zemmour mentions the "heroic patriotism" of President Zelensky, but immediately adds that "Putin is a patriot" who "defends his country." He tries a third way: to confine the war to a regional dispute and direct media attention to immigration. "The confrontation between Russian and Ukrainian brothers distracts us from the real clash of civilizations," he declared on Thursday at the Agriculture Fair. "Our fundamental problems are in the south, not in the east." His team illustrates this with videos of African migrants trying to enter Europe.

title: "Former European leaders working for Russian companies are contributing to a major discredit of politics."
lead: "These former politicians serve a regime that threatens our values and security interests," writes Vice Admiral Patrick Chevallereau.
text_eng: Tribune. The invasion of Ukraine by Russia is leading Europe into a zone of dangers it has not experienced in decades. Vladimir Putin's revisionism, which has led him to almost deny Ukraine's right to exist, prompted him on Monday, February 21, to recognize the independence of the self-proclaimed republics of Donetsk and Luhansk, and then on Thursday, the 24th, to launch a massive invasion. A proponent of resolving disputes through violence, Vladimir Putin had in the weeks prior set up all the ingredients for a plan of political, military, and economic coercion, playing on provocation and threats, while practicing duplicity in meetings with Western counterparts. Now, it is the start of an all-out war with a former Soviet republic that wanted to live as a democracy. In France, the benevolence of extremists who are viscerally hostile to democracy, flouting international law, attacking our own security interests, Putin still benefits from the goodwill of our extremists who, both on the left and right, question our alliances, share the same anti-American obsession, or are fascinated by autocrats, even advocating - despite the war against Ukraine - in favor of an alliance with him. Other circles of our political class cultivate a disturbing ambiguity. The Ministry of Armed Forces had been very clear about those who threaten our interests by publishing its "Strategic Update" in January 2021. Russian disinformation campaigns, components of hybrid strategies aimed at challenging the international order, and the unraveling of arms control treaties are bluntly pointed out. Through its intimidation practices and destabilizing military capabilities, Russia is described as a power that requires our utmost vigilance. Recent events confirm the extent of the threat. Numerous actions have validated this analysis: a show of force against Georgia in 2008 (a country now deprived of 25% of its territory!), annexation of part of Ukraine in 2014, violent clandestine actions on European soil in Vrbetice, Czech Republic (2014), or in Salisbury, England [where former Russian double agent Sergei Skripal and his daughter Yulia were poisoned with Novichok], in 2018, cyberattacks and electoral manipulations in the United States (2016), and violation of the Intermediate-Range Nuclear Forces Treaty. In short, a gangster-like behavior on the international stage, now striking the Ukrainian people. In Africa, France's security interests are being challenged by Russia's mafia-like presence, which operates in the Central African Republic and the Sahel under the guise of a group of mercenaries that is one of the tools of Putin's foreign policy. Gerhard Schröder is on the payroll of the Russian gas giant Gazprom, and former French Prime Minister François Fillon has joined the petrochemical group Sibur. Unfortunately, it is in this context of open conflict that former European ministers have taken the risk of compromising their reputation and independence by participating in the governance of Russian companies that operate under direct influence of the Kremlin. After former German Chancellor Gerhard Schröder, who is on the payroll of the Russian gas giant Gazprom, and former Austrian Minister of Foreign Affairs [Karin Kneissl], who works for another Russian oil giant, Rosneft, former French Prime Minister François Fillon joined the petrochemical group Sibur in December 2021. Is it acceptable for these political leaders, who have represented the sovereignty of European nations, to deliberately serve the Kremlin's oligarchy? Operating in a corrupt power ecosystem, brilliantly described by journalist Catherine Belton in her book Putin's People [Farrar, Straus and Giroux, 2020, untranslated], do these former European leaders truly believe that their presence on these boards is not a boon for Moscow's propaganda? How can we accept these connections? Do they not risk downplaying, or even contributing to, the operations of this regime that threatens our democratic values and security interests? Because these lucrative collaborations inevitably raise suspicions of a risk of "collusion with the enemy," they at the very least cast a major discredit on politics, which is a democratic danger in itself. Instruments of Influence So, let us demand a national political clarification. Unfortunately, this seems to be delayed by those who prefer to dodge the issue or act offended. Yet, it would be healthy for any candidate in the presidential election to distance themselves from these venal practices that seriously harm the independence of our democratic life and may cast doubt on France's diplomatic reliability with our allies. They should stop invoking a supposed right to "do business": it is rather a matter of state affairs! In Russia, these companies are part of the political system. The activities of former European ministers in these groups provide instruments of influence to a power that is currently acting against the security of our country and its citizens. Let us quickly move towards a European legal clarification. Why not consider a high authority of European public life that would ensure that certain "lucrative conversions" do not weaken us in the hybrid war being waged against us by adversaries who are completely uninhibited? It is all the more important, at a time when these multifaceted conflicts also involve economic battles - such as over the price of energy - that these state-owned companies are tasked with leading. How can we interpret the presence of these former European leaders in Russian groups participating in energy coercion that Russia will impose on us in retaliation to European sanctions? This is not just an ethical question. It is also - and above all - a geopolitical issue. While Russia is currently involved in the crisis at hand, this problem will expand. The 21st century is already witnessing clashes between powers, both economically and militarily, using all the weapons at their disposal. It is therefore essential, for the interest of the French and Europeans, that this presidential election be an opportunity to clarify things and clean up the rules of the game. Attempts to justify the unjustifiable are unworthy of France.

title: Ukrainian crisis: The West announces a series of sanctions against Russia.
lead: The strongest retaliatory measure was taken by Berlin, which suspended the authorization of the Nord Stream 2 gas pipeline. In the White House, Joe Biden imposed financial sanctions against Russia and approved new deliveries of "defensive" weapons to Ukraine.
text_eng: The green light given by the Russian Parliament on Tuesday, February 22, for a military operation in Ukraine is "the beginning of an invasion" of the country, denounced the President of the United States, Joe Biden, while unveiling initial sanctions against Moscow, along with its Western partners. These measures are currently modest compared to those promised in the event of a large-scale invasion. The fear of a military escalation in Ukraine is at its peak since Vladimir Putin recognized the independence of the secessionist entities of Luhansk and Donetsk, located in the east of the country on Monday. According to Washington, 150,000 Russian soldiers have been deployed near the borders of Ukraine. In Moscow, the Deputy Minister of Defense, Nikolai Pankov, read a request from the Russian President to deploy troops in the entities of Donetsk and Luhansk before the Senate, citing that a "Ukrainian army of 60,000 men with heavy armor" is ready to attack. Vladimir Putin signals the end of the peace process. Just after receiving approval from the members of the Duma, the Russian Parliament, Mr. Putin once again denounced the alleged abuses committed by Kiev against the separatists in Donbass. He left doubts about the timeline for sending armed forces, demanding "demilitarization" of Ukraine, suggesting that it would be "better" for Ukraine to abandon its ambition to join NATO and choose "neutrality" instead. "I did not say that our soldiers will go there now... It will depend, as they say, on the situation on the ground," he said during a surprise press conference. Shortly after, Russian diplomacy announced the imminent evacuation of its diplomats from Ukraine. Mr. Putin also claimed the entire administrative regions of Luhansk and Donetsk for the separatists, whose territory far exceeds the areas under their control. Moscow has also established diplomatic relations with the two regions. The President of the Russian Federation also announced that the Minsk agreements, signed in 2015 to establish a ceasefire in eastern Ukraine, "no longer exist," thus signaling the end of the ongoing peace process under Franco-German mediation. Berlin suspends Nord Stream 2 gas pipeline authorization. Among Western nations, the strongest retaliatory measure was announced by Berlin, which suspended the Nord Stream 2 gas pipeline, a flagship project for both Berlin and Vladimir Putin. The possible definitive halt to this colossal project was announced by the German Chancellor Olaf Scholz himself. Scholz said during a press conference that the Ministry of Economy would re-examine the certification process in light of Russia's actions. The Nord Stream 2 project, led by the Russian state-owned gas giant Gazprom and partly financed by European energy companies including the German Uniper and the French Engie, is supposed to allow Russia to supply Germany with twice as much gas while bypassing Ukraine. Nord Stream 2 connects the two countries with a 1,230-kilometer pipeline under the Baltic Sea with a capacity of 55 billion cubic meters of gas per year, following the same route as its operational twin Nord Stream 1 since 2012. The project, estimated at over 10 billion euros, was co-financed by five European energy sector groups. Germany is the main advocate within the European Union (EU) for the gas pipeline, which it believes will help achieve the energy transition it has committed to, while making its territory a European gas hub. Ukraine, in particular, feared losing the revenue it receives from the transit of Russian gas and becoming more vulnerable to Moscow. Its president, Volodymyr Zelensky, reiterated his call for an "immediate" halt to the project on Tuesday morning. His foreign minister welcomed the suspension as "politically and morally justified." On Twitter, Dmitry Medvedev, the deputy chairman of Russia's Security Council, sarcastically remarked, "Welcome to a new world where Europeans will soon pay 2,000 euros per cubic meter of gas!" Joe Biden announces financial sanctions At the White House, U.S. President Joe Biden denounced on Tuesday "the beginning of a Russian invasion of Ukraine," announcing financial sanctions against Russia and new deliveries of "defensive" weapons to Ukraine. He announced a "first round" of sanctions aimed at cutting Russia off from Western financing and targeting "Russian elites" as well as financial institutions. "We are imposing broad sanctions on Russian sovereign debt. This means we are cutting off Russian government from Western financing," he stated. If Russia "continues its aggression," the U.S. will continue to provide defensive assistance to Ukraine, he emphasized. "I have authorized the redeployment of U.S. forces already positioned in Europe to reinforce our Baltic allies, Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania," added the White House occupant. Biden reiterated that the U.S. has not given up hope of finding a diplomatic solution. "There is no doubt that Russia is the aggressor, so we are aware of the challenges we face," he said. "Nevertheless, there is still time to avoid the worst-case scenario that will inflict huge suffering on millions of people." » Meeting canceled between Russian and American foreign ministers. The American Secretary of State, Antony Blinken, announced on Tuesday that he will not meet as planned with his Russian counterpart, Sergey Lavrov. "Now that we see the invasion has begun and Russia has clearly rejected all diplomacy, it makes no sense to meet at this stage," Blinken said during a joint press conference with the Ukrainian Foreign Minister, Dmytro Kuleba. Blinken and Lavrov were originally scheduled to meet on Thursday, February 24. This meeting was even supposed to serve as a precursor to the summit proposed on Sunday evening by Emmanuel Macron between the American President Joe Biden and his Russian counterpart Vladimir Putin. The EU announces "a package of sanctions." The Twenty-Seven have approved a series of sanctions against Russia. "We have agreed on a first package of sanctions unanimously," announced the French Foreign Minister, Jean-Yves Le Drian, following the meeting of EU member states' foreign ministers in Paris. These sanctions, which still need to be formally adopted to take effect, "will hit Russia hard," added the EU Foreign Minister, Josep Borrell, specifying that asset freezes and visa bans will target in particular the 351 Russian deputies of the Duma who approved the recognition of the independence of separatist territories. "The sanctions will also target twenty-seven individuals and entities who contribute to undermining or threatening the territorial integrity of Ukraine, its sovereignty, and independence," detailed Josep Borrell, mentioning "actors in the defense sector playing a role in the invasion" of the country, "those who have launched a disinformation campaign against Ukraine," and "banks financing Russian officials and other operations in these separatist territories." The EU also plans to target "economic relations between [these] two regions and the EU," and finally "the ability of the Russian state and its government to access our financial market and European capital markets to refinance their debt," he added. London sanctions banks. Across the Channel, the British Prime Minister, Boris Johnson, announced sanctions targeting three oligarchs close to the Kremlin and five Russian banks. Minimal measures for London, the financial stronghold of Russian billionaires. "Today, the UK is sanctioning the following five Russian banks: Rossiya, IS Bank, General Bank, Promsvyazbank, and Black Sea Bank, and we are sanctioning three individuals with very significant wealth," said the head of government in Parliament. "This is the first tranche, the first barrage of what we are prepared to do," he added. Among the three individuals concerned is the businessman Guennadi Timtchenko, a close associate of Vladimir Putin. "All assets they hold in the UK will be frozen and the individuals concerned will be banned from traveling here," Boris Johnson stated. The British Prime Minister also opposed international matches in Russia, such as the UEFA Champions League final on May 28th in Saint Petersburg.

title: Russia announces partial withdrawal of its peacekeeping troops from Armenia.
lead: Yerevan reproaches Russia, its traditional ally, for not intervening during the Azerbaijani offensive that allowed the disputed enclave of Nagorno-Karabakh to be retaken in September 2023.
text_eng: On Thursday, May 9, Russia announced the withdrawal of some of its soldiers and border guards from Armenia, following a meeting between President Vladimir Putin and Armenian Prime Minister Nikol Pashinyan. Yerevan has been distancing itself from Moscow for several months. The two leaders met on Wednesday evening in Moscow on the sidelines of a meeting of the Eurasian Economic Union, which includes both countries. The number of soldiers affected has not been specified by the two countries. Hayk Konjorian, head of the Civil Contract party (the ruling party in the Armenian Parliament, which also includes Mr. Pashinyan), stated on Thursday that the agreement concerned Russian military and border posts established in five Armenian regions since the end of 2020. However, the withdrawal does not seem to affect the main Russian military base in the country, located in Gyumri (with around 3,000 soldiers). "Mr. Pashinyan stated that at the present time, due to the evolving situation, this need no longer exists," Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov stated on Thursday. "President Putin has therefore agreed, and the withdrawal of our soldiers and border guards has been decided," he added, as reported by Russian news agencies. Armenia's closer ties with the West Armenia, a traditional ally of Russia, criticizes the Russian peacekeeping force for not intervening during Azerbaijan's offensive that led to the recapture of the disputed enclave of Nagorno-Karabakh in September 2023. This attack forced around 100,000 of the 120,000 Armenians living in the mountainous region to flee to Armenian territory. Since the fall of 2020, following a war lost by the Armenian separatists of Nagorno-Karabakh, 2,000 Russian peacekeeping soldiers had been deployed there to enforce the ceasefire terms. Faced with their inaction during the Azerbaijani offensive, Yerevan began a diplomatic rapprochement with the West. Armenia notably joined the International Criminal Court at the end of January, obliging it to arrest Vladimir Putin if he were to set foot on Armenian territory. Armenian authorities had already announced in early March the end of the presence of Russian border guards at Yerevan's international airport. On April 17, the Kremlin stated that Russian soldiers deployed in Nagorno-Karabakh had begun their withdrawal.

title: "Olympic truce" and scent of commercial battle on the first day of Xi Jinping's visit to France
lead: Emmanuel Macron and the Chinese leader issued a joint call on Monday for a ceasefire in Ukraine on the occasion of the Paris Games this summer. As for trade disputes, progress seems to be very limited.
text_eng:
At dinner, cognac was given all the honors, symbolizing an evening of highs and lows in the relationship between France and China. From Bernard Arnault (LVMH) to Carlos Tavares (Stellantis), most of the top executives of French companies and some celebrities, such as Mireille Mathieu and Sophie Marceau, gathered on Monday, May 6, at the Elysée Palace for the state dinner hosted by Emmanuel Macron in honor of his Chinese counterpart, Xi Jinping. Earlier in the day, the guest assured the French president that cognacs would not be subject to additional tariffs by Beijing at this stage, while Chinese authorities complete an antidumping investigation against them. Amidst toasts, crab meat and Bresse chicken supreme prepared by top chefs, the evening was meant to conclude a busy first day of the visit, aiming to bridge differences on current pressing issues. Following a simple welcome ceremony at Les Invalides and various meetings at the Elysée Palace, Xi Jinping and Emmanuel Macron jointly called for an "Olympic truce" during the Paris Games this summer. Their joint statement came shortly after the Russian Ministry of Defense announced upcoming exercises to "train in the preparation and use of non-strategic nuclear weapons," a move criticized by the United States but barely mentioned in public by Macron and Xi. Deep divergences While this potential "Olympic truce," already supported by a UN resolution, could temporarily halt fighting during the sports events of the Olympics (July 26 to August 11) and possibly the Paralympics (August 28 to September 8), in hopes of initiating uncertain discussions for a more lasting ceasefire, Moscow has already rejected such a prospect. It could take advantage of the situation in the spring to further its position on the ground, while Kiev, demanding the withdrawal of Russian forces as a precondition for negotiations, is wary of a pause that could solidify its enemy's territorial gains. The joint statement between Beijing and Paris masks deep divergences on the Russian aggression, which is shaking Europe and granting China an unprecedented say in the continent's security. "Conflicts can only be resolved through negotiations," emphasized the Chinese president, urging both parties to "resume contact and dialogue." This was a way to encourage Kiev's allies to exercise restraint in their military support, more than two months after the French president did not rule out sending military reinforcements to Ukraine. While the United States warns Beijing about the support China is providing to the Russian war effort and asks Europeans to relay the message, Emmanuel Macron has welcomed the "commitments" made by Xi Jinping, for the first time at this level according to the Elysée, not to supply weapons to Moscow, while also exerting more control over the export of dual-use (civil and military) materials to Russia. "We oppose using the Ukrainian crisis to shift blame onto others, tarnish a third party, and trigger a new Cold War," Xi Jinping warned in response to Western criticisms.
