He believes that an "international conference" should finally bring together Russians and Ukrainians to try to find a negotiated solution to the war. However, China seems to be boycotting the meeting organized by Switzerland on June 15 and 16, in close consultation with Kiev, to discuss the terms of the "peace plan" proposed by Volodymyr Zelensky. Russia has also refused to participate in this meeting. 

Apart from cognac, which has been granted a reprieve from potential sanctions, progress is barely more tangible regarding the numerous trade disputes affecting the rebalancing of relations between France, Europe, and China. The discussions held on Monday mainly allowed French and Chinese leaders to speak frankly. Macron stated to the press, without taking questions, that the talks had helped "clarify" disagreements "to improve the economic relationship and give it a new impetus." Earlier in the day, Ursula von der Leyen, President of the European Commission, emphasized the current stakes following a trilateral discussion with the two presidents. "As we have shown before, we will defend our companies, we will defend our economies. We will never hesitate to do so when necessary," she assured, calling on China to reduce its industrial overcapacity: "As Chinese domestic demand does not increase, the world cannot absorb China's excess production," she warned. Xi Jinping, however, refuses to consider a truce on the matter: "The so-called issue of Chinese overcapacity does not exist," he stated, believing that the "Chinese new energy industry" actually helps "increase global supply and alleviate global inflation pressure." While electric vehicles - targeted in Europe by an anti-subsidy investigation denounced by Beijing - and low-cost Chinese solar panels will be essential for the energy transition, as Chinese leaders argue, Europeans are concerned about their industry being decimated by Chinese products. For a "fully loyal" trade aiming to be conciliatory, Emmanuel Macron intends to continue exchanges after sixty years of Franco-Chinese diplomatic relations, but insists on the notions of "respect" and "loyalty." "There is no logic of decoupling vis-à-vis China, it is a will to preserve our national security as you do, yourself. A will of respect and mutual understanding, reciprocal, and a will to continue to open up trade, but to ensure each time that it is fully loyal, whether it be tariffs, aids, market access," Emmanuel Macron developed in the afternoon in front of a group of French and Chinese businessmen gathered near the Elysée for economic meetings. By the way, the Head of State invites Chinese companies to invest more in France. French investments in China are three times more important than those of Chinese companies on our territory, he noted. Earlier in the day, the Minister of Economy, Bruno Le Maire, made a call in this direction: "France welcomes all industrial projects. BYD and the Chinese automotive industry are welcome in France," he said during a meeting with Chinese and French automotive sector bosses. The French minister had visited a BYD factory in Shenzhen in July 2023, but the world leader in electric vehicles had finally chosen to set up its first European site in Hungary, where Xi Jinping is to visit from Wednesday to Friday, after a stop in Serbia. As for Mr. Macron and Mr. Xi, they were to fly to the Hautes-Pyrénées on Tuesday to continue their discussions at the Col du Tourmalet, a high point of the mountain stages of the Tour de France. The weather was expected to be just as chilly as in Paris.

title: European elections 2024: "Despite pollsters claiming that the gap between the Bardella and Hayer lists is difficult to make up, Macron firmly believes in it."
lead: The speech on Europe scheduled for Thursday, April 25 confirms it: from start to finish, the adventure that began in 2017 remains centered around the figure of a man who is not afraid of anything. However, in a context of budgetary slippage, the impact may be less strong today, as noted in her column by Françoise Fressoz, editorialist at "Le Monde".
text_eng: Once again, all eyes are turning towards him, everything depends on him, and everything is organized around him. The speech on Europe that the head of state is to deliver on Thursday, April 25, at the Sorbonne, in memory and as a continuation of the one he had delivered on September 26, 2017, will mark the majority's entry into the campaign for the European elections. The real one, the one that matters. This is how the Macronian narrative unfolds. Everything that happened before is destined to fade away: the poor polls, the rise of the National Rally (RN), the difficulty that the lead candidate Valérie Hayer (Renew) has been facing for weeks in embodying and defending the executive's European policy in a campaign dominated by national concerns: purchasing power, health, immigration, security. Also swept aside are the government's initial surprise and subsequent divisions in the face of the sudden increase in public deficits, as well as the saturation of the television space by violent incidents involving minors. When the President speaks, everything will truly begin. If, on the contrary, he remains silent, everything will continue to drift aimlessly. From start to finish, the adventure that began in 2017 remains centered around a man who believes in his destiny, fears nothing, and displays unwavering optimism to his close circle, while many others in the majority doubt and seek shelter. Despite pollsters claiming that a 10-point or more gap between the Bardella and Hayer lists is difficult to overcome, he firmly believes it can be done. This campaign is not lost, the President swears by it! Certainly, the European cause deserves to be championed loudly and proudly, as it is inseparably linked to the actions and achievements of the Presidents of the Republic. Emmanuel Macron is all the more inclined to engage as he has never been ashamed of Europe nor allowed himself to be dominated by it. Unlike François Mitterrand, he did not have to undertake the unpopular austerity measures of 1983 to demonstrate the deep conviction that salvation lies within the European Union. He did not, like Jacques Chirac, transform into a resigned European after being a critic of "subordination." The European routine disrupted In 2016, European construction was his act of faith as a candidate faced with the exhaustion of the right and left, undermined by their internal contradictions on the subject. In 2017, Europe was the battleground for the President he had become, emphasizing the concept of European sovereignty, supported by that of European identity. The goal was not to erase nations, but rather to prevent their erasure by increasing their ability to act in a globalized world. This new approach, at the time, disrupted the European routine, awakening suspicion towards the French unhealable pretension to leadership. In practice, both concepts have proven relevant in light of the continuous rise of threats: the pandemic, Russia's aggressiveness, the war in Ukraine, the risk of American disengagement, the rise of protectionism, the need for democracies to defend their model against aggressive autocrats, and so on. Developed across a wide spectrum (security and defense, border control, ecological and energy transition, digital, industry), the strengthening of European sovereignty has experienced varying fortunes over the past six years, but the results are sufficiently convincing to be claimed. They will serve as a springboard for a new wave of proposals, in defense as well as in industry and finance, as Europe, increasingly marginalized by the China-US competition, does not have the optimal organization to resist. The Sorbonne speech is first and foremost intended to make an impact on the European stage, like the one in 2017. In a context of budget slippage, however, the ripple effect may be less strong than the original. A possible downgrade of France's credit rating is feared the next day, which would bring the head of state back to this reality: if France wants to be influential, it must keep its finances in order! The camp of progressives has fragmented. On the domestic front, the ineffective move at the beginning of the year shows that the presidential firepower has diminished. When, in February, Emmanuel Macron sounded the alarm about the war in Ukraine and the Russian threat, the far right should logically have been put on the defensive due to its complacency towards Vladimir Putin. The handicap was quickly overcome, and the Ukrainian issue was swiftly brushed aside. Unwilling to detail his European project filled with shadows and contradictions, Jordan Bardella, the president of the National Rally, centered his entire campaign around insecurity and the "incompetence of the Mozart of finance," in order to turn the European election into an anti-Macron referendum, presented as a decisive step towards the alternation in 2027. The fact that the head of state enters the arena is not displeasing to him. It is around the rejection of the presidential figure that the National Rally seeks to maintain the momentum of the useful vote. In 2019, two camps clashed: the progressives and the nationalists, namely Renaissance and the National Rally. This time, the camp of progressives has fragmented. There is Emmanuel Macron, but also Raphaël Glucksmann (Place publique), who advocates for a Europe that is powerful but more social and more ecological than the one now embodied by the President of the Republic. If the challenge for Emmanuel Macron is to rally his 2019 electorate to stand against the National Rally, it is this competition that he must manage to contain. Having contributed to advancing Europe, wanting to continue doing so is certainly a strong argument with this electorate, but it is no longer enough. The rightward shift in his second term, the almost structural unpopularity brought on by the pension reform, and recent setbacks on environmental issues are working against the president. By heavily relying on himself and his rhetoric, Emmanuel Macron risks realizing how much power can isolate.

title: "China supports Moscow's arms production."
lead: While the United States and the United Kingdom announced on Friday a ban on importing aluminum, copper, and nickel produced in Russia, Moscow is bypassing European sanctions by turning to Beijing to support its war effort in Ukraine. This demonstrates the close ties between the two countries, as noted by Jean-Michel Bezat, an economic journalist at "Le Monde."
text_eng: David O'Sullivan is a very busy senior official. As the European Union's (EU) special envoy for the implementation of sanctions against Russia, this seasoned Irishman based in Brussels is tasked with ensuring that Russia does not circumvent them. Russia has been successful in doing so since the first package came into effect at the beginning of the war triggered against Ukraine in February 2022. Despite sanctions on oil and coal, strategic components, companies, executives, and politicians, Brussels has not completely cut off all exchanges with Russia. New sanctions are being added as the months go by. In early March, a thirteenth package targeted two hundred entities and individuals without focusing on a specific sector, following the ban on diamond trade on January 1st. The EU was protesting against the death of Russian opposition leader Alexei Navalny on February 16th in an Arctic penal colony. However, gas, oil, and uranium are still being exchanged, albeit indirectly. Not to mention certain metals, a lucrative activity for Moscow, although the sanctions have reduced the value of trade from 23 billion euros in 2022 to 14 billion in 2023. Copper disguised as scrap metal. The United States and the United Kingdom announced on Friday, April 12th, the ban on importing aluminum, copper, and nickel produced in Russia, as well as restrictions on over-the-counter derivatives trading. Crucial for their trade, the London and Chicago metal exchanges will no longer accept them. "This will prevent the Kremlin from injecting more money into its war machine," said Jeremy Hunt, the UK's Chancellor of the Exchequer. According to Russian customs data, sanctions have had an impact: in 2023, Russia's exports to the European continent dropped by 68% and by 28% globally, showing that trade with countries refusing sanctions did not compensate for the loss of the European market. However, every day brings new ways to circumvent the sanctions. While the Treasury announced the embargo on metals, American officials revealed that China supports Moscow's arms production by selling machine tools, electronic components, and technologies for drones or missiles. The two countries exchange copper disguised as scrap metal, as reported by Reuters. And large quantities of goods imported from the West by Turkey, the Arabian-Persian Gulf, and Central Asia are re-exported to Russia to support its war effort. It would take ten Mr. O'Sullivans to monitor all this traffic...

title: In the United States, a new aid package for Ukraine has been adopted by the House of Representatives after six months of deadlock.
lead: Four texts were voted on Saturday regarding aid to Kiev, Israel, and Taiwan, as well as the threat of a TikTok ban. To put these separate texts to a vote, Republican speaker Mike Johnson had to overcome the rebellion of some of his own party's lawmakers.
text_eng:
There were Ukrainian flags waving, standing lawmakers, shouts of protest and approval. After more than six months of repeated failures, futile reformatting, Republican blockades, and a disaster for the United States' image in the world, the House of Representatives adopted a new aid package for Ukraine on Saturday, April 20. An aid package of $60.8 billion (57 billion euros) that this country needs more than ever, as its ammunition is running out and it is facing Russian strikes without effective protection. By a vote of 311 to 112, the text passed with a margin almost as comfortable as the three other projects rushed through on Saturday. They all concerned U.S. foreign policy and its financing. The outcome is a relief for supporters of Ukraine, who did not want to give the Kremlin a victory by default and abandonment. But it also speaks volumes about the hostage-taking of Congress for months by a minority of the Republican Party, representing the MAGA movement ("Make America Great Again," Donald Trump's slogan). It was suspected: there was a broad bipartisan majority to extend military support to Ukraine, but it fell victim to the chaos within the ranks of the Grand Old Party. It took the Speaker of the House of Representatives, Mike Johnson, to ignore the criticisms of this radical Republican wing to finally allow this majority to speak out. "Today, members of both parties in the House voted to advance our national security interests and send a clear message about the power of American leadership in the world," Joe Biden said in a statement. The Democratic president called on the Senate to confirm this package of bills as soon as possible - the vote is scheduled for April 23 - so that he can sign them. Meanwhile, his Ukrainian counterpart, Volodymyr Zelensky, expressed his "gratitude" on X network to all those who have helped "keep history on the right track." "The vital American aid project passed today in the House will prevent the war from spreading, save thousands and thousands of lives, and help our two nations become stronger," added the Ukrainian leader. A total of $95 billion Four separate texts were adopted on Saturday, totaling $95 billion. The first one is about national security. It includes new sanctions against Iran, the possible sale of frozen Russian assets, and finally, the threat of banning TikTok in the United States if its Chinese owner did not agree to sell it. A project on this issue had already been adopted by the representatives on March 13, but the Senate refused to take it up, showing the ambiguity of the two parties on this issue. The second part provides $8.1 billion for Taiwan and the Indo-Pacific region. The third concerns a $17 billion aid package for Israel, as well as $9 billion in humanitarian aid, prioritizing assistance for Palestinians. Over $5 billion is specifically allocated for the expansion and strengthening of the Iron Dome and David's Sling missile defense systems. An allocation of $2.4 billion will be dedicated to U.S. operations in the Middle East, seen as crucial in preventing regional escalation. Due to the devastating toll of the military offensive in the Gaza Strip – where over 34,000 Palestinians, mostly civilians, have been killed according to Hamas – criticism has intensified on the left against the continuation of unconditional American aid to the Israeli state. However, the drone and missile attack launched by Iran against Israel on the night of April 13-14 has once again placed the defense of this crucial ally in the Middle East against the Tehran regime at the forefront of bipartisan concerns. "This is not a game."

The most debated aspect in the House, which had been stalled for six months, was the aid to Ukraine. The amount is $60.8 billion, with the primary beneficiary, aside from the country at war, being the American military-industrial complex. It includes nearly $14 billion for the acquisition of military equipment and advanced weapon systems; $13.4 billion to replenish U.S. military stocks; $11 billion for Pentagon operations in the region; $13.7 billion for Ukraine's acquisition of American defense systems, and $9 billion in economic assistance loans to Kiev. The Pentagon has indicated that deliveries will begin as soon as the Senate votes. This will be a huge relief for the Ukrainian military, but the losses suffered and the damage caused during the parliamentary delays in Washington cannot be magically compensated. "Three of our key adversaries, Russia, Iran, and China, are working together and are aggressors worldwide," explained Speaker Mike Johnson after the Saturday votes. "They pose a global threat to our prosperity and security. Their advancement threatens the free world and requires American leadership. If we turn our backs at this moment, the consequences could be terrible." These statements reflect a classic conservative view of the United States' role in the world, distinguishing between good and evil. This approach is continuously denounced by the Trumpist movement as warmongering. When faced with important decisions, Mike Johnson is known to pray, both at home and within the Chamber, between sessions. The Republican speaker, unknown to the general public at the time of his appointment in late October 2023, following the dismissal of his predecessor Kevin McCarthy by his own group, had been under immense pressure for weeks. An evangelical Christian, a conservative martial, he had an existential choice to make, one that would determine both his future and his place in history: prioritize his career or contribute to the preservation of Ukraine. "This is not a game," he explained on Friday. "This is not a joke. We cannot make political calculations about this. We must do what is right, and I will allow each member of the Chamber to vote according to their conscience and will." "We put America last." This slow conversion to action, after months of careful calculations that outlined a parliamentary paralysis, was notably fueled by classified briefings from U.S. intelligence services and the Pentagon. These had become increasingly pessimistic in recent weeks about Ukraine's ability to resist Russian assaults. The massive disadvantage in ammunition – one Ukrainian shell for every five fired by the Russians – and in anti-missile defense systems led to a revision of earlier forecasts from late 2023, which had predicted that the current year would be marked by a form of stability on the front lines, without major breakthroughs. The idea of a Ukrainian collapse was no longer taboo. To bring the separate texts to a vote, Mike Johnson had to overcome the opposition of some Republican lawmakers and rely on the support of Democratic voices. Three lawmakers associated with the MAGA movement had stood out by threatening to impose a vote of no confidence against him: Marjorie Taylor Greene (Georgia), Paul Gosar (Arizona), and Thomas Massie (Kentucky). The latter stated on Saturday that "sending $100 billion abroad without strengthening our own borders shows that America is being put last." This criticism would have more relevance if Republicans had not blocked the adoption of a bipartisan text from the Senate in February regarding securing the border with Mexico. Before taking action in the House, Mike Johnson had to make a pilgrimage to Mar-a-Lago, Donald Trump's residence in Florida. "I think he's doing a really good job," the former president generously conceded about the speaker when he welcomed him on April 12. On that day, the Republican presidential candidate for November did not oppose the idea of providing new aid to Ukraine, expressing interest in the concept of a loan to Kiev. This is a ruse: the $9 billion in economic support granted to Ukraine in the form of credits will most likely never be repaid. But Mike Johnson was able to weakly rally Donald Trump to his text project, as the former president was also focused on his own legal troubles. In the House, the defiant Republican lawmakers still received support from one of the ideologues of the MAGA movement, former special advisor to Donald Trump, Steve Bannon, host of the "War Room" podcast. "Mike Johnson is a good man," he said during a conference in Florida. "Mike Johnson did not rise to the occasion and Mike Johnson must go, just like Kevin McCarthy." However, on Saturday, these MAGA lawmakers backed down from putting their vote of no confidence to a vote. Just a postponement, before a new settling of scores? That's what Marjorie Taylor Greene promises. Mike Johnson can breathe a sigh of relief nonetheless. He is still in his position and has just earned the respect of most of his peers.

title: "Hosting Xi Jinping is a policy of complicity in the Uyghur genocide."
lead: The sociologist Dilnur Reyhan, a French woman of Uyghur origin, calls on President Macron not to remain silent on Beijing's genocidal policy when meeting with Xi Jinping.
text_eng: At a time when the Russian state is rightfully banned from the Paris Olympics and severely sanctioned by Europeans for the aggressive war it is waging in Ukraine, at a time when its recently re-elected leader, Vladimir Putin, is the subject of an arrest warrant from the International Criminal Court (ICC), Emmanuel Macron is hosting Xi Jinping in France, the president of a country, China, which continues with impunity its genocidal policy against the Uighurs. China continues to use forced labor and subjects hundreds of thousands of Uighurs to slavery, millions of Uighurs have been imprisoned in camps and prisons because of their ethno-religious identity, families of French Uighur origin are still detained in China, the vast majority of Uighurs in France have lost all contact with their relatives and live under constant threat and intimidation. But France welcomes, in the name of a proclaimed reopening of the country after the Covid years and a policy of rapprochement with China, the architect and main perpetrator of the genocide targeting my people and inexorably destroying them. Our nights filled with nightmares, our disrupted genealogies, our sense of powerlessness, and our fear of being erased from the history of humanity therefore make no sense to Emmanuel Macron. Inviting the genocidal ogre to the Elysée, sharing a "more personal" time with him, all this to improve our commercial relations and to celebrate sixty years of "diplomatic friendships," what a strange banquet of ashes! Concentration camps and prisons But what, exactly, will he still have the right to discuss with Xi Jinping? The planned annexation of Taiwan? Tibetan children forcibly separated from their families and placed in state orphanages? The repression of dissident voices and political prisoners? Uighur victims, in Europe and in France, of transnational repression? Chinese ghost police stations violating France's territorial sovereignty? On January 20, 2022, in a solemn resolution, the National Assembly condemned the genocide of the Uighurs, recognized that the Chinese regime was imprisoning and torturing Uighurs, Kazakhs, and other Turkic peoples in concentration camps and prisons, that Uighur women were forcibly sterilized, that the Uighur population had halved in record time – in some prefectures, this drop is 84%; it recognized that hundreds of thousands of Uighur children were separated from their families to be assimilated into Chinese culture in state orphanages or Han families. The National Assembly recognized that Uighur identity was criminalized, Uighur language banned; it recognized that Uighur elites (artists, intellectuals, businesspeople, athletes, religious leaders...) were arrested and sometimes tortured, killed, disappeared. Even the parliamentary group of the President of the Republic - undoubtedly with his consent - overwhelmingly supported this resolution. Mr. Macron has even intervened in the past in favor of an Uyghur woman who survived the hell of the camps. He knows very well what is going on. But he remains silent and does nothing to stop this genocidal policy and to hold those responsible accountable. The Uyghur issue does not deserve such contempt. Yet it concerns France, which is connected in multiple ways with China. It concerns the Uyghurs residing in France, many of whom are also French. As French or Uyghurs in France, we are not only devastated, psychologically and sometimes physically, by the genocide: we live under the threat, intimidation, and insecurity. China benefits in France from numerous and powerful relays. Sense of injustice Welcoming our oppressor with all the honors of a state visit only reinforces the sense of injustice, powerlessness, and insecurity of the Uyghur diaspora in France. The message being sent is that the lives of the Uyghur people weigh little against the billions of Chinese yuan. This policy of contempt is also a policy of complicity, as economic cooperation with China strengthens Chinese racial capitalism that relies on forced labor. Mr. President, I have repeatedly called on you before your official trips to China or before Xi Jinping's visit to France, to pay attention to the crimes of this regime and Chinese colonialism, which is intrinsically linked to the policy of eradicating the Uyghurs. Neither the open letter co-signed by MEP Raphaël Glucksmann in 2019 [published in the newspaper Libération], nor the 250,000 signatories in 2021 of our national petition, have been enough for you to listen to us. As a Frenchwoman of Uyghur origin, as a professor specializing in Uyghur studies and colonialism, I am outraged, on behalf of the Uyghur diaspora in France, by your "friendship" with the oppressor of the Uyghur people. Words have meaning: it is indeed, in the Uyghur region, an ongoing genocide. You, who have acknowledged the tragic mistakes of French policy in Rwanda without being able to cleanse the honor of France, permanently tarnished for having supported the Hutu government that would perpetrate the genocide of the Tutsis, do not remain in history as the one who remained silent on the Uyghur genocide, knowing and doing nothing when you could have.

title: At the European Parliament, the National Rally, a constant supporter of authoritarian regimes.
lead: The study of the votes in the European Parliament sessions of the National Rally MEPs during the 2019-2024 term paints a picture of international relations as the far-right party envisions them. Their inconsistent decisions show little regard for the defense of human rights.
text_eng: Jordan Bardella likes to describe the European Parliament as a mere "rubber-stamp chamber." An assembly whose sole role would be to endorse decisions made by the "technocrats of Brussels." However, within the community Hemicycle, the Members of the European Parliament from the National Rally (RN) outline their vision of the world through their votes in session. Support for authoritarian regimes, refusal to denounce the application of the death penalty or other abuses against political opponents, ignorance of cases of violations of fundamental freedoms: the hundreds of decisions made by RN MEPs in Brussels and Strasbourg, analyzed by Le Monde, offer a panorama of international relations as envisioned by the far-right movement. Their positioning is all the more free as the place does not benefit from the media exposure - and possibly controversy - of the National Assembly. During the last term (2019-2024), RN members only had to justify their unwavering support for the Russian regime. The invasion of Ukraine decided by the President of Russia, Vladimir Putin, has indeed highlighted a constant support for the Kremlin. Until the outbreak of the war in February 2022, Jordan Bardella and his colleagues systematically voted against, whether it concerned the denunciation of the Russian law on "foreign agents" (December 19, 2019), the resolution following the poisoning of the main opponent Alexei Navalny (September 17, 2020), or the support for the repressed civil society and particularly for the human rights organization Memorial - Nobel Prize 2022 - (December 16, 2021). Principle of non-interference But the attention paid to the conflict in Ukraine since its outbreak has not convinced the RN troops to fall in line with the criticism of Vladimir Putin. Contrary to a large majority of the community assembly, the French delegation of the Identity and Democracy group now prefers to abstain on most texts in support of Kiev, whether it is to fear the "escalation of war" (October 6, 2022), to demand the creation of a "tribunal for the crime of aggression against Ukraine" (January 19, 2023), or to provide "unwavering support from the EU [European Union] to Ukraine" (February 29). The Russian regime no longer faces the reproach of the RN as before, even when it came to acknowledging the "inhumane detention conditions" endured by Alexei Navalny (ten abstentions and one opposition on February 16, 2023). "It is not up to the EU to decide whether a particular state has done things right. Diplomacy, as well as the military and defense, fall within the competence of the States. (...) We tried to adhere to this principle throughout the term," defended Jordan Bardella after the death in detention of the Russian opponent, announced on February 16. The study of Front National votes in the European Parliament quickly dismisses the doctrine that the president of the National Rally claims to have defended for five years. When a country accused of repression is among the opponents of the National Rally, the latter disregards all "sovereignty." Thus, its elected officials have voted on resolutions calling for the defense of oppressed opponents in Venezuela (March 14), Cuba (September 16 and December 16, 2021), Nicaragua (September 15, 2022), or Bolivia (April 29, 2021). The National Rally's variable application of a principle of non-interference in diplomatic affairs, depending on its proximity or affinity for the leaders in power, is particularly evident in the Maghreb. In 2023, its MEPs voted for a resolution condemning the detention of a journalist in Algeria (May 11), after unanimously opposing texts based on the same grounds regarding Tunisia (March 16) and Morocco (January 19). Blindness Marine Le Pen's party has often been criticized for its unwavering support for illiberal Polish and Hungarian leaders. For Poland, they opposed resolutions on the criminalization of sex education (November 14, 2019), on the "de facto ban on abortion rights" (November 26, 2020), or on the deterioration of the rule of law (September 16, 2021). For Hungary, they voted against resolutions on "violations of EU law and the rights of LGBTIQ citizens" (July 8, 2021) or on the disregard for the rule of law (June 1, 2023). Each time, the far-right MEPs invoke the democratic legitimacy of their allies and praise their alleged resistance to the European Commission. The coordinator of the National Rally delegation, MEP Thierry Mariani, denounces the European Parliament's desire to act as an "NGO" to deny certain members of the Twenty-Seven their "national competences." But this blindness to the repeated and documented violations of fundamental rights is assumed well beyond the EU's borders. Since 2019, the National Rally has consistently refused to criticize numerous authoritarian regimes, such as Egypt, whose President Abdel Fattah Al-Sissi's "fight against Islamism" is invoked to oppose several resolutions noting a deterioration in human rights (December 18, 2020, and November 24, 2022); an argument repeated to vote against a text denouncing the detention of human rights activist Ahmed Mansour in the United Arab Emirates (September 16, 2021). Thierry Mariani repeats that European Parliament resolutions, without binding value, "serve absolutely no purpose," and that their content is often much more complex than their title alone. But the National Rally delegation assumes the "consistency" of the line drawn by its votes on texts that are nevertheless devoid of legislative significance. Mariani also mocks the "acrobatics" of the MEPs from the presidential party who protest in the Community Hemicycle against leaders with whom the head of state is forming partnerships. "No team discipline." Behind the voting explanations of the RN MEPs, there is also a more vehement anti-Atlanticism than that carried by Marine Le Pen on the national stage. Because they refuse to be blindly pro-United States, they allow themselves to defend regimes that the three-time presidential candidate could not publicly support as easily. Near Russia, Belarus benefits consistently from the abstention of the former National Front MEPs when it comes, in recent years, to denouncing "inhumane treatment and hospitalization" of an opposition leader (May 11, 2023) or a "new wave of mass arrests" against opposition activists (February 8). China receives the same considerations from the RN. In five years and on dozens of votes, Jordan Bardella and his colleagues have only voted in favor of one resolution, on January 17. It concerned Beijing's growing influence on the EU's "critical infrastructure." They have never supported texts that express concern, for example, about forced labor and the situation of the Uighurs (abstention on December 17, 2020), violations of fundamental freedoms in Hong Kong (opposition on January 20, 2022), or the abduction of Tibetan children and "practices of forced assimilation" (abstention on December 14, 2023). As the European elections approach, the RN leadership knows that particular attention is paid to the recent votes of its MEPs. Sometimes instructions are given from Paris to prioritize abstention over opposition, in order to avoid hindering their "de-demonization" efforts. The party's choices during the last session of the European Parliament, from April 22 to 25, will not change the geopolitical orientations defended over the past five years, sometimes even in opposition to their allies in the Identity and Democracy group. "Radical right parties are still not accustomed to the negotiation and compromise that are necessary for majority groups," confirms Awenig Marié, a political science doctoral student at the Free University of Brussels. Within their own group, the RN MEPs vote without being subject to any team discipline. Therefore, they express themselves freely, according to their ideas or the message they wish to convey to their voters.

title: Sergei Shoigu dismissed by his friend Vladimir Putin and replaced by a manager at the Ministry of Defense.
lead: Andrei Belousov, long-time economic advisor to the Russian president, will have to continue the transformation of the economy in service of the military. As for the rest, the government reshuffle shows a concern for stability.
text_eng: Minister of Defense for twelve years, Sergei Shoigu did not survive the turmoil caused by two years of large-scale war against Ukraine. A close friend of Vladimir Putin, whom he takes hiking in the taiga every summer, Mr. Shoigu, 68, was dismissed on Sunday, May 12, and will be replaced by a civilian, economist Andrei Belousov. Sergei Shoigu's star, an experienced politician accustomed to government portfolios since 1991, had dimmed from the outset of the failed invasion of Ukraine in 2022. At the time, the Russian army appeared ill-prepared and far less modernized than Sergei Shoigu claimed, known more for his communication skills than as a reformer. The massive corruption scandal affecting his ministry, which erupted at the end of April and implicates even his deputy, Vice Minister Timur Ivanov, served as a trigger. For some observers, like former Kremlin adviser Abbas Galliamov, the logic is the opposite: the minister is not dismissed because of the corruption scandal, but the fact that it broke shows that the decision to remove Mr. Shoigu was already made. The extent of theft within the Ministry of Defense had been known for years and apparently accepted at the highest level. However, Mr. Shoigu's proximity to the president earned him a smooth landing as secretary of the Security Council, a position previously held by Nikolai Patrushev, who was removed from his duties. This other loyalist of Vladimir Putin will certainly be granted an honorary position in turn. Reflecting the close-knit and clan-like functioning of the Russian state's summit, his son, Dmitry Patrushev, is expected to be appointed as deputy prime minister in the coming days. Prolonged confrontation with the West Paradoxically, this reshuffle at the helm of the Ministry of Defense comes at a time when the Russian army is achieving success on the battlefield. After withstanding the Ukrainian counteroffensive in the summer of 2023, the Moscow army has advanced in the Donbass region and can now afford to launch an offensive in the Kharkiv region. The profile of the new minister - whose appointment still needs to be confirmed by the Federation Council - has also surprised. Mr. Belousov, 65, is indeed a loyalist of Vladimir Putin, but he is primarily an expert in economic matters, with no experience in the military field. A longtime Kremlin adviser, he has been the first deputy prime minister of Mikhail Mishustin's government since 2020. However, his appointment follows a logic of further militarization of the Russian economy to face not only the challenges of the war in Ukraine but also a prolonged confrontation with the West. Moscow has, for now, managed this transition, partly thanks to China's support and by adapting its industrial tool early on. Mr. Belousov's mission will be to continue this effort over time. The man, who is very religious, is known to be a supporter of a strong state-controlled economy, a vision shared by Vladimir Putin. He has also been behind several tax increases. A foreign businessman who knows him well points out that Andrei Belousov has a reputation for integrity and seriousness within the government, and that he understands the needs of the private sector. On Sunday evening, Dmitry Peskov, the Kremlin spokesperson, indirectly confirmed this new direction for the position of Minister of Defense, by noting that military budgets continue to grow, reaching 6.7% of GDP (not including secret expenses, which represent 30% of the Russian budget). Therefore, the new minister will not only have to channel these immense financial flows and calm appetites, but also avoid the emergence of significant imbalances in the economy, which is already showing signs of overheating. In this configuration, the conduct of the war should be entirely the responsibility of the military high command, or even the Kremlin. Despite weakened authority, Valery Gerasimov will "for now" retain his position as chief of the general staff, Mr. Peskov also emphasized. This clarification does not reveal any potential changes... Maintaining stability at the top. Mr. Putin's priority, despite the unexpected dismissal of his friend Shoigu, seems to be maintaining stability at the top, which is almost obsessive for him. The government reshuffle following his inauguration for a fifth term on May 7 ultimately results in a limited number of movements. Mr. Mishustin, prime minister since 2020, retains his position, as do most ministers, including Sergei Lavrov, foreign minister for twenty years, whom the Russian media frequently report as wanting to leave. Denis Manturov, responsible for industry and trade, who has so far overseen the reorganization of the defense industry, is promoted to first deputy prime minister. Among the newcomers, four governors have been called back to Moscow, including the one from the Kemerovo region, Sergei Tsiviliov, appointed to energy, and especially known for being the husband of a supposed niece of Vladimir Putin. Another sign of a tightening of power and the primacy given to personal loyalty ties, Boris Kovalchuk, son of Vladimir Putin's close associate Yuri Kovalchuk, is appointed head of the Audit Chamber.

title: "This is one of the most spectacular geopolitical effects of the war in Ukraine: the strengthening of the China-Russia axis, in opposition to the United States."
lead: In the global game of balancing great powers, Russia's pivot towards China and its potential integration into a "Pax Sinica" are prompting Washington to ask Europeans to put pressure on Beijing, analyzes Sylvie Kauffmann, columnist at "Le Monde".
text_eng: Before the visit of the German Chancellor, Olaf Scholz, to China, which ends on Wednesday, April 17, and before the trip of Chinese President Xi Jinping to Europe, where Emmanuel Macron will host him in Paris on May 6, Washington has hammered a message to the Europeans: since you maintain high-level relations with Beijing, use them to pressure China to stop assisting Russia in its war in Ukraine. This message, actively relayed in recent weeks by Stéphane Séjourné, the French Minister of Foreign Affairs, in Beijing, by American leaders themselves, and by Olaf Scholz, who complied on Tuesday, reflects the United States' concern about one of the most spectacular geopolitical effects of the war in Ukraine: the deepening China-Russia relationship. To convince those who doubted that this deepening has a concrete impact on the course of the war and partly explains the resilience of the Russian economy, Washington made public, on April 12, previously classified data: for example, in the last quarter of 2023, China provided over 70% of Russia's imports of machine tools used in the production of ballistic missiles. And for the entire year of 2023, 90% of Russian imports of microelectronics, crucial for the manufacturing of missiles and tanks, came from China. A significant trend. Why this sudden emphasis from the Americans on the Chinese factor? Washington was surprised by how quickly the Russian industry managed, despite Western sanctions, to recover and produce an arsenal in quantity that makes a difference on the ground against struggling Ukrainian forces, slowed down by the slowness of Western supplies. The considerable revenue contribution that Chinese and Indian imports of Russian oil have provided to the Kremlin was known; these have now compensated for Moscow's loss of the European market. But the material assistance provided by China, the extent of which was previously underestimated, seems to play an important role, even if it does not cross the red line of delivering lethal weapons. Another explanation is the observation of this significant trend in the evolution of power balances between major powers, which is the strengthening of the Russo-Chinese axis. For the United States, which measures everything in terms of the rise of Chinese power, this is not a trend to be neglected: after all, the element that cements the Sino-Russian relationship is the common opposition to American dominance. This rapprochement is not entirely new: Moscow turned to China as early as 2014 when relations between Russia and Western countries cooled following the annexation of Crimea, which led to a first wave of sanctions. Commercial exchanges between China and Russia have significantly intensified since then. However, the European Union remained an important economic partner for Moscow - until 2022. Once the large-scale war against Ukraine was launched, Russia had to pivot completely towards China. The meeting of the two presidents, Putin and Xi, on February 4, 2022 in Beijing and their joint statement proclaiming an "unlimited friendship" had been seen as an important step in this rapprochement. This was three weeks before the invasion of Ukraine; speculations have never ceased about whether Putin had warned Xi of the offensive he was planning. But their relationship has solidified since then. "The only way for Russia to support its confrontation with the West is to integrate Russia's economy, intellectual resources, and military technology into a 'Pax Sinica,' a Chinese-dominated order with Eurasia at its geographical core," writes Alexander Gabuev, an expert at the Carnegie think tank, in Foreign Affairs. Russia is in the process of locking itself into a vassal relationship with China." A somewhat forced marriage. Thus, not only was the objective sought by those in the West who advocated for dialogue with Vladimir Putin - to prevent Russia from falling into the Chinese orbit and to anchor Russia to Europe - not achieved, but the war in Ukraine has, in practice, thrown Russia into the arms of China. However, Beijing does not seem to approach this somewhat forced marriage with love in its eyes. Despite frequent contacts between the leaders - Putin is expected in Beijing again in May - the phrase "unlimited friendship" has not been repeated, and the two countries have not formed an alliance. Some Chinese scholars and experts who speak out externally emphasize reservations about the authenticity of Russia's pivot towards Asia: according to Guan Guihai from Peking University, Putin's stubbornness on Ukraine is explained by his fear of losing Russia's European identity. If Russia were to lose Ukraine, he writes on the Council on Foreign Relations website, "its link with Europe would be substantially weakened, its strategic space would move away from the heart of Europe, and Russia would become more of an Asian country." Another Chinese reservation: the two countries do not share the same vision of the evolution of the world order. "Russia seeks to undermine the existing regional and international order through war, while China aims to resolve conflicts peacefully," writes academic Feng Yujun in The Economist, who is also unimpressed by Putin's military performance in Ukraine, where the "myth of Russia's invincibility has collapsed." The suzerain is not gentle with the vassal. Faced with this China-Russia axis, the United States are strengthening their Indo-Pacific bloc, while asking Europeans to have a "tough conversation" with Beijing about Ukraine. However, it remains to be seen if said Europeans are willing to do so.

title: In response to Israel's maneuvers, the ICC prosecutor warns against any obstruction of justice.
lead: For several days, the Prime Minister of the Hebrew State, Benjamin Netanyahu, who fears being placed under arrest warrant, is urging his allies to put pressure on the International Criminal Court in The Hague.
text_eng: These are not arrest warrants, but it is a scathing statement issued on Friday, May 3, by the prosecutor of the International Criminal Court (ICC). In this text, Karim Khan demands that "all attempts to obstruct, intimidate, or influence" the employees of his office "cease immediately." The admonition targets the Israeli prime minister and his allies. Concerned that the Court may issue arrest warrants for several senior officials of the Israeli state, including himself potentially, Benjamin Netanyahu has been urging his Western partners for several days to exert pressure on the institution. Referring to threats against the Court and its staff, the prosecutor's office notes that, "even without consequences, [the threats] can constitute an offense against the administration of justice." The ICC's criminal code "prohibits intimidation and influence peddling, whether by coercion or persuasion," the statement further explains. Obstructing justice is punishable by the Court with a five-year prison sentence and a fine. From a reliable source, it is specified that the prosecutor is soon to submit his arrest warrant requests to the judges. This step was initially planned for this week but has been postponed. The three judges will need to review the evidence attached to the case before validating or rejecting the requests. On April 16, according to Israeli television, Benjamin Netanyahu held an emergency meeting to counter the risk of arrest warrants being issued. On Friday, April 26, on the social network X, he criticized the ICC, speaking of "moral and legal bankruptcy" and "antisemitism," arguing that it has not opened an investigation into the crimes committed by the Bashar Al-Assad regime in Syria, a territory over which the Court does not have jurisdiction. The Israeli prime minister had already made these accusations in 2021 when the institution opened an investigation into crimes committed by Israel in the occupied territories since 2014. The procedure includes attacks carried out by Hamas in southern Israel on October 7, 2023, and the offensive launched in retaliation by the Israeli state against the Gaza Strip. "Strong" magistrates Since the first attempt by the Palestinian Authority to bring the case to the Court in 2009, Israel has been using the same political, economic, and security levers to keep the judges in The Hague at bay. In recent days, according to the Israeli press, Mr. Netanyahu has reportedly called several allies of Israel, such as Austria and the Czech Republic, who had opposed the "State of Palestine" becoming a member of the ICC in 2015. The prime minister aims to persuade the Court's member states to stop funding it if it issues warrants. In Washington, American senators are considering possible retaliatory measures. The United States already has a range of laws against the ICC, to which they have not joined and with which they cooperate "à la carte," when its investigations serve their interests, such as the one targeting Russian President Vladimir Putin. The Israeli Prime Minister has also asked Washington to influence the countries from which the three judges who will be responsible for validating arrest warrants originate. These judges are from Romania, Benin, and Mexico. "They are strong and have already resisted other pressures," commented several legal experts in The Hague in 2021, during the deliberations that preceded the opening of the investigation. According to the American news site Axios, Benjamin Netanyahu warned the US administration that if warrants were issued, it would cause the "collapse" of the Palestinian Authority by blocking the transfer of customs duties collected by Israel on Palestinian imports. Israel has used this threat several times, notably in 2014 when the Palestinians were considering becoming members of the ICC. Contribution from Ireland This investigation is the most sensitive ever undertaken by the Court. In the coming months, the prosecutor will need the cooperation of states to advance his investigations, question witnesses, and also protect them. He will also need the collaboration of UN agencies, which Israel continues to challenge. So far, the Assembly, which brings together the 124 States parties to the Court, has not spoken out. However, on Friday, Ireland signed two cooperation agreements with the Court's registry, announcing a contribution to a fund dedicated to security, particularly in the field of information technology, and witness protection. Declan Smyth, the chief legal officer at the Irish Ministry of Foreign Affairs, said he was "deeply troubled by the increasing threats facing the International Criminal Court and those who cooperate with it," adding that "the security of those involved in the judicial system is an essential element of the rule of law."

title: The US Secretary of State, Antony Blinken, is in Beijing to maintain a fragile relationship between the United States and China.
lead: Areas of agreement are rare between the two global powers, but both Beijing and Washington have an interest in maintaining a minimum level of dialogue to prevent the escalation of tensions.
text_eng: The United States and China are talking, and that's a start: in a tense, even conflictual relationship on many issues, the visit of U.S. Secretary of State Antony Blinken to Shanghai and then Beijing helps to maintain dialogue. In a positive sign, Mr. Blinken was finally received on Friday, April 26, by Chinese leader Xi Jinping, who struck a relatively conciliatory tone: "China is happy to see the United States confident, open, prosperous, and thriving. We hope that the United States can also consider China's development from a positive perspective," said the Chinese president, according to local state media. This was a reference to what China sees as U.S. efforts to "contain" China, hindering its economic and technological development. In the morning, Mr. Blinken had a five-hour discussion with Foreign Minister Wang Yi, who had already addressed this issue in a harsher tone: "The United States has adopted a series of measures aimed at preventing the development of China's economy, trade, science, and technology. This is not fair competition, it is containment. This will not eliminate risks, but create more," warned the Chinese foreign minister, mentioning the risk of entering a "negative spiral." Mr. Blinken, for his part, indicated that he had expressed his concerns to Mr. Xi regarding China's support for Russia, which includes providing materials, components, and machine tools essential to the Russian war effort against Ukraine. "Russia would struggle to continue its assault on Ukraine without China's support," Blinken told reporters after his meeting with Xi. He also addressed the issue of Taiwan, which Beijing dreams of annexing, and tensions in the South China Sea, reaffirming the "unwavering" support of the United States for the Philippines, an American ally in the region. He emphasized, however, the importance of dialogue, especially military dialogue, which resumed following Xi Jinping's meeting with Joe Biden in San Francisco in November 2023. "We are committed to maintaining and strengthening communication channels to advance this agenda, and once again, to manage our differences responsibly to avoid any miscommunication, misperception, or miscalculation," Blinken said. This is indeed progress: China, which had largely isolated itself from the world during the "Covid years," had suspended military-to-military dialogue in response to the visit of Nancy Pelosi, Speaker of the U.S. House of Representatives, to Taiwan in August 2022. In early 2023, attempts to restore exchanges had faltered after the incident involving a Chinese spy balloon drifting in U.S. airspace, eventually shot down by the U.S. Air Force. Washington advocates for a strong relationship with Beijing while keeping communication channels open to prevent any unintended escalation. Both sides are also seeking to maintain appearances as the Chinese economy has experienced a significant slowdown since 2022. Relations with the United States have a significant impact on the confidence of economic actors, both Chinese and foreign, who are considering investing in China. Beijing is actively courting major foreign companies. Chinese leaders are also cautious ahead of the November American elections. Hostility towards China is one of the few bipartisan consensus topics in the United States, leading to a tense atmosphere during the campaign as both sides seek to demonstrate their firmness against the "Chinese threat." On Wednesday, April 24, Joe Biden signed a law that would force the sale of TikTok to an American company or, failing that, ban the app in the United States. The bill had been passed with a large bipartisan majority the previous week. Mr. Blinken had initially started his visit with a more relaxed agenda, highlighting the peaceful economic and cultural ties that the United States seeks to maintain with China. Upon arriving in Shanghai on April 24, he visited the Yu Garden, dating back to the 16th century, like any good tourist. In the evening, he attended a basketball game and spent the following day with American and Chinese students from the Shanghai campus of New York University, as well as with American entrepreneurs. In early April, the visit of the U.S. Treasury Secretary, Janet Yellen, did not lead to any concrete agreements. Shortly after her departure, the Russian Foreign Minister, Sergey Lavrov, was warmly received by Xi Jinping in a much more cordial atmosphere than with the Americans. The Chinese leader praised the joint efforts of both countries to "reform global governance." Lavrov, on his part, stated that "consolidating and strengthening" relations with China was "the priority of Russian foreign policy," according to Chinese state media. Russia announced on Thursday, April 25, that Vladimir Putin would visit Beijing in May, marking his first foreign visit since his reelection. This visit is likely to take place after Xi's visit to Europe in early May, during which he is scheduled to visit Serbia, France, and Hungary.

title: The glide bombs, the "ultimate weapon" of the Russian forces, or a sign of the Ukrainians' lack of equipment?
lead: Two years after the start of hostilities, the Russian army seems to have found a way to break the balance between firepower and defense means with these low-cost improved Soviet-era bombs.
text_eng: "These bombs completely destroy any position. All buildings and structures are pulverized after the impact of just one of them, and they drop sixty to eighty on us in a single day. I'll let you imagine the conditions in which our soldiers are fighting," wrote Egor Sugar on X February 16, one of the Ukrainian defenders of Avdiivka, a few hours before retreating with his comrades. It is a field of ruins that the Russian forces overran the next day, after four months of siege and unprecedented bombardments. But the capture of this locality in the Donetsk oblast, which could open the way to the major urban areas of Kramatorsk and Sloviansk, is one of their most resounding victories. The so-called "gliding" bombs mentioned by Egor Sugar played a decisive role in this battle, and they continue to sow such terror in the Ukrainian trenches that many observers see them as the weapon best able to tip the balance in Moscow's favor, after months of stalemate. The Russian army is now dropping them in large numbers on the town of Chasiv Yar, one of the last strongholds on the road to Kramatorsk. Their destructive power, combined with the numerical superiority of the Russians, will likely allow Russian troops to break through the defense lines built at great expense since the failure of the Ukrainian offensive in the summer of 2023, warned several high-ranking officers interviewed anonymously by the American online journal Politico at the beginning of the month. Up to 3 tons Two years after the start of hostilities, the Russian army seems to have found a way to make a difference with these gliding bombs. Its arsenal has been full of them for decades, as they are smooth bombs from the Soviet era called "FAB," highly explosive bombs that the Anglo-Saxons nickname "dumb bombs," meaning unguided bombs, to which foldable wings have been added after release, allowing them to glide, and a satellite guidance system. This kit, called the "Unified Planning and Correction Module" (UMPK in Russian), transforms them at little cost into KAB, or corrected trajectory aerial bombs, weapons that are both precise and devastating with their 500 to 1,500 kilograms of explosives. The Russian Minister of Defense, Sergei Shoigu, even unveiled a 3-ton version on March 21, six times the payload of a Franco-British Scalp-Storm Shadow missile, at a cost that is incomparable. According to Michael Peck, from the think-tank Center for European Policy Analysis, the UMPK kit, inspired by the American JDAM kits, guided bombs by GPS in service since the late 1990s, costs around 20,000 dollars (just under 19,000 euros), in addition to the modest cost of the bomb itself, while the cost of a cruise missile is around a million dollars (approximately 940,000 euros). Depending on the altitude and speed at which they are dropped, the range of these bombs can reach up to 90 kilometers, which most of the time leaves Russian bombers out of reach of the surface-to-air missiles currently possessed by Ukrainian forces. Powered only by gravity, they also do not leave any thermal trace that could make them vulnerable, and the UMPK kit's electronics have been designed to protect them from jamming. "The only way to protect oneself from them is to shoot down the aircraft carrying them," recently summarized Ukrainian Minister of Foreign Affairs Dmytro Kuleba. According to the Ukrainian military staff, Russian aviation lost thirteen aircraft in two weeks during the month of February, which could not be verified. The cost-benefit ratio seems to have convinced Moscow to continue down this path. Glide bombs "allow for sustained and precise bombardments with acceptable attrition rates for aviation," confirms Frederik Mertens, a researcher at the Centre for Strategic Studies in The Hague (Netherlands). A force multiplier In the first ten weeks of the year, Russian fighter-bombers dropped more than 3,500 of them, as stated in mid-March by Ukrainian Deputy Minister of Defense Ivan Gavryliouk in an article published by the Ukrinform agency. Over 3,000 glide bombs were reportedly used in the month of March alone, as highlighted by Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky in early April to support his requests for additional air defense means. For now, Ukrainian forces have "almost no countermeasures," as recently emphasized by a contributor to the Telegram channel DeepState, close to the Ukrainian Ministry of Defense. However, glide bombs are far from impossible to intercept, according to Frederik Mertens: "They are small and have a relatively low signature, but they are neither particularly fast, stealthy, nor maneuverable. Advanced defense systems should be able to shoot them down quite easily, but since they are relatively cheap and abundant, using rare and expensive missiles is only viable to defend key targets." While not the ultimate weapon, "precision bombs change the balance between firepower and front line protection," he continues. "They allow to pierce defenses that can resist a torrent of 152 or 155 mm shells." They have enabled Russian forces to "multiply their striking power, which makes things easier for ground troops, especially against fortified defenses, and against an adversary lacking in air defense capabilities," also notes Marina Miron, a researcher at the War Studies department of King's College London. "At the moment, the Ukrainians cannot adapt to them," she emphasizes, pointing out that precision bombs also play a role in communication. For Moscow, they have become a deterrent and intimidation element, she explains, doubting in particular that the Russian aviation has aircraft capable of launching the FAB-3000 bombs recently unveiled by Sergei Shoigu. "Patriots, Patriots, Patriots" For Ukraine, it is a strong argument to quickly obtain advanced weapons, especially American Patriot missiles, which would allow them to keep Russian bombers at a more respectable distance, as well as the F-16s that the country has long been requesting. Its allies have promised around sixty of them, with the first expected this summer. "The F-16 fighters will be absolutely essential," says Frederik Mertens. The question remains as to what armaments they will be equipped with. "Will they only receive air-to-ground missiles?" Marina Miron wonders. In that case, they will not be able to target Russian aircraft. It will also depend on where they will be deployed, refueled, and maintained, because the Russian military has projection capabilities that go well beyond the front line. According to Justin Bronk, an expert in defense systems and researcher at the Royal United Services Institute (RUSI), a British think tank, it is even unlikely that the F-16s will eliminate the threat of glide bombs, regardless of their armament: "Russian bomber fighters launch them from far enough away that Ukrainian F-16s will have difficulty targeting them, especially considering the threat posed by Russian missile batteries." "Because the Ukrainians lack aircraft and pilots, have only limited weapon stocks, and must face aircraft such as the SU-35 and MIG-31 or surface-to-air missiles like the S-400 and Pantsir, they are now on strategic, operational, and tactical defense," Mr. Mertens continues. "If the shortage of Ukrainian interceptors reaches a level where Russian forces can venture beyond the front lines, the consequences will be even more disastrous. This would allow them to conduct the aerial campaign that has not yet taken place." It is still unknown what exactly the $61 billion (approximately 57 billion euros) American aid approved on April 20 by the U.S. House of Representatives will consist of, but the expectations are clear: "At the end of March, Dmytro Kuleba summarized, 'Give us those darn Patriots!'"

title: In Russia, Vladimir Putin, recently inaugurated, reappoints his prime minister.
lead: Mikhail Mishustin, former head of the federal tax service, was appointed prime minister in 2020. He is seen as a technocrat who faithfully carries out measures dictated by the Kremlin.
text_eng: Russian President Vladimir Putin, inaugurated for a fifth term on Tuesday, maintained on Friday, May 10, Mikhail Mishustin as the head of his government, after weeks of intense speculation about a possible reshuffle in Moscow. Vladimir Putin is expected to appoint or confirm his ministers, following his electoral victory in March in a presidential election devoid of any opposition. "A lot has been done in difficult conditions, and it seems to me that it would be good for us to continue working together," the president told his prime minister on Friday, according to a Kremlin report. "I think we are on the right track," he assured him, saying he had submitted his nomination to the State Duma, the lower house of the Russian Parliament. Unsurprisingly, the Duma then approved the nomination. Possible reshuffle Mikhail Mishustin, former head of the Federal Tax Service, was appointed prime minister in 2020. He is seen as a technocrat who faithfully implements measures dictated by the Kremlin. "We will do everything in our power to develop our economy, to justify the trust of our people," he promised Vladimir Putin on Friday. The names of the other ministers are expected to be announced in the coming weeks. Observers strongly suggest that the Russian president may reshuffle his government, more than two years after the start of an offensive in Ukraine that has disrupted the Russian economy and society. Vladimir Putin generally surrounds himself with allies whose loyalty is unquestionable, while ensuring that no senior official gains enough power or popularity to be seen as a rival or successor. After nearly a quarter of a century in power, the Russian leader will remain in office until 2030, with the possibility of serving another term until 2036.

title: Crisis in Georgia: "Clearly, the Georgian Dream party has made its choice and that choice is Russia."
lead: In Tbilisi, where the Orthodox Easter interrupted the protests against the bill on "foreign influence," which will return for a third reading in Parliament on May 13, opponents see Moscow's shadow in the authoritarian turn of the ruling party.
text_eng: The protesters have packed up, Parliament has closed its doors, calm has temporarily settled, on Sunday, May 5, on Rustaveli Avenue in the center of Tbilisi, the Georgian capital, nearly deserted due to the Orthodox Easter celebrations. After three weeks of mobilization against the controversial law on "foreign influence" wanted by the government and contested by a large part of the population, this Easter truce is more of a waiting game, with each side taking the opportunity to refine its strategy for the next step, the adoption of the law in the third and final reading, scheduled for May 13, when the deputies return from vacation. The young pro-European Georgians of "Gen Z," who are at the heart of the protest, promise to block the doors of Parliament, camp all around, come in large numbers, and "not give up." The authorities are just as determined, Bidzina Ivanichvili, the founder and leader of the Georgian Dream, the party in power for nearly twelve years, threatened, during a rally on Monday, April 29, to "punish" his political opponents after the next elections, raising fears of a turn towards authoritarianism and repression. In a speech with conspiratorial undertones, the billionaire mentioned the foreign threats facing the country. According to him, they come from the "world party of war," a euphemism for Americans and Europeans, as well as internal enemies, namely NGOs and "pseudo-elites." These enemies, he criticized, are "stateless," an accusation straight out of the USSR of the 1930s. He assured that the new legislation aims only to strengthen the transparency of NGO financing, which, without it, are "the main instrument for the appointment of a Georgian government by foreign powers." However, he said nothing about the Russian army's occupation of 20% of Georgian territory (Abkhazia and South Ossetia) and avoided mentioning the war in Ukraine. Social and religious conservatism His speech marks a rupture. Until now, the Georgian Dream had tried, at least in appearance, to display a semblance of pro-Western rhetoric, maintaining year after year that obtaining EU candidate status and integration into NATO were its objectives. Now, its leaders openly look towards Russia, with which Georgia has had no diplomatic relations since the war that opposed them in the summer of 2008. The Georgian public opinion, which ardently wishes to see the country align with the Old Continent, is the main obstacle to rapprochement with Moscow, or at least with its societal model. "We feel European," repeated the young protesters, who speak English rather than Russian, in the streets of Tbilisi in early May. Many Georgians who support European integration fear witnessing a creeping Russification of society. The social and religious conservatism of the ruling party, which is vehemently anti-LGBT, homophobic, focused on family and Orthodox faith, aligns closely with the "traditional values" championed by Vladimir Putin and Patriarch Kirill, the head of the Russian Orthodox Church, whose influence on the Georgian patriarchate remains significant. "Bidzina Ivanichvili's speech is an open declaration of a shift in foreign policy. After such statements, one can think that anything is possible. Clearly, Georgian Dream has made its choice, and that choice is Russia," observes Nika Gvaramia, founder of the country's main opposition television channel, Mtavari. The ultimate test will not be the adoption of the law in the third reading but the legislative elections scheduled for October, which will truly determine if Georgia can maintain its prospects for accession. "The law will likely be adopted, but its authors will come out so discredited that they could lose the elections," hopes Mr. Gvaramia, convinced that the apolitical, leaderless "Gen Z," which generally does not vote, could turn out to vote this time to block Georgian Dream. It could be a pipe dream. Once the law is adopted, the possibilities for international organizations to monitor the elections will be limited. "Through this law, the ruling party seeks to deepen societal polarization before the elections, to make it harder for international observers to monitor the elections," confirms Kakha Gogolashvili, senior researcher at the Rondeli Foundation in Tbilisi. For him, Bidzina Ivanichvili, who amassed his fortune in Russia in the 1990s, wants to halt the EU integration process, which he deems harmful to his interests. That's why he brought the law back to Parliament for adoption after an unsuccessful first attempt in March 2023, which had triggered widespread protests, forcing him to backtrack. "In essence, Bidzina Ivanichvili's oligarchic regime does not need integration. If he reforms the country according to European standards, he will lose all his leverage over the state institutions he has confiscated, including the judiciary and the central bank. Not to mention the lack of transparency that he will be required to address," argues the researcher. Finding official information has become difficult, he notes: "Government decrees are no longer published, nothing is known about gas transit contracts even though this information should be accessible since these are public enterprises." "Ivanichvili is afraid of Putin" Most critics of the "Russian law," as Georgians call it, see Moscow's hand behind the Tbilisi Parliament's vote. The text being adopted is indeed similar to the one ratified by the Duma in 2012, which was used to silence opposition to Vladimir Putin's regime. Everywhere Russia is influential, in Belarus, Kyrgyzstan, Abkhazia (a Georgian province invaded by the Russian army in 2008), and finally in the Serbian Republic of Bosnia, a law on "foreign agents" has already been adopted. "The Georgian Dream party has been ordered by Russia to pass this text, there is no doubt about it," says Nika Gvaramia. "Bidzina Ivanichvili is afraid of Putin, he knows his cruelty. Moreover, it is not excluded that the Russians have threatened him," suggests Kakha Gogolashvili. The Kremlin is all the more determined to bring Georgia back into its fold as relations have significantly cooled with Armenia, the latter being tempted to pivot towards the West after being abandoned by the Russian ally during Azerbaijan's reconquest of Nagorno-Karabakh. According to several observers, the loss of Armenia was a shock to the Russian government, which has always been obsessed with controlling its neighborhood. "Anything can be expected," is the phrase of the moment in Tbilisi. Traumatized by the 2008 war, the population fears seeing Russian tanks at the gates of Tbilisi again one day, a scenario that Mr. Ivanichvili consistently presents as the only possible bulwark against the belligerent northern neighbor. The tanks are still far away, but hybrid warfare has already begun. "To restore its control over Georgia and Armenia, the Russian leadership uses hybrid methods, it buys off elites, sows confusion and insecurity, and stifles civil society," summarizes Kakha Gogolashvili.

title: Speech by Emmanuel Macron at the Sorbonne: "On the defense of Europe, progress has been minimal since 2017."
lead: If the head of state's intervention at the prestigious university shows his awareness of the extent of the Russian threat, there is still much to be done to implement a real change in strategic direction, according to former ambassador Michel Duclos, in an opinion piece in "Le Monde".
text_eng: President Macron's assessment of Europe is certainly not negligible, as he made clear in his speech at the Sorbonne on April 25, 2024. However, on one issue, progress has been minimal since his first "Sorbonne speech" in 2017, especially given the destabilizing risk posed to the Old Continent by Russia's aggression in Ukraine. This issue is the defense of Europe. Why do Europeans find it difficult to do more in this area? We might be tempted to summarize this complicated subject in a simple formula: underestimation of the Russian threat for a long time, and overestimation of the American security guarantee (at least, its sustainability). The French certainly professed not to fall into the second of these two pitfalls, but they went very far in the first. And from this perspective, Mr. Macron has been a particularly zealous continuator. This is the dark side of his European policy. The meeting at Brégançon in August 2019 illustrated a surprising confidence in dialogue with the Kremlin, even after the invasion of Ukraine, not to mention a series of unfortunate statements ("not to humiliate Russia," for example). Under these conditions, it is difficult to play a leadership role in Europe, especially to convince our partners of the need to move towards greater strategic autonomy. Some argue that the French authorities' illusion about Russia was shared by Germany and many others, including American foreign policy gurus such as Henry Kissinger or Zbigniew Brzezinski. Fine, but France did not have as pressing interests in the matter as Germany, while being less removed from the Russian reality than post-Cold War America. The UK did not fall into the trap. From 2011-2012, and even more so from the annexation of Crimea in 2014, it should have been obvious that Vladimir Putin had chosen a ruthless confrontation with the West. Collective blindness. In fact, it was not only Mr. Macron who went astray, but a good part of the French political class, as was evident in 2017 when he was the least "pro-Russian" of the presidential candidates. His predecessor, Mr. Hollande, was more insightful than many others, but he too wanted to believe in "dialogue" with Russia by inventing the so-called "Normandy negotiations" (Germany, France, Ukraine, Russia) to resolve the Ukrainian crisis. How to explain this collective blindness? One possible key to understanding lies in the fact that French politicians live, in terms of foreign policy, on a distorted version of General de Gaulle's legacy. They equate this heritage with the challenge to the "policy of blocs," leading to dialogue with Moscow to counterbalance American dominance. The same inspiration - the same strategic compass - has survived the end of the Cold War, with formulas such as "multipolarity" or "France, a balancing power." However, this approach attributed to General de Gaulle corresponded to a specific period, that of détente in the mid-1960s. During the Berlin Crisis (1960-1961) or the Cuban Missile Crisis (1962), or even after the invasion of Czechoslovakia (1968), de Gaulle did not hesitate to support allies; it would never have occurred to him to seek common ground with Nikita Khrushchev or Leonid Brezhnev. Unfortunately, the current situation regarding Russia today more closely resembles that of the early 1960s than détente, and is even worse as war now rages in the heart of the Old Continent. "Europe as a power." This is why it is time for France to change its strategic compass. Are we heading in that direction? In any case, President Macron confirmed in his second speech at the Sorbonne a more relevant assessment of European security issues: "The essential condition for our security is that Russia does not win the war of aggression it is waging in Ukraine," he reiterated once again. The president finds new arguments in this observation for the "Europe as a power" that he has long advocated.
