To be fully understood by its partners, it will undoubtedly be necessary for France to increase the level of military support our country provides to Ukraine, which is currently lower than that of Germany. For example, at a time when the Russians are increasing their strikes on the rear, could the French not do more for Ukrainian air defense, as the Germans did by sending an additional Patriot battery? French diplomacy will also have to deploy - given the differences that separate us from Berlin on these issues - a clever strategy so that the next European Council meeting in June makes strong decisions on new sources of European-level financing, both to further arm Ukraine and to support a European defense industrial base. For example, the "community preference" for armaments advocated by the president in his speech is not self-evident beyond our borders. A change in strategic compass implies other reorientations. However, it should not abolish France's vocation to be a bridgehead to the global South. Our country could set the goal of contributing to the emergence of two coalitions among nations: A progressive North-South coalition, serving global issues such as development and climate; and a "stabilizing" coalition, bringing together Western countries and their allies as well as Southern states (which generally seek stability), in order to contain revisionist powers - aggressive or potentially so - with Russia under Putin's leadership serving as a model.

title: European elections: Raphaël Glucksmann denounces the "brutalization of public debate" and accuses the "unbowed" movement, following his removal from a demonstration.
lead: The socialist Member of the European Parliament had to be evacuated from a demonstration in Saint-Etienne on May 1st after being booed with shouts of "Get out", "Glucksmann get out" and "Palestine will live" by protesters.
text_eng: Following his expulsion from the May 1st demonstration in Saint-Etienne, the candidate of the Socialist Party (PS) and his Place publique movement in the European elections, Raphaël Glucksmann, denounced on Thursday, May 2nd, on the set of France 5 a "toxic atmosphere" in public debate. "At some point, it has to stop, because the violence of words always leads to violence in actions. We need to return to reason, to republican debate," he declared. The MEP had to be escorted out by his security service after being heckled with shouts of "Get out", "Glucksmann, leave" and "Palestine will live" by protesters who threw paint at him. The socialist candidate once again pointed the finger at Jean-Luc Mélenchon and the "insubordinate circles" who, according to him, "orchestrated a violent, deceitful campaign against [his] person, against [his] votes in the European Parliament, against our positions." "And that's what produced this atmosphere in Saint-Etienne," he criticized. Like the day before, Raphaël Glucksmann once again accused communist and "insubordinate" militants of preventing him from participating in this May 1st gathering. A few hours earlier on Thursday, the president of the La France insoumise (LFI) group in the National Assembly, Mathilde Panot, had denied any involvement of sympathizers in this action, accusing the socialist candidate of having "lied" on this matter. According to Ms. Panot, the MEP "exaggerated [this incident] himself to accuse the 'insubordinates'." "Raphaël Glucksmann, apologize!", also thundered Jean-Luc Mélenchon in a message posted on X Wednesday afternoon. According to LFI, this action was carried out by the Young Communists of Loire, who admitted their participation. But TF1 and LCI obtained the testimony of a militant, former substitute of an unelected LFI candidate in the 2022 legislative elections, claiming, with an "insubordinate" flag on his shoulder, "to have been one of those who expelled him." "I am attacked by radical and violent militants from the Young Communists and the 'insubordinates' and I should apologize? Well, I will not apologize," Mr. Glucksmann replied on Thursday evening. "They do not intimidate us." "I want to tell these people who brutalize public debate that they do not intimidate us," he added, stating that his campaign "is designed as an antidote to this constant brutalization of public debate." The socialist candidate expresses his intention to conduct "a campaign on the subject of the election, Europe." "What Europe of defense against Putin? What Europe of ecological transition facing the prospect of climate collapse?" he listed. Asked about the reasons for this action, Mr. Glucksmann believes that "it is probably because our camp has momentum [in the polls]." The socialist candidate is placed in third position – leading the left and approaching the candidate from the presidential camp, Valérie Hayer – according to the fourth wave of the electoral survey conducted by Ipsos, in partnership with the Center for Political Research at Sciences Po (Cevipof), the Montaigne Institute, the Jean Jaurès Foundation, and Le Monde. "They are getting upset because we are ahead, that he [Jean-Luc Mélenchon] thought he owned the left," Mr. Glucksmann continued. "I want to show that there is still a humanist, pro-European left that stands firm, that is not intimidated by violence." The socialist candidate has received several endorsements from the left, including that of the national secretary of the Communist Party, Fabien Roussel, who stated that "nothing justifies Raphaël Glucksmann being excluded from a mobilization on May 1st in Saint-Etienne." "The social movement gains nothing from practicing diktat and exclusion," the deputy from the North had stated in a message on X. "The excessive conflict and anger fueled by some are bearing fruit," also criticized the (socialist) president of the Occitanie region, Carole Delga. "This is the society they propose to us, it is not ours." "I will always stand against any form of violence in politics. Politics can sometimes be a fight in the noble sense of the term, but it must always be done with respect for the integrity of individuals," reacted the Prime Minister, Gabriel Attal, during a visit to Beaugency (Loiret).

title: Arrest in Bavaria of two suspected Russian spies allegedly working for the Putin regime.
lead: According to the justice system, the two men were gathering information with the intention of carrying out attacks against German industrial and military sites, including the American base in Grafenwöhr, where Ukrainian soldiers are trained in operating combat tanks.
text_eng: Once again, Germany must acknowledge that it is a prime target of destabilization operations carried out by Russia in Europe. Six weeks after the Russian channel RT broadcasted a discussion between German Air Force officers discussing the possibility of delivering Taurus cruise missiles to Ukraine, the federal prosecutor's office in Karlsruhe, responsible for terrorism cases in Germany, announced on Thursday, April 18, the arrest of two men suspected of planning sabotage acts in favor of Russia from German territory. Arrested on Wednesday in Bayreuth, in the state of Bavaria, Dieter S. and Alexander J. both hold dual Russian and German citizenship. In its statement, the prosecutor's office emphasized the role of the first individual. Aged 39, the man allegedly fought from 2014 to 2016 alongside pro-Russian separatists in the self-proclaimed "People's Republic of Donetsk" in eastern Ukraine. Since October 2023, he had been in contact with a "person linked to Russian intelligence services" to "discuss potential sabotage acts aimed at undermining the military support provided by Germany to Ukraine in the context of the aggression led by Russia." During their exchanges, he reportedly expressed readiness to carry out terrorist attacks and arson on industrial and military sites in Germany. For this purpose, "he had notably gathered information on potential targets, especially American military installations, through photos or videos that he then transmitted to his contact," the prosecutor's office added. Regarding the second individual arrested on Wednesday, Alexander J., 37, it was mentioned that he "provided assistance" to the first individual, "at the latest in March of this year." Throughout Thursday, several German media outlets provided further details from the prosecutor's office. Among the potential targets for attacks, it was revealed that the American military base in Grafenwöhr was included, where Ukrainian soldiers are trained in operating M1 Abrams battle tanks, located about forty kilometers from Bayreuth, where the two men were apprehended. "Responding in an offensive and determined manner" An extensive article from the weekly magazine Der Spiegel, published on Thursday evening, provided additional insights. It was revealed that Dieter S. shared several messages from the far-right party Alternative for Germany (AfD) on his Facebook page, including a photoshopped image depicting the Vice Chancellor and Green Party Minister of Economy, Robert Habeck, accompanied by the message: "Disconnect him now!" As for Alexander J., who was a student in Russia before studying in Bayreuth, Der Spiegel revealed that he posted on social media in 2022 a photo of corpses in the Donbass, expressing outrage that "not a single bastard from the European Union expresses a word of compassion towards them." The seriousness of this matter in the eyes of the German government is evidenced by the reaction of four federal ministers to the arrest of the two men. "We will never accept espionage activities taking place in Germany," declared Chancellor Olaf Scholz on Thursday afternoon on the sidelines of the European Council in Brussels. "This is a particularly serious case of espionage in favor of Putin's criminal regime. But we will not be intimidated," stated Interior Minister Nancy Faeser. "We know that the Russian government is interested in our country. Faced with this threat, we must respond aggressively and decisively," echoed Justice Minister Marco Buschmann. "The suspicion that Putin is recruiting agents to carry out attacks from German territory is to be taken very seriously. We will not allow [the Russian leader] to import his methods of terror into Germany," declared German Foreign Minister Annalena Baerbock in a tweet specifying that "all this has been conveyed to the Russian ambassador," Sergey Nechaev, who was summoned on Thursday to the German Ministry of Foreign Affairs. "Truly alarming situation" Unsurprisingly, the Russian diplomat did not back down. "No evidence has been provided to us regarding possible links between the individuals arrested and organizations connected to Russia," assured the Russian Embassy in Berlin on Thursday, dismissing as "absurd and ridiculous" the espionage allegations made against its country by the German government. In Berlin, Russia's repeated interference causes serious concerns, whether it be cyberattacks, the spread of fake news, or overt support for the far-right AfD party. "It is clear that we are relatively ill-equipped in terms of counter-espionage and that, in many areas, we still have not fully grasped the threat posed by Russia," expressed concern Roderich Kiesewetter, a security and defense specialist within the Christian Democratic Union (CDU) parliamentary group in the Bundestag, on Thursday. For Konstantin von Notz, president of the parliamentary oversight body responsible for monitoring intelligence services within the Bundestag, the situation is "truly alarming." In a statement published on Thursday, this Green Party member made it known that he "urgently demand[ed] the federal government to investigate this type of large-scale espionage and influence operations," deeming it urgent for Germany to be "more resilient and better equipped" against such threats in the future.

title: European elections 2024: National Rally gets rid of its candidate in Mayotte, author of racist remarks
lead: In 2017, Saidali Boina Hamissi had referred to Comorians as "vermin" and "cockroaches." The government also accuses him of being "sexist," a conspiracy theorist, and an "admirer" of Putin. Faced with an outcry, the far-right party removed him from Jordan Bardella's list.
text_eng: It should have been his day of glory. On Sunday, April 21st, Marine Le Pen, on a visit to Mayotte, had Saidali Boina Hamissi cheered. The leader of the National Rally (RN) deputies just announced that the party's departmental delegate on the island is joining the list led by Jordan Bardella for the European elections on June 9th. An enthronement in front of around 300 members of several citizen collectives, gathered at the request of the Committee for the Defense of Mayotte's Interests (Codim), at the Ngwézi crossroads in the south of the island. One of the most radical places of anger against the government during the dam crisis in February. The leader of the far-right party hails Jordan Bardella's "historic" decision to "for the first time" place a Mahoran on his list for the European elections. Although Saidali Boina Hamissi is only in 41st position, with no chance of being elected, it is a way to "support Mayotte, which is in a desperate situation," Marine Le Pen emphasizes, before a round of applause. The candidacy of the party's departmental representative has been as unprecedented as it has been short-lived. Faced with the controversy sparked by posts on his Facebook page, Mr. Hamissi was removed from the RN list, Franceinfo reported on Wednesday, April 24th. The reason: "racist and conspiratorial" remarks he made, revealed by Libération on Monday. In September 2017, he referred to Comorian residents in a neighborhood of Mamoudzou as "vermin" and "cockroaches." "Extremely serious remarks" The day after his presence on the RN list was announced, two ministers were quick to criticize him. Mr. Hamissi "participated in conspiracy theories, made extremely serious and dangerous remarks about women's supposed submission to their spouses," accused government spokesperson Prisca Thevenot. "Sexist, racist, and... an admirer of the Russian president. In short, the ideal candidate for the National Rally," quipped Marie Guévenoux, Minister Delegate for Overseas France. Asked about the words "vermin" and "cockroaches" used by his Mahoran candidate, Sébastien Chenu, vice-president of the RN, said he was "not a fan of this kind of language," which "shocks" him. However, he added: "When people are pushed to the limit, it's no surprise that an expression is a bit strong." A reference to the situation in the 101st French department where the "Mayotte clean sweep" operation against gang delinquency, illegal immigration, and illegal housing began last week. Contacted by Le Monde on Wednesday afternoon, Mr. Hamissi said he was not "officially aware of [his] removal from the list," while regretting this decision. "I remind you that Marine Le Pen received an extraordinary welcome from the Mahoran population," he emphasized, almost in defiance. "But if the government and the press want to target me to eliminate me..." Mr. Hamissi believes "he has nothing to reproach himself for." "My words have been distorted and taken out of context of the moment. They date back to 2017," defends the departmental delegate of the RN. According to him, the words "vermin" and "cockroaches" were uttered after a motorist was attacked by a "group of twenty to thirty people who came out of nowhere, at night." "It's a post published by someone else that I shared. People in mainland France cannot understand the unbearable situation that the Mahorais are experiencing due to delinquency," he says, implying that this kind of speech does not shock many people in Mayotte. Regarding Vladimir Putin, Mr. Hamissi assures that he "does not support him," but "approves" of the Russian president's policy towards migrants. "Dirty tricks" Aged 63, an IT engineer, Mr. Hamissi became the departmental delegate of the RN in 2022. He had previously joined the Les Républicains party before, according to Mayotte La 1ère, being active in the CGT and the Socialist Party when he was younger and living in mainland France. In Mayotte, Mr. Hamissi has not held any elected office. As the RN candidate in the 2022 legislative elections, he achieved a score of 2.64% in the first round in the 2nd district. Locally, the most well-known face of the RN remains the former president of the departmental council (2011-2015) Daniel Zaïdani, who is very upset about this controversy. "When I see how the overseas minister tried to create a buzz to hide her lack of action on Mayotte... She is more interested in dirty tricks," protests this departmental councilor, also the spokesperson for the RN on the island. "Mr. Hamissi is a person of great quality who has our full support," adds Mr. Zaïdani. "He is fighting for Mayotte, while we are facing a government of inaction that only makes false promises, like the 'Mayotte' law, which will be postponed for two months." Mr. Hamissi is not expected to be replaced by Daniel Zaïdani on Jordan Bardella's list. The former president of the departmental council is awaiting the decision of the Mamoudzou judicial court after an initial judgment on March 19 in a case of misappropriation of public funds. The public prosecutor has requested a one-year suspended prison sentence and a five-year ban on holding any public office with provisional enforcement. Another thorn in the RN's side in Mayotte.

title: Agriculture holds a special place within the European Union.
lead: The Common Agricultural Policy is the EU's largest expenditure item. Fearing that the anger of the farming community would be exploited by extremists, the Twenty-Seven, at the beginning of the year, loosened their grip on environmental constraints.
text_eng: In Europe, agriculture is definitely not a subject like any other. The crisis that shook the farming world at the beginning of this year saw the Twenty-Seven take radical measures in record time to respond to it. The greening of the Common Agricultural Policy (CAP) was largely sacrificed, aid to Ukraine reduced, and the free trade agreement with the Southern Common Market (Mercosur) postponed. As for the Green Deal, designed to bring the European Union (EU) to carbon neutrality by 2050, it largely spares farmers, despite the urgency of fighting climate change. There is a disconnect between the weight of the agricultural world in the Old Continent and the importance of the CAP, which distributes over 50 billion euros in aid annually throughout Europe and represents a third of the community budget. But, as President Emmanuel Macron emphasizes, the CAP, which remains the EU's largest expenditure item today, must, above all, ensure the Twenty-Seven their "food sovereignty." Recent history has indeed shown that it can be dangerous to depend on third countries that are not always very friendly: Europeans will long remember their helplessness in the face of a China that refused to sell them masks during the Covid-19 crisis or the consequences of Moscow's decision to cut off their gas supply. Fear of a widespread movement That's not all. As the European elections approach, scheduled from June 6 to 9, the Twenty-Seven, led by France, feared that rural anger would fuel a movement similar to that of the "yellow vests" on a community scale and that it would fuel an already growing far-right vote, according to polls. "Vladimir Putin and his friends are doing everything in their power to poison public debate throughout Europe," accused Ursula von der Leyen, the President of the Commission, on February 23, denouncing the Kremlin's instrumentalization of certain farmers' protests. In the demonstrations, there were "people who do not hide their support for the Kremlin and pursue objectives other than the interests of farmers," said Czech Prime Minister Petr Fiala on the social network X on February 19. In this context, the Twenty-Seven did not hesitate when, in January, farmers protested across Europe, from France to Germany, Italy to the Netherlands, Spain, Portugal, Romania, Poland, and Hungary. At the heart of their grievances was the European Union (EU), which they accused, among other things, of designing a too bureaucratic CAP, concluding trade agreements to their disadvantage, adopting a costly Green Deal, or organizing unfair competition from Ukrainian products, which entered the domestic market duty-free since the start of the war. Environmental setbacks Be that as it may, Europeans have unraveled the environmental aspects of the CAP, whose new version had come into effect in 2023 and was supposed to apply until 2027. Among other things, on March 26, they decided to lift the obligations to leave 4% of the land fallow, to organize crop rotations, or to cover the soil during certain periods. As for small farms of less than 10 hectares (65% of operators, 11% of aid), they will simply be exempt from inspections and penalties. "I have the impression that some are talking about reducing bureaucracy to actually eliminate the environmental obligations of the CAP," regretted the German Minister of Agriculture, Cem Ozdemir, on March 26. On March 28, in front of his troops gathered in Dunkirk (Nord), the president of the National Federation of Agricultural Trade Unions, Arnaud Rousseau, welcomed "unexpected progress" achieved at the European level. Under pressure from several countries that wanted more for their farmers, notably Italy under Giorgia Meloni, the Commission also proposed on April 15 to extend, beyond June, the relaxation of the state aid regime for the agricultural sector. The "unconditional" support for Kyiv that Europeans want has also incurred the wrath of farmers. On April 8, they decided that from June 6, imports of Ukrainian poultry, eggs, sugar, corn, honey, oats, and groats will be taxed when they exceed the average level they had reached between mid-2021 and late 2023. France also wanted imports of duty-free soft wheat and barley to be capped. But it did not get its way. Finally, the environmental part of the Green Deal, which Europeans had planned to tackle after a first package of legislation aimed at reducing CO2 emissions, is now limited to a minimum. The Commission has abandoned several legislative initiatives on animal welfare or chemical substances. It has also buried the text that reduced pesticide use by 50% and extended the authorization of glyphosate by ten years. It even went so far as to propose lowering the protection status of wolves.

title: Ukraine: Kharkiv TV tower collapses following a Russian strike.
lead: Volodymyr Zelensky denounces "an obvious intimidation so that terror is visible throughout the city."
text_eng: An amateur video captures the collapse of the Kharkiv TV tower on Monday, April 22, after being hit by a Russian strike. "Employees were safe during the alarm. There are no human casualties," said Oleh Synehoubov, the head of the regional military administration, in a message on Telegram. Volodymyr Zelensky denounced on Monday evening "an obvious intimidation to make terror visible throughout the city" in a video posted on Telegram. "Efforts are underway to restore the signal," he assured.

title: The murky ties between the president of the IOC and Vladimir Putin highlighted in an investigative book
lead: If the relations between Moscow and the International Olympic Committee have deteriorated due to the tightening of sanctions against Russia, Thomas Bach has long been considered an ally by the Kremlin, reveal two German journalists in the book "Putin's Oligarch".
text_eng: The president of the International Olympic Committee, the German Thomas Bach, at the opening of an executive committee meeting of the IOC at the headquarters of the organization in Lausanne, Switzerland, on December 8, 2015. LAURENT GILLIERON / AP The charge is precise, documented, and relentless. "Like the Russian oligarchs who have managed to survive until now, ours has followed the rule to the letter: money and power in exchange for unconditional loyalty and political fidelity to Vladimir Putin," explain the German journalists Johannes Aumüller and Thomas Kistner. Their target? Thomas Bach, 70 years old, at the helm of the International Olympic Committee (IOC) since 2013, portrayed as "the oligarch" of the Russian head of state, in an investigative book (Putins Olygarch, dtv, 320 pages, 17.99 euros, not translated), published just under three months before the opening ceremony of the Paris Games (July 26-August 11). The work of the two reporters from the Süddeutsche Zeitung, the major left-leaning daily newspaper in Munich, is the result of several years of research and interviews. It shows that Thomas Bach has long been in Moscow's corner, despite the repeated denials of the main party involved and the recent crisis between the Kremlin and the Olympic movement, which suggests that the two sides are now irreconcilable; to the point that Russia is increasingly organizing parallel events, such as the BRICS Games in June and the Friendship Games in September. Johannes Aumüller and Thomas Kistner presented their book on Monday, April 29, in the Bavarian capital, emphasizing that it could be read as a "handbook of corruption at all levels of global sports governance," which it traces with as much meticulousness as relish in previous episodes, most of which are known but with fewer details. This includes the saga of the German sportswear manufacturer Adidas, presented as the original matrix of corruption. This "true CIA of sneakers," which used all intelligence techniques to establish its power and networks, especially in the communist bloc USSR-GDR during the last two decades of the Cold War. A young lawyer - an Olympic gold medalist in team fencing in Montreal in 1976 - is part of the setup, in the circle of Horst Dassler, the son of Adolf Dassler, the brand's founder: his name is Thomas Bach. Alfons Hörmann, a key witness The centerpiece of the book is the sworn testimony of Alfons Hörmann, former president of the German Olympic Committee, who succeeded Mr. Bach in 2013. He shared with the authors a story that had remained secret until now, establishing the long-standing closeness between the IOC president and the Russian autocrat, as well as the foundations of the pact sealed between them. We are in February 2011. At that time, Mr. Hörmann was the head of the German Ski Federation. Important international events are taking place in the Bavarian resort town of Garmisch-Partenkirchen. It is essential that the cream of German sport be present and show unity, as Munich and Garmisch are candidates to host future Summer and Winter Games. Shortly after arriving in the Alps, Mr. Bach "apologized for the next day and, citing absolute confidentiality, told me that he would fly to Moscow at the invitation of Vladimir Putin," recalls Mr. Hörmann. He had explicitly stated that he would be transported by plane to and from Moscow on a private aircraft. Less than twenty-four hours later, Thomas Bach is back in Garmisch. To Alfons Hörmann, he reports that the conversation with the then Russian government leader turned out to be "pleasant and fruitful." "Putin told me that he agreed with my candidacy for the presidency of the IOC and that he would actively support it," confides the former fencing champion. "(...) Now, I am sure that I can become it." This will be done, two years later. On September 10, 2013, as the applause of the International Olympic Committee's bigwigs has not yet subsided at a congress in Buenos Aires, Thomas Bach's cell phone vibrates in his pocket. On the line, a man is determined to be the first to congratulate him: the master of the Kremlin. "A targeted disinformation campaign" Does this connection explain the extraordinary passivity of the IOC around the revelations, starting at the end of 2014, of an institutionalized doping system in Russia? This is what Johannes Aumüller and Thomas Kistner suggest in their investigation. "The greatest sports scandal in history was initially taken lightly by the institution," they claim, "then there were multiple pressures on the World Anti-Doping Agency. Each time, it was about allowing Moscow to save face, and until very recently." The hesitations lasted six years, from half-measures to half-sanctions. At one point, playing the national anthem was banned, then Russians were not allowed to display their flag or nationality during events, competing under the baroque designation of "Russian Olympic athlete," and then under the banner of the Russian Olympic Committee at the Tokyo Summer Games. On February 24, 2022, Kremlin troops invaded Ukraine. Its athletes were temporarily banned from the global sports scene, before a question stirred the IOC headquarters in Lausanne, Switzerland, a few months later: how will Russian athletes be able to participate in the Paris Games? To placate Putin, at all costs. This decade of compromise is nevertheless coming to an end in the fall of 2023, when it becomes clear to Moscow that the presence of Thomas Bach at the head of the organization is no longer sufficient to defend its interests, and that the President of the IOC will eventually give in to international pressure by toughening sanctions. On October 12, 2023, the Russian Olympic Committee is suspended for having placed four organizations from occupied Ukrainian regions under its authority. It is now open war with the IOC leadership, exchanging accusations. The International Olympic Committee did not respond to the questions from the authors of "Putin's Oligarch." Instead, it stated: "Your new questions suggest that they are based on misleading information, which could be part of a targeted disinformation campaign that has been ongoing for several months against the IOC and its president." "Putin's Oligarch," dtv, 320 pages, 17.99 euros, untranslated. DTV VERLAG Serge Enderlin (Munich, special correspondent). The space for contributions is reserved for subscribers. Subscribe to access this exchange space and contribute to the discussion. Subscribe Contribute Reuse this content.

title: Against Israel, Iran employed in the air the same methods as Russia in Ukraine, but with a different outcome.
lead: The hundreds of drones and missiles launched by Tehran were met with the effectiveness of the air defense systems of the Israeli state and the assistance provided by its allies.
text_eng: "The sound of Shahed drones, tools of terror, is the same in the skies of the Middle East and Europe." In a message posted on April 14 on the social network X, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky condemned the Iranian attack on Israel. But he mainly drew a parallel between what his country has been experiencing for the past two years and what the State of Israel has just endured, facing the largest aerial attack in its history on the night of April 13-14, with around 330 drones and cruise or ballistic missiles launched against its territory. In fact, Tehran drew heavily from the methods used by Moscow in Ukraine to plan its attack on Israel. Like their ally, the Iranians preceded their wave of missiles with a massive drone deployment to saturate enemy air defense systems. "The drones were launched well before the ballistic missile firing, most likely in the hope that they would arrive in Israel's air defense zone at roughly the same time as the cruise missiles and drones," say researchers Brian Carter and Frederick W. Kagan in a note published on April 14 by the American Enterprise Institute think tank, stating that "the Russians have repeatedly used this approach against Ukraine." However, the result achieved by the Iranians was significantly inferior to that of the Russians. According to the Israeli military, 99% of the projectiles fired by Tehran were intercepted: only five to seven ballistic missiles out of the 110 to 120 launched reportedly hit Israel, and all the drones and cruise missiles were destroyed before reaching their target. In comparison, the Ukrainians currently intercept only half of the ballistic missiles sent against them by Moscow and three-quarters of the drones and cruise missiles, which are easier to hit because they are slower. Foreign aircraft This difference is explained by the number and quality of Israeli anti-aircraft systems, which are incomparable to what Ukraine has. Faced with rocket fire from Palestinian Hamas or Lebanese Hezbollah since the early 2000s, Israel has developed a comprehensive multi-layered air defense system, including the Iron Dome for short-range defense and Arrow systems for long-range defense. "The Israelis have the best combination of anti-aircraft systems in the world," confirms a military source. However, Israeli performance also owes much to the assistance provided by its allies in the region. A significant portion of Iranian projectiles were reportedly intercepted by air-to-air missiles fired from fighter jets flying over Jordan or Iraq. Analysts estimate that several dozen American, British, and Jordanian aircraft were involved in the operation. According to the Pentagon, the United States has intercepted more than 80 drones and at least six Iranian ballistic missiles. France has also contributed, although it does not wish to provide details. "We launched our aircraft and intercepted what we needed to intercept," Emmanuel Macron acknowledged on Monday on BFM-TV. According to our information, the six Rafale fighter jets currently deployed at the projected airbase H5 in Jordan, as part of the "Chammal" operation against the Islamic State organization, were involved. The French multimission frigate in the Red Sea, tasked with protecting commercial ships against Houthi drones or missiles, was not used in contrast. Ground-to-air missile batteries Besides the quantity and quality of the equipment, the geographical configuration also played a role in Israel's successful outcomes. Iran is over 1,000 kilometers away from Israel, while Russia shares a border with Ukraine. The Israelis and their allies had time to detect the launch of Iranian projectiles and determine their trajectory, making interception easier. Additionally, the Iranian attack had been anticipated. As evidence, several American refueling aircraft were in the area at the time of the launches, allowing the fighters to remain airborne for the necessary duration. For the Ukrainians, the Iranian failure nevertheless demonstrates that it is possible to counter massive drone and missile attacks, such as those that have destroyed 80% of the country's power grid in recent weeks. However, this requires having an adequate number of ground-to-air missile batteries and modern combat aircraft equipped with high-performance air-to-air missiles. This is precisely what Kiev has been requesting from its Western allies for months.

title: Foreign policy, a source of frustration for the European Parliament
lead: The "resolutions" in this matter, adopted one after the other by Members of the European Parliament, have no binding value and mainly serve to exist in a field where the institution has few prerogatives.
text_eng: LETTER FROM BRUSSELS If one attends as a neophyte the plenary sessions of the European Parliament, one might believe that Members of the European Parliament play an important role in foreign policy. Within the Strasbourg hemicycle, they debate and vote, every month, on texts covering diverse topics such as human rights in China, press freedom in Morocco, or corruption in Lebanon. However, this legislative hyperactivity has limited impact, often boiling down to the echo it generates (or not) in public debate. In fact, the "resolutions" adopted by the elected officials one after the other have no binding value. They mainly serve to exist in a field where the European Parliament has few prerogatives. The Member States of the European Union (EU), very attached to their sovereignty, have greatly circumscribed, in the treaties, the role of the community institutions, and even more so of Strasbourg, on the international stage. The institution can be informed, or even consulted, it can hear from officials or make recommendations, but it is rarely involved in decisions related to foreign affairs or defense. It does not even have a ceremonial role in the external representation of the EU. It is unfortunate because "there is [in the European Parliament] a significantly higher proportion of elected officials interested in international affairs than in national parliaments," as highlighted by Isabelle Marchais in a report on the "European Parliament eager to strengthen its role on the international stage," published by the Jacques Delors Institute in February. Before continuing: "faced with the gap between their desire to act and the strict institutional limitations," Members of the European Parliament who are fans of foreign policy can develop a "sense of frustration." Roberta Metsola very active on the international stage Nevertheless, they are active, as evidenced by their numerous trips around the world, the forty-five delegations between the EU and third countries that structure their relations outside the Old Continent, or these informal friendship groups that form and dissolve over the course of legislatures. Moreover, the parliamentary committee on foreign affairs, which meets in Brussels, is one of the most sought after by Members of the European Parliament – a place of prestige, therefore, more than efficiency – and former heads of state or government and ministers of all kinds are overrepresented there. The President of the institution, Roberta Metsola, is very active on the international stage. She was seen in Kyiv a few days after the invasion of Ukraine by Russia on February 24, 2022, outpacing her counterparts from the Commission (Ursula von der Leyen) and the European Council (Charles Michel), even if it annoys them. Or in Israel after the massacres of October 7, 2023, perpetrated by Hamas. "Roberta Metsola has managed to plant a kind of geopolitical flag on the institution," notes Isabelle Marchais. Making the most of the limited powers at its disposal, the Strasbourg assembly has nevertheless succeeded, over the years, in carving out a small place on the international stage. Resolutions, while not having the force of law, contribute to this parliamentary diplomacy that allows Members of the European Parliament to exert pressure on the Member States or the Commission, to bring a debate to the public stage. With varying degrees of success! Undoubtedly, the fight of the (Place publique) elected official Raphaël Glucksmann on the tragedy of the Uyghurs, which led the European Parliament to adopt a resolution in December 2020 calling for sanctions against the Chinese political leaders responsible for their repression, has advanced this cause. The Commission in Brussels has in any case taken it up, and the Twenty-Seven have just adopted legislation against the use of forced labor by companies. Levers of influence The European Parliament also has levers of influence that do not fall strictly under foreign policy but contribute to it. As a co-legislator, it has a say on subjects such as trade, ecological transition, industry (including defense), health, asylum and immigration policy, and energy, which have – or may have – an external dimension, and therefore influence the EU's relations with the rest of the world. The institution, which must pronounce on the EU budget, also intervenes in the financing of the EU's external action and can control how European funds are spent. Under its pressure, in August 2023, the Commission actually canceled subsidies intended for an Islamist Turkish association. Ultimately, the influence of the European Parliament on the international stage is mainly measured by how third countries perceive it. And from that point of view, it is important. The Ukrainian President, Volodymyr Zelensky, understood this, as he found it useful, in February 2023, to visit the European Parliament, gathered in an extraordinary plenary session in Brussels, where he was applauded after an emotionally charged sequence. Sanctioned MEPs Authoritarian regimes, in particular, pay close attention to what is said about them in the hemicycle. Their diplomats and consultants are well known to the Members, to whom they plead their case, making the Strasbourg institution a place of influence that can, as seen with the Qatargate, become a prime target for foreign interference. Morocco, among others, is very present in the corridors of the European Parliament, where it defends its positions on the Western Sahara issue. In this context, some foreign powers, when they are not satisfied with the treatment they receive, do not hesitate to target the European Parliament. Thus, in September 2023, Tunisia banned five Members of the European Parliament from entering the country, following a debate in plenary during which the "democratic regression of Tunisia" and the "controversial" agreement it had reached with the Commission on migration were denounced. In March 2021, after the EU had taken retaliatory measures against Chinese officials in the Uyghur dossier, Beijing, among other actions, sanctioned five elected officials, including Raphaël Glucksmann. In a way, one could see it as the price of fame.

title: Gaz: "Equinor has dethroned Gazprom and has established itself as the strategic supplier of the Old Continent."
lead: Less threatened by geopolitical risks, the European Union remains dependent on the significant Norwegian supply source. But the role of the small Scandinavian kingdom may not last. The production of the United States and Qatar, coming onto the market in the next two years, would allow Europeans to negotiate better prices, points out Jean-Michel Bezat, an economic journalist at "Le Monde".
text_eng: A Norwegian giant has quietly replaced a Russian colossus in the European gas market. Two years after the start of the war in Ukraine, Equinor has dethroned Gazprom and has established itself as the strategic supplier to the Old Continent. Its energy security is thus less threatened by geopolitical uncertainties. However, it remains dependent on the Stavanger company and other local producers for 30% of its needs, slightly less than the 35% from the Russian state-owned group. This highlights their importance, especially for Germany, which thanked Oslo for stepping in to replace Gazprom in just a few months. The Norwegian Minister of Energy, Terje Aasland, repeats that his country will be "a stable and long-term supplier" for decades to come. There is no reason to doubt this. And there is little risk that Norway will invade its Swedish neighbor... Yet, a leak at Equinor's liquefied natural gas (LNG) production plant in Hammerfest on April 23 led to a surge in spot prices when the plant was shut down. This phenomenon was already observed a year earlier during long maintenance operations at Europe's largest LNG plant located above the Arctic Circle. When a dominant player faces difficulties, markets tend to overreact. The country's gas revenue reached 120 billion euros in 2022 and 55 billion in 2023, feeding the immensely wealthy sovereign fund intended for future generations, already filled with petrodollars. This has led to accusations of "profiting from the war" and taking liberties with its environmental commitments by boosting fossil energy production. Putin's adventurism Norway benefited from an emergency situation, its proximity to the EU-27, and its previous position as the EU's second-largest supplier. The visits of German leaders and the Commission, whether in Oslo, Stavanger, or at the giant Troll field, attest to the crucial role of the small Scandinavian kingdom. This situation may not last, even though this source of supply remains important. The production of new liquefied gas trains, notably in the United States and Qatar, will enter the market within two years, providing Europeans with greater flexibility to negotiate better prices. In the meantime, Equinor is doing everything to continue playing a key role as a reliable supplier. Precisely the role Gazprom played from the 1970s until the Russo-Ukrainian conflict. If Russia were not the aggressor in Ukraine, it could benefit from the status of a "war victim" given the suffering caused by Vladimir Putin's adventurism. Gazprom announced in early May a loss of 6.4 billion euros in 2023, the first in twenty-five years. The war deprived it of almost the entire European market outside of LNG, its main export outlet, without China being able to compensate for this loss. She also does not give any gifts to Moscow, and the "eternal friendship" displayed by the two countries is purely superficial: the Middle Kingdom has no friends, it only has interests.

title: In France, the growing concern of defenders of individual freedoms
lead: Dissolutions, bans on demonstrations, police summonses have multiplied since the establishment of the state of emergency in 2015, with accelerations in 2017 and following the terrorist attack by Hamas on October 7, 2023. Some see these as violations of the rule of law.
text_eng: More than thirty political groups or associations have been dissolved since 2017; multiple bans on demonstrations against pension reform or in support of Palestine; bans on political meetings such as the one by Jean-Luc Mélenchon in mid-April; police summons of opposition figures - for example, Mathilde Panot, president of the La France insoumise group in the National Assembly - for "apology of terrorism"; the conviction of a CGT union leader to a one-year suspended prison sentence for the same offense... Is France experiencing an authoritarian shift, as denounced by part of the left and the far left? Since October 7, 2023, the day of the Hamas terrorist attack in Israel followed by the Israeli military response, mobilization around the defense of Gaza residents has been intensifying: calls for demonstrations are multiplying, as are protests in universities. Daily political positions, often controversial, are being taken. Most of the time, they come from the left and the far left, who denounce a risk of "genocide" by Israel. The Minister of Justice, Eric Dupond-Moretti, issued a circular as early as October 10, 2023, calling for "a firm and rapid penal response" to antisemitism and "apology of terrorism," which, according to the text, is constituted by "statements praising the attacks [by Hamas], presenting them as a legitimate resistance to Israel." "First shift" after 2015 But the repressive turn did not begin on October 7. The first cuts in the exercise of certain freedoms are a bit older, note those who regret them. "A first shift occurred after 2015 and the state of emergency following Islamist attacks, then with the integration of certain measures into common law," says Arié Alimi, lawyer and member of the League of Human Rights (LDH). Furthermore, since that date, many social movements have taken place, sometimes harsh, resulting in a very forceful police response. For example, opposition to the labor law (2016), the "yellow vests" movement (2018-2019); the movement against the "global security" law (2021), and then against the pension reform (2023)... "During this period, we have seen an increase in police violence, a brutalization of law enforcement," continues the lawyer. "On the right to demonstrate, there has been a paradigm shift: before, freedom was the principle and prohibitions were extremely rare." According to Stéphanie Hennette-Vauchez, professor of public law at Paris-X and author of Democracy in a State of Emergency. When the exception becomes permanent (Seuil, 2022), the current phenomenon is complex and multifaceted. "The first aspect involves clear political choices: Emmanuel Macron's presidency is marked by a peak in administrative dissolutions, by bans on demonstrations." "The very broad use of the offense of apologizing for terrorism should also make us question," says the academic. She adds a second element to her reflection: "The challenge by political leaders of the established standards of the rule of law, that is, the principle that public action is constrained by the law." Stéphanie Hennette-Vauchez cites Gérald Darmanin, who said he "assumes" not to respect a decision of the European Court of Human Rights regarding the expulsion of an Uzbek suspected of being an Islamist. Another example: the way the government behaved with the Constitutional Council, at the end of December 2023, during the adoption of the "immigration" law. Gérald Darmanin, Elisabeth Borne, and Emmanuel Macron all acknowledged that the text contained provisions contrary to the Constitution. "All of this put together erodes the edifice of the rule of law," worries Mrs. Hennette-Vauchez. "We are not in Russia." Yves Sintomer, a political scientist and professor at Paris-VIII, tempers: "We must not overestimate the seriousness of the situation. We are neither in Putin's Russia nor in Pinochet's Chile. We must not deny certain worrying facts, such as the repressive turn, but also the bans on demonstrations and pressures on academic freedoms." On this last point, he describes a "climate of self-censorship and a sense of threat never experienced before." The other question that arises is whether these restrictions affect all political families. Marion Ogier, a lawyer and member of the LDH and the Syndicat des avocats de France (SAF, left), is convinced: certain struggles are particularly targeted. "As soon as it concerns certain themes like Palestine, the defense of Muslims, the fight against Islamophobia or police violence, the risk of prohibition is very high. Each time, it is assumed that this will lead to disturbances or anti-Semitic messages," she believes. However, in terms of dissolutions, organizations from the far right, far left, or advocating for political Islam have been targeted in recent years, even though these three currents are of different natures and do not represent the same danger, especially for the safety of individuals. The same goes for bans on meetings and demonstrations. Precisely, the Police Prefecture "is considering" banning the annual parade of the radical far right, in tribute to the nationalist militant Sébastien Deyzieu, who died in 1994, scheduled for May 11 in Paris. "This demonstration is likely to pose several public order difficulties," notes the Prefecture.

title: Tibetans and Uighurs in France are mobilizing against the visit of Chinese President Xi Jinping.
lead: More than a thousand protesters, mainly Tibetans and Uighurs, gathered in Paris on Sunday to denounce the visit of Chinese President Xi Jinping.
text_eng: Chinese President Xi Jinping landed at Orly airport at 2 p.m. on Sunday, May 5, for a two-day state visit to France, welcomed by Prime Minister Gabriel Attal. As during official trips to China, a welcoming committee of hundreds of Chinese in France was present at the airport exit, waving Chinese and French flags and singing in Mandarin: "we warmly welcome you." But elsewhere in Paris, over a thousand Tibetans, Uighurs, and Chinese critics of the regime protested against the visit of the Chinese leader, who is also scheduled to visit Serbia and Hungary. On Saturday, two Tibetan activists were arrested after hanging a large banner between two lampposts in front of the Arc de Triomphe, proclaiming: "Europe, say no to Xi's genocide." They were released after about ten hours in custody. On Sunday afternoon, despite the rain, over a thousand people gathered at Place de la République, including a large majority of Tibetans from France and even from Europe: three buses had been chartered from Belgium and one from the Netherlands for the occasion. Due to a lack of authorization from the prefecture, the march that was supposed to reach Bastille became a static gathering. "A genocide is still ongoing." On the microphone, a young activist denounced the policy of forced assimilation of Tibetan children, who are sent to boarding schools far from their families, the fate of the Panchen Lama, the second highest-ranking figure in Tibetan Buddhism who disappeared in 1995 at the age of 6, the construction of dams leading to population displacement, and the closure of Tibet to foreign visitors - including journalists - and to Tibetans in exile. "Mr. President Emmanuel Macron, we strongly urge you to have the political courage to discuss Tibet with your Chinese counterpart, Mr. Xi Jinping," she concluded in this open letter to the French president. At the same time, a few dozen Uighurs - fewer in number in France than Tibetans - and their supporters gathered in front of the Madeleine church for a politically engaged play. The gathering was marked by tensions when counter-protesters waved Chinese flags. "In this context where a genocide is still ongoing, the French president's reception of the oppressor of the Uighur people is incomprehensible to us. It is an encouragement for China to continue its crimes to the end," denounced sociologist Dilnur Reyhan, founder of the Uighur Institute of Europe, during a press conference in Paris on Friday, May 3. "For the Uighur people and especially for the French Uighurs, our president Emmanuel Macron is giving us a slap in the face," she criticized. On the Place de la République, despite slogans in support of East Turkestan (Xinjiang), Hong Kong, Inner Mongolia, and Taiwan, the Tibetan community represented the overwhelming majority of the protesters present. "There are many Tibetans in France and they know how to mobilize. However, I have never seen so few Chinese people at this kind of event. They are really afraid," noted sinologist Jean-Philippe Beja, who came to witness the gathering. "I am very careful." Among the few Chinese citizens present, a young graduate, 25 years old, with short hair, looked at the crowd, his face hidden behind a surgical mask: "I came to show support, but I am very careful because we know that there may be Chinese agents in the crowd," he testifies. He is on the phone with his parents in China, showing them the demonstration on video. His father worries about seeing him talking to a journalist. Further away, a few young Chinese people, their faces wrapped in scarves, hold a banner denouncing the Chinese dictatorship. One of them wears a mask with the likeness of Vladimir Putin. To conceal his identity - he wants to be able to visit his family in China - but also to send a message: "Xi Jinping's visit is not only problematic because of the repression taking place on the other side of the world: his support for the Russian invasion in Ukraine represents a security threat to Europeans!" he warns. After his stay in Europe, Xi Jinping is expected to receive a visit from Vladimir Putin in Beijing in May. While some Tibetans wear masks in the colors of their flags, most are there with their faces uncovered. They know they cannot go back home for fear of being sent to prison. Many have fled repression in Tibet, embarking on a risky journey through the Himalayas to reach India or Nepal. "It took my family 15 days," says Tsering, 22, huddled under an umbrella with a friend. She left Tibet in 2012. "It's very complicated because most Tibetans cannot obtain a passport. We have to go through forests, mountains, at night," she describes. After ten years in India, she moved to France four years ago for her studies. "We are angry and frustrated: we can hardly communicate with our families in China, and Xi Jinping is welcomed in Europe as if he were a benefactor, but China kills! They lie when they say everything is fine in Tibet," she denounces. A man, 30 years old, with long black hair, holds his 6-year-old son's hand, born in France. He left Tibet about ten years ago. "I wanted to study Tibetan, but there it doesn't exist. At best, they only teach rudiments to children. And you had to ask for permission for everything: "Pilgrimages are prevented under various pretexts, and going to the monastery to pray is almost forbidden." The young father, who prefers to remain anonymous, recounts that his brother spent five years in prison for illegally selling livestock, and came out with significant mental health issues. "I dare not imagine what tortures he endured. Today, he can no longer do anything... for this life, it's over for him." He hopes at least that Tibet will not be forgotten.

title: In Ukraine, the feeling of being relegated to the background and less supported than Israel.
lead: The involvement of the United States, the United Kingdom, and France in protecting Israel against the Iranian attack is fueling bitterness among Ukrainians as their country faces a campaign of systematic Russian bombardments on its energy infrastructure.
text_eng: The prompt intervention of Israel's allies to intercept the hundreds of drones and missiles launched by Iran against its territory on the night of Saturday, April 13 to Sunday, April 14, has triggered a wave of bitterness in Ukraine, a country that has been daily struck by the Russian army for over two years. On Monday, April 15, only the British Foreign Secretary, David Cameron, attempted to justify the impossibility of a similar intervention to protect Ukrainian territory from Kremlin bombings, stating on the radio Leading Britain's Conversation, "If we want to avoid escalation and a broader European war, I think the thing to avoid is NATO troops directly engaging Russian troops. That would pose a risk of escalation." "Obviously, Ukrainians see a difference in approach when the United States, Great Britain, and France literally cover the sky over Israel," acknowledges Bohdan Yaremenko, former head of the foreign affairs committee of the Verkhovna Rada, the Ukrainian Parliament. "It is very difficult to accept being considered a second-class partner," he adds. On social media and among his circle, the founder of the charitable organization dedicated to the military Come Back Alive, Vitaliy Deynega, makes this observation: "A Ukrainian life is worth less than an Israeli life." "Of course, we can blame our partners and Western countries. But what will that change?" The Ukrainian president, also expressing his bitterness, stated in his address to the nation on Monday evening, "By defending Israel, the free world has shown that such unity is not only possible, but also 100% effective," said Volodymyr Zelensky. "The same is possible to protect Ukraine." Allies' slowness This feeling of being relegated to the background is amplified by the fact that the country has been facing a systematic Russian bombing campaign on its energy infrastructure for several weeks, with authorities warning of the risk of a new Russian offensive in the coming months. As several thermal and hydroelectric power plants have already been destroyed or damaged, the Ukrainian president continues to call on Western allies to provide new defense systems and ammunition. On Saturday, the German defense minister was the first, and so far the only one, to announce the delivery of a new American Patriot anti-aircraft defense system. The deputy director of the think tank New Europe Center, Sergiy Solodkyy, also expresses concern about the slowness of Kiev's allies in delivering new ammunition, especially as the country is in a particularly difficult situation on the front line. Failing to be able to protect the Ukrainian sky, "Western countries could certainly provide more weapons," says the expert in international relations. Because Ukraine lacks not only planes or missile shields, but also ordinary weapons, such as artillery shells. According to Sergiy Solodkyy, this lack of willingness and effectiveness from Western partners has led to creating "a feeling of being abandoned or forgotten" among Ukrainians: "It's a total disaster, a catastrophe, and I believe that is the main emotion currently." For Bohdan Yaremenko, a deputy of the ruling party Servant of the People, "Ukraine is on the brink of the abyss." For several months, a military aid package of $60 billion (56 billion euros) from the United States has been blocked in Congress due to political disputes between the Biden administration and its Republican opponents. Without these funds, "Ukraine will lose the war," recently warned Volodymyr Zelensky. On Monday, the Republican leader of the House of Representatives, Mike Johnson, under pressure after the Iranian attack, announced that a vote will take place in the coming days on separate projects for aid to Ukraine and Israel. "A matter of survival" Delays in support from Western allies of Kiev have immediate consequences on the front lines as the administration faces a shortage of ammunition and Ukrainian authorities struggle to recruit new soldiers. "The main issue currently facing Ukraine is whether the U.S. Congress will approve the aid package," explains Deputy Bohdan Yaremenko, referring to it as "a matter of survival." The former diplomat also links the approval of this aid to the motivation of Ukrainians to enlist, as the Parliament approved, on Thursday, April 11, a highly controversial bill on new mobilization rules. "We must be able to provide guarantees that joining the army does not mean a one-way ticket," explains the deputy. Lately, as Kiev forces build fortifications along the front line to repel enemy assaults, the Russian army has intensified its bombardments with guided aerial bombs dropped from planes. Vitaliy Deynega, from Come Back Alive, ensures that the bulk of Ukrainian resources are currently focused on drones purchased through charity funds. "This is one of the main reasons why we are not defeated," says the former deputy defense minister. "Over the past six months, we have shown that we can make Russia pay a heavy price for what it is doing, even without Western help. But the price to pay is very high." On Saturday, the commander-in-chief of the armed forces warned that his understaffed army was struggling to stop the intensifying Russian offensive on multiple fronts. "The situation on the eastern front has significantly deteriorated in recent days," Oleksandr Syrsky wrote on the Telegram app. Moscow's efforts are now focused around the small mining town of Chasiv Yar, located 15 kilometers west of Bakhmut. On the European side, the most appreciated initiative remains that of the Czech Republic, which involves making joint purchases of hundreds of thousands of artillery shells from outside the European Union (EU). The country has been mobilizing for months to persuade other states to finance a fund aimed at buying ammunition that the EU is unable to produce in sufficient quantities. On Monday, in an article in the British newspaper Financial Times, Czech Prime Minister Petr Fiala indicated that the country was striving to find 300,000 munitions, with 180,000 set to be "delivered to the Ukrainian front in the coming months."

title: "The European elections must help unmask the far right."
lead: Researchers Hector de Rivoire and Arthur de Liedekerke remind, in an opinion piece in "Le Monde", that behind a superficial ideological transformation, far-right parties, in a favorable position for the European elections in June, remain profoundly dangerous, notably due to their sympathies towards Russia.
text_eng:
As the European elections in June approach, polling trends indicate that far-right parties are gaining momentum. In an era of conflicts and strengthening of military alliances, it is imperative to understand the geopolitical project and vision of Europe that these political parties truly represent. These elections should help unmask the extreme right. While far-right parties are not homogenous across the continent, they are united by a set of values. Among these are the defense of a very conservative vision of society, a common ambiguity regarding the illiberal regime of Russian President Vladimir Putin, the exaltation of nationalism, and a latent Euroscepticism. The electoral ambitions of these parties have led to an opportunistic evolution of their programs, and many of them now acknowledge the need to address strategic issues on a continental scale, such as managing immigration and supporting pro-natal policies. The calls for exiting the European Union (EU) and abandoning the common currency have gradually faded from their programs. While in 2017 the National Rally (RN) in France advocated for a "Frexit," it is no longer part of their agenda. Geert Wilders' Party for Freedom, which won the last elections in the Netherlands, has also removed EU exit from its political platform. In Italy, in 2014, Giorgia Meloni, newly elected as the leader of the post-fascist party Brothers of Italy, declared: "We say to Germany, which turns a deaf ear: Italy must leave the euro." However, she abandoned this project when she became the Italian prime minister in 2022. Ms. Meloni now works hand in hand with Ursula von der Leyen, the President of the European Commission, to address the migration crisis. Cosmetic changes The EU institutions have demonstrated their effectiveness in response to the eurozone crisis, and the recovery plan following the Covid-19 pandemic has shown their ability to finance innovation and the future of the continent. Even today, the reception of Ukrainian refugees, economic support, and consensus at the European Council on military aid to Kyiv demonstrate the development of a common strategic culture. These successes have led to a better perception of Europe by its citizens; 72% of them now value their EU membership. Far-right parties in Europe have taken note of public opinion, and while significant differences remain, one might think that these parties have transformed their stance on European issues. However, it is crucial for those who truly believe in the European project not to be deceived by these cosmetic changes. These parties aim to overturn the table from within by changing the balance of power in Brussels. Firstly, it is well known that the Members of the European Parliament from far-right parties are among the most absentees in the Brussels Hemicycle, a habit that betrays their true intentions for Europe. Old habits die hard: while the Sweden Democrats (SD) had abandoned the idea of a "Swexit" in 2019, it resurfaced in February in a column by Jimmie Akesson, the leader of the SD. Michel Barnier, former European Commissioner and chief Brexit negotiator, was not mistaken when he, on February 20, identified these parties as wanting to transform the EU into "a mere international trade union." This is the reality of the à la carte Europe concept advocated by the National Rally's lead candidate in the June 9 election, Jordan Bardella. Converging illiberal interests. 

Lastly, the conflict instigated by Russia at our borders should trigger a wake-up call. The hesitations of the American Congress, which is reluctant to release funds for Kyiv, highlight more than ever the need for Europeans to unite in supporting our Ukrainian neighbors, both for the credibility of our strategic posture and for the guarantee of the inviolability of the Baltic and Romanian borders. Just two years ago, the National Rally made a military alliance with Russia a cornerstone of its defense program. Today, the Freedom Party of Austria (Freiheitliche Partei Österreichs) and the Alternative for Germany openly display their pro-Kremlin sympathies, and while these parties' priorities are not dictated by Russia, their illiberal interests converge. Let us not be mistaken about the stakes in the upcoming elections. Despite an apparent programmatic overhaul that has won over some opinions, these political parties pose a real threat to the founding principles of the EU and, for the most part, remain carriers of a dangerous ideological affinity with the Kremlin.

title: In Israel, Netanyahu fears being placed under international arrest warrant by the ICC.
lead: The Israeli press expects the International Criminal Court in The Hague to initiate proceedings against the Prime Minister himself, as well as against the Defense Minister, Yoav Gallant, and the Chief of Staff, Herzi Halevi.
text_eng: Benjamin Netanyahu, the mastermind behind the war in Gaza, is facing an arrest warrant from the International Criminal Court (ICC)? This scenario has never caused as much concern in Israel. In a message posted on the social network X on Friday, April 26, the Israeli Prime Minister assured that "Israel will never accept any attempt by the ICC to undermine its inherent right to self-defense." For about ten days, he has been taking seriously the possibility of facing charges along with other senior Israeli officials by the ICC. "Even if the ICC will not affect Israel's actions," he further writes, "it will set a dangerous precedent that will threaten soldiers and officials of all democracies fighting against wild terrorism and gratuitous aggression." A well-informed source in The Hague confirmed to Le Monde on Sunday an "imminent event," but did not specify the nature of the charges or the names of those involved. The prosecutor must have any arrest warrant request validated by three judges, and this final step is now said to be completed, according to the same source. Several Israeli leaders have told The New York Times, under the condition of anonymity, that they fear the Israeli Prime Minister himself may be subject to an arrest warrant. The Israeli press has also mentioned the names of Defense Minister Yoav Gallant and Chief of Staff Herzi Halevi. In March 2021, the ICC prosecutor's office opened an investigation into crimes committed in the occupied Palestinian territories. This procedure targets all violations of international law perpetrated by Israel in the West Bank and Gaza Strip since 2014, including those ongoing in the offensive against Hamas in the Palestinian enclave, which has resulted in nearly 35,000 deaths. During a press conference in Cairo on October 29, 2023, Prosecutor Karim Khan also confirmed his jurisdiction for the attack carried out on October 7, 2023, in southern Israel by Hamas, which resulted in 1,170 deaths. At least two Hamas officials could also be prosecuted, according to information from Le Monde. Diplomatic Action The first signs of concern in Israel appeared in mid-April. Following a visit by David Cameron and Annalena Baerbock, the British and German foreign ministers, Benjamin Netanyahu convened an "emergency discussion," according to Israeli Channel 12, to coordinate a diplomatic action to counter the arrest warrants. Under the Trump administration, sanctions were imposed on the then ICC prosecutor, Fatou Bensouda, to dissuade her from pursuing charges against American officials for alleged crimes in Afghanistan, and against U.S. allies, especially Israel. They were lifted by President Joe Biden, but Secretary of State Antony Blinken has since reiterated the United States' opposition to any investigation targeting Israeli officials. Like Israel, Washington is not a member of the ICC and argues that it is therefore not competent to prosecute its nationals or those of the Hebrew state. Mr. Biden had, however, welcomed the issuance, in March 2023, of an arrest warrant against Vladimir Putin, even though Russia is also not a member of the ICC. Independent of the UN, the ICC actually has legal jurisdiction to investigate perpetrators of crimes committed on the territory of member states. This was the case in Afghanistan and has been the case in Palestine since 2015. The ICC's investigation is completely separate from the proceedings initiated before the International Court of Justice, the highest UN court responsible for disputes between states. South Africa accuses Israel of genocide against Palestinians in Gaza. It is also handling a complaint from Nicaragua, which accuses Germany of complicity in genocide and has asked the judges to impose emergency measures. Their decision will be announced on Tuesday.

title: By waiting too long, Europeans risk letting the situation in Georgia deteriorate and the trap close in on them.
lead: In Brussels and in European chancelleries, they are closely monitoring the rising temperature in Georgia, where the Parliament has just adopted the controversial bill on "foreign influence." But without knowing what to do to avoid reliving the Ukrainian precedent, analyzes in her column Sylvie Kauffmann, editorialist at "Le Monde."
text_eng: It is almost a tradition: when a crisis erupts, the Baltic States head to the Caucasus. Upon arriving in Tbilisi on Wednesday, May 15, the foreign ministers of the three Baltic States, accompanied by their Icelandic counterpart, cannot help but remember the eventful journey their leaders made to the same Georgian capital sixteen years ago. The atmosphere was tense back then as well. It was Russia once again that triggered the tension in the summer of 2008 – the same Russia that had annexed Georgia twice in history and whose tanks had just invaded the country again on the night of August 8. The Russian army intervened in Georgia, officially to protect the Russian-speaking minority in a separatist province bordering Russia, South Ossetia, which the Georgian forces had just bombed, falling into a trap of provocation. President Nicolas Sarkozy hastily left Beijing, where he had attended the opening ceremony of the Olympic Games, and jumped on his plane to negotiate a ceasefire between Moscow and Tbilisi on behalf of the European Union, whose rotating presidency was held by France. As he left Moscow for Tbilisi on August 12, carrying a ceasefire agreement approved by Vladimir Putin, his three Baltic colleagues – the Estonian, the Lithuanian, and the Latvian Prime Minister, along with the Presidents of Poland and Ukraine – were heading towards the Georgian capital by road after an exhausting journey. The Russians, who controlled Georgian airspace, allowed the French presidential plane to land, but not the Polish president's, who was forced to land in Azerbaijan. In Tbilisi, Nicolas Sarkozy and the Eastern European presidents did not stand united. Firstly, they were not on the same level – like the Russians, the French president made sure they knew that, as they later recounted. Secondly, they did not have the same objective: Paris, with the support of Berlin and Brussels, prioritized stopping the Russian offensive at all costs. The Baltic States, Poland, and Ukraine had come to support the besieged Georgian people and their President, Mikheil Saakashvili. Sixteen years later, Paris and Berlin have more respect for the Baltic capitals, whose voices carry more weight in Brussels since the invasion of Ukraine justified their warnings against Vladimir Putin. However, the recent trip of the Baltic ministers to Georgia, at a critical moment when a law on "foreign influence" is being adopted, leading to tens of thousands of protesters taking to the streets every evening, is once again a separate initiative. In Brussels and European capitals, the situation in Georgia is being closely monitored, but a cautious approach is preferred. The matter is indeed very concerning. Here is a democratically elected government, supported by a party, Georgian Dream, with known Russian connections, but officially supporting closer ties with the European Union. The EU, although somewhat wary, eventually granted Georgia candidate status in December, both because it knows it is the wish of the majority of the population and to prevent the country from falling back under Moscow's influence. However, a few months later, in April, the government took a deliberately anti-European measure by reintroducing a bill it had to abandon in 2023 due to public pressure: this bill, which closely resembles a repressive law in effect in Russia, labels any association receiving 20% or more of its funding from abroad as a "foreign-influenced agent." Adding to the complexity, Georgia's President, Salomé Zourabichvili, a former French diplomat, openly switched to the opposition and supports the protesters. Very pro-European, she promised to veto the law, but Parliament can override her veto. It is clear to everyone that the adoption of this law would halt Georgia's EU integration process. What to do? Taking a heavy-handed approach, feared in Paris, could backfire as the EU might be accused of foreign interference. Prime Minister Irakli Kobakhidze has already used this tactic by calling the presence of a Lithuanian deputy among the protesters on Monday "an insulting attitude" towards Georgian sovereignty; some Russian media outlets do not hesitate to denounce a Western assault on the Tbilisi government. Issuing a joint warning among the Twenty-Seven? Good idea! So good that Hungary rushed to block it. So, what to do? On Tuesday in Tbilisi, the American Jim O'Brien, Director of European Affairs at the State Department, brandished the threat of sanctions. The EU is still far from this. Ultimately, the visit of the Baltic states, who are set to meet with President Zourabichvili, the Foreign Minister, and the opposition, at least signals the presence of representatives from European governments. By delaying too much, Europeans risk letting the situation deteriorate, and the trap closing in on them. There is another precedent besides that of 2008: the Association Agreement between Ukraine and the EU. When, in 2013, Putin ordered the then-president, Viktor Yanukovych, not to sign it, Ukrainians revolted. This led to the Maidan, the "dignity revolution," but also a bloody crackdown, the overthrow of the regime, and, in retaliation, the annexation of Crimea by Russia, aggression in the Donbass - and, for over two years now, large-scale warfare.

title: War in Ukraine: Joe Biden enacts aid plan for Kiev ensuring not to "abandon its allies"
lead: After the enactment of the law, which provides for $61 billion in aid to Ukraine, the US president announced that the United States would be sending military equipment to Kiev "in the coming hours."
text_eng: Just a few hours after the final adoption by the American Congress of the gigantic aid package for Ukraine, President Joe Biden signed the bill on Wednesday, April 24. This signature comes after the bill was voted on in the House of Representatives on Saturday, and then in the Senate, overnight from Tuesday to Wednesday. The law, which overall includes an aid package of $95 billion (approximately 89 billion euros) for Ukraine, Israel, Taiwan, and an ultimatum to TikTok, received broad support from the other component of the American Congress. A large portion of this aid will go to Kiev, which is facing a complicated situation on the battlefield against Russia: $61 billion (approximately 57 billion euros) is allocated to the war in Ukraine. President Biden announced that the United States will be sending military equipment to Ukraine "in the coming hours." "This is an investment in our own security," assured Joe Biden on Wednesday after signing the bill. For the Democratic president, who is running for reelection, this vote is also a message to Moscow: "We do not bow to anyone. Anyone. And certainly not to [Russian President] Vladimir Putin." "We do not abandon our allies, we support them. We do not let tyrants win, we oppose them. We do not stand by as spectators to the world's developments, we shape them," he further stated, praising the political consensus reached between Democratic lawmakers and a number of Republican officials after months of difficult negotiations on the bill. "This is what it means to be a global superpower," Joe Biden reiterated, who had been calling for these funds for months. Congress answered the "call of history."
