On social media, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky expressed his gratitude to the U.S. Senate for approving vital aid for Ukraine. The Kremlin, on the other hand, downplayed its significance. "All the new batches of weapons are surely already prepared and will not change the dynamics on the front," said Dmitry Peskov, the spokesperson for the Russian presidency, on Wednesday. The adoption of this aid package is a relief for the Ukrainian army, which has been facing a shortage of recruits and ammunition, under constant pressure from Russian troops in the East. According to President Zelensky, "long-range capabilities, artillery, and air defense of Ukraine are essential tools to more quickly restore a just peace." The United States is the main military support for Kiev, but Congress had not approved a large package for its ally for nearly a year and a half, mainly due to partisan disputes. The American president and the Democratic Party have remained supportive of this aid, presented as an investment in the security of the United States against what they see as aggressive actions by Russia. The Republicans, led by Donald Trump, have become increasingly hesitant, and the Speaker of the House of Representatives, conservative Mike Johnson, had long blocked the bill. But the Republican leader eventually backed the resumption of military and economic aid, with the justification: "I prefer to send ammunition to Ukraine than to send our boys to fight." This aid plan also authorizes President Biden to confiscate and sell Russian assets to fund the reconstruction of Ukraine. This idea is gaining traction among other G7 countries. A significant portion of the aid will also be used to replenish the stocks of the American military and will benefit arms factories in the United States.

title: Olaf Scholz in China: the dangers of every man for himself
lead: By advocating in China for an "open and fair" competition, the German Chancellor seems to ignore that Beijing only understands the balance of power. Europeans are taking the risk of weakening themselves if they do not act together.
text_eng: The visit of German Chancellor Olaf Scholz to China from Sunday, April 14 to Tuesday, April 16, leading a significant economic delegation, proves it. When it comes to relations with the Middle Kingdom, the Social Democrat positions himself much more in continuity with Angela Merkel than in a break with her. "We are living in a changing era" (Zeitenwende), the Chancellor solemnly declared at the Bundestag on February 27, 2022, three days after the start of the Russian invasion of Ukraine. Germany was forced to recognize, albeit reluctantly, that it had been mistaken in relying for years on the rationality of Vladimir Putin and on the everlasting supply of Russian gas to power its industry. But this change of era did not only concern the German-Russian relationship. It was also about defending democracy and the rule of law against powers that only know the law of the jungle. The Zeitenwende meant the end of Wandel durch Handel ("change through trade"), the German political theory that by trading with Western democracies, dictatorships would necessarily liberalize. In this sense, China was also involved. While in March 2023 the President of the European Commission, Ursula von der Leyen, emphasized the need to "reduce the risk" towards China, many German industrialists - especially the largest ones - continue to believe that the main risk is, on the contrary, not to invest enough in the world's second-largest economy, which aspires to become the first. Currently, no less than 5,000 German companies operate in China. A figure that Olaf Scholz, the former mayor of Hamburg, is the last to underestimate. "Open and fair" competition Between his Green Minister Annalena Baerbock, who calls Xi Jinping a "dictator," business circles divided on "derisking," and his own party, the Social Democratic Party, which seems to believe that China can influence Russia's position towards Ukraine, Olaf Scholz is walking a tightrope. Nevertheless, by advocating for "open and fair" competition in China, the Chancellor seems to ignore that Beijing only understands power dynamics. Far from making any compromises, Xi Jinping is more than ever betting on his heavily subsidized industry to conquer new markets and ensure satisfactory economic growth despite a stagnant domestic demand. The General Secretary of the Chinese Communist Party is actually a conservative who swears by a supply-side policy, convinced that supporting domestic demand would encourage laziness among the Chinese and, consequently, the decline of China. Olaf Scholz cannot change this mindset. At the very least, he could take into account the reservations of many European countries towards Beijing and draw conclusions from the "Zeitenwende" that he himself theorized. In March 2019, when he came to Paris, Xi Jinping was received at the Elysée not only by Emmanuel Macron but also by Angela Merkel and Jean-Claude Juncker, who was then President of the European Commission. In spring 2024, the German Chancellor is visiting China three weeks before a state visit that the Chinese President is preparing to make to France. A "every man for himself" scenario is looming, with Xi Jinping likely to be the only winner.

title: Raphaël Glucksmann: "Mr. Macron, so far, what have you achieved with your strategy of accommodation towards the Chinese Communist Party?"
lead: The lead candidate of the Socialist Party and Place publique in the European elections denounces, in an open letter, the "obsequiousness" that the head of state is showing towards the Chinese leader. Xi Jinping is being received on May 6 and 7 for a state visit to France.
text_eng: Mr. President, in this letter, I will not invoke morality or grand principles, but rather security and sovereignty. I will not appeal to idealism, but to realism. Yes, to realism. Not in the sense, obviously, that cynics give to this term, equating it with resignation and indifference. No, the realism I am talking about acknowledges the power of ideologies and prepares for major disruptions. On Monday, May 6th, you will be meeting with Chinese President Xi Jinping for his first visit to Europe since the pandemic. You will be rolling out the red carpet and even inviting him [on May 7th] to a place dear to your childhood in the Pyrenees, an intimate location meant to give this relationship that you dream of a special touch of friendship. And as usual, you will justify such obsequiousness towards a dictator by claiming the necessity of being realistic. But there is nothing "realistic" in your obsequious attitude. The first commandment of realism is to base your policy on reality. The fundamental reality of Europe in 2024 is Vladimir Putin's war in Ukraine. And this war, Mr. President, is financially and diplomatically supported by Chinese President Xi Jinping. Without China's assistance, Russia would not have been able to withstand Western sanctions and sustain such a war effort. Our dependence is immense. The Russo-Chinese alliance is not temporary. When the leaders of the world's two most powerful dictatorships celebrate their "unlimited friendship" in Moscow following the invasion of Ukraine, it is not a bluff but an essential geopolitical fact, based on a shared hostility towards us, a hostility much deeper than you seem to believe. This alliance has one goal: to revise the international order and weaken our democracies, both externally and internally. As head of the European Parliament's special committee on foreign interference, I have dissected the destabilization efforts of our political and economic systems by Russia and China. I have analyzed the cooperation between these two dictatorships in the attacks against our nations, our information space, and our businesses. We come to the second fundamental reality of Europe in 2024 on which we must base our domestic and foreign policy. We have become a continent of consumers: consumers of security produced in the United States, consumers of energy produced in the Gulf, and consumers of goods produced in China, especially in strategic sectors of the ecological transition. China currently holds 60% of zero-carbon industries, as explained by the think tank Strategic Perspectives. She controls over 80% of polysilicon, a key element in the photovoltaic industry, and 70% of lithium cell manufacturing for electric vehicles, according to the German think tank Merics. Our dependence is immense. And this Chinese grip is not the result of simple market logic. A geopolitical and industrial steamroller. It responds to a clear geopolitical plan that Xi Jinping shamelessly announced in 2020: "China must strengthen the dependence of international production chains on China to build a powerful capacity for response and deterrence." The Made in China 2025 plan, launched in 2015, the "dual circulation" policy presented during the 14th five-year plan in 2021, as well as massive subsidies and selling at a loss are essential components of Beijing's geopolitical and industrial strategy, aimed at eradicating our production capacities in Europe. And in the face of this geopolitical and industrial steamroller, what have you done, Mr. President, in the past seven years to protect our producers and our sovereignty? Nothing. Or very little, as shown by the liquidation of the French photovoltaic company Systovi in April. This company had invested in a promising sector, yet it was crushed in a few weeks by Chinese competition based on the enslavement of Uighurs in polysilicon production and a strategy of selling solar panels at a loss in Europe. The leaders of Systovi told me how much the public authorities' capitulation to such unfair competition prevented any development of a European industry in the most promising and strategic areas. I received the same message in all the factories I visited in recent months, from Wallglass in Belgium to the Clavaux site in the French Alps. So, Mr. President, let's be realistic, let's judge a policy by its concrete results and not by its moral intentions. So far, what have you achieved with your accommodating strategy towards the Chinese Communist Party? What have you achieved with your gestures of friendship perceived in Beijing as signs of weakness? What have you achieved by acting as if China does not support Russia, as if Chinese interference on our soil does not exist, or as if China is not constantly engaging in hostile trade practices? A strategic failure. What have you achieved by remaining silent about the deportation of the Uighur people, acting as if millions of human beings are not being herded into camps, sterilized, tortured, and enslaved? What have you achieved by remaining silent about the repression of Tibetans or Hong Kongers and the military threats against Taiwan? Nothing. You have obtained nothing more than symbolic tributes flattering your ego. However, a realistic policy cannot be based on feelings, good or bad. The lessons from the total failure of your five years of charm offensives towards Vladimir Putin do not seem to have been learned. From Versailles in 2017 to the Hermitage Museum in 2018, you sought to establish a personal relationship with the Russian tyrant and justified all these flatteries by invoking, now tiresome, that famous "realpolitik": we have all seen the disastrous result of this strategic failure. Flattering an authoritarian empire fundamentally hostile to the interests and principles of our nations brings us nothing and costs us a lot. There is no realism in this position, but rather narcissism and short-term thinking. Being realistic means recognizing that there are conflicts too deep to be soothed by charm. It is not about not talking to China or not receiving Chinese leaders, but about being firm, not remaining silent in the face of crimes committed there or attacks carried out here, and accepting power dynamics. This is the only way to earn respect from geopolitical and ideological adversaries. It is also the only way to build that European strategic autonomy you rightly often talk about. It is finally the only way to transform the European Union into an ecological and global political power that has a say in world affairs. Putting an end to French presidential narcissism. Let's be serious: how can the Lithuanians, for example, trust France when you alone decide to flatter the tyrant who has triggered a real trade and political war against them? [Since 2022, China has imposed economic sanctions on Lithuania in retaliation for the opening of a Taiwanese diplomatic mission in its capital, Vilnius.] How can we build a European industrial power without breaking away from the dependencies methodically built by the Chinese regime and without going through a moment of tension with the regime that is patiently eradicating our productive capacities? Ultimately, Mr. President, the question posed to us in this year 2024 is the old, burning question of Hamlet: to be or not to be? To be or not to be truly European? If we decide to be truly European, then we must defend the European common interest and put an end to the French presidential narcissism that leads most Elysée tenants to embrace dictators once they are powerful. This policy isolates France in Europe. If we decide to truly be European, then we must be ready to defend our principles and interests, particularly through a show of strength against this Chinese communist regime that is favored by our multinational corporations hungry for profits and dividends. We are the world's largest market. We are the world's leading trading power. Let's stop thinking of ourselves as weak, lost, or declining. There is more strength in our nations than just the vague memory of a lost grandeur that we revive through the meaningless and degrading red carpets we roll out for tyrants. There is more power in Europe than you seem to think. We will truly be powerful when we truly want to be. Today, all we lack is the political courage to want it and act accordingly. Let's put our market at the service of our principles and strategic interests. In short, let's be politicians, not just merchants or aesthetes. And then, and only then, will we be powerful.

title: Josep Borrell, EU's chief diplomat: "A regional war is not in anyone's interest, especially not the people of Gaza."
lead: The High Representative of the European Union for Foreign Affairs emphasizes the need for de-escalation in the Middle East, but acknowledges the limitations of the EU in influencing the parties involved. He also advocates for "restoring the balance of power" between Ukraine and Russia.
text_eng: Former Spanish Foreign Minister Josep Borrell has been the High Representative of the European Union (EU) for Foreign Affairs since 2019. Following the attack by Iran on Israel, he urged Tehran not to engage in a "new escalation" and convened an emergency video conference of the EU's foreign ministers on Tuesday, April 16. Since the beginning of the conflict between Israel and the Palestinian Hamas, the European leader has repeatedly called for a ceasefire in the Gaza Strip, stating that the enclave had become an "open-air cemetery." What role can the EU play in contributing to peace in the Middle East, after the unprecedented strikes carried out by Iran on Israel on the night of April 13-14? The EU only has diplomatic strength, its power of persuasion. It is clear that some member countries have more influence on Israel than others, notably Germany, which has excellent relations with the Israelis. The Americans could, if they wanted, use other resources, particularly through their arms deliveries to Israel. In the past, they have made binding decisions. But today, I do not believe they want to use the leverage they have. The political goal of the EU is to prevent escalation. It's like a game of chess where everyone moves their pieces. Israel attacked an Iranian diplomatic representation in Syria; Iran retaliated. It is now Israel's turn to react. We must keep in mind the shock caused by the Hamas attacks on October 7, 2023, and the security failures on Israeli public opinion. But we warn Israel by saying that it is in everyone's interest to avoid regional confrontation. This is also what we told the Iranians following the attack on the consulate in Damascus: it is not in anyone's interest, especially not in the interest of the people in Gaza, to have a regional conflict, as it would mean that the war will not end. The Hamas attack was unprecedented, and so was the Israeli response. We are sowing the seeds of hatred for three generations. Now more than ever, the only possible solution is separation [between the two peoples]. In this context, were you surprised by the Iranian response? We were warned several days in advance. After the attack, the Iranian Foreign Minister pointed out to me that they had only targeted military installations, implying that it was a measured response. When one wants to cause damage, they do not send drones that take six hours to reach their target. This does not excuse or diminish in any way this attack, which is a first on Israeli territory. But the fact that the missiles and drones could be intercepted was part of the strategy. Why did the Iranians choose this strategy? At the moment, neither Hezbollah nor Iran are ready for war. In a military situation, everyone analyzes the balance of power at play... Can the EU consider imposing new sanctions against Iran? To implement sanctions, there must be sanctionable actions. There have been discussions about listing the Revolutionary Guards as a terrorist organization, as former U.S. President Donald Trump did in 2019. However, for such a classification, a judicial authority of a member state must consider that this organization has committed a terrorist act. This has not been the case so far. Regarding the Israel-Gaza conflict, has the EU not shown its limitations in diplomacy? The strength of the EU lies in its unity. Unfortunately, on this issue, Europeans have shown clear and deep divisions. This demonstrates the limits of a EU that votes unanimously. Some, like France, have changed their position and now call for an immediate ceasefire, while others still believe that it would not allow Israel to defend itself. I always try to present a consensus position: if cutting off water, electricity, and food to a besieged population is contrary to international law in Ukraine, then it is the same in Gaza. If we do not accept this universalist position, we are accused of having a "double standard." Do you share the concern of Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky regarding Russia? It is a "dynamic stalemate." The fighting is very intense. But the breakthrough that Ukraine hoped for has not occurred, and for now, there is also no Russian breakthrough. The Russians have the upper hand and are taking pieces of territory at enormous human costs. The balance of power needs to be restored. There is an asymmetry in this story: Russia does not need to win; for them, it is enough not to lose. Ukraine, on the other hand, must win to repel the invasion. Demographically, the difference is huge, and there is a wear and tear effect in Ukraine. It is up to us to provide them with offensive capabilities. That is why I convened a council of foreign and defense affairs on April 22 to see how we can meet Ukrainian demands for ammunition and, perhaps more urgently, for anti-aircraft defense. I am reaching out to member states to tell them that if they do not have anti-aircraft batteries, they will need to purchase them. Progress is being made slowly, but there are huge differences in contributions from member states. Could France do more compared to Germany? Everyone could do more to live up to our commitments. My role is not to point fingers at any particular country, but to build a common position. What matters to me is the cumulative figure, but I know well that this sum is made up of very different terms. The problem is not time, but quantity. In a year, it may be too late. Before discussing the reconstruction of Ukraine, it is better to prevent its destruction. It is cheaper to buy [American] Patriot systems than to rebuild a power plant targeted by the Russians. Are you afraid of Donald Trump returning to the White House? Putin will not stop until the American elections [in November] have taken place. When he sees how Republican lawmakers are behaving [by suspending the vote on aid to Ukraine in Congress], he has good reasons to think that a Republican administration would be a more comfortable scenario for him. What will be the impact of a breakthrough by the far right in the European elections in June? Europe finds itself between two wars. It has entered a world where there is less cooperation and more competition; a fragmented world where major alliances are not sufficient to deal with the emergence of new actors; a world where transitions are accelerating in terms of climate, demographics, with migratory phenomena, and digital, with consequences on work. All of this breeds fear, which drives towards extreme solutions. I fear this dynamic and I will do my best to prevent an alliance between the European People's Party and parties to its right. These elections are crucial, as it is not in our countries that the pace of ecological, migratory, or technological transformations will be decided alone.

title: "France can provide assistance to the energy sector in Ukraine."
lead: The Ukrainian Deputy Minister of Energy, Mykola Kolisnyk, and humanitarian activist Svitlana Romanko are calling on France, in an article in "Le Monde", to facilitate the transfer of equipment intended to repair Ukraine's energy infrastructure, targeted by Russia.
text_eng: As Vladimir Putin continues his brutal assault on Ukraine, one of the most critical fronts where France can provide assistance is in the energy sector. Russia's relentless attacks on power plants in Ukraine pose a serious threat to the country's energy security, economic functioning, and the well-being of its citizens. Since March 22, Russian forces have been targeting Ukrainian energy systems almost daily. DTEK, Ukraine's largest private energy company, reported that five out of its six major coal-fired power plants had been damaged, resulting in an 80% capacity loss. On the second anniversary of the invasion in February, DTEK announced that 252 of its employees had been killed while working on-site to keep the system operational. The recent attacks in Kharkiv, Ukraine's second-largest city, left at least 700,000 people and 400 localities across Ukraine without electricity. In the face of Russian attacks on energy infrastructure, France can help Ukraine withstand the Kremlin's assault and save thousands of lives by strengthening its air defense, protecting its energy resources from Russian missile attacks, and preventing power outages. These measures are urgently needed, as well as temporary power supplies and self-powering systems for critical infrastructure such as hospitals, water pumping stations, and heating plants, using solar energy and other distributed energy sources. Immediate Needs Currently, Ukraine's centralized energy system, dating back to the Soviet era, is in a very fragile state, with 50% of its infrastructure damaged by rocket and drone attacks. Russian airstrikes have damaged Ukraine's largest hydroelectric plant, DniproHES, and the Dnipro reservoir dam, contributing to a capacity deficit. Many coal-fired power generation units have also been extensively damaged, and to replace them, Ukraine needs to build new estimated 12 gigawatts of capacity. Ukraine's total investment needs to rebuild its electricity sector, severely damaged by Russian attacks, could present an opportunity for targeted aid funds or guaranteed foreign investments. The immediate needs for supplying equipment to Ukraine, spare parts for repairs, and new production capacities are covered by the effective Ukraine Energy Support Fund platform. This initiative, established by the Ukrainian Ministry of Energy and the European Commission, has already raised over 400 million dollars [375 million euros]. This fund is an effective tool that allows businesses to obtain the equipment and parts needed to restore damaged facilities. This mechanism enables manufacturers and suppliers from the EU to send equipment through funds allocated by their own governments to support Ukraine. Financing the reconstruction of Ukraine also ensures the energy security of EU countries and could enable French energy sector companies to create jobs and increase their production capacities. The example of Lithuania, which offered to transfer equipment from a decommissioned power plant, is a good one. There are such power plants throughout Europe, and the Ukrainian Ministry of Energy would be grateful to France for supporting them by providing spare parts. It is also important to emphasize the need for sanctions against Russian energy companies (Novatek, Rosatom, and their subsidiaries) to put an end to the Kremlin's global energy market expansion and the use of energy as a hybrid influence weapon. Long-term security The financial estimate of losses due to attacks on the Ukrainian power system during the two years of war was estimated, before March, to be at least $11 billion [€10.3 billion], not including losses due to the occupation of the Zaporizhia nuclear power plant. From a strategic point of view, we need French engineering and expertise for the overall modernization of energy systems, especially using climate-friendly technologies to make them more resilient and sustainable. Cooperation between France and Ukraine in this area can contribute to long-term energy security, which is essential to winning the war. We must rapidly develop the market for renewable energy solutions, bringing new technologies, expertise, local equipment production, and investments to Ukraine. More must be done to establish export credit and investment mechanisms that enable the deployment of new technologies and help Ukraine meet its energy needs securely. To prevent millions of Ukrainians from freezing in their homes, France could facilitate the transfer of equipment for repairing energy infrastructure. This measure would be beneficial as it would involve the French industry. Furthermore, the French government should contribute to replenishing the Energy Support Fund to address the current threats to the energy system. By taking decisive actions, it is possible to ensure a safer future for Ukraine and its people. It is time for France to contribute to restoring hope and show solidarity with Ukraine in the face of this major challenge. France and Ukraine can join forces to fight back. Let's make it happen.

title: Xi Jinping's visit to France did not help to dispel the clouds hanging over the bilateral relationship.
lead: On Tuesday, the two heads of state visited the Hautes-Pyrénées. A "personal" moment spoiled by wintry weather.
text_eng: On the second day of Xi Jinping's state visit to France, the Chinese and French presidents ditched their ties, a sign of relaxation between the leaders. The Elysée wanted to stage a more personal moment, after a very official agenda in Paris on Monday, May 6. It was an opportunity to discuss important topics without an army of advisers, in hopes of influencing the position of the president of the second world power, as France hoped. For China, the moment allowed them to show a president warmly received everywhere in the world. However, when they arrived at the Col du Tourmalet, a thick blizzard welcomed the two heads of state: the beautiful postcard moment hoped for did not happen. This was emblematic of the entire state visit, which did not dispel the clouds threatening the bilateral relationship. The main progress was the commitment of the two presidents to an Olympic truce in the ongoing conflicts during the Paris Games this summer. The Elysée also welcomed a joint declaration on the situation in the Middle East, mentioning the two-state solution. On the contrary, commercial issues led to firm exchanges on Monday between Xi Jinping and Emmanuel Macron, supported by Ursula von der Leyen, the President of the European Commission, invited by France. While Mr. Xi dismissed the "so-called overcapacity problem," his French counterpart affirmed his willingness to "continue to open trade, but to ensure each time that it is fully fair." The Elysée was at least pleased to have obtained a reprieve for cognac, which was threatened by a Chinese anti-dumping investigation launched in retaliation for the EU investigation into subsidies for Chinese electric vehicles. A "friendly but also very frank" dialogue Should we see in this gesture – not confirmed by the Chinese side – a desire for appeasement? "Xi is trying to limit the damage, but he knows very well that we are going to take measures: that's what bothers him the most, in the context of China's economic slowdown," says sinologist Jean-Pierre Cabestan, a researcher at the Asia Centre. Since 2023, Chinese officials have been sending messages of economic openness to try to attract investors back after three years of closure due to the zero-Covid policy implemented in the country. While household consumption struggles to recover, exports are booming. In this context, it is difficult to imagine the European Union making concessions: "We lost on solar panels, on wind turbines, we are not going to continue losing on electric vehicles," adds Mr. Cabestan. On Tuesday, Mr. Macron offered a bottle of armagnac to his counterpart before sitting down at the altitude restaurant L'Etape du Berger, owned by a long-time friend of the French president, along with a list of very "terroir" gifts from the Southwest: A beret, a black pork ham, a wool blanket, a signed jersey from the latest winner of the Tour de France, and vintage 2008 wine, the year of the Beijing Olympics – nothing is left to chance. All of this is meant to lighten the atmosphere for this somewhat disrupted day due to heavy rains: there was no agenda for the lunch between the two presidential couples, which finally started at 2 p.m. "Today's sequence embodies the intimacy that we seek between the two leaders in order to have a dialogue that is both friendly and very frank. Few leaders in the world have this type of moment with the Chinese president," the Elysée Palace rejoices. The folk group Les Pastourelles de Campan performing in front of the Chinese and French presidents and their wives, in front of the restaurant L'Etape du Berger, at the Tourmalet (Hautes-Pyrénées), on May 7, 2024. MORGAN FACHE FOR "LE MONDE" Human rights addressed "in private" The visit also shows the distance between the Chinese regime and Europe. In Paris as well as in the small villages of the Pyrenees crossed by the presidential caravan, Xi Jinping was cheered by groups of students and members of the Chinese diaspora all wearing the same white cap and waving huge Chinese flags – and a few French flags for good measure. An impressive mobilization capacity of overseas Chinese. "This is new compared to what we have known in the past," notes Jean-Pierre Cabestan. "It will please Chinese television. No protesters in favor of human rights but supporters of China: this shows that China has become powerful and that France remains silent on these issues." While gatherings in support of the Uighurs and Tibetans did take place in Paris on Sunday, the security measures put in place on Tuesday prevented any demonstration, with hundreds of police officers and gendarmes along the route. The topic of human rights was indeed discussed on Monday, the Elysée Palace indicates, but "in private," for reasons of efficiency, with Paris deeming "megaphone diplomacy" counterproductive. It is better to negotiate on individual cases, especially those of relatives of French Uighur citizens detained in China. At 7 p.m., after a farewell ceremony, Emmanuel Macron gave one last hug to Xi Jinping, and the imposing Chinese delegation flew off to Serbia, then Hungary, two states close to Russia. Upon returning to China, Mr. Xi is expected to host Vladimir Putin, for their fourth meeting since the start of the war in Ukraine. Paris hopes that the two days of exchanges will have allowed Mr. Xi to understand how crucial the Ukrainian issue is for Europe, and that he will be able to convey a message of peace to his Russian counterpart. But there is no guarantee that Emmanuel Macron's efforts will be enough to challenge the "limitless friendship" between Mr. Xi and Mr. Putin.

title: Xi Jinping on State visit to France on May 6 and 7, Ukraine on the agenda of his meeting with Emmanuel Macron.
lead: The Chinese president and his wife are scheduled to land in Paris on the evening of Sunday, May 5, before being received by Emmanuel and Brigitte Macron on Monday, May 6. A state dinner is planned at the Elysée Palace. The two couples will then travel to the Hautes-Pyrénées the following day.
text_eng: Chinese President Xi Jinping is expected to make a state visit to France on Monday, May 6 and Tuesday, May 7 to celebrate the 60th anniversary of diplomatic relations between the two countries, and also to discuss international crises with Emmanuel Macron, including the war in Ukraine. This visit to France by the Chinese president comes a year after the visit of the French president to Beijing and Guangzhou in April 2023, as highlighted by the Elysée Palace in a statement. Xi Jinping is scheduled to land in Paris on the evening of Sunday, May 5. The Chinese president and his wife, Peng Liyuan, will then be received by Emmanuel and Brigitte Macron in Paris on Monday, May 6, where a state dinner is planned at the Elysée Palace. On May 7, the two couples will travel to the Hautes-Pyrénées, to the Col du Tourmalet, a famous climb of the Tour de France, where the French President wants to share a more intimate moment with his counterpart. He had visited the area frequently in his childhood to see his maternal grandmother, Germaine Noguès, who passed away in 2013 and lived in Bagnères-de-Bigorre. "The discussions will focus on international crises, notably the war in Ukraine and the situation in the Middle East, trade issues, scientific, cultural, and sports cooperation, as well as our joint actions on global challenges, including climate urgency, biodiversity protection, and the financial situation of the most vulnerable countries," added the French presidency. Similar to a year ago in China, the President of the European Commission, Ursula von der Leyen, will be involved in these discussions during a trilateral meeting in Paris on Monday. This marks the beginning of the Chinese President's first European tour since the Covid-19 pandemic, which had led the Asian country to significantly reduce interactions with the rest of the world. After his visit to France, Xi Jinping is set to visit Serbia, and then Hungary from May 8 to May 10, as confirmed by Chinese authorities on Monday. Described as a highly political visit with no major contracts expected, Paris hopes to attract new Chinese investments, particularly in electric batteries. Lin Jian, a spokesperson for the Chinese diplomacy, stated that the two leaders will try to "make new contributions to peace, stability, development, and progress in the world." "Given China's significant partnership with Russia, especially in diplomatic and commercial matters, the French President aims to 'encourage it to use its leverage [to exert pressure] on Moscow to change Russia's calculations and contribute to a resolution of this conflict,'" explained the Elysée Palace. "He will also address concerns about the activities of certain Chinese companies that could directly contribute significantly to the Russian war effort," added an advisor. Chinese authorities officially claim neutrality regarding the conflict in Ukraine and call for a peaceful solution, but they have never condemned the Russian invasion. Russian President Vladimir Putin, a privileged partner of Beijing, is set to visit China in May. A year ago in China, Emmanuel Macron had urged Xi Jinping to "bring Russia to reason" regarding Ukraine "and bring everyone to the negotiating table." Shortly after, the Chinese president called his Ukrainian counterpart, Volodymyr Zelensky, for the first time since the conflict began in February 2022. However, the diplomatic progress expected by Paris on the Russo-Ukrainian front stopped there. German Chancellor Olaf Scholz also asked Xi Jinping in mid-April to pressure Moscow to stop its "senseless campaign" in Ukraine, while affirming German-Chinese support for a peace conference scheduled for June in Switzerland. The Chinese leader also met with U.S. Secretary of State Antony Blinken last week, during which he called on the United States to "be partners, not rivals." The American Secretary of State, in turn, expressed his concerns to China about its support for Russia, stating that the invasion of Ukraine would be more "difficult" without Beijing's support. Last year, Xi Jinping hosted Emmanuel Macron in Canton for a tea ceremony at the residence of the governor of Guangdong Province, where his father, Xi Zhongxun, had lived while holding that position from 1978 to 1981.

title: In Ukraine, online disinformation worries the authorities.
lead: The Telegram application, which has become indispensable for staying informed about the war, is also used by Russia to fuel the concerns and divisions within Ukrainian society.
text_eng: Fast, efficient, and requiring little internet connection, the Telegram application has established itself, in just over two years, as the primary source of information for Ukrainians. The platform perfectly meets the demand for immediacy in a country at war. It is a patchwork of thousands of information channels allowing people to stay informed about the military situation in the country while tracking the trajectories of Russian drones and missiles during air attacks. But as Kiev has been trying for several years to rid itself of Russian influence in the country, Telegram is also one of the last digital bridges between Ukraine and Russia. The two countries are engaged in a fierce information war on the platform. As the situation worsens for the Ukrainian army, the total freedom enjoyed by users of the network has prompted some officials to sound the alarm. At the end of March, a bill aimed at better regulating Telegram was even presented to Parliament. "What is happening on Telegram is a major source of danger to national security," said Mykyta Potouraev, a deputy from the ruling party Servant of the People and a signatory of the bill. The lawmaker, also the head of the parliamentary committee for humanitarian and information policy, advocates for new rules that would allow Kiev to hold Telegram accountable in order to control channels accused of serving Moscow's interests. This is something the platform with 900 million subscribers has always refused to do. Total anonymity "The main danger is that it is a network that is practically unmoderated," argues Mykyta Potouraev, who is also convinced that the application founded and led by a Russian citizen (naturalized French in 2021), Pavel Durov, is controlled by the Russian domestic intelligence service (FSB). Pavel Durov, exiled in Dubai since 2017, denies any connection to Russia, claiming the application's total neutrality. Nevertheless, the platform has become the favorite application of Ukrainians. The percentage of those using it as a source of information has increased from 21% in 2021 to 72% in November 2023, according to a survey by the media support organization Usaid-Internews. As a result, all officials in the country have their own channels. However, while Telegram remains very popular among authorities, it is also criticized for its operating mode that allows complete anonymity for content creators. This creates an ecosystem where reliable sources and propaganda coexist with illicit content. Moreover, while some official propaganda sources identify themselves as such, the application is full of channels that leverage their anonymity to disseminate destabilizing content. "Obviously, these channels do not say they are from the FSB," says the annoyed Deputy Potouraev. "This is a disinformation operation where certain channels managed by Russians may present themselves as pro-Ukrainian." "Playing on our own difficulties" At the head of the organization CAT-UA (for Communication Analysis Team-Ukraine), tasked since the beginning of the invasion with monitoring trends on social networks in order to "fight against manipulations," Artem Zakhartchenko, 41, observes an evolution in the techniques used by Russia to target the population of his country. If, at first, "they tried to convince us by highlighting their own strategic narrative," today, "they have understood that they must play on our own difficulties," affirms this university teacher, with the aim "to weaken Ukrainian society and exacerbate our divisions." Hundreds of information channels on Telegram thus focus on the most sensitive topics for Ukrainians: the struggling military mobilization, the precarious situation on the front line, the lack of support from allies while the country suffers from an ammunition shortage, corruption scandals... All crucial issues for a population tired of official Ukrainian speeches, often considered too far from reality. These destabilization attempts take various forms. Some channels, which present themselves as pro-Kiev, even go as far as writing in Ukrainian to reinforce this impression. Others maintain a facade of neutrality. All have in common the massive dissemination of content taken from Russian propaganda. The social network TikTok is also widely used. Anait Khoperiya, from the Center for Countering Disinformation, a state organization, claims not to see a specific narrative in the campaigns of Telegram channels. "Their methods consist of using a true story and intensifying the dissemination of a single case," she explains, citing the example of the city of Odessa, where many instances of violence by army recruiters against civilians have been filmed. "The Russians will amplify the effect of these videos by spreading them as much as possible and by accompanying some of them with fake debates," asserts this Ukrainian. These maneuvers also involve creating photo montages disseminated in Russian media and picked up by Telegram channels. The teams of the Center for Countering Disinformation regularly publish denials on their website: "False information about Volodymyr Zelensky buying a manor from the King of England"; "False information about the mobilization of 1,000 students from the Taras Shevchenko National University [in Kiev]"; "False information about border guards being authorized to open fire on 'fugitives' from the country." Artem Zakhartchenko, from CAT-UA, acknowledges that Russia may not necessarily be behind the handling of certain sensitive issues in the country, but that it could be due to political opposition, activists, bloggers, and ordinary Ukrainian citizens. The difference is that "Russian agents will do it by playing on emotions, outrage, and amplifying them," he says. "It is important to have internal discussions. But the Russians use our democratic procedures to fuel our divisions."

title: War in Ukraine: Kiev announces the arrest of two "agents" who were planning to assassinate Volodymyr Zelensky on Moscow's orders.
lead: Other "high-ranking officials" from military and political spheres were also targeted, such as the head of the military intelligence service (HUR), Kyrylo Boudanov, and the head of the Security Service of Ukraine, Vassyl Maliouk.
text_eng: Ukrainian authorities claimed on Tuesday, May 7, to have thwarted a Russian plot to assassinate President Volodymyr Zelensky. The Ukrainian Security Service (SBU) announced in a statement that they had "disrupted a network of agents" from the Russian Federal Security Service (FSB) who were planning "the assassination of the Ukrainian president," and had arrested two Ukrainian officers who were part of it. Other "high-ranking officials" from military and political spheres were also targeted, such as the head of the Ukrainian military intelligence service (HUR), Kyrylo Boudanov, and the head of the SBU, Vassyl Maliouk, the same source specified. Since the beginning of the Russian invasion on February 24, 2022, Kiev has reported several assassination attempts targeting President Zelensky, attributed to Moscow. Russia has long been known to have agents in the Ukrainian military and security apparatus, although Ukraine's resistance to the Russian attack and the failure of the offensive on Kiev in the spring of 2022 have shown the limits of Moscow's intelligence services. This time, the SBU claimed to have arrested "two colonels" from the Ukrainian State Security Service responsible for the security of public officials. "They were arrested a few days ago," a source within the Ukrainian law enforcement told Agence France-Presse. These two officials allegedly "passed confidential information" to Russia and had been recruited by the FSB before the 2022 invasion, according to the SBU. The suspects are charged with "high treason" and "preparations for a terrorist attack" and face life imprisonment, according to the source. They reportedly sought to recruit military personnel "close" to President Zelensky's security service in order to "take him hostage and kill him," the SBU further reported. Multiple attempts according to Kiev In a video released by the SBU, a man with a blurred face recounts that he had recommended to the FSB a person willing to "block" President Zelensky, likely when he was about to record his traditional evening address. One of the members of this alleged network had obtained drones and explosives, the SBU claimed. This network had planned the "liquidation" this week of Kyrylo Boudanov, whose death would have been a "gift" to Vladimir Putin for his inauguration on Tuesday, according to the SBU. One of the arrested colonels was tasked with observing a building where Mr. Boudanov was supposed to arrive by car and passing this information to the FSB so that Moscow could strike the site with a missile. This colonel was then supposed to launch a drone attack against any potential survivors, after which a second Russian missile was to be sent to "erase the traces" of the drone's use, still according to the SBU. Kiev has repeatedly denounced assassination attempts against its president and other high-ranking officials or their relatives. For example, in April 2024, a man suspected of assisting Russian intelligence in preparing an attack against Volodymyr Zelensky was arrested in Poland, according to Polish and Ukrainian prosecutors. Russia has been repeatedly accused of killing opponents of the Kremlin, both in the country and abroad, but has always denied these allegations. In recent months, the situation has been more favorable on the front for the Russian army, which had suffered humiliating defeats in the spring and fall of 2022. While the Ukrainian army lacks ammunition and recruits, Russian troops are on the offensive in the east of the country and are carrying out strikes on populated areas throughout the country.

title: "Xi Jinping, the Red Prince", in nine episodes on France Inter
lead: Expected in Paris on May 6 and 7, the Chinese president is the subject of a widely educational and particularly well-produced podcast.
text_eng: FRANCE INTER - ON DEMAND - PODCAST He has not visited Europe since 2019. Xi Jinping is expected in Paris on May 6 and 7, as France and the People's Republic of China celebrate the 60th anniversary of their diplomatic relations this year. On this occasion, France Inter offers a podcast in nine episodes about the "red prince," Chinese President Xi Jinping. Dominique André, who was a correspondent for Radio France in Beijing from 2007 to 2010 and then from 2015 to 2021, immediately reveals the difficulty of filming conditions when she returned in the fall of 2023: "No one is willing to talk about Xi Jinping openly, people are afraid. And the surveillance cameras that film everything with their facial recognition system further complicate our work as journalists." Nevertheless, the result is quite remarkable. In nine episodes of about thirty minutes each, the listener, immersed in the history of the Chinese president, gains a clearer understanding of the "tyrant's" game - the title of episode 6. But let's go back, especially since, with a welcome concern for pedagogy, the episodes are largely chronological. Episode 1, therefore: Dominique André recalls how this red prince, born in 1953, will experience exile in the "yellow land" after his father, Xi Zhongxun (1913-2002), was ousted from power by Mao Zedong in 1962. And in the following episode, how this exile allowed him to present himself as a man of the people. "Seven perils" In episode 4, we are in 2008: Beijing hosts the Olympic Games, China gets its revenge during the subprime crisis, and Xi Jinping is still largely unknown abroad. In 2012, he nevertheless becomes the party's general secretary and president of the central military commission. It's the time for the former fallen prince to take revenge. "Document No. 9," a list of instructions from the central committee to the cadres of the Chinese Communist Party (leaked by the dissident journalist Gao Yu, who was then sentenced to six years in prison), reveals the "seven perils" that would endanger the party's authority. Namely: Western constitutional democracy; universal values; civil society; neoliberalism; Western journalism practices; historical nihilism; criticism of the reform, openness, and socialist nature of the Chinese regime. In short, as episode 5 summarizes very well, Xi Jinping's ideological line becomes clear for the first time. In 2017, a gigantic exhibition is organized in Beijing in honor of the top Chinese leader. Everything is there: high-speed trains, space conquest, nuclear power plants. However, as CNRS researcher Jean-Philippe Béja questions, the issue remains of how to achieve this dream of a prosperous country: "By putting a million Uighurs in prison? By expanding surveillance of all citizens? By prohibiting any form of expression from civil society? By fighting against new private enterprises? By imposing an extremely limited way of thinking? The risk is that this dream becomes a Chinese nightmare, and that is increasingly the case for many people inside China," points out the sinologist. Beijing's support for Moscow Dominique André tells us that he has observed a "great weariness among the population," mentioning unemployment and brain drain. Therefore, "where is Xi Jinping heading?" questions the latest episode, especially considering that the Chinese president, weakened by the Covid-19 pandemic (episode 8), is also expected to address a significant diplomatic aspect, as highlighted by the recent statement from Stéphane Séjourné to his Chinese counterpart, Foreign Minister Wang Yi: "We expect China to send very clear messages to Russia." Indeed, after over two years of war in Ukraine, Beijing's diplomatic and economic support for Moscow remains strong, and here, as in the Israel-Hamas conflict, Xi Jinping has made Vladimir Putin a strategic ally and a friend of circumstance. Didactic, enriched with archives and interviews with numerous specialists, this podcast by Dominique André and Romain Weber (well-known to listeners of "Affaires sensibles") is particularly successful. This is also thanks to impeccable production that incorporates various soundscapes and the choice of actor Yannick Choirat for narration.

title: Xi Jinping in France: Ukraine and trade tensions at the heart of the visit's issues
lead: The Chinese president, on a state visit to France on May 6 and 7 before visiting Serbia and Hungary, wants to encourage Europeans to distance themselves from the United States.
text_eng: Xi Jinping has never visited Kiev and does not seem to have any intention of doing so. However, the war in Ukraine is expected to dominate the upcoming tour that the Chinese president is preparing to make in Europe, as the conflict continues to drag on, twenty-six months after the Russian invasion. The Communist leader arrived on Sunday, May 5th, in the afternoon in France, where he is expected to stay for two days before heading to Serbia and Hungary. Welcomed at Orly airport by Prime Minister Gabriel Attal, Xi Jinping will be officially received on Monday morning at the Elysée Palace by the President of the Republic, Emmanuel Macron, for a series of meetings and an official ceremony at Les Invalides. A state dinner will then be held to commemorate the 60 years of relations between France and the People's Republic of China. The following day, the Macron and Xi couples will travel to the Hautes-Pyrénées, the department where one of the President's ancestors lived, for a high-altitude stop at the Col du Tourmalet, presented by the Elysée as "a personal sequence, with their wives, to create a framework for a frank and friendly exchange." In April 2023, China had organized a similar sequence for Emmanuel Macron in Canton (Guangzhou), with a tea ceremony at the former residence of Xi Jinping's father, who was governor of Guangdong province. The tour program, the first in Europe for Xi Jinping since 2019, was long uncertain and fiercely negotiated: while the Elysée acknowledges that it is not in control of its guest's agenda, Paris is pleased to be the first stop on his trip, asserting its preeminence over Belgrade and Budapest, both close to Russia and open to Chinese influence on the continent. The arrival of the top Chinese leader in Serbia on Tuesday evening will coincide with the 25th anniversary of the NATO bombing of the Chinese embassy in Belgrade in 1999. A symbol for a leader, like Vladimir Putin, who is concerned with challenging the Atlantic Alliance and the presumed hegemony of the United States. "Evolving the Russian position" "It is not in China's interest today to have a Russia that destabilizes the international order (...) We must therefore work with [Russia] to build peace," believes Emmanuel Macron, as stated in an interview with The Economist magazine published on Thursday, May 2nd. It is about "asking China to use the levers at its disposal to evolve the Russian position," according to sources at the Elysée, without being too optimistic about China's stance at a time when the war is dragging on, possibly turning in favor of the Kremlin. While Western countries accuse China of supporting the Russian war effort, Mr. Xi is preparing to host Vladimir Putin in Beijing once again. Xi Jinping, on the other hand, has only spoken to Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky once over the phone, a year ago. Since then, the latter has been asking his allies to convey his messages to his Chinese counterpart, as explained at the Elysée. To this day, China has no intention of participating in the Peace Conference organized by Switzerland on June 15 and 16, in close consultation with Ukraine. Russia has already warned that they will not be attending. "We will not be there if Russia does not come," said the Chinese ambassador to France, Lu Shaye, ahead of Xi Jinping's visit. This diplomat is quick to adopt the Russian narrative, shifting the responsibility for the conflict onto NATO. According to him, Western countries should reconsider China's "position on the political settlement of the Ukrainian crisis" published in February 2023. China, which has never condemned the invasion, does not mention war and its proposal, from Kiev's perspective, favors Russia, as it does not demand the withdrawal of its troops, risking to solidify its territorial gains. Resolving the trade deficit Ukraine is not the only sensitive issue, as Europeans seek to "rebalance" their relations with a China that now has the ability, through Russian aggression, to influence the security of the Old Continent. France aims to one day reduce its trade deficit, which exceeded 40 billion euros in 2023, particularly by gaining access to the Chinese market for the export of meat and dairy products. While the signing of major contracts is not expected during Xi Jinping's visit, Paris hopes to attract investments in France in the electric vehicle sector, where Chinese companies have a clear technological advantage. However, there are also numerous contentious issues, especially at the European level. As during her visit to China in April 2023, the French president can count on the presence of Ursula Von der Leyen on Monday to address these issues. In Beijing, the President of the European Commission had taken a firmer tone compared to that of Mr. Macron, who was then keen on restoring ties weakened by the Covid-19 pandemic. This time, it seems difficult to avoid a direct confrontation regarding the European investigation into Chinese subsidies for electric vehicles, actively supported by France. While the Elysée states that this issue falls under the European Union, China's initiation of an anti-dumping investigation on brandies and cognacs in January, 96% of which come from France, shows that China holds a grudge against Paris. At a time when Brussels is increasing trade defense measures against China, a relaxation on this issue seems unlikely. "With elections looming, both in Europe and the United States, it would be unwise for Beijing to start a trade war that could fuel harder lines on both sides of the Atlantic. However, once these deadlines have passed, it is likely that Beijing will come to concretely test the determination and unity of Europeans," says François Chimits, a researcher at the Mercator Institute for China Studies (Merics). French "strategic autonomy" While relations between Brussels and Beijing are becoming increasingly tense, particularly since the Twenty-Seven referred to China as a "partner, competitor, and systemic rival" in 2019, Beijing favors capitals more open to it. Thus, China is now the largest foreign investor in Serbia, a candidate country for the EU, ensuring the construction of infrastructure there. The two partners signed a free trade agreement on the sidelines of a "Silk Road" forum in October 2023 in Beijing. At the other end of the Belgrade-Budapest railway line, being renovated by Chinese companies, Xi Jinping will then spend three days in Viktor Orban's Hungary. The illiberal leader, quick to clash with European institutions, is the only leader of the Twenty-Seven to have attended the October forum. He systematically opposes European resolutions condemning China's behavior. New infrastructure projects are expected to be concluded during Xi Jinping's visit to this country, which has become one of the main destinations for Chinese investments in the EU, thanks to the construction of battery and electric vehicle factories. Discussions are also expected to focus on the implementation of a recent bilateral agreement providing for the presence of Chinese police officers in Hungary's tourist areas. "The message sent by Xi is clear: opposition to NATO, influence struggle with Americans in Europe, and support for illiberal regimes," analyzes Marc Julienne, director of the Asia Center at the French Institute of International Relations (IFRI), in a note. In the context of this trip, it is French "strategic autonomy" that interests Beijing. "China is obviously eager for any possible criticisms from France against the United States on this or that aspect of American policy," notes François Godement, a specialist in China at the Montaigne Institute. Sharp criticisms While the Elysée claims to be able to "talk to everyone," the staging of a personal relationship with dictator Xi Jinping, reminiscent of Emmanuel Macron's invitation to Vladimir Putin to Fort de Brégançon (Var) in 2019, has sparked sharp criticisms. "Giving the impression that we are on a middle ground between China and the United States is terrible. It weakens us because our partners do not know exactly where we stand." "We may be in competition with the Americans, but we are in the same system. Over there, we are facing people who are destroying us from within," laments the (centrist) senator for French citizens living abroad, Olivier Cadic, who was a victim of a Chinese cyberattack in 2021 along with 116 other parliamentarians from different countries critical of Beijing. Beyond strategic issues, Chinese and French leaders must finally discuss human and cultural exchanges, which have suffered greatly from China's closure during the pandemic and the increasingly strict control of the Chinese society by the Communist Party. However, there are no plans regarding Chinese interference, whether in France or the rest of Europe.

title: In the United States, the House of Representatives will vote on separate bills for aid to Ukraine and Israel.
lead: After months of resistance, Republican speaker Mike Johnson promised to bring to a vote projects of assistance to Kiev and the State of Israel, desired by the White House but opposed by the most extreme faction of his party.
text_eng: After months of tug-of-war between the Biden administration and its Republican opponents, the House of Representatives will vote this week on separate projects to aid Ukraine, Israel, as well as Taiwan and other allies, announced the Republican leader of the assembly, Mike Johnson, on Monday. A $61 billion (€56 billion) package for Kiev, requested by the Democratic president, has been stalled in Congress for months, as has another $14 billion package, this time for Israel. "This week, we will consider separate bills" including those to "fund our Israeli ally" and to "support Ukraine in its war against Russian aggression," Mike Johnson stated on the social network X. The other bills are aimed at "strengthening our allies in the Indo-Pacific region" – including Taiwan – and at "adopting additional measures to counter our adversaries and strengthen our national security," he added. The passage of these bills, however, is far from guaranteed, as the speaker has only a tenuous grip on his troops, a substantial portion of whom are opposed to any aid to Ukraine. And while some Democrats refuse to send weapons to Israel, complex bipartisan coalitions will need to be formed. In order to appease his most radical wing, Mr. Johnson is also considering including a provision in the legislative package for some of the aid to be in the form of repayable loans, and for another portion to be funded by the sale of frozen Russian sovereign assets. Deep bitterness in Kiev. Published following a meeting of Republican lawmakers at the Capitol, this announcement comes two days after an unprecedented attack by Iran on Israel. The Islamic Republic fired hundreds of missiles and drones on Saturday night in retaliation for a deadly attack on April 1 against its consulate in Damascus, attributed to Israel. In this context, the Western military support that helped Israel intercept almost all of the Iranian missiles and explosive drones has caused deep bitterness in Kiev. "By defending Israel, the free world has shown that such unity is not only possible, but also 100% effective," noted Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky on Monday evening. "The same is possible to protect Ukraine," he affirmed. The day before, he had urged supporters of Kiev not to "close their eyes to the Russian missiles and drones" targeting Ukraine. By far the largest military supporter of Israel and Ukraine, the United States has not sent significant aid to Kiev since December 2023, due to the Republican blockade in Congress. A $61 billion package of military and economic assistance for Ukraine was indeed adopted by the Senate, with a Democratic majority, in February. However, Republicans in the House of Representatives are refusing to consider the bill, citing, among other reasons, another dispute over immigration. Mike Johnson is facing potential removal. Just a few months before the presidential election – scheduled for November 5 – the issue has turned into a showdown between Joe Biden and his Republican rival Donald Trump. Recently, Mike Johnson declined to comment on the future of aid to Ukraine, stating that negotiations were still ongoing. In response, Donald Trump reiterated his support for the speaker, who is facing a potential censure from the most extreme faction of the Grand Old Party (GOP). During a meeting in Miami, the two men discussed Ukraine, with the former president expressing a preference for loans rather than direct aid. Reacting to Mike Johnson's announcement, one of Donald Trump's closest allies in Congress, Marjorie Taylor Greene, immediately declared herself "firmly against the current plan." "This is a new misguided direction from the speaker," criticized the lawmaker – who initiated the censure procedure – after the meeting at the Capitol. This representative from the most extreme faction of the GOP accuses Mr. Johnson of not adequately defending conservative interests and therefore wants to remove him from his seat.

title: After Iran's attack on Israel, Russia and China are rallying behind Tehran against Washington.
lead: Moscow and Beijing, who have not condemned the Iranian attack on Israeli territory, see this crisis as an opportunity to drive a wedge between the West and the rest of the world.
text_eng: Behind the calls for restraint, issued in unison by most capitals, the Russian and Chinese diplomacies have firmly sided with Iran in the crisis that pits this country against Israel. Neither Moscow nor Beijing condemned the massive aerial attack carried out by Tehran on the night of Saturday, April 13, to Sunday, April 14. On the contrary, during a telephone conversation on Monday between the Chinese Foreign Minister, Wang Yi, and his Iranian counterpart, Hossein Amir Abdollahian, the former reiterated that "China strongly condemns and firmly opposes the attack on the consular section of the Iranian embassy in Damascus [on April 1]," according to the account of this conversation published by the official Chinese news agency Xinhua. In contrast, Beijing "takes note of Iran's statement that the action taken was limited and constituted an act of self-defense." During a call on Tuesday with his Iranian counterpart, Ebrahim Raisi, Vladimir Putin also noted Iran's willingness "to avoid further escalation." Russia's Permanent Representative to the United Nations, Vassily Nebenzia, had already mentioned the day before a "legitimate response" from Iran and preferred to emphasize the "hypocrisy" of the West on the matter. "Simona, remind me when Israel condemned the strikes by the Kiev regime on Russian territory," said Russian Foreign Ministry spokesperson Maria Zakharova to the Israeli ambassador in Moscow, Simona Galperin. The Iranian raids were even greeted with a form of euphoria in Russian propaganda channels, which first described Tehran's "victory" and Israel's "panic" before adopting the rhetoric of "limited strikes" with a simple "warning" value. "Iran has become the center of gravity of the Muslim world," said the NTV channel on Monday. A vital ally of Moscow There is more than just a gesture of goodwill to an important partner in these positions. Iran is a vital ally of Moscow in its war against Ukraine and provides drones and missiles to Russia. It is also one of Beijing's main suppliers of hydrocarbons, and China has always opposed sanctions imposed on Tehran. In mid-March, the three countries were still conducting joint military maneuvers in the Sea of Oman. The Russian and Chinese stances are primarily in line with the shift that occurred after the Hamas attacks on Israel on October 7, 2023, which both countries condemned belatedly and often in ambiguous terms. Since that date, Beijing has been keen on taking the exact opposite stance of the United States and the G7 in Middle Eastern affairs, positioning itself as the self-proclaimed spokesperson of the "Global South." It is also in the name of the confrontation with the West that Moscow has sacrificed its good relations with Israel and a regional policy marked until then by a concern for balance. The war in Ukraine is an absolute priority for Russia, and tensions in the Middle East are analyzed through this lens. They are seen as a way to drive a wedge between the West and the rest of the world, with the hope of diverting Western attention and resources away from Kiev. Russian analysts quoted in the press also suggest that an escalation could lead to an increase in energy prices, benefiting the Russian economy. However, neither Moscow nor Beijing have an interest in an uncontrolled escalation. Not only is the region vital for China's energy supplies, but there is also uncertainty about whether the Iranian regime could withstand severe shocks – a question that also applies to Damascus, Moscow's main stronghold in the region. Furthermore, a military escalation could reduce Tehran's ability to supply weapons to Moscow. The formation of a new "axis of evil" – a term coined under George W. Bush that is now being used to describe the converging interests of Moscow, Beijing, Tehran, and Pyongyang – could also work against its members, as economist Vladislav Inozemtsev writes on Telegram: "For now, discussions about such an 'axis' are purely rhetorical, and each of the issues it presents is being addressed separately. (...) This could change if Western leaders decide that these various actors constitute a single adversary."

title: Poland wishes for the emergence of a "geopolitical power" in Europe.
lead: The parliamentary address by the Minister of Foreign Affairs, Radoslaw Sikorski, six months after the victory of the Democratic coalition over the PiS populists, marks a major turning point in the country's foreign policy.
text_eng: The annual foreign policy presentation exercise was familiar to him, having practiced it seven times between 2007 and 2014, a period during which Radoslaw Sikorski was already leading Poland's diplomacy in tandem with Prime Minister Donald Tusk. Since then, a parenthesis of eight years of governance by the conservative nationalist Law and Justice party (PiS) has significantly tarnished the country's image on the international stage. The same two protagonists have returned to power. On Thursday, April 25, the Minister of Foreign Affairs presented his vision on Poland's place and the nature of the European project before the Sejm, the lower house of Parliament. Under the Tusk I and II governments, despite a desire to align with the "mainstream" of European politics, Warsaw showed a certain caution towards any idea of deep integration, viewing the European Union (EU) more as a vast market rather than a political "power Europe," as cherished by France. From this perspective, history has imposed a rupture on Europeans that Poland has finally acknowledged: in the context of the war in Ukraine, Radoslaw Sikorski emphasizes that "the EU has become a geopolitical project. Poland's role is to support this process." Rhetoric of the "decline of the West" First, it was necessary to take the time to recall the countless errors and shortcomings of his predecessors to explain how far the country has come. Mr. Sikorski listed these mistakes: chronic conflict with European institutions, the country's marginalization in international structures, deteriorating relations with key partners and neighbors (except for Hungary), deep-seated anti-German sentiment, alignment with Donald Trump's United States against the EU, and closer ties with openly pro-Russian nationalist political parties. Not to mention the rhetoric of the "decline of the West" as an ideological reference point. Mr. Sikorski also mentioned Zdzislaw Krasnodebski, a sociologist and philosopher who is the main ideologue of PiS in European affairs, who in August 2022, after the Russian invasion of Ukraine, stated that the main threat to Poland's sovereignty came "more from the West than from the East." Finally, the politicization of the civil service at the expense of competence criteria, which also affected the Ministry of Foreign Affairs, was very damaging. In mid-March, Mr. Sikorski dismissed over fifty ambassadors with a stroke of a pen, sparking controversy. Poland is thus realizing its "return to Europe" and aims to be a constructive partner in its development. For this, he must resolve an unbearable dilemma, that of the "choice between mom and dad," as it is said in the corridors of Polish ministries, that is, between the United States and the European Union. In this regard, Radoslaw Sikorski wishes to develop "strategic harmony" with Washington, a term preferred over "strategic autonomy" advocated by Paris. For a "realistic reform" of the EU. But it is in the Weimar Triangle, this platform of cooperation between Poland, France, and Germany, that he sees the political potential as an engine for European construction. The Visegrad Group, a circle comprising Poland, Hungary, the Czech Republic, and Slovakia, is relegated to managing infrastructure projects or border protection. Warsaw appreciates the change in Paris's position and its greater "sensitivity" on the Russian issue. As for Berlin, "German investments in the security of our region would be a fair correction of past mistakes," the minister affirms. Poland is ready to discuss concretely the European defense, both from an industrial and political perspective. The government of Donald Tusk supports the idea of a "rapid reaction armed force" that could consist of 5,000 to 10,000 men, tasked with responding to crises threatening the continent's interests and funded by the European budget. Regarding the debate on the reform of European institutions, in view of future enlargements, Radoslaw Sikorski clarified a previously ambiguous position by advocating for "a realistic and pragmatic reform" of the EU. "We are not convinced that a modification of the treaties is necessary, but we cannot exclude that some states may make it a condition for their consent to enlarge," he explained. Poland thus remains open to extending qualified majority voting at the expense of the right of veto. "The question remains open as to which majority voting system would be the most equitable and which areas it should cover," added the minister. Facilitating Ukraine's accession to Western structures remains a strategic priority for the country, as does aid for its reconstruction. "Poland is ready to cooperate with a non-imperial Russia that respects the rights of other nations to self-determination (...) If we have the will, Russia will lose this war. Putin's only hope lies in our lack of determination." The rivalry between China and the United States is considered "natural," but it must be conducted "peacefully." "We do not want to choose between our economic and security interests," Mr. Sikorski insists. Following this presentation, President Andrzej Duda, affiliated with PiS, stepped up to defend his party's foreign policy, denouncing a speech riddled with "manipulations" and "propaganda." During the minister's speech, the entire right side of the Hemicycle, occupied by the PiS, remained empty.

title: Why Vladimir Putin is the big winner of the Gaza war
lead: The Russian president benefits the most from Western inconsistencies and the paralysis of the United Nations, a dynamic that only a proactive revival of the peace process could reverse.
text_eng: For a quarter of a century, Vladimir Putin has been taking advantage of the mistakes made by the United States in the Middle East to systematically rebuild the power of his country in that region, as well as in Europe. As early as 2001, the Russian president justified his fierce repression of Chechen nationalism by associating it with George W. Bush's "global war on terror." Ten years later, he launched his own "war on terrorism" against the Syrian revolution with the support of the Islamic Republic of Iran. When Barack Obama refused to strike Bashar al-Assad in 2013, despite the chemical bombings in Damascus, the Kremlin leader understood that he could, six months later, go on the offensive against Ukraine, first by annexing Crimea, then by fueling the war in the Donbass region. When Putin decided to intervene directly in Syria in 2015, he deepened collaboration with Iran, particularly in the field of drones, a collaboration that proved valuable to him in 2022 during the widespread invasion of Ukraine. The war techniques developed and normalized in Syria are also devastating in Ukraine. Neutralizing Netanyahu without discouraging Iran Western leaders, however, continued to treat Europe and the Middle East as two separate theaters, even as Putin skillfully played on both fronts. Washington did not even react to the trap that eventually closed at Israel's northern border: Benjamin Netanyahu became convinced that only Moscow could contain Iran's intervention in Syria, before realizing, too late, that Israel had to act directly and now depended on Russia's approval to carry out such strikes. This unprecedented vulnerability of Israel explains its lack of military support for Ukraine since 2022, despite Kiev's urgent requests in the face of Russian waves of drone bombings of civilian infrastructure of Iranian manufacture. Nevertheless, this did not prevent Volodymyr Zelensky from displaying unwavering solidarity with Israel on October 7, 2023, after attacks by Hamas. But Netanyahu then refused to welcome him, once again not to upset Putin. The Israeli prime minister also did not dissuade his "Christian Zionist" allies in the U.S. Congress from continuing to block aid to Ukraine, while voting for exceptional assistance to Israel. The Russian army greatly benefited from this blockade for six months, intensifying its pressure on Ukraine on all fronts. At the same time, it allowed Israel to increase its strikes against Iranian targets in Syria, culminating with the bombing of the Iranian consulate in Damascus on April 1. The Iranian response on April 13 was unprecedented in scale, replicating the waves of multifaceted attacks launched against Ukraine against Israel. These strikes were fortunately thwarted by 99%, thanks to the mobilization of the United States and its allies, while Zelensky regretted that such mobilization had never been as effectively demonstrated in support of Ukraine. A diplomacy of combat Besides these remarkable tactical and operational achievements, Russia was able to position itself in the Middle East as a paradoxical champion of international law, after having long violated it in Syria and Ukraine. Moscow's propaganda had little difficulty in highlighting the "double standards" of Western discourse, as the emotions stirred by the carnage in Gaza intensified over the months. The painstaking work of Ukrainian diplomacy to popularize its cause beyond Europe, and above all in the Arab world, was thus compromised. As for American diplomacy, after blocking calls for a "truce" in Gaza at the UN Security Council, it finally rallied to the principle of a ceasefire on March 22, only to be met this time with a Russian veto against a text deemed "hypocritical." A month later, the United States found themselves alone in opposing the admission of Palestine to the UN, a veto that confirmed their diplomatic isolation. Once again, the Kremlin is quick to point out the inconsistencies of the White House in the face of the "two-state solution." Ukraine will struggle to withstand the Russian invader's onslaught if it continues to make advances in the Middle East.
