The Kremlin's latest maneuver consists, in the name of "inter-Palestinian reconciliation," in favoring the integration of Hamas into the Palestine Liberation Organization (PLO). While the Islamist movement had previously committed to supporting any diplomatic progress of the PLO, but leaving it to negotiate alone, such integration would jeopardize the revival of the Israeli-Palestinian peace process. Moreover, Moscow is counting on an Israeli offensive against Rafah, which, inevitably devastating, would further discredit Joe Biden, both domestically and internationally. Because Putin, like Netanyahu, is hopeful of seeing Donald Trump return to the White House. The only way to avoid such a catastrophic scenario for Europe is to resume talks between Israel and the PLO as soon as possible, based on the historic agreement of mutual recognition in 1993 between the nationalisms of the two peoples. As soon as possible and with determination, before it is too late.

title: "The construction of the Hindu nationalist narrative has taken on an unprecedented international dimension under Narendra Modi."
lead: If international politics is not at the heart of the elections taking place until June 1st, the Indian Prime Minister is using his image and India's position in the world to secure a third term, according to India specialist Jean-Luc Racine, in an opinion piece in "Le Monde".
text_eng: The rise of India is beginning to change the landscape of international affairs in the open electoral competition that started on April 19 and will end on June 1. The idea that foreign policy is not the main concern of voters is likely still valid, but something is changing, as the construction of the Hindu nationalist narrative has taken on an unprecedented international dimension under Narendra Modi, who has been in power for ten years. A triple electoral rhetoric is at play. The first part of the discourse acknowledges this rise in power, with India now being the most populous country in the world. In 2019, it became the fifth largest economy in the world, surpassing the UK and France. Electoral argument: give me a third term, and I will make India the third largest economy in the world – a feasible goal when comparing IMF growth forecasts for 2024: 6.8% for India, 0.9% for Japan, 0.2% for Germany. Long-term goal: India should be a developed country by 2047, the 100th anniversary of its independence. Beyond growth, it would be necessary to improve the poor human development index, which ranked India 134th globally in 2022. Nevertheless, the government tells us that India entered the auspicious times of Amrit kaal, a concept from Vedic astrology, in that year, the 75th anniversary of independence, heralding a flourishing economy. G20 Presidency The second part of the official rhetoric ties India's rise to its "civilizational ethos," finally rediscovered and allowing it to eliminate any remnants of colonialism that may have persisted in minds after independence. A portrait is painted of an India that defines itself as Bharat, its Sanskrit name, the "mother of democracies," despite the traditional caste structure; an India that aspires to be the world's guru, vishwa guru, but also the friend of the world, vishwa bandhu. The Indian presidency of the G20 exemplified this conjunction between nationalist discourse, claimed position in the world, and electoral strategy, with New Delhi having the presidency schedule changed to assume the position that ended five months before the general elections. The Sanskrit saying "The world is one family" inspired the Indian G20 motto: "One Earth, One Family, One Future." The G20 logo not only featured the lotus, the "national flower" of India, but also a symbol of the Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP), Narendra Modi's party: the globe rests on this lotus, suggesting that India carries the world... At the very least, it guides it, asserts the government: Against the effects of climate change, Narendra Modi is promoting the philosophy of lifestyle for environment, in line with his vision of Indian civilization, adding that "the world can benefit from our ancestral traditions." In his message to the nation launching the BJP's electoral program, Narendra Modi boasts: "Across the world, multiple respected voices also say that India's time has come." The third part of the speech details the global areas of action: security, diplomacy, economy, and soft power. In terms of security, the focus is on combating terrorism and protecting borders. On the latter point, the government asserts, against all evidence, that "not an inch has been yielded" during the severe clashes in 2020 between Chinese and Indian troops in Ladakh, along the disputed Line of Actual Control with China. The main opposition party, the Congress Party, accuses the Prime Minister of "lying and sleeping" on this issue. "Voice of the Global South" In the diplomatic field, the government takes credit for the joint declaration of the G20, which was long uncertain due to Ukraine. Russia is not mentioned, but Modi's remark to Putin in 2022 is reiterated: "This must not be the time for war." India does not impose sanctions on Russia, but the United States and Europe (including France) court it. India also aims to be a "voice of the Global South," one of its leaders, as emphasized in the program: "We have demonstrated India's independent thinking and action for the good of humanity." As for foreign entities, official or unofficial, that mention the country's democratic weakening, the Minister of Foreign Affairs responds by denouncing in these critics "bad habits" inherited from colonial times. The manufacturing sector accounts for only 14% of India's GDP? No matter: Modi "guarantees" (the key word of the electoral program) that India will be "a global industrial hub" in many sectors, including areas where much work is needed, such as semiconductors. Currently, the quest for investments and energy needs govern a foreign policy that strengthens ties with Gulf countries, which the Indian Prime Minister has visited several times, even as his election campaign now goes as far as accusing the opposition of wanting to rob Hindus for the benefit of Muslims. In addition to the realpolitik implemented in power relations and the advocacy for a more balanced multilateralism in favor of the South, Narendra Modi's India celebrates its soft power, globalized through yoga and Ayurvedic medicine, through the International Solar Alliance, and through "the pharmacy of the world" which, more than the West, has distributed Covid-19 vaccines to countries in the South. A global network of Thiruvalluvar cultural centers will also be built – Thiruvalluvar being a Tamil sage born over two thousand years ago – as a way to counter the Confucius centers promoted by China. Ultimately, beyond the delicate socio-economic issues, many voters will decide based on the overall image they have of the regime, and even more so of Narendra Modi, who emphasizes the need for a strong mandate to deal with international tensions. Nearly two-thirds of them, according to a credible survey by the Centre for the Study of Developing Societies, believed in 2023 that India's influence in the world had increased under Modi. A third term, if confirmed, should further intensify the weight of Hindu nationalism domestically. In the field of foreign policy, continuity is expected to prevail, to increase power by playing on the contradictions of the world's disorder.

title: Video: Ukraine: A triple Russian strike on a major city in the North has killed at least 17 people.
lead: Following the bombing of Chernihiv, Volodymyr Zelensky stated that Ukraine did not have enough air defenses to protect itself.
text_eng: At least seventeen people were killed, and over sixty injured, in a triple Russian strike on Wednesday in Chernihiv, a large city in northern Ukraine. The death toll may rise, as "people are likely still trapped under the rubble of the partially destroyed building," authorities warned at midday. Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky argued that Ukraine lacked sufficient air defenses to prevent this attack, which is likely the deadliest in this historic city located about fifty kilometers from the border with Belarus, a Russian ally, and about a hundred kilometers north of Kiev. As Western aid, especially from the United States, dwindles, Ukraine desperately urges its partners to deliver more weapons and air defense systems.

title: In Georgia, thousands of protesters against the "foreign influence" law were dispersed by the police.
lead: The Prime Minister had warned against any disturbances before a festive and musical gathering in front of Parliament. The protesters were removed before the third reading of this law inspired by Russia.
text_eng: Once again, Georgian and European flags joyfully flew in the night from Sunday, May 12, to Monday, May 13 in Tbilisi, where thousands of protesters had gathered near the Parliament in a festive atmosphere, despite the cold and rain. A stage and a huge sound system had been set up in front of the building by the organizers of the demonstration, much to the delight of the participants who danced, sang, and chanted slogans for much of the night. Determined to occupy the space permanently, the protesters planned to block access to the building for the deputies expected on Monday morning at 9 am to review, in a third reading, the Russian-inspired law on "foreign influence" deemed contrary to Georgia's European aspirations. But at dawn, as the ranks of the protesters had thinned out, significant police forces appeared, ordering those present to leave the area and clear the entrances to the Parliament. The police quickly managed to disperse the protesters without using force, but without needing to resort to tear gas and water cannons. Earlier on Sunday, Prime Minister Irakli Kobakhidze had warned against any disturbances. "I want to warn members of radical opposition groups that they will be held accountable for their violent actions before the law," he warned, while his Interior Minister, Vakhtang Gomelauri, promised "a sentence of up to four years in prison" for those attempting to block the Parliament's entrances, of which there were three. "Tomorrow or the day after, we will adopt this law," assured the Prime Minister during an information meeting in the capital, mentioning a support of over 61% of the population for the text, according to a survey that no one had heard of until now. The protesters, he estimated, should not abuse their right to protest. Mr. Kobakhidze also emphasized that the legislation being adopted aimed to increase transparency regarding the funding sources of non-governmental organizations and media. "Pseudo-elite fed by a foreign country" The protesters do not believe a word of it, convinced that the law has only one purpose: to silence civil society, as is the case in Russia where a similar legislative text, adopted in 2012, has slowly but surely stigmatized all critical voices of Vladimir Putin's regime, labeled as "foreign agents." In recent days, Russian lawmakers have further tightened the screws by depriving those recognized as "agents" of the right to be elected, according to a list updated weekly in Moscow. "I know very well the effects of this law, I can precisely tell you what will happen here if it is adopted," explains Anna, a young Russian refugee in Tbilisi since the beginning of the invasion of Ukraine in February 2022. Like her, thousands of opponents of Putin's regime are trying to rebuild their lives in Georgia, especially in Tbilisi, where the walls are covered with anti-Kremlin and pro-Ukrainian slogans: "F... Russia", "F... Putin", "Russians out", "Glory to Ukraine". The police are trying to disperse the protesters gathered near the Parliament in the center of Tbilisi on Monday, May 13, 2024. The European Union and the United States, the main funders of this country of 3.7 million inhabitants, have warned the Georgian authorities of their aversion to the "Russian law", as it is nicknamed here. This did not prevent Bidzina Ivanichvili, founder and honorary president of the ruling party Georgian Dream, the strongman of Georgia, from defending the text with conviction, calling its detractors "pseudo-elite fed by a foreign country". The government's obsession with tracking foreign influences is surprising given that most senior Georgian officials own apartments in European capitals, place their assets in foreign banks, send their children to study in Europe or the United States. Mr. Ivanichvili himself has held a French passport since 2009 and his fortune, estimated at $4.9 billion (4.5 billion euros), has not been invested in Georgia. The "anti-Western rhetoric" of the authorities puts Georgia on a "precarious trajectory," warned the U.S. State Department earlier this month. James C. O'Brien, its Deputy Assistant Secretary for European and Eurasian Affairs, is expected in the Georgian capital on Tuesday, May 14, for discussions with the government and the opposition. "Focus on the elections" Protesters can also count on the support of the pro-European President Salomé Zourabichvili, who is now in conflict with Georgian Dream, which had her elected in 2018. On Sunday, the president invited the protesters to thwart any "provocations" that may be planned by the hotheads of the ruling party. "Stay very cautious, which does not mean you should be afraid," she said. "The important thing now is to focus on the elections in October," she told Le Monde on Saturday in an interview. Busy preparing for the day after, she said she is currently in talks with the opposition to set up a "platform bringing together democratic and pro-European forces." Zourabichvili is convinced that young Georgians, known to be apolitical and non-voters, "will go to the polls in October." The main driving force behind the protest, the youth may have lost a battle, but refuses to give up. Thus, students from several universities in Tbilisi, Batumi, as well as some of their professors, have announced that they will go on strike starting Monday.

title: "To make Russia pay: faced with the suffering of Ukrainians, Westerners have made it a political mantra... but have proceeded slowly."
lead: After six months of blockages, the American Congress has just voted for a support plan for Ukraine on Saturday, April 20th. However, the lack of money to finance Kiev's rearmament is due to a lack of global political will, according to Jean-Michel Bezat, an economic journalist at "Le Monde," in his column.
text_eng: In the trenches of Ukraine, under the heavy fire of Russian cannons, soldiers have more immediate concerns than the ongoing negotiations in Washington. But what played out in the American Congress on Saturday, April 20, did not leave them indifferent. After six months of Republican obstruction, the House of Representatives adopted a $61 billion (57 billion euros) plan to support Ukraine. This will allow Ukraine to renew its military equipment and purchase the ammunition that it sorely lacks in the face of the superiority of Russian firepower. What had been discussed two days earlier, on the sidelines of the World Bank and International Monetary Fund spring meeting on April 17 and 18, concerns them just as much: the use of the $300 billion from the Russian Central Bank, frozen since the beginning of the war in February 2022. This scenario gained credibility, and the plan voted on at the Capitol authorizes the United States to sell Russian assets to finance the reconstruction of Ukraine. The $61 billion "package" may make a dip into Russian funds less urgent, but it does not rule out hitting Moscow in the wallet. "Make Russia pay!" Faced with the suffering and needs of Ukrainians, Western countries have made it a political mantra... but they have proceeded slowly. While the shortage of ammunition can be explained by insufficient industrial capacity, the lack of money to rearm Kyiv was due to a lack of political will. Until the situation changed in early 2024: faced with the risk of a Ukrainian defeat and the paralysis of Congress, Europeans were seized with a sense of urgency. Graduated response The use of Russia's sovereign assets – excluding the assets of oligarchs – is all the more justified, according to its supporters, as it has reignited a campaign of massive destruction of critical infrastructure such as power plants and electrical grids to undermine the economy. Despite the courage and ingenuity of Ukrainians, the gross domestic product (GDP) is 25% lower than it was before the war. However, the financial response from Western countries is graduated, with Europeans being more cautious than Anglo-Saxons. The former are the most exposed. After the annexation of Crimea in 2014, Moscow reportedly transferred a large portion of its liquidity to Euroclear, thinking it would be safer in Brussels than in New York. Thus, the Belgian financial company ended up with the perilous management of $200 billion. The Twenty-Seven emerged from their wait-and-see approach at the end of March to agree on the use of the interest from the invested capital (around $3 billion per year). These gains would not be reinvested in favor of the Russian Central Bank, but rather paid to the European Peace Facility, the mechanism for reimbursing states that purchase military equipment for Kyiv. Ursula von der Leyen, President of the European Commission, believes that 1 billion could be granted as early as July. Other solutions have been considered, such as Ukraine issuing a bond backed by Russian assets, but with a high risk of default by the country. Another option is for the G7 countries to discuss this at their mid-June summit to be held at the Borgo Egnazia Hotel in Savelletri di Fasano, in Puglia, Italy. An unprecedented decision remains the nuclear option of confiscation. Treasury Secretary Janet Yellen and British leaders are no longer ruling it out. As early as 2023, former senior US officials like Democrat Larry Summers and Republican Robert Zoellick argued that there are "moral and legal arguments" to do so, citing a US law from 1977. The European Central Bank fears that using this drastic measure could undermine the credibility of the euro, as 20% of global savings are held in the European currency (and 60% in dollars). French Economy Minister Bruno Le Maire states that there is "no legal basis" for confiscation. Emmanuel Macron is concerned that it could "weaken Europe." Germany is equally hesitant, aiming to uphold the principle of sovereign immunity, avoid alarming "third" countries, and prevent destabilizing "international financial stability," as warned by its Economy Minister Christian Lindner at the latest International Monetary Fund meeting. These "third" countries include powerful nations like China, India, Brazil, and the Gulf petro-monarchies. However, there is no indication that they would be deterred from placing their reserves in Europe and the United States. The decision would be unprecedented in history. Reparations have always been demanded after a war, and by the victors. Even if Ukraine regains its territorial integrity, Russia will not be defeated like Germany in 1918 or Iraq in 1991, which was ordered by the United Nations to pay $52 billion, mainly to Kuwait. Russia will remain a nuclear superpower and one of the five permanent members of the UN Security Council. Kremlin spokesperson Dmitry Peskov has warned Europeans of the "damage that such decisions could cause to their economy, their image, and their reputation as reliable guarantors of property inviolability." Regardless of Moscow's violations of international law and human rights, the "voice of Putin" knows that a confiscation would be condemned by countries in the global South. Most of them have refused to ostracize Russia, and some are even actively involved in circumventing Western sanctions. The fate of Russia's $300 billion "treasure" could also be discussed as part of a peace agreement, which is unlikely in the short term. But the cost of just the destruction of buildings and infrastructure is increasing and now amounts to 152 billion dollars, with the cost of reconstruction at 486 billion, more than Ukraine's GDP, according to the World Bank. How could Russia escape its responsibilities?

title: European elections: "Jordan Bardella has become the king of evasion"
lead: In light of the political influence claimed by the National Rally, the evasiveness of its lead candidate regarding its European project comes across as an admission of weakness, analyzed in her column by Françoise Fressoz, editorialist at "Le Monde".
text_eng: In every election campaign, there is a champion of avoidance. During the 2022 presidential election, much criticism was directed at candidate Macron for hiding behind the war in Ukraine to avoid entering the electoral arena, saying as little as possible about his projects and vision because it could unbalance his "both/and" approach and cause him to lose voters on both sides. At the time, his strategy proved successful: the incumbent president was reelected by taking full advantage of the elevated position his role provided. However, the two years that followed showed how costly a rushed campaign can be in terms of losing momentum and increasing dissent. This time, the man practicing avoidance is Jordan Bardella. The president of the National Rally (RN), who leads his party's list in the European elections on June 9, is not seeking to legitimize his project, which involves being both in and against, advocating for the "Europe of nations" but wanting to turn Frontex into an agency to combat illegal immigration, denying any submission to Vladimir Putin's Russia but refusing to vote in the European Parliament for any aid to Ukraine. Since launching his campaign on March 3 in Marseille, the far-right party's lead candidate has behaved as if saying as little as possible about European issues is the best way to avoid problems. Three times, Jordan Bardella has refused to debate with his opponents. On Thursday, April 25, he left a press conference he had called to counter Emmanuel Macron's without engaging in a question-and-answer session with journalists, claiming that the President had not deigned to do so. A few days later, he cited health concerns to erase the negative impression left by the incident. Becoming the king of evasion, Jordan Bardella also practices the art of shifting blame. It is still unclear what he plans to do with his cumbersome German ally in the European Parliament, the AfD, whose lead candidate is under scrutiny by the justice system for his ties to China and Russia. For someone claiming to want to govern the country one day, there is nothing very reassuring about this; for someone claiming to respect the people, there is also nothing very engaging. Anti-Macron Referendum One could attribute Jordan Bardella's avoidance tactics to his youth. At 28 years old, one cannot master everything, and that is normal: from "no to the euro" to "yes, but," the RN's European doctrine has fluctuated so much in recent years that it can be confusing. However, one could just as easily invoke hubris, the feeling of omnipotence that eventually seizes a politician and leads them to a position of superiority. First candidate at the age of 23, party leader at 25, a star on the social network TikTok with over a million followers, the ambitious man from the RN has a few reasons to have a big head, but that's not it either. Jordan Bardella avoids developing the European theme as much as possible because that's not where his momentum lies. It is on the national level. The RN candidate has "nationalized" his campaign to the maximum, turning it into an anti-Macron referendum. He is calling for a dissolution the day after the election, designed to be the ultimate springboard before the big shift in 2027. And, so far, the gamble is paying off beyond his expectations. His list is leading in the polls with 32% in the latest Ipsos survey for Le Monde, 15 points ahead of the majority party. Electoral experts make the same observation: starting from a high base, the RN is strengthening in its popular strongholds and expanding towards executives and retirees. Its momentum is fueled by dissatisfaction with purchasing power, the poor functioning of the healthcare system, the sense of insecurity, and rejection of immigration. "The country is overwhelmed by fears, hatreds, resentments that have been expressed in a violent series of events and an apocalyptic debate totally dominated by the identity obsessions of the far right," laments the former first secretary of the Socialist Party, Jean-Christophe Cambadélis, on his blog Nouvelle Société, pointing out the dangers of a "silo democracy" that is developing thanks to social networks and the increasingly evident polarization of the media system. Lack of influence As long as the national momentum benefits him, Jordan Bardella has no reason to change his stance. It allows him to embody the useful vote and to surpass those who, to the right of the political spectrum, seek to represent the alternation: the lists led by François-Xavier Bellamy (The Republicans) and by Marion Maréchal (Reconquête!) are currently marginalized, making it uncertain whether these two parties will have a say in the 2027 presidential debate. The more the RN rises on the national stage, the more its refusal to develop its European project appears as a sign of weakness. It reflects its lack of influence on the European stage and the few prospects available to it over the next five years. In a recent note, the Montaigne Institute points out that, even in the event of a surge of national populist parties on June 9, the divisions among them are such, especially on Ukraine or the Atlantic Alliance, that the creation of a coalition seems unlikely. Furthermore, the only two nationalist currents that hold weight and govern in Europe - those of Giorgia Meloni in Italy and Viktor Orban in Hungary - form a competing party in the European Parliament, de facto limiting the possibilities of influence and support that the leaders of the RN claim. If, for this party, changing life means changing Europe, there is an inconsistency, not to say an imposture.

title: Eurovision 2024 overshadowed by the conflict in Gaza, Dutch contestant disqualified
lead: The Swedish police estimate that up to 20,000 people could demonstrate on Saturday in Malmö against the participation of Israeli singer Eden Golan. Dutch contestant Joost Klein was excluded following a complaint filed by a female member of the production team.
text_eng: The Eurovision final will take place on Saturday, May 11th in the evening in Malmö, Sweden, amidst tension due to the conflict in Gaza and protests against the participation of the Israeli contestant in the competition. Police reinforcements have come from all over Scandinavia, as well as from Denmark and Norway, to ensure the security of the event, which is expected to draw nearly 100,000 fans from eighty countries. Following a first demonstration organized in Malmö on Thursday, May 9th, around sixty associations, gathered under the collective "Stoppa Israël nu" ("Stop Israel now"), called for a second gathering today to protest against Israel's participation in Eurovision. Starting from Stortorget, the main square of Malmö, the protesters (between 8,000 and 10,000, according to the police) are now arriving at their final destination in Mölle Park, east of the city, where a strong police presence is visible. Like on Thursday, the demonstration has been peaceful. Many families are present, coming from all over the region. There were many keffiyehs and Palestinian flags in the streets of Sweden's third-largest city, which has the largest Palestinian community in the country. Researchers at the medical faculty of Lund University residing in Helsingborg, Hani Alsatadi and Neserin Ali, both of Palestinian origin, came to protest with their two children aged 3 and 1. Hani, who still has relatives in the northern Gaza Strip, has lost several family members in the bombings. They denounce the "guilty silence" of Western governments and are pleased to see the mobilization of the people of Malmö: "It is a diverse city and those who are here today represent it perfectly," they told Le Monde. Israeli singer Eden Golan, 20 years old, secured her spot in the final on Thursday night with the song "Hurricane," whose original version had to be modified as it was considered to allude to the Hamas attack that bloodied Israel on October 7th. The Dutch contestant, Joost Klein, who had been suspended the day before, was excluded from the Eurovision final on Saturday. According to the European Broadcasting Union (EBU), his suspension is not related to the conflict in Gaza. "The Swedish police investigated a complaint filed by a female member of the production team following an incident that occurred after his performance during Thursday night's semi-final," the EBU explained in a statement. "While the legal process is ongoing, it would not be appropriate for him to continue participating in the competition." The Dutch broadcaster Avrotros deemed the disqualification of the singer "disproportionate," in a statement sent to Agence France-Presse, expressing shock at the decision. "We deeply regret it and will revisit the matter later," the statement added. Initially, twenty-six countries were supposed to compete on Saturday to succeed Sweden as the winner of this competition, which was watched by 162 million viewers in 2023. Calls for boycott A protest against Israel's presence at the 68th edition of the Eurovision Song Contest in central Malmö, Sweden, on May 11, 2024. LEONHARD FOEGER / REUTERS The European Broadcasting Union (EBU), which oversees this major music event, confirmed in March the participation of Eden Golan despite criticisms. More recently, nine of the participants, seven of whom are in the final, have called for a lasting ceasefire in the Gaza Strip, where Israel is carrying out military operations. Israel has been participating in Eurovision since 1973, winning it for the fourth time in 2018. "It's truly an honor to be here (...), to proudly represent ourselves," said the Israeli contestant on Thursday, who was ranked second among the favorites behind Croatia on Saturday morning. On Friday, the far-left Spanish party Sumar - whose leader Yolanda Diaz is the third-ranking official in the government - launched a petition to demand Israel's exclusion from the final "at a time when its troops are exterminating the Palestinian people and destroying the entire region." Berlin responded by stating that "calls for boycotts against the participation of Israeli artists" were "completely unacceptable," while Paris emphasized that "politics have no place at Eurovision." Disrupted neutrality The neutrality claimed by the EBU is being challenged like never before. On Tuesday, Swedish singer Eric Saade appeared with a Palestinian keffiyeh wrapped around his arm. In Belgium, the unions of the Flemish public television channel VRT briefly interrupted the broadcast of the semi-final on Thursday evening to air a message condemning "human rights violations by the State of Israel." This action was regretted by the EBU, which had banned Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky from speaking at the contest last year in the name of political neutrality. This year, the war in Ukraine has been overshadowed by the conflict in Gaza. On Thursday, nearly 12,000 people, including climate activist Greta Thunberg, had already protested in Malmö against Israel's participation. Before the semi-final, Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu had stated that Eden Golan had "already won," praising her in a video message for successfully facing "a terrible wave of antisemitism." "I believe everyone is safe," Eden Golan said at a press conference. Swedish police assured that "there was no threat directed at Eurovision." Last summer, Sweden raised its terrorist threat level after acts of desecration of the Quran. On the festive side, the 2024 edition offers a wide range of music genres, from ballads to electro. Malmö, the third largest city in Sweden, hopes to offer fans "the time of their life," as Abba sang, who had brought the Eurovision crown to the country half a century ago. This year, many songs "address mental health - many young artists say they do not feel well and struggle with their identity," as is the case with Nemo (Switzerland), explains Andreas Önnefors, a specialist of the contest. Eden Golan assured that her song "Hurricane" is about "a young girl going through her own problems, her own emotions." France, which placed 16th in 2023, is represented this year by the singer Slimane, who will perform the song "Mon amour."

title: European Union: "The great enlargement of 2004 marked the beginning of a long process of reunification of the Old Continent."
lead: Twenty years later, the integration of ten new countries mostly from the former Eastern bloc remains one of the most unexpected and structuring successes of the European Union, as highlighted by Philippe Ricard, a journalist at "Le Monde", in his column.
text_eng: The anniversary went almost unnoticed, as the continent is shaken by the return of war on its soil. The large-scale invasion of Ukraine by Russia has the potential to overshadow one of the most unexpected and structuring successes of the European Union: the great enlargement of 2004, which has just celebrated its 20th anniversary. The lingering conflict underscores the importance of this event. On May 1, 2004, ten countries, including three former Soviet republics and most of the former Eastern Bloc states, joined the European Union: Poland, Hungary, the Czech Republic, Slovakia, Slovenia, Estonia, Latvia, Lithuania, as well as Cyprus and Malta, completed a long accession process that began in the aftermath of the end of the Cold War. Romania and Bulgaria followed in 2007. In these countries, the enthusiasm is real, while the old members harbor ambivalent feelings. Supported by the United States, this major enlargement confirms the victory of Western Europe over the defunct communist regimes once under the tutelage of the now dismantled Soviet Union. However, it also marks the beginning of a long, and sometimes painful, process of reunification of the Old Continent, following that of Germany, as the "old" and "new" Europe initially struggled to rediscover each other. Just remember the divisions caused by the war in Iraq: in 2003, the candidate countries to the Union, also NATO applicants, rallied behind the United States to overthrow Saddam Hussein, against the advice of France and Germany. "They missed a good opportunity to keep quiet," former President Jacques Chirac quipped at a European summit in Brussels: "These countries were both, let's say it, not very well-mannered and somewhat unaware of the dangers of too quickly aligning with the American position," the French president added at the time. Economic catch-up This outburst not only caught the entourage of the former head of state off guard, but also permanently altered relations between the "new members" and France, which had long been very reserved about this acceleration of history. Two years later, in 2005, the controversy over the "Polish plumber," accused of "social dumping," would, in France, lead to the victory of the "no" vote in the referendum on the European Constitution. It highlighted both the risks of competition between two Europes with often disparate living standards and the spectacular economic catch-up initiated by these countries. Two decades later, the great enlargement is not fully digested, although exchanges have significantly deepened among the Twenty-Seven. Cultural and political differences remain pronounced between "old" and "new" members - terms that have fortunately fallen into disuse - as shown over the past fifteen years in debates on migration issues. Few Central European states have also joined the euro and the monetary union, which is considered the ultimate step in European integration. Some, starting with Viktor Orban's Hungary, are instead accused of illiberal drifts and violations of the rule of law. However, Poland demonstrates that this trend is not irreversible. Since the return to power of Donald Tusk in December 2023, after the defeat of the previous ultraconservative and Eurosceptic majority, Warsaw has made it a point to redouble its European efforts: its very Atlanticist Minister of Foreign Affairs, Radoslaw Sikorski, has recently argued for making Europe a "geopolitical power." This discourse echoes calls for "Europe as a power" made by Emmanuel Macron. The influence now claimed by Poland gives an idea of the weight that Central European countries are likely to have, especially since the outbreak of the war in Ukraine. The invasion led by Russia has indeed validated their strong stance against the Russian threat, while seriously undermining the authority of Berlin and Paris, accused of being lenient towards Vladimir Putin's Kremlin. A gap that Emmanuel Macron seeks to overcome: "We also missed an opportunity to listen to you," he said in Bratislava almost a year ago, to counter the distant remarks of Jacques Chirac: "That time is over." The war in Ukraine has also changed the nature of enlargement by accentuating its geopolitical dimension. This issue is not new, as the integration of post-communist countries has gone hand in hand with the enlargement of NATO in the post-Cold War years. It is now a stated objective to curb the imperialist threat from Russia. Ukraine, Moldova, and the Western Balkans are candidates, as well as Georgia, which is torn between its European aspirations, with the government playing the anti-Western and pro-Russian card. "The revival of the integration process is clearly guided by the situation in Ukraine, which raises an important question: to what extent can enlargement bring security to neighboring states while strengthening the security of EU member states?" observes Piotr Buras, head of the Polish office of the European Council on Foreign Relations. In Kiev, as in Warsaw, Brussels, Berlin, or Paris, the answer is now self-evident.

title: What is included in the $95 billion aid package to Ukraine, Israel, and Taiwan approved by the US House of Representatives
lead: All these projects must return to the Senate, which is majority Democrat, sometime next week before potentially being signed into law by the President of the United States, Joe Biden.
text_eng: After long and difficult negotiations, the American House of Representatives adopted, on Saturday, April 20, an important aid package for Ukraine, Israel, and Taiwan, supported by lawmakers from both sides. American lawmakers voted for this massive package of over 95 billion dollars (89 billion euros), which had been requested for months by the President of the United States, Joe Biden. $60.8 billion in aid for Ukraine The text on Ukraine includes 60.8 billion dollars (57 billion euros) to assist Kiev in its fight against Russia. In the chamber, lawmakers waved Ukrainian flags in a show of support for Kiev, amidst jeers from Trumpist lawmakers. Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky, whose country is at war with Russia, immediately praised the assistance as something that "will save thousands and thousands of lives." Such aid would allow the Ukrainian army to catch its breath in this high-intensity conflict that has exhausted many of its fighters and dampened the morale of its troops. On Friday, the Ukrainian president had alerted UN member countries during a video conference speech. The Ukrainian army "can no longer wait" in the face of Russian pressure, he declared, requesting at least seven additional Patriot anti-aircraft systems as well as an acceleration of deliveries of all kinds of military equipment. The United States is the main military supporter of Kiev, but Congress has not approved a large aid package for its ally in nearly a year and a half, mainly due to partisan disputes. This aid will be distributed throughout the year 2023, but it has dried up. The text presented on Wednesday includes nearly 14 billion dollars to train, equip, and pay the Ukrainian army. Around 10 billion dollars dedicated to economic assistance for the energy and infrastructure sectors will be sent in the form of a loan. The idea was suggested by Donald Trump, who believes that the United States should "stop giving money without expecting to be repaid." However, this debt can be forgiven. A large part of the package will also be used to replenish the stocks of the American army and will return to armament factories in the United States. This aid package also authorizes President Biden to confiscate and sell Russian assets to finance the reconstruction of Ukraine. Democratic President Joe Biden and his party in Congress are very supportive of new aid for Ukraine. However, Republicans, led by Donald Trump, are increasingly reluctant to finance a conflict that is dragging on. In the midst of an election year, the issue has turned into a remote duel between the two presidential candidates. After months of deliberation, Republican House leader Mike Johnson finally lent his support to the $61 billion package for Ukraine. "To be frank: I'd rather send ammunition to Ukraine than send our boys to fight," he argued, with some emotion, during a press conference. Thirteen billion dollars in military aid to Israel The plan being debated in Congress includes $13 billion in military aid to Israel, a historic ally of the United States, currently at war with Hamas. These funds will be used to strengthen the Iron Dome, Israel's missile defense system. Over $9 billion is also allocated to "address the urgent need for humanitarian aid in Gaza and other vulnerable populations around the world," according to a summary of the text. The project, however, prohibits direct U.S. funding to the United Nations Relief and Works Agency for Palestinian Refugees, UNRWA. Israel accuses some of its employees of involvement in the October 7 attack carried out by Hamas. Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu praised the aid as "defending Western civilization," while the Palestinian presidency described the assistance voted in favor of Israel as an "aggression against the Palestinian people." This money risks "resulting in thousands of Palestinian casualties in the Gaza Strip" and in the West Bank, said Nabil Abu Rudeina, spokesperson for Palestinian Authority President Mahmoud Abbas, denouncing a "dangerous escalation." Eight billion dollars to counter China As requested by Joe Biden, this text allocates over $8 billion to counter China militarily by investing in submarines and economically by competing with major Chinese projects in developing countries. Several billion dollars are allocated to Taiwan, an island of 23 million inhabitants that China considers one of its provinces. Threat of TikTok ban The bill also includes a provision to ban TikTok in the United States if the social network does not sever ties with its parent company, ByteDance, and more broadly with China. TikTok has been closely monitored by U.S. authorities for several months, with many officials believing that the short video and entertainment platform allows Beijing to spy on and manipulate its 170 million users in the United States. All these projects are expected to return to the Senate next week before potentially being signed into law by Joe Biden. The upper chamber could consider the texts as early as Tuesday, according to Senate Democratic leader Chuck Schumer. The president's spokesperson, Karine Jean-Pierre, announced on Friday that the flow of American aid to Ukraine would resume "immediately" after the adoption of this bill by both chambers of Congress.

title: Russia is increasing its presence in Libya, much to the dismay of Western countries.
lead: Moscow is increasing the deployment of troops and equipment in the region, thus strengthening an influence that could impact migration flows to Europe.
text_eng: Piece by piece, the strategic edifice outlined by Moscow in North Africa is solidifying. Russian presence in Libya, already tangible since 2019 in the form of paramilitary units (former Wagner Group), has experienced a sudden acceleration since the beginning of the year that has not gone unnoticed by Western powers feeling powerless. "Russia has been transferring military personnel and Russian fighters to Libya for the past three months," concludes a note published on Friday, May 10 by All Eyes on Wagner. This international investigative collective on Russian networks in Africa adds that "the delivery of equipment and military vehicles from Syria to Libya constitutes the most visible aspect of [this] increased involvement." Citing the figure of 1,800 Russians now deployed across the country, All Eyes on Wagner notes that two Moscow naval vessels - landing ships - departed from the Syrian naval base of Tartous and arrived on April 8 at the port of Tobruk in Cyrenaica. Supported by photos, the investigative collective asserts that vehicles and weapons, such as 2S12 Sani mortars or BTR and BM armored transport vehicles, were unloaded. This would be "the fifth delivery" of this kind to Tobruk in forty-five days. Western diplomatic sources consulted by Le Monde confirm this increased Russian presence in this pivotal state in North Africa, a junction between the Mashreq and the Maghreb. "The increase concerns more equipment than personnel," however, nuances a diplomat from a European state. These recent movements in Libya, he adds, are part of "a broader Russian breakthrough" aimed at "installing pro-Moscow governments everywhere in East and West Africa. The only missing piece is Chad to divide Africa in two." Chad itself is also the subject of eager Russian interests. Projection platform If Libya is crucial in this African offensive, it is because it serves as a platform for projecting equipment and personnel to neighboring states: war-torn Sudan, Niger, and Mali - and further south Burkina Faso - all led by Kremlin-friendly juntas, and potentially Chad. The strategic hub of this new setup is located in Jufra, a Libyan district 350 kilometers south of the Gulf of Sirte, where equipment and personnel arrive from Tobruk before being redirected to the regional theaters coveted by Moscow. The major development is that "the Russian state is no longer afraid to show its direct involvement in Libya," notes Jalel Harchaoui, an associate researcher at the Royal United Services Institute for Defence and Security Studies. When Wagner entered Libya in 2019 by supporting the self-proclaimed Libyan National Army (LNA) of the dissident Marshal Khalifa Haftar - the "strongman" of Cyrenaica (East) - in his failed assault on Tripoli, Moscow was in official denial. This era is over as the Russian Deputy Minister of Defense, Yunus-bek Evkurov, has already made four visits to Benghazi, the political and military base of Khalifa Haftar, since August 2023. This Libyan marshal, who has established a parallel authority in Cyrenaica to the government in Tripoli, himself traveled to Moscow at the end of September, where he met with Vladimir Putin. Since then, the Russian presence has become more noticeable as the African branch of the former Wagner mercenary group, decapitated by the disappearance in August 2023 of its leader Yevgeny Prigozhin, was taken over by the Ministry of Defense in Moscow under the new label of Africa Corps. Political challenge Faced with this Russian push in Libya, alarm bells are ringing in Western chancelleries, which are vainly seeking a response. In addition to the risk of Moscow's influence spreading in the regional periphery, the United States and Europe are confronted with two challenges. The first is the potential establishment of a Russian military presence on the coast in the form of a naval base, in Tobruk or Sirte, which would pose a direct threat to NATO forces in the Mediterranean. Sirte would represent a nightmare scenario for Western powers as the city, located at the juncture of Cyrenaica (East) and Tripolitania (West), is only 600 kilometers from the coast of Sicily. The idea of a Russian base in Sirte is actually an old plan that Moscow had failed to impose on Muammar Gaddafi in the years 2009-2010. The city is now under the control of Marshal Haftar, under whose shadow Russian paramilitaries are locally active during military maneuvers alongside the LNA, as noted by researchers from All Eyes on Wagner. During an exercise on March 16, Russian Pantsir anti-aircraft systems were displayed at the Ghardabiya air base, on the outskirts of Sirte. The other challenge is more political. Beyond its military presence in Cyrenaica (Tobruk, Sirte) and in southern Fezzan (Tamanhint, Brak Al-Shati), Moscow is engaging in diplomatic activities on all fronts, including in Tripoli - a city that is supposed to be under Turkish influence - where its embassy reopened at the end of February. The new ambassador, Aydar Aghanin, fluent in Arabic, is holding numerous meetings with Libyan political figures of all affiliations. By combining military assets and diplomatic capital, Russia is accumulating resources for the future. "Moscow is likely to establish itself as a mediator, the preferred interlocutor of Libyan factions," anticipates Jalel Harchaoui. Even worse, as migration flows from Sudan and Niger pass through regions controlled by Marshal Haftar, and therefore de facto by Russian paramilitaries, Moscow is strengthening its position vis-à-vis the European Union in the long run.

title: During his inauguration, Vladimir Putin solidifies the confrontation with the West for the long term.
lead: "We look towards the future with confidence," declared the Russian president as he began his fifth term in the Kremlin, during a ceremony attended by the French ambassador.
text_eng: "Together, we will prevail." By dedicating the first and last words of his inauguration speech to war on Tuesday, May 7, Vladimir Putin firmly placed his new mandate, the fifth in the Kremlin, under the sign of confrontation, not with Ukraine, too insignificant to even be mentioned, but with the West, whose "policy of aggression" will not prevent Russia from becoming "even stronger." Re-elected on March 17 with officially 87.28% of the vote, the Russian leader greeted, on Tuesday, from the first seconds, the "heroes participating in the special military operation," some of whom were among the approximately 3,500 guests - members of the elite gathered under the gilding of the presidential palace, as well as ambassadors from foreign countries, including that of France. Just as quickly, he mentioned "our historical lands that have defended the right to join their homeland," in reference to the Ukrainian regions annexed by force in March 2014 and September 2022. Even though this meticulously organized and lavish ceremony leaves little room for improvisation, some developments are notable. While Mr. Putin made a point, during his early terms, of mentioning the country's democratic development and the rights of citizens, hand on the Constitution, these terms disappeared from 2012, a year marked by massive protests over alleged electoral fraud. "A sacred duty" On Tuesday, Vladimir Putin preferred to prioritize "defending the interests and security of the Russian people," seeing this mission as "a sacred duty" - he then attended a mass during which the Orthodox Patriarch Kirill called him, before correcting himself, "Your Majesty" (or "Your Highness," in a more modest translation). True to his line of ensuring a form of normalcy in the daily lives of the Russian people, the president did not forget to mention the projects for "development" and the "quality of life for families." For the first time, the ceremony was broadcast by almost all local television channels - including sports and entertainment channels, with laudatory comments from the presenters. "We look to the future with confidence," emphasized Mr. Putin, as the internal and external situation seems to confirm these optimistic assurances: on the Ukrainian front, his army is advancing, albeit slowly and at the cost of significant losses; his defense industry is running at full capacity, supported by China, to the point of overheating the Russian economy; the elite and society as a whole show, if not cohesion, at least loyalty; the opposition is stifled, its leaders dead or in prison. A reshuffle to come The only uncertainty in this post-election sequence concerns not a hypothetical change in direction, but the extent of the expected government reshuffle. According to the Constitution, Prime Minister Mikhail Mishustin has submitted the resignation of his government. However, analysts consider his reappointment likely, as they believe Vladimir Putin is not inclined to reignite speculation about his succession. The fate of several ministers is drawing attention, particularly that of Sergei Lavrov, who has been the Minister of Foreign Affairs since 2004 and has been rumored to be leaving on several occasions, as well as Sergei Shoigu, who has been weakened by the disastrous start of the invasion of Ukraine and implicated in a large-scale corruption scandal in his ministry. While addressing the Russian people primarily, Vladimir Putin also took care, in a relatively short speech, to send a message to Western countries: "We do not reject dialogue with Western states," he asserted. "The choice is theirs: do they intend to continue trying to contain Russia's development, to pursue a policy of aggression and continuous pressure against our country, or to seek the path of cooperation and peace? (...) I repeat, discussion is possible, including on issues of security and strategic stability, but only on an equal footing." Nuclear exercises In support of this speech, the Kremlin leader had announced the upcoming conduct of nuclear exercises by the "non-strategic" forces of the Southern Military District, bordering Ukraine, an initiative that the Ministry of Defense explicitly linked to the "threats" made by "various Western leaders" against Russia. The Kremlin spokesperson, Dmitry Peskov, specifically named Emmanuel Macron and his "very dangerous rhetoric," as the French president had mentioned the possibility of deploying French troops on Ukrainian soil. The attitude of Western countries towards Vladimir Putin's inauguration was also closely watched. While most European countries chose not to attend the ceremony, ambassadors from six countries were present: Malta, Hungary, Slovakia, Greece, Cyprus, and France. The presence of the French ambassador, Pierre Lévy, at the Kremlin echoed Emmanuel Macron's words, spoken on Monday during Xi Jinping's visit, stating that France "is not at war with Russia or the Russian people" and "is not seeking a change of regime in Moscow." French diplomacy, it is also argued, recognizes a state and its institutions without commenting on their officeholders. The French Ministry of Foreign Affairs, which criticized the conditions of Vladimir Putin's reelection, intends to maintain channels of discussion. However, as if to emphasize on the Russian side that no reconciliation is possible with France, which has positioned itself in recent months as a leading supporter of Ukraine, Mr. Lévy was summoned to the Ministry of Foreign Affairs on Monday for the third time this year. The British ambassador has also been summoned, but chose not to be present on Tuesday, as did the United States ambassador. "We do not consider this election [on March 17] to be free and fair, but he [Vladimir Putin] is the president of Russia and will continue to hold this role," nuanced the spokesperson of the U.S. State Department, Matthew Miller, in front of the press. On May 9, for the ceremonies marking the anniversary of the Soviet victory in World War II, eight foreign heads of state, mostly from former Soviet countries, are expected in Moscow.

title: "Portrait of a Broken World": Defending the European Utopia in a Fractured World
lead: As citizens from 76 countries are called to vote in 2024, the magazine "Le Grand Continent" delves into the geopolitics of a world in permanent campaign.
text_eng: The review of reviews. 2024 is a unique election year. Citizens from seventy-six countries are called to vote. While some elections are particularly scrutinized, such as the presidential election in the United States, this phenomenon has become commonplace. Because "there is no longer a clear distinction between the electoral campaign phase and the governing phase," notes the writer Giuliano da Empoli, who directs the new print edition of the online magazine Le Grand Continent. In the past, governmental realism followed the lyricism of electoral conquest. "We campaign in poetry but we govern in prose," summarized American Democratic Senator Mario Cuomo (1932-2015) in a stroke. Today, we have entered a logic of permanent campaigning, observes Giuliano da Empoli. However, poetry - even political poetry - has not become the rule of democratic life since digital media maintain the public space in a lingering agonistic climate. It must be said that the world is fractured. Divided between supporters of ecological transition and climate skeptics, Europe sees two "lifestyles" opposed, between "multiculturalists and progressives on one side, and conservatives and identitarians on the other," analyzes political scientist Jean-Yves Dormagen. The European Union, an essential objective The conflict in Ukraine has strengthened the European belonging of this nation led by Zelensky, this "Churchill with an iPhone," but Europe must help more, according to British historian Timothy Garton Ash: "Ukraine has made its European choice. Europe must now make its Ukrainian choice coherently." The conflict in Gaza, on the other hand, increases divisions within the European Union (EU). "The simultaneity of these two conflicts poses problems of coherence (…) vis-à-vis the rest of the world," acknowledges Josep Borrell, EU foreign policy chief. In summary, writes this advocate for a ceasefire in Gaza and a two-state solution, "our lack of unity" on the Israeli-Palestinian conflict "has weakened our credibility in defending international law." An impression of "double standards" is particularly prevalent in the Global South, a notion criticized by political scientist Bruno Tertrais. He sees it as an "intellectual and political trap" that "values the idea of a political confrontation with the West," while researcher Aude Darnal, on the contrary, defends the "most polysemic and neutral expression available" to designate the Southern States, despite their heterogeneity. Contemporary of a "broken world," the young people in charge of Le Grand Continent are not part of a "disenchanted generation." That is why, in his afterword, Spanish novelist Javier Cercas displays an avowed Europeanism and federalism. Certainly, Europe must "stop being an elitist project and become a project of the people," but, he writes with lyricism, the EU embodies "the most ambitious, urgent, revolutionary, and necessary project of the 21st century."

title: Georgia, dilemma and challenge for the European Union
lead: The openly pro-Russian orientation of the government is a headache for Brussels, which granted the former Soviet republic of the South Caucasus official candidate status for the European Union in December 2023.
text_eng: Europeans are still having a hard time believing it. Just four months after granting Georgia official candidate status for the European Union (EU), they are stunned to see the return to Parliament of the controversial bill on "foreign influence" in this former Soviet republic in the South Caucasus. The text, modeled after a 2012 Russian law aimed at silencing civil society and independent media, is considered incompatible with democratic and European values. Its final adoption, scheduled by June, would jeopardize the EU integration, enshrined in the Georgian Constitution and desired by 80% of the population. In Tbilisi, thousands of people have been protesting every day for the past three weeks demanding its withdrawal, amidst increasing repression. However, unlike in March 2023, when the bill was first introduced in Parliament before being abandoned under pressure, the government now seems deaf to the protests and international condemnations. In Brussels, the situation is causing great concern. Following the repression in the streets of the Georgian capital, the President of the Commission, Ursula von der Leyen, issued a statement on May 1 condemning "the violence in the streets of Tbilisi" and expressing her "concerns regarding the law on foreign influence." An unusual intervention. "It's not every day that the president speaks out. This shows the level of concern within the Commission," said sources within the European executive. The Twenty-Seven are even more alarmed as Georgia's strongman, the billionaire oligarch and founder of the ruling party Bidzina Ivanichvili, delivered a resolutely anti-Western speech on Monday, April 29, in pure Kremlin style. In his vehement and conspiratorial diatribe, he equates Westerners, the opposition, and Georgian civil society to enemies, accused of fomenting a revolution to overthrow the government and destabilize the country. This discreet man, who obtained French nationality in 2010 and was awarded the Legion of Honor by France in 2021, also announces the repression of any government opponent after the legislative elections, scheduled for October. He concludes by asserting, against all evidence, that "in 2030, Georgia will join the EU." Credibility test for the Twenty-Seven Besides the European institutions, the Member States are also extremely concerned. Lithuanian Foreign Minister Gabrielius Landsbergis stated on the social network X that he was "heartbroken to hear Ivanichvili's vision for Georgia's future (...). We are shocked to see Georgians being led towards non-freedom by those who fear freedom the most." So far, the ruling party, Georgian Dream, has been using a double discourse by claiming to want to join the EU while doing everything to "sabotage" this process, according to the opposition and civil society. By openly expressing pro-Russian views, the Georgian authorities are breaking away from this ambiguity and challenging the Twenty-Seven: how to react to prevent the country from deviating from its European trajectory? What leverage to use to bend the authorities without pushing the country into Moscow's arms or penalizing the population? The Georgian issue is both a dilemma and a credibility test for the EU. "By granting candidate status to Georgia, the Europeans wanted to avoid losing [this country] geopolitically, satisfy the population, and prevent Bidzina Ivanichvili from sliding towards authoritarianism and Russia, but the opposite is happening," laments Thorniké Gordadzé, a researcher and former Georgian Minister in charge of European integration. "It is the first time there is an openly pro-Russian government since the country's independence [in 1991]. We can no longer remain on the fence as the Europeans have done so far; measures must be taken to prevent the country from falling into Moscow's hands." A simple reminder In the first place, Ursula von der Leyen dispatched the Director-General of the Commission for Enlargement Negotiations, Gert Jan Koopman, one of the most important EU representatives on enlargement issues, to Tbilisi. He came with a clear message: if Georgia intends to join the EU, it cannot continue on this path. Meanwhile, the European Parliament's Foreign Affairs Committee expressed concern and called on the Georgian government to "stop using force against peaceful protesters." MEPs also denounced the reappearance in the Georgian Parliament of the bill that "constitutes an attack on independent media and civil society organizations. If adopted, this law will jeopardize Georgia's progress towards the EU." A diplomat in Brussels urges hammering this message to the Tbilisi authorities: "The accession issue is what crystallizes the protests against the bill, and there is broad opposition in a country overwhelmingly in favor of the EU. Therefore, this point must be emphasized." Despite the concerns, "it is still too early to discuss a next step beyond condemning Georgia," according to the European diplomatic service. "The discussion has just begun among the Twenty-Seven on the subject." During the upcoming European Council meeting in June, the Twenty-Seven could remind, in the conclusions, of the commitments made by Georgia as a candidate for EU membership. Will a simple reminder be enough to make Tbilisi step back? This seems unlikely. The Twenty-Seven have other tools at their disposal, such as sanctions targeting Bidzina Ivanichvili, a halt to European financial aid - the largest donor to Georgia, providing around 80 million euros per year - or the outright withdrawal of candidate status for the EU, as suggested by Green MEP Viola von Cramon-Taubadel. "A victory for Putin" Georgia had requested this status following the Russian invasion of Ukraine in 2022, along with Ukraine and Moldova, two other former Soviet republics. After an initial refusal in June 2022, Georgia was granted the status in December 2023 despite lagging reforms - only three out of twelve recommendations had been met. "This status is abusive," says Viola von Cramon-Taubadel, "because not only has the government clearly indicated that it will not make the reforms, but it is also using it to its advantage in view of the upcoming legislative elections." She regrets that today, the Twenty-Seven find themselves "in an extremely difficult situation, because if it is withdrawn now, it is also a victory for Putin... It's a lose-lose game." However, these options are not officially discussed today. "It's still too early, we need to keep our options open if the law is definitively adopted," explains a European diplomat. "We will have to redefine our relationship with this regime." Furthermore, sanctions seem difficult to consider towards Georgia, as the EU has never seriously threatened Serbia or Bosnia-Herzegovina, two other candidate countries for accession. "When you look at how Europe has reacted to what is happening in Serbia, where we have seen a degradation of democratic principles for ten years, or in Bosnia-Herzegovina, where Milorad Dodik, the leader of Republika Srpska [Serb Republic], one of the two entities of the country, has repeatedly provoked Brussels without ever being sanctioned, there is little to expect," says Wouter Zweers, a specialist on the South Caucasus at the Clingendael Institute in The Hague. In fact, as with any sanction, unanimity among the Twenty-Seven is required. However, while Lithuania or Croatia are calling for a firm response to Tbilisi, Slovakia or Hungary, whose ties with Georgia have strengthened, are less inclined. The issue is expected to be discussed at the next Foreign Affairs Council on May 27th.

title: "Nuclear Now" on Paris Première: when Oliver Stone uses France as an example
lead: Is nuclear power essential in the fight against climate change? A plea to Americans by the three-time Oscar-winning filmmaker.
text_eng: PARIS PREMIÈRE - SUNDAY, MAY 5 AT 9 PM - DOCUMENTARY Will nuclear power help save the world? Filmmaker Oliver Stone no longer doubts it, since reading A Bright Future by Joshua S. Goldstein and Staffan A. Qvist (PublicAffairs, 2019, not translated). The book draws on the energy choices of European countries, including France, to demonstrate that nuclear energy is an essential element in the fight against climate change. "We no longer have time to be afraid," says the three-time Oscar-winning filmmaker: Best Adapted Screenplay for Midnight Express, directed by Alan Parker (1978), Best Director for Platoon (1986) and Born on the Fourth of July (1990). His fame has opened doors for him, especially in Russia, during the two years of investigation required to make this long plea for nuclear energy. Urgency justifies everything in Oliver Stone's eyes: oppressive music, shocking shots, all with a common thread. Whether denouncing the horrors of war, a conspiracy (JFK, 1992), or expressing his near-fascination with Fidel Castro (Comandante, 2003) or Vladimir Putin (Conversations with Mr. Putin, 2017), he continues to settle scores with his country. Lobbying by the "Seven Sisters" This film is also aimed at the American public, which paradoxically partly contributes to its appeal, especially in the historical first part. Drawing on key figures in science, from Pierre and Marie Curie to Stephen Hawking, this sometimes overlooked recap recounts the global development of civilian nuclear power through the prism of the United States: the first Soviet plant in 1954, American in 1958, French in 1964 (in Chinon, Indre-et-Loire)... France is cited as an example several times, as well as Sweden, Germany, Japan, the USSR, and Russia. The commentary, subjective at times, asserts that uranium U92 is "a natural energy source" and uses the term "clean energy." This flashback also allows the director to highlight his alignment with Presidents Eisenhower and Kennedy, who publicly supported nuclear energy. After the bombs on Hiroshima and Nagasaki - referred to here as the "original sin" - revealed the horror of these weapons to the world, generating fear and revulsion among populations - which Oliver Stone considers excessive - the disasters at Three Mile Island (1979), Chernobyl (1986), and Fukushima (2011) continued to fuel the public's aversion to nuclear energy. Oliver Stone sharply criticizes the manipulation of public opinion by the "Seven Sisters" oil majors (Exxon, Shell, Gulf, BP, Chevron, Mobil, and Texaco); viewers, however, lament the downplaying of the human and environmental tolls of the Chernobyl and Fukushima accidents. In any case, "public opinion's pessimism reaches a critical point." Japan is shutting down its nuclear power plants. Regrettable subjectivity. The second half of the film will focus on turning things around. Firstly, by providing an interesting, global overview of fossil fuels and energy policies in India, Russia, and China. Oliver Stone targets outsourcing: "California boasts of having a clean state, but it's China burning its coal." He emphasizes: "With China and the United States accounting for half of global carbon emissions, the key to decarbonization depends on these two countries." The final part, on advancements in civil nuclear energy, however, disappoints. Admitting to knowing nothing about it, the filmmaker's successive enthusiasms for micro-reactors or nuclear fusion (still in the research stage) become tiresome. Instead, while decarbonizing the global economy requires reducing our consumption and developing wind, hydro, and solar energies, the concept of frugality is not mentioned once. And renewable energies are only discussed by emphasizing their limitations. A regrettable subjectivity given the stakes. Indeed, with the increasingly visible effects of climate change and the shutdown of Russian gas pipelines following the invasion of Ukraine, "nuclear power is gaining ground worldwide, among political leaders and public opinion," as reported in Le Monde. The Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change says the same, ranking nuclear power among the possible solutions for decarbonizing the planet. We no longer have time to argue.

title: European leaders are calling for more sanctions against Iran.
lead: The twenty-seven heads of state and government, gathered in Brussels on Wednesday evening, want to expand their retaliatory measures against the Tehran regime, while avoiding any escalation in the Middle East region.
text_eng: After the massive attack by Iran against Israel on April 12, "we have decided to impose sanctions against Iran; we wanted to send a clear message," said Charles Michel, the President of the European Council, on Wednesday, April 17. The European Union (EU) will sanction in particular the producers of drones and missiles from the Islamic Republic. After more than four hours of discussions, this was however the only concrete decision that the heads of state and government finally agreed upon. The Twenty-Seven also call on "Iran and its allied groups in the region to cease all attacks and urge all parties to show the utmost restraint and refrain from any actions that could escalate tensions in the region." This last remark also applies to Israel. "For us," emphasized German Chancellor Olaf Scholz, "it is important that this moment be used for a new de-escalation and that Israel [takes] advantage to strengthen its own position (...) and not respond with a massive attack." Regarding retaliatory measures, "our duty is to expand them," declared French President Emmanuel Macron before the summit. "We are in favor of sanctions that can also target all those who help produce missiles and drones used in the attack." "The idea is to expand the current regime of sanctions [related to drones] linked to Iran and Russia," specified Josep Borrell, the EU's foreign policy chief, as early as Tuesday, while also restricting missile supplies. Likewise, the diplomat added, "this sanctions regime should be applicable to deliveries from Iran's allies in the Middle East, Lebanon, Yemen, Iraq, Syria, or elsewhere." These restrictive measures remain largely symbolic, as Tehran has so far always managed to circumvent them. Some countries, including the Netherlands, Sweden, and Austria, are also pushing for sanctions against the Iranian Revolutionary Guard Corps. "All means to strengthen sanctions should be considered," stated Austrian Chancellor Karl Nehammer. "There are already sanction lists for these forces," reminded his German counterpart, Olaf Scholz. "Additional measures targeting the Revolutionary Guards require legal checks." On Tuesday, Mr. Borrell stated in Le Monde that the legal conditions are still not met. Kiev demands more anti-aircraft defense systems. This reinforcement of sanctions was also requested on Wednesday afternoon by the foreign ministers of the G7, meeting in Capri, Italy. During this meeting, David Cameron, the British Foreign Secretary, called for the member countries - Germany, Canada, the United States, France, Italy, Japan, and the United Kingdom - to impose "coordinated sanctions" on Iran, accusing it of being behind "malicious activities" in a region already destabilized by the unprecedented attack carried out by the Palestinian Hamas against Israel on October 7, 2023. In this context, however, the issue of Israel's anti-aircraft defense against Iranian drones and missiles - considered effective - was also discussed on Wednesday by Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky. Speaking via video conference, he reminded that "here, in Ukraine, in our part of Europe, unfortunately, we do not have the level of defense that we all saw in the Middle East a few days ago (...). Our Ukrainian sky and that of our neighbors deserve the same security." Earlier, Estonian Prime Minister Kaja Kallas also questioned in front of a few journalists the inability of the Twenty-Seven to offer Kiev the same protection that some provide to Israel. "These are the same drones attacking both countries," she recalled. "If the same ally can shoot them down there, they can do it in Ukraine as well." While Kiev does not have the Israeli Iron Dome, Ukraine is mainly requesting more anti-aircraft defense systems from its Western allies. It is currently lacking at least seven of them. While Germany has already decided to send one more, Olaf Scholz called on his counterparts to follow his example. "It is useful immediately and we want to encourage others to do the same," he said. Gathered on Wednesday afternoon at NATO, Dutch Prime Minister Mark Rutte, Czech Prime Minister Petr Fiala, and Danish Prime Minister Mette Frederiksen announced their intention to respond to Ukraine's request, without specifying how. "The issue is not money, but finding systems to send within NATO or outside to protect the country that is currently under fire from Russia," emphasized the Dutch leader. During this meeting, NATO Secretary General Jens Stoltenberg clarified that allies could transfer more stocks to Ukraine, even if it means no longer meeting their commitments of minimal military equipment to hold to meet the organization's criteria. "This signal from NATO provides new perspectives," concluded Belgian Prime Minister Alexander De Croo.

title: Ramon Fonseca, one of the two lawyers involved in the "Panama Papers" scandal, has died.
lead: Mr. Fonseca died at the age of 71 in a hospital in the capital of Panama, as announced by Mrs. Mc Donald, a lawyer from the former company, without specifying the causes of death.
text_eng: Panamanian lawyer Ramon Fonseca, one of the two founders of the law firm at the center of the "Panama Papers" scandal, has died at the age of 71 while awaiting judgment in connection with this global scandal that broke out in 2016. In the night, Mr. Fonseca "passed away, he was hospitalized, which is why he did not attend the trial," which began on April 8 and had not yet delivered a judgment, said Guillermina Mc Donald, a lawyer for the former company at the center of the scandal that revealed how celebrities from around the world hid money from tax authorities. Mr. Fonseca died in a hospital in the capital of the small Central American country, Ms. Mc Donald announced, without specifying the cause of death. During the trial, which ended on April 19, the maximum sentence for money laundering – twelve years in prison – was requested against Ramon Fonseca and Jürgen Mossack, the two founders of the law firm Mossack Fonseca. The court was expected to deliver its judgment within thirty days. "Any judicial process carries an enormous burden for a person who is accused, and even more so in the circumstances in which this process took place," said Mr. Fonseca's lawyer, Daika Indira Levy, to AFP after confirming her client's death. The case came to light in 2016 after the publication of an investigation, the "Panama Papers," conducted by the International Consortium of Investigative Journalists, of which Le Monde is a part. Based on the leak of 11.5 million documents from the Mossack Fonseca firm, it revealed that heads of state and government, prominent political figures, and personalities from the financial, sports, and artistic worlds hid properties, businesses, capital, and profits from tax authorities. To this end, they created opaque companies through the Panamanian firm to open bank accounts and establish shell companies in several countries to conceal money, some of which came from illegal activities, according to the consortium's investigation. The case was brought to the big screen. Among the personalities mentioned are, among many others, Russian President Vladimir Putin, former Prime Ministers of Iceland Sigmundur David Gunnlaugsson, Pakistan Nawaz Sharif, and the UK David Cameron (current British Foreign Secretary), former Argentine President Mauricio Macri, as well as football star Lionel Messi and Spanish filmmaker Pedro Almodovar. The scandal led to the closure of the Mossack Fonseca firm and severely damaged the country's image. The accusation against Mr. Fonseca and Mr. Mossack includes money laundering, corruption, and other financial crimes.
