Mossack and Fonseca are accused of having facilitated, through their law firm, the creation of opaque companies in which executives of the German multinational Siemens deposited millions of euros outside the company's real accounts. This "slush fund" is said to have been used to hide money related to the payment of commissions. The Panamanian firm, according to the accusation, was also used to store money from a large scam in Argentina. "Truly, there has been a great injustice (...) I repeat that my partner and all the people who worked with me were serious, honest, and correct individuals," Mr. Mossack had declared during his plea on April 19. The case has been brought to the big screen. Ramon Fonseca, who has written several novels, was portrayed by Spanish actor Antonio Banderas in a film directed by Steven Soderbergh, "The Laundromat," based on the Panama Papers scandal (2019).

title: The United States is including long-range ATACMS missiles in their arms deliveries to Ukraine.
lead: In the first military aid package of one billion dollars, the White House agreed on Wednesday to include projectiles capable of reaching targets located 300 kilometers away, with an accuracy of a few tens of meters.
text_eng: A sigh of relief passes through the ranks of Ukraine supporters in Washington. On Wednesday, April 24, President Joe Biden signed the bill adopted the day before by the Senate, a few days after the House of Representatives, which grants $61 billion (57 billion euros) in aid to Kiev. The package is part of a larger $95.3 billion package, also benefiting Israel and Taiwan. In a speech, Joe Biden acknowledged that the parliamentary process "was a difficult path," an understatement given six months of negotiations and reformatting of the project, blocked by a minority of radical Republican lawmakers. A first installment of one billion dollars was immediately announced by Washington, including long-range missiles. The president emphasized that this support for Kiev represents an "investment" in the security of Ukraine, Europe, and the United States. He noted that Russia also benefits from valuable support: Iranian drones, ballistic missiles, North Korean artillery shells, and Chinese electronic components. Joe Biden stressed that military deliveries stimulate American production: Patriot missiles are made in Arizona, Javelins in Alabama, artillery shells in Ohio or Pennsylvania... Finally, he paid tribute to the Republican and Democratic leaders in Congress, who managed to agree on a text that ultimately gathered a very large majority of lawmakers. "There is bipartisan consensus for this kind of American leadership," Joe Biden said. "We do not abandon our allies, we support them. We do not let tyrants win, we oppose them. We do not just watch the world evolve as spectators, we shape it." Kiev troops on the brink of collapse in several places In the afternoon, Jake Sullivan, national security adviser, appeared before the press at the White House. He did not rule out that "Russia may make additional tactical gains in the coming weeks" in eastern Ukraine, due to the human and material disadvantage currently suffered by Kiev. But the adviser indicated that "the structural dynamics of this conflict favor the party defending its own territory." According to Jake Sullivan, the Ukrainian army has managed to destroy 700 armored vehicles and about 250 tanks since the beginning of the year. However, on the retreat since the failure of their summer 2023 counteroffensive, Kiev's troops are on the brink of collapse in several places on the front line. On April 20 and 21, the Russians broke through the Ukrainian line for nearly 10 kilometers at Otcheretyne, a town in Donbass located northwest of Avdiivka, raising fears for a moment of a breakthrough in Kiev's defensive system. The city of Kharkiv, located in the northeast of the country and with a population of 1.4 million inhabitants before the war, is daily under Russian fire. A sign of the growing concern in Kiev, "the key [to the conflict] is now speed," acknowledged Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky in a message broadcast on the X network on Wednesday, adding that "any leader who does not waste time saves lives." The first delivery to Ukraine, estimated by the Pentagon at one billion dollars, is one of the largest since the beginning of the war. The last delivery on March 12 amounted to 300 million dollars. The most notable fact: the presence of more advanced ATACMS (Army Tactical Missile System) surface-to-surface missiles. Since October 2023, the versions provided to Kiev of this GPS-guided and inertial system projectile had a limited range of 165 kilometers. The new delivery will include a version capable of reaching targets located at 300 kilometers, with a precision of just a few dozen meters. This capability would bring within reach of Ukrainian Himars rocket launchers the strategic Kerch Bridge, located southeast of Crimea, about 250 kilometers from the front. Similarly, the Russian fleet in the Black Sea, already significantly reduced, will become even more vulnerable. The ability to target a symbolically important target like the Kerch Bridge explains the German Chancellor Olaf Scholz's stubborn refusal to approve the delivery of Taurus missiles by his country. Jake Sullivan clarified on Wednesday that the ATACMS missiles would be used only "within the sovereign territory of Ukraine." New redefinition of American red lines According to information from The New York Times confirmed by an official source, the United States has already begun delivering these longer-range ATACMS in February. The repeated Russian attacks on Ukraine's essential civilian infrastructure motivated this new redefinition of American red lines regarding deliveries. Jake Sullivan also mentioned the transfer of ballistic missiles from North Korea to Russia. Upon receiving these ATACMS, the Ukrainians began using them, The New York Times reported, citing two recent targets: Russian troops in the port city of Berdiansk and a military airfield in Crimea. The new massive delivery from the United States also includes 155mm shells, which the Ukrainians desperately need to counter the Russian artillery, which fires five to ten times more projectiles than its adversary in some areas of the front. Additionally, Bradley infantry fighting vehicles have been announced. Even though it is an old armored vehicle - it was introduced into the US army in 1981 - Ukrainians praise its versatility and especially its armor, which saves the lives of many soldiers, an essential resource to allow Kiev to continue its war. The question remains how long it will take for all this American equipment to reach Ukraine. "In the coming hours," promised Joe Biden. According to various sources, some of it has been prepositioned in Germany and Poland to be delivered as quickly as possible.

title: War in Ukraine: American military aid will not be enough to rebalance the power dynamics in 2024.
lead: The American Congress finally voted at the end of April for a new aid package of 60.8 billion dollars (56 billion euros), which has already enabled deliveries of weapons, used notably in Crimea. However, it will take time before the effects are truly felt.
text_eng: Will American military aid be enough to turn the tide of the war in Ukraine? And, most importantly, will it arrive soon enough to stop the increasing Russian attacks along the front line, in the Donbass but also now in the Kharkiv region, in the northeast of the country, where Moscow's troops resumed their offensive on May 10th? After several months of indecision, the U.S. Congress approved a new aid package of $60.8 billion on April 23rd to Ukraine, in addition to the approximately $44 billion in military support provided by Washington since February 2022. Recognizing the urgency to replenish Kyiv's depleted arsenals, President Joe Biden immediately authorized the first delivery of equipment worth $1 billion, including Stinger anti-aircraft missiles, Javelin anti-tank missiles, Bradley armored vehicles, 155mm shells, and more. A second shipment of $400 million was also announced on May 10th. Some of these equipment, prepositioned in Germany and Poland awaiting congressional approval, have already arrived on the ground. Several Russian sites were targeted at the end of April and beginning of May with long-range ATACMS (Army Tactical Missile System) missiles, a capability previously unavailable to the Ukrainians. Fired from a Himars rocket launcher, these GPS and inertial-guided projectiles can reach targets up to 300 kilometers away, nearly double the range of the version initially provided by the U.S. to Kyiv. Crimea, occupied by Moscow's forces since 2014 but still claimed by Ukraine, is reportedly a primary target, according to pro-Russian networks. On April 28th, an anti-aircraft defense unit was allegedly targeted at Cape Tarkhankut, the westernmost point of the region. On April 30th, the Djankoi airbase in northern Crimea was hit by ATACMS, destroying or damaging S-400 and S-300 surface-to-air missile systems. On May 4th, a ballistic missile launch system Iskander, located in Luhove, northeast of Eupatoria, was reportedly bombed. "There is a sense that the Ukrainians are seeking to diminish the potential of Crimea, which serves as the backyard for Russian operations in the south of the country," says a French military source. Clearing the skies of Russian anti-aircraft defenses in the peninsula could also be a prelude to a new attempt to bomb the strategic Kerch Bridge, located southeast of Crimea, about 250 kilometers from the front line. Used by Moscow to supply its troops, the bridge has been the target of several attacks in 2022 and 2023, but remains operational for now, even if the heaviest freight trains no longer use it. Reintroducing "strategic ambiguity" As rapid and significant as these new American deliveries may be, they will not be enough to reverse the balance of power, at least not in the short term, according to military analysts. Since the fall of 2023, Russia has had the initiative on the ground and could maintain it for several more months. "Washington's long delay means that it will take time to repair the damage to Ukrainian capabilities. Ukraine will lose ground to Russia this summer. The question is to what extent and at what cost Ukrainian forces can make the Russians pay for their gains," says Jack Watling, a researcher at the British think tank RUSI, in a note published on May 2 by Foreign Affairs magazine. White House National Security Advisor Jake Sullivan himself acknowledged that the new American military aid will not "instantly tip" the situation on the battlefield, but should only help "hold the line" during the year 2024. "You can't flip the switch instantly," he explained at a forum held on May 4 in Washington, adding that one should still expect "Russian advances in the period ahead." Indeed, Ukrainians seem to still be short of ammunition, with a very favorable ratio of fires to Russians, ranging from five to ten to one depending on the front, not to mention their difficulties in replenishing the ranks of their units, despite a toughening of mobilization conditions for the population. In Western chancelleries, concern is palpable. "Even if it's not the preferred option, a Ukrainian collapse is possible at any time and we must be prepared for it," says a French diplomatic source. According to the French government, it is in this light that we must analyze the statements of Emmanuel Macron, who reaffirmed, in an interview with the British weekly The Economist published on May 2, that Western intervention on Ukrainian soil should not be ruled out a priori. "If the Russians were to break through the front lines, if there were a Ukrainian request - which is not the case today - we should legitimately consider sending troops," declared the French president. According to the state summit, these statements aim to reintroduce "strategic ambiguity" between the West and Russia. "Since the beginning of the war, we have given [Vladimir] Putin too much comfort by clearly stating our intentions and red lines. Doubt about the other's intentions must change sides," they explain in Paris. Failing to convince Moscow to abandon its conquest of Ukraine, this strategy could moderate the zeal of Russian offensives, diplomats in France believe. One way, it is explained, to save time until the arrival of a new significant Western military assistance, expected from 2025 onwards, when the European and American defense industries will have ramped up production.

title: The surprising presence of the French ambassador at Vladimir Putin's inauguration
lead: France displayed a message of confusion rather than firmness by sending its ambassador, Pierre Lévy, to the Kremlin without coordination with its main European partners, at a time of extreme tension with Russia.
text_eng: The presence of the French ambassador, Pierre Lévy, among the few Western diplomats who agreed to attend the inauguration ceremony of President Vladimir Putin in Moscow on Tuesday, May 7, was surprising for several reasons. The first reason is that the election through which Mr. Putin obtained a fifth presidential term with 88.5% of the votes from March 15 to 17, took place under such undemocratic conditions that France, like most Western countries, merely acknowledged it, emphasizing that Russian voters were deprived of a genuine choice and strongly condemning the repressive context in which it was held. For reference, opposition leader Alexei Navalny had died a month earlier in mid-February in unclear circumstances - Mr. Lévy had actually attended Navalny's funeral in Moscow with his Western colleagues. It is therefore all the more paradoxical that France sent its ambassador to endorse, by his presence, an election whose conditions it had disapproved of. The second reason is that the vast majority of Western countries boycotted this ceremony. Apart from France, no G7 country was represented. As for the European Union (EU) countries, the French ambassador found himself in the company of only his colleagues from Hungary, Slovakia - known for their complacency towards Moscow - Malta, Cyprus, and Greece. The EU ambassador conspicuously stayed at home. Germany, on its part, chose to recall its ambassador to Berlin for consultations to signal its condemnation of cyberattacks attributed to Russia. At a time when France continues to advocate and promote European unity, breaking ranks in this way sends an incomprehensible signal of division. Russian state media did not hesitate to highlight this. Ambiguity The third reason is that, on the eve of the inauguration ceremony, the same French ambassador had been summoned to the Russian Ministry of Foreign Affairs for the third time since the beginning of 2024, to be reproached for the "provocative" policy of the French authorities. The French Ministry of Foreign Affairs had timely denounced this "misuse of diplomatic channels for the purpose of information manipulation and intimidation." To honor the Kremlin with his presence twenty-four hours after being scolded by his own government is a strange way to show France's outrage. The fourth reason, last but not least, is that this president, enthroned on Tuesday like a tsar by Patriarch Kirill, had invaded Ukraine, an independent state, without reason on February 24, 2022, and has since been waging a large-scale aggressive war that is shaking the entire European continent. Around fifty countries, including France, are providing military aid to Ukraine against Russia. Poutine is the subject of an arrest warrant from the International Criminal Court for war crimes. In justifying the presence of its ambassador on Tuesday, Paris argued that France did not want to "cut all ties" with Russia. Preserving communication channels in times of war can indeed be useful. However, doing so in this manner, without coordination with our European partners, at the very moment when Russia is raising the stakes by once again waving the nuclear threat, contradicts the strong stance that France has displayed in recent months and appears more as ambiguity than strategy.

title: Oksana Leuta and war, on stage as in Ukraine
lead: Since the invasion of her country, this former Russian teacher at the French high school in Kiev has been working as a fixer for foreign journalists. Also an actress, she is performing in a show in Paris until April 27 that combines the stories of other female artists dealing with war.
text_eng: Oksana Leuta has just arrived in Paris from Kiev, after a twenty-four-hour journey involving two trains and a plane. On this 20th of April, she disembarks with a gentle smile and her hair in two braids, to begin her first scouting at the Théâtre de Chaillot. She managed to snatch a few hours of sleep at the hotel, where she is staying with her seventy-year-old mother, Anna. "All night, she confused the noise of the planes, which we are not used to hearing in Kiev, with that of drones," she whispers. At Chaillot, both of them will take the stage from the 24th to the 27th of April to recount their story impacted by the war, as part of Radio Live - Vivantes, a show conceived by radio producer Aurélie Charon and film director Amélie Bonnin. It is a mix of spontaneous interviews, exchanges, musical and video creations, which will then go on tour to Lille, Lyon, Rennes, or Grenoble. Alongside them, two other women grappling with contemporary geopolitical conflicts will share their stories: Syrian screenwriter and actress Hala Rajab and Bosnian filmmaker Ines Tanović. "We must share, inform, raise awareness," says 38-year-old Oksana Leuta. When Russia invaded Ukraine in February 2022, she may have felt "that the suffering of Ukrainians was unique." But now, during rehearsals at Chaillot, she hears about the same displacements and similarly unpunished crimes in former Yugoslavia. And of course, about this insidious habituation to violence, common to all. Interpreter, intermediary, and negotiator. At the beginning of the war, this daughter of teachers, a lover of French culture and language, left her position as a Russian teacher at the French school in Kiev. "Teaching a language means teaching a culture, a civilization. I couldn't imagine discussing Pushkin and Tolstoy while my land was being targeted by Russian bombs." She then improvised as a fixer for French journalists, a profession she knew nothing about, which involves being an interpreter, intermediary, and negotiator for interviews. In short, a "problem solver," as she puts it. A part-time actress for years, she never gives up on theater. On May 30, 2022, she was in Donbass with Frédéric Leclerc-Imhoff, 32, when the BFM-TV reporter was killed by shrapnel. "I had committed to rehearse a play directed by Dima Levytskyi right after. To bid farewell to Frédéric, I joined his family and friends in Poland, where his body was repatriated to Paris. I remember seeing people boarding the plane with the coffin, and I was left standing there on the tarmac, taking the bus alone to Lviv the next day, ready to rehearse..." In this year 2022, war infiltrates everywhere. One would like to focus on warm-up exercises and learning lines. But what to do after watching these videos of torture of Ukrainian soldiers that are being exchanged on smartphones? Even Dima Levytskyi's instructions are barely reaching Oksana Leuta's ears: "That's when we realized that I had ruptures in my eardrum" due to the explosion of the shell that killed the reporter. "I no longer go down into the shelters." In recent months, while supporting journalists from France Télévisions or Le Monde, she also attended a training session in Kiev with Ariane Mnouchkine and her company, the Théâtre du Soleil, and performed, in Portugal and Poland, "Silence, silence, silence, please," a play by the director from Donbass Pavlo Yurov. At the mention of her favorite show, "Anthracite" (2014), by the same Yurov, her eyes fill with sadness: "What a joy it would be to perform that again! But today, half of the actors from the play have fled abroad and the others are in the army." The future is only a few weeks away. The nights she used to spend in a queer club where she was a manager are now marked by alert sirens, which she ignores. "I no longer go down into the shelters," she admits. "What matters to me is sleep." Sometimes her sleep is filled with bloody dreams that end with the death of Vladimir Putin. "Before, I imagined the day of victory," says Oksana Leuta. "My spectacular outfit, the three-day non-stop party, champagne flowing." Today, even though in the United States, the House of Representatives passed a $61 billion (57 billion euros) aid package to Ukraine on April 20, a decision approved by the Senate on April 23, Oksana Leuta is less optimistic. On her left ring finger, she wears a silver ring shaped like the map of her country. "This is Ukraine as I knew it until 2014: with Crimea, where I used to go on vacation every summer, where I took my high school students camping. My country before the war. And how I dream of seeing it again."

title: Jordan Bardella, the invisible of the European Parliament
lead: During his previous term, the head of the National Rally's list in the European elections was not very involved in parliamentary work and with his delegation. He acknowledges this low commitment, preferring to focus on his presence in the media and on the ground.
text_eng: Before returning, possibly with the largest delegation from the upcoming European elections, Jordan Bardella left Strasbourg quietly on Wednesday, April 24. The young man in a hurry did not attend the last lunch of the National Rally (RN) MEPs at a restaurant in the European quarter where everyone paid their own share. Nor did he show up the day before at the cocktail party organized by the Identity and Democracy (ID) group, where his party is a member. There was also no sign of him during the last plenary session of the term on Thursday, April 25: Jordan Bardella had a morning TV appearance and a press conference in response to Emmanuel Macron's speech on Europe at the Sorbonne that day. The week turned out to be a good summary of the far-right leader's time in the European democracy temple. Focused on his media rise and role as president of the National Rally – but very little on his other mandate as a regional councilor in Ile-de-France – Jordan Bardella did not pretend to be interested in the European Parliament or his own delegation. According to observations by Le Monde, he only participated in the petitions committee, the only one he is registered in, during remote meetings between 2020 and 2022 due to the Covid-19 pandemic. When criticized for his non-existent parliamentary work, Jordan Bardella points to his party's polling intentions and says he prioritizes his presence in the media and on the ground. "The fight we lead at the National Rally may be more useful than the fight led [in the European Parliament] by [The Republicans' lead candidate for the European elections François-Xavier] Bellamy or [The Unsubmissive France's lead candidate Manon] Aubry, who haven't changed anything in the lives of the French," he said on Franceinfo on February 12. He sticks to a strictly tribunician role, vilifying the European Union in one-minute speeches recorded in the empty Strasbourg hemicycle. The party shares them on social media, giving Bardella's diatribes considerable exposure and a sense of grandeur. Jordan Bardella's displayed disdain for parliamentary work is reminiscent of what Jean-Luc Mélenchon claimed at the same place between 2009 and 2017. Serving Fidesz In early April, Jordan Bardella showed rare involvement in parliamentary work for his delegation. As Le Monde learned, the RN president tried to pressure his MEP Gilles Lebreton to manipulate a report on lifting immunity. A member of the European Parliament's Committee on Legal Affairs, Mr. Lebreton had been entrusted with a report on the lifting of immunity of a Hungarian deputy, a member of the Renew group (center) and a political opponent of Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orban. Anna Julia Donath is being prosecuted in Hungary for allegedly obstructing a search of another opponent of the prime minister. Gilles Lebreton, a law professor whose heart clearly beats for justice as much as for the far right, saw this as a case of fumus persecutionis, a prosecution aimed at silencing opponents. Jordan Bardella, who may have seen an opportunity to assist Fidesz, Viktor Orban's party, with whom the RN would like to ally in the next term, did not care about this. Perhaps not including Gilles Lebreton on his list did not help the RN president convince the jurist. According to several sources within the European delegation of the National Rally, Jordan Bardella has shown more interest in the Brussels faction since being elected head of the party in November 2022. He initiated a change in stance on the Russian issue, choosing to attend the visit of Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky to Brussels in February 2023, against the majority opinion, and shifting from voting against to abstaining on most texts concerning the invasion of Ukraine. By entrusting the "foundation" of the ID group to his close associate, MEP Mathilde Androuët, he also expressed a desire to use this funding source provided by the European Union to finance more substantive work. Within the group, it is surprising that Jordan Bardella did not take advantage of these five years and available funds to train and enlist a few technicians or jurists - especially since his Parisian entourage is considered insufficient for his ambitions. His European office, described as idle, has had three different compositions in less than five years. The least demanding committee of the European Parliament in Brussels, the heart of European parliamentary work, remains a terra incognita for the RN president. It was during the Covid-19 pandemic, via video conference, that he discovered the committee in which he had chosen to sit. His last presence dates back to May 2022, which was also the last session accessible remotely. The petitions committee, known as the least demanding in the European Parliament, allows citizens to raise issues with deputies, often seen as disconnected from their constituents. Almost all other members of the petitions committee participate in at least one other committee. "If he asks for my opinion, I would recommend he continue the same," said Philippe Olivier, special advisor to Marine Le Pen, also a Member of the European Parliament. When asked if he had ever attended a meeting of his committee in Brussels, Jordan Bardella did not respond. To defend his record, the president of the RN generally highlights his attendance rate at plenary sessions, which are held once a month. It stands at 93%, a high figure at first glance. However, very few French deputies miss these sessions, which are just a two-hour train ride (free) from Paris and pay 350 euros. Out of the 79 French MEPs, nine are doing worse than Jordan Bardella: one has health issues, four have almost deserted after being sidelined by the RN, another lives in French Polynesia, and the last three have joined the institution less than a year ago.

title: The Chinese President, Xi Jinping, is arriving in France for an official visit.
lead: Xi Jinping, who is coming to celebrate the sixty years of diplomatic relations between France and China, will have back-to-back meetings on Monday, May 6, with Emmanuel Macron and Ursula von der Leyen.
text_eng: Xi Jinping arrived in Paris on Sunday, May 5th, where Emmanuel Macron intends to advocate for "reciprocity" in trade and the search for a resolution to the war in Ukraine in the face of a Chinese president who continues to show support for Russia. Returning to Europe for the first time since 2019, the Chinese president organized his tour under the seal of diplomatic balance: after the state visit to France, which has been asking him for a year to use his influence to "bring Russia to reason," he will visit Serbia and Hungary, two countries that have remained close to Moscow. "In the afternoon of May 5th, local time, the President [of the People's Republic of China], Xi Jinping, arrived in Paris by plane to begin a state visit to France," announced the official Chinese television CCTV. He was welcomed around 4 p.m. by the French Prime Minister, Gabriel Attal, at Orly Airport. Trade Disputes On Monday, Xi Jinping, who is celebrating the sixty years of Franco-Chinese diplomatic relations, will have a series of meetings with Emmanuel Macron, who has consulted in advance with German Chancellor Olaf Scholz. In the morning, the President of the European Commission, Ursula von der Leyen, will join the Franco-Chinese duo at the Elysée Palace for a session that is expected to address trade disputes. Threatened to be caught between the American and Chinese economies, both heavily supported by the public sector, the European Union has in recent months increased investigations into Chinese state subsidies in several industrial sectors, particularly in electric vehicles, accused of distorting competition. "We must act to ensure that competition is fair and not distorted," said Ursula von der Leyen in a statement released from Sunday night to Monday. "China is currently producing more with massive subsidies than it sells due to its weak domestic demand. This leads to an oversupply of subsidized Chinese products, such as electric vehicles and steel, resulting in unfair trade," she accuses. "Europe cannot accept such practices that distort the market and could lead to deindustrialization in Europe," she insists. In previous discussions with the Chinese president, "I have clearly indicated that the current market access imbalances are not sustainable and must be corrected," she adds. "Contributing to a resolution" of the Ukrainian conflict In the afternoon, after a ceremonial welcome at Les Invalides, and before a banquet at the Elysée Palace, Emmanuel Macron and Xi Jinping will meet one-on-one for the most political segment, and then address the press. The French president plans to ask his Chinese counterpart to support the "Olympic truce" for "all" conflicts during the Paris Games this summer. Paris wants to ensure at the very least that China, the main ally of Russian President Vladimir Putin, does not clearly support his war effort against Kiev; or even "encourage it to use the leverage" it has over Moscow to "contribute to a resolution of this conflict," according to the Elysée. Emmanuel Macron had delivered the same message a year ago during his own state visit to China, with modest results. On Tuesday, the French president will nonetheless try to drive the point home in the Pyrenees, during a more personal outing between the two men, accompanied by their wives. The objective of this lunch at the Col du Tourmalet, where he spent his vacations as a child at his grandmother's, is eminently diplomatic: to break the imposing protocol to establish a more direct dialogue, particularly on Ukraine. "We understand the upheaval caused by the Ukrainian crisis for Europeans. China is not the cause of this crisis, and it is not a party to or participant in it," said Xi Jinping in a column published in Le Figaro. "We hope that peace and stability will quickly return to Europe, and we intend to work with France and the entire international community to find good ways to resolve the crisis," he added. On the sensitive issue of human rights, Emmanuel Macron prefers to discuss "disagreements" behind closed doors. Paris has also not made the Taiwan issue a priority, despite it being at the heart of strong tensions between the United States and China. Several hundred Tibetan activists gathered on Sunday at Place de la République in Paris to denounce China as "a predator" and "a colonial regime." "No to Chinese totalitarianism" could be read on a banner.

title: For Ukraine, the military aid from the United States should help to "stabilize the situation" and then "regain the initiative."
lead: The money released by Washington should be used to strengthen the front line as a new Russian offensive is expected in the coming weeks.
text_eng:
The Ukrainians have learned that in war, things rarely go as planned. Along with feelings of gratitude and relief after the US Congress vote on military aid to Ukraine on Saturday, April 20, there are many unanswered questions about the future of the conflict. Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky praised the American support, stating that it will "save thousands and thousands of lives" and keep history on the right path. He added that this support will be felt by soldiers on the front line, as well as by cities and villages suffering from Russian terror, and that it will bring a just end to a war that Putin must lose. The $60.8 billion (57 billion euros) aid from Washington will help the Ukrainian armed forces strengthen both the thousand kilometers of the front line and the anti-aircraft defense of cities.

In recent weeks, faced with the deadlock in the US Congress, Kiev had been increasingly pessimistic. The idea of a military collapse was no longer taboo. Zelensky had warned on April 7, "If Congress does not help Ukraine, Ukraine will lose the war." Ukrainians find it difficult to grasp the reality of the military situation and the concrete consequences of this new American aid. While they have been reading testimonies from fighters for months about the lack of ammunition against the Russian firepower, they also see that Moscow has not significantly advanced despite its military superiority.

The Russian army, which initially aimed to conquer Ukraine in a few days to overthrow the government and neutralize its armed forces, has only managed to take two cities in Donbass after eighteen months of stabilizing the front lines: Bakhmut and Avdiivka. The Russian offensive continues on these two fronts, currently threatening Tchassiv Yar, west of Bakhmut. The Ukrainian military commander, General Oleksandr Syrsky, recently mentioned "a significantly deteriorated situation" on the eastern front, while President Zelensky admitted in an interview with NBC after the vote in the House of Representatives that Ukraine has "lost the initiative" and that the new American aid should help "stabilize the situation" and then "regain the initiative."

For Ukrainians, these difficulties on the front line mainly mean suffering for the fighters. However, it is hard to imagine that the same Russian army, inching forward for months near Tchassiv Yar, could once again threaten the country with a general invasion. The prospect of a collapse of the front is not ruled out in military circles in Kiev, even if it is considered that catastrophic speeches may also have a tactical dimension aimed at obtaining more Western support. "Russia has regained the strategic advantage. The question is which major city could we lose this year before having the ability to go back on the offensive," testifies, anonymously, an officer from the general staff. The Russian offensive of 2024 is expected "between May and July," predicted President Zelensky, "in June," declared General Kyrylo Boudanov, head of Ukrainian military intelligence. The question is whether American military aid will help counter these bleak prospects. An officer from a combat unit, listing the military achievements accomplished, against all odds, by the Ukrainians over the past two years, puts into perspective, even if it were to happen, a potential significant territorial loss. Instead of talking about a collapse, he calmly mentions what could be "a bad year in a very long war."

title: In Georgia, the Parliament adopts the controversial bill on "foreign influence."
lead: The text is similar to the one passed in Russia in 2012 to silence opponents of Vladimir Putin. Demonstrations have been taking place every day for over three weeks in several cities in Georgia in an attempt to prevent its adoption.
text_eng: The Georgian Parliament adopted, on Tuesday, May 14, the controversial bill on "foreign influence", despite significant protests against the text. According to its critics, the law diverts the Caucasus country from Europe to bring it into Moscow's orbit. Thousands of Georgians marched in the streets following the adoption of the bill on Tuesday. Thirteen protesters were arrested "for disobeying police orders," according to the Ministry of Interior. The wife of activist David Katsarava claimed that her husband was beaten by the police after his arrest. During the third and final reading, lawmakers voted 84 to 30 in favor of the bill, as shown on public television. In debates leading to the bill's adoption, majority lawmakers physically clashed with opposition members, as seen on television. Around 2,000 protesters gathered in front of the Parliament in the late afternoon, surrounded by a large police presence. The Prime Minister of Georgia, Irakli Kobakhidze, had promised that the bill would be passed despite ongoing protests for over three weeks in several cities. The new law requires independent media and civil society organizations to register as entities "pursuing the interests of a foreign power" if they receive more than 20% of their funding from abroad. NGOs vow to never comply with the law. "I will never register as a foreign agent," warned Baia Pataraia, a leader of a major women's rights organization in Georgia. Non-compliance with the law could result in hefty fines or closure. Eka Gigauri, head of Transparency International's Georgian branch, also stated she would never submit to the foreign influence law. Georgian NGOs are extremely dependent on foreign funding. For the EU, it is seen as a "barrier to the European perspective." The text is similar to the one passed by the Russian Duma in 2012 to silence opponents of President Vladimir Putin, which Mr. Kobakhidze denies. For him, without this law, Georgia will "share the fate of Ukraine." This is the strong argument of the Georgian Dream party, the ruling party, convinced that the European Union (EU) and the United States are seeking to instrumentalize Georgia in order to "open a second front" against Russia in the Caucasus. "This law will be a serious obstacle for Georgia in its European perspective," said the EU spokesperson, Peter Stano, before the vote. Georgia has been an official candidate for EU membership since December 2023. Public opinion was not consulted, even though consultation between the political power and civil society is among the recommendations made by Brussels in the framework of Georgia's candidacy for accession, recognized in December 2023. The U.S. diplomacy, on the other hand, urged Georgia to "change course" after the adoption of this law. It is not yet "clear whether the Georgian Parliament will try to override a possible veto," emphasized the spokesperson for the U.S. executive, Karine Jean-Pierre. "We will see what the Parliament will do," but this law "will force us to fundamentally reassess our relationship with Georgia," she added. On his part, U.S. Deputy Secretary of State James O'Brien, who met with Georgian Prime Minister Irakli Kobakhidze in Tbilisi on Tuesday, warned that Washington would impose "financial sanctions and travel restrictions on individuals involved." In addition, the $390 million (360 million euros) aid allocated this year by the United States to Georgia would be "reviewed if we were to be considered adversaries rather than partners," he stressed. The foreign ministers of the Baltic countries and Iceland are traveling to Georgia to protest against the bill. "We call on the Georgian government to withdraw this bill and reaffirm its commitment to European integration," said Lithuanian Foreign Minister Gabrielius Landsbergis to Agence France-Presse, stating that the bill "was incompatible with the country's pro-European orientation." The Icelandic, Estonian, Latvian, and Lithuanian ministers plan to meet with Georgian President Salome Zourabichvili, who opposes the law, as well as the Speaker of Parliament and the Minister of Foreign Affairs, added Mr. Landsbergis. They are also expected to join the thousands of people who have taken to the streets to protest against the law, he added.

title: European elections 2024: Saidali Boina Hamissi, a candidate on the RN's list, criticized for his conspiracy theories and racist remarks.
lead: After the revelations by "Libération", two ministers denounced the online content published by the representative from Mayotte of the party, who will be on Jordan Bardella's European list, as announced by Marine Le Pen during a visit to Mayotte on Saturday.
text_eng: Two ministers strongly criticized, on Tuesday, April 23, the presence on the list of the National Rally (RN) of Saidali Boina Hamissi, a Mahoran whose Libération revealed had made conspiracy and racist remarks on his Facebook page. The ministers also called him out for his openly expressed admiration for Vladimir Putin and his support for polygamy. During a visit to Mayotte, the leader of the National Rally's deputies, Marine Le Pen, announced that the party's departmental representative, aged 63, was joining the list led by Jordan Bardella for the European elections, "probably in a non-eligible position, unless the voters decide otherwise." "It's a signal that is being sent to Mayotte, to say that, historically for the first time, there is a Mahoran candidate who will be included on a list for the European elections," she declared on Outremer La 1ère last Saturday. "As usual with Marine Le Pen and the National Rally, (...) they have allies whose statements they don't really check: this gentleman clearly admires [Russian President Vladimir] Putin, he supports polygamy," denounced the Minister Delegate for Overseas, Marie Guévenoux, on Sud Radio. Saidali Boina Hamissi "participated in conspiracy theories, made extremely serious and dangerous remarks about women's supposed submission to their spouses," criticized the government spokesperson, Prisca Thevenot, on LCI. On Monday, Libération had indeed denounced "violently racist and conspiratorial content" posted by the RN representative in Mayotte on his Facebook page. Among the posts highlighted by the newspaper were messages calling the Comorian residents of a neighborhood in Mamoudzou "vermin" and "cockroaches," as well as messages about women's submission to their husbands. There were also conspiracy posts in which Saidali Boina Hamissi expressed his rejection of anti-Covid vaccines, and "several recent posts [in which] he praises the Russian leader [Vladimir Putin] and denounces France's support for Ukraine," as reported by Libération.

title: Iranian drones sow terror in Ukraine.
lead: The Iranian Shahed drones ("martyr" in Persian) have become a major component of the indiscriminate bombings ordered by the Kremlin on Ukrainian territory.
text_eng: The drone war is raging in the skies of Ukraine. Kiev has long been able to rely on the undeniable superiority provided by the Turkish-made Bayraktar TB2 drones, with the possibility of local production in the future. These drones played a key role in halting the Russian offensive on February 24th, to the point of being celebrated in a song praising Ukrainian resistance. President Zelensky warmly welcomed Selçuk Bayraktar, the Turkish industrialist who gave his name to these drones and happens to be the son-in-law of the Turkish president, on September 9th: "Everyone in Ukraine knows what Bayraktar is and knows your company. Thank you and President Erdogan, thank you for your support and that of the Turkish citizens in this war against Russian aggression." Moscow is trying to compensate for such weakness by acquiring Iranian drones of the Shahed type since this summer, which were heavily used during the bombings on October 10th and 11th on civilian targets in Ukraine. Kamikaze drones Unlike the sophisticated Turkish Bayraktar drones, the Shahed drones are relatively simple. However, they have a range of 2,500 kilometers and can fly at over 185 kilometers per hour for the Shahed-136 model, of which hundreds have reportedly been delivered to the Russian army. They are produced by Iran Aircraft Manufacturing Industries, a company known by its Persian acronym HESA and placed on Western sanctions lists due to its involvement in the nuclear program. This type of drone has already carried out spectacular attacks, in September 2019, against oil installations in Saudi Arabia, and then in January of this year, against the territory of the United Arab Emirates, attacks claimed by pro-Iranian rebels in Yemen but largely attributed to Iran itself. These drones are called "kamikazes" because of their "sacrifice" during the raid. The Islamic Republic has chosen to call them "martyrs," a term so loaded that Russia has renamed them... "geranium-2." Thus, the Shahed-136 becomes Geran-2 in Russian! On September 13th, the Ukrainian army claims to have shot down a Shahed-136 not far from Kupiansk, a strategic locality in the north of Donbass, which was reconquered a few days later. On September 25th, these drones were used against an administrative building in Odessa, before a strike hit the Kiev region a good week later. It is now clear that the Shahed-136 drones are intended for deep strikes, while Ukrainian leaders denounce Russia's transfer of Shahed-136 to Belarusian territory in order to better target Kiev and the northeast of the country. Iranian drones, launched from Belarus or Crimea, are at the heart of the terror campaign carried out on October 10 and 11 by Russia against civilian targets across Ukraine. Kiev claims to have shot down thirteen out of the twenty-four drones launched on October 10, as well as the thirteen launched the following day, an assertion that is currently unverifiable. However, it reflects the determination of the Ukrainian government to confront this threat from Iran. From Syria to Ukraine, it is puzzling that a military superpower like Russia does not have its own drones. On the other hand, it is not surprising to see Iran supporting, even indirectly, the Russian aggression against Ukraine. Since March 2011, the two countries have coordinated their unconditional support for Bashar Al-Assad's crackdown on any form of dissent in Syria. It was the retreat of U.S. President Barack Obama in August 2013, despite Al-Assad's chemical bombing of Damascus suburbs, that convinced Putin to annex Ukrainian Crimea six months later. The Islamic Republic, with its shock troops engaged early on the Syrian stage (alongside pro-Iranian militias from Lebanon and Iraq), pressured Moscow to also engage directly, which was done in September 2015. It is in Syria that the Kremlin normalizes terror techniques against the civilian population, which are later applied in Ukraine. General Sergei Surovikin, recently appointed to lead the "special military operation" against Ukraine, has left the worst memories in Syria, just like his predecessor, General Alexander Dvornikov, who earned the nickname "butcher of Syria." It was indeed in Syria that the Russian war machine should have been stopped at all costs before, emboldened by its impunity, it inevitably turned towards the European continent. The realization of such a sequence came too late to avoid the ongoing tragedy. It is all the more urgent to emphasize that the same Shahed-136 drones that have brought mourning to Ukrainian cities are the ones the Tehran regime uses to strike Kurdish opponents even on Iraqi territory. While dissent continues unabated in Iran, a month after the fatal beating of young Mahsa Amini, the slogan "Woman, Life, Freedom" continues to galvanize anti-regime demonstrations. This demand for a free life, in the face of blind repression, is not fundamentally different from the admirable resistance that the Ukrainian population opposes to the Russian invasion. If one were to forget, instruments of death, called "martyr" or "kamikaze," are there to remind in the skies of Ukraine.

title: War in Ukraine: Belarusian opposition's marathon to establish official relations with Kiev
lead: Former diplomat Valery Kavaleuski, advisor to exiled opposition leader Svetlana Tsikhanovskaya, has been sent on a mission to Ukraine to establish contacts with the authorities.
text_eng: The decoration of the office set up in an imposing building in the Ukrainian capital is limited to two flags, discreet but enlightening clues: the blue and yellow of Ukraine; the red and white of democratic Belarus. The main objective of the representation of Belarusian democratic forces, opened in June, is to establish official relations with the authorities in Kiev and to secure a meeting, which has not yet taken place, between the main figure of the opposition, Svetlana Tsikhanovskaya, and the Ukrainian president, Volodymyr Zelensky. "We wish for a relationship that would allow us to adopt a joint approach on Belarus and on how we could win the war in Ukraine," explains Valery Kavaleuski, in charge of foreign affairs for the opposition leader, visiting Kiev on Monday, October 17. The Belarusian delegate, who is no stranger to Ukraine, has been sent on a mission to establish contacts with the Kiev authorities. "We have appointments and unofficial meetings with officials from the Ukrainian government and members of the Verkhovna Rada," the Ukrainian Parliament, he explains, without going into details. Resistance from within Valery Kavaleuski is a former diplomat who worked for a time in the United States before joining the ranks of the democratic opposition that emerged in August 2020. At the time, the dictator Alexander Lukashenko's reelection with 80% of the vote sparked an unprecedented wave of protests in the country, harshly suppressed by the Minsk regime. The main opposition candidate in the presidential election, Svetlana Tsikhanovskaya, has since been living in exile in Vilnius, the Lithuanian capital, like the majority of opposition members and civil society. Many of these organizations have also sought refuge in Ukraine. At the end of February, as Moscow's forces launched their offensive on the Ukrainian capital from Belarusian territory, the democratic opposition began a communication campaign to unite forces between the two countries. "For us, Ukraine is a priority, as its fate is closely linked to the internal situation in Belarus," explains the charge d'affaires. Ukrainians know that they will necessarily have to deal with Lukashenko's regime to win this war. It is crucial that Belarus becomes democratic and no longer poses a threat. The opposition relies on the support of Western partners, its extensive network of organizations established abroad, and its ability to act directly on Belarusian territory to convince Ukraine. In Vilnius, Svetlana Tsikhanovskaya's team has a wealth of information on the situation in the country. At the beginning of the war, Belarusian resistance fighters notably distinguished themselves by disrupting the railway traffic on which the Russian forces depended to transport weapons and military vehicles. This network, called the "railway partisans," is dependent on a structure called Bypol, which brings together former Belarusian police officers who resigned after the repression of the summer of 2020. From their exile, these individuals organize resistance from within. "These people work in Belarus, on the ground," emphasizes Valery Kavaleuski. "It is important for Ukrainians to understand that we can be useful to Ukraine at the international level, but also, and most importantly, in Belarus." Some indifference The numerous calls from the Belarusian opposition to establish official relations are currently met with some indifference from the side of Kiev. And the silence was already noticeable before the war. After the 2020 protests, the Ukrainian authorities had discreetly supported the opposition movement and refrained from imposing economic sanctions, unlike other countries in the region, such as Lithuania or Poland. "In a way, Ukrainians contributed to keeping Lukashenko in power during difficult times, while Western countries, the European Union, and the United States imposed sanctions against his regime," laments the opponent. However, the diplomat assures understanding the current caution of Ukrainians. "If we compare with the position of France or Germany, for example, the stakes are not as high as for Ukraine." The establishment of official relations between the Belarusian opposition in exile and Kiev could be considered a "provocation" in the eyes of the Belarusian leader, as he "can still inflict damage and harm the Ukrainian population. The Belarusian territory is used by Russia to launch attacks since the beginning of the war," Valery Kavaleuski recalls. On an international scale, discussions and exchanges with Ukrainian diplomats stationed in foreign capitals are simpler and clearer. "They are always willing to meet, exchange, and listen to us. The mutual interest is there."

title: "France: The Putin networks" on France 2: between seduction and disinformation
lead: An investigation for "Complément d'enquête" explores, from RT France to connections in Africa, passing through the digital tracking unit Viginum, how the Kremlin plays cat and mouse with French politics.
text_eng: FRANCE 2 - THURSDAY, OCTOBER 27 AT 11 PM - DOCUMENTARY On this February 11, Frédéric Petit, a member of the Democratic group (MoDem and Independents) representing the 7th constituency of French citizens living abroad (Germany, Central Europe, and the Balkans), cannot foresee that he will be trapped when entering the Luxembourg Palace. Believing he is attending a parliamentary meeting on the situation in Ukraine, his speech is broadcast live on a pro-Russian Ukrainian television channel, interspersed with statements from close associates of Vladimir Putin, such as the Ukrainian oligarch Viktor Medvedchuk or the deputy Leonid Slutsky. The latter is "banned from Europe," specifies Laure Pollez, one of the three journalists who investigated the Poutine networks in France, aired on the evening of Thursday, October 27, but the only one to appear on screen. The documentary, as we have seen it, does not mention that Viktor Medvedchuk was arrested on April 12 by Ukrainian forces, detained, and exchanged on September 22. Instead, it focuses on uncovering how such a deception was orchestrated. After this enticing introduction, Vladimir Putin's charm offensive towards French (right-wing) leaders is illustrated by a segment dedicated to the relationship between the Russian president and François Fillon, tracing back to their friendship's origins in Sochi in 2008 when both men were prime ministers. The Kremlin's connections with members of the RN (Marine Le Pen, Thierry Mariani, Philippe Olivier) are showcased through exclusive documents provided by The Dossier Center, an organization led by Vladimir Putin's opponent, Mikhail Khodorkovsky, and through testimonies from diplomats. The final part, focusing on information manipulation, unfolds like fiction. It starts with a report on RT France, the Russian channel banned from broadcasting since March 2 (the documentary explains why), where only the presenter Samantha Ramsamy speaks openly. Standing up to Western powers, Laure Pollez's investigations then take her to Africa (where RT is broadcast) to dissect the false "mass grave" incident in Gossi, Mali - two days after the official departure of the "Barkhane" force from that base, a tweet was posted on April 21: "This is what the French left behind," with a photo of buried bodies. "Wagner's militiamen staged this scene," claims France; Russia denies it. The inquiry provides a unique glimpse into the workings of Viginum, the new French unit established to combat foreign interference. It reveals how MediaPatriot, the media group owned by Yevgeny Prigozhin (head of the Wagner mercenary group), attempted to discredit Emmanuel Macron before the 2022 presidential election. But who is behind these fake articles? How are they published? An article provided ready-made is paid 300 euros to be published in Cotonou (Benin). Russia sends aid and stands up to Westerners, explains an African journalist, "and it is appreciated." Disinformation has a bright future ahead. Despite its ban, RT France is once again broadcasting its programs live and in French via the conspiracy platform Odysee.

title: By withdrawing from the cereal agreement, Moscow is betting on a worsening of the global food crisis.
lead: Depriving Kiev once again of the ability to sell its agricultural production through the Black Sea could worsen the global food crisis.
text_eng: The agreement on grain exports from Ukrainian ports on the Black Sea, the only significant compromise negotiated between Kiev and Moscow since the beginning of the conflict in February, has collapsed. Even though cargo movements to and from these ports had not immediately stopped on Monday, October 31, the decision announced by the Russian side on Saturday raises concerns of a new increase in wheat prices and a worsening of the global food crisis. The agreement, named the "Black Sea grain initiative," was negotiated in July under the auspices of the UN and Turkey. It allowed Ukraine to export millions of tons of grains that were previously subject to a strict Russian blockade in the Black Sea, where the grain ports are located. On Sunday evening, Russian representatives at the Joint Coordination Center, responsible for inspecting ships passing through the Bosphorus Strait, announced their departure from this body "for an indefinite period." The fate of the remaining ships in transit is still uncertain. "Insignificant" damages Moscow justifies its decision by blaming a "terrorist attack" attributed to the Ukrainian army, whose drones reportedly struck the Russian fleet based in annexed Crimea's Sevastopol on the night of Friday to Saturday. Described as "massive" by Russia, the strike allegedly caused only "insignificant" damage to a Russian navy minesweeper, without any casualties, according to Moscow. Ukrainian sources present a different picture of this attack, which they claim involved the joint use of aerial and maritime drones. According to them, four Russian military ships were damaged, including the fleet's flagship, the frigate Admiral Makarov, used to launch Kalibr missiles towards Ukraine and which replaced the cruiser Moskva, sunk in April. The Kiev government and its Western allies are held responsible by Moscow, which accuses Ukraine of carrying out the attack against the Black Sea fleet with the support of the UK. Russian diplomacy mentions the involvement of "British specialists" in the operation without providing evidence. Russia also alleges that civilian ships supposedly transporting grain may have been used in this context. On Monday, Russia carried out new attacks on infrastructure throughout Ukrainian territory. On Sunday, President Zelensky accused Moscow of blocking Ukrainian grain-carrying ships in the Black Sea since September. Was the Sevastopol incident, humiliating as it was for Moscow, used as a pretext? On Sunday, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky called the Russian decision "predictable" and accused Moscow of blocking Ukrainian grain-carrying ships in the Black Sea since September. Currently, he revealed, more than 170 ships are stranded at sea, carrying about 2 million tons of goods. "This is an obvious attempt by Russia to resort once again to the threat of widespread famine in Africa and Asia," he emphasized in a video address from Kiev, broadcasted on Saturday night. On Sunday, the European Union, NATO, and the United Nations also urged Russia to reconsider its decision. The Russian withdrawal should not come as a surprise, as the grain agreement was hanging by a thread. Signed in Istanbul on July 22 under the auspices of the United Nations and Turkish diplomacy, it was set to last for 120 days. On November 19, it was due to be renewed or expire. However, in recent months, Russia has been trying to reject it, even claiming that it had "aggravated" the global food crisis. The Russian position was clear well before the attack on Sevastopol: Vladimir Putin had complained to his Turkish counterpart, Recep Tayyip Erdogan, about the disadvantages of the agreement, stating that his country was unable to export its cereals and fertilizers not due to direct sanctions, but because of logistical and financial disorganization. Russian exports had dropped by 22% in July and August compared to the previous year, also affected by a strong ruble. This stance was reiterated by Maria Zakharova, the spokesperson for the Russian Ministry of Foreign Affairs, on Friday, October 28, during a press briefing in Moscow. "This situation does not suit us," she declared. "The accounts of some banks are frozen, so payments cannot be made, all because of Western sanctions." On the same day, the United Nations Secretary-General, Antonio Guterres, urged Moscow and Kiev to renew the agreement. Market stabilization Since the first ship left Odessa on July 28, more than 9 million tons of foodstuffs have been exported, with over 5 million tons going to African and Asian countries. The establishment of this corridor helped stabilize markets and lower prices, which had reached record levels during the Russian invasion of Ukraine. For Kiev, these exports brought in foreign currency and prevented the stored grain from spoiling during the spring. However, Moscow claims that the agreement was undermined by the fact that some of the cereals were exported to Europe, a point not mentioned in the document. By exiting the agreement, the Russian government hopes to regain flexibility, especially as its military situation is challenging and Europe is proving less vulnerable than expected to its energy blackmail. The Russian Minister of Agriculture, Dmitri Patrushev, who is also the son of the Secretary General of the Security Council, Nikolai Patrushev, stated that his country was ready to "provide up to 500,000 tons of cereals for free to the poorest countries over the next four months," with the support of Turkey. Ankara, which played a leading role in the "cereal initiative," has not commented on the Russian withdrawal. Eleven ships were still able to be inspected on Sunday before Moscow's representatives withdrew definitively. Meanwhile, Russian exports continue freely. An investigation by the Financial Times, published on October 30, also shows how Russia manages to export cereals harvested in the occupied areas of southern Ukraine by passing them off as Russian grain.

title: Mysterious drone thefts worry Norway: seven Russians arrested within a few days.
lead: In response to the invasion of Ukraine, Norway, like several other Western countries, has banned Russians from flying over its territory, whether by plane or drone. Any violation is punishable by three years in prison.
text_eng: Norway, which is now the main supplier of gas to Europe, is alarmed by the increasing number of mysterious drone flights on its territory, with suspicions pointing to Russia, believed to be conducting more espionage activities since the beginning of the war in Ukraine. Amid numerous reports of unmanned aircraft flights, some near offshore oil platforms, the Norwegian police announced on Wednesday, October 19, the arrest of a Russian citizen, the seventh person detained in just a few days. All are accused of illegally flying drones or violating photography restrictions in the kingdom, which shares a border with Russia in the Arctic. On Wednesday, a few hours after a new drone flight briefly disrupted air traffic at Bergen Airport (West), the country's second-largest city, Norwegian Prime Minister Jonas Gahr Store indirectly pointed the finger at Russia. "It is obviously unacceptable for foreign intelligence to fly drones over Norwegian airports," he said, as reported by NRK. "The Russians do not have the right to fly drones in Norway." In response to the invasion of Ukraine, Norway, like several other Western countries, has banned Russians from flying over its territory, whether by plane or drone. Any violation is punishable by three years in prison. The Russian embassy in Oslo denounces a "psychosis." Now in charge of investigating the various incidents involving drones, the Norwegian counterintelligence services (PST) say they cannot draw conclusions at this stage. "There is an increased risk of espionage from Russia due to the war in Ukraine," said PST's deputy director, Hedvig Moe, at a press conference on Wednesday. "And we consider the threat of sabotage against Norway to be more real than it was before the war," she emphasized, although she noted it remains "unlikely." Drone sightings and the alleged sabotage of the Nord Stream 1 and 2 gas pipelines in the Baltic Sea at the end of September have prompted Oslo to enhance security around its strategic, particularly energy-related, infrastructure. Ships, aircraft, and National Guard troops have been deployed. According to security services, drones could be used for espionage purposes or to sow fear about Norway's ability to ensure the security of its energy deliveries. Since the reduction in Russian gas supplies, Norwegian gas is considered essential to meet Europe's energy needs. Criticized by Russia, the arrests of Russian citizens demonstrate the increased vigilance of Norwegian authorities. The latest arrest involves Andrei Yakunin, son of former Russian Railways chief Vladimir Yakunin, who is considered close to Russian President Vladimir Putin. Aged 47 and holding dual Russian and British nationality, he was arrested on Monday in Hammerfest in northern Norway. "The suspect admitted to flying a drone in Svalbard," said police official Anja Mikkelsen. Located a thousand kilometers from the North Pole, Svalbard is a Norwegian archipelago strategically located in the heart of the Arctic. Due to its special legal status allowing foreign nationals to exploit its resources, this territory is home to a fairly large Russian mining community. The Russian Embassy in Oslo, on the other hand, criticizes the "psychosis" that, according to them, has taken hold of Norway. On its Facebook page, it lamented the ban imposed on Russians from flying drones, calling it an "unjustified" and "discriminatory" measure.

title: Oleksii Reznikov, the Ukrainian Minister of Defense, does not believe that Putin will use nuclear weapons.
lead: He claims that Moscow, with its accusations of a "dirty bomb," is primarily seeking to undermine the support of Western countries.
text_eng: During a press conference held on Wednesday, October 26, in Kiev, the Ukrainian Minister of Defense, Oleksii Reznikov, stated that he did not believe that the Russian President, Vladimir Putin, was considering using nuclear weapons in Ukraine. According to the former lawyer, the Russian forces "have done many stupid things since the beginning of the war, but they have continued to be pragmatic." The nuclear threat has escalated as Russia, in recent days, has been making accusations against Ukraine, claiming that Ukraine is preparing a "dirty bomb," a conventional weapon (such as a missile) combined with radioactive materials. Ukraine and its Western allies have denied these allegations, while asserting that Russia, facing difficulties on the military front, might precisely attempt to detonate a "dirty bomb" in order to justify, in advance, the use of a form of nuclear weapon. The stakes of relations with China and India On Wednesday, October 26, for the first time, Mr. Putin echoed the accusations of senior Russian officials regarding the use of a "dirty bomb" for provocation. During the day, the Russian army conducted its annual nuclear deterrence exercise with missile tests capable of carrying nuclear warheads. The Minister of Defense believes, however, that Russia would not risk using nuclear weapons, notably due to the crucial importance of its relations with China and India. The Russians "are trying to have agreements with these countries, especially to provide them with gas and oil. However, these countries have sent them very clear signals: do not cross the red line of nuclear weapons," stated Oleksii Reznikov. Ukraine's allies have informed Russia that the use of nuclear weapons would have catastrophic consequences for the country. According to the Minister of Defense, the nuclear threat is primarily aimed at undermining the support of Western countries. Regarding the Ukrainian counteroffensive to reclaim the occupied region of Kherson in the south, he acknowledged that the situation was more complicated than during the counteroffensive in the east of the country in early September. Oleksii Reznikov explained that the rainy season makes the movement of combat vehicles "very difficult" and expressed his desire to spare his troops: "We do not share [with the Russians] the philosophy of cannon fodder, we try to keep our soldiers alive." For Bakhmut, a city in the Donbass region under Russian fire for months, the minister simply reiterated the army's talking points: "the situation is difficult, but under control."

title: War in Ukraine: Vladimir Putin declares martial law in the annexed territories
lead: The Russian president justified this measure by stating that Kiev was refusing to negotiate with Moscow and, according to him, was continuing to "bomb" the civilian population in these regions. Martial law will come into effect starting Thursday at midnight.
text_eng: Facing the advance of the Ukrainian army, Russian President Vladimir Putin ordered, on Wednesday, October 19, the imposition of martial law in the four Ukrainian territories of Donetsk, Luhansk, Kherson, and Zaporizhia, which are partially occupied by Moscow and officially annexed by the Russian Federation in September. Mr. Putin announced this measure during a televised meeting of his security council. Martial law will come into effect from Thursday at midnight, a measure that the Ukrainian diplomacy described as "null and void." These territories "had already imposed martial law before their attachment to the Russian Federation, but [it was] necessary to formalize this regime in accordance with Russian legislation," stated Mr. Putin. He justified this measure by claiming that Kiev refused to negotiate with Moscow and continued, in his view, to "bomb" the civilian population in these regions. "The [Ukrainian] neo-Nazis openly use terrorist methods. (...) They send groups of saboteurs to our territory," he added, denouncing notably the attack on the Crimean bridge and "nuclear facilities," without specifying which ones. Enhanced security regime According to the published decree, the Russian government must propose concrete measures to be implemented in these territories within three days. The Russian Constitution provides a series of provisions in case of martial law imposition (mentioned in article 87): strengthening security measures, curfew, prohibition of public gatherings, evacuation of strategic enterprises, prohibition of leaving the affected territories, isolation of foreign citizens, establishment of military censorship in telecommunications... "Vladimir Putin now finds himself in an incredibly difficult situation," commented Joe Biden from the White House. "It seems that the only tool left at his disposal is to persecute Ukrainian citizens" in order to "intimidate them into surrender, but that is not what they will do," added the American President. The decree signed by Mr. Putin also establishes an enhanced security regime, described as a "medium-level reaction regime," in the annexed Crimean peninsula since 2014, as well as in the Russian regions of Krasnodar, Rostov-on-Don, Belgorod, Bryansk, Voronezh, and Kursk, located near Ukraine. In the central federal district, where the capital Moscow is located, an enhanced alert regime is also established, according to this decree. This enhanced regime in Moscow will not, "at this stage," bring any changes to the daily life of the city's residents, stated its mayor, Sergei Sobyanin. "No measures are being implemented to limit the normal pace of life in the city," he emphasized. "Poutine, we will take the necessary measures to improve the security of civilian and critical facilities," said the mayor of Moscow, where there is already a significant police presence. Fear of a "deportation" of the residents of Kherson In addition, the Russian administration of the Ukrainian region of Kherson assured on Wednesday that the evacuation of civilians had begun. It plans to move "50,000 to 60,000" people to the other side of the Dnieper in a few days, as the Russian army struggles to contain the Ukrainian counter-offensive in the area. According to the secretary of the Ukrainian National Security and Defense Council, Oleksiy Danilov, what is happening is more like "the preparation for the massive deportation of the Ukrainian population" to Russia "in order to change the ethnic composition of the occupied territories." "A crime that should be condemned by the United Nations and that has already been committed in Crimea," he added. "Russia is carrying out deportations, like in the Soviet era," said Ukrainian deputy Sergiy Khlan. "Its goal is to create a kind of panic in Kherson and an image [to fuel] Russian propaganda." "If you look at the list of things that collaborators recommend taking with them, it is a list of items for a one-way trip," Mr. Khlan also criticized, stating that, in his opinion, the residents of Kherson would be evacuated not to the Crimean Peninsula, bordering the region, but to the Krasnodar Peninsula in the Kuban region of Russia itself. Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky also warned residents of the annexed regions against a forced recruitment campaign by the Russian army. "Try to leave the occupied territory. And if you cannot do so and find yourself in Russian military units, try at the first opportunity to lay down your arms and move towards Ukrainian positions," he said.

title: At the Frankfurt Book Fair, Volodymyr Zelensky urges authors to describe the terror imposed by Russia.
lead: The President of Ukraine communicated via video link on Thursday, October 20, during the event that is hosting forty-six Ukrainian publishers until Sunday, with no Russian stands.
text_eng: "Instead of importing culture, Russia imports death," declared Volodymyr Zelensky on Thursday, October 20, in a speech broadcast via video from Kyiv at the Frankfurt Book Fair, one of the main global publishing events held in Germany until Sunday, October 23. According to the Ukrainian president, the two major absentees from this edition, Russia and Iran, are "less present in the cultural field," but "increasingly present where everything is destroyed." Like he did at the last Cannes Film Festival, the Ukrainian president intertwines culture, politics, and soft power, as his country is under fire from Russian bombs. He urged publishers and authors from around the world: "Do everything you can to make known the extent of the terror that Russia has brought to Ukraine and, before, to Chechnya, Georgia, Syria, and African countries with the mercenaries of the Wagner group." He emphasized: "Books, scripts, articles, reports are the responses" to the ignorance of the "murders committed by the Russians," of the "corruption," of this "anti-democratic regime." In his view, this "lack of knowledge" explains the leniency that the Russian president receives from "many public figures in Europe," from various spheres: "politics, business, NGOs, and media." Working under bombs Forty-six Ukrainian publishers are participating in the Frankfurt Book Fair. Over thirty have made the journey, sometimes under difficult conditions. "It took us between two and three days with buses, trains...," details Yulia Naidukh from the Ukrainian Book Institute. They are all there to defend the national cultural vitality. "No Russian publishing house has condemned the war or expressed support for Ukraine," noted Oleksandr Afonin, president of the Association of Ukrainian Publishers and Bookstores. This is why "it is important for us that there is no Russian pavilion" at the Fair, says Zoia Shevchuk, who also works for the Ukrainian Book Institute. Only notable opponents of Vladimir Putin are welcome this year. Such as Leonid Volkov, a close associate of Alexei Navalny, or Irina Sherbakova, a member of the Russian NGO Memorial, co-recipient of the Nobel Peace Prize. Russian science fiction writer Dmitry Glukhovsky, who has denounced the war in Ukraine, is also expected. On the stage of the Ukrainian booth, many guests come and testify. Above the platform, a bright red light flashes every time the air raid sirens go off in Ukraine. It is impossible to forget the war. This is the challenge for Ukrainian publishers: to continue working under bombs. "Nobody really understands, but if we don't work, it's worse, we feel like we're dying, we only think about war, bombings, and family members who stayed in Ukraine," says Alena Litovska, an editor at Kalamar, a publishing house specializing in children's books. Having left her husband in Kyiv and moved to Austria with her two children, she can finally present some of her new releases. Her printer, the only one producing pop-up and pull-tab books, is located in Kharkiv. In the eye of the storm. At the same booth, Taiwanese publisher Rex How, CEO of Locus Publishing Company, was presenting his latest work, fifty speeches by Volodymyr Zelensky delivered since 2019. In its next edition, he will be able to add the one from the Frankfurt Book Fair.

title: Brazilian presidential election: Lula's victory brings relief to foreign heads of state, from Macron to Biden
lead: "Together, we will unite our forces," assured the French president, while his American counterpart praised "free, fair, and credible elections."
text_eng: The victory of Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva, after four years of power by the far-right Bolsonaro government, was met with great relief in foreign capitals. The first congratulations came from newly elected left-wing presidents in Latin America - for the first time, the five largest economies on the continent are governed by the left. "Long live Lula," tweeted Gustavo Petro, the Colombian head of state, even before the final results had been announced. "Lula. Joy!" followed Gabriel Boric in Chile, Alberto Fernandez in Argentina, Pedro Castillo in Peru, Xiomara Castro in Honduras, Luis Arce in Bolivia, and Andres Manuel Lopez Obrador in Mexico. Outside of the American continent, one of the first heads of state to speak out was French President Emmanuel Macron, who referred to the election as "opening a new chapter in Brazil's history." "Together, we will unite our forces to face the many common challenges and renew the bond of friendship between our two countries," he promised. Relations between Jair Bolsonaro and Emmanuel Macron had been terrible. A violent dispute had erupted between them in August 2019, against the backdrop of fires in the Amazon. Lula aims to redirect Brazilian diplomacy. American President Joe Biden praised the "free, fair, and credible elections." Madrid mentioned an opportunity for "social justice" and the fight "against climate change," while Berlin called it "a victory for democracy and the climate." Russian President Vladimir Putin also congratulated the winner, calling for "constructive cooperation" between the two countries.
