A similar reaction came from Beijing, where a spokesperson for the Ministry of Foreign Affairs expressed a desire to elevate the partnership with Brazil to "a higher level." Over time, Jair Bolsonaro had become a pariah on the international stage. Apart from Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orban, who had supported him before the first round of the presidential election, Trump supporters, and a few oil-rich Middle Eastern monarchies, the far-right leader had few allies left. Lula supported his intention to redirect Brazilian diplomacy by emphasizing the centrality of the climate emergency and by sealing a strategic partnership with the European Union through a renewed free trade agreement between Mercosur and the Twenty-Seven.

title: "The economic sanctions against Russia are effective, but only in the long term."
lead: Agathe Demarais, Chief Economist of the Economist Intelligence Unit, had to manage the implementation of sanctions against Russia in 2014.
text_eng: In 2014, Agathe Demarais was a commercial attaché at the French Embassy in Moscow, where she had to deal with the sanctions imposed against Russia following the invasion of Crimea and the war in Donbass. Today, as Chief Economist at the Economist Intelligence Unit, she is publishing a book in November on the subject of sanctions, titled "Backfire: How Sanctions Reshape the World Against US Interests" (Columbia University Press, untranslated). The main weapon used by Western countries after the invasion of Ukraine was that of sanctions. Does it work? It all depends on what the initial objective was. The first objective was to send a strong message to Russia, one that was neither a simple diplomatic statement nor an act of war. From that perspective, it has been successful. I believe Putin did not expect such a united reaction from Western countries. The oil embargo, which will soon be in place, is hurting. Certainly, Russia is now selling its oil to China and India, but at a significant discount. Currently, the rise in oil prices caused by the war is offsetting this, but next year, the global economic slowdown is expected to lead to a decrease in prices. The second objective was to complicate the financing of the war. Again, this is working: the Russian economy is estimated to lose about four percentage points of GDP this year. This is a significant impact, even if it is less than the initial forecasts. The third objective is the slow suffocation of the Russian economy. Russia is a rentier economy, heavily reliant on the exploitation of raw materials, which requires access to financing and cutting-edge technologies, especially for drilling in the Arctic. China can replace some of the financing, but it lacks the necessary technologies. Currently, Russia is living off its reserves. However, the sanctions have not brought an end to the war or crippled the Russian economy... Sanctions work, but only in the long term. They are a slow poison that takes time to take effect. In Iran, it took ten years of sanctions before the regime agreed to compromise and sign the nuclear deal. Are sanctions counterproductive because they have a heavy economic cost, especially in Europe? It is Russia that imposes this cost, not the sanctions. There are no sanctions on gas, but Putin decided to gradually use this weapon this summer by cutting off the supply. Similarly, the rise in oil prices after the start of the war is not a result of the sanctions: the oil embargo will only be in place at the beginning of next year. It is essential to be cautious and not listen to what Russia is saying, as it is trying to divide Western countries on the subject and fuel extremism. Poutine had tried to make people believe that the sanctions were blocking the export of grains and cereals and risking causing hunger in the world. That was false. The Russians had imposed a blockade on Ukrainian ports and the Ukrainians had trapped them, but there were no sanctions on food exports. Did Moscow manage to take measures to counter the sanctions? The Russian central bank managed to calm the financial panic at the beginning of the war. However, it had to impose strict capital controls. Regarding oil, Russia managed to find other outlets, but with gas, it's not really possible: the pipelines connected to China do not compensate for exports to Europe. As for semiconductors, it's difficult to know. There are rumors that they go through Serbia and Turkey.

title: Russia: shooting that killed at least eleven on a military base raises questions
lead: Soldiers were killed on Saturday near Belgorod in what the Russian Ministry of Defense is presenting as "a terrorist attack." Other sources mention Muslim soldiers who refused to fight.
text_eng: Two days after the incident, the deadly shooting that occurred on Saturday, October 15 at the Russian military base in Soloti, in the Belgorod region, continues to raise many questions. These questions stem from the minimal communication from the Russian Ministry of Defense, which only issued a brief statement ten hours after the incident. According to the ministry, eleven soldiers were killed and fifteen others were injured in what is described as a "terrorist attack" carried out by "two citizens from a CIS country" - the Commonwealth of Independent States, comprising nine former Soviet republics. "During a shooting training exercise (...), the terrorists opened fire with automatic weapons" before being shot down "in a return fire," the military further writes, without providing more details on the identity of the victims and the perpetrators of the shooting. The exact number of casualties is also subject to debate. Several independent media outlets have cited witnesses who mentioned a higher number of fatalities. Sota, one of the very few independent media outlets still maintaining teams in Russia, reports a death toll of twenty-two and sixteen injured, based on an anonymous testimony. The same source also mentions a third assailant who managed to escape. Independent media and bloggers accustomed to relaying information from the army or the FSB (Russian security service) also agree that these assailants were Tajik citizens who volunteered for the "special operation" in Ukraine. Since September, Moscow has been offering Russian nationality to foreigners who volunteer. "Cannon fodder" Astra, a media outlet formed in exile by former members of several dissolved publications, quotes another witness who provides a fairly detailed, albeit independently unverifiable, account of the events. According to this source, who is reportedly among the injured, a conflict arose when three mobilized soldiers from different Muslim regions of Russia stated they did not want to fight. The senior officer at the base then gathered the soldiers to rally them, mentioning a "holy war" and asserting that "Allah must be a coward if he does not allow you to fight for your country." These words reportedly sparked outrage among Muslim soldiers and even led to a confrontation. It was an hour and a half later, as a training session was about to begin, that the two or three Tajiks allegedly opened fire on their comrades, armed with automatic weapons and a machine gun. This source mentions twenty-nine killed, including the senior officer. It also claims that the Tajiks in question had repeatedly complained about not being able to perform their prayers. There is also doubt regarding the identity of the victims. Since the end of September, several testimonies have revealed that the Soloti base houses around a hundred conscripts from the Bryansk region who refuse to go into combat. These soldiers claim that their officers want to send them into battle in the Lyman region after minimal training. On October 15th, a group of women from the Bryansk region even published a video addressing Vladimir Putin to explain that their husbands and sons were being treated as "cannon fodder," untrained, subjected to pressure, and even theft. Accidents and suicides The precise identity of the victims - conscripts or volunteers - is an important point, as the mobilization declared on September 21st has caused tensions in Russia. The first deaths are now being sent back from the front. In total, seventeen conscripts have also died on Russian soil (not counting those from Soloti), victims of accidents, sudden health problems, or suicides. Among the many violations of the rules meant to regulate the mobilization, the systematic absence of medical examinations or even brief interviews is highlighted. Even observers close to the government fear that the army is incorporating thousands of men with unsuitable profiles. On October 14th, Vladimir Putin stated that the mobilization process could last another two weeks. However, recent days have seen an intensification of these efforts. Videos from across Russia show men being arrested on the streets or in public transport, and recruiters going as far as positioning themselves in building lobbies to distribute summonses. Independent reports also mention summonses being handed out in recent days to Central Asian nationals with only residence permits.

title: Kazakh oil under Russian surveillance
lead: Four-fifths of the black gold that Astana exports passes through Russian territory to reach the Black Sea, via the Caspian Pipeline Consortium, of which Moscow is a 31% shareholder. This pipeline is vital for the European Union.
text_eng: Suddenly, the autumnal torpor of Astana, the Kazakh capital battered by the winds of the steppes, gives way to a disconcerting security frenzy. On this Wednesday, October 12th, thousands of police officers are stationed every 100 meters along the main avenues. Entire streets are closed to traffic. Vehicles are randomly searched around the airport. Schoolchildren are asked to stay at home. The reason: the city is hosting a series of regional summits until the weekend, including a meeting between the Russian President, Vladimir Putin, and his Turkish counterpart, Recep Tayyip Erdogan. Their host, the Kazakh President, Kassym-Jomart Tokayev, displays the diplomatic smile required. Nothing on his face hints at the delicate line he is walking. "He does not support the war in Ukraine and is increasingly distancing himself from Moscow, but he absolutely cannot afford to upset Putin," summarizes Maximilian Hess, a specialist in Eurasia at the Foreign Policy Research Institute, an American think tank. And for good reason: the Kazakh economy is highly dependent on Russia - it accounts for 11.5% of exports and 42.1% of imports - with the two countries sharing a 7,500-kilometer border to the north. Moreover, 80% of the oil that Kazakhstan exports passes through Russian territory to reach the port of Novorossiysk on the Black Sea via the Caspian Pipeline Consortium (CPC), in which Moscow holds a 31% stake. This pipeline is also vital for the European Union (EU): the oil that flows through it represents 56% of Romania's black gold imports, 25.6% of Italy's, 15.2% of France's, and 8% of the Netherlands'. Russia does not hesitate to leverage this dependence. Since the start of the war in Ukraine on February 24th, it has already suspended the delivery of Kazakh oil via the CPC four times. Officially, for various maintenance reasons. In reality, to remind Astana to fall in line whenever it asserts its independence from Moscow too strongly. "Blackmail" On June 19th, transit was interrupted for several days in response to the St. Petersburg summit: Tokayev had stated, in front of Putin, that he did not recognize the separatist republics of Luhansk and Donetsk. On July 6th, there was another cutoff: two days earlier, Kazakhstan had expressed readiness to send more oil to the EU. "In some ways, this is reminiscent of the blackmail imposed on Europe around the Nord Stream gas pipelines," notes Lucas Anceschi, an expert on Eurasia at the University of Glasgow (Scotland). And this comes at a cost to the former Soviet Republic: black gold accounts for 60% of its exports and 40% of its budget revenues. "A month of pipeline suspension means one less percentage point of GDP for the year," calculate experts at the World Bank. "A senior Kazakh official fears that if Russia were to cut it off permanently, our economy would collapse. The Kazakh oil sector mainly developed after the country's independence in 1991. To understand what is at stake for this Central Asian country, a look at a map of the region is necessary. Its territory is vast – five times the size of France – and its population is small (19 million inhabitants). Above all, 'it has no access to a sea other than the Caspian, and is sandwiched between Russia and China, placing it, despite itself, at the heart of the geopolitical maelstrom between these two powers,' explains Agathe Demarais, director of global forecasting at the Economist Intelligence Unit, the independent research center of The Economist magazine. The Kazakh oil sector mainly developed after independence in 1991. To exploit its main deposits in Tengiz, Karachaganak, and Kashagan, it called upon foreign companies in joint ventures with a state company – Chevron, ExxonMobil, ENI, or Total. 'Frustration among oil workers.' The problem lies in limited access routes to the country due to its geography. In addition to the CPC, a pipeline with limited capacity supplies China. 'For Europe, the other option is shipping by boat across the Caspian to connect to the Baku-Tbilisi-Ceyhan pipeline, passing through Azerbaijan, Georgia, and Turkey,' details Michaël Levystone, a research associate at the French Institute of International Relations' Russia/NEI Center. Or through the pipeline connecting Baku to the Georgian port of Supsa. In both cases, a much more expensive route that, for now, is not feasible on a large scale due to insufficient infrastructure. 'It will take a few years of investment before we can seriously consider it,' admits the senior Kazakh official mentioned earlier. This has not prevented Mr. Tokayev from getting closer to Mr. Erdogan in recent months to develop this export route. All while continuing the 'multi-vector' foreign policy deployed by the country for years: maintaining good relations with everyone (Russia, China, Europe, United States) in a delicate and precarious balance... Especially precarious as the domestic situation is also complex. 'The population's frustration is constantly growing, especially among oil workers who resent seeing oil money sucked up by the capital without benefiting from it,' points out Aina Shormanbayeva, founder of a local human rights NGO. 'Popular protests could erupt at any moment.' This was the case in January, in response to rising fuel prices. To suppress the protests, partly infiltrated by violent groups, Mr. Tokayev called for help... from Russia, without it necessarily indicating a closer relationship with Moscow." "In Central Asia, everything is of great complexity," notes Matt Gertken, a geopolitical expert at BCA Research, a consulting firm. Before concluding: "For all these reasons, Europe would be wrong to consider Kazakh oil as a completely reliable resource."

title: South Africa has "no reason to prevent" the arrival of a Russian megayacht.
lead: The "North" is owned by Alexei Mordashov, an oligarch close to Vladimir Putin and targeted by Western sanctions imposed after the invasion of Ukraine.
text_eng: South Africa has "no reason to prevent" a luxury megayacht believed to belong to one of the richest Russian oligarchs from docking in its territorial waters, said Vincent Magwenya, a presidential spokesperson, on Thursday, October 27 in response to a warning from the opposition. "South Africa has no legal obligation to respect the sanctions imposed by the United States and the European Union," he said during a press briefing. In accordance with these sanctions, several authorities, including Spanish, Italian, French, and Fijian, have seized yachts linked to Russian oligarchs to put pressure on President Vladimir Putin and his inner circle. The megayacht Nord, valued at $500 million, is said to be owned by Alexei Mordachov, a steel magnate close to Mr. Putin. Like other Russian oligarchs, Mr. Mordachov is targeted by Western sanctions imposed after Russia's invasion of Ukraine. The Nord left Hong Kong last week, where it stayed for three weeks, and could reach South Africa on November 8 or 9. The South African opposition warned on Wednesday that the government could face sanctions if it allowed the boat to dock. The mayor of Cape Town, Geordin Hill-Lewis, wrote to the government asking them to block the entry of the ship into the country's territorial waters. He criticized on Wednesday a "lax approach towards money launderers and racketeers," as well as "those who resist sanctions." South Africa has "a moral duty to do what it can to protest against unjust war, state terrorism, and blatant human rights violations," he emphasized. Pretoria has maintained a neutral stance since the start of the Russian invasion of Ukraine on February 24, refusing to join Western calls to condemn Moscow.

title: Conflict in Nagorno-Karabakh: Vladimir Putin organizes a summit with Armenia and Azerbaijan.
lead: Engrossed for eight months in his offensive against Ukraine, Mr. Putin wanted through this summit for Russia to reclaim its traditional role as a mediator in this region, where Western powers are conducting their own mediation efforts.
text_eng: One month after clashes that resulted in 286 deaths between Armenians and Azerbaijanis – the heaviest toll since the resumption of fighting in 2020 – Russian President Vladimir Putin brought the warring parties together for a summit in Sochi, in southwest Russia. These two former Soviet republics have been disputing control of Nagorno-Karabakh since the 1990s, alternating between direct confrontations and positional warfare. "In our common opinion, it was a very useful meeting that created a very good atmosphere for potential future agreements," stated the Russian president at the conclusion of the tripartite summit. "For its part, Russia will do everything to find a definitive and comprehensive resolution to the conflict in Nagorno-Karabakh," he assured, adding that "it is in everyone's interest to normalize relations." In a joint declaration adopted at the end of the summit, Baku and Yerevan committed to "not resort to force," as well as "to settle all disputes solely on the basis of mutual recognition of sovereignty and territorial integrity." They also emphasized "the importance of active preparations for the conclusion of a peace agreement between Azerbaijan and Armenia to ensure lasting and long-term peace in the region." Traditional role as arbiter Distracted for the past eight months by its offensive against Ukraine, which has caused embarrassment to Moscow's traditional partners, Mr. Putin wanted, through this summit, for Russia to reclaim its traditional role as an arbiter in this unstable region, where Western powers are conducting their own mediation efforts. The Russian president initially held a one-on-one meeting with Armenian Prime Minister Nikol Pashinyan. "The most important thing is to ensure peace and create conditions for development," said Mr. Putin to Mr. Pashinyan. The latter, on his part, emphasized that his priorities were the withdrawal of Azerbaijani forces from the areas of Nagorno-Karabakh where Russian peacekeeping troops are deployed, as well as the release of prisoners of war. The Russian president then met with his Azerbaijani counterpart Ilham Aliyev, who thanked him for giving "impetus to the normalization process." Over 6,500 deaths in 2020 The war in the fall of 2020 between Armenia and Azerbaijan, which resulted in over 6,500 deaths on both sides, ended in an Armenian military defeat and a peace agreement sponsored by Moscow. However, sporadic clashes have continued to erupt, despite the presence of Russian troops, whether in Nagorno-Karabakh or at the recognized border between the two countries, notably in September. These talks under Russia's auspices come at a time when Western capitals have become more actively involved in mediating the conflict between Armenia and Azerbaijan. The President of the European Council, Charles Michel, and the French President, Emmanuel Macron, thus organized negotiations between Mr. Pashinyan and Mr. Aliyev in Brussels in August. Russia, which views these initiatives in a region it considers its sphere of influence with disfavor, and the European Union exchanged sharp criticisms regarding their respective mediation efforts. Mr. Macron had notably accused Russia of wanting to "destabilize" the peace process, with Vladimir Putin denouncing the remarks as "unacceptable." "Russia has always sincerely sought to resolve conflicts, including those concerning Karabakh," argued Mr. Putin in mid-October. Turkey, an ally of Baku, has also made mediation efforts, with its President, Recep Tayyip Erdogan, recently meeting with Mr. Aliyev and Mr. Pashinyan in Prague. Nearly 30,000 deaths in the 1990s Before the negotiations, Mr. Pashinyan announced on Saturday that he was willing to extend the presence of the 2,000 Russian peacekeeping troops for up to twenty years, expressing hope for a proposal from Mr. Putin to that effect. The Azerbaijani President, emboldened by his military victory in 2020, vowed to repopulate Karabakh with Azerbaijanis, as this region predominantly inhabited by Armenians has been beyond Baku's control since a first war - which claimed nearly 30,000 lives - in the 1990s, during the dissolution of the USSR.

title: War in Ukraine: Moscow admits a "tense" situation on the ground for its army
lead: The Russian army once again heavily targeted Ukraine's energy infrastructure on Tuesday, while acknowledging that its own troops were facing difficulties, especially in Kherson, where Moscow intends to evacuate the population.
text_eng: Such an admission is extremely rare in times of war. Through the voice of General Sergei Surovikin on Tuesday, October 18, Moscow acknowledged that its army was facing serious difficulties in the face of the Ukrainian counteroffensive in the south and east of the country. "The situation in the zone of the 'special military operation' can be described as tense. The enemy does not give up its attempts to attack the positions of Russian troops," declared the general in charge of operations in Ukraine for the past ten days. "The Ukrainian regime is seeking to break through our defense" by gathering "all its reserves" for the counteroffensive, and the situation is particularly "very difficult" in Kherson (South), specified Sergei Surovikin. The capital of the eponymous region occupied by Russia since spring and annexed in September, the city is currently the target of Ukrainian strikes targeting its "social, economic, and industrial infrastructures," according to the Russian general. These bombings are causing disruptions in the supply of electricity, water, and food, which pose a "direct threat to the lives of the inhabitants." This justifies, in the eyes of Sergei Surovikin, the fact that "the Russian army will ensure above all the safe evacuation of the population." "Subsequent actions concerning the city of Kherson itself will depend on the military situation," he added, saying without further details "not ruling out a very difficult decision." For his part, the leader installed in the Kherson region by Moscow, Vladimir Saldo, announced an evacuation of residents from several localities to the left bank of the Dnieper River to allow the Russian army to set up "large-scale defensive structures" in the face of a "vast counteroffensive" prepared by Ukrainian forces. Ukrainian bombings in Russia The Russian army is indeed on the defensive on most of the front in Ukraine. It has been retreating since September, both in the north and east and south of the country. The only area where it is still advancing is the city of Bakhmut (East), which it has been trying to take from the Ukrainians since the summer. In Russia itself, the Ukrainian army bombed two villages in the border region of Kursk, according to Moscow. In Belgorod, also bordering Ukraine, Ukrainian fire hit a railway station, injuring one person, according to the local governor. Russia has not lost its bite, as it bombed "the military command and energy systems of Ukraine" again on Tuesday, announced the Russian Ministry of Defense, ensuring that "all targets were hit" thanks to unidentified precision and long-range weapons. "Since October 10th, 30% of Ukrainian power plants have been destroyed, causing massive power outages throughout the country," acknowledged Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky, who reiterated his refusal to negotiate with his Russian counterpart, Vladimir Putin. "The situation is now critical," added a presidential adviser, asking all of Ukraine to "prepare" for possible "power, water, and heating outages." As winter approaches, "1,162 localities remain without electricity," according to the Ukrainian emergency services. On Monday, "kamikaze" drones, which caused the deaths of at least nine people, had already caused power outages in three regions. Iran in the crosshairs Moscow's use of Iranian drones, according to Kiev, was further supported on Tuesday by the Ukrainian armed forces headquarters, stating that the Russian army had sent "43 Iranian-made Shahed-136 drones" in the "last twenty-four hours," of which "38 were shot down by Ukrainian soldiers." This "call for help" to Iran is "the Kremlin's acknowledgment of its military and political failure," reacted President Zelensky in the evening. "We do not have such information," responded Kremlin spokesperson Dmitry Peskov to a journalist's question about the use of Iranian drones Shahed-136 in Ukraine. "Russian technology is being used, with Russian names," he added, while Iran, for its part, reiterated that it has never "exported weapons to any of the warring parties." At the same time, as a sign of increasing tensions on the international stage since the start of the war on February 24th, Russian Foreign Minister Sergei Lavrov suggested a reduction in Russian diplomatic presence in Western countries. "The most important thing is that there is no work there since Europe decided to close itself off to us, to suspend all economic cooperation with Moscow," he explained, saying he wants to prioritize Asia and Africa.

title: The Kakhovka Dam, a new focus of the Ukrainian counter-offensive
lead: According to Ukrainian authorities, an explosion of the dam of this hydroelectric power plant near the front line could result in several thousand casualties and lead to the flooding of dozens of communities.
text_eng: He was captured at the beginning of the invasion. The Kakhovka hydroelectric dam, built on the Dnieper River, was a priority target for the Russians at the start of the conflict, as it helps supply water to the Crimea Peninsula, annexed by Moscow. With the start of the Ukrainian counteroffensive at the end of the summer, it has become the focus of the warring parties, who accuse each other of wanting to destroy it. Kakhovka is located about 60 kilometers east of Kherson, the first major city to fall into Russian hands in March. The front line is situated about forty kilometers to the north. 16 meters high, 3,850 meters long Built on the Dnieper, the structure, partly made of concrete and earth, is 16 meters high and 3,850 meters long. It is one of the largest infrastructures of its kind in Ukraine. According to the website of the Ukrainian operating company, Ukrhydroenergo, the power of the hydroelectric plant is 334.8 megawatts (MW). Built in 1956, during the Soviet period, the Kakhovka hydroelectric dam sends water into the North Crimean Canal, which runs from southern Ukraine and crosses the entire Crimean Peninsula. Upstream from the structure is the Kakhovka reservoir, an artificial water reservoir formed on the Dnieper River, 240 kilometers long and up to 23 kilometers wide. Ukraine accuses Russia of wanting to cover its retreat While the Russian army said it wanted to "evacuate" the population of Kherson in the face of the advance of Ukrainian troops, Kiev fears that Moscow may be seeking to destroy the dam to cover a possible retreat. Russia's goal would be to stop the advance of Ukrainian troops in the region and protect Russian forces, said one of the presidential advisers, Mykhailo Podolyak, on Twitter. On Thursday, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky openly accused the Russians of mining the infrastructure, the destruction of which could have dramatic consequences. "If the dam is destroyed (...), the North Crimean Canal will simply disappear," and it would be "a large-scale catastrophe," he warned on Thursday evening. "More than 80 localities, including Kherson, would be in the fast flooding zone," said Mr. Zelensky before the European Council. "This could destroy the water supply to a large part of southern Ukraine," and affect the cooling of the Zaporizhia nuclear power plant, which draws its water from this artificial lake of 18.18 billion cubic meters, he added. On Friday, Ukrainian Prime Minister Denys Chmyhal called for international intervention. "We call on the UN, the EU, and other organizations to conduct an international observation mission of Kakhovka," he said. According to him, a potential explosion of the dam of this hydroelectric plant "could cause several thousand casualties and [result in] the flooding of dozens of localities." Pro-Russian forces say they fear "a massive missile strike." The Russian occupation authorities in Kherson deny any mining of the Kakhovka dam and denounce President Zelensky's "lies." While it is impossible to verify the claims of both Ukrainians and Russians, an analysis note published on Wednesday by the Institute for the Study of War (ISW), a non-governmental American organization, notes that Russian sources accuse Ukrainian forces of bombing the hydroelectric plant and widely disseminate animations describing the flood zones in case of dam failure. Vladimir Saldo, the governor of Kherson Oblast installed by the Russian army, explained on Telegram that he has information about Kiev's "preparation for a massive missile strike on the Kakhovka hydroelectric plant dam. In this context, there is an imminent danger of flooding in the territories of the Kherson region and massive destruction of civilian infrastructure." On Tuesday, General Sergei Surovikin, head of Russian troops in Ukraine, had advanced similar ideas. The Russian counter-fire strategy According to the ISW, this could be a way to prepare the ground for an attack on the Kakhovka hydroelectric plant dam and blame the Ukrainians for it. The Russian authorities "probably intend" to use these warnings about a supposed Ukrainian attack on the infrastructure to create a narrative "allowing them to damage the dam and blame Ukraine" for the resulting damages, the organization analyzes. "Russian military personnel may believe that the dam breach could cover their retreat from the right bank of the Dnipro and prevent or delay Ukrainian advances on the river," the Institute further states. Such a maneuver would allow the Kremlin, still according to the ISW, to "overshadow the news of a third humiliating retreat of Russian forces" and contribute to Russian propaganda that Ukraine is a "terrorist" state deliberately targeting civilians. On the ground, an advisor to the Ukrainian presidency, Kirill Timoshenko, reported on Friday "88 localities retaken" from Russian forces in the Kherson region.

title: In Rome, Macron advocates for dialogue between Kiev and Moscow.
lead: The head of state is scheduled to speak on Sunday at a meeting of the Community of Sant'Egidio and meet with Pope Francis on Monday.
text_eng: While the fighting continues to rage in Ukraine, the proposal may seem incongruous in some European capitals, including Kiev, which do not want to hear about a peace agreement with Russia: Emmanuel Macron was supposed to take advantage of his trip to Rome on Sunday, October 23 and Monday, October 24, to nurture the perspective, which he knows is still distant, of negotiations between the two enemy countries, eight months to the day after the start of the Russian invasion. Upon his arrival on Sunday afternoon, the head of state is invited to open the annual conference of the Community of Sant'Egidio, entitled "The Cry of Peace." He was then scheduled to have dinner with intellectuals and religious leaders hosted by the founder of this movement, Andrea Riccardi, known for his efforts in interreligious dialogue and mediation in Africa, Latin America, and the Balkans. Andrea Riccardi explained in an interview with Le Monde that he wants to "recreate and promote a culture of peace on the international stage" during the Rome gathering. On Monday morning, Macron will meet with Pope Francis, particularly to discuss Ukraine, less than a year after his last visit to the Vatican in November 2021. In his speech at Sant'Egidio, the head of state is expected to place the conflict between Ukraine and Russia in the current international context of an increasingly fragmented world. The Elysée Palace stated in advance of the visit that "the idea will be to take into account and face the various crises that are affecting our planet today, whether they are related to climate, inequalities, or the war that Russia is conducting in Ukraine, and to reflect on the different ways to build peace." Officially, there is no talk of launching any mediation, even though Macron has been trying in recent weeks to advance this perspective so that France and Europe can be at the discussion table when the time comes. At the launch summit of the European Political Community in Prague on October 6, he discussed with Turkish President Recep Tayyip Erdogan the goals and methods to be put in place to organize potential talks, as Erdogan is currently the only leader in a position to mediate between Moscow and Kiev. For the head of state, these talks cannot take place until the Ukrainians, who are in the midst of a counter-offensive in the Donbass and Kherson region, decide when they believe they have achieved their war objectives. Without openly stating it, Paris, however, fears an attempt to retake Crimea by force, fearing that it could be a pretext for a new escalation by Russia. This viewpoint sharply contrasts, on the continent, with the positions of Ukraine's staunchest allies, who support its efforts to reclaim all occupied territories, including the peninsula unilaterally annexed by Moscow in 2014, and who only hope for one thing: for Vladimir Putin to be judged for war crimes or aggression. In the meantime, the trip to Rome should allow Emmanuel Macron to find receptive ears to his approach and eager to rely on him to make their message of peace heard. Pope Francis himself has called in vain for an end to the fighting. He has also sought to act as a mediator both before and after the start of the Russian invasion, while avoiding a break with the Patriarch of the Russian Orthodox Church, Kirill, who is a religious endorsement for Vladimir Putin. His efforts had been poorly received by Ukrainians, who do not appreciate seeing the head of the Catholic Church equate the warring parties. "Dialogue remains a necessity" "Talking about peace is not a betrayal of Ukrainians. My first visit to Ukraine dates back to 1989, and I understood the Ukrainians' desire for independence," justifies Andrea Riccardi. Those who continue to talk to Putin are right. We are not naive; we know the nature of the Russian interlocutors, but dialogue remains a necessity. Macron is the only European leader who has maintained a tough political line towards Russia while keeping the door open." According to the founder of Sant'Egidio, "peace in Ukraine cannot be achieved without Washington or Beijing, but it must be done with the help of Europe on a position that would be close to that of Emmanuel Macron." "Now, and it's absurd, peace is considered utopian or as a pro-Putin concept. But in the current context, with the nuclear risk, it is necessary to start talking about peace again," says Andrea Riccardi, referring to the recent nuclear confrontation threats made by the Kremlin. However, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky has definitively ruled out talking to Vladimir Putin since Moscow's illegal annexation of the occupied territories in late September. On the Ukrainian side, it is believed that the conditions for restarting mediation, desired by Kiev before the February invasion, are no longer in place. "Currently, we cannot restart such a process, with nearly a fifth of our territory under Russian occupation. A ceasefire under these conditions would only lead to a legitimization of this situation," says Andrii Yurash. Ukraine's ambassador to the Holy See hopes that "President Macron will be able to invite the Pope to take into account not only the horror of war but also the political causes of this horror, namely Moscow's desire to destroy [their] country."

title: Amory Lovins, scientist: "Vladimir Putin has just blown up the era of fossil fuels."
lead: In an interview with "Le Monde", the American scientist Amory Lovins, a fervent advocate of energy efficiency and renewables, believes that the war in Ukraine has triggered a global and resolute movement in Europe on the matter, supported by the United States.
text_eng: He is nicknamed the "Albert Einstein of energy efficiency." In 1976, Amory Lovins published a notable article, "Energy Strategy: The Road Not Taken?", in which he described two paths for the United States in this regard: the "hard" path, based on centralized technologies aiming to produce more, and the "soft" path, combining efficiency, renewable development, and phasing out fossil fuels. Since then, this American researcher, awarded the Right Livelihood Award in 1983 - considered an "alternative Nobel Prize" - and founder of the Rocky Mountain Institute, has never stopped advocating for energy savings. In 1976, you described two different energy paths for the United States. Today, what path do you see for Europe? I think that Vladimir Putin, the Russian president, has just triggered the end of the fossil fuel era. He has sparked a global and resolute movement in Europe, supported by the United States, to abandon Russian fuels and accelerate efficiency and renewable energies. Europe was already heading in that direction, but it is now doing so much faster. Could the war in Ukraine therefore play in favor of energy transition? This war has established a global carbon tax much larger than what economists wanted to implement to protect the climate. Of course, it is not exactly a "tax," it is not being applied in the right way, and it is causing a lot of difficulties. But as a "price signal" [the increase in energy costs and its impact on transportation, for example], it will greatly stimulate the shift to efficiency and renewables. And if Europe continues to move away from oil, gas, and coal with the vision and determination it has shown so far, then the great suffering inflicted by Vladimir Putin will not have been entirely in vain. "Some countries have managed to save energy very quickly by 10% or 20% in situations where there was a sense of solidarity and public utility." My four grandparents were from Ukraine. In my bag, I have a yellow and blue hat [in the colors of Ukraine]: wearing a hat is one of the things you can do here in Europe. With it, you won't be cold when you lower your radiator by 2°C or 3°C, and that can make all the difference. I was born in 1947, and what is happening now is the most fundamental change I have ever witnessed. Do you think calls for sobriety will actually have an impact? Some countries have managed to save energy very quickly by 10% or 20% in situations where there was a sense of solidarity and public utility. The goal of reducing consumption by 10% in two years, as set by France, is realistic and could probably be exceeded. Many will find ways to use less energy without any real discomfort. Most people don't realize the extent of the savings that can be achieved through technical solutions. Can you give us an idea? In your life, you could probably save 80% of your energy consumption, maybe even more. I live at 2,200 meters altitude, near Aspen, Colorado, where it can get as cold as -44°C. Yet, in a patio in the middle of our house, we have already grown bananas without any heating system! Built in 1983, it is very well insulated, the building is airtight, with ventilation and heat recovery. The windows look like double glazing but insulate as if there were sixteen, or even twenty-two layers. There are twice as many windows as in a normal house but all are more or less oriented towards the south – in the future, archaeologists will probably conclude that it was a temple! But is such an example generalizable? The approach we took to design our house is not only suitable for cold climates, it has been tested and also works very well in Bangkok, Thailand. Almost everyone lives in a climate that falls between that of Thailand and Colorado! As for mobility, I drive a BMW i3 made of carbon fiber, lighter than a conventional car by about 300 kg. Initially, the automotive industry said that carbon fiber was too expensive. But this car was the most popular electric vehicle in Europe until Tesla came along; 250,000 were sold and all were profitable for BMW. How did the manufacturers do it? First, they used fewer batteries since less weight needed to be moved. And they radically simplified the manufacturing process, which saves energy. This is what you call "integrated design"… Yes, you optimize your car as a system, not each component independently. Similarly, in my house, we didn't just optimize the insulation or windows, but the entire house, which cost less to build than a conventional house. The heating system is the most expensive part, just like batteries are for electric vehicles. Why do you think it's important to also rethink how we manufacture... pipes? Today, more than half of the world's electricity is used for motors, and half of the motors operate pumps and fans that move fluids in pipes and conduits. Installing wide, short, and straight pipes instead of thin, long, and twisted ones could reduce friction by at least 80% or 90%, and thus improve their efficiency. This also means that the pumping system decreases significantly, so the cost of capital decreases. And it's very simple: you design large pipes and small pumps instead of long pipes and big pumps! You should also install the pipes first, and the equipment afterwards: a plumber knows that if a drain has a right angle bend, it will get clogged. If everyone did this, we could save about one-fifth of global electricity, which is half of the electricity produced from coal. However, this is not included in standard engineering manuals or climate bills. Why? Because it's not a technology, and everything is organized around technology! It's more of a design method: how to put the pieces together. It's easy to do physically but difficult to conceive psychologically, because it's not what you're used to seeing. We need to bend minds, not pipes! However, not everything is so simple. In France, comprehensive and efficient renovations are still very rare, for example... Yes, it seems that the goal is to act on as many buildings as possible rather than to save the most energy and carbon... Some of the comprehensive renovation methods can be complex, but new ones are emerging. Take the example of social housing in the Netherlands: there is now an approach called "EnergieSprong," which involves laser scanning a building, then factory-manufacturing a super-insulating "blanket" that is then placed on its exterior walls. Efficient equipment, such as heat pumps, are installed, as well as a solar roof. The building consumes five times less energy. Instead of paying 150 euros per month to their energy supplier, tenants pay 80 to 120 euros to repay the investment. In addition to efficiency, you advocate for the deployment of renewables. What explains why some oppose them? Why do renewable energies cover 80% of Denmark's electricity needs? I think it's related to the fact that there is a real local commitment and that most wind and solar capacities are owned by individuals, cooperatives, or communities. If every time the turbine turns, it puts euros in your pocket, that helps! I'm not saying there are no conflicts, but if you look at the ridiculously long time it takes to get permission to install solar panels on a roof in Paris, you think that surely there is a better way. So, do you believe in rapid and massive development? Every year, the world installs electrical production capacities and removes others. Do you know what proportion of these net additions of capacities are renewables? 95%. In one of these good years, nuclear power represents 0.5% of net capacity additions. It's just a distraction. You have been a long-time opponent of nuclear power, but in the face of the climate emergency, isn't it necessary to not exclude any low-carbon solutions? I simply take economic indicators and market rationality seriously. The more concerned you are about climate change – and I am – the more important it is to invest wisely and discerningly in solutions that will save the most carbon per euro. "Renewables now cover the increase in global demand, and the supply of fossil fuels has shifted into permanent decline." Analysts at Bloomberg New Energy Finance say a new nuclear kilowatt-hour costs five to thirteen times more than a new solar or wind kilowatt-hour; others mention a factor ranging from three to eight. This means that when you buy a nuclear kilowatt-hour instead of a solar or wind kilowatt-hour, you pay three times more. In other words, you are substituting three to thirteen times less fossil fuel per euro, and you are doing it more slowly. So, for reasons of cost and speed, investing in new nuclear power is not an efficient climate solution. We simply cannot afford to invest in all solutions. Renewables are being deployed, but CO2 emissions continue to rise... It is very likely that the peak use and emission of fossil fuels occurred in 2019. With nearly 300 gigawatts of new capacity per year, renewables now cover the increase in global demand, and the supply of fossil fuels has shifted into permanent decline. For many years, renewables simply added to fossil fuels instead of replacing them, but that is no longer the case. Do you still think achieving carbon neutrality by 2050 is possible? We need both strong efficiency and strong growth in renewable energies. These two elements combined put us roughly on track for a 2°C warming; we need to go twice as fast to stay below the 1.5°C threshold. But there are so many untapped energy-saving opportunities that I believe it is possible. And we have made excellent progress in energy savings this year, as expected with this price signal. You mention practicing "applied hope," what does that mean? It is a daily practice of doing things to try to create a world worth hoping for. Focusing on what can be done, not just talking about it. As the British essayist Raymond Williams said, being truly radical means making hope possible. I don't think we can compel people to act by making them despair.

title: "It is not too early for a closed-door consultation between Ukraine and its main supporters."
lead: As the risk of a head-on collision between Russia and NATO increases, we cannot leave the responsibility of defining the goals of war and the timeline for potential negotiations solely to the Ukrainians, argues former diplomat Michel Duclos in an opinion piece in "Le Monde".
text_eng: Since the end of September, the war in Ukraine has entered a new phase, with even higher risks of escalation. Despite a series of setbacks, Russian President Vladimir Putin has not changed his interpretation of the conflict: he still believes he can annihilate the resistance of Ukrainians and break the determination of Westerners, especially Europeans. This is the meaning that must be given to the so-called partial mobilization he has carried out, the annexation of four Ukrainian regions, the continuation of the hydrocarbon blackmail and clandestine actions (sabotage of the Nord Stream gas pipelines), the renewal of nuclear threats, and finally, the campaign of more or less indiscriminate strikes deep into Ukrainian territory. This last point may be the most important, at least in the short term. On one hand, it significantly raises the cost of Ukrainian resistance – for example, think of the disruption of electricity distribution. On the other hand, it reveals a willingness to remove all inhibitions in the use of force. This may seem strange, but in the previous phase, both parties imposed certain limits, such as the sanctuarization of Russian territory and restrictions on Russian strikes on Ukrainian critical infrastructure. While the Russian army is losing ground in Kherson, one may wonder if we are not entering a spiral that makes a frontal clash between Russia and NATO almost inevitable. Western countries are not at war with Russia. However, a multifaceted show of force is underway between Moscow and the United States and their allies. One point that may not have received enough attention: in the current situation, a "third force" – albeit not coherent – is playing an increasingly crucial role, these are countries that could be called "uninhibited middle powers." For example, India and Turkey, by expanding their trade with Russia, are easing the burden of Western sanctions, while Saudi Arabia is doing the same by deciding with OPEC+ to reduce oil production quotas. Iran, on the other hand, is supplying Moscow with drones that are striking Ukrainian cities. Uncomfortable position The policies of these powers – some of which are "friends" of the West – contribute to weakening Western strategy and strengthening the Kremlin's resolve. It is feared that Russia may obtain the microprocessors its defense industry vitally needs through one of them. Could escalation lead to nuclear risk? An odd "deterrent" dialogue with historical references has begun between the Russian and American presidents. On September 21, Mr. Putin referred to the "precedent" of Hiroshima. Let's suggest that there may be, not now but at a later stage of the confrontation, a moment when the question could arise for the Russian leaders. Mr. Biden responded to Mr. Putin on October 6 by talking about apocalypse and mentioning the Cuban crisis. It is worth noting that the Cuban crisis ended with a Russian-American compromise. Are we at a point in the Ukrainian crisis where diplomacy can come into play? The answer is no. Americans and Europeans must support Ukraine more, as the French government fortunately does. They should also consider their means of action towards "uninhibited middle powers." However, it is not too early to engage in closed-door consultations between Ukraine and its main supporters on what a negotiating position could be when the time comes. It is important to avoid a public debate on the "war aims," but to leave it to the Ukrainians alone to define them, and the timing of a possible negotiation eventually puts them in an uncomfortable position. During the Second World War, summits occasionally brought together the "great allies." Under obviously different modalities, there is a similar need today for a "war council" between major transatlantic partners. Should there be a "summit" in the literal sense or a discreet discussion among senior officials? With which countries around the table? It is up to the governments to find the most effective formulas. In any case, the points mentioned above should be high on the agenda.

title: Is the future of the European Union in the East? Not certain.
lead: With the war in Ukraine, Europe's attention has shifted to the East. But before embodying the future of the EU, Central Europe still has weaknesses to address.
text_eng: Analysis. For many commentators, the origin of the current rift between France and Germany is to be found "in the East". As a consequence of the enlargements of 2004 and 2007, Brexit, and now the promise of accession made to Ukraine, the geographical center of the European Union (EU) has de facto shifted towards Central Europe. Germany, positioned in the "east of the West", is simply acknowledging this reality. On the other hand, Paris, with its notorious lack of interest and persistent disdain towards the former communist countries, may have missed the direction of history. Can those wrongly referred to as the "Eastern countries" when they are actually at the heart of Europe embody the future of the Old Continent? Their determination to rise to this status is undeniable. Just listen to what Viktor Orban regularly says. "Central Europe is the future of Europe," asserts the Hungarian nationalist prime minister, explaining that it is "not just a geographical concept, but a political, economic, and cultural reality." In his view, Hungary and its neighbors are destined to catch up economically with the West, to impose a more intergovernmental vision of the EU, and to defend a culturally white and Christian Europe. Although his pro-Russian message has blurred Mr. Orban's credibility since the start of the war in Ukraine, the core of his prediction is shared in Poland, by far the largest country in the region with its thirty-eight million inhabitants. Together, Budapest and Warsaw dream of weakening the powers of the Commission and the European Parliament and of warding off any threat of sanctions for their authoritarian drifts. Politically, Germany is far from aligning itself with this vision. In his speech delivered in Prague on August 29, Chancellor Olaf Scholz explained that he is in favor of lifting the unanimity principle and he received Mr. Orban in a cold manner during his recent visit to Berlin. To the extent that Mr. Orban described the SPD as the "most hostile party to Hungary in Europe" upon his return. Many dream of Germany. Nevertheless, economically, the interests of large German companies have sometimes led Berlin to tolerate regional authoritarian drifts. Thanks to their skilled and cheap labor force, the countries of Central Europe have provided a crucial competitiveness boost to the German industry, either by emigrating by the hundreds of thousands to Germany or by hosting subcontracting factories. The GDP per capita of the Czech Republic already exceeds that of Spain, and Poland's is approaching it. The largest battery factory in Europe is soon to be built in Hungary by a Chinese company. For the same reasons, Germany advocates much more than France for an enlargement towards the Balkans. The six countries in the region that are not yet part of the EU have a combined population of nearly 20 million, many of whom already dream of Germany. France, on the other hand, continues to see any enlargement as a threat to its influence and as a risk of social dumping. In its defense, most of the Balkan candidate countries are no longer making much effort to align themselves with European principles. However, with its setbacks in Africa and the failure of the Union for the Mediterranean project, Paris no longer has a comparable zone of influence to leverage within the EU. The miracle of German-style economic development in Central Europe is reaching its limits. The region is experiencing full employment and factories are desperately seeking workers. However, the demographics are deeply depressed despite pro-natal policies. Even if Ukraine joins the EU one day, it is one of the countries experiencing the highest population decline in the world. This reality, which predates the war, is more reminiscent of an ultra-peripheral zone than a thriving core. Poland suggests that a massive European Marshall Plan for Ukraine after the war could help reverse this trend, but this remains highly speculative. Trojan Horse Under pressure from employers, governments in the region are indeed starting to open up to non-European immigration, but local populations remain largely hostile for now, as shown by Romanian filmmaker Cristian Mungiu, the best Zeitgeist filmmaker in Mitteleuropa with his latest film, R.M.N.. Demographics are crucial in the political balances of the EU because they form the basis for calculating the qualified majorities that approve directives. Another argument often put forward for the future of Central Europe is its long-standing critical stance towards Russia. The war in Ukraine is a moral victory for capitals that refused to compromise with Moscow, while Berlin and Paris were trying to appease Vladimir Putin. "We in Central Europe knew very well what the threat of Russian imperialism was and its passivity in the face of this imperialism. Today, we can see that our voice was prophetic," said Polish Prime Minister Mateusz Morawiecki in September. However, the idea that Central Europe constitutes a homogeneous bloc on this issue is a Polish lie. While the Baltic countries are on this side, the Czech and Slovak neighbors, now staunch supporters of Ukraine, have long been sending more ambiguous messages. Bulgaria is torn apart. As for Hungary and its ally Serbia – which is not yet part of the EU – they continue to be seen as pro-Russian Trojan horses in the heart of the Old Continent. Surprisingly, Poland is less critical of its ally Viktor Orban's pro-Putin propaganda than of Franco-German hesitations.

title: In Ukraine, crowdfunding is at the forefront.
lead: Comedian Serhiy Prytula's foundation is behind the collection of nearly 83 million euros from individuals, which has enabled the Ukrainian army to acquire drones and even a satellite. The comedian, who had just entered politics, set aside his ambitions in the early days of the invasion.
text_eng: The time when Serhiy Prytula used to wake up Ukraine with slightly crude jokes and his perfect son-in-law smile is long gone. It was on the Novyi Kanal channel, from 2008 to 2011, when he was one of the three star presenters of "Pidiom," a TV show sponsored by a soluble coffee brand. Almost all Ukrainians remember it. For nearly twenty years, this man from the western part of the country was a celebrity on radio and television in the early programs that mixed entertainment and information in the Ukrainian language - at that time, Russian still dominated the media landscape. Nowadays, his days are more serious, more important as well. Serhiy Prytula, 41 years old, with a wife and three children, heads one of Ukraine's most well-known charitable organizations: the Prytula Foundation. Since the beginning of the Russian invasion on February 24, his teams have raised nearly 3 billion hryvnias (around 83 million euros) for the benefit of the Ukrainian armed forces. On the foundation's website, with one click, you can choose to contribute to the purchase of drones, vehicles, communication equipment, funding for mysteriously named "cotton" secret operations, or the repair of captured Russian military equipment on the battlefield. More than 200 "volunteers" provide humanitarian aid and a project for kit houses for refugees are also offered. "We are what is called 'volunteers,' people who help our army have the equipment that the state does not have the ability to buy or does so very slowly," explains Serhiy Prytula between meetings with military personnel placing orders. "I have faster logistics, no bureaucracy, I can buy and deliver to the front." In February, there were ten in the organization, today there are over two hundred. With bases scattered throughout Ukraine to store and quickly transport equipment to the front. The man retains an easy and friendly demeanor from his TV years. He sprinkles jokes and witty remarks here and there in the conversation. But he is now also filled with unwavering concentration and determination. Since the start of the Russian offensive in February, he has lost friends, feared for his life and those of his loved ones. He has changed, of course. But who in Ukraine has not changed? An action man. The clean-shaven face of the 2000s presenter has given way to a salt-and-pepper three-day beard. No more shirts and suits, he dresses in t-shirts and sweatshirts, sometimes covered with a bulletproof vest when the situation demands it. Indeed, in recent months, Serhiy Prytula has personally delivered the equipment purchased through his foundation. "But the soldiers told me to stop, because a good volunteer is not a dead volunteer," he says with a hearty laugh. Nevertheless: these past few months have turned him into a man of action. "With the other private foundation 'Reviens vivant,' Prytula's foundation is the most well-known in Ukraine," notes sociologist Ioulia Shukan, a lecturer in Slavic studies at the University of Paris-Nanterre. "But it should be known that behind these charitable organizations, there is a myriad of small structures that also help the Ukrainian army. Since 2014, very flexible forms of action and support have developed, from preparing rations for soldiers to purchasing drones. Practically everyone in the country is involved today." A professionalized version of Ukrainian mutual aid, the Prytula Foundation is a highly professionalized version of this mutual aid that emerged in the wake of the Maidan revolution. Its popularity skyrocketed in June when a special collection was launched to purchase three Bayraktar, these Turkish combat drones. In three days, nearly 17 million euros were raised, enough to acquire four. But, in a twist, the Turkish company decided to donate the drones. In close discussion with the Ukrainian Ministry of Defense, Prytula's teams reinvested the money in an already-orbiting satellite acquired from a Finnish company. "Ukrainians, you are in space. We have bought a satellite," he announced on his YouTube channel, with a huge backlit map of Ukraine behind him. "But it's more than a satellite, as we now have access to a whole database of satellite images (...). You can be proud of yourselves." The video has 1,200,000 views. It shows Prytula halfway between a news anchor and a politician thanking his supporters. A trajectory similar to that of Volodymyr Zelensky. Recently, after the Russian army shelled the entire Ukrainian territory in retaliation for the sabotage on October 8 of a bridge connecting Russia to Crimea, he teamed up with a Ukrainian blogger to launch a collection titled "You have enraged Ukrainians" to purchase kamikaze drones. Result: nearly 9.8 million euros in twenty-four hours. His trajectory obviously brings to mind that of Volodymyr Zelensky, an actor and comedian who became president of Ukraine in 2019 and has enjoyed immense popularity among his compatriots for the past seven months. Moreover, having been involved in stand-up and show business circles, the two men, while not friends, have known each other for a long time. Before the war, Prytula had criticized the president's positions – not clear enough for his taste – towards Russia and certain oligarchs. The commitments of this native of Ternopil (West) are longstanding. As a child, he fervently supported Ukrainian athletes – then assimilated with Soviet teams – he spoke, sang, and danced in Ukrainian. He remembers, as a student, participating in the "Orange Revolution" in 2004 and especially in the one in 2014. He recalls dedicating part of the budget of his show at the time to support pro-democracy protesters at Maidan Square in Kiev, and later volunteers in Donbass. A political party on hold. He founded his foundation in 2020 to help Ukrainian hospitals cope with the Covid pandemic. He entered politics in 2019 during the legislative elections. Serhiy Prytula found himself in thirtieth position on the list of Holos ("Voice") - a liberal, pro-European, and social-democratic party. He was not elected as a deputy. In 2020, under the same banner, he ran for mayor of Kiev and finished third. Just before the Russian invasion, in early February, he announced the creation of a party called "24 August," in tribute to Ukrainian independence day in 1991. Centrist, patriotic, pro-European, anti-oligarchs, the movement was supposed to be made up of activists, army volunteers, and figures from civil society. However, the specifics of this nascent party remained uncertain. When asked about it today, he says it's a different life. "I am out of the political game. Because there is only one player who can win today: our army. Ukrainians don't care about politics right now, we are in survival mode, the survival of our state. After that, we'll see." For now, there is a sense of unity around President Zelensky. But after the war, it is likely that the legitimacy of Ukrainian politicians will be measured by their role in this conflict. And Serhiy Prytula's likability will work in his favor. In the meantime, he has resumed public management studies in Lviv. And his Twitter account still describes him as a "Ukrainian politician."

title: "The idea that France could be a mediator in the Ukrainian conflict is a pure illusion."
lead: The cautiousness of Emmanuel Macron, as well as his desire to treat Russia and Ukraine equally in the current invasion war, could marginalize France in Europe, note researchers Adam Baczko and Gilles Dorronsoro in a column in "Le Monde".
text_eng: In the Russo-Ukrainian war, France has chosen a shaky position by attempting to reconcile two contradictory approaches. On one hand, President Macron calls for dialogue with Moscow and negotiation, in line with the European tradition of balancing powers to counterbalance American influence on the continent. The desire to appease Russia is also fueled by a pro-Russian stance, predominant on the far right and among the Republicans, who argue that humiliating Russia would be an unforgivable mistake. On the other hand, as a member of NATO and the European Union (EU), France modestly contributes to the collective effort and supports Ukraine both diplomatically and militarily. However, far from creating a favorable position for France, this ambiguity has so far only brought disadvantages. Instead of consolidating its position as a leading military power in the EU, the current policy signals its marginalization in the long term. The idea that France could mediate in this conflict is a pure illusion; the UN or Turkey, for very different reasons, are better positioned to play that role. The war is escalating. Indeed, Putin has clearly shown his disdain for European leaders, whom he sees as subordinate to the United States, and will reject them as mediators. Furthermore, the willingness to dialogue with Russia, justifiable before the invasion, is increasingly seen as a position of weakness among Ukrainians and, beyond that, in Central and Eastern European countries. Rightly or wrongly, many of our European partners do not see France as a reliable ally in this crisis. The suspicion, often directly expressed in Ukraine and Poland, is that France is willing to negotiate a makeshift peace at Ukraine's expense. In any case, Ukraine will not accept giving up its sovereignty, especially since military victories in Kyiv, Kharkiv, and Kherson have galvanized a society mobilized to defend its independence, its place in Europe, and a democratic future. As recent developments show, the war is escalating: Ukraine is fighting for its survival as a nation, and Putin for the future of his regime. The strengthening of the Ukrainian army and mobilization in Russia clearly illustrate the intensification of a conflict that now involves entire societies. The idea that Russia should not be humiliated has never made sense, especially as Putin shamelessly annexed nearly a quarter of its Ukrainian neighbor, well beyond the Donbass. Russia's economic and military exhaustion. Any solution other than the liberation of the occupied territories will only entrench a new conflict, which can be reactivated at any time, following the model of pro-Russian enclaves in Transnistria, Abkhazia, and South Ossetia. From this point of view, the European retreat organized by Merkel and Sarkozy during the 2008 Georgian crisis clearly demonstrated the futility of this strategy in the face of a regime seeking to rebuild an empire on its borders. These positioning errors have serious consequences, especially considering that the most likely scenario today, due to Russia's economic and military exhaustion, is a Ukrainian victory - even the reconquest of Crimea is not entirely unthinkable. The defeat of the Russian army and the inevitable ensuing crisis in Moscow will have repercussions on other conflicts fueled or created by Russia (Transnistria, South Ossetia, Abkhazia) and on the stability of the Belarusian regime. We can already see the effects in the conflict between Azerbaijan and Armenia and in the diminishing influence of Russia in Central Asia. With France sidelined and lagging behind, the possibility - for the first time in history - of a political unification of Europe cannot be ruled out. Whether this scenario materializes or not, the redefinition of intra-European balances condemns France's current policy, which will have missed the opportunity to establish itself as a central player in European security matters. French policy is consistently lagging behind the dynamics of the Ukrainian conflict, resulting in France having to give up its ambition to be the leading nation in security matters within the EU, while Germany openly expresses its ambition to form the most powerful army on the continent. This loss of leadership will have consequences in the civilian domain. The reconstruction of Ukraine - costing hundreds of billions of euros - is already underway, and the position of French companies will depend at least in part on France's policy. Consequently, France must act more cohesively on two levels. France's limited engagement Firstly, less ambiguous communication involves abandoning the rhetoric of mediation, which has done more harm than good, and sending clear signals to governments and public opinions in Eastern European countries. Secondly, military assistance, especially in terms of training and supplying military equipment, must align with the role France aims to play in European security architecture. However, while the UK offers significant military support and plans to train nearly twenty thousand troops this year, France will only train two thousand and still provides too little weaponry. This French caution also contrasts with Germany, which is increasing its supply of armored vehicles and, following the US model, is proposing a program to supply tanks to Eastern European countries willing to give their Soviet-made equipment to Ukraine. European security, and therefore our own, is at stake today on the front lines of Kherson and the Donbass; France must support Ukraine more firmly, as it is in its interests and values.

title: "USSR, the Red Empire", on Arte: in-depth chronicle of the history of the Soviet Union.
lead: A substantial German documentary recalls the communist empire founded by Lenin and disappeared in 1991. Academic, but informative.
text_eng: ARTE - TUESDAY, OCTOBER 25 AT 8:55 PM - DOCUMENTARY The late USSR (1922-1991), born just a century ago from the union between Russia, Belarus, Ukraine, and Transcaucasia (a region consisting of Georgia, Armenia, and Azerbaijan), has inspired numerous documentaries. Recently, some have even made an impact with their quality, the interest of the archives, and the testimonies collected - such as "The Red Army" (2021) by Michaël Prazan, or "Gulag, a Soviet Story" (2019) by Patrick Rotman, Nicolas Werth, and François Aymé. This time, with this extensive German documentary in three parts, the authors did not want to focus on a specific aspect but chose to chronologically unfold the entire history of the Union of Soviet Socialist Republics, which Vladimir Putin considers the disappearance of as "the greatest geopolitical catastrophe of the last century." This history lesson, somewhat unsurprising in its form but valuable in its reminders, and sometimes surprising with anecdotes about daily life, naturally benefits from numerous filmed archives. The most interesting and rare ones come from the distant Asian republics, Georgia, or the Baltic countries. Not to mention excerpts from quite bewildering fiction films. From "Cosmic Voyage" (1936), through "Letters from a Dead Man" (1986) and "The White Sun of the Desert" (1970), there is something for everyone. Diversity and quality of witnesses Another strength of the documentary is the diversity and quality of the witnesses gathered. Russians, Kyrgyz, Lithuanians, Belarusians, Ukrainians, or Georgians, the women and men interviewed on camera were, in the USSR, political leaders, police officers, or military personnel. But also intellectuals, artists, or ordinary citizens recounting their adolescence. Their words are worth more than any history lesson. Among these testimonies, that of Svetlana Alexievich stands out. Born in Ukraine to a Belarusian father and a Ukrainian mother in 1948, she won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 2015. A journalist and writer of exceptional works that help us better understand life in the USSR (from "The Unwomanly Face of War" in 1985, to "Voices from Chernobyl" in 1997, and "Second-Hand Time" in 2013, to name a few), she recounts, in a gentle voice, the role of women in war, housing problems, the scourge of alcoholism, secret conversations in communal kitchens... In three times fifty-two minutes, the entire history of the largest country in the world, populated at its peak by 240 million inhabitants, unfolds. A complex and tumultuous political, cultural, military, and social history. A red history.

title: In Norway, several Russians accused of illegally piloting drones near strategic sites.
lead: On four occasions in less than ten days, Russian nationals have been arrested for flying drones over Norwegian territory.
text_eng: This is the seventh Russian national arrested in Norway in less than two weeks. On Wednesday, October 19, the police in Finnmark, in the far north of the country, revealed that they had arrested a 47-year-old man two days earlier in the port of Hammerfest. He has been placed in provisional detention for two weeks on suspicion of flying a drone in the Norwegian archipelago of Svalbard, in violation of the ban on Russians flying over the country's territory since the beginning of the war in Ukraine. According to Norwegian media, the man is believed to be Andrei Yakunin, the son of Vladimir Yakunin, former head of Russian Railways and close to President Vladimir Putin. He was traveling on his yacht, the Firebird, under British flag, currently docked in the port of Hammerfest. During his arrest, the Norwegian police seized a drone and electronic devices, which are currently being analyzed. On Wednesday at noon, the Norwegian Intelligence Service (PST) announced that they would coordinate the investigations related to the multiple arrests that have occurred in recent days. The first arrest took place at the Storskog border post on October 11. A 50-year-old man, holding two Russian passports and an Israeli passport, was arrested as he was about to enter Russia. In his luggage, border guards found two drones and a series of partially encrypted photos and videos. "Ordinary tourists" On October 13, in the town of Mosjoen in the west of the country, the police arrested three Russian men and a woman in their twenties suspected of violating a ban on photographing a strategic site. The following day, a 51-year-old Russian national was also arrested after being seen photographing Tromso Airport. According to investigators, he was in possession of a drone and various equipment with which he photographed Kirkenes Airport near the border with Russia and Norwegian military helicopters. At the moment, the four cases seem to have no connection, except that all the suspects are Russian nationals. Nevertheless, they are causing some anxiety in Norway, less than a month after leaks were discovered about the Nord Stream gas pipelines in the Baltic Sea, while unauthorized drone flights have been observed repeatedly near Norwegian gas and oil platforms. On Wednesday, Hedvig Moe, the number two at PST, stated that these activities could be "used for espionage purposes or to sow fear and uncertainty about, among other things, Norway's ability to supply gas to Europe." A few hours earlier, air traffic had to be briefly interrupted at Bergen Airport, the country's second-largest city, after drones were observed. On its Facebook page, the Russian Embassy in Oslo denounces the "hysteria" of Norwegians and laments that "ordinary tourists" could be targeted.

title: Italy: Giorgia Meloni, leader of the far-right party Fratelli d'Italia, presented her future government.
lead: Matteo Salvini has been appointed as deputy prime minister; the pro-European Antonio Tajani, as minister of foreign affairs.
text_eng: After her party's historic victory in the legislative elections in September, Giorgia Meloni is set to become the first woman to hold the position of Prime Minister in Italy on Friday, October 21st. This 45-year-old Roman, who managed to "de-demonize" the far-right party Fratelli d'Italia to come to power exactly a century after Mussolini, was tasked by the President of the Republic, Sergio Mattarella, to form a government. She presented the members of the executive on Friday afternoon, who will be sworn in on Saturday morning at the Quirinal Palace. Matteo Salvini, leader of the League, an anti-immigration party, returns to the position of Deputy Prime Minister, which he had already held after the elections in 2018. At that time, he also held the position of Minister of the Interior, which he coveted again this time, but Giorgia Meloni vetoed it. A pro-European appointed as head of diplomacy It is a technocrat who takes the position of Minister of the Interior in this nationalist government focused on security and immigration issues. Matteo Piantedosi, 59, has had a career in the prefectural and senior civil service, largely at the Ministry of the Interior, where he was chief of staff to Mr. Salvini. A Eurosceptic herself, Ms. Meloni has chosen a convinced European to lead diplomacy, the former President of the Strasbourg Parliament Antonio Tajani. He ticks all the boxes to reassure Brussels and European capitals: a former European Commissioner and Vice-President of the European People's Party (EPP, right-wing), this 69-year-old Roman has a network that will prove very useful. Giancarlo Giorgetti, 55, also pro-European, has been appointed to the economy. This discreet but powerful figure of the League was already a minister in the outgoing government of Mario Draghi, in charge of economic development. At odds with Matteo Salvini, he remained a loyal supporter of Mr. Draghi until the end. Forced to juggle with turbulent allies As the third largest economy in the eurozone faces, like its neighbors, a difficult economic situation due to the energy crisis and inflation, Georgia Meloni's task is even more daunting as she will have to manage two turbulent allies: not only Matteo Salvini, but also Silvio Berlusconi, the declining leader of Forza Italia. The two men, who arrived separately with their respective delegations at the Quirinal Palace on Friday, are reluctant to accept the authority of Giorgia Meloni, whose party won 26% of the votes in the elections on September 25, compared to only 8% for Forza Italia and 9% for the League.
