Giorgia Meloni, after her meeting with the Italian President, Sergio Mattarella, addresses the press at the Quirinal Palace on October 21, 2022. The far-right leader is the first woman to lead a government in Italy. FRANCESCO AMMENDOLA / AFP Herself an Atlanticist and in favor of supporting Ukraine against Russia, Ms. Meloni had to face this week the controversial statements of Mr. Berlusconi, who claimed to have "reconnected" with Vladimir Putin and blamed Kiev for the war. She felt compelled to set the record straight on Wednesday by stating that Italy is "fully part" of Europe and the North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO). A talented speaker, Ms. Meloni, a conservative Christian opposed to LGBT+ rights and with the motto "God, country, family," has nevertheless promised not to touch the law allowing abortion. Record inflation Inflation in the peninsula increased by 8.9% year-on-year in September, and Italy risks entering a technical recession next year, alongside Germany. The room for maneuver is limited by a colossal debt representing 150% of the gross domestic product (GDP), the highest figure in the eurozone after Greece. Giorgia Meloni has given up advocating for an exit from the euro, but she has promised to defend her country's interests more in Brussels. This, while growth depends on nearly 200 billion euros in subsidies and loans granted by the European Union as part of its post-pandemic recovery fund. Caught between the hammer of her "allies" and the anvil of Brussels and the markets, Ms. Meloni appears to be walking a tightrope, almost a tradition in this country known for its chronic government instability. Two far-right presidents in Parliament Regarding two other key positions in the Italian Republic, the Senate has elected Ignazio La Russa as its president, the founder of Fratelli d'Italia with Giorgia Meloni. This 75-year-old Sicilian has militated his whole life on the far right, following in the footsteps of his father, a former local leader of the National Fascist Party under Mussolini who gave his son, as a second name, that of the dictator, Benito. The lower house of Parliament also chose a controversial figure as its president: Lorenzo Fontana, a close ally of Mr. Salvini, with ultraconservative positions on abortion and same-sex marriage. This devout Catholic denounced the Brussels sanctions against Moscow after the annexation of Crimea in 2014 and described Vladimir Putin's Russia as a "model" society.

title: In Russia, "mobilization challenges the foundations of Putin's power."
lead: In an interview with "Le Monde," Russian sociologist Lev Gudkov notes that the Russian society's attitude towards the "special operation" in Ukraine is more about the absence of resistance or opposition rather than genuine support.
text_eng: Lev Gudkov is a sociologist and scientific director of the Levada Center, the last independent polling institute in Russia, classified as a "foreign agent" by the authorities. To what extent can we trust the polls conducted in Russia? The data we collect is reliable. Those who claim that polls cannot be trusted in an authoritarian or totalitarian regime, and that people are afraid to respond, have a poor understanding of these issues. This is often the case with liberals who struggle to accept that they are in the minority. Refusals to respond are around 40%. However, most of the time, these are technical refusals from people who are busy and do not have the time. We estimate that refusals motivated by political considerations are around 10% to 15% of those surveyed. They are mainly found in two categories of the population: elderly, less educated women living in small towns, who are loyal to the government but feel they have no political opinion to express; and young people who show no interest. In contrast, educated men in large cities want to voice their opinions. They are against the regime and have no other means of expression. Is the support for the "special military operation" real and widespread in the Russian population? Formally, it stands at around 75%. However, in my opinion, it would be more accurate to speak of an absence of resistance or opposition rather than support or endorsement. We study the level of declared support, as well as the emotions triggered by specific events. We observe that the "special operation" evokes mixed feelings. There is indeed pride, satisfaction, but also, at the same level and often in the same individuals, negative emotions: shock, confusion, depression, despair, shame... This indicates significant dissatisfaction, even guilt, and also a clear awareness that Vladimir Putin's blitzkrieg has failed. "It's a mentality of subjects rather than citizens, based on fear and dependence on the state." Furthermore, the hypocritical term "special military operation" was chosen precisely to reassure. The justifications for its initiation have constantly evolved without coherence. Today, what the Russian public is being told is about a total war triggered by the West... All of this is part of a context of propaganda and censorship. Trust in television has dropped from 70% to 55% in a few years, which is still a high level. At the same time, all other sources of information have been eliminated, and opposition and civil society structures destroyed. Do people believe in the reasons used to justify the conflict? To a large extent, yes. Once again, there is only one opinion, which is based on the firmly rooted idea that Russia is a besieged fortress. Anti-Ukrainian propaganda is also old. It began in 2004, with the "Orange Revolution" [after Viktor Yanukovych's victory in the Ukrainian presidential election, whose results were deemed falsified by the opposition], and then grew in intensity and aggressiveness after 2014, with the annexation of Crimea and the start of the war in Donbass. The result is that people expected a major war. In recent years, steadily, over 70% of respondents said they feared a war. This is a very high level, placing this concern in second position, after concerns about the health of loved ones. "Skull - Russia," 2019, Mr. Strange. MR STRANGE Opinions seem very changeable. The same surveys show that 60% of Russians would support Vladimir Putin if he launched a new attack on Kiev, and 75% would support him if he immediately stopped hostilities... That's the key point. These opinions are primarily guided by conformity and identification with the State. In a significant part of society, the prevailing idea is that the State is a master who is always right and is accountable to no one. It is a mentality of subjects rather than citizens, based on fear and dependence on the State. The State also confers an identity: "If the State is strong, then so am I, despite poverty, corruption, humiliation..." "The 'Homo Putinus' is indeed an extension of the 'Homo Sovieticus.' It is the same but more cynical, and with even less faith in the future." This is why, once again, I speak of an absence of opposition rather than support. It is a passive position, without cost or responsibility. Only 10% of respondents say they feel responsible for what is happening in Ukraine. For a large part of Russians, politics, like war today, is a field that has nothing to do with them. [The German political scientist and philosopher] Hannah Arendt said it well: this passivity is the foundation of a totalitarian or authoritarian State. Is this apathy a consequence of the Soviet experience? Of course, similar logics are found. The Soviet system consecrated the reign of doublethink, a mixture of external approval and distancing. Today as then, people do not really believe in elections; they consider it does not concern them. It is simply a proof of loyalty that, for an individual, is inexpensive. The Homo Putinus is indeed an extension of the Homo Sovieticus. It is the same but more cynical, and with even less faith in the future. The cult of strength and violence is supposed to compensate for this lack of perspective and offer the ingredients of self-respect. At present, the government demands more than just superficial support. The discourse is much more demanding, more radical than during the years when only stability was discussed. Does it work? Very polarized positions, in any direction, only exist among very marginal groups. For example, Vladimir Putin inspires deep and enthusiastic love in a minority segment of the population, mainly middle-aged women. For others, especially those whose income is somehow linked to the State, approval is mainly due to lack of choice. Similarly, regarding the war, there are hawks and aggressive patriots. They can be seen on television, their comments can be read on the Internet – where they rejoice in the bombing of Ukrainian cities... Propaganda tries to make it seem like they represent the majority, but it is a core group that does not make up more than 6% or 8% of the population, mainly retirees, which can expand on occasion. The majority, on the other hand, may adhere to some propaganda speeches, but aspires to a peaceful life, without shocks. The "mobilization" announced on September 21 is a shock. What effect does it have on public opinion? It is obviously a shock. There is no more pretending: you have to go, or flee. It is a radical psychological change: war is no longer a distant thing, fought with "high-precision weapons," on foreign soil. Moreover, no one believes that this mobilization will be "partial," as the government claims. The myths in which many Russians have placed their pride – a strong State, a powerful army – are being tarnished. This shock goes beyond the question of who leaves or stays, or the effects on the economy. It is a questioning of many certainties. The war was supposed to be quick and obviously victorious, since we have the second largest army in the world and extraordinary weapons. Until the end, the Ministry of Defense concealed the situation by issuing soothing statements. When the mobilization was announced, reality became apparent. Failure became evident, and we can already see a new rise in negative sentiments: fear, confusion... The percentage of people saying they feel proud of the "special operation" dropped from 51% to 20%. And this is just the beginning. The myths in which many Russians have placed their pride – a strong State, a powerful army – are being tarnished. If we continue the parallel with the Soviet period, there were other reasons for pride at that time – from cultural production to space conquest. Today, there are none. This will likely increase resentment and violence in society. It also strikes at the foundations of Putin's power. The president's popularity has been built not only on the promise of stability, but also on the idea of strength, on victorious wars, starting with Chechnya, and on protecting the people from external threats. His legitimacy and credibility are being questioned. Right after the announcement of mobilization, his approval rating has already dropped from 83% to 72%. This is still very high, of course. This process of delegitimization will be slow. Could it lead to a protest movement? No, not in the near future. It would first require a clear military defeat, a serious and prolonged crisis, for people to radically reconsider their relationship with the authorities. This is not the case yet. It would also require organizations that are capable, not only of offering an alternative discourse, but above all of convincing people that they can change something. As early as August, 48% of those surveyed were in favor of halting hostilities, but those willing to openly say so make up a very small minority. Once again, the most widespread belief in Russia is that the individual cannot change anything.

title: Power struggle between Europeans and Russians in the Caucasus.
lead: France's mediation efforts between Armenia and Azerbaijan are irritating Moscow, the traditional arbiter of tensions in this region.
text_eng: The mediation efforts undertaken by Emmanuel Macron between Armenia and Azerbaijan did not take long to stir up tensions with Vladimir Putin's Russia. While Russia is preoccupied with its setbacks in the war in Ukraine, risking weakening its positions in the Caucasus, it nonetheless defends its traditional role as an arbiter in the region, especially in the face of Europeans. The recent clash between the Russian and French heads of state gives an idea of the intensity of the struggle for influence. On October 6, in Prague, during the first summit of the European Political Community, Emmanuel Macron had praised the gathering, along with the President of the European Council, Charles Michel, of the leaders of the two warring countries, Nikol Pashinyan for Armenia, and Ilham Aliyev for Azerbaijan. After several hours of discussions, it was agreed to send a civilian mission from the European Union to the border between the two states to verify, following the resurgence of violence in September, that it was being respected by Azerbaijani forces. A few days later, on France 2, Emmanuel Macron stated that Moscow had "played the game of Azerbaijan with Turkish complicity" during the 2020 conflict between Baku and Yerevan for control of the enclave of Nagorno-Karabakh. "It is a destabilization maneuver by Russia, which, in the Caucasus, seeks to create disorder to weaken and divide us all," he said, adding that France would "never" abandon the Armenians. "Lack of understanding" These statements provoked a strong response from those concerned. "I think there is a lack of understanding of the course of the conflict in these statements and, evidently, of information from France on the positions of the parties," reacted Vladimir Putin on Friday, October 14, during a summit of former USSR countries in Astana, Kazakhstan. He judged Macron's remarks as "incorrect" and "unacceptable." Ilham Aliyev, also present in Astana, criticized the statements as "insulting, unacceptable, and false." "We no longer see any possibility (...) for France to play any role in the normalization of Azerbaijani-Armenian relations," he declared. The incident comes at a bad time, as Europeans and the United States seek to restart negotiations for a peace treaty between the two enemies. Essentially, Moscow does not appreciate the civilian mission announced in Prague, which should be deployed in the coming days. "We see this as a new attempt by the EU to interfere in every way possible in the normalization of relations between Armenia and Azerbaijan, to undermine the mediation efforts of our country," reacted Maria Zakharova, the spokesperson for the Russian Ministry of Foreign Affairs, on October 11. The Russians "not credible" For the EU, this mission is a first step, but above all a symbolic victory against Russia, now unable to play the role of arbiter it claims in the region. "For the first time since the first war in Upper Karabakh, we will have an international presence on the ground that is not Russian," rejoices a French diplomatic source. "It is the beginning of a more substantial involvement of Europe in the peace process." So far, only the Russians had the ability to act, "but today, they are nowhere to be found," adds the same source, "and they are not credible on the issue of territorial integrity" - even less so since the annexations, on September 30, of four Ukrainian provinces. Beyond this mission, the Elysée identifies a second "point of effort" within the framework of the Organization for Security and Cooperation in Europe (OSCE, of which Russia and Azerbaijan are members): "We are trying to obtain an agreement from all members to be able to deploy a mission with a much broader scope, which will more generally address peace and security issues for people," indicates a French diplomat. Another way to challenge Russian tutelage in the region. Azerbaijani President Ilham Aliyev and his Russian counterpart Vladimir Putin, on October 13, in Astana (Kazakhstan). VYACHESLAV PROKOFYEV / AP Relations between Azerbaijan and Armenia escalated after the attack by Baku forces on September 13 and 14 on several dozen Armenian cities and villages, including Goris, Jermuk, Kapan, Vardenis, and Sotk. These clashes, the deadliest since the end of the war in Upper Karabakh in 2020, have resulted in at least 286 deaths on both sides. According to Yerevan, Azerbaijani soldiers took advantage of the situation to advance 10 square kilometers into Armenian territory, of which they now occupy over 50 square kilometers. The first meeting of the border demarcation commissions was held on May 24. The next one is scheduled by the end of October in Brussels.

title: "Vladimir Putin does not understand Europe."
lead: The former French ambassador to Moscow, Jean-Maurice Ripert, considers, in an interview with "Le Monde," that the Russian president is "a victim of his own isolation" facing a European Union that does the opposite by sharing its sovereignty.
text_eng: Jean-Maurice Ripert is a diplomat, former French ambassador to Russia from 2013 to 2017, during the annexation of Crimea and the war in Donbass, and then to China between 2017 and 2019. He analyzes the attitude of the Russian president and his vision of Europe. How did the mutual misunderstanding between Vladimir Putin and Europe arise? Vladimir Putin came to power in 1999, at a time when Europe was strengthening its union, while Russia mainly maintained bilateral relations with each of the states. He was a lieutenant colonel in the KGB, a master of networks, in a predatory system that emerged in the 1990s, particularly in Saint Petersburg, and he quickly showed that he would use force, as in Chechnya. We accepted him as the president of Russia. We failed to anticipate that the country would return to its old demons. Mr. Putin continued his predatory policy as far as possible. He ended up becoming a caricature of himself. He did not realize that the European Union [EU] was becoming a power and that the Russia-United States duopoly was itself evolving. He did not understand that the world had changed. Europe was the largest economic partner, the top investor. Russia should be the richest country on the planet, but it remains a rich country inhabited by poor people. I would like to point out that it is the only place in the world where you can submit a bill to Parliament in the morning and see it enacted the same evening – the Russians have nicknamed this "the crazy printer." Do you think Mr. Putin has changed? I do not believe for a second that he has changed. When asked if Vladimir Putin was crazy, the neuropsychiatrist Boris Cyrulnik replied that he had the rationality of his delusion. The Russian president is perfectly rational within a reasoning that has little to do with reality. Who holds Russia today? The far-right nationalists, the oligarchs dependent on Mr. Putin, and the security forces. I do not know how long this can last. Since the invasion of Ukraine at the end of February, the Russians have voted with their feet; hundreds of thousands of them have left. Vladimir Putin's project is based on a nationalist myth, on a form of Russian empire, in the ethnic sense of the term. The authorities were constantly in confrontation, contestation, and complaints towards European ambassadors. Everything was used to destabilize them and create a power struggle. I myself have often been summoned at odd hours by deputy ministers for all sorts of reasons. It was a form of intimidation. In 2008, during the French presidency of the EU, it was as if the problem was resolved [after the lightning war by Russian forces against Georgia, which resulted in the amputation of 20% of its territory]. Then, in 2014, the annexation of Crimea reverberated like a thunderclap, before war broke out in the Donbass. However, it was the Russians who fomented unrest in Donetsk and Luhansk with gang leaders supported by Russian and Ukrainian oligarchs. And yet, the annexation of Crimea, fundamentally, did not change much. Europe wanted to return to normal relations with Moscow... European ambassadors prepared the Brussels meetings on sanctions in 2014. And suddenly, within the EU, a group of countries (Poland, the Baltic States, and others) emerged with a different opinion and some experience with Russia. This radically changed things in European politics, transatlantic relations, NATO... Suddenly, the concept of Europe from Lisbon to Vladivostok took a serious blow. We shifted to a slightly less naive perspective... This, in my opinion, was an extremely important turning point. The balance of power within the EU began to shift. Russia's aggressiveness even accelerated the debate on Europe as a power. But in France, how did we go from the firm tone used by Emmanuel Macron during Vladimir Putin's visit to Versailles in 2017, to the more conciliatory tone during his reception at Brégançon in 2019? As a diplomat, I will always be in favor of dialogue, but when is it interpreted as a sign of weakness? Once again, Mr. Putin does not understand the EU, which does the exact opposite of him by sharing its sovereignty, while he wants to reunify the Russian empire. He is a victim of his own isolation. He underestimated our capacity and intentions, despite our disagreements. He underestimated Joe Biden and his ability to react, and certainly did not expect Finland and Sweden to request entry into NATO. Europe may be slow, but that does not mean it is weak. The harm it inflicts with sanctions is not masochism, it is evidence of its determination. Vladimir Putin has attacked the democratic world. Democracies have reacted. I emphasize democracies, not the West. He was wrong about everything, just as he is still wrong about his international supporters. Forcing China to choose sides is absurd. Beijing will never choose the Russians over the Americans. That's not how the world works anymore. How do you explain then that Kremlin proxies have had significant influence in France and Europe? Some of Vladimir Putin's propagandists act out of conviction or a historical love for Russia, while others act out of financial interest. When you have devoted your life to Franco-Russian relations, experiencing such a rift is complicated; you become blinded and ready to make excuses for Mr. Putin. There are also those who sell themselves to Russian interests for money, such as tax exiles or former politicians who are paid by Russian companies, like former German Chancellor Gerhard Schröder or former French Prime Minister François Fillon [who resigned from his Russian positions on February 25]. Many French or German companies struggled to accept the sanctions after 2014. While I was ambassador in Russia, I saw in Moscow or St. Petersburg, like other European diplomats, representatives of political parties, almost all from the same side, having high-level meetings with Russian officials. We had no doubt, at the time, that they probably would not leave empty-handed. There are indeed parties that have never hidden receiving funding from Russian banks.

title: In Italy, Berlusconi "reconnects" with Putin and causes trouble within the coalition.
lead: Two audio recordings of the former prime minister are disrupting the formation of Giorgia Meloni's government with her right-wing allies.
text_eng: These confessions have had the effect of a bomb in the Peninsula. Two leaked recordings in the press within a few hours shed harsh light on the fractures within the right-wing majority and on the future of the Forza Italia party (center-right) within the future government coalition being formed by the future Prime Minister and leader of the Fratelli d’Italia party (post-fascist), Giorgia Meloni. In an audio excerpt recorded by a Forza Italia parliamentarian and broadcast on Wednesday, October 19, Silvio Berlusconi can be heard discussing the war in Ukraine with party members gathered in a room at the Montecitorio Palace, the seat of the Chamber of Deputies. "I have reestablished contact with President Putin, a bit... well, quite a bit," confides the former prime minister, who recounts that for his birthday - he turned 86 on September 29 - the Russian leader sent him "twenty bottles of vodka and a very kind letter." "I replied with bottles of Lambrusco and an equally kind letter. He told me I was one of his five 'true friends,'" he continues. The second recording is even more compromising for the Cavaliere, as he explains to his troops that Vladimir Putin would have gone to war because of President Volodymyr Zelensky. "I don't see how Putin and Zelensky can come to the negotiating table. Because it's just not possible. Zelensky, in my opinion... forget it, I can't say it," Berlusconi stops. A final sentence applauded by the Forza Italia parliamentarians. "Meloni is a hostage of the pro-Russians." The sequence could seriously undermine Italy's credibility on the European stage, especially as Giorgia Meloni has always made her support for Ukraine and NATO a strong line in her political program. The applause from the 45 FI parliamentarians also raises questions about the party's real stance on the Ukrainian conflict. Will Antonio Tajani, former President of the European Parliament and coordinator of Forza Italia, still be able to secure the position of Minister of Foreign Affairs as planned? Many observers saw the possibility fading on Thursday evening, shortly after the controversy sparked by the Italian billionaire. "Silvio Berlusconi has always placed himself at the center of attention," notes Giovanni Orsina, a professor at Luiss University in Rome and a specialist in Italian right-wing politics. "But by following a political rationality, a clear line. I must admit that I have never seen a destructive Berlusconi like this before." On Thursday, the Italian press, across all political tendencies, echoed with dismay the toxic atmosphere within the future ruling coalition. "Silvio, stop!" urged the newspaper Libero, despite its right-wing stance, emphasizing that "things could end badly." "Meloni is the hostage of the pro-Russians," was the headline in La Repubblica. "Schizophrenia" Besides her pro-Putin sympathies that weaken the majority coalition, it is Silvio Berlusconi's own ability to continue making politically coherent choices that is being questioned. On Tuesday evening, on the private channel La 7, deputy Alessandro Cattaneo learned live from the presenter that he had been elected leader of the Forza Italia group in the Chamber of Deputies. Silvio Berlusconi had slipped his name to his troops, without informing the interested party. "An episode that gives the feeling of a party deprived of a guide," notes Giovanni Orsina. Silvio Berlusconi's wavering on Vladimir Putin, according to him, is a kind of "schizophrenia," while the parliamentarians of Forza Italia have voted in recent months in favor of all of the Draghi government's amendments on the war, without any ambiguity. "I still believe that the Forza Italia line will remain firm and pro-Ukraine," the political scientist wants to believe, "but the situation has certainly become much more difficult for Giorgia Meloni." The one who is called to govern Italy did not speak directly, but through a statement from Fratelli d'Italia in which she does not hide her firmness. "Italy fully, and with its head held high, is part of Europe and the Atlantic Alliance, whoever does not agree with this cornerstone cannot be part of the government," warns Giorgia Meloni. Putting out the fire Less than two hours later, Silvio Berlusconi tried to put out the fire by explaining that his personal position and that of Forza Italia "do not differ from that of the Italian government, the European Union, the Atlantic Alliance, neither on the Ukrainian crisis nor on other major international policy issues." But the damage is done. Thursday morning, the first consultations began at the Quirinal Palace around the President of the Republic, Sergio Mattarella, who, constitutionally, is the guarantor of Italy's international treaties. Initially group by group, these consultations are set to conclude on Friday with a meeting with the united coalition. Regardless of the names of the ministers who will make up Giorgia Meloni's government, Italy has just achieved the feat of a government not yet born but already in full crisis.

title: Poutine believes that the world is entering its "most dangerous" decade since 1945.
lead: During an international conference in Moscow, the Russian president outlined his geopolitical vision, in which the war he is waging in Ukraine is part of a "tectonic shift in the entire global order."
text_eng: According to Russian President Vladimir Putin, speaking on Thursday, October 27 in Moscow, the world has entered its "most dangerous" decade since the end of the Second World War, and the conflict in Ukraine is an illustration of the struggle against Western domination. "We are at a historic moment. We are probably facing the most dangerous, most important, most unpredictable decade since 1945," declared the Russian leader at the Valdai discussion forum in Moscow. "The West, without clear unity, is not able to lead the world, but it is desperately trying, and most of the world's peoples cannot accept it," he stated, deeming the planet in a "revolutionary situation." According to him, the assault on Ukraine is part of this "tectonic shift in the entire world order." "Russia will never tolerate the diktat of aggressive, neo-colonial West," he added. Shortly before, he had described his confrontation with Western powers, particularly in the context of the offensive against Ukraine, as a fight for the very survival of Russia. "Russia is not challenging the West, Russia is just defending its right to exist," he further asserted, accusing Americans and other Westerners of wanting to "destroy, erase [Russia] from the map" in a new indictment against his geopolitical rivals, who support and arm Ukraine against Russian soldiers. "Dirty bomb": IAEA investigation Regarding Russian accusations that Kiev is developing a "dirty bomb," the Kremlin leader called on the International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA) to go to Ukraine "as soon as possible." "The IAEA wants to come (...). We are in favor, as soon as possible and in the broadest possible way, because we know that the authorities in Kiev are doing everything to cover up the traces of these preparations," he stated. "We even know approximately where this is being done [in Ukraine]," added the Russian leader. Ukraine and its Western allies have all strongly condemned Russia's "clearly false" allegations. The IAEA, for its part, announced in a statement on Thursday evening that it would visit "this week" two Ukrainian sites at Kiev's request "to detect any diversion of nuclear material." According to Mr. Putin, Ukraine wants to use such a radioactive weapon "so that later they can say it was Russia that carried out a nuclear strike." The Russian president downplayed his past threats of a possible Russian use of nuclear weapons, stating that he had only responded to threats from his adversaries. "We have never talked about the possibility of using nuclear weapons. We have only made allusions in response to statements from other countries," assured the Russian president. For him, Ukraine and Western countries assert that Moscow is escalating nuclear tensions in order to "influence neutral countries to say to them: 'Look at Russia! Do not cooperate with it!'" He then accused the United States of being "the only country in the world to have used nuclear weapons against a non-nuclear state," referring to Japan in 1945. "If he does not intend to do so, why does he talk about it so much? Why does he talk about [Russia's] capabilities to resort to tactical nuclear weapons?" responded U.S. President Joe Biden in an interview with NewsNation. "His approach to this issue is very dangerous," he said. Alleged "biological weapons" Referring to the economic situation of his country, Mr. Putin claimed that the "peak of economic difficulties" had passed. According to him, "the Russian economy has adapted (...). What is happening is ultimately beneficial for Russia and its future, including in the economic sphere." In the evening, the Russian Ministry of Foreign Affairs also announced that Russia had requested the United Nations Security Council to open an international investigation into Washington's alleged involvement in the development of biological weapons in Ukraine. Shortly after the start of its offensive in Ukraine, Russia accused Washington of funding a biological weapons program in Ukraine. Both the United States and Kiev denied the existence of laboratories intended to produce such weapons in Ukraine, with Washington calling it a "pure invention." The UN, through Adedeji Ebo, Deputy High Representative for Disarmament Affairs before the Security Council, stated that it was "not aware" of such a program.

title: Midterms 2022: Europeans concerned about a possible reconsideration of American aid to Ukraine.
lead: As the mid-term elections approach, some Republicans want to reconsider the support provided to Kiev since the beginning of the war.
text_eng: Lingering British crisis, new Italian government dominated by the far right, Hungarian obstructions, and Franco-German tensions: Europe is shaking on all fronts. An additional storm threatens, coming from the United States and linked to the war in Ukraine. Could the bipartisan unity that has prevailed in Washington since February regarding the massive aid provided to Kiev be at risk of crumbling in the event of a Republican majority following the midterm elections scheduled for November 8? The latest polls, showing a rise in support for the Grand Old Party in voting intentions, give hope for at least regaining control of the House of Representatives, or even the Senate. However, within the House, a faction within the Republican Party is pushing for more oversight or even a reduction in aid to Ukraine. During a visit to Washington on Friday, October 21, to prepare for French President Emmanuel Macron's state visit in early December, French Minister of Foreign Affairs Catherine Colonna emphasized the need to maintain a united front among allies. "We must not give the Kremlin the opportunity to divide us," warned the diplomat during a speech at the Center for Strategic and International Studies, a think tank. "If Russia's actions go unanswered, wars of conquest could become the norm," she cautioned, reiterating these remarks later during a joint press conference with Secretary of State Antony Blinken. In May, the Senate approved a large $40 billion aid package for Ukraine. In the House, 57 Grand Old Party representatives voted against it (28% of the group). Their numbers appear to be growing, fueled by rising concerns about inflation among voters. Some weariness and even exasperation are starting to show within a segment of the Republican base, tempted by isolationism and sometimes fascinated by Vladimir Putin's brutal authoritarianism. Their leading advocate is Fox News host Tucker Carlson, who consistently shifts blame, accusing his own government of instigating and encouraging the current war. According to him, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky is a "client" of the Biden administration. No "blank check" for Kiev. The general public, however, observes Ukraine from a distance. Unlike in Europe, the war does not resonate as a familiar and close crisis. In a Pew Research Center poll from September, 37% of respondents believed that U.S. support for Ukraine was at an adequate level, but 20% thought it was too high, compared to only 7% in March. One in three Republicans now considers this aid to be excessive. Kevin McCarthy, the Republican leader in the House of Representatives, explained this week that there would be no "blank check" signed by his party for Kiev in case of a majority in Congress. He linked his comment to the recession that the United States could enter. In reality, the lawmaker is thinking a lot about his personal trajectory. Aspiring to become the House Speaker in January 2023, he needs to win the favor of the lawmakers from the MAGA movement ("Make America Great Again," Donald Trump's slogan). However, the former president holds a strong resentment against McCarthy. He blames him for allowing a House inquiry commission to be formed on the January 6, 2021 assault, which he considers 100% hostile despite the presence of two Republicans. On Friday, this commission formally issued a subpoena to Donald Trump to testify. A stronger consensus in the Senate On the other hand, in the Senate, the Republican leader, Mitch McConnell (Kentucky), issued a clear statement calling on allies to act "faster and more proactively" in support of Ukraine. He mentioned specifically anti-aircraft defense systems, long-range firing capabilities, as well as economic and humanitarian aid. McConnell assured that a future Republican majority in the Senate would ensure the "timely delivery of necessary weapons and greater assistance to Ukraine's allies." In the Senate, the consensus between Democrats and Republicans seems to hold better, even though some candidates – like J.D. Vance in Ohio – have also expressed skepticism about American investment. An argument often raised, beyond the significant sums allocated, is that Joe Biden would be more concerned about Ukraine's borders, a non-strategic state in their eyes, than those of his own country, especially the one with Mexico, where a record number of migrants have been apprehended this year. "These guys don't understand. It goes far beyond Ukraine," explained Joe Biden during a visit to Philadelphia on Thursday. "It's Eastern Europe. It's NATO. It's really serious, with significant consequences... They have no sense of American foreign policy." Democrats are considering voting on a new aid package for Ukraine in Congress between the November elections and the next term. It could amount to several tens of billions of dollars and garner support from some Republicans with traditional foreign policy convictions. Such aid would ensure continuity of American policy towards Russia in the course of 2023.

title: Rishi Sunak shows his support for Ukraine during his first diplomatic exchanges.
lead: The new Prime Minister of the United Kingdom has spoken with Volodymyr Zelensky and Joe Biden, after receiving congratulations from numerous heads of state.
text_eng: It was Volodymyr Zelensky who received the honor of the first call from Rishi Sunak to a foreign leader. The new British Prime Minister assured the Ukrainian President of his "unwavering support" and hoped that they would "meet in person soon," according to a statement from Downing Street. Earlier, Mr. Zelensky had congratulated the new British leader. "I wish you success in overcoming all the challenges facing British society and the world today. I am ready to continue strengthening the Ukrainian-British strategic partnership together," said the Ukrainian President on Twitter. Ukraine was at the heart of the first words addressed by U.S. President Joe Biden to the new British Prime Minister. "I am pleased that we are strengthening our cooperation on essential global security and prosperity issues, including continuing to firmly support Ukraine," he wrote on Twitter. During their first phone call later in the day, the two leaders also agreed "to hold Russia accountable for its aggression, confront the challenges posed by China, and ensure sustainable and affordable energy resources," emphasized the White House in a statement. Congratulations from Emmanuel Macron and Olaf Scholz Congratulating Mr. Sunak, Emmanuel Macron wrote on Twitter: "Together, we will continue to work to address the current challenges, including the war in Ukraine and its multiple consequences for Europe and the world." As for German Chancellor Olaf Scholz, he said he was "looking forward to continuing our cooperation and partnership within NATO and the G7 as close friends." In Moscow, however, where British support for Ukraine in the form of diplomatic support, arms deliveries, and combat training for Ukrainian soldiers is clearly viewed negatively, the tone was decidedly cold. "At this moment, we see no prerequisites, no basis, and we have no hope for any positive changes in the near future," reacted Kremlin spokesperson Dmitry Peskov to the press. He assured that Russia remained "open and ready to discuss difficult issues at the negotiating table," but not "at the expense of its interests." The European Commission attentive to Brexit compliance The European Commission urged Mr. Sunak to "fully" respect the agreements reached with the European Union after Brexit. "In these difficult times for our continent, we rely on a strong relationship with the UK to defend our common values, in full respect of our agreements," wrote the President of the Brussels executive, Ursula von der Leyen, on Twitter. "I remain determined to work intensively and constructively to promote such a partnership, in full respect of our agreements. This includes our work on common solutions within the framework of the protocol for Ireland and Northern Ireland," stated the European Commissioner responsible for post-Brexit relations with the UK, Maros Sefcovic. This topic was discussed during a meeting between Joe Biden and Rishi Sunak. The two leaders "discussed their shared commitment to protecting the achievements of the Good Friday Agreement in Northern Ireland," as reported by the White House, and the "need to reach a negotiated agreement with the European Union on the Northern Ireland Protocol," which outlines the post-Brexit arrangements in this British province. Support from Boris Johnson Former Conservative Prime Minister Boris Johnson had revisited the key text of the Brexit agreement, the Northern Ireland Protocol, signed and ratified by both parties, and unilaterally questioned the post-Brexit customs status of Northern Ireland. Mr. Johnson was in fact one of the first personalities to publicly congratulate his former Chancellor of the Exchequer, despite their notoriously strained relationship: "Congratulations to Rishi Sunak on this historic day," tweeted the former leader. "It is time for every Conservative to give our new Prime Minister their full and unwavering support."

title: Moscow places its Ukrainian conquests under martial law and evacuations.
lead: The exceptional regime implemented, which restricts movements and allows for the control of communications, also applies to the Russian border regions and grants local authorities exceptional powers.
text_eng: Russia is not at war, but a part of what it considers its territory is now living under martial law. For the first time in the history of the Russian Federation, Vladimir Putin signed a decree to this effect on Wednesday, October 19, and justified this decision to his Security Council by explaining that "Ukrainian neo-Nazis openly use terrorist methods and send saboteur groups to our territory." This state of emergency now applies to the four Ukrainian regions annexed by Moscow at the end of September – Luhansk, Donetsk, Zaporizhia, and Kherson. Martial law, a set of nineteen measures restricting the rights of citizens, is governed by a constitutional law from 2002. It allows for limitations on movement in these territories or at their borders, the prohibition of gatherings, the imposition of censorship, control of communications, including private ones, confiscation of property from individuals or businesses, requisitioning of citizens for military work, and even forced relocation of the population. This last point is particularly significant as evacuations began on Wednesday in the Kherson region, where the Ukrainian army is conducting a significant counteroffensive. The governor (Russian) of this region, Vladimir Saldo, also stated in the evening that "under martial law, power is transferred to the military." More than the explosion of a truck on the Crimean Bridge in early October, it is the Ukrainian advance in these annexed territories that motivates the measure. After military mobilization and the start of a campaign of strikes on Ukrainian infrastructure, martial law is supposed to complete the preparation of Russia for battle. Because the entire country is affected. From the border regions of Ukraine to Moscow, various special regimes, more or less reinforced, give local authorities exceptional powers, ranging from population displacement to the closure of administrative borders of regions, including Internet control. A point in the decree signed by Vladimir Putin provides that the governors of all Russian regions can take "other appropriate measures" if they deem it necessary, without further details. For legal experts, this provision amounts to a "blank check" given to regional authorities and the establishment of a "de facto martial law" throughout the country. On Wednesday evening, the governors of the border regions of Ukraine took turns assuring that the situation was perfectly under control and that no specific measures were adopted. Only the governor of Belgorod, the region most affected by Ukrainian bombings, announced the extension of school holidays. "This fiercely recalls what prevailed during the Covid pandemic," comments exiled journalist Farida Roustamova. "Governors are given exceptional powers that they do not want, because from the center [Moscow], nothing is under control anymore. After the 'partial' mobilization, it's 'partial' martial law." Another reminiscence of the Covid-19 period is the Prime Minister, Mikhail Mishustin, and the Mayor of Moscow, Sergei Sobyanin, being given new responsibilities, with the establishment of a coordination council for the former, and the organization of territorial defenses for the latter. Both men are known to belong to the 'peace party,' meaning the camp of moderates. Evacuations This desire to involve all officials is also seen at the other end of the spectrum, with the spotlight on General Sergei Surovikin, the new commander-in-chief of the 'special operation' in Ukraine and a hero of the most radical fringes of the elite. Television first crafted his image as an inflexible man. Then, during an interview broadcast on television on Tuesday, he was tasked with discussing a 'tense' military situation and warning that 'difficult decisions' awaited the country. The next day, evacuations from the region and city of Kherson began. The goal is to move up to 60,000 people to the left bank of the Dnieper, using ferries. According to authorities, the aim is to protect these civilians from Ukrainian bombings in populated areas. The maneuver would also cover the rotation of military units, which are also having difficulty crossing the river. According to testimonies collected by Le Monde, the population seemed to remain calm. 'It's the collaborators who are panicking!' assures one of the 70 employees of the Kherson theater who stayed behind (out of 250 before the invasion). 'Everyone I spoke to today, our actors and employees of the Kherson National University, are saying: 'Why do we have to leave our city? We will be here to welcome our own.' According to Vladimir Kramar, a 24-year-old professor now a refugee in Germany but still in contact with friends who stayed behind, 'many stores and individuals are already refusing to accept rubles in exchange for goods - after all, what's the point if the Ukrainian army will soon be here?' 'Controlling the population to facilitate military operations' For now, local authorities insist that they have no intention of abandoning Kherson to the Ukrainian army - the only regional capital captured by Moscow, but its isolated position on the right bank of the Dnieper makes it vulnerable. However, Russian military observers report troop rotations that could suggest preparations for a retreat. Some administrations have already been relocated." "The Russians are currently threatened with encirclement in Kherson and risk, in the short or medium term, being unable to evacuate civilian populations, even though Vladimir Putin has committed to protecting them since their annexation to Russia," notes Thibault Fouillet, a researcher at the Foundation for Strategic Research. "To avoid a reverse Mariupol scenario and the humiliation of having to request the opening of humanitarian corridors from the Ukrainians, Moscow prefers to preemptively evacuate the city." Evacuating a portion of the civilians, at least the most loyal ones, also allows the Russians to retain the option of engaging in fierce urban combat, similar to what the Ukrainians did in Sievierodonetsk, where Moscow's troops took two months to seize the city at a significant cost. According to experts, the imposition of martial law in the annexed regions should also be understood in this context. "This measure allows for controlling the population to facilitate military operations, by imposing curfews or relocating people from one place to another," notes Thibault Fouillet. "It is a way for the Russians to anticipate the next stages of the war."

title: War in Ukraine: the theory of staging, a very strategic argument for pro-Russians
lead: Ukrainians allegedly disguised as injured for photos, massacres that would be scenes for the cinema, a president filmed on a green screen... The Kremlin's propaganda uses all the tricks to deny images considered embarrassing.
text_eng: According to those who spread these rumors, nothing would be true. The woman with a swollen face, featured on the front page of the newspaper Libération on October 11th? An extra, made up for the photo. The speeches of Volodymyr Zelensky? Sequences filmed in a studio, against a green screen. As for the videos portraying Russian soldiers as aggressors, it would be Hollywood cinema, in scenes filmed by the American director Sean Penn, believe the most conspiratorial. Since the beginning of the invasion in Ukraine, the Kremlin and its supporters in the French-speaking conspiracy sphere have been contesting the authenticity of images that embarrass them, as if Kiev was behind a massive operation to manipulate reality. Each time, this theory is based on documents taken out of context. A video was shared this fall to prove that the alleged massacres in Ukraine are fictitious. In fact, one of the "corpses" lying under tarps briefly lifts his arm. In reality, it was a performance to raise awareness about the risks of climate change, filmed by Austrian television. The excerpt had already been misused by the conspiracy sphere to denounce the alleged fake deaths of Covid-19 in early 2022. The "comedian-president" In this war that is also fought on the communication front, the Ukrainian president, Volodymyr Zelensky, has the particularity of having been a professional actor, and even played the role of a president in the series "Servant of the People" before taking on the role in real life. Shortly after his election in 2019, Vladimir Putin joked about his professional skills: "It seems that he is a good specialist in his field. But it's one thing to play a role, it's another to be someone." The Russian president continues to nickname Volodymyr Zelensky "the comedian," accusing him of duplicity and cheating. A rhetoric echoed by the French conspiracist Silvano Trotta, a relay of Kremlin propaganda, who claims, regarding the Ukrainians: "In addition to being Nazis, they are also manipulators. Their president being an actor, they know how to do it." On October 11th, a well-known Twitter account of far-right conspiracy theorists published a video of Volodymyr Zelensky in a recording studio, stating that all "videos of #Zelensky outdoors in Kiev are fake. In reality, he films behind a green screen in Poland. He sends hundreds of thousands of people to the slaughterhouse but he hides quietly in Hollywood." Not only does he present a very personal sense of geography, but above all, he forgets to specify that the sequence shows the preparation of a speech given simultaneously and in hologram by the Ukrainian president in four rooms dedicated to new technologies, including VivaTech, in Paris, on June 16 - and absolutely not his video clips in the streets of Kiev. Conversely, in March 2020, Marine Le Pen's former right-hand man, Florian Philippot, was outraged by a video where Volodymyr Zelensky was shooting at Russian deputies - without noticing, or wanting to notice, that the excerpt in question came from a trailer of his series in 2017: for once, it was indeed a fiction for television. Turning massacres into movie scenes It is of course possible that some images are indeed staged. For example, at the beginning of October, the overly perfect video of a Russian armored vehicle and its occupants surrendering without resistance raised more than one eyebrow. But the Kremlin and its supporters not only denounce suspicious images when they exist. They systematically cast doubt on all images embarrassing for Moscow, even when the proof of their veracity has been provided. Denial was total when hundreds of civilian bodies were discovered in Bucha in April. Pro-Russian circles immediately multiplied theories accrediting a staged event: the dead would have been placed by the army or by the Ukrainian police, or they were extras, or even mannequins... This kind of argument had already been used during the war in Syria. In 2017, China claimed that the viral images of little Omran, a Syrian child rescued from the rubble of Aleppo, photographed dazed and covered in dust, were staged by the rescuers. In 2018, based on manipulated images from the filming of a pro-regime feature film, a chemical attack on a neighborhood in Ghouta was described by Russia as a "staged scene in front of the cameras" by the White Helmets, a Syrian rescue organization. A strategy of "desensitization" "This type of disinformation is produced as part of the aggressor's strategy, known in English as Darvo [acronym for "deny, attack and reverse victim and offender"], to attack and discredit victims of violence," explained Stephanie Lamy to Le Monde in April, author of "Agora toxica, civil society in the Internet age" (Editions du détour, 2022). What may seem like grotesque and unworthy gestures is part of a real strategy. "The use of falsehoods and lies has been carefully thought out," says Dimitri Minic, a researcher at the French Institute of International Relations and a specialist in Russian strategic culture. Logical tricks, the inversion of cause and effect, the manipulation of intentions, all of this has been theorized by the Russian army to deceive and even reformat the opponent during psychological-informational confrontation. Russian disinformation does not make war unreal, but seeks to make Ukrainian victims less credible to the international public opinion. "It is a way to cut off any possibility of identification or empathy, a process of dehumanization, even desensitization," analyzes Marie Peltier, author of Obsession: Inside the Conspiracy Theory Narrative (Inculte editions, 2018). The goal is to prevent us from being affected by what is happening."

title: Anger in Iran, kamikaze drones in Ukraine, Article 49.3 in France... October in drawings
lead: Once a month, "La Matinale" offers you a review of the news in illustrations, signed by members of the international collective of cartoonists Cartooning for Peace and published on the front page of "Le Monde".
text_eng: In October, the news was notably marked by the continuation of the Iranians' mobilization against the mullahs' regime, a fuel shortage in France caused by strikes in refineries, the resignation of Liz Truss after only forty-four days at Downing Street. And the continuation of the war in Ukraine, where Russia now uses kamikaze drones. "La Matinale" offers you an overview in eleven drawings, classified by theme. Article 49.3 and fuel shortage in France Drawing published on the front page of "Le Monde" dated October 12, 2022, signed by the French cartoonist Urbs. URBS The fake suspense ended on October 19 in the National Assembly when the Prime Minister, Elisabeth Borne, engaged her government's responsibility by triggering Article 49.3 to pass the first part of the budget bill without a vote. The next day, the same process was repeated for the revenue side of the Social Security budget. The motions of censure subsequently filed by the New Popular Ecological and Social Union (Nupes) and the National Rally were unsurprisingly rejected on October 24 by the deputies. Two days later, Elisabeth Borne again used Article 49.3 to pass the entire Social Security financing bill. Drawing published on the front page of "Le Monde" dated October 20, 2022, signed by the French cartoonist Urbs. URBS The strike by employees of TotalEnergies refineries, demanding salary increases, led to fuel shortages and long queues at gas stations. By Wednesday, October 26, the movement was only affecting two sites of the group, in Normandy and in the Rhône, but more than 20% of gas stations were still facing supply difficulties in twenty-five departments, with the Paris region being particularly affected. War in Ukraine Drawing published on the front page of "Le Monde" dated October 11, 2022, signed by the Ukrainian cartoonist Vladimir Kazanevsky. KAZANEVSKY Peace negotiations with Moscow are currently impossible because "no one believes" the Russian authorities, said Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky on October 10. His defense minister, Oleksii Reznikov, ruled out any territorial compromise with Russia, especially regarding Crimea, annexed by Moscow in 2014, and the Donbass, partly controlled by separatists supported by Moscow, where Moscow organized "referendums" for annexation. Drawing published on the front page of "Le Monde" dated October 21, 2022, signed by the Mexican cartoonist Darío. DARÍO Russia used kamikaze drones to attack Ukrainian cities, including the capital, Kiev, on October 17. These Shahed-136 drones, of Iranian origin and repainted in Russian colors, are used to target civilian areas and terrorize populations. According to Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky, Moscow has ordered around 2,000 of these military vehicles from Tehran. The European Union (EU) reacted on October 20 by adopting a series of sanctions against Iran. Political instability in the United Kingdom Cartoon published on the front page of "Le Monde" dated October 26, 2022, signed by Brazilian cartoonist Amorim. AMORIM The swift resignation of Liz Truss on October 20, after only forty-four days at the helm of the United Kingdom, marks the failure of the promises made by Brexit supporters during the 2016 referendum. The project to transform the island into a tax haven "liberated" from the social, financial, and environmental rules of the EU had been set aside by Boris Johnson, due to Covid-19. Liz Truss, on the other hand, wanted to implement it, causing financial panic and leading the country to the brink of collapse. Rishi Sunak succeeded her on October 25, becoming the third Conservative British Prime Minister of 2022. Presidential election in Brazil Cartoon published on the front page of "Le Monde" dated October 5, 2022, signed by Swiss cartoonist Herrmann. HERRMANN In Brazil, Jair Bolsonaro will face Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva in the second round of the presidential election on Sunday, October 30. The incumbent president had achieved a higher score than predicted by polling institutes in the first round on October 2, gathering 43.2% of the votes. Since the beginning of his term on January 1, 2019, the far-right leader has systematically dismantled nature protection agencies. Since that date, deforestation in all ecosystems has reached 42,000 square kilometers, equivalent to the surface area of Switzerland, and that of the Amazon, in particular, has increased by 75% compared to the previous decade. Protests in Iran Cartoon published on the front page of "Le Monde" dated October 6, 2022, signed by Algerian cartoonist Dilem. DILEM The anger of the population in Iran shows no signs of abating, six weeks after the death on September 16 of Mahsa Amini, a 22-year-old Iranian Kurdish woman, following her arrest by the morality police for violating the strict dress code of the Islamic Republic. Her death sparked a widespread protest movement against the regime of the mullahs, with Iranian women, many without headscarves, at the forefront. FIFA World Cup in Qatar Cartoon published on the front page of "Le Monde" dated October 13, 2022, signed by Brazilian cartoonist Amorim. AMORIM One month before the opening of the FIFA World Cup on November 20 in Qatar, a sense of unease is spreading in French society. In the media and on social networks, the debate is growing on the relevance of watching the competition. The main reasons cited include: The working conditions often deplorable inflicted on the workers who built the tournament's infrastructure, the numerous deaths on the construction sites, and the high environmental cost of the upcoming competition. CCP Congress in China Drawing published on the front page of "Le Monde" dated October 18, 2022, signed by the Taiwanese cartoonist Stellina. STELLINA The 20th Congress of the Chinese Communist Party, held from October 16 to 22, saw Xi Jinping re-elected as the party's leader, ensuring him a third presidential term in March 2023. Xi had broken a taboo in 2018 by amending the Constitution to serve more than two presidential terms. The closing ceremony was marked by the eviction of former President Hu Jintao (2003-2013), escorted out apparently against his will. Winter Games in... Saudi Arabia Drawing published on the front page of "Le Monde" dated October 7, 2022, signed by the French cartoonist Urbs. URBS The Olympic Council of Asia (OCA) announced on Tuesday, October 4, that the desert kingdom of Saudi Arabia will host the 2029 Asian Winter Games. The events will take place in Neom, a futuristic megacity under construction in the northwest of the kingdom, a project worth several hundred billion dollars led by Crown Prince Mohammed Ben Salman.

title: Xi Jinping's history lesson for the hierarchs of the Chinese Communist Party
lead: The Chinese president took the new leadership of the CCP, which emerged from its 20th congress, to Yan'an, a city in Shaanxi that was the endpoint of the Long March in 1935.
text_eng: It is a tradition: after each congress of the Chinese Communist Party (CCP), the General Secretary, Xi Jinping, takes the new party leadership to visit a symbolic location. In 2012, it was Shenzhen, a symbol of China's opening to the world. In 2017, Shanghai, where the party was founded in July 1921. And last week, it was Yan'an, a city in Shaanxi, in the northwest of the country, where Mao Zedong and his supporters stayed from 1935 to 1948 after the Long March, before invading Beijing and seizing power. The objective of this Chinese-style team-building excursion is to "cherish the glorious achievements of the revolutionary old generation" and "demonstrate the firm determination of the new leadership to pass on the revolutionary genes." According to the statement released following this visit on October 29, Xi Jinping particularly emphasized the importance of the 7th CCP Congress held in Yan'an in 1945. A congress that "confirmed Mao Zedong's ideological leadership position within the party," according to propaganda. "Thanks to the Yan'an work style rectification movement, the entire party was united under Mao Zedong's banner and achieved unprecedented unity and solidarity within the party," the statement specifies. The unity was even stronger as Mao did not convene another congress until 1956, a fact conveniently omitted by the propaganda. In Mao's footsteps, the message conveyed by this staging is, of course, that following the 20th congress, where he secured a third term, Xi Jinping fully aligns himself with Mao's legacy and that the other members of the Politburo Standing Committee must "resolutely implement" the "firm and just leadership" defined by Xi. The photos and videos released during this visit show Xi Jinping constantly lecturing the other six members of the CCP leadership, as if these sixty-year-olds with over thirty years of experience were discovering the party's history. At the same time as this visit was taking place, the Minister of Foreign Affairs, Wang Yi, who was promoted to the Politburo, called his Russian counterpart, Sergey Lavrov, to report on the "success" of the 20th congress. On this occasion, Wang Yi stated that "China will also firmly support the Russian side, under the leadership of President Vladimir Putin, in uniting the Russian people and guiding them to overcome difficulties and eliminate disturbances, achieve strategic development goals, and enhance Russia's status as a major power on the international stage." This statement, which only briefly mentions the "Ukrainian issue" in the very last line, seems to be intended to confirm that, after the 20th congress, China is maintaining its course of "unlimited friendship" with Russia, as defined by Xi and Putin on February 4th in Beijing. While China is not providing military support to Russia, it also sees itself more than ever in a "struggle" against the West and its values.

title: In Russia, feminists against war.
lead: Exiled since March, Daria Serenko leads a highly committed resistance movement opposed to Vladimir Putin's regime, both inside and outside the country.
text_eng: The movement was born in secrecy on February 25, following the entry of Russian troops into Ukrainian territory, but as its co-founder Daria Serenko immediately points out, "we were not starting from scratch." The feminist anti-war resistance (Feminists Against War, FAS) brings together 45 organizations that already existed in various sectors, along with dozens of anonymous activists in sixty cities in Russia, not to mention those who had to go into exile. It is a network increasingly determined to take action and make itself heard. With her asymmetrical short hair and direct gaze, Daria Serenko, who visited Paris in early October, is categorical: the violence in Ukraine fuels domestic violence, and vice versa. "War and women's rights are closely linked," she explains, "because on one hand, men returning with their traumas pose a real danger to women. On the other hand, those who commit the worst crimes [on the battlefield] are often the same ones who are most brutal at home." The 29-year-old activist, one of the few public figures of the movement, does not forget to mention the primary violence, that of power. "Vladimir Putin is the stupidest representation of Russian masculinity," she says. "Unfortunately, he serves as a role model for some Russian men, but he does not represent us. We laugh about it, even though it's hard to laugh in a dictatorship." A poet, literature teacher "fired from everywhere," the young woman left Russia to seek refuge in Georgia two weeks after the creation of FAS and a final stay in prison from February 7 to 23, just before the start of the war. Accused of "extremism" – the presence of opposition leader Alexei Navalny's Anti-Corruption Foundation logo on her Instagram account was enough – she was arrested at the same time as her friend Maria Alekhina, a member of the feminist punk group Pussy Riot. Placed under house arrest, Alekhina managed to escape in April disguised as a food delivery person. "The time for peaceful resistance is over." In Russia, the feminist movement has continued to grow as societal repression has increased, especially since the decriminalization of domestic violence law was passed in 2017, with strong support from the Orthodox Church. But it was the war that united its action. Born in Siberia and now living in Moscow, Daria Serenko, who is also an LGBT activist, herself became involved in 2014, after Russia's first aggression against Ukraine, the annexation of Crimea, and the start of armed conflict in the Donbass. "War is a setback, a crucible of conservatism," she emphasizes. "During World War II, women took over men's roles at home, only to be excluded from important positions again. And voilà! They were then sent back to the reproductive front." On October 7th, in Paris, the activist, invited to testify at a forum organized by the association Russia-Liberties, bluntly exposed her current perspective: "The time for peaceful resistance is over. I have always been in favor of peaceful demonstrations, but now I am not anymore." In fact, the FAS activists, connected through a constantly buzzing Telegram channel, have shifted to the offensive with their limited means. In Russia, they distribute Genska Pravda ("The Truth of Women"), an underground newspaper printed on personal printers and distributed discreetly like the samizdats of dissidents during the USSR era, in order to "break the information blockade." They organize, at their own risk, commando operations such as the one that involved installing 2,000 memorials in Russia overnight in tribute to the dead of Mariupol, the martyred Ukrainian city: crosses, sometimes even with names, planted in courtyards "in the same way as Ukrainians forced to bury their loved ones at the foot of their buildings." They also participate in sabotage actions by Russian "partisans" against strategic sites. Over 200 activists are currently facing legal action. On October 21st, a court in St. Petersburg sentenced Alissa Droujina to five days in prison for displaying a banner in the city that read "The zinc coffin on wheels is already on your street." According to the accusation, the young woman is part of the Feminist Anti-War Resistance and her banner was supposed to appear on the Telegram group to be shared by others. This channel - which has 42,000 subscribers - is full of drawings, stickers, and slogans ready to be disseminated. One of them shows Vladimir Putin immersed in a bathtub filled with blood poured by the Minister of Defense, Sergei Shoigu. The "partial" mobilization has multiplied the determination. Most of the detained feminists have been sentenced to administrative penalties, but several are still in detention. This is the case, notably, of Alexandra Skotchilenko. Arrested since March 31st, this 32-year-old musician, accused of being part of a "radical feminist group," faces a ten-year prison sentence under the law adopted at the beginning of the war on "fake news," for swapping price tags with anti-war slogans in a supermarket. "By replacing something very ordinary with something different, unusual, we show that there is not a single place in our country untouched by war, and we do not allow people to simply turn a blind eye to what is happening," advocates the FAS channel. "We document the war with quotes from Ukrainian women," explains Daria Serenko. The "partial" mobilization, decreed at the end of September by Vladimir Putin, has multiplied the determination of the feminists. Volunteers, who are already assisting deported Ukrainians seeking to leave Russia, as well as opponents facing threats of prosecution, have also mobilized to help men at risk of being conscripted. "In Dagestan, women have come out to protest against the mobilization, as well as in Chechnya where, for the first time in a long time, one hundred and twenty women dared to demonstrate. [Ramzan] Kadyrov [the Chechen leader] brought their husbands and told them, 'Either you beat them, or we will take care of it,'" reports Daria Serenko. "We are also taking care of homosexuals and transgender individuals who did not have time to change their documents and are being mobilized," she adds - although often attacked, changing gender on civil documents is still possible in Russia. From their countries of asylum, the activists grouped in associations abroad act as intermediaries, "even though it is becoming increasingly difficult with the closing of borders." Several of them, trained as lawyers or psychologists, offer their services online anonymously on both sides. The imposition of martial law in border regions on October 19 adds to the concern. And it is not a question of generation. "Recently, a babushka [grandmother] threw a Molotov cocktail into a Sberbank branch in Moscow shouting 'no to war'!" laughs Daria Serenko. She notes another phenomenon that could increase the number of women mobilized alongside FAS. "Many 'cargo 200' are arriving," she says, using the coded term well known in Russia since the Soviet war in Afghanistan, which refers to the dead evacuated from a battlefield. For the feminist activist, "the war is entering homes," and it is no coincidence, she says, that the most intense reactions come from territories like Dagestan, from where a portion of the troops sent to the front originated and where there are heavy losses: "Many women also understand that there is discrimination. The anti-war movement, continues Daria Serenko, enthusiastically, will play an important role because the state is trying to silence grieving families, yet already, women, supporters, and minorities are forming a rapidly growing collective." The activist remains realistic, however: "We have studied several wars, such as in Yugoslavia, Vietnam, or Afghanistan, and, on average, anti-war campaigns do not gain traction before three years... This was the case with the Soldiers' Mothers Committee for Chechnya." Born in 1989 against the treatment of conscripts in the Russian army, this human rights organization had indeed spread with over 200 active committees throughout Russia by 1997, three years after the start of the first Russo-Chechen war (1994-1999). In Ukraine, the president, Volodymyr Zelensky, who constantly appeals to the mothers and wives of Russian soldiers, often invokes this memory.

title: "Dirty bomb": beyond the accusations between Kiev and Moscow, what is it about?
lead: Since October 23, Russian and Ukrainian forces have been accusing each other of preparing to use a "dirty bomb." What does this unconventional weapon consist of?
text_eng: Since Sunday, October 23, Moscow has claimed that Ukraine has entered the "final phase" of preparing a "dirty bomb" that it intends to use on its own territory. Kiev's alleged objective is to accuse Moscow of launching it. These allegations have been denied by Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky and several of his allies. However, a question arises regarding this unconventional weapon, which has never been used before and does not require possession of a nuclear weapon: what does it consist of?

title: Giuliano da Empoli awarded the grand prize of the French Academy
lead: The Italo-Swiss author is being rewarded for his critical novel about Russian President Vladimir Putin, "The Wizard of the Kremlin."
text_eng: This is the most coveted and prestigious literary prize among the many awarded by the Immortals. Italo-Swiss Giuliano da Empoli received, on Thursday, October 27, the grand prize for the novel from the French Academy for "The Mage of the Kremlin" (Gallimard, 280 pages, 20 euros, digital 15 euros), a novel critical of Russian President Vladimir Putin. Giuliano da Empoli, 49 years old, born in Neuilly-sur-Seine, was a political advisor to the Italian Prime Minister Matteo Renzi. In his novel, published in April by Gallimard, he provides an overview of the power mechanisms in Russia over the past thirty years. The long monologue he imagines in the mouth of the fictional Kremlin advisor Vadim Baranov is inspired by the career of Vladislav Surkov, co-founder of Vladimir Putin's United Russia party for the 2001 presidential election. Vadim Baranov reveals the cynicism of a president he calls "the Tsar," offering the opportunity to revisit the events that have shaken Russia's history since the breakup of the Soviet Union in 1991. "My book is truly imbued with a certain French literature, which has had its place in the Academy for a long time, and which dissects power, observes it," said the winner to the press. Mr. da Empoli received nine votes, compared to five for Jean Michelin, a career military officer, and three for Le Monde journalist Pascale Robert-Diard. This choice was not very surprising as the prize is presided over by a secretary of the French Academy with expertise in Russian affairs, Hélène Carrère d'Encausse. Mr. da Empoli could also win the Goncourt Prize on November 3, as he is one of the four finalists. Winning both of these literary prizes in the same year is an achievement accomplished by few authors, as both Academies generally value the independence of their choices.

title: "The Ice Age of Putin's Russia: The Man Who Dared to Oppose Putin" - when Bill Browder recounts his crusade to pass the "Magnitsky Act"
lead: The American businessman who became a British citizen is behind the law, adopted by the Obama administration, which allows for the sanctioning of government officials who violate human rights.
text_eng:
Book. Initially, nothing predisposed William Browder, the head of a speculative investment fund, to become a staunch advocate for human rights. But ten years spent in Russia changed the trajectory of this 58-year-old American businessman, who became a naturalized British citizen. Through sheer determination, he succeeded in getting the "Magnitsky Act" passed in the United States in 2012. Originally targeting thirteen Russian officials for their role in the disappearance and torture death of Sergei Magnitsky, the lawyer for Hermitage Capital, the sanctions expanded in 2016 to include other state officials, such as those involved in the murder of Saudi journalist Jamal Khashoggi or the repression of Uighurs in China.

To achieve this result, Bill Browder led an incredible crusade, which he describes in detail in his narrative - the second after "Red Notice" (Kero, 2015). With him, we jump from one plane to another, rubbing shoulders with not always well-intentioned lawyers, motivated whistleblowers, corrupt police officers, and skeptical politicians. We enter the offices of the powerful, become familiar with the circles of a global elite where fortunes are made. And one also becomes frankly paranoid reading about all the maneuvers Moscow attempted to thwart Browder's plans: legal harassment, witness poisoning, surveillance, dirty tricks, buying complicity... Endemic corruption.

When he ventured into capitalism - "to annoy" his family, as he confessed to Vanity Fair in 2017 - Bill Browder, the grandson of the former General Secretary of the Communist Party in the United States, Earl Browder, had no idea what awaited him in Russia. At first, business was going rather well, even very well: "At the peak of my career, I was responsible for managing $4.5 billion invested in Russian securities," he writes. However, colossal sums disappeared into the depths of endemic corruption that he set out to expose. Barred from entering Russia in 2005, targeted by Interpol red notices, the businessman was accused of embezzlement until Sergei Magnitsky, who remained in Russia, uncovered a fraudulent tax refund of $230 million from the fund to Russian shell companies. Things went so wrong that Browder fought even harder, drawing the wrath of Russia up to the highest levels. In July 2018, during his bilateral meeting with former U.S. President Donald Trump in Helsinki, Finland, while Russian interference in American elections was still scandalous, Vladimir Putin mentioned Bill Browder as one of the most acute points of contention between the two countries. The attack failed. Since then, Canada and the European Union have in turn adopted texts inspired by the Magnitsky Act.

title: Russia suspends the agreement on Ukrainian grain exports after a drone attack on its ships.
