lead: Very critical of this agreement signed in July under the auspices of Turkey and the UN, Moscow had been looking for a pretext to denounce it for several weeks.
text_eng: The agreement on grain exports from Ukrainian ports, vital for global food supply, was suspended by Russia on Saturday, October 29th. Moscow justified this suspension by a drone attack targeting the Russian Black Sea fleet stationed in the bay of Sevastopol, in annexed Crimea, on Saturday morning. Ukraine, on the other hand, denounced it as "a pretext" and called for pressure on Russia to "once again commit to respecting its obligations" under the agreement concluded in July under the auspices of the United Nations (UN) and Turkey, the only agreement between Moscow and Kiev since the conflict began on February 24th. The Joint Coordination Center responsible for overseeing this agreement confirmed that no cargo movements had been approved for Sunday. Nine cargoes were able to pass through the Black Sea maritime corridor on Saturday, and "more than ten others" are ready to do the same in both directions. According to the Ukrainian Minister of Infrastructure, Oleksandr Kubrakov, a ship loaded with 40 tons of grain was supposed to depart from Ukraine to Ethiopia on Sunday, "but due to Russia's blockade of the grain corridor, exports are impossible," he wrote on Twitter. In his daily video posted on the internet, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky stated that the Russian decision "did not actually date from today." "Russia began exacerbating the global food shortage in September when it started blocking the movement of ships carrying our agricultural products," he said. A "scandalous" decision, according to Biden "This is a transparent intention by Russia to once again threaten large-scale famine in Africa and Asia," he added. According to him, at least 176 ships carrying over two million tons of grain were already blocked by Moscow. "A strong international response is needed. At the UN level, and especially at the G20 level," from which Russia should no longer participate, he said. The President of the United States, Joe Biden, for his part, deemed Russia's decision "scandalous." "There was no reason for them to do this," he told the press. Secretary of State Antony Blinken, on the other hand, stated that Moscow is "once again using food as a weapon," exacerbating already severe humanitarian crises and food insecurity worldwide. The UN Secretary-General, Antonio Guterres, expressed deep concern about the situation, as reported by his spokesperson in a statement on Sunday. Mr. Guterres is engaged in "intense consultations" to urge Russia to reverse its decision and has decided to postpone his departure for one day to attend the Arab League summit in Algiers scheduled for Tuesday. He notably spoke with the European Union's foreign policy chief, Josep Borrell, who called on Moscow to "return to the agreement" in a tweet. "The EU will do its part to tackle the global food crisis," added Mr. Borrell. The cereal agreement allowed for the export of millions of tons of grains that had been stuck in Ukrainian ports since the beginning of Russia's invasion of Ukraine. The blockade had caused a spike in food prices, raising fears of famines. London accused of complicity by Moscow Russian President Vladimir Putin has been critical of this agreement in recent weeks, emphasizing that his country's exports, as another major cereal producer, were suffering due to international sanctions. Moscow justified this suspension by citing a drone attack on Saturday morning in annexed Crimea. "The preparation of this terrorist act and the training of military personnel from the 73rd Ukrainian center for special maritime operations were carried out by British specialists based in Otchakov [Otchakiv in Ukrainian], in the Mykolaiv oblast, Ukraine," the Russian defense ministry said on Telegram. The British defense ministry responded by denouncing "false information" intended to "distract attention." "The maritime drones were moving in the security zone of the 'cereal corridor,'" the Russian defense ministry said on Sunday, claiming to have recovered drone debris and that some of these drones had "navigation modules made in Canada." One of them may have been launched "from one of the civilian ships chartered by Kiev or its Western masters for the export of agricultural products from Ukrainian seaports," it judged. Moscow also accused London of being involved in the explosions that damaged the Russian Nord Stream 1 and 2 gas pipelines in the Baltic Sea in September and promised to bring the issue to the UN Security Council. The British defense ministry denounced a "fabricated story" by Russia to "distract attention from its disastrous handling of the illegal invasion of Ukraine."

title: Belarus takes another step towards direct involvement in the war in Ukraine.
lead: After the announcement of the creation of a joint military alliance between Russia and Belarus, a "hidden mobilization" is reportedly underway, according to the independent Belarusian newspaper "Nacha Niva."
text_eng: Since the beginning of the invasion in Ukraine, Belarus has been serving as a logistical platform for its Russian ally. On February 24, Moscow's troops had departed from Belarusian territory in an unsuccessful attempt to conquer the capital, Kiev. On October 10, a portion of the missile barrage that struck all of Ukraine also came from Belarusian territory. A week later, Ukrainian authorities claimed that the "kamikaze drones" that hit Kiev on Monday, October 17, were launched from Belarus. So far, the Belarusian army has not participated in the fighting on Ukrainian territory, but concerns about its direct involvement in the conflict and the opening of a new front in the north of the country have increased since Belarusian dictator Alexander Lukashenko announced on October 10 the creation of a joint military group with Russia. Officially, this force will be purely defensive and aimed at securing the Belarusian border against "terrorist" attacks allegedly being prepared by Ukraine, Poland, and Lithuania, according to the leader. "We have not killed anyone [in Ukraine] and do not plan to do so. No one has asked us to participate in this operation," he assured on October 14 on the American channel NBC. Formalized complicity When asked about the size of this new force, Lukashenko stated that the Belarusian army would be the "base" of the group, without specifying how many Russian soldiers would be involved. "It is not necessary to ask for 10,000 or 15,000 people from Russia now, because there are other problems there, the ones you know," added the Belarusian leader. The first Russian soldiers of this joint force have already arrived, as announced by the Belarusian Ministry of Defense on Saturday, with supporting images. Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky accused Moscow of wanting to "drag Belarus into the war" and requested an international observation mission from the G7 at the border between Ukraine and Belarus. "We know what the Russians are capable of since 2014 [the start of the war in Donbass], but we never imagined that Belarus would submit so cowardly to Moscow," sighed a Ukrainian diplomatic source. In their view, the formation of this joint military group is not a turning point but rather "the formalization of complicity" between Minsk and the Russian government. The head of the Ukrainian Parliament's Foreign Affairs Committee, Oleksandr Merezhko, called on Friday for diplomatic relations to be severed with Belarus. "In my opinion, the question of [this] rupture (...) is overdue. It is increasingly difficult to have diplomatic relations with a country led by a dictator," stated Merezhko, a member of the ruling party. Analysts doubt that Belarusian soldiers could pose a significant threat to Ukraine. With only 48,000 men, the Belarusian army, poorly equipped and unprepared, is one of the smallest in the post-Soviet space, and has few modern weapons. However, Kiev claims to "not exclude anything" and is preparing for all scenarios, including a possible new invasion from Belarusian territory. Belarusian resistance groups On the other hand, the Belarusian population does not seem ready to see its army directly engage in the Ukrainian conflict. According to a study published in August by the British think tank Chatham House, 70% of Belarusians are hostile to it. Eager to avoid new international sanctions, Alexander Lukashenko himself is reluctant to send troops. But his room for maneuver is narrower than ever in the face of Vladimir Putin, to whom he has become indebted since he owes his political survival to him after his contested re-election in August 2020. Has he received instructions from the Kremlin? According to the independent Belarusian newspaper Nacha Niva, Mr. Lukashenko would have decided to carry out a "hidden mobilization" aimed at "increasing the personnel of combat units". This mobilization, of an unknown scale to date, would be carried out "under the pretext of verifying military readiness". The newspaper claims to "know with certainty that, so far, only residents of rural regions of Belarus are receiving summonses" and that large cities "have not yet been affected". "Emphasis is placed on specialties lacking in the Russian army," it specifies, citing drivers, cooks, and technical personnel in particular. Belarusians have been fighting in Ukraine since the beginning of the war, but within the Ukrainian armed forces, against the Russians. Opponents of Lukashenko's regime have formed two regiments, totaling theoretically over a thousand soldiers. Belarusian resistance groups have also organized a "railway war" in their country and carried out dozens of sabotage acts to slow down the advance of Russian troops.

title: "Erdogan is acting with only one goal in mind: the presidential election of 2023 in Turkey."
lead: Recep Tayyip Erdogan aims to embody Turkish political Islam of the 21st century. But more than for the reconstruction of an empire, it is primarily for electoral purposes that he uses the international stage, analyzes Olivier Bouquet, a professor of Ottoman history, in an article for "Le Monde".
text_eng: Recep Tayyip Erdogan: a sultan pursuing a neo-Ottoman expansionist and anti-Western policy? Perhaps. But let's listen carefully to the speeches he delivers at an increasing pace. Let's not overestimate the importance he places on international politics. Between the national holiday on October 29, 2022, and the celebration of the centenary of the founding of the Turkish Republic on October 29, 2023, Erdogan thinks and acts with only one deadline in mind: the presidential election scheduled for the end of spring 2023. His actions abroad stem from personal ambition, which follows a policy of history. Unlike Vladimir Putin, he must face the ballot box. More than the reconstitution of an empire, the invasion of a neighbor, or the shifting of borders, he wants to win elections. But he has a problem: he has built his popularity around the values promoted by his political party, the Justice and Development Party (AKP). However, the judiciary is under the control of the government, and corruption scandals have multiplied. As for development, it is now just a memory: average purchasing power had tripled since 2002, but between 2013 and 2022, GDP per capita dropped from $12,600 to $7,500 (according to World Bank figures). "Are you ready?" Erdogan often asks his base. Certainly not to become a poor but proud nation. Putin made that bet, he did not. To make people forget record inflation rates (the official rate of 83% per year is underestimated), he presents himself as the president of future investments. Preparing the nation for war In his speeches, international politics come after the litany of figures on social housing created, infrastructure built, and hospital beds opened. It is conveyed through catchy phrases, bravado where he invokes history through calculated provocations. On September 3, in Samsun, on the Black Sea, where Mustafa Kemal had landed on May 19, 1919, before launching the war of independence that led to victory against the Greek occupying forces, he warns his Mediterranean neighbor: "And you, Greece, look at history, return to history (...) Do not forget Izmir." Not forgetting Izmir, the Greeks know, is to recall what happened a century earlier, almost to the day: the great fire that occurred following the entry of Kemalist troops on September 9, 1922, resulted in tens of thousands of deaths. It is preparing the Turkish nation for war, without necessarily taking the risk that it will occur. It is subjecting Turkish history to a memory of resentment. As historian Edhem Eldem pointed out in his inaugural lecture at the Collège de France in 2017, "Turkey is cliopathic [sick of history], through its myths and inventions, but above all through its fears, complexes, silences, taboos, denials, and denialism." Rhetoric of revenge: "We have been colonized in our own country," Erdogan had said almost ten years ago, before interpreting the clauses of the Treaty of Lausanne (July 24, 1923) in a revisionist manner. History will mark a turning point if he believes that this will help him confront the wall of economic realities and extend his policy of denial (the disastrous effect of low interest rates), he will multiply declarations against the West ("them") to his electorate ("us"). Between the coming weeks and the elections in June, possibly moved up to May 2023, he will provoke the former powers, the "West" that he challenges by speaking to it in the second person singular and accusing it of always wanting to weaken Turkey. He will use the international stage for electoral purposes. He will follow a timeline that will lead him from the abolition of the sultanate (November 1, 1922) to the celebration of the centenary of the Republic (October 29, 1923). It is then that history will definitively mark a turning point for him. Kemalist historiography has made Atatürk the "unique man" (tek adam) of 20th-century republican Turkey. Erdogan wants to be the one for 21st-century Turkish political Islam. He is preparing to write a new national narrative. It is up to us to read it. It is up to us to understand it. Neither laugh nor cry, but understand, Spinoza taught.

title: In Ukraine, Russian bombings are severely impacting energy infrastructure.
lead: According to Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky, 30% of the country's power plants have been destroyed by Russia. More than 1,100 communities were without electricity on Tuesday.
text_eng: Winter is approaching and Ukraine is seeing its energy capabilities greatly reduced. Approximately one third of the country's power plants, which are top targets of Russian bombings, have been destroyed in a week, according to Kyiv. "Russian armed forces continued to strike with high-precision, long-range aerial and naval weapons the military command and energy systems of Ukraine," the Russian defense ministry acknowledged in its daily report on Tuesday, October 18, ensuring that "all targets have been hit." Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky confirmed that "30% of Ukrainian power plants have been destroyed" and lamented "massive power outages across the country." On Tuesday, over 1,100 localities were still without electricity. Up to 4,000 communities have been affected by power cuts in the past ten days. Power outages, water shortages, and heating issues According to the Ukrainian Minister of Territorial Development, Oleksi Tchernychov, 45 energy facilities are among the 400 damaged infrastructures since October 10. Additionally, 180 civilian buildings have reportedly been hit. "Such terrorist actions by the aggressor mobilize us and make us even stronger," emphasized the senior official. "The situation is critical now throughout the country, as our regions are interdependent," stated the deputy head of the presidential cabinet, Kirill Timoshenko, who called for "the entire country to prepare for the possibility of power outages, water shortages, and heating issues." The new attacks on Tuesday resulted in at least one death in Mykolaiv (South) and two in Kyiv, according to authorities. Power outages were again reported in the capital and other regions. The previous day, Russian strikes, carried out with the help of kamikaze drones among others, resulted in at least nine deaths, including five in Kyiv, and caused power cuts in three regions. Concerns surrounding the Zaporizhia nuclear power plant In the energy sector, the Ukrainian operator Energoatom accused the Russian army on Tuesday of having "abducted" two executives from the Zaporizhia nuclear power plant (South), the largest in Europe, which has been occupied by Moscow's forces since March. The site has been regularly targeted by bombings and power cuts since the start of the Russian invasion on February 24, raising fears of a nuclear disaster. "Over the past twenty-four hours, the enemy launched ten missile attacks and fifty-eight aerial attacks, and conducted up to sixty multiple rocket launcher strikes," summarized the Ukrainian military headquarters on Tuesday morning. The Ukrainian military also reported the Russian army's deployment of forty-three "Iranian-made Shahed-136 drones," of which "thirty-eight were shot down by Ukrainian soldiers," and claimed "twenty-two strikes" carried out by the Ukrainian air force on Monday. "The Kremlin spokesperson, Dmitry Peskov, replied to a journalist's question about Moscow using Iranian drones in Ukraine, saying, 'We do not have such information. Russian technology is being used, with Russian names.' While Tehran continues to deny exporting weapons to 'warring parties,' the United States has threatened to sanction companies or states collaborating in Iran's drone program following Monday's attacks."

title: TotalEnergies: Patrick Pouyanné, a tough boss
lead: At 59 years old, the person who has been leading TotalEnergies since 2014 has become a divisive public figure. From the invasion of Ukraine to the energy crisis, from taxing windfall profits to the climate emergency, he has been involved in all the upheavals of the year.
text_eng: That's so typical of him. On Tuesday, October 18, after three weeks of blockades at refineries and fuel depots, Patrick Pouyanné was "tired." Tired of hearing repeated claims that he had "increased his salary by 52% in 2021." So, the CEO of TotalEnergies "threw out" a tweet. Just like that, all by himself. He justified this 2021 increase by explaining that it was meant to bring his compensation back to its normal level of around 6 million euros annually, as he had "voluntarily" reduced it in 2020, the year of Covid-19. The boss thought he was setting the record straight mathematically. Instead, he was publicly criticized. Mocked by members of the New Popular Ecological and Social Union (Nupes) in unison, labeled "out of touch" by Laurent Berger, the head of the CFDT union. Even the Minister of Economy and Finance, Bruno Le Maire, who had been equally scolding both the strikers and the TotalEnergies management during the conflict, called on business leaders to show "basic decency." "France is a strange society where I should be ashamed and apologize for what I earn" – Patrick Pouyanné, CEO of TotalEnergies Faced with this general outrage, many would dodge the issue, attributing it to a moment of confusion. Not Patrick Pouyanné. "I'm not out of touch, it's just that I can't stand dishonesty. That's precisely why I chose the business world over politics," the industrialist who was an advisor to Edouard Balladur at Matignon (1993-1995) and chief of staff to François Fillon at the Ministry of Information Technologies (1995-1997) before joining Elf Aquitaine twenty-five years ago, told Le Monde. And he added provocatively: "At least we're not talking about the 52% anymore, the debate has shifted to the absolute value of my compensation. I stand by it. France is a strange society where I should be ashamed and apologize for what I earn." "Dialogue of the deaf" At 59, the man who has been leading the world's fourth-largest oil company since 2014 has become a divisive public figure. Even before the pump incident, he had been involved in all the societal upheavals this year, from the invasion of Ukraine to the energy crisis, from taxing superprofits to climate urgency. In the days of "Les Guignols de l'info," this Polytechnique graduate built like a rugby forward would have had his puppet, like the famous "J6M" for "Jean-Marie Messier, master of the world," the former head of Vivendi. Undoubtedly, the CEO of TotalEnergies would have been parodied by the cult Canal+ show, as a giant straight out of "Tintin in the Land of Black Gold," with a keffiyeh and jerrican, renamed "6P" ("Patrick Pouyanné, oil polluter profits Putin"). Instead, he got a hashtag, comparing the "contribution to society over compensation" ratio of a business leader and a footballer. If their show of strength against the CGT has caused the government's popularity to drop, Patrick Pouyanné sympathizes, but no more. "I manage a company with a long-term perspective. It's not the one who shouts the loudest who is right," he insists, more saddened by the troubles faced by motorists. "We witnessed a real dialogue of the deaf, with, on one side, the management, which refused to negotiate under pressure, claiming that there was a scheduled meeting, and, on the other side, the CGT, which did not help foster social dialogue," laments Geoffrey Caillon, CFDT TotalEnergies coordinator. "We distributed the equivalent of 15% of our payroll." The CEO acknowledges a misjudgment. "The decision to pay one month's salary to all of our 100,000 employees worldwide was made in July," he reveals. On July 28, the multinational corporation reported a record net profit of $10.6 billion (€10.7 billion) for the first half of the year. "I had informed the unions in September that we would be generous. My HR director had suggested specifying the amount, but I preferred to wait for the negotiations that started in November," he admits. A human resources department that later decided, during the conflict, to make public the average salaries of the refinery workers. When gas stations found themselves in dire straits, Patrick Pouyanné claims to have been caught between two fires. On one side, Prime Minister Elisabeth Borne urged him to resolve the conflict, and on the other, reformist unions as well as the rest of the employers secretly encouraged him not to give in to the CGT. In fact, on Tuesday, October 18, during the small celebration for the 40th anniversary of the French Association of Private Enterprises, the lobby of major business leaders, some of his peers rebuffed him in the aisles of the Centre Pompidou in Paris, concerned that the 7% salary increase granted by TotalEnergies would become a benchmark. "We distributed the equivalent of 15% of our payroll. I don't think many French companies do that," says the energy company executive. Traveling in mid-October to San Diego, California, and then to Qatar, the CEO managed everything remotely, appearing sometimes with disheveled hair in the middle of the night. Should he have stayed in France? He dismisses it: "Teams works great. I run a global company, business must go on." "There is enough to put the executives in custody." A matter of habit: leading TotalEnergies means managing crises. Even tragedies. The sinking of the oil tanker Erika off the coast of Brittany in 1999, followed two years later by the deadly explosion of the AZF factory near Toulouse, tarnished the company's reputation in France. To the point that Christophe de Margerie, the previous CEO, had toyed with the idea of offering "Total doormats" on which the French could vent their frustrations at will. Perhaps it should be considered again? Not a day goes by without the multinational being targeted, dragged to court over its oil or gas activities in Myanmar (the group eventually left the country), in Uganda, Mozambique, or South Africa. "TotalEnergies embodies the face of evil in the 21st century. Its obsession with profit endangers humanity" - Claire Nouvian, founder of the marine conservation association Bloom. Claire Nouvian, the founder of the marine conservation association Bloom, whose father was also an executive at Total, minces no words: "TotalEnergies embodies the face of evil in the 21st century. Its obsession with profit endangers humanity." Raphaël Glucksmann, Member of the European Parliament (Progressive Alliance of Socialists and Democrats in the European Parliament), adds: "Total is emblematic of this profit-driven race at the expense of human rights." On his Twitter account, MP François Ruffin (Nupes) chimes in: "There is enough to put the [group's] leaders in custody." The disgrace spreads like an oil spill, sticking to Patrick Pouyanné like oil on a seagull's wings. Even in his own stronghold, the Ecole Polytechnique. In June, during the graduation ceremony on the Saclay campus (Essonne), where X-Mines was acting as the patron of the promotion, some students turned their backs on him. Unprecedented. The insult was limited: having had a scheduling conflict, he had recorded a video. "The young generation remains in the 'just have to' mentality." And to think that the students themselves had chosen him. At X, tradition dictates that the outgoing class designates the patron for the new admits. A mentor with a generous streak, who, as a reward for this honor, donates over 100,000 euros to the Kès (the student union), to fund beer nights and ski weekends. In early 2017, the young engineers had therefore chosen TotalEnergies to pamper the X 2017 class. "That was before the ecological awakening of X," notes Benoit Halgand, one of the rebellious graduates. Following this, Jacques Biot, then president of the school, had proposed to Patrick Pouyanné, during a visit to the campus, to set up a research center on renewable energies there. "I thought I was doing good by putting our 200 to 300 researchers at Saclay rather than in the United States, as some members of my executive committee recommended. The authorities were delighted," recounts the boss, before concluding: "I was naive." In January 2022, he threw in the towel in the face of strong opposition from students against the invasion of the private sector, supported by Greenpeace and Anticor. "He did not expect this resistance at all. He did not anticipate this shift in the new generation," continues Benoit Halgand. The father of four children defends himself: "I listen to society. I have two sons with strong environmental convictions. That makes me think. The younger generation accuses us of not taking our responsibilities. But they stay in the 'just have to' mindset. It's up to us to convince them that our path is the right one." In 2022, nearly 17 billion euros distributed to shareholders. Patrick Pouyanné is certainly not an environmentalist. Yet, even his critics acknowledge that he accelerated, ahead of his competitors, the shift to move Total away from oil. And he is evolving. In 2016, he declared that "electricity is the future of energy." A shock in a company dominated by exploration-production lords. It's as if, at Haribo, they declared "the future belongs to kale." "At one point, I thought he was going to lose Total," said a board member who requested anonymity. His strategy is to use oil revenues to invest in renewable energies: 4 billion euros in 2022. "This puts us in the top five globally," the CEO assures. "We will increase it to 6 billion. I wrote to my 60 country heads to present me with multi-energy projects." "I warned my competitors: soon, we will be accused of orchestrating oil scarcity" - Patrick Pouyanné However, in 2022, he distributed nearly 17 billion euros to shareholders in the form of dividends and share buybacks. Some in the financial community see this as evidence of "cynicism": "Climate is a gimmick, dividends remain Patrick Pouyanné's priority," says an investor. "We face conflicting demands. I also get scolded by shareholders who want me to invest more in oil, with Brent near $100," retorts the CEO. And he brings the oil rig back to the village square: "Crude prices didn't rise because of the war in Ukraine; it started before. Supply is decreasing as oil companies reduce their investments, and demand is still there. I warned my competitors: soon, we will be accused of orchestrating oil scarcity." "Disruptive for politicians" "The speech is well rehearsed," sighs a renowned climatologist. "Oil companies present themselves as benefactors of humanity, of the middle class, by supporting lower fuel prices and helping poor countries in need of energy. Even if Total is not the worst major, its core business, whether we like it or not, remains exploration." Cécile Duflot, the former leader of the Greens, now head of Oxfam France, concludes: "TotalEnergies' model is not suited to a scenario that limits global warming to 2°C. It is up to the State to create the conditions that drive companies to change." Now, the revolt of the "yellow vests" or the midterm elections in the United States (on November 8) are here to remind us of an axiom that Patrick Pouyanné always keeps in mind: nothing is more political than energy. Joe Biden, the Democratic president, who had slowed down the granting of drilling permits in the United States after his election in 2020, saw his popularity plummet as gasoline prices rose. Since then, he has urged Chevron and ExxonMobil to pump, pump like Shadoks on amphetamines. It is probably because of these ambiguities that the politicians approached by Le Monde for this portrait did not respond. "Patrick Pouyanné is unsettling for politicians. He is not just any CEO. He is the boss who faces energy crises, inflation, superprofits, and climate issues all at once. It is difficult for them to speak positively about him. And they cannot speak negatively either, because TotalEnergies is also a source of French pride," says Julien Vaulpré, co-founder of the consulting firm Taddeo. "Moreover, he likes to stand his ground, which personalizes conflicts. He emphasizes his rationality, but today, that is not enough to convince." "Do you think we are not human beings?" Some elected officials even treat him like a pariah. At the request of the mayor of Lyon, Grégory Doucet (Europe Ecologie-Les Verts), the Opera and the Museum of Fine Arts in the city deprived themselves of subsidies from the TotalEnergies Foundation, one of the richest in France, in 2022. In 2019, during the municipal election campaign, Anne Hidalgo (Socialist Party), mayor of Paris, took a stand against TotalEnergies becoming a partner of the 2024 Olympic Games. "We were being courted by the authorities. We were hesitating. As soon as the controversy started, we decided to stop," explains Mr. Pouyanné, before adding in a tough-guy tone: "We're not going to spend 100 million euros just to get slapped." However, behind this tough facade, a thinly veiled bitterness emerges. When asked what it feels like to be the most hated CEO in France, for once, he avoids the question: "There is a Parisian aspect to all this. With the discounts, a month and a half ago, we were the most loved." In reality, this animosity affects him. So much so that sometimes his emotions overwhelm him. In March, he rushed to RTL, a few hours after an attack by Yannick Jadot, the Green Party candidate for president, against the oil company, a shareholder in several Russian companies. "I am an angry CEO. When Mr. Jadot accuses the 100,000 employees of TotalEnergies of being complicit in war crimes, it is very serious. Do you think we are not human beings?" he defended himself, his voice breaking, holding up a photo of two Ukrainian employees fighting on the front lines. "He had good relations with Putin," Libération saw it as a performance "worthy of the Cours Florent." Yet, Patrick Pouyanné is not an actor. Russia is a sensitive subject for him and for the group, far beyond the economic aspect. It was at an airport in Moscow that Christophe de Margerie met his death on October 20, 2014, when his Falcon-50 collided with a snowplow. For a long time, "Papou" - as some call him internally - could not mention his mentor without tears in his eyes. "I cannot break the contract in Russia, unless I am expropriated by the Russians or if Europe imposes sanctions," - Patrick Pouyanné Under the leadership of "Big Moustache," the nickname of the deceased CEO, the French group embarked on an incredibly complex project: to build a natural gas liquefaction plant in the Yamal Peninsula, in Siberia, 600 kilometers north of the Arctic Circle, where temperatures can reach -50 °C. A technological feat that made this engineering company proud. In 2017, the first LNG cargo was shipped to Europe in the holds of an ice-breaking LNG carrier adorned with beautiful white bacchantes, named Christophe de Margerie by Vladimir Putin himself. Just a month after his appointment, Patrick Pouyanné was received by the Russian president at his residence in Sochi, on the Black Sea coast. "He had good relations with Putin. He was a key player in the economic relationship with Russia. Once a year, he led the CEOs of the CAC 40 to meet him," said Sylvie Bermann, former French ambassador to Moscow. Such was the relationship that President François Hollande, acting as a relay for Pierre Ferracci, owner of Paris FC, had asked the TotalEnergies CEO to intercede with the Kremlin for a Russian investor to support the Parisian Ligue 2 club. Patrick Pouyanné complied in Saint Petersburg, at a dinner for the Mariinsky Theatre patrons organized by Vladimir Putin, quickly put in his place by the Russian president. A "parallel diplomacy" "Regarding Russia, it's not a matter of emotion. There was a violent aggression that we condemn," the CEO cuts in, "on the contrary, I seek to bring rationality to protect the company." He adds: "When my competitors say they have left Russia, it's false. The issue for me is that TotalEnergies has committed to buying liquefied natural gas for twenty-five years. I cannot break this contract, unless I am expropriated by the Russians or if Europe imposes sanctions." Like all CEOs of major oil companies, Patrick Pouyanné deals with the powers that be, placing him in a unique category within the French establishment. And beyond. In September, as the German Chancellor, Olaf Scholz, was heading to Qatar to secure Germany's LNG supply, the French CEO was signing a major contract with the emirate and posing with a smile next to the Minister of Energy, an old acquaintance from his years in Doha between 1999 and 2002. In a country like Angola, where the French oil company refines 50% of the country's hydrocarbon production, the head of the TotalEnergies subsidiary easily secures a meeting for a minister on a trip than the French ambassador. This has led to accusations of conducting a "parallel diplomacy" against the multinational: the CEO "can be received in certain countries with the respect worthy of a head of state, and the group can use this to pressure French diplomacy," denounces the NGO Friends of the Earth. "He is respected because he is a man of his word." "Another fantasy," retorts the CEO, "when I meet the Emir of Qatar, I talk to him about my business. Not about France's. However, I can be useful to French diplomacy." He recounts that, following a visit to India a few weeks after the Russian invasion of Ukraine, he alerted the French Ministry of Foreign Affairs about the North-South divergence regarding the perception of the conflict. He emphasizes that on October 11, TotalEnergies signed an agreement to exploit a gas field in Lebanese waters under a treaty negotiated under the auspices of the White House, in consultation with the Elysée, concerning the maritime borders between Lebanon and Israel. This did not prevent Emmanuel Macron from distancing himself from the group regarding the controversial gas pipeline that is supposed to connect Uganda and Tanzania. "The State does not finance this project and does not support it either," declared the president on France 2 on Wednesday, October 12. While Christophe de Margerie charmed with his friendliness, his successor cultivates a more direct style. "He is respected because he is a man of his word, which counts a lot internationally," assures Momar Nguer, former member of the TotalEnergies executive committee. Whether enjoying very spicy curry at Indian billionaire Gautam Adani's or Wiener schnitzels in Davos, his dining manners impress as much as his geopolitical analyses. "I've seen major competitors seek his advice," testifies Bertrand Deroubaix, former head of public affairs at the group. "When he gives you a dressing down, it's staggering." Patrick Pouyanné travels the world in two Bombardier business jets acquired by the group. In July, he was called out by the Twitter account @l’aviondebernard, which tracks billionaires' jets, for flying between Paris and Marseille just after calling for "moderation" among the French. When asked if he plans to switch to trains for domestic travel, his "no" is firm and final: "These two planes are tools for me and my colleagues. During the Covid crisis, we were very happy to be able to continue traveling to sign contracts, in Iraq, for example." And to specify, like a grand lord, that these jets run on nearly 40% biofuel, produced in one of TotalEnergies' refineries, of course. "The most precious thing is my time and that of my colleagues," he explains. Everything at his place is timed. Woe to anyone who hands him a double-sided printed document, because he only tolerates single-sided, which he finds more efficient to read. He consumes reports, PowerPoints, and other business reviews. In the shadow of the photocopier, near his office on the 44th floor of the tower in La Défense, near Paris, suitcases lie around. Not to store the boss's shirts, but to stuff them with files on Friday evening, to go through during the weekend. He even reads emails in his inbox from disgruntled motorists about the cleanliness of a gas station's toilets. And then, he bombards his teams with messages, no matter the hour. He gets involved in everything, decides everything, down to the seating plan for a lunch. Working next to this steamroller-like volcano requires a lot of physical and moral resistance. When Patrick Pouyanné was head of refining, his team would put candies on the meeting table to relax the poor souls facing his questioning. He has crushed many collaborators, not competent enough, not fast enough for his taste. "Where there's a will, there's a way," he simply replies, having adopted his father's maxim, who was a regional customs director. Met in a Parisian café, a former executive of the group, who left several years ago, is on the verge of tears when recalling that period of her life: "When he scolds you, it's shocking. It turns your brain into mush. Everyone looks at their feet. I wish I could have stood up and said, 'Enough.'" "He has gained confidence." "He speaks his mind, whether it's a quality or a flaw," sums up a close associate. "Patrick has improved," assures a board member, "he was thrust into the leadership of Total during a difficult period when the price of oil was collapsing. Since then, he has gained confidence. The more self-assured you are, the more you trust others." If most of his collaborators endure his outbursts of anger – the others leave – it's because they believe that Patrick Pouyanné manages the company well. He breathes TotalEnergies, he lives TotalEnergies, which he wants to leave his mark on: he changed the name in 2021, launched the construction of the tallest tower in La Défense to house the headquarters. By the end of the year, he plans for the group to be debt-free. Ready for a new cycle? In 2024, the former French Petroleum Company will celebrate its 100th anniversary, and Patrick Pouyanné will mark ten years of reign... and conflicts.

title: Gérard Bensussan: "The Russian case is a very good subject for observing denialism."
lead: In an opinion piece in "Le Monde", philosopher Gérard Bensussan analyzes the discourse of President Putin and his associates, characterized by language operations that seek to "make reality disappear and replace it with its counterfeits".
text_eng: Since February 24, 2022, to mark this now historic date, but one could go back many years before, Russian war propaganda has been using the old recipes of tyranny - denial, lies, and the substitution of the reality of the crime scene with a narratively reconstructed invention - in order to force the truth, to transfigure it through "fictioning." These language operations, sometimes crude, sometimes sophisticated, aim to make reality disappear and replace it with counterfeits. In the statements of Putin and his cronies - Lavrov, Peskov, Prigojine, and others, who would need to be extensively quoted - one can easily pick out some samples of this counterfeiting of reality: - Ukraine is governed by Nazis, in power since Maidan, who oppress and persecute, like their predecessors, the brave defenseless Russian-speaking minorities. - There is no war between Russia and Ukraine, but a special law enforcement operation, like during the colonial era of the "events" in Algeria, against bandits who threaten the security of the brave and peaceful citizens. - Russia has never attacked anyone, it is a peace-loving power harassed, threatened, and ultimately attacked by NATO, that is, by the Americans assisted by their regional vassals, the Baltic and Polish, who hate a Russia that is nonetheless benevolent - which is only defending its land and homeland against brutal and cynical aggressors. - The West is driven by an age-old and almost racist hatred of Russia; it simply wants to exterminate it, wipe it off the map. A summit of the art of counterfeiting The purpose and function of the referendums organized in the four regions of eastern Ukraine represent a summit of this art of counterfeiting: since Ukraine is not attacking Russia, but Russia is attacking Ukraine (reality), it is enough to transform a part of Ukraine into Russia (fiction). Obviously, reality does not fail to make a comeback, even in official propaganda discourse, sometimes in comical forms. Counterfeiting is sometimes pushed to the point of delirium, reaching a point where it exposes itself as such. When it is pointed out to him that Zelensky can hardly be both "Nazi" and "Jewish" at the same time, the Russian Minister of Foreign Affairs, Sergey Lavrov, explains that after all, Hitler himself was Jewish - before retracting his statement because it contradicts the actual reality, so to speak. To counter Putin's propaganda discourse, it is necessary, and sufficient, to repeat tirelessly a simple, obvious, absolutely essential fact - which Russian denial continuously seeks to erase or cover with a more or less opaque veil of forgetfulness -: it is indeed Russia that is occupying Ukraine in flagrant violation of the United Nations Charter. There is not a single Ukrainian soldier in Russia, but an occupying army waging war against Ukraine on its soil, against its people. A nihilistic staging of history Historical denial has always existed, reaching its peak with the denial of the Holocaust, the second death of the Jews who died. Inextricably linked to conspiracy theories, of which it is the most acute "critical" form, it persists before our eyes regarding the Armenian genocide, the genocide of the Tutsis, and the persecution of the Uighurs. Russia has often been a fertile ground and an effective activator of historical denial. Almost all contemporary conspiracy theories feed off of it. This is because there has never been a genuine and deep reflective and memorial return in Russia regarding the crimes of Stalinism, the Gulag, the Holodomor, which the NGO Memorial tried to initiate but was banned by Putin, as Germany did in an exemplary manner for its own past. The Russian case, especially today in times of war, is a very good subject for observing historical denial. Lies, denial, and (self) fiction are its integrated elements. They "reinforce each other" and contribute to the production of a pseudo-reality, a facade covering and masking the desolation of the truth, akin to Potemkin villages, a theatricalization or a nihilistic staging of history. Reconstructing events and reshaping reality The totality of a mobilization is only "partial," military defeat is a victory, and the corpses are those of actors. This historical denial also involves a projection of guilt from perpetrators to victims. It reconstructs events, reshapes reality – something Russia shamelessly practices around everything that has happened since at least 2014. Retrospective historical denial projects its own vices (Ukraine portrayed as an aggressive, invading, occupying power) onto the enemy, previously transformed into virtues (the eternal victim Russia, a good mother, victim of Western plots). This is what is happening with the Nord Stream 1 and 2 sabotage case: while it is likely a deliberate action by Russia, it immediately attributes criminal responsibility to the Americans. An old trick, but one that, in the eyes of Putin and his clan, can always be useful – akin to Brecht's play "The Resistible Rise of Arturo Ui" (1941), clinically described by Freud as the "logic of the cauldron": "What are you talking about! You never lent me a cauldron! Besides, it was leaky!"

title: The war in Ukraine complicates the quest for "European sovereignty."
lead: The Central, Baltic, and Nordic European countries accuse Germany and France of underestimating the Russian threat and are turning to the United States to ensure their security.
text_eng: The question is at the heart of tensions between France and Germany. Emmanuel Macron and Olaf Scholz were supposed to try, on Wednesday, October 26, in Paris, to bring their positions closer on sovereignty issues, in areas such as defense and energy, where the French and Germans are far from being in agreement. While no breakthrough is expected, the two leaders should at least try to save face, a few days after the indefinite postponement of a Franco-German ministerial council. For Europeans, it is one of the cruel paradoxes of the Russian invasion of Ukraine: the return of war to the continent validates, on paper, the quest for "European sovereignty" that Emmanuel Macron has been advocating since 2017 and that the German Chancellor shares, according to the speech he delivered in Prague on August 29. "European sovereignty essentially means that we become more autonomous in all areas, that we take on greater responsibility for our own security, that we are even more united in defending our values and interests in the world," the German Chancellor had said at the time. In reality, the conflict makes this ambition difficult to achieve, as the current urgencies test the independence and cohesion of the Twenty-Seven, starting with that of the Franco-German duo. "The geopolitical context supports Emmanuel Macron's agenda, but the pursuit of European sovereignty may not necessarily align with the French views," says Thierry Chopin, special advisor to the Jacques Delors Institute. "Germany has the capacity to react without following the French line." For him, the "change of era" advocated by Olaf Scholz from the beginning of the conflict, with the creation of a special fund of 100 billion euros to modernize the German army, demonstrates that Berlin has means that France lacks. The decision of the German coalition, at the end of September, to commit 200 billion euros to help households and businesses affected by the soaring energy prices confirmed this feeling. Hence the fear, in Paris, of a new gap, especially in military and economic terms. "Admission of weakness" The concern is all the more acute as Germany sees its position as a pivotal country in the center of Europe being reinforced, especially in the event of further enlargements to the Balkans, or even one day to Ukraine, while France fears being marginalized on the western flank of the continent. "This surge of tensions can also be an admission of weakness on the part of Paris," says Thierry Chopin. Another difficulty: the longer the war lasts, the more the balance of power evolves within the European Union (EU). Thus, the authority of the Franco-German duo is being contested like never before by the states of Central, Baltic, and Scandinavian Europe. For them, Paris and Berlin have made multiple mistakes in dealing with Moscow and are too timid in their support for Kiev. "We were right and you were mistaken about Russia," repeats the Polish Prime Minister, Mateusz Morawiecki. "Do you believe that Europe would be better off if we had followed Germany's recommendations all these years?" The countries that emerged from the Soviet bloc are gaining influence and are no longer hesitant to make their voices heard, although they are not leading forces. "The shift of Europe's center of gravity to the East was theorized by Scholz in his speech in Prague," notes Tara Varma, director in Paris of the European Council on Foreign Relations. "However, I do not believe that the center of gravity has shifted yet: the debate on Ukraine and Russia is led by the Baltic states and the countries of Central Europe, but they are not currently formulating alternative proposals to those of the Franco-German duo." "The indispensable value of the transatlantic partnership" To defend their sovereignty, this part of Europe is turning to Washington rather than Paris or Berlin. In the urgency created by Vladimir Putin's decision to go to war, the United States is making a major comeback and NATO is experiencing a revival on the European continent, at the expense of calls for a "European defense" repeated by Emmanuel Macron. The French President had hoped to take advantage of Donald Trump's presidency, who considered NATO "obsolete," to encourage Europeans to strengthen their military cooperation and defense industry. However, the decisive commitment of the Joe Biden administration in support of Ukraine has changed the situation: American aid to Kiev is crucial both militarily and financially and far surpasses the efforts made by Europeans. "For a number of countries, the American presence is so reassuring that there is no need for an alternative and no need to deepen European integration," observes Tara Varma. According to her, the "American narrative on NATO is very strong, especially in Central, Baltic, and Northern Europe." "In frontline countries, the Russian invasion is an extremely tangible threat and the response must be American," adds Tara Varma. As a sign of the increased attractiveness of the American umbrella in the face of the threat, Finland and Sweden are on the verge of joining the Atlantic Alliance. Germany is not immune to this transatlantic enthusiasm, to the point of neglecting Franco-German defense projects in favor of equipping itself with American equipment - much to the chagrin of French authorities, who hope instead that some of the military budgets freed up by Berlin will strengthen continental programs, such as the Future Combat Air System (FCAS). "The fact that a staunch supporter of the transatlantic relationship, in the person of President Biden, was elected to the White House is a blessing for all of us, as Olaf Scholz reminded in Prague. We have witnessed the indispensable value of the transatlantic partnership over the past few months." "The EU has demonstrated its adaptability." In passing, Europe has become aware of its energy dependence on Russia and is trying to break free from it in favor of other suppliers who are not hesitant to sell their resources at a high price. The energy decoupling initiated with Russia amid the "gas war" waged by Vladimir Putin could lead to new dependencies, especially on the United States. "It will be necessary to accept that European sovereignty requires a new sharing of national sovereignty," warns Thierry Chopin. "This is something very difficult on defense and energy issues, but similar to what happened at the time of the creation of the euro on the monetary level," in a context of major geopolitical upheaval following the fall of the Berlin Wall in 1989. A new leap in integration, however, appears complicated in a context of rising sovereignist forces, which erode the maneuvering room of minority governments, as seen in France and Spain. "Over the past fifteen years, the EU has demonstrated its adaptability. It has accelerated its integration process following successive crises: the global subprime crisis, waves of refugees, Brexit, and the Covid-19 pandemic. But these turbulences have also awakened strong nationalist currents within the member states," recently assessed political analyst Ivan Krastev in an interview with diplomat Michel Duclos on behalf of the Montaigne Institute. This nationalist awakening affects many EU member states, starting with countries on the front line facing the Russian threat, such as Poland, where the eurosceptic majority in power is accused of violating the very foundations of the rule of law. Following the establishment of a conservative government supported by the far-right in Sweden, the rise to power in Italy of the post-fascist Giorgia Meloni is just the latest example.

title: In Ukraine, the head of a flagship aviation company has been arrested for collaborating with Russia.
lead: Viacheslav Bogouslaev is the president and the main shareholder of the Motor Sich factory, the flagship of the Ukrainian defense industry. He is accused of continuing to supply engines to Moscow.
text_eng: Detained for nearly a week at the Loukianovski prison in Kiev, Viacheslav Bogouslaev, the president and main shareholder of the Motor Sich aviation factory – the flagship of the Ukrainian defense industry – risks spending the rest of his days behind bars. At 83 years old, the oligarch is accused by the Ukrainian Security Service (SBU) of "collaborating" with Russia. According to the SBU, he allegedly provided engines to the Russian army through intermediaries in Europe, the Middle East, and Southeast Asia. Russia uses these engines to produce and repair combat helicopters, especially models like the Mi-24, Mi-28, Mi-35, which were heavily used during the conflict in Ukraine, especially at the beginning of the war. These acquisitions allowed Moscow to maintain its fleet despite sanctions, as stated by the SBU in a statement on its website. The management of the factory, based in Zaporijia, a city located in a region in southern Ukraine partly controlled by Russian forces, "acted in collusion" with the defense conglomerate Rostec, which is on the list of Russian companies subject to Western sanctions. Tightly controlled by the Kremlin, Rostec categorically denies the accusations. "Until 2014, Ukrainian engines were installed on our helicopters, that's well known. But after that date, we equipped them with engines produced in Russia," the state-owned company stated on its website. A criminal investigation has been opened Arrested on Sunday, October 23 in Zaporijia, and then transferred under tight security to Kiev, Viacheslav Bogouslaev and Oleg Dziouba, the head of commercial operations for Motor Sich abroad, are accused of establishing "a transnational channel for the wholesale delivery of aircraft engines to the aggressor country," according to statements from the Ukrainian authorities. A criminal investigation has been opened. Placed in pre-trial detention for two months by the Chevtchenko district court in Kiev, the two suspects deny any involvement. "This is certainly a misunderstanding," Viacheslav Bogouslaev said during his hearing on Monday, October 24. Widely commented on, mostly positively, on Ukrainian social media, the arrest of the director of Motor Sich has outraged the "workers' collective" of the company, which published an appeal to Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky on the factory's website. The text describes the accusations against "a person who has dedicated his entire life to Ukraine, who lives and works next to us under daily bombardments, at the risk of his life" as "scandalous." To dispel these impressions, the SBU released on YouTube some snippets of conversations intercepted between the boss and his contacts in the Russian defense industry. During a phone call with a man presented by the SBU as Russian General Anatoli Sitnov, Mr. Boguslaev proposes to deliver certain spare parts through Croatia. A good businessman, he commits to happily supply Russia with all the engines it needs. He also expresses his certainty that President Volodymyr Zelensky will be ousted from the country: "He will leave, he will be removed, that's all." In a conversation dated from March, he tells a Russian official, whose identity is not revealed, how a Russian Iskander ballistic missile "fell on the factory's territory." "We are not bothered at all... We do not feel any animosity towards you. In fact, we understand you," he says in a honeyed voice. The "Red Baron" Viatcheslav Boguslaev seemed untouchable. With a personal fortune estimated at over 300 million dollars (300 million euros), and strong connections with the Russian elite in the military, police, and security services, he was a prominent figure in Ukrainian politics and economy. A former deputy, and once an advisor to Presidents Leonid Kuchma (1994-2005) and Viktor Yanukovych (2010-2014), he was a leading figure in the Party of Regions (pro-Russian), which he financed. In 2000, he received the Order of Heroes of Ukraine, the country's highest civilian honor, for "his outstanding contributions to the development of Ukrainian engineering." His trajectory is in line with that of all the "Red Barons," these former Soviet-era factory directors who became millionaires after the collapse of the USSR through the confiscatory privatizations of the companies they led. Trained as an engineer, Mr. Boguslaev took over Motor Sich in 1988, a time when all land properties and industrial assets belonged to the state. When the country declared its independence in September 1991, opening up to a market economy, the former Soviet nomenklatura seized control of the industrial jewels. In the southern regions of Ukraine especially (Mykolaiv, Odessa, Zaporizhia, Dnipropetrovsk), the military-industrial complex built during the Soviet era still thrives. Motor Sich, an expert in aircraft and helicopter engine manufacturing, is a coveted flagship. Mr. Boguslaev quickly becomes its main shareholder. Russia is his biggest customer. Change of priority From 1991 to 2014, most of the major Ukrainian factory directors did not hesitate to display their good relations with Moscow. Many held Russian citizenship, made frequent trips to Russia, and maintained cordial relations with the Kremlin. In 2014, Vladimir Putin annexed Crimea and Kiev decided to sever ties. Seeing their orders decline, the management of Motor Sich had to seek new partners. In 2019, talks were held with the Chinese Beijing Skyrizon Aviation, from the Xinwei group, but with no results. That year, an investigative report was published on the close collaboration between Motor Sich and the Russian Ministry of Defense, without eliciting any reaction from the Kiev government. "In 2019 alone, the factory supplied over a hundred helicopter engines to Russia. These supplies continued at the same pace, even after the Russian invasion in February," stated the author of the investigation, independent Ukrainian journalist Yevhenii Plynskyi, during an interview broadcast on October 25 on the opposition channel Current Time (Nastoiashchee Vremia). Motor Sich's CEO "could have single-handedly grounded all of Russia's aviation at the beginning of the invasion," noted Valeri Romanenko, a former military officer, quoted by the same channel. If the Security Service of Ukraine (SBU) waited so long to arrest him, it is likely due to his infiltration by Russian intelligence services, a situation that persisted after the outbreak of the war in February. The arrest of Viacheslav Boguslaev signals a change in priorities. The hunt for collaborators is intensifying.

title: Olga and Sasha's journal: "Russia means fear, death, terror for so many people in the world today."
lead: Sasha and Olga are finally reunited in Paris to celebrate Olga's birthday. It's an opportunity for Sasha to have a moment of respite away from the war in Ukraine, even though the aerial attacks on Kiev eventually come back to their minds. For the thirty-sixth week, the two sisters share their daily lives with "M Le magazine du Monde."
text_eng:
Olga and Sasha are two Ukrainian sisters. The older one is 35 years old and works as a wine merchant in Paris, where she has been living for seven years. The younger one, who is 33 years old, lives in Kiev with her parents and grandmother. Since the beginning of the conflict, the two sisters have agreed to keep a journal for "M". This week, Sasha and Olga are finally reunited in Paris to celebrate Olga's birthday. It's a chance for Sasha to have a moment of respite away from the war, although the airstrikes in Kiev still weigh on their minds. 

Tuesday, October 25th
Olga: I wake up feeling joyful. Sasha has arrived safely in Warsaw, what a relief! We were in constant communication while she was on the train between Kyiv and Warsaw. I couldn't relax until she crossed the border. She is taking her flight to Paris at 4 pm. I return to the apartment, prepare the last few things, make dinner, and head out. I meet Yanis [her partner] at Charles de Gaulle. We are early, waiting by the gates. Such suspense! The plane is thirty minutes late. There she is, coming out with a smile. Finally, she is by my side! 

Sasha: Last night, we were stuck at the border for five hours. The Polish side takes the crossing very seriously, border guards thoroughly check luggage and papers. I arrive in Warsaw at 10 am. I feel something that no longer exists in Ukraine: relief. The terror leaves me, it no longer consumes me constantly. I am tired and calm at the same time. I have several hours of waiting at the airport, and I am delighted. I sit down to read the news from my country. It's strange not being there anymore. I already miss Kyiv. I decide to delete several Ukrainian media apps from my phone to take a real break. I keep only one: Monitor - a chat that compiles open source data on attacks. I even put on silent the alarm that notifies me of air raid sirens in Kyiv. I arrive in Paris at 7 pm, Olga and Yanis are waiting for me. I am with family, in my second home. I feel at peace but also a little sad to have left behind my beloved country. 

Wednesday, October 26th
Olga: We wake up, I go prepare breakfast, I can't even find the words to express the joy of cooking for my sister! I know that wine has become very expensive in Ukraine, so I have selected some good bottles, we will drink them later. Tonight, Sasha is supposed to accompany me to choir practice. We sing at the Sorbonne, the halls are beautiful, and I am excited for my sister to see them. But I am starting to have a bad sinus infection, and we have to cancel our walk. I stay in bed. In the end, I can't go to choir practice. What a shame, it's unfair! I still have the right to be in good health when my sister flees the war to come see me for two weeks! 

Sasha: Today, Olga is not feeling well. I think it's due to the accumulation of stress and fatigue. I am going to the center of Paris to meet up with my friend S., who has been in France since April. On February 27, she crossed the border between Ukraine and Moldova on foot. We grab a coffee, then head to her place for an aperitif. S. rents a small apartment near the Ecole-Militaire metro station. From her window, I can see the Parisian rooftops and the dome of Les Invalides. When the sun sets, it's incredibly beautiful. S. tells me all about her adventures in Paris. What surprises me is that she doesn't want to know anything about my life in Ukraine. Maybe she has built a barrier in her mind. But I know she misses Kyiv a lot. The evening comes, and I rush home to meet Olga. The three of us have dinner with Yanis. We chat into the night, share memes and funny videos, and can't stop laughing. I feel very happy.

Thursday, October 27, Olga: Last night, Sasha woke up suddenly, she was scared. She didn't understand that she was at my place, safe. She also startles every time a plane or helicopter flies over the building. Just like mom did when she visited in July. I hope so much that they will recover quickly after the war! I can't help but wonder if the "rachists" [a contraction of "Russians" and "fascists"] will stop sending missiles when they evacuate our territories. And the drones? Will they leave people alone? Will a normal, peaceful life – the one I knew back there – resume? Or will the constant threat of bombings and attacks continue? No one has the answer. The only thing I tell myself is that when Putin [Olga and Sasha have chosen not to capitalize "Putin," "Russian," and "Russia"] disappears, the war will end. Despite my sinusitis, I am going to work. Tonight, we finally saw Elisa [Mignot, a journalist, keeps this journal with M]! It's the first time she and Sasha have met after communicating every day for eight months. This moment was very moving! The three of us talked a lot tonight, about everything and nothing, about life and war. We talked about Ukraine, but also about other wars. I remembered my friend Jihâd, a Franco-Syrian I met in 2011 in Nantes during a training for FLE [French as a Foreign Language] teachers. When the war in Ukraine started, Jihâd wrote to express his support. He told me that in Syria, his family had also suffered from "Moscow's savage politics." He also spoke about people who, "even though they have the means to be well-informed, repeat Russian propaganda." He warned me that I would encounter it. I started following the war in Syria in 2015 when our "dear neighbor" attacked that country by supporting Bashar Al-Assad. I remember the images of Aleppo... Today, in my mind, they are getting closer to those of Mariupol. Two cities destroyed by the bombs of the rachists. Russia signifies fear, death, terror for so many peoples in the world today. Sasha: This morning, I teach my classes online, I am inspired and in good shape. The morning passes quickly, the classes follow one after the other. Then, I go to the Fnac to discover the new releases, the books, and especially to choose my 2023 calendar. I love this tradition! I want one with seasonal fruits and vegetables to put in my kitchen. I take a walk and meet Olga at her cellar. Life seems very calm, simple, and I feel happy by my sister's side. One thing interrupts this feeling of happiness: the sound of airplanes in the sky - even though, of course, it's civilian aviation - and the noise of the city. I don't know why, in Kyiv, we unconsciously avoid making too much noise because it hurts everyone. Tonight, we're having dinner with Elisa. Olga met her after Maidan, in 2014, when they worked together to collect testimonies about the revolution. We were waiting for her at the shop when she arrived. We stayed together very late and missed the last metro. We took the last bus. Olga was tired, but I was happy to be in the city at a late hour. It hadn't happened to me since February 24. The city is alive, illuminated. There is no sense of the end of the world, like in Kyiv when the curfew starts at 11 p.m. Friday, October 28. Olga: This morning, I went to a hairdresser. I wasn't sure what I wanted to do and at the last moment, I told her, "Cut it short." They say that hair absorbs both positive and negative energies. I wanted to get rid of all the weight I've been carrying these past months of war. Sasha said it suited me very well. In the afternoon, I listened to an interview with Christo Grozev, director of the online investigative media Bellingcat and responsible for investigations on Russia. The site has just published the results of an investigation on Russian military engineers tasked with choosing targets and calculating the trajectory of missiles that strike Ukraine daily. Bellingcat has identified about thirty individuals, whose portraits have been circulated. I look at the photos of these people. They are young women and men who are about our age, Sasha and me. Their job is to provide the data that allows the rachist army to target civilians, to take the lives of people like them. But who are they? A catastrophe for humanity. Sasha: This morning, I linger in bed while Olga goes to the hairdresser. I don't watch the news, I just want to know if mom, dad, grandma, and Dmytro [her partner] are okay. It's strange to feel how much I wish to dedicate myself here to rest and this "war break." Undoubtedly because I know that, no matter what happens, I will return to this reality of missiles, kamikaze drones, curfews, power outages, and sirens. So what's the point of thinking about it constantly? Yanis is preparing a surprise party for Olga's 35th birthday. This afternoon, we are going shopping. In the evening, we are going to have dinner at Yanis's friends' place. Olga will join us, and I have my first raclette. The atmosphere is very warm, I listen to them, the conversation topics are simple and daily, talking about movies, sports, news. I don't participate much, it seems like I have forgotten how to talk about simple things. Saturday, October 29th Olga: Today is my birthday! I wake up in a good mood, knowing that I will spend the day with my sister and my love. I don't need anything else, I am fulfilled! Sasha has been coming to Paris for my birthday for five years now. We usually start the day with a nice breakfast and a glass of champagne. But today, we motivate each other: we are going to do pilates in the nearby park. We need to burn the calories after last night's raclette! One might think we have forgotten about the war. We try to distance ourselves, but messages about air strikes bring us back to reality. Our parents and loved ones are there. We do our best to get news from them. We plan to go to a restaurant tonight for my birthday, Yanis will pick us up after his work meeting. We first have to pick up some friends. We ring the doorbell at their place, the door opens and there... "Surprise!" Yanis has organized a surprise birthday party. I am so touched, no one has ever done this for me. All my friends are here. My Sachounya also knew, but she kept the secret. I am happy! Sasha: It's Olga's birthday, my favorite celebration. In the morning, we decide to take our time. Pretending to have work stuff, Yanis left to prepare the party. Olga was disappointed, but my mission was to organize the best day for her. After the pilates class I gave her in the park - the weather in Paris is so summery! - we bought some pastries and croissants and organized a birthday brunch just for the two of us. We toasted with crémant, listened to music, sang. What a joy to have a sister and celebrate her life! The party lasted until 3 in the morning. I hope Olga has gathered many positive emotions. I am surprised to discover that her friends read the journal we keep, and I realize they already know quite a lot. Understanding that many people we don't know know about us is a funny experience. Elisa told us that after last week's article, readers had asked Le Monde if I had arrived safely in France. It brought tears to my eyes. Sunday, October 30th Olga: Today, rest. I realize that since Sasha arrived, I haven't been following the news much, I'm enjoying every moment. I don't want to know what Putin is saying in his latest speech, I don't want to know the details of the fighting in the east... Not during these two weeks. Sasha: Sleeping in with aspirin. We're not leaving the apartment. I still check the news of the week, afraid of missing something or not knowing what's happening in Kyiv, on the front lines, or in the minds of world leaders. I've heard from Dmytro, Mom, and Olga and I called our grandmother. I feel like I'm with family, safe. Without the cancer that is the war in Ukraine, which follows me everywhere and hurts me. Yet, that's not enough to make me not want to go back home. Monday, October 31st. Olga: I wake up to a beep on my phone. There's a new attack on Kyiv. I immediately text Mom, babushka [her grandmother], and Dad. Everyone is safe. The attackers have targeted strategic buildings again. There's no water or electricity in several neighborhoods of Kyiv. Yesterday, grandma was saying how "wonderful" it was to have electricity again! One thing reassures me: Sasha is next to me, safe. Sasha: I wake up with Olga because her phone keeps beeping non-stop. There's an attack on Ukraine. I learn from my neighbors' chat that water is cut off almost everywhere. Mom still has some, she's filling her bathtub. Dmytro, on the other hand, has none left. My friends hear explosions in Kyiv, there's a lot of smoke on the left bank. I was supposed to go to the gym this morning, but I'm glued to the chats with my friends and family.

title: Could the energy crisis lead to a blackout in France this winter?
lead: Nuclear power plants undergoing maintenance, threatened gas imports, dams facing water shortages... Is our electrical system ready for the winter of 2022?
text_eng: The energy crisis is hitting France hard. Between the gas restrictions imposed by Vladimir Putin, the nuclear power plants shut down, and the dams lacking water after summer droughts, the French electricity production park is under pressure... to the point that the risk of shortage, even blackouts, is dominating public debate. To prevent the worst, the government is proposing an austerity plan to get through the winter. But is the risk real? Will power cuts be necessary? Sources: Outlook for the electricity system for autumn and winter 2022-2023 (RTE report)Unavailability of the nuclear power plant (RTE service)Hydraulic stock (RTE service)Annual national wind and solar production park from 2001 to 2021 (energy network open data)Real-time national consumption data (Eco2mix)Annual national production park by sector (energy network open data)RTE electricity balance sheet for 2021 and 2020.

title: Bridge collapse in India: at least 137 dead, nine people arrested for involuntary manslaughter
lead: Five hundred people were participating in the Diwali festival, the festival of lights, on the Morbi bridge before the disaster, according to authorities.
text_eng: In India, the death toll has risen after the collapse of a suspension bridge in the Indian state of Gujarat. At least 137 people died on the evening of Sunday, October 30, as announced on Monday by the local police chief, P. Dekavadiya – a previous toll had reported 130 deaths. Nine people were also arrested on Monday in connection with the collapse of the bridge located in the city of Morbi, about 200 kilometers west of Ahmedabad, the main city in the state of Gujarat. These nine individuals are all associated with a company that was carrying out renovation work on the bridge. They are now under investigation for involuntary manslaughter, said police officer Ashok Kumar Yadav in a statement, and a complaint has been filed against them. Authorities estimated that around 500 people, including children, were celebrating the Diwali festival on the bridge, which dates back to the colonial era, when the supporting cables gave way shortly after nightfall. According to the local police chief, around fifty children are among the victims, with the youngest being a 2-year-old boy. A local politician, Kalyanji Kundariya, told the press that he lost twelve family members in the disaster, including five children. Morbi's police chief, P. Dekavadiya, informed Agence France-Presse that over 130 people had been rescued. At least fifteen injured individuals are hospitalized. Rescue operations are still ongoing. The suspension bridge, 233 meters long, was built in 1880 over the Machchhu River with materials imported from England, according to local media. Sandeepsinh Jhala, the head of the Morbi municipality, noted that no compliance certificate had been issued before the bridge was reopened to the public last Wednesday. The Gujarat tourism department describes the "grand suspension bridge" as an "artistic and technological marvel." Surveillance footage shows the structure swaying before collapsing, sending dozens of people into the river. A rescue operation involving divers, boats, and dozens of soldiers was still underway on Monday. Prime Minister Narendra Modi, who was in Gujarat, his home state, expressed on Twitter being "deeply saddened by this tragedy" and announced compensations for the victims. Modi, expected to visit the site on Tuesday, also stated that he had "rarely felt so much pain in his life." Russian President Vladimir Putin offered his condolences to the families of the deceased on Monday, according to a statement from the Kremlin. Japanese Prime Minister Fumio Kishida expressed being "deeply saddened." Nepal, Bhutan, and the United Kingdom also extended their condolences.

title: Poutine, crime, and power in the "Herald Review"
lead: The quarterly magazine delves into the unspoken aspects of the war in Ukraine, particularly viewing the conflict as a tool used by the Putin clan to maintain their privileges and pass them on to their "heirs" in a mafia-like sense.
text_eng: Review of reviews. What if there was not a huge misunderstanding about the reasons for the invasion of Ukraine by Russia on February 24th? Is the nature of this war truly ideological? Did the defense of the unity of the Russian world and the hatred of Western values ​​push Vladimir Putin, a former KGB officer stationed in East Germany at the time of the fall of the Berlin Wall in 1989, to methodically restore the fading glory of an empire, risking everything? Former correspondent for Le Monde in Moscow from 1998 to 2001 and co-founder of the news site Mediapart, which co-publishes the Revue du crieur, François Bonnet provides a completely different interpretation here. The seizure of power by Putin in 1999, following an aging Yeltsin (1931-2007), the first president of the Russian Federation, is likened to a palace revolution within the Sicilian Cosa Nostra or the Neapolitan Camorra, explains François Bonnet. Putin eliminated Prosecutor Skuratov, who wanted to bring down Yeltsin, ensured his protection, and then seized power with around thirty friends, most of whom are still by his side in 2022. "For a quarter of a century, European leaders have always refused to integrate, in their relations with Russia, the distinctly criminal and mafia dimension of the Putin regime," he writes. Drawing on, among other sources, the book by Roberto Scarpinato, one of the most famous anti-mafia prosecutors in Sicily, "The Return of the Prince: Power and Criminality" (La Contre Allée, 2012), the author decrypts that what is at stake in Russia today is much more the passing of the torch and, as in the "family," the transmission to the second generation of acquired powers, with complete impunity. Acts of war To complement the file on the unspoken aspects of the Ukrainian conflict, historian Jean-Arnault Dérens and geographer Laurent Geslin, both journalists as well, detail the ramifications of the Orthodox Churches from the 17th century to the present day, as well as the disputes between the patriarchates of Moscow, Kiev, Constantinople, and all the Churches of Eastern Europe.
