It is as complicated to follow as the succession war of Schleswig-Holstein, which led, in the 19th century, to the reunification of Germany by Bismarck, but very enlightening. Also noteworthy is a report by Canadian photographer Adrienne Surprenant on the acts of war in Ukraine. In addition to this international and geopolitical detour of the cultural review, it dedicates an article, written by human rights activist Erika Campelo, to the struggles of Brazilian feminists against Bolsonaro, as the second round of the Brazilian election will take place on October 30th. On her part, the journalist and activist Sihame Assbague, who, according to Wikipedia, identifies with "political anti-racism," is investigating the neutrality of entries on the collaborative encyclopedia.

title: Targeted by pro-Russian rumors, the French ambassador to Kiev denies having resigned.
lead: Messages widely shared on social media, including by a member of the National Rally, claimed that Etienne de Poncins had left his position in Ukraine.
text_eng: "Resignation of the French Ambassador in Ukraine", "the French Ambassador in Ukraine has left Kiev and said that Zelensky has also left!"... While new Russian strikes hit the Ukrainian capital on Tuesday, October 18, many pro-Russian accounts were circulating a rumor about the resignation of the French Ambassador in Ukraine, Etienne de Poncins. The French Ministry of Foreign Affairs assures Le Monde that "there has never been any question of a resignation." "The ambassador is at work, in Kiev," adds the Ministry of Foreign Affairs, denouncing "an attempt at manipulation." Shared several thousand times on social media, the false information had been denied by the ambassador himself. "This is 'fake news,' please remove it immediately," he posted on Twitter Wednesday morning. He was responding to a tweet, since deleted, from the departmental delegate of the National Rally in Mayenne, Jean-Michel Cadenas. Unsuccessful candidate in the last legislative elections in the 2nd district of Mayenne (16% in the first round), he had relayed the rumor that Etienne de Poncins had resigned. Without any source, he quoted what was presented as a statement from the ambassador ("We no longer know what we are defending here, even Volodymyr Zelensky is no longer here in Kiev, why do you continue [sic] to sacrifice the Ukrainian people"), while adding his comment: "We wonder too!" At the origin of this fake quote, a completely fabricated piece of information, sometimes accompanied by a reference to an interview with the French Ambassador in Kiev in which he recounted, on October 11 on BFM-TV, having "spent the afternoon in the basement" during the Russian bombings on Kiev the day before. The implication: shocked and demoralized, Mr. de Poncins would have since given up his position. In passing, the alleged verbatim repeats a popular refrain among pro-Kremlin circles: the idea that Volodymyr Zelensky had fled Kiev and was filming himself against a green screen, supposedly from Poland. The rumor was spread, among others, on Monday night by Agora Actualités, a Facebook page located in Algeria that spreads many pro-Kremlin rumors, before being picked up by many other similar pro-Russian African news pages, such as International Panafricaniste. However, its dissemination by a Facebook page presenting itself as RT Afrique's has popularized it. This last message, still without a shadow of a source, was picked up in France by the conspiracy theorist influencer Silvano Trotta, for example. "Nothing in the media... except the Russian media. Would they lie when it will be easily verifiable in the following days?" wondered the pro-Kremlin antivax ufologist on Tuesday night, on the verge of realizing that, once again, he was spreading false information. Update, Wednesday, October 19 at 4:37 p.m.: addition of the reaction from the Quai d'Orsay

title: In the port of Hong Kong, the embarrassing presence of a Russian megayacht
lead: The "North", owned by a Russian oligarch under sanctions, has dropped anchor in the bay of the special administrative region, likely at the invitation of Beijing.
text_eng: It has been over a week since the sleek silhouette of the Nord, the megayacht of one of the wealthiest Russian oligarchs, Alexei Mordashov, appeared in the bay of Hong Kong. The 142-meter-long vessel anchored in full view of the gleaming towers of the International Finance Center (IFC) and the ferry routes connecting Central to the neighboring islands. A large Russian flag flies on its rear deck. Despite protests from the United States and Europe, the presence of this symbol of the Putin regime was explicitly embraced by the new head of the special administrative region. Former police officer John Lee stated that Hong Kong did not impose sanctions "without legal basis." Nevertheless, as Hong Kong seeks to reassure the Western world of its financial reliability, and with an upcoming international summit where Hong Kong hopes to attract top figures from Wall Street and the City, "the presence of the Nord certainly gives a bad impression," according to an unnamed Western banker. In the maritime industry, professionals were surprised by the Nord's choice of anchorage. "Why place it right in the middle of Hong Kong Bay if not to ensure everyone sees it?" they wondered. It would have seemed more normal and discreet for the Nord to anchor on the Gold Coast, the western coast of the New Territories of Hong Kong, specially equipped to accommodate superyachts. A heavyweight in the Russian economy, the price of the Nord, launched from the German Lurssen shipyards in 2021, was estimated at over 500 million euros (including a pool, cinema, and two helipads) with a maintenance cost of around 100,000 euros per day. However, the issue with the Nord in Hong Kong has nothing to do with its features; simply put, its owner, Alexei Mordashov, 57 years old, is one of the oligarchs targeted by the sanctions imposed by the United States and Europe following the outbreak of the war in Ukraine. "I have absolutely nothing to do with the current geopolitical tension and I do not understand why the European Union has imposed sanctions on me (...) nor how these sanctions against me will contribute to resolving the dreadful conflict in Ukraine," he reacted at the time. While the man was already a billionaire before Vladimir Putin became president, he remains a symbol and a heavyweight in the Russian economy. His fortune, amassed in mining and steel, was $29 billion before the sanctions. He is also one of the main shareholders of Rossiya Bank, closely linked to financing the Putin system. Mr. Mordashov's other vessel, the Lady-M, was seized in March in Italy. According to Marine Tracker, a website specialized in real-time tracking of ships across the oceans, the Nord left the Seychelles at the beginning of March, at the start of the operations to seize the assets of key figures of the Russian regime. It arrived in Vladivostok, Russia, in April. Its appearance in Hong Kong on October 5 immediately caused a stir, not only among Western diplomats. The war in Ukraine is a sensitive topic in Hong Kong. The riots of 2019 were partly inspired by the Euromaidan movement of 2013. Some heroes of this revolution, like the military man Volodymyr Parasyuk, became celebrities among the youth of Hong Kong at that time. Three years later, unable to express hostility towards the Nord, a portion of the Hong Kong population is discussing its presence with a mix of sarcasm and fatalism on encrypted social media. "It's reassuring to know that we can always count on Russian money when American banks have left Hong Kong," posted an internet user on the LIHKG forum (pronounced Lindeng). Unwelcome guest "Hong Kong's reputation as a financial center depends on respect for international laws and standards," quickly responded the U.S. consulate in Hong Kong. "The possible use of Hong Kong as a refuge (...) questions the transparency of the business environment." "The European Union is aware of the efforts of certain Russian individuals and companies to circumvent European sanctions," said the European representation in Hong Kong, adding that "since October 6, individuals or companies that assist targeted individuals in evading sanctions could themselves be sanctioned." In other words, by not imposing Western sanctions, Hong Kong could be exposed to secondary sanctions, as well as individuals and companies that facilitated the arrival and stay of the yacht in Hong Kong by handling its customs formalities, organizing the boat's refueling, ensuring its security, etc. The local government emphasized that it always scrupulously implements sanctions decided by the United Nations but not those "unilaterally adopted by independent jurisdictions." On Tuesday, the Chief Executive, John Lee, once again dismissed Western protests, stating, "As an international financial center, our regulatory system is in line with the rest of the world. But if a certain place takes measures and those measures have no legal basis, we have no reason to do the same." John Lee took the opportunity to describe the imposition of sanctions on individuals as "barbaric," reminding that he too was "sanctioned" by the United States, along with about a dozen Hong Kong leaders. Behind these boastful statements, however, there is no doubt that Hong Kong would have been better off without this cumbersome guest. "International finance is still dominated by the United States and the dollar. Hong Kong cannot go too far in its confrontation with Washington, London, or Europe," observes a Western banker. The territory is trying to restore its reputation. The current priority for Hong Kong is therefore to make people forget the last three anni horribiles: the 2019 riots that drove away Chinese tourists, followed by the harsh and erratic handling of the Covid-19 pandemic, alongside Beijing's brutal reassertion of political and social control... All of this has destroyed Hong Kong's reputation as a free city and driven away hundreds of thousands of well-educated Hong Kongers, as well as many expatriates who gave Hong Kong its valuable label as an international city. In addition to the International Finance Summit, which some of the biggest names in the industry have already promised to attend in November, the Hong Kong Sevens rugby tournament, Hong Kong's equivalent of the Venice Carnival, canceled three times, is also set to take place in November. As the territory tries to restore its reputation, Hong Kong has no interest in becoming a haven for blacklisted oligarchs. However, the risk exists, says Bart Jan Kimman, a maritime broker and superyacht agent in Hong Kong: "Other superyachts owned by other sanctioned oligarchs are currently waiting in Vladivostok... As the ice approaches, they are wondering where to go, and Hong Kong is obviously a destination of choice if they feel protected there..."

title: War in Ukraine: Faced with Russian missiles and drones, what anti-aircraft equipment is Kiev demanding?
lead: Fearing an escalation of strikes on civilians and infrastructure in the country, Kiev is urging its allies to strengthen its defense capabilities against aerial attacks.
text_eng: The calls from the Ukrainian government have been heard. French President Emmanuel Macron committed on Wednesday, October 12, to deliver "radars, systems, and [anti-aircraft] missiles to protect [the Ukrainians] from attacks, especially to protect them from drone attacks." The French head of state made this announcement after a series of massive Russian strikes a few days earlier, which targeted several Ukrainian cities, including the capital, Kiev. Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky even called for the creation of an anti-aircraft shield over Ukraine, prioritizing the delivery of high-tech anti-aircraft defense systems to protect the population and strategic infrastructure in case of increased Russian strikes. But what anti-aircraft equipment is being referred to? What systems does Ukraine have? Since the beginning of the conflict, several allied countries have pledged to strengthen Ukrainian anti-aircraft defense by providing self-propelled anti-aircraft guns (Gepard) or individual short-range missiles (Stinger). The latter, quickly deployable by ground troops to target helicopters or low-flying combat aircraft, have proven very effective against Russian aviation. However, air defense is "multilayered by nature, it is the multiplicity of capabilities that allows for effectiveness," explains Thibault Fouillet, a researcher at the Foundation for Strategic Research (FRS). A single system, even if highly technological and only adapted for missile interception, will not be able to combat drones. In addition, Ukraine has short and medium-range anti-aircraft systems dating back to the Soviet era. These mainly include the S-300, put into service in the 1980s, and the Buk surface-to-air defense system, also of Soviet manufacture. "Even though these means may seem limited, interception is effective since President Zelensky mentioned a few days ago the destruction of 43 out of 84 missiles and 13 out of 24 drones," says the military expert. What equipment has been promised? Nasams (provided by the United States) However, the Ukrainian government believes its equipment is not sufficient and is eyeing the advanced systems held by some Western countries. Starting with the Nasams systems. "These systems are extremely effective, modern, and designed for intercepting the most technologically advanced threats in high-intensity situations," comments Thibault Fouillet. Therefore, they are perfectly suited for missile defense and anti-drone operations. This surface-to-air missile system, manufactured by the American company Raytheon and the Norwegian company Kongsberg, can intercept any flying object within a radius of about 25 kilometers. It would allow Ukrainians to deter Russian aviation from venturing into their airspace and, thus, protect ground troops from aerial attacks during an offensive. However, no Nasams has yet arrived on Ukrainian soil. The United States confirmed last summer the sending of eight systems of this type, two of which are expected to be delivered in the coming weeks or months. The other six batteries are expected to follow later. On the other hand, the United Kingdom announced the upcoming delivery of Amraam missiles, compatible with Nasams. "These weapons will help Ukraine defend its sky from attacks and strengthen its defense alongside American Nasams," said British Defense Minister Ben Wallace. An IRIS-T (provided by Germany) "A new era of air defense has begun in Ukraine": in a tweet published on October 11, Ukrainian Defense Minister Oleksii Reznikov celebrated the delivery by Germany of the first model of the IRIS-T air defense system, manufactured by the German company Diehl. With a range of 40 kilometers, this modern system, not yet in service within the German army, can destroy cruise missiles and aircraft up to 20 kilometers in altitude. "It will allow Ukraine to protect an entire large city from Russian air strikes," German Chancellor Olaf Scholz had argued in June. Germany has promised to deliver four systems of this type, with a second one expected to be shipped to Ukraine before the end of the year. A Crotale NG (provided by France) Emmanuel Macron confirmed the sending of French anti-aircraft systems, without specifying the type of batteries that will be delivered. However, according to the Ministry of Armed Forces, France is preparing to deliver Crotale NG batteries, a short-range anti-aircraft system (15 kilometers) dating back to the 1990s and capable of shooting down aircraft at low and very low altitudes. In total, the French army has twelve of them. "They are equipped with detection, identification, and sector surveillance radars, and have a capacity of eight missiles per launcher," details the Ministry of Armed Forces. The Ukrainian government was requesting a much more modern French anti-aircraft system: the SAMP/T Mamba. This medium-range surface-to-air system, capable of destroying targets beyond 100 kilometers, is manufactured by the Franco-Italian consortium Eurosam. France currently has eight units, equipped with eight supersonic missiles per launcher. According to the Ministry of Armed Forces, these batteries are capable of providing "a protective bubble for a moving ground force and forces deployed in a theater of operations." MARIE-LAN NGUYEN / WIKICOMMONS Hawk (provided by Spain) Spain has also promised to send four American medium-range air defense systems Hawk. This decision was welcomed on Thursday, October 13, by NATO Secretary General Jens Stoltenberg, who considers the defense of Ukrainian airspace a "priority." The Alliance has encouraged its allies to provide additional air defense capabilities to Kyiv, even if it means pushing for the delivery of older surface-to-air systems to Ukraine while waiting to produce and deliver the more modern systems promised to the country's authorities. The Hawk systems were deployed in the U.S. military in the early 1960s and are no longer used by the United States, which now prefers more sophisticated Patriot systems. Washington is considering providing Ukraine with these long-range batteries, but this complex and extremely costly system requires a lot of maintenance and training. According to Agence France-Presse, Spain has modernized some Hawk systems in recent years to extend their lifespan. Romanian army soldiers deploy a Hawk PIP III R surface-to-air missile launcher during a joint military exercise with the U.S. military in 2016. INQUAM PHOTOS / REUTERS All of these systems are essential "because they enhance the Ukrainian multi-layered capability by offering new effects, particularly in the deficient areas of medium and long-range and anti-missile defense," summarizes Thibault Fouillet.

title: Ukraine's electrical grid, a new "battlefield" for Russia
lead: With the bombings targeting energy infrastructure, "about 40% of thermal electricity production is now out of service," the Ukrainian energy minister told "Le Monde."
text_eng: Faced with setbacks on the military front, the Russian army has found a new target to try to weaken Ukraine: its electrical grid. Since October 10, these infrastructures have been pounded by missile and drone strikes across the country. As winter approaches, more than 30% of the country's power plants have been destroyed in a week, according to Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky. "The Russian leadership has ordered the energy network to be turned into a battlefield. The consequences are very dangerous, once again, for all of us in Europe," warned the head of state on October 20 during his address to the European Council. The country has had to implement temporary power outages in Kiev and in ten regions. Two days later, new strikes on power plants left more than 1.5 million people in the dark. The extent of the damage "is comparable, if not greater" than the attacks carried out from October 10 to 12 against energy installations, stated the national operator, Ukrenergo. In total, "about 40% of thermal electricity production [from fuel sources] is now out of service," Energy Minister German Galushchenko told Le Monde. After the initial attacks, "millions of people were left without light, water, communication, unable to call their loved ones or find out through the news if new bombings were taking place," noted the minister, who responded in writing to Le Monde's questions. Today, "Ukraine's electrical grid remains stable despite the bombings, but hundreds of thousands of consumers are still without electricity, mainly in frontline regions," he added. The work of technical teams hindered Authorities are quickly repairing damaged infrastructures wherever possible. After the October 10 attacks, electricity supply was restored in four thousand cities and for millions of consumers. However, in frontline regions, combat, bombings, and mines laid by Russian troops are hindering the work of technical teams, who must wait for authorization from the Ukrainian armed forces before intervening. Caution is also necessary in recently liberated territories, where "the Russians are mining critical infrastructures," leading to the "loss of many sector specialists." To keep the electrical grid stable, authorities have urged citizens to limit their consumption, especially the use of high-energy appliances such as heaters, irons, washing machines, and dishwashers during peak hours. Businesses, on the other hand, are encouraged to limit the use of outdoor lighting. "We need to save energy even more," says Mr. Galushchenko, Minister of Energy. The call has been heard: Ukrainians have "voluntarily reduced their energy consumption by about 10%," affirms the Minister of Energy. "This has significantly helped to reduce the duration of power outages," he explains. "But now, we need to save energy even more: at least a 20% reduction in energy consumption is necessary to stabilize the power system." Especially since Ukraine is also suffering from the occupation, for seven months, of the Zaporizhia nuclear power plant. Before the invasion, this plant, with a total capacity of 6,000 megawatts, represented 40% of the country's nuclear production. Ukraine is now deprived of it. To avoid overheating of the energy networks during peak hours, the authorities are carrying out "emergency shutdowns." On Sunday, Ukrenergo carried out power cuts in Kiev. They alternately affected different neighborhoods of the capital, divided into three groups, and theoretically should not last more than four hours. International aid In the streets of Kiev, on October 24. Power cuts are becoming more frequent in the Ukrainian capital. SERGEI SUPINSKY / AFP Ukraine also relies, in part, on the assistance of its international partners. According to the Minister of Energy, the country has received "more than 850 generators as part of humanitarian aid." Mr. Galushchenko, however, assures that they do not currently need to import energy from Europe. "When we synchronized the Ukrainian and European energy systems, we of course took into account emergency assistance options from Europe or even commercial imports. But for now, we see this as a backup option," he explains. Ukraine was synchronized with the Russian power grid until the invasion on February 24. It then operated autonomously until its power grid was connected to the European network in mid-March. Instead of importing energy, Kiev is now trying to store its own energy and fuel resources - coal and gas. Ukraine had started these exports a few days after the European Union granted it official candidate status for membership. Electricity exports to the European Union (EU), which had begun in July, have been suspended. Ukraine had started these exports a few days after the EU granted it official candidate status for membership. "We were ready to increase the volume of exports to 2 gigawatts," assures the Minister of Energy. This would allow the EU not to buy billions of cubic meters of Russian gas, the aggressor country. According to him, stopping exports was one of Moscow's objectives, with this cooperation between Ukraine and the EU preventing Russia, in his view, from "resorting to its preferred tactic: energy blackmail." Faced with the risk of a cold winter without electricity or heating, the population is being pushed to resume the path of exile. "Russia is causing a new wave of Ukrainian migration to EU countries," warned Mr. Zelensky on Thursday. To prevent these new departures and to keep Ukraine from being plunged into darkness, Kiev is urging its international partners to provide more air defense systems. "We have already received some, and these systems are already successfully countering enemy fire. But this is not enough to ensure complete protection," warns Mr. Galushchenko. According to authorities, the damages caused by Russia to Ukraine's energy sector amount to several billion dollars.

title: Elon Musk, Bill Gates... "We need to imagine ways to control the tendency of billionaires to replace governments."
lead: Thanks to their financial power, Elon Musk and Bill Gates are multiplying initiatives in geopolitical, health, and climate areas, thereby weakening democracies and international organizations, as explained in his column by Philippe Bernard, an editorialist at "Le Monde".
text_eng: They believe themselves to be the masters of the world, but no one has elected them. They are not dictators and they ask us to call them "benefactors." From Carnegie to Rockefeller, the influence of the great American philanthropists is not new. But their successors in the 21st century have global, even interplanetary ambitions, and do not bother with morals. Most importantly, they wield their power in a world dominated by the influence of social networks, where democratic states and international organizations are weakened. The most problematic is also the richest person on the planet: Elon Musk is not satisfied with being the CEO of Tesla, whose valuation surpasses that of all other car manufacturers combined, and leading SpaceX, an essential partner of the Pentagon and NASA. The man, with a fortune estimated at $220 billion (equivalent in euros) according to Forbes magazine, dabbles in geopolitics, and he has the means to do so. Connected to more than 2,200 satellites of his Starlink network, the 25,000 terminals he delivered for free to Ukraine ensure the country's civilian and military communications in the midst of devastated infrastructure. They enable the guidance of missiles towards Russian targets as well as the operation of hospitals and banks. However, the versatility of Ukraine's benefactor, his potential double-dealing, is chilling. In mid-October, the 51-year-old billionaire publicly raised the idea that he might stop funding Starlink in Ukraine, before reconsidering and boasting about it. "Happy to help Ukraine," he tweeted, a network he ultimately acquired on Thursday, October 27, for $44 billion. Previously, he had sparked the anger of Volodymyr Zelensky - and likely pleased Vladimir Putin - by advocating for his own "peace plan" that included Ukraine relinquishing Crimea to Russia. Elon Musk's geopolitical whims and conflicts of interest - such as proposing to attach Taiwan to China, a country where Tesla has a factory - worry even US authorities. Also concerning is the presence of foreign investors in his bid to acquire Twitter. The billionaire's plans for the blue bird social network, where he has 110 million followers, and his desire to weaken moderation procedures to better "free" expression, are troubling. The clash between Elon Musk's libertarian beliefs - maximum individual freedoms, minimal state intervention - and the international responsibilities that his success de facto gives him, allowing him to bypass American diplomacy, is inevitable. The limits of "philanthrocapitalism" have already been felt by Bill Gates. The Covid-19 pandemic, which he claims to have foreseen and fought on the front lines with his foundation, the wealthiest in the world (worth 70 billion dollars), has shed harsh light on the contradictions and limits of the "philanthrocapitalism" he embodies. His defense of property rights over patents, aggressively practiced at Microsoft and contributing to his wealth, has proven disastrous in enabling rapid vaccination of populations in poor countries, of which he claims to be a champion. Under pressure, he had to concede an exception for Covid-19 vaccines. However, Bill Gates' complicity with pharmaceutical companies has become very visible. The image of the man who dedicated his fortune to "saving lives" has abruptly given way to "fake news" claiming he seeks to exterminate or control populations through vaccine passports or microchips. The financial power of the Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation, which spends more on health than many states, has allowed him to lead the alliance of private organizations that has taken on the international management of the fight against Covid-19. This coalition, "strengthened by close relationships with pharmaceutical companies, has replaced governments, but without being held accountable like governments," notes an investigation by Politico and Welt. Dramatic health inaction Such criticisms add to those highlighting the contradictions between the health and climate goals proclaimed by Bill Gates and his investments in polluting or harmful industries, or his choices favoring intensive fertilizer use in Africa. Intended to combat food insecurity and poverty, the "green revolution" promoted and funded by his foundation is fiercely criticized for increasing the financial dependence of small African farmers on multinational fertilizer and pesticide companies. The Gates Foundation has wielded this exorbitant power in African health for years. Its leader has positioned himself as the de facto health minister of the continent, supplementing but also reinforcing the dramatic health inaction of many states and contributing to their weakening. This claim to lead public policies is also concerning in the field of climate change where Bill Gates positions himself as a leader. American journalist Tim Schwab points out "the scant references, untested solutions, and staggering financial conflicts of interest" of the billionaire. He especially emphasizes that "the assertion of his power without democratic basis comes precisely at a time when democratic institutions have become essential to address the issue of climate change." The whole question is to imagine new ways - fiscal, parliamentary, judicial - to subject the apprentice masters of the world to a minimum of control. Otherwise, billions of dollars will continue to give a few self-proclaimed benefactors the right to take the place of states and international organizations to "save the world."

title: Italy: Giorgia Meloni promises that Rome will remain "a reliable partner of NATO in support of Ukraine"
lead: "The leader of the far-right party also stated in front of the deputies that Italy will respect European rules, and she defended herself against any sympathy for fascism."
text_eng: In a speech to the deputies, the new Italian Prime Minister, Giorgia Meloni, strongly reaffirmed Italy's membership in the European Union (EU) on Tuesday, October 25th. Italy is "fully part of Europe and the Western world," declared the leader of the post-fascist Eurosceptic party Fratelli d’Italia. The far-right leader attempted to ease concerns raised by her party's historic victory in September. She assured that Italy's approach was not to "slow down and sabotage European integration," but to make the community machinery work better, in her general policy speech before the Chamber of Deputies, which later in the evening voted confidence in her government. The vote, to be followed by a similar procedure in the Senate on Wednesday, was purely procedural, as Ms. Meloni had a comfortable majority in the Chamber thanks to Silvio Berlusconi's Forza Italia party and the League, the far-right party of Matteo Salvini. The successor to Mario Draghi, whom she also paid tribute to in her speech, is assured of winning this vote of confidence, as her coalition has an absolute majority in both chambers. "A reliable NATO partner" "Italy will respect European rules," Ms. Meloni also assured, although Rome also wants to "help change those that do not work." The EU is "a common home to face challenges that member states can hardly face alone," she said, judging that Europe had not done enough in this area in the past. Giorgia Meloni promised that Italy would remain "a reliable partner of NATO in support of Ukraine, which is resisting Russia's aggression." The new Italian leader affirmed: "Giving in to Putin's energy blackmail would not solve the problem, it would worsen it, opening the way to new claims and blackmail and future energy price increases even higher than those we have experienced in recent months." "Stopping illegal departures" Ms. Meloni also sought to reassure about her party's stance, denying any "sympathy" or "proximity to antidemocratic regimes." "For no regime, including fascism," insisted the former admirer of Mussolini in her youth. Regarding the issue of immigration, the new Prime Minister stated on Tuesday that her government intends to "stop illegal departures" from Africa to the Peninsula and "break the human trafficking" in the Mediterranean. As inflation rose by 8.9% year-on-year in September, she committed as a "priority" to "strengthen support measures for households and businesses, both for energy bills and fuel." "An financial commitment that will drain a large portion of the available resources," she acknowledged.

title: Faced with tensions between Paris and Berlin, rekindle the dynamics of Franco-German dialogue.
lead: Editorial from "Le Monde". Differences of opinion are emerging at the worst moment, as Vladimir Putin tests European solidarity and the Franco-German leadership is being challenged within the Twenty-Seven.
text_eng: Three months before the sixtieth anniversary of the Elysée Treaty, which sealed the reconciliation between France and Germany while laying the foundations for cooperation between the two countries, bilateral relations are at a low point. Emmanuel Macron has not yet found in Chancellor Olaf Scholz the partner he had hoped for, following the departure of Angela Merkel, who, along with him, was the architect of the European recovery plan. The two main engines of the European Union (EU) even seem to no longer be pulling in the same direction. It is not only the strength of the Franco-German partnership that is at stake, but also its structuring role within the European project, which is threatened with weakening. These disagreements have objective causes. The Russian invasion of Ukraine has shattered the German certainties on which the country had built its prosperity. Between the end of access to cheap energy from Russia and the questioning of peace in Europe, Germany must reinvent its economic model while improvising a geopolitical role it had renounced after the capitulation of 1945. This is a real earthquake that could only deeply reshape the relationship with France. In fact, points of contention have accumulated in recent weeks, leading to the unexpected postponement of the Franco-German ministerial council scheduled for October 26. In defense, several joint projects are at a standstill, whether it be the future aircraft or the next generation of tanks. The missile shield project led by Germany within a group of 14 countries, without France, which is pursuing its own project, has further strained relations. Possible convergences In energy, Berlin is blocking the principle of capping gas prices pushed by France, while Paris does not want to hear about a gas pipeline project connecting Spain to the rest of Europe to supply the German industry. Economically, France did not appreciate that the German Chancellor unveiled a massive €200 billion support plan for its economy without prior notice, interpreted as a challenge to competition principles within the EU. Even the pace of European construction is subject to debate. While Berlin advocates for expansion to the east, Paris argues for deepening integration, without fearing a multi-speed Europe. These differences of opinion come at the worst moment, as Vladimir Putin tests European solidarity and the Franco-German leadership is challenged within the Twenty-Seven. The Baltic countries and Poland criticize their two powerful partners for their lack of clarity and moderation on the Russian threat, despite the warnings they had issued. The rift within the tandem is undermining its credibility and legitimacy to set the tone for European politics. There is an urgent need to reassess the Franco-German relationship, even if it means addressing uncomfortable issues. Emmanuel Macron and Olaf Scholz were supposed to address this during a lunch at the Elysée on Wednesday, October 26. Despite the current tensions, which may take some time to resolve, the obstacles are not insurmountable. The speech by the German Chancellor in Prague in August had sparked hope by resonating with several points raised by Emmanuel Macron four years earlier at the Sorbonne. It is time to focus on possible convergences to try to revive a dynamic without which the EU risks falling apart.

title: NATO is testing its nuclear deterrence capabilities during an annual and "routine" military exercise.
lead: The maneuvers are taking place in the context of the war in Ukraine and the threats made by Vladimir Putin to use his nuclear arsenal. Russia could organize exercises similar to those of the Alliance.
text_eng: "Steadfast Noon" versus "Grom"... NATO forces and Russia have engaged in a dangerous choreography this October. Eight months after the Russian invasion of part of Ukraine and despite Vladimir Putin's threats to use his nuclear arsenal, the Atlantic Alliance officially launched, on Monday, October 17, a large-scale nuclear military operation called "Steadfast Noon" over Belgium, the United Kingdom, and the North Sea for two weeks. On the other hand, Russia could quickly organize a similar exercise called "Grom." NATO maneuvers are considered "a planned and routine exercise," warned NATO Secretary General Jens Stoltenberg on October 12. "It would send a very bad signal if we suddenly canceled a long-planned routine exercise because of the war in Ukraine," he added. "That would be absolutely the wrong signal to send." Fourteen alliance countries, excluding France but including the Netherlands, Poland, and Spain, are participating in this training led by the Supreme Headquarters Allied Powers Europe, based in Mons. About sixty combat aircraft, including Tornado and F-16 jets, as well as surveillance and tanker aircraft, will participate in the exercise organized more than 1,000 kilometers from the Russian border, according to NATO. "No real weapons" "As in previous years, American long-range B-52 bombers will take part in the exercise, but this year they will take off from North Dakota," the military organization said. "It is about testing logistics, transporting bombs from one base to another, testing decoys, conducting intelligence, surveillance missions," explained William Alberque, director of strategy at the International Institute for Strategic Studies (IISS) in Berlin. NATO emphasizes that all this is done without "any real weapons." NATO does not intend to disclose further details on the number of troops, types of equipment used, or maneuvers launched regarding an operation that takes place annually from a different European base. In 2021, it was held in Southern Europe, from an Italian base. "For years, these large-scale rehearsals were completely classified," Alberque recalled. "Now, the Alliance is a bit more transparent, but it still remains very secretive." According to the organization, "this exercise contributes to ensuring that the Alliance's nuclear deterrence remains safe, secure, and effective," emphasized its spokesperson, Oana Lungescu. However, NATO is not expected to be the only one testing its procedures and organization in the event of a nuclear attack. According to the spokesperson of the White House National Security Council, John Kirby, Russia could quickly organize a similar military exercise called "Grom" (thunder, in French). "Every year, the Russian army organizes this exercise in October," confirms William Alberque. It is therefore legitimate to think that they will do the same this year." For a few days, Moscow mobilizes its missile-launching submarines, intercontinental missile sites, and command center. A crucial maintenance These trainings come against a backdrop of escalating tensions between Russia and NATO. After Vladimir Putin's statements in September about the possible use of nuclear weapons if his territory was "threatened" by allied armies, NATO officials have been extremely firm, assuring that they take the provocations of the Kremlin leader very seriously. U.S. President Joe Biden mentioned the risk of a "nuclear apocalypse" on October 6. "Upon analysis, Mr. Putin's statements seem more like a posture to force the West to negotiate or, at the very least, to push Ukraine to do so," Alberque assesses. It is precisely based on this analysis that Western strategists believe that maintaining regular nuclear training is essential. "In terms of deterrence, routine is good," confides a NATO source. "Going beyond that exposes one to errors or misunderstandings." Military personnel still remember the consequences of the "Able Archer" episode in 1983. At that time, this exercise conducted by NATO in November was so realistic, in a tense international climate between Americans and Russians, that the Soviet high command believed a nuclear strike was imminent. Nuclear forces and two Soviet air bases were put on alert. In Belgium, the current exercise organized by NATO has caused concern among environmental parties and organizations, who believe that the country is acting in contradiction to the non-proliferation treaty it has signed. Even though the Kleine-Brogel air base in Limburg has hosted several editions of this annual exercise in 2012 and 2017. In mid-October, Belgian Defense Minister Ludivine Dedonder tried to reassure the population by stating that no real weapons would be dropped and that the operation posed no danger. The Kleine-Brogel air base, which employs around 1,500 people, houses Belgian F-16 aircraft whose pilots are trained in the use of nuclear weapons, as well as a U.S. Army support group responsible for ammunition supply. American F-35 aircraft that Belgium has committed to acquiring will also be stationed there.

title: Russia: Alexei Navalny claims to be targeted by new charges punishable by up to thirty years in prison
lead: The 46-year-old opposition figure, considered to be the main critic of Vladimir Putin, specified that he had received a notification informing him of the opening of this new criminal case while he is already imprisoned.
text_eng: In Russia, imprisoned opposition leader Alexei Navalny claimed on Thursday, October 20, to be facing new criminal charges for "promoting terrorism," "inciting extremism," "financing extremist activities," and "rehabilitating Nazism." "The lawyers have calculated that it adds up to about thirty years [in prison], taking into account the penalties provided for each of these articles in the criminal code," he said in a message released by his team on social media. The 46-year-old anti-corruption activist, considered the main critic of Russian President Vladimir Putin, specified that he had received a notification informing him of the opening of this new criminal case while he is already imprisoned. Already sentenced to nine years in prison, he sarcastically remarked, "I am a criminal genius of the world. (...) You all thought I had been in isolation, in prison for two years, but in fact I was actively committing crimes," praising Russian investigators for their "vigilance." According to him, these new accusations are partly related to videos published by his allies in exile, who continue to advocate against the Russian government from abroad. Alexei Navalny was arrested in January 2021 upon his return to Russia after surviving a serious poisoning attempt, which he attributes to the Kremlin. In March 2022, he was sentenced to nine years in a "strict" regime for what he considers to be fictitious charges of "fraud." He continues to convey messages denouncing Vladimir Putin and his intervention in Ukraine to his lawyers, which are then published online by his team. This summer, he repeatedly claimed to have been placed in a disciplinary cell in his penal colony near Vladimir, 200 kilometers east of Moscow.

title: Italy: Berlusconi says he has "reconnected" with Putin and attacks Zelensky; discomfort in the right-wing coalition
lead: Her remarks have caused astonishment for Giorgia Meloni, the leader of the post-fascist party Fratelli d’Italia, who is working to form a government with Forza Italia and the League, the respective parties of Silvio Berlusconi and Matteo Salvini.
text_eng: In Italy, former Prime Minister Silvio Berlusconi received a lot of criticism on Wednesday, October 19, after expressing a desire to "reconnect" with Russian President Vladimir Putin and blaming Kiev for the invasion of Ukraine. The Italian press widely reported on remarks made by Silvio Berlusconi to members of his party, Forza Italia (right-wing), which were leaked despite initial denials from his circle. The likely future prime minister, Giorgia Meloni, was described as being "hostage to pro-Russians." An audio recording was later released, causing astonishment among party leaders, including Giorgia Meloni of the post-fascist party Fratelli d'Italia, who is working to form a government with coalition allies Forza Italia and Matteo Salvini's League (far-right). Berlusconi, who celebrated his 86th birthday on September 29, stated, "I have reconnected a bit with President Putin, quite a bit, in the sense that for my birthday, Putin sent me twenty bottles of vodka and a very kind letter. I responded by sending him bottles of Lambrusco and a very kind letter. He told me I was one of his five true friends." Forza Italia promptly issued a statement clarifying the party's and its leader's position on Russia, stating it is "in line with that of Europe and the United States." The left-wing opposition strongly criticized the statements, with former Prime Minister Enrico Letta denouncing the shift towards a more ambiguous stance towards Russia by the new majority. The declarations by Berlusconi could jeopardize the position of Antonio Tajani, a close ally and pro-European, who was being considered for the position of Minister of Foreign Affairs. Meloni's circle tried to downplay the situation, with Fratelli d'Italia deputy Francesco Lollobrigida stating, "I would avoid involving Tajani." However, according to sources within Fratelli d'Italia cited by La Stampa, "Berlusconi might have killed Tajani." During the election campaign, Berlusconi had already sparked outrage by claiming that Putin had wanted to overthrow the Kiev government to replace it with "good people." In a further leaked audio recording on Wednesday evening, Berlusconi appears to shift the blame for the war onto Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky. "In 2014 in Minsk, Belarus, an agreement was signed between Ukraine and the two newly established republics in Donbass for a peace agreement where no one attacks anyone. A year later, Ukraine disregarded this agreement and began attacking the borders of the two republics," Silvio Berlusconi told his lawmakers. He mentioned "five, six, seven thousand deaths" in these conflicts, and then added, "Zelensky arrives and triples the attacks against the two republics," later reiterating his statements from the end of September that Mr. Putin had been "pushed" by his population and entourage to invade Ukraine. Faced with the backlash from these recent statements, Silvio Berlusconi clarified in a statement that his "personal position and that of Forza Italia do not deviate from that of the Italian government, the European Union, the Atlantic Alliance, neither on the crisis in Ukraine nor on other major international policy issues."

title: On Ukrainian television, the difficult balance between military censorship and expression of pluralism.
lead: Since the beginning of the war, the country's five free channels have been broadcasting the same news bulletins, while the opposition denounces an increasing politicization of this tool by Volodymyr Zelensky.
text_eng: It is a kind of never-ending telethon, which has been going on for almost eight months. Twenty-four hours a day, seven days a week, the news on Ukrainian digital television is identical and broadcast simultaneously on five channels, due to martial law. Because of the Russian invasion that has been devastating the country since February 24, the military imposes strict censorship: newsrooms must wait four hours before broadcasting images of Russian strikes. This is to avoid helping the enemy "correct their aim." It is also primarily to maintain cohesion within the population in the face of adversity and to combat Russia's attempts to destabilize through social media. "Television is a prime target for the Russians," the Minister of Culture and Information, Oleksandr Tkachenko, told Le Monde. "That's why their missiles have continuously targeted television towers in Kiev, Kharkiv, Rivne, Vinnytsia, and elsewhere. When they capture a city, they always start by cutting off Ukrainian television to replace it with their channels, even before taking control of administrative buildings." The central directive of the program broadcast by Ukrainian television channels is to boost the morale of viewers by emphasizing good news and ridiculing the invader as much as possible. The military also do their part. Responding on Saturday, October 15, to a pressing question from the host about the imminent recapture of the Kherson region by the Ukrainian army, the head of the press service of the 63rd Mechanized Brigade, Oleg Zelinsky, first responded in the most conventional manner: "I can't tell you, because it's forbidden." Then he grabbed a large slice of watermelon, previously out of frame, and took a bite, triggering laughter in the studio. This vegetable is a symbol of the Kherson region. This sketch, performed by a namesake of the comedian turned president, Volodymyr Zelensky, illustrates the communication efforts made by the government to keep the home front mobilized. The telethon, called "Information Union - together we are stronger" in Ukrainian, is designed by five television channels. Three belong to oligarchs: Inter (Dmytro Firtash, under house arrest in Austria), 1+1 (Ihor Kolomoisky, banned from entering the United States), and ICTV (Viktor Pinchuk, son-in-law of former President Leonid Kuchma). The other two are public channels: Perchy and Rada, the parliamentary channel. Each channel produces a six-hour segment and then hands over to the next, creating a cycle of thirty hours. "This cooperation allows us to deal with extreme and unforeseen situations," explains Natalia Zagumenna to Le Monde, executive producer of the public channel Perchy ("Première"), located just behind Khreshchatyk, the main artery of Kiev. The basement of the building has just been converted to accommodate a live studio. "In case of an air raid, which is very common at the moment, we move the live broadcast here in five minutes," says the producer. When asked about the diversity of opinions broadcast on her channel during wartime, Ms. Zagumenna denies any interference from the authorities. "All points of view are expressed on our channel, with the exception of [the party] OPZJ, which poses a danger to national security. Otherwise, there is no blacklist," she explains. OPZJ (Opposition Platform - For Life) is a pro-Russian party led by Viktor Medvedchuk, a close associate of Russian President Vladimir Putin, and whose several members collaborate with the Russian army in occupied areas. "We even sometimes give a voice to Russians, like the opposition figure Garry Kasparov," she continues, adding, "There are no good Russians, but there are useful Russians." Asked about the rare appearances of former President Petro Poroshenko on the channel, the producer responds that an interview is being prepared. This invitation is confirmed by Mr. Poroshenko's press service director, Andriy Zhigulin. While Perchy has a good reputation, the same cannot be said for other channels. "Since the beginning of the war, Rada has engaged in two Black PR campaigns against Poroshenko, first trying to tarnish his image by alleging a supposed closeness to Medvedchuk, then insinuating without evidence that he had evacuated his family to London, without ever giving him the opportunity to defend himself on air," laments Otar Dovzhenko, co-founder of the media think tank Detektor Media. Controversy Another controversy surrounds the government's decision in March to exclude three news channels (Espresso, Priamy, and 5 Kanal) from the public digital bouquet, which is freely accessible throughout the country. These three channels (still available online and through paid subscriptions) broadcast the telethon but reserve some airtime for their own content. Priamy and 5 Kanal are known to be close to Petro Poroshenko, even though he sold his shares in November 2021 due to the so-called anti-oligarch law. Introduced by President Zelensky, the law was supposed to reduce the influence of oligarchs who had dominated the political and media landscape over the previous three decades. In practice, Petro Poroshenko was personally targeted. In July 2022, Ukraine's wealthiest individual, Rinat Akhmetov, abruptly shut down his television channel Ukraine, which had long been the most watched in the country, officially to avoid falling under the anti-oligarch law. "It's mainly because he doesn't need it anymore: due to the war, there is no longer any political life, so there is no longer a need to influence public opinion," points out Otar Dovzhenko, specifying that the telethon "weighs down the financial results of the channels, as there is no longer any advertising on air, hence no revenue. Akhmetov has already lost a lot of assets during the war." "For years, I have worked to prevent television from being confiscated by plutocratic interests. As a result, when the war broke out, the State entrusted television to groups owned by oligarchs. And the Rada, which should showcase parliamentary life, has been turned into an instrument of influence in service of the government," rages Vitali Portnikov, editorialist and presenter on the Espresso channel. "In times of war, when the survival of the State is at stake, there should be no political debate or discussion on military decisions," argues this highly respected opposition journalist. "On the other hand, the government should focus on the immediate protection of the country and above all, refrain from engaging in political intrigues aimed at ensuring its continuation in power after the war."

title: Thierry Mariani and Yves Pozzo di Borgo targeted by an investigation for corruption linked to Russia.
lead: The Paris public prosecutor's office is investigating possible acts of corruption and influence peddling related to Franco-Russian Dialogue, an association co-chaired by RN MEP Thierry Mariani.
text_eng: One of the most influential political relays of Russia in France is now in the sights of the French justice system. According to information from Le Monde, Dialogue franco-russe is at the center of two preliminary investigations opened on September 29, 2021 by Junalco, the division of the Paris prosecutor's office responsible for combating organized crime. This association, created in 2004 under the patronage of Jacques Chirac and Vladimir Putin, brings together French and Russian political leaders and businessmen with the stated goal of developing cooperation between the two countries. These two investigations target in particular Thierry Mariani and Yves Pozzo di Borgo, two of the most active members of the association known for their public positions favorable to the Kremlin. Mr. Mariani, a Member of the European Parliament and a regional councilor for the National Rally (RN), has been co-chairing Dialogue franco-russe since 2012 and openly defends French economic interests in Russia. Mr. Pozzo di Borgo, a former senator and councilor in Paris, a former member of the centrist party Union of Democrats and Independents (UDI), is also a staunch supporter of Vladimir Putin's foreign policy. Trips funded by Russia The existence of the investigations was confirmed to Le Monde by the Paris prosecutor's office, which, however, refused to comment on the identities of the individuals targeted. "These procedures are still ongoing and their development is covered by secrecy," the prosecutor's office said. According to Le Monde, they have been entrusted to the Central Office for the Fight against Corruption and Financial and Fiscal Offenses (OCLCIFF). The first investigation, which concerns exclusively Mr. Mariani, is based on suspicions of breach of trust and money laundering related to the association – that is, possible embezzlement of funds belonging to the association. It has already led to searches. The second, potentially much more explosive, seeks to establish possible acts of corruption and influence peddling by Thierry Mariani and Yves Pozzo di Borgo, also related to the association. Opened five months before the outbreak of the war in Ukraine, these judicial procedures stem from a report by the anti-money laundering unit Tracfin in May 2021. This report concerns several suspicious financial transactions related to Dialogue franco-russe and mentions the names of several French political leaders, including Mr. Mariani and Pozzo di Borgo. According to the information provided to the justice system, the two men would have benefited from services such as all-expenses-paid trips, funded by Russia through entities that actually serve as intermediaries and fronts for the Kremlin. The justice system is trying to determine to what extent these gifts may have served as direct quid pro quos for positions favorable to Russia. Among the questionable financial operations highlighted by Tracfin are also the flows passing through YPDB Conseil, the consulting company created by Yves Pozzo di Borgo a few weeks after his departure from the Senate in 2017. According to the trade register, this company experienced exponential growth between 2018 and 2020, allowing its president and sole shareholder to grant himself generous dividends (103,838 euros in 2020). However, investigations conducted by the anti-money laundering unit of the French Ministry of Finance have shown that this company generated 85% of its turnover with the Russian bus manufacturer Bakulin Motors Group, suspected of having business ties with a Kremlin-linked oligarch. Difficult criminal offenses to prove The work of the investigators, who will have to establish whether these facts fall under a criminal classification, appears delicate. Certainly, neither Thierry Mariani nor Yves Pozzo di Borgo - who remain presumed innocent - make a secret of their support for Russia, nor do they hide sponsored trips by foreign powers or entities linked to them. Thierry Mariani had notably defended Vladimir Putin after the annexation of Crimea in 2014 and condemned the Western sanctions imposed on Russia. His on-site visits have been widely publicized, such as a visit in July 2015 organized in a Russia under sanctions, then in Crimea, with around ten parliamentarians, including Mr. Pozzo di Borgo. But for the justice system, the challenge is to establish the existence of a corruption pact, by tracing a direct and concrete link between the financing of these services and any subsequent benefits. The offense of passive corruption is the act of requesting or accepting an advantage in exchange for the performance - or non-performance - of an act related to one's function. Passive influence peddling, on the other hand, involves accepting or requesting an advantage to use one's influence in exchange. "The question is whether, and at what point, these individuals cross into illegality." "These are individuals known for lobbying and who openly admit it, so the question is whether, and at what point, they cross into illegality," explains a judicial source summarizing the difficulty of the exercise. Faced with these challenges in legal interpretation, this source does not rule out the possibility that it may ultimately be impossible to prove the commission of offenses. The investigation will also need to provide evidence of the irregular or fraudulent nature of certain financial flows. When contacted by Le Monde, Thierry Mariani stated that he was "not aware" of such investigations. "I am still on the same line: I have been interested in Russia since I learned Russian in a military school. Every time I have been to Russia, I have declared my trips to the European Parliament. It's transparent, you can clearly see who is paying." In the past, Mr. Mariani had stated that he had never received any money in connection with his trips as a guest. He now adds that he defends "a position that consists of saying that our current policy [towards Russia] is neither in the interest of France nor in the interest of European security." "If I support [Franco-Russian Dialogue] and remain its president despite all the troubles it may cause me, it is because I remain convinced that it is in our best interest to re-establish healthy and peaceful relations with Russia as soon as possible," he concludes. Example of Russian interference On his part, Yves Pozzo di Borgo refused to respond. "I no longer have contact with the press (...) It is a principle for me to maintain my tranquility," he stated. However, this discretion does not apply to social media, where the former senator openly expresses his admiration for the Putin regime. This is evident in his recent posts on Twitter, such as on September 21, where he wrote: "The NATO narrative relayed by Macron, who wants us to believe that the war in Ukraine is the Ukrainian people reacting to Russia when it is actually a civil war in which many Ukrainians want to join Russia! Let democracy take its course!" Or on September 25: "The NATO narrative tries to make us believe that Russia is isolated! At the General Assembly, we saw Macron, the man of the Americans, speaking to an empty room! On the other hand, Lavrov, the Russian minister, was overwhelmed with requests for meetings and appointments!" Since the Russian invasion of Ukraine, the Franco-Russian Dialogue association has faced some financial setbacks with "the desertion of its French sponsors," as reported by La Lettre A in August. The association also had to vacate its premises on the Champs-Elysées, which were provided by the Russian Railways company (RZD). This company has been placed under international sanctions for its role in the war in Ukraine and has been "inactive" in France "since July 29, 2022," according to the commercial register. Regardless of their judicial outcome, the ongoing investigations by the Paris prosecutor's office are an additional and emblematic illustration of Russia's attempts to interfere in Western countries. According to a leaked American diplomatic cable on September 13 by the Associated Press, Russia has secretly spent over 300 million dollars trying to influence political actors in more than twenty countries worldwide since 2014.

title: "In Xi Jinping's China, the anti-religious repression signifies the Party's inability to control minds."
lead: The decline of Marxist utopia and the collapse of traditional ethics have created a moral and spiritual void that official ideology cannot fill, unlike religions, whose revival is spectacular, notes Claude Meyer, an expert on Asia, in an opinion piece in "Le Monde".
text_eng: The death of Mikhail Gorbachev has sparked contrasting reactions in China: Beijing acknowledged the constructive role he played in normalizing relations between the two countries after thirty years of rupture, but the press did not hesitate to heavily criticize the man behind perestroika. Deng Xiaoping had already considered glasnost to be a "foolish" policy that would lead to the collapse of the USSR, as it prioritized political reform by promoting freedom of expression and association, which would lead to massive demonstrations, strikes, and the awakening of national identities. This was the opposite of the policy pursued by President Xi Jinping, whose main goal is the revitalization of the Chinese Communist Party (CCP), which he deemed to be in decline when he came to power in 2012. Obsessed with the example of Gorbachev, he draws inspiration from Mao Zedong, advocating a return to socialist, even Maoist, ideals. The 20th CCP Congress, held from October 16 to 22, endorsed his approach and renewed his leadership for a third five-year term, with a possible fourth term in 2027 when he will only be 74 years old. At the previous congress in 2017, he successfully had the two-term limit abolished, which had been imposed after Mao's death, and even had his "thought" enshrined in the Constitution alongside that of the Great Helmsman. However, how can the second largest global power, which is highly capitalistic in many respects – holding the world record for billionaires – embody communist ideals? Nevertheless, according to some observers, China has never truly stopped being communist and is becoming increasingly so under Xi Jinping. In terms of its political regime, it is undeniable that the "new Mao" is pushing the CCP back to its red, even Maoist, roots. Additionally, as a sign of vitality for a party that has been in power for seventy years, membership is increasing rapidly among younger generations, although it is difficult to discern their true motivations, whether ideological convictions or career calculations. However, this perspective focuses on the ideological dimension promoted by the CCP without considering two essential points. Firstly, the specifics of the Chinese economy. While the state plays a significant role, it does not make the country communist. Social inequalities are more pronounced than in the United States, and there are even more billionaires, enthusiastic followers of Deng Xiaoping's belief that "It is glorious to get rich." Many of them are members of the Party and some are even in the Parliament. But more than just the economy, this viewpoint overlooks the profound societal changes that have occurred in China over the past few decades. Are citizens truly convinced today of the benefits of a Marxism-Leninism revitalized by the thoughts of Mao and Xi Jinping? How then to explain, for example, the spectacular resurgence of religions in recent decades? The shift in direction by Deng Xiaoping dealt a fatal blow to the collectivist dream of the Maoist era, replaced by the religion of economic efficiency and the worship of money as king. Rampant consumerism, business dealings, and widespread corruption: the decline of the Marxist utopia and the collapse of traditional ethics have created a moral crisis and a spiritual void that the official ideology cannot fill, unlike religions. The CCP boasts 98 million members but cannot ignore that over 350 million citizens are turning away from Marx and Lenin to find meaning in their lives through religion. With at least four times more believers than CCP members, can one then speak of a "communist" China, rooted in the collectivist and atheistic utopia of the Maoist era? This religious dynamic, a challenge to the militant atheism of the CCP, faces a repression that has worsened alarmingly under Xi Jinping. This is a major difference with Putin's Russia, where there is an objective alliance between power and the Orthodox Church. The Kremlin leader considers the Orthodox religion as an integral part of Russian identity and, as such, stands alongside Moscow Patriarch Kirill for major religious celebrations. In return, the patriarch is a fervent supporter of Putin, even giving a theological dimension to the invasion of Ukraine, a "legitimate" operation as it is necessary to combat the "forces of evil"... "Sinicizing" Christians In China, repression is particularly brutal against the Uighur Islam and Tibetan Buddhism, but it also does not spare Christianity, whose underground churches thrive and refuse to join official "patriotic" associations. The rise of Christianity deeply concerns the government, as the number of believers will soon surpass that of CCP members. Hence the fierce energy of the Party to suffocate Christian communities and "sinicize" this "Western" religion to serve socialist values under the exclusive authority of the Party. The aim is to eradicate faith in Christ by replacing it with faith in the CCP and in its leader, now almost deified, as Mao was. This religious policy reveals the regime's weaknesses and ideological obsessions: repression signifies the CCP's inability to control minds, sinicization is part of the ideological offensive that China is waging, particularly against the West. Will this containment strategy succeed in restoring a truly communist China? Some researchers doubt this, as they see it becoming, by 2030, the first Christian country in the world.

title: Korea, megabasin, Russian blockade... The five key pieces of information from the weekend.
lead: These last two days have also been marked by the double bombing in Somalia and the second round of the presidential election in Brazil.
text_eng: Did you miss the news over the weekend? We summarize the main information from the past two days for you. Korea: 153 dead in a stampede during Halloween At least 153 people died and 134 were injured in a stampede in Seoul, the South Korean capital, on Saturday night during Halloween festivities. The final death toll could be even higher, with authorities reporting 2,642 missing persons. The French Ministry of Foreign Affairs reported on Sunday that there was a French national among the deceased. Nearly 100,000 people, according to media estimates, had gathered in the Itaewon district, known for its nightlife, for the Halloween party, the first in Seoul since the beginning of the Covid-19 pandemic. At this stage, it appears that many people, caught in crowd movements, fell on top of each other in a narrow street, said the chief of the Yongsan fire station. The government will "rigorously investigate the cause" of the tragedy "to ensure that such an accident does not happen again," promised South Korean President Yoon Seok-youl, lamenting "a tragedy, a disaster that should not have happened." Violence during a protest against the "megabasin" in Saint-Soline Violent clashes broke out on Saturday during a gathering organized by environmental activists in Sainte-Soline, in the Deux-Sèvres region, to try to block the construction of a water reservoir. The clashes between protesters and law enforcement reportedly left dozens injured on both sides. "Sixty-one gendarmes were injured, twenty-two seriously," tweeted Interior Minister Gérald Darmanin, who stated that "this figure shows that it was not a peaceful demonstration but a very violent gathering." Julien Le Guet, spokesperson for the collective "Bassines non merci!" who himself was hit in the head, reported "fifty real injuries," casting doubt on the figure provided by the interior minister. Several thousand people had gathered in the Deux-Sèvres region against a megabasin project intended for agricultural irrigation. The mobilization continued on Sunday, with masked militants cutting one of the pipelines meant to supply the future reservoir. Ukraine: Russia suspends the agreement on cereals Accusing Ukraine of attacking its fleet in Crimea, Russia suspended on Saturday the agreement on the transport of cereals from Ukrainian ports. Moscow claims that drones that allegedly hit at least one of its military ships in Sevastopol Bay had entered the secure zone for transporting Ukrainian cereals. Kiev denounced a "false pretext" and called on the international community to pressure Moscow to "once again respect its obligations." According to Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky, at least 176 ships carrying over two million tons of grain loaded in Ukraine are blocked by Moscow. UN Secretary-General Antonio Guterres expressed deep concern about the situation regarding the agreement, which is vital for global food supply. In Somalia, a double bombing kills over 100 people. A double car bombing has killed at least 100 people on Saturday in the city center of the Somali capital, Mogadishu. The first car bomb exploded in front of the Ministry of Education, near a busy intersection in the capital. The second explosion occurred as rescuers arrived at the scene and people gathered to help the victims. This type of attack, which has not been claimed, is usually attributed by Somali authorities to jihadist militants al-Shabaab, who regularly carry out attacks in the capital and major cities of the country. The Islamist group, linked to Al-Qaeda, has been fighting the internationally-backed federal government since 2007. Between Lula and Bolsonaro, Brazilians choose their president. Nearly a month after the first round, Brazilian voters are called to the polls on Sunday to choose between Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva, the left-wing candidate, and Jair Bolsonaro, the incumbent far-right president. While polls have been predicting a third four-year term for Lula for months, after his presidencies from 2003 to 2010, Jair Bolsonaro, underestimated in pre-first round opinion polls, is benefiting from momentum indicating a closer second round than expected. And also... Chad. The Chadian Prime Minister announced on Sunday that the French-Australian hostage, abducted on Friday in a province in the east of the country bordering Sudan, has been released in "good health." DRC. After months of tensions, the Democratic Republic of Congo on Saturday expelled the Rwandan ambassador, whom Kinshasa accuses of supporting the M23 rebellion (for "March 23 Movement," a former Tutsi rebellion that resumed arms at the end of 2021). India. At least sixty people died on Sunday evening in India in the collapse of a suspension bridge spanning a river in the west of the country. The bridge had just reopened to the public after six months of renovation work. Painting. Officials at the Kunstsammlung museum in Düsseldorf, Germany, discovered that New York City 1, a painting created in 1941 by the Dutch abstract painter Piet Mondrian, had been hanging upside down for seventy-seven years.

title: "China's support for the countries of Central Asia represents a medium-term threat to the entire region."
lead: Weakened, Russia is no longer able to ensure the security of the former Soviet republics in Asia. China is lying in wait to strengthen its economic dominance there, notes Marie Charrel, a journalist at "Le Monde," in her column.
text_eng: During a series of regional summits between October 12 and 14, several state leaders paraded through the Kazakh capital, Astana, including those from Qatar, Azerbaijan, Tajikistan, Kyrgyzstan, Iran, Iraq, Belarus, Pakistan, as well as Chinese Vice President Wang Qishan, and the presidents of Turkey, Recep Tayyip Erdogan, and Russia, Vladimir Putin. This diplomatic activism reflects the role that Kazakhstan dreams, albeit ambitiously, of playing on the international stage: a bridge between Eurasia and the Middle East. However, behind the scenes, another game is unfolding, both geopolitically and economically: the seismic shift triggered by the war in Ukraine is also reshuffling the cards in Central Asia. A tectonic shift is underway. It poses a risk of destabilizing the countries involved, but also offers them the opportunity for strengthened independence. Until now, Russia provided military security for the region through the Collective Security Treaty Organization, which includes Belarus, Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan, and Tajikistan. However, weakened by the war in Ukraine, Moscow is no longer able to act as the region's policeman in the former Soviet republics. This has led to the resurgence of old conflicts in recent weeks, particularly between Armenia and Azerbaijan, as well as between Tajikistan and Kyrgyzstan. It also raises hopes for greater emancipation of the region from Moscow. On October 14 in Astana, the President of Tajikistan, Emomali Rahmon, stated that "Russia should not ignore the interests of the small countries of Central Asia, as it did during the Soviet era," while his Kazakh counterpart, Kassym-Jomart Tokayev, did not even personally greet Vladimir Putin at the airport – unimaginable just a year ago! Redrawing the map of exchanges These aspirations are not new in Kazakhstan, which does not recognize the territorial annexations in Ukraine and welcomes Russians fleeing conscription. Like Ukraine, the country suffered a terrible famine under Soviet rule and has for years pursued a so-called "multi-vector" policy: cultivating good relations with Moscow as well as with Beijing, Ankara, Washington, and Brussels. This has always irked Russia. Since February, zealous allies of Putin have sometimes threatened to "denazify" Kazakhstan, like Ukraine. Beijing has put a stop to this. For his first visit since the beginning of the Covid-19 pandemic, Chinese President Xi Jinping visited Astana on September 14, where he declared support for the "national independence, sovereignty, and territorial integrity" of Kazakhstan. In other words: Beijing will intervene if Moscow sets foot in the country. This scenario is unlikely, but "these words from Xi Jinping mark a major turning point," explains Annette Bohr of the London-based think tank Chatham House. Until now the dominant economic power in Central Asia, China is now getting involved in security matters. In the short term, this support from the communist giant may be a relief for the country of the steppes. But in the medium term, it represents a threat to the entire region. In recent years, China has already significantly expanded its influence there, by capturing some of the local resources (Kazakh uranium and oil, Turkmen gas), and through the "new silk roads". These have regained new interest in light of the Ukrainian conflict, which could reshape the map of commercial exchanges. Kyrgyzstan and Uzbekistan thus wish to build, with Beijing's support, a railway connection to bypass Russia and link China to Europe. Other projects are also in the pipeline. However, the era of generous infrastructure loans, as it was ten years ago, is over. The growth of the Middle Kingdom is slowing down. It will be more demanding towards its neighbors. "Faced with a socially struggling Central Asia, the conditions for China to easily and without obstacles impose itself are met," summarizes Sébastien Peyrouse from George Washington University. Like Russia, China will sabotage any attempt at an independent foreign policy in the region, particularly that of Astana, without Europe and the United States, so far away, really opposing it.

title: "Poutine wants to freeze us to death in the darkness": in Kharkiv, Russian strikes terrorize civilians
lead: The second largest city in Ukraine, like elsewhere in the country, is being bombed by Russian missiles targeting energy infrastructure in order to deprive residents of electricity and gas as winter approaches.
text_eng: It is 8:15 AM on Tuesday, October 18th, in Kharkiv, in eastern Ukraine. Nine powerful explosions ring out within three minutes, shaking the walls. Customers come out of the store at a gas station to scan the horizon. "It's the anti-aircraft defense," someone hesitantly suggests, between assertion and question. "No, those are strikes," confidently replies a gas station employee. "Look, you can see the smoke," shouts a third person, pointing to two plumes of dust rising rapidly diagonally in the sky. The question is settled. Two customers quickly snap photos with their smartphones, while others have already turned away to go about their business. The morning traffic of cars in the streets of Kharkiv continues as if a terrifying event had not just occurred.
