The second-largest city in Ukraine, located in the northeast of the country, is being bombed by Russian missiles fired from the neighboring region of Belgorod. It has become a grim routine for this major industrial center, which, until the Russian invasion in February, was a stronghold of Russian-speaking culture with a population of one million. The industry has come to a halt, a third of the city has fled, and many of those who remain – especially those under 30 – prefer to speak Ukrainian out of disgust for their neighbor. "The Russians missed their target today, no electrical or energy infrastructure was hit," explains Serhii Magdysiouk, director of the municipal services department for the Kharkiv region, to Le Monde in a weary tone. The man has his work cut out for him. "The Russians have been trying to demolish our infrastructure since the beginning of August. They are primarily targeting electrical substations. Four have been destroyed, including a very important one. They are also targeting high-voltage lines and power plants." Kharkiv regularly experiences power outages, and areas in the east of the region, recently liberated but still close to the front line, have neither electricity nor gas. "There are still 1.3 million inhabitants in the region, and all are at risk of being deprived of gas and electricity this winter," sadly admits the official. According to him, Ukraine's Western allies could help by providing transformers. "They can easily be transported here by rail. Thanks to their mobility, transformers can be quickly moved in case of danger." Russian missile salvos have been targeting Ukraine's energy infrastructure for the past two weeks, sparing no region of the country. In an alarmist tone, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky stated in his daily video address on Tuesday that "since October 10th, 30% of Ukrainian power plants have been destroyed, causing massive outages throughout the country." For her advisor Kirill Timoshenko, "the situation is now critical throughout the country, as our regions are dependent on each other." The Ukrainian emergency services have counted 1,162 localities without electricity. "No one is safe anymore." "Putin wants to freeze us to death in the dark," raged Nadejda, a stylish and talkative 65-year-old retiree living in Saltivka, a residential area in the east of Kharkiv, on Monday evening. Wrapped in a bright red down jacket, she firmly states that she will not leave her home, no matter what happens. "There is no heating in my building, no gas, no water," she complains, sitting on a frail bench at the foot of her staircase. "I have nowhere to go, and I don't want to be relocated to a 'obchejitié' [kind of workers' dormitory] with only one toilet for 50 people." The retiree lives on Lessia-Serdiouka Street, in a building severely affected by Russian bombings. Many windows are broken, and shell impacts can be seen on the facade. During the height of the fighting in March, Nadejda left with her daughter and granddaughter to seek refuge in Malmö, Sweden. But she made the "bad decision" to return home alone after a month. "Now, the journey is no longer free across Europe for Ukrainian refugees." Given the damage to her building, the municipality has temporarily abandoned repairing the water and gas pipes. "At my age, I am forced to carry jerry cans of water to the 8th floor, without an elevator, because those haven't been restored either," she laments. The building, which has 242 apartments, currently houses only 23 residents. At the bottom of the neighboring staircase, Ihor, 42, is busy clearing the apartment he shares with his mother, Marina. "The building suffered because it is on the front line. Across the street, there were Russians trying to take the city," recounts the unemployed mechanic. "In my apartment, there were Ukrainian soldiers, which is why we were heavily shelled. All the walls, all the furniture are riddled with shrapnel!" For several months, he couldn't return home due to the fighting. Today, he insists on staying in his home out of fear of looting. "We're lucky to have electricity, otherwise it would be unbearable," his mother, Marina, remarks. The rest of the family lives with friends in a central neighborhood. "But now that the Russians have been pushed far from the city [the border is 30 kilometers away], they are firing missiles that mostly hit the city center. No one is safe anymore," she grumbles. Further up Natalii-Oujvi Street, the scene is one of devastation. A 16-story building is torn open and threatens to collapse at any moment. No housing is intact, and the neighborhood is so quiet that a closed curtain or a spoken word immediately attracts attention. The fate of each building will be decided on a case-by-case basis. Serhii, 51 years old, is trying to repair the pipes in the basement himself. "Since everyone left last winter, during the fighting, they burst due to freezing. Everything needs to be changed quickly, otherwise the building will be permanently uninhabitable," he explains. No one is currently living there because the power has not been restored. "The situation is getting worse day by day." The situation is more favorable in the building across the street. On the 4th floor, Ihor, 57 years old, proudly shows off the new windows he has just installed. They had all been blown out by explosions. "Now we are waiting for all the residents to do the same, because if there are still holes in the facades, the communal services will not reconnect the central heating or water." It all depends on the occupants of three apartments out of the 42 in the building, of whom there is no news. "They are probably refugees abroad," grumbles Vladimir Mikhailovich, the building's syndic manager. The prospect of a winter without heating or electricity is swelling the ranks of Ukrainians displaced by the conflict. In Kupiansk, a medium-sized city located 120 kilometers east of Kharkiv, evacuation buses were boarding a few dozen destitute people towards the west of the country on Tuesday. "Here, the situation is worsening day by day. At home, we have no electricity, no water, no gas," says Alexandre, a 43-year-old farm worker. "On February 24, the Russians took the city without a fight. Then the Ukrainians took the city back without a fight a month ago. Kupiansk was intact then. But now, every day, the Russians are destroying the city with their artillery. What is the point of this revenge?" Russian forces are only 15 kilometers from Kupiansk and the artillery frequently thunders. "The conditions are too dangerous for us to work on repairing the lines. The residents scold me every day, but I can't do anything," laments Mykola Koulba, a municipal services employee. Day after day, the Russian army's onslaught is slowly but surely pushing Ukraine towards darkness.

title: In Kyiv, drone attacks have killed at least four people.
lead: "The terror must lose, and it will lose," declared the Ukrainian president, Volodymyr Zelensky.
text_eng: The day had not yet dawned when the first explosion was heard in downtown Kiev. Drone attacks struck the Ukrainian capital on Monday, October 17, killing at least four people. A residential building was hit, as emphasized by the mayor of the capital, Vitali Klitschko. "This is not the first building in our city where people have been killed," he recalled. "Russia is a terrorist country," repeated the official, while rescue teams continued to clear the rubble to find potential victims of the strikes. President Volodymyr Zelensky denounced on social media an enemy who "acts surreptitiously, kills civilians, targets homes, and infrastructure." "Terror must lose, and it will lose," declared the Ukrainian president, specifying that a "young family expecting a child" was among the victims. In Kiev, according to Ukrainian rescue services, a kamikaze drone also hit a nine-story administrative building. Besides the four dead, there are currently 3 injured. The Russian army, for its part, congratulated itself on hitting all its targets with "high-precision weapons." Four other deaths in the Sumy region Several strikes targeted crucial infrastructure in three regions, including the capital, leaving "hundreds of localities" without electricity, as stated by Ukrainian Prime Minister Denys Chmyhal, mentioning "five drone strikes" of presumed Iranian origin on Kiev and "missile attacks" on the Dnipro (Central-East) and Sumy (North-East) regions. In the latter, an administrative building was destroyed by a missile strike targeting a power transformer. Shortly after midday, the death toll was four, while three people were rescued from the rubble. In the Dnipro region, a cruise missile strike on a transformer injured one person. The Ukrainian nuclear operator, Energoatom, announced that the Zaporizhia nuclear power plant (South), at the center of concerns for several months, had to be powered by its backup diesel generators to cool its reactors after being disconnected from the grid by Russian bombings.

title: Andreï Kourkov, writer: "Ukrainians have stopped believing in a happy end in this war."
lead: The Ukrainian author believes, in an interview with "Le Monde", that a potential peace agreement or ceasefire will not mark the end of the conflict.
text_eng: The Ukrainian writer Andrey Kurkov, 63 years old, author of novels translated worldwide such as "The Penguin" or "The Gray Bees" (Liana Levi, 2000 and 2022), is also a knowledgeable commentator on current events in Ukraine (Journal of Maidan, Liana Levi, 2014) and the Russo-Ukrainian conflict, which he chronicles in a war journal. The second volume, "Our Daily War," was released in early February in French (Noir sur blanc, 256 p., 22.50 euros). Through his writings and public interventions, he has become one of the voices of Ukraine abroad. On February 24, 2022, Russia began its military invasion of Ukraine, a major turning point in this armed conflict that has been ongoing for eleven years. What assessment do you make of these past three years? These have been the most tragic years of independent Ukraine. It is the second time in its history that the very existence of the country has been challenged by Russia, with the same violence as during the war of independence from 1917 to 1921. The difference is that this time, Ukraine is much more united and stronger, and the world supports its independence. How has the war changed your life? Everything has changed. Material things no longer have value. I feel like a nomad. I feel at home here in Kyiv, but everything I own belongs to the war, to the unknown. Almost all my texts are about the war, almost all my thoughts revolve around the war. I have lost part of my sense of humor. I have accepted violence against human beings as almost natural. My next novel, for example, contains a scene that is so violent that I could not have written it before the war, without the war. I had limits, self-censorship. I avoided using violence in my texts, finding it neither pleasant nor acceptable. It is now acceptable in literature. Your novels are imbued with irony. In their mockery of the Russian invasion, Ukrainians have also been noted for their sense of humor. How can humor survive the war? Humor will survive, I am sure, but in a different way. Personally, I may have become more cynical. My humor has become darker. In Ukraine, in 2022, there was a lot of humor, especially from the soldiers, as in those videos recorded between battles. They did it to motivate the population, their friends. This ended in 2023, with the disappearance of optimism, the feeling of euphoria, and the idea that everything would end quickly and well for Ukraine. Some fear that the unity and spirit of resistance displayed by Ukrainians are starting to fade... People are not as strong as they were at the beginning of the war. The spirit of resistance is still there, but Ukrainians are tired and traumatized. Some are now looking for enemies within, not on the front lines. Russia is participating in this division, with its disinformation campaigns. On social media, the most active accounts regarding the war are fake, Russian ones. In the first installment of your war journal, "Journal of an Invasion" (Noir sur blanc, 2023), you wrote that Ukrainians are "programmed to conquer, be happy, survive the most difficult circumstances, to love life." Are you still optimistic? I am optimistic, but the question of survival is no longer the same. Soldiers realize that, for others than themselves, survival no longer means joining the fight, but rather hiding. At the beginning of the war, the majority of Ukrainians were not thinking about their personal survival. They behaved as the owners of the country: if you belong to a land and that land belongs to you, you must defend it. Today, there are no longer volunteers to join the army; there are conscripts who accept the situation, and other conscripts who are not happy. This is the consequence of the end of a belief, for many, in a happy ending. Faced with Russia continuing to advance and send more soldiers, some think that we cannot win the war. How do you write about war? Are we seeing the emergence of a war literature in Ukraine? This literature has always existed, but the best novels about wars are usually written after the fighting ends. Today, we have journals, essays, memoirs. Between 300 and 400 books have been written by soldiers about the war in Donbass since 2014 – often weak from a literary point of view, but very sincere. There are also professional writers who have become soldiers, like Artem Chapeye, Markiyan Kamysh, or Oleksandr Mykhed, whose writings are highly regarded. I believe they will write good novels after the war. As for me, I cannot imagine writing fiction about the current war at the moment. For my novels, I delve into the events of 1917 to 1921, with historical material, which is easier. I think I understand the events and people of today, but inventing characters when real people are experiencing real situations would be too heavy a responsibility. What has the war changed in Ukrainian culture? Over a hundred writers, poets, and translators have been killed. This means that many books that should have been written will never exist. It's the third year passing almost without a new Ukrainian novel. And more and more Ukrainians are reading translated foreign novels. I think they are also tired of reading Ukrainian non-fiction literature about the war. In bookstores, I notice they stay away from it. There is already too much war in reality, in the news, in conversations. Ukrainian of Russian-speaking origin, now writing in both languages – fiction in Russian and non-fiction in Ukrainian – what is your view on the debate surrounding the use of the Russian language in Ukraine? In public discourse, it is no longer possible to use the Russian language without being criticized, or even labeled as a traitor. However, it still exists in private life and on the streets. Currently, bookstores no longer sell my books published in Russian. It is not a legal ban, but rather the fear that an activist might cause a scandal and demand a boycott of their bookstore. Russian books – including mine – are sold at book markets or online. That being said, even before the war, my novels had larger print runs in Ukrainian translation than in the original Russian version. I accept this situation because the Russian language was used by [Russian President, Vladimir] Putin as one of the reasons for the aggression against Ukraine. The paradox is that after the war, there will be far fewer Russian speakers in this country, as many of them were in the Eastern and Southern regions, and many have been killed by the Russian army or have fled abroad. After the war, we will have discussions about Russian-language culture in Ukraine, and there will obviously be people who will fight against the use of the Russian language. More generally, how do you experience the rejection of Russian culture in Ukraine? Russian culture has always been instrumentalized – at least since the beginning of the Soviet Union – by the political power. The idea was to russify Ukraine through cultural means, with monuments glorifying Russian writers, and later with television. The country had more than 800 streets named after [Alexander] Pushkin [1799-1837], while none bore the name of Ukrainian writers from the 1920s-1930s, the majority of whom were killed by Stalin. Today, in territories occupied by the Russian army, Ukrainian books are destroyed and replaced by Russian books in libraries. In front of the ruins of the Mariupol theater, the Russian army displayed portraits of Pushkin, [Fyodor] Dostoevsky [1821-1881], and [Leo] Tolstoy [1828-1910]. It is war through culture. This wall between Ukrainian and Russian cultures will exist for a long time, perhaps for decades. A Ukrainian does not see the difference between Russian culture and Russian politics. Do you think Russia could one day accept Ukraine's independence? Putin, no, but post-Putin Russia, perhaps. This is Putin's last war, he does not want to lose it. I think he suffers greatly from the lack of victory. In Ukraine, there is talk of the need for a "victory" against Russia. In your opinion, what would constitute a victory? Victory is for Ukraine to remain independent. She currently does not have the means to free all the occupied and annexed territories, but she cannot accept, nor legitimize, a change in borders. If there is a peace agreement or ceasefire, it will come with temporary conditions, it will not mark the end of the conflict. It will only work until the next agreement, or the next war. Are you afraid of a war that could last a long time? I don't see the energy, neither in Russia nor in Ukraine, to continue a war of such intensity for years. But Putin does not seem to want to end it. As for the United States, is [President Donald] Trump capable of stopping the war? It's not clear. How do you imagine peace? The silence during the night, without the sound of sirens. No people mobilizing others in the streets. Many people in cafes, drinking beer and vodka. And maybe, especially in villages, more people in churches than before, praying for the peace to continue [he laughs]. But, after the war, Ukraine will have to maintain a huge army to survive. Society will be different, much more militant and militarized than before. There will be political tensions and violence. Ukraine will lose part of its traditional tolerance.

title: Jeanette Vizguerra, a figure in the "sanctuary cities" movement in the United States, has been arrested by immigration police for deportation.
lead: This Mexican woman, mother of four children, three of whom were born in the United States, had been living in Colorado for thirty years.
text_eng: She had become the face of the church and "sanctuary city" movement, institutions that defend migrants. Jeanette Vizguerra, 53 years old, was arrested on Monday, March 17, by immigration police in Denver, Colorado, and transferred to a detention center, from where U.S. authorities intend to deport her from the country where she has lived for nearly thirty years. A mother of four children, three of whom were born in the United States, the migrant rights activist was arrested in front of the Target store where she was making deliveries. Originally from Mexico and arriving in 1997, Jeanette Vizguerra had been subject to a deportation order since she was arrested for a traffic violation in Denver in 2009, and found in possession of a fake social security card, a document necessary for employment. In 2017, after Donald Trump came into power, she sought refuge in a church in Denver to evade immigration police, who at the time refrained from pursuing undocumented immigrants in "protected" areas such as places of worship, schools, or hospitals. This exception was revoked by Donald Trump upon his return to the White House on January 20. A symbol of resilience in the Latino community, the voice for undocumented immigrants had been named one of Time magazine's 100 most influential people of 2017. After two years of confinement in a church basement, she had obtained a suspension of her deportation order thanks to the intervention of local officials. This suspension, renewed several times, had expired in February 2024. "We finally got you" About a hundred protesters gathered on Tuesday outside the Aurora detention center near Denver to denounce the fact that the activist had been targeted directly. Luna Baez, her daughter, stated that the police had celebrated their operation: "We finally got you," they reportedly said. Jeanette Vizguerra often participated in protests outside the center where she is currently detained, holding a sign calling for the dissolution of immigration police. She had helped inform undocumented families of their constitutional right to resist Donald Trump's mass deportation policy. Democratic officials in Colorado protested against the arrest of a migrant who had become a "pillar" of the city's community organizations. Denver's Democratic mayor, Mike Johnston, who was threatened with financial retaliation by the Department of Justice if he did not abandon his sanctuary city policy of not cooperating with federal police, strongly reacted. "This is not an immigration law enforcement issue," he said. "It's a Putin-style persecution of political dissidents." He continued, "This is the big lie of the Trump administration. It's not about security." This is political theater and political retaliation. It does not make this country safer. It plunges it into arbitrariness." Jeanette Vizguerra's lawyers have filed a petition for a writ of habeas corpus with the court to challenge the legality of her detention. In a phone call to her daughter broadcast to the protesters, the voice of the migrant affirmed that she would fight her deportation "all the way to Mexico" if necessary.

title: Ukraine and Europe alone against Russia
lead: The violent altercation that occurred between Donald Trump, J.D. Vance, and Volodymyr Zelensky at the White House on Friday, February 28, illustrates the hostility of the new American administration towards Ukraine and marks a breaking point in the relationship between the allies.
text_eng: Vladimir Putin achieved a remarkable success in his war against Ukraine on Friday, February 28. He achieved this without firing a single missile, without even having to say a word. This success was offered to him by Donald Trump in the Oval Office of the White House in Washington. The Ukrainian president, Volodymyr Zelensky, who came to sign an agreement allowing the United States to exploit the mineral resources of his country in exchange for their military support since the Russian invasion of February 24, 2022, was able to witness the hostility of the new administration. This hostility was illustrated by the unprecedented aggressiveness of an American vice president, J.D. Vance, towards the leader of a democratic country at war with a dictatorship. Bitter exchanges, accusations, threats - never in the history of the United States had differences been so bluntly and publicly exposed between two allies who are drifting further apart. The hasty departure of the Ukrainian president from the White House, apparently asked to leave by his hosts, indicated that a breaking point had been reached. It is difficult to see how this rupture can now be avoided. Since the new direction taken by U.S. diplomacy, which aims to negotiate directly with Moscow for a resolution to this war on terms dictated by Russia, a dispute has been growing between Washington and Kiev, with the main European countries supporting the latter on this issue. This concerns the security guarantees requested from Washington to prevent a fragile peace from turning into a mere respite for the Russian war machine, tested by a conflict it thought it would win in three days, before new offensives aimed at reconstituting what Moscow considers its sphere of influence on European soil. By refusing any commitment on this point, Mr. Trump is increasingly playing into the hands of the Kremlin, adopting, down to the last comma, Moscow's view of the ongoing conflict, including its origins, as shown by the dramatic votes at the United Nations on February 24. During his first term from 2017 to 2021, the Republican consistently embraced the views of the Russian president, regularly praising him. His alignment with Moscow's denials of proven interference in American elections at the Helsinki summit in July 2018 caused consternation, even within his party, which has since become completely silent. Reversal of alliances Outraged by the brutality of Mr. Trump and Mr. Vance on Friday, European leaders quickly expressed their support for Mr. Zelensky, with the notable exception of Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orban, who thanked the American president. However, the ambush set for the Ukrainian leader in the Oval Office only confirms their fears: The transatlantic rift is deep, and the alignment of Mr. Trump's discourse with that of Mr. Putin suggests a shift in alliances. Europe is now alone facing Russia. This is a huge challenge, which will dominate discussions when these European leaders meet in London on Sunday, March 2, with British Prime Minister Keir Starmer. He initially invited them to report on his meetings at the White House on Thursday, February 27, three days after President Emmanuel Macron's, and to draw lessons from the new transatlantic situation. But the tone changed radically between the lavish praise Mr. Trump showered on Thursday, apparently eager to divide the British and Europeans, and the public humiliation of the Ukrainian president the following day. For both Mr. Starmer and Mr. Macron, the time is no longer for charm offensives to try to appease Washington, which has yielded little results. It is necessary to stand together, accelerate the schedule for building a strong European defense, and prepare public opinion for the abrupt shift towards a different world.

title: Donald Trump launches peace negotiations in Ukraine with Vladimir Putin, breaking the unity of the allies.
lead: Without consulting with European leaders, the American president held lengthy discussions on Wednesday with the Kremlin leader. He also briefly called Volodymyr Zelensky, who is set to meet on Friday in Munich with an American delegation led by Vice President J.D. Vance.
text_eng: In diplomacy, choreography is never a secondary matter. It allows for the staging of power dynamics. The way Donald Trump approached the war in Ukraine on Wednesday, February 12, is significant: he sees it as a bilateral issue with Moscow, with the sole purpose being peace, however fragile. The American president had previously refused to comment on his phone calls with Vladimir Putin since the transition period. This time, he detailed their morning exchange in a post on his Truth Social network. Only after that did Donald Trump call Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky. Washington thus declares the peace negotiations open, which have already been the focus of confidential exchanges for several weeks. The conversation between Donald Trump and Vladimir Putin lasted about ninety minutes according to the Kremlin; it is the first bilateral contact of this level since Russia's invasion of Ukraine in February 2022. The American president mentioned a few topics discussed: Ukraine, as well as the Middle East, artificial intelligence, energy, the "power of the dollar," and inevitably identified his interlocutor's vanity as a vulnerability. "He used my very strong campaign slogan, that of 'COMMON SENSE'," he rejoiced on Truth Social. The two leaders have decided to "work very closely together," including visiting each other's countries, as Donald Trump specified. In the afternoon, the American president revealed that he could meet his Russian counterpart in Saudi Arabia at an unknown date. The overall tone of the message was very warm towards the Russian president, who is the subject of an arrest warrant from the International Criminal Court for war crimes. "I want to thank President Putin for his time and efforts for this call, and for the release, yesterday, of Marc Fogel," Donald Trump concluded. Sentenced to fourteen years in prison in Russia for drug possession, Marc Fogel was released by the Russian authorities before being triumphantly received at the White House on Tuesday evening. In return, the United States agreed to release Alexander Vinnik, a Russian convicted of cryptocurrency-related crimes. Donald Trump sees this exchange as the possible beginning of a virtuous bilateral cycle. Vladimir Putin, on the other hand, demonstrates his skill here, ready to compromise on the trivial while focusing on the essential: preventing a democratic and liberal development of Ukraine, tied to the West. Unlike his American counterpart, the Russian president does not need a positive outcome in these negotiations. But the holding of these talks contributes to his diplomatic detoxification. American red lines, all to the disadvantage of Kiev. After his meeting with Volodymyr Zelensky, Donald Trump made a shorter comment. "The conversation went very well," he said. Like President Putin, he wants to make peace. For now, the American president has not publicly expressed clear support for the Ukrainians. He has emphasized the need to somehow repay the efforts already made by Washington, by using Ukrainian rare earth lands as collateral, rich in metals. The first important stage of negotiations will take place in Munich (Germany) on February 14. The Ukrainian president will meet with Vice President J.D. Vance and Secretary of State Marco Rubio on the sidelines of the annual security conference. The Secretary of State is part of the team designated by Donald Trump to negotiate with Moscow. The president also mentioned his national security advisor, Mike Waltz, CIA director John Ratcliffe, but not General Keith Kellogg, who was appointed as a special envoy on this issue. This demotion is accompanied by a promotion for Steven Witkoff, a real estate entrepreneur and golf partner of Donald Trump. He, who was involved in negotiations on Gaza, is now also taking charge of negotiations on Ukraine, a subject he knows little about. According to conservative Fox News host Sean Hannity, the chief propagandist of Trumpism on the network, Steven Witkoff reportedly met with Vladimir Putin for over three hours in Moscow, on the sidelines of his trip to retrieve Marc Fogel. This meeting has not been officially confirmed. In Brussels, U.S. Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth bluntly outlined the Trump administration's red lines, all unfavorable to Kiev. "The United States does not believe that Ukraine's accession to NATO is a realistic outcome for a negotiated settlement," he said. This is a complete continuity with the Biden administration. The Democratic president, influenced by the Cold War, feared above all a nuclear escalation with Russia. In an interview with Time magazine in June 2024, he said the following about resolving the conflict: "Peace looks like making sure that Russia never, ever, ever, ever occupies Ukraine again. And that doesn't mean NATO, that they become part of NATO, it means we have a relationship with them like with other countries, where we provide weapons so they can defend themselves in the future." Sense of urgency from the United States The second red line outlined by Mr. Hegseth is the refusal of any American deployment in Ukraine. He mentioned "European and non-European troops" sent as part of a "non-NATO mission." The third red line is to consider it an "unrealistic goal" to return to the pre-2014 Ukrainian borders. Once again, these two points were shared by the Biden administration. The huge difference lies in their statements at the start of the negotiations. The United States immediately forfeits any leverage by stating what will not happen, without obtaining any concessions from the Russian aggressor. The only remaining argument is the net of sanctions still in place against Moscow. This hasty approach gives a sense of rush on the part of the United States. Donald Trump believes it is possible to achieve a ceasefire in the "not too distant future." He does not question the principle of supporting Ukraine, but rather the financial conditions. "We will continue as much as we need to," said the president. Behind these supposedly balanced statements, Wednesday represents a major turning point. "Any agreement negotiated from a position of weakness will be exploited by the Kremlin," notes Maria Snegovaya, an expert at the Center for Strategic and International Studies in Washington. It will not hold in the long term, as Putin's essential goals remain unchanged: total control over Ukraine and a revision of post-Cold War borders. Such an agreement could increase the risk of a broader war in the future. No room for Europeans in the negotiations Through this individual approach, Donald Trump breaks the united front presented by Western countries since the beginning of the war. The new American president does not foresee any role for Europeans in the negotiations: he treats them like a master with a metal ruler, correcting them for their insufficient level of security spending, inventing fictitious amounts. But the most serious rupture is with a cardinal principle of the Biden administration: "Nothing about Ukraine without Ukraine." The Democratic president did not want an agreement behind the victim's back, but he also refused to publicly discuss a realistic end to the conflict, giving the impression of a war without a solution. Donald Trump, on the other hand, abruptly discards this collective approach, hostile to the aggressor. He takes the risk of replacing it with a dictate in Russian, or a parody of a temporary ceasefire.

title: Liubov Tsybulska, Ukrainian specialist on Russian disinformation: "For the Russians, the dream comes true: an American president repeats their propaganda"
lead: The director of the Ukrainian NGO Join Ukraine, and former director of the Center for Strategic Communications and Information Security, described to "Le Monde" the "huge shock" of Ukrainians upon hearing Donald Trump deliver the same speech as the Russians.
text_eng: Liubov Tsybulska, a specialist in Russian disinformation, is the director of the Ukrainian NGO Join Ukraine, and the former director of the Center for Strategic Communications and Information Security in Kiev. With a tired voice after another night punctuated by two waves of Russian drone attacks on the capital, she describes the "huge shock" felt by Ukrainians upon hearing the United States under Donald Trump echoing the same discourse as the Russians regarding the war in Ukraine, and the damage that this discourse causes. Donald Trump has accused Ukraine of starting the war, called Volodymyr Zelensky a "dictator," and claimed that the Ukrainian president's approval rating was 4% (when in reality it is over 57%). How are Ukrainians reacting to these false claims from the new American administration? It's a huge shock. What's frightening is that it's not just Russia and its allies spreading disinformation, but now the United States, which used to be our strongest ally. Today, the president of the most powerful democracy is repeating Russian propaganda. How can Trump say that Ukraine attacked Russia? In the U.S., facts are now being replaced by lies and confusion. We have been attacked by Russia, we know it is our enemy. But being attacked by an American president is very new to us. Trump goes even further than Vladimir Putin, who claims that Zelensky is "illegitimate" but has not called him a "dictator." When a country that shares your values on democracy, freedom, and human rights repeats Russian lies and spreads even more extreme narratives, it is truly shocking. For the Russians, the dream is coming true: an American president is echoing their propaganda. What are the consequences of this discourse? Disinformation has significantly increased because the statements of the U.S. government, which amplify Russian propaganda, are much more widely covered in the Western world. It also has a very demoralizing effect on Ukrainians. Today, the phrase I hear most often in Ukraine is, "We woke up in a new world, without rules." Has Russian propaganda evolved since Donald Trump's return to the White House? It has remained fairly consistent overall. But in recent weeks, it has been emphasizing that Russia's allies are strong and Ukraine is weak. There is also a sense that Russia has warmer relations with the United States because it is not being criticized, whereas under [former U.S. president] Joe Biden, the criticisms were constant. Russian propaganda also ridicules our government, insists that elections need to be held, and claims that Ukraine does not want peace. This is false, of course. We are ready to negotiate, but we want real peace and to be involved in the process. We have already had ceasefires, with the Minsk agreements 1 [in 2014] and then Minsk 2 [in 2015]. The Russians have always violated them. Believe us, we know Russia, it never keeps its promises. Never. But the worst thing in these political and geopolitical negotiations is that we forget the essential: the fact that Ukraine is a sovereign and independent country, that the Russians have killed and tortured countless Ukrainians, and that thousands of children have been kidnapped and forcibly taken to Russia. It is very important that political leaders and the media do not lose sight of this in the discussions. How does Russian propaganda refer to the ceasefire supported by Trump? There is not a single indicator showing that Russia wants a ceasefire or peace negotiations. They say they want to take as much territory as possible. They send tons of drones and missiles to Ukraine, because it is cheap and effective [Russia launched 267 drones against Ukraine on February 23, a record since the start of the invasion]. They send drones with and without explosive charges to exhaust our anti-aircraft defense, including the Patriot interceptors provided by the United States, because they know we are running out of this missile system. And now, the United States may completely stop supplying us with them. The Russians want to wear us down until we demand an end to hostilities in any way possible. In Kiev, there is not a single night without drone attacks. Are those who supported you in the United States speaking out today? We feel they are a bit quiet at the moment, although a few voices stand out. It will probably take them some time to unite against what Trump is saying. We make a distinction between the American people on one hand, and Elon Musk and Donald Trump on the other. We are very grateful to the American people. From the first day of the Russian aggression, we knew they were on our side. What is ironic is that Russian propaganda has been accusing us of being Nazis for years - which is false, as Ukrainian far-right movements have never succeeded in entering Parliament. And today, senior American leaders are spreading far-right rhetoric and making Nazi gestures, but Russia says nothing... Do the Russians say they want to attack other countries in Europe? We have been monitoring their propaganda for a long time. It's no secret: the Russians openly say they will not stop there, and mention Poland and the Baltic countries as next targets. They also say a lot of negative things about Germany. The moment is crucial. The whole of Europe must be very cautious and more united than ever in the face of this strange alliance between the United States and Russia. Our values and security are threatened. If Russia succeeds in Ukraine, what would prevent it from going further? The Russians have been building up their military capabilities for years, especially in the last three years. The nature of war has changed significantly since the beginning of the invasion in February 2022. It has become much deadlier, largely due to the widespread use of drones capable of destroying tanks. Europe may not be fully aware and prepared to face this threat. What is the mindset of Ukrainians today? Beyond the shock caused by Trump's speech, the prevailing idea in Ukrainian society is that no one can force us to give up our land. Those who have no idea about this war cannot decide our fate. It is important to have this narrative within the country, as it affects how we fight. We understand that we will fight for as long as it takes, until the last drop of blood, because there is nothing worse than the rule of the Russians. Because they kill, torture, and violate, and it is better to fight than to live under their occupation.

title: Germany in shock over the loss of the American friend
lead: Foundation of the reconstruction of West Germany after the Second World War, the country's alliance with the United States had become an almost identitary element for many Germans, who view the geopolitical shifts of the Trump administration as a seismic event.
text_eng: Within the grounds of the former Tempelhof airport in Berlin, an old propeller plane commemorates a crucial moment in German history. It recalls the Berlin Airlift (1948-1949), eleven months during which American planes of this type supplied West Berlin, whose communication routes were blocked by the Soviets. These aircraft were affectionately nicknamed Rosinenbomber ("Raisin Bombers") by Berliners, as they dropped thousands of candies over the city for the children until the blockade was lifted. For many Germans, these planes symbolize the friendship and freedom guaranteed by the United States against dictatorship. Did the elders think back to this moving event when they heard J.D. Vance's speech at the Munich Security Conference on February 14, nine days before the legislative elections where the far-right party Alternative for Germany (AfD) is credited with 20% of the votes? In a few sentences, the American vice president trampled on one of the founding pillars of the Federal Republic of Germany: the transatlantic relationship. "The threat that concerns me most regarding Europe is not Russia, not China, not an external actor. It is the threat from within," he declared, accusing Europe of abandoning the values of freedom of expression and democracy, and calling for the abandonment of any cordon sanitaire against far-right groups. In Munich, he met not with the chancellor, Olaf Scholz, but with the AfD president, Alice Weidel, who had already been endorsed in January by Donald Trump's billionaire advisor, Elon Musk. The violent attack by the American president on his Ukrainian counterpart, Volodymyr Zelensky, on Wednesday, February 19, sealed the rupture. For Germany, this represents a deeper turning point than the Russian attack on Ukraine in February 2022, which marked the return of war to Europe and shattered its conception of international trade as a guarantor of stability. The link with the United States is of a different nature. It is a fundamental marker of modern Germany, one that imposed democracy and the rule of law against the Nazi horror. Any leading politician, especially within the Christian Democratic Union (CDU-CSU) party, must reaffirm their commitment to the transatlantic relationship. They may even have been close to one of the associations that embody it, such as the influential Atlantik-Brücke ("Atlantic Bridge"), founded in 1952 and chaired between 2009 and 2019 by a certain Friedrich Merz (CDU), a favorite for the position of chancellor after the elections. The Americans played a major role in the political, economic, and institutional reconstruction of Germany after World War II. They organized the denazification process of institutions and imposed the country's attachment to the North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO). They inspired the major principles of the Basic Law (1949), such as respect for human rights protected by a Supreme Court, the separation and balance of powers, and federalism. They made a significant contribution to the monetary reform of 1948, which introduced the Deutsche Mark. The German "economic miracle" would have been inconceivable without the monetary stability on which the Marshall Plan was implemented. Discredited in terms of military power, West German identity was largely built on the economic and monetary strength resulting from these transformations. Neither a friend nor a model, this identity is marked by very strong memories and bilateral ties. On the town hall of the Berlin district of Schöneberg, a plaque commemorates the day in June 1963 when President John F. Kennedy shouted, "Ich bin ein Berliner" ("I am a Berliner"), in support of the population. These words echo Ronald Reagan's speech in 1987, when he addressed Soviet leader Mikhail Gorbachev with, "Tear down this wall!", considered a herald of the end of the Cold War and reunification. American intellectual influence on economic theory, especially in the 1990s, is considerable. The prestigious University of Bonn, the Free University of Berlin, and those in Munich developed based on the model of major universities in the United States, which are essential for many high-level careers in research and higher education in Germany. "What we are experiencing is an absolute break. The entire post-World War II order is being buried," notes economist Jens Südekum, a professor at the University of Düsseldorf. "Without America?" read the headline of Die Zeit weekly on its Thursday, February 20 edition. "The Western Schism," declared one of the directors of the Frankfurter Allgemeine Zeitung, the major Frankfurt newspaper, in an editorial on the same day. Germany, which had delegated its defense to Washington and greatly benefited from the "peace dividends" after 1990, is now in dire straits. Chancellor Scholz's restraint on the Ukrainian issue, justified by respect for American precedence on security matters within NATO, is no longer sustainable. In Berlin, the government resulting from the elections on Sunday, February 23, will have to react very quickly. The debt brake, a constitutional rule limiting the public deficit to 0.35% of the country's gross domestic product, on which the outgoing coalition foundered, seems outdated given the enormous expenses required for security. But the most significant change is likely cultural. For the first time in eighty years, America can no longer be considered either a friend or a model. Berlin will have to urgently redefine, with European partners who wish to do so, France at the forefront, the foundations of its strategic autonomy.

title: Giorgia Meloni, caught in her contradictions on the crisis of the transatlantic relationship, benefits from the divisions within the opposition.
lead: The President of the Italian Council is providing measured support to Ukraine and the European rearmament plan while also expressing support for the Trump administration. In her country, she must deal with a pacifist movement that affects different parties, including those in her coalition.
text_eng: Caught in a delicate position on European defense and the transatlantic crisis, the Italian Prime Minister, Giorgia Meloni, escaped through a controversial statement on Wednesday, March 19, attacking a founding text of anti-fascism and the European idea. While presenting to the Chamber of Deputies the position Italy will defend at a European Council dominated by defense issues on Thursday, the far-right executive leader harshly criticized the supposed support of the opposition for the "Ventotene Manifesto" for European unity. "I don't know if this is your vision of Europe, but it certainly isn't mine," Giorgia Meloni concluded her speech as chaotic outcries came from the left benches before a suspension of the session. The executive leader was referring to a text written in 1941 by the anti-fascists Ernesto Rossi and Altiero Spinelli while they were under house arrest by the Mussolini regime on the small island that gave its name to the manifesto. Previously, the Prime Minister had quoted passages out of context, implying that the European unity advocated in Ventotene and presented as a reference by her opponents had an authoritarian content and was hostile to private property. By accusing her adversaries of aligning themselves with dogmatic and liberty-restricting Europeanism, she omitted to mention the parts of the manifesto dedicated to criticizing totalitarianism, doctrinaire communism, economic oligarchies, and racism. These were all issues that, according to her, should be banned from a united Europe with a common armed force that its authors hoped would emerge after the war and the defeat of fascist and Nazi regimes. One of the two authors, Altiero Spinelli (1907-1986), who was a member of the European Commission from 1970 to 1976, is still considered one of the great European figures offered by Italy to the continent, although his resolutely federalist vision was sidelined. "Giorgia Meloni's statements represent the new guise of Euroscepticism: saying that one is for a Europe that is not that of the founders," accused her fellow countryman, the MEP (Renew Europe) Sandro Gozi. The Prime Minister thus chose to discredit two of the early supporters of European unity at the end of an intervention dedicated precisely to Europe, which echoed the substance of her speech the day before in the Senate. She had then endeavored to defend, on a tightrope, her positioning in an international power field disrupted by the arrival of the American President, Donald Trump. Rejecting any decoupling with Washington First, Giorgia Meloni reiterated her support for Ukraine, albeit in a more discreet manner since the deterioration of relations between Kiev and Washington. She also defended in a very nuanced way her support for the European Commission's €800 billion rearmament plan, describing it as "grandiose compared to the nature of what is proposed." Her solution for Ukraine's security also involves a hypothetical extension of Article 5 of the North Atlantic Treaty without integrating Kiev into the Atlantic Alliance. This provision states that an attack on one of the signatories of the treaty should be considered as targeting all others. However, the Italian leader once again praised the efforts of the Trump administration for peace in Ukraine, rejecting any tendency towards transatlantic decoupling and repeating her criticisms of the possible deployment of European troops in the country. While emphasizing the necessary unity of the West. Giorgia Meloni, the only European leader invited to the inauguration of the American president on January 20, declared her adherence to the speech given in Munich by Vice President J.D. Vance, denounced as a direct attack on the European democratic model. The council president also cultivates her friendship with the oligarch Elon Musk, a key figure in Trumpism in Washington, with whom she shares some ideas. As she reiterated on Wednesday, she still believes in her role as a bridge between the two sides of the Atlantic, refraining from any criticism of Washington, whether on the plans for the annexation of Greenland or on tariffs. However, this positioning has not yet allowed her to be highlighted by Donald Trump, despite the promising start of their relationship. "Giorgia Meloni continues to see Donald Trump as a better ally than anyone in Europe. She wants to maintain her privileged relationship with him and believes that over time other countries will rally to her line, and above all, she wants to give reassurances to her League allies who try to be more Trumpist than her," summarizes Lorenzo Castellani, a political science researcher at Luiss University in Rome. The council's vice president, Matteo Salvini, leader of the League, an extreme right-wing party and a pillar of the coalition, and his troops maintain a constant escalation in favor of Donald Trump and Vladimir Putin, portrayed as working alone for peace against a Europe they consider bellicose and firmly reject rearmament. His stance is close to that of Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orban, whom he met on Wednesday in Brussels along with the French representatives of the National Rally, Marine Le Pen and Jordan Bardella. In Italy, a pacifist rhetoric rooted in public opinion. However, Matteo Salvini's stance contrasts with the more pro-European positions of Antonio Tajani, the leader of another party in the ruling coalition, Forza Italia (center-right). Tajani aims to stay close to the line of the European People's Party, currently dominated by the Christian Democratic Union (CDU) of Friedrich Merz, the likely future German chancellor, who advocates for the "independence" of the European Union (EU) from the United States. Tajani does align, however, with some of the opposition's positions, which are against European rearmament, drawing from a deeply ingrained pacifist rhetoric in public opinion that sees war as a scourge that the production of weapons would only perpetuate at the expense of public services. This cross-cutting peace party includes former anti-establishment populists from the Five Star Movement and a faction of the Democratic Party (PD, center-left), which includes the PD's own general secretary, Elly Schlein. Schlein has opposed the EU's rearmament plan, in disagreement with key figures in the movement such as former prime ministers Enrico Letta and Paolo Gentiloni. On March 12, ten out of the 21 PD members of the European Parliament abstained from voting on the Commission's defense policy, distancing themselves from the Progressive Alliance of Socialists and Democrats in the European Parliament group. "A large part of Italians, especially on the left, cannot see Russia as a strategic threat and view the situation in Ukraine through the lens of pacifist idealism," explains PD deputy Lia Quartapelle. The international situation is therefore dividing the opposition. In the absence of a united front from opponents, the majority can still afford to show its own divergences.

title: Vladimir Putin is buying time to assert himself better against Donald Trump.
lead: Moscow manages to impose its pace on Washington, while continuing its offensive against Kiev and making Europe the scapegoat for the failure of negotiations, analyzes journalist Benjamin Quénelle.
text_eng: Frustrated but confident, Donald Trump acknowledged, "It is possible that Russia is dragging its feet." More than two months after his return to the White House, his negotiations for a "peace deal" are bogged down, thwarting his dream of quickly ending three years of war in Ukraine. On the other side, Vladimir Putin's Kremlin, which suddenly raised the idea of placing Ukraine under "transitional administration" under the auspices of the UN to organize a presidential election and negotiate a peace agreement with the new authorities on Friday, March 28, has so far only offered a few false concessions. However, it has reiterated its maximalist positions and imposed its own pace: playing for time to continue advancing on the battlefield and negotiating from a position of strength around the negotiating table. After two and a half hours of telephone conversation between Donald Trump and Vladimir Putin on March 18, and then twelve hours of discussions between their delegations on March 24 in Riyadh, no date has been set for a summit between the two presidents. Above all, there has been no joint statement, a prelude to an agreement. Instead, there have been only separate statements. Each time, Washington trumpets, Moscow tempers. Their statements announce illusory progress. But the analysis of the texts confirms that the content differs. After the phone call between the two presidents, the White House enthusiastically announced a ceasefire on energy infrastructure; the Kremlin quickly reduced this limited cessation of hostilities to thirty days. After the laborious talks between envoys in Riyadh, the United States congratulated themselves on obtaining a truce in the Black Sea; Russia warned that this would only happen after the lifting of Western sanctions that, according to Russia, target the export of Russian cereals and fertilizers. Asymmetric position "In all that is left unsaid, these agreements of appearances hide the reality: Donald Trump did not obtain a total ceasefire. He thought he was the strongest, but Vladimir Putin dominates," summarizes a senior European diplomat well-versed in the negotiations on Ukraine. "Trump does not want peace, he mainly wants to be left alone," he quips, recalling that Washington's priority is no longer the security of Ukraine or Europe, but a general Russian-American rapprochement with lucrative economic contracts. Faced with Donald Trump's inexperienced delegation at this level, Vladimir Putin, on the contrary, relies on old hands in tortuous negotiations: in Riyadh, he first sent Sergey Lavrov, a loyal foreign minister since 2004, then Sergey Besseda, former head of the FSB's fifth service responsible for operations abroad and now an advisor to the director of this organization inherited from the KGB. From Moscow's perspective, everything is under control. Are these talks that are getting bogged down real negotiations to reach a compromise or just discussions serving as a facade to justify an agreement already reached from the start? The two partners are in an asymmetrical position. Washington, not at war with Russia, is negotiating as if representing Ukraine. Acting as both a player, mediator, and judge, Donald Trump seeks less to protect an ally than to glorify himself as an unparalleled peacemaker to better launch his four-year term. Moscow, repeating its ultimatum on "demilitarization" and "denazification" of Ukraine, takes advantage to expand its demands, asking to push NATO back to its 1990 borders, and even mentioning control over Odessa. Chief of the armed forces and chief historian, Vladimir Putin wants a first victory for his May 9 parade on Red Square. But potentially in power until 2036, he has all the time to maintain strategic initiative and impose his scenario. In reality, for now, there is no ceasefire on energy targets or in the Black Sea. On the contrary, with heavy losses, Russian troops are advancing in Donbass as in Kursk, the Russian region partially occupied by the Ukrainian army since August 2024. Its full reconquest is, in the eyes of the Russians, a prerequisite before any ceasefire. On the same day as the negotiations in Riyadh, Moscow launched its missiles on Sumy, a Ukrainian city near Kursk, injuring 101 people, including twenty-three children, according to the city hall. The Kremlin is therefore continuing its offensive. But this does not prevent it from setting its preconditions for a truce. Accustomed to inventing a narrative to justify its ends, it demands the lifting of sanctions on agricultural products and fertilizers that, in reality, do not exist. In fact, it takes advantage to demand that Russian producers, exporters, and insurance companies, but especially the public agricultural bank Rosselkhozbank, regain their reconnection to the international payment system Swift. Clear tactic It is an old request from Moscow since the negotiations in 2022-2023 on the Black Sea grain agreement. These sanctions lifts are impossible to implement unilaterally by the United States without coordination with the Europeans. Especially since Swift is based in Belgium and Russia's exclusion from this network was one of the first measures taken by the European Union (EU) against Moscow in 2022. But Moscow's tactic is clear: to shift the blame for the failure of its negotiations with Washington onto... Europe. Long mocked as a puppet in the hands of the United States, the EU is now targeted as the "enemy number one" by the Kremlin and its media outlets. Excluded from the Russo-American talks, it is accused of being the main "sponsor" of the war in Ukraine. The Kremlin therefore has every reason to claim to be "satisfied" with the renewed dialogue with the White House, "pragmatic and constructive": in his usual strategy of uncertainty, Vladimir Putin is not seeking an agreement, but is using Donald Trump as a tool of manipulation to advance his demands, to the detriment of Europe.

title: Vladimir Putin offers Donald Trump an initial success
lead: The two leaders have agreed to open official negotiations. However, on the topic of Ukraine, the Kremlin leader is reiterating his strict conditions.
text_eng: Since Donald Trump's return to the White House, Moscow had been waiting for a "signal." After the phone call from the American president on Wednesday, February 12, Vladimir Putin can now deploy his usual tactic: charm and patience to assert himself. The Kremlin leader, who had praised Donald Trump's "intelligence" and "pragmatic" character on January 24, is now inviting him to Moscow. The last visit of an American president to the Russian capital was in 2009, when Barack Obama visited; Vladimir Putin was then prime minister. Three years after the start of the Kremlin's "special military operation" in Ukraine, an invasion launched under the presidency of his despised predecessor, Joe Biden, the mere prospect of this trip offers Donald Trump a first success in closing what he called "this ridiculous war." Vladimir Putin had already stated that the "special military operation" could have been avoided if Donald Trump "had been president, if they had not stolen the victory from him in 2020." At the end of their hour and a half conversation on Wednesday, he promised him "peace talks." It will be necessary to "address the root causes of the conflict," as Dmitry Peskov, Kremlin spokesman, emphasized after the meeting. "In fact, nothing has changed fundamentally. The discussion promises to be lengthy. Perhaps even until the summer," warns a Russian observer of Kremlin international politics by phone from Moscow, who prefers to remain anonymous. Putin knows that Trump is pressed for time and wants quick results. Here in Russia, on the contrary, we have all the time in the world." For the first time since the conflict began, a team of American negotiators is officially expected in Moscow. "But the Kremlin's preconditions remain the same," the same source assures, recalling that hopes of reconciliation after Donald Trump's first election in 2016 were quickly dashed. Today, Moscow demands the surrender of Ukraine, its formal renunciation of joining NATO, and control of the occupied Ukrainian territories (the four southern and eastern regions, Crimea annexed in 2014). "Nothing to discuss with London or Brussels." By negotiating directly with Washington, without even a word for the Europeans, Moscow confirms what Nikolai Patrushev, a close associate of Vladimir Putin, had stated on January 14: "Negotiations on Ukraine must take place between Russia and the United States - without the involvement of other Western countries. We have nothing to discuss with London or Brussels," explained the former director of the Federal Security Service, the FSB (one of the successors of the KGB), in a rare interview with Komsomolskaya Pravda, one of the most loyal newspapers to the Kremlin. With Yuri Ushakov, the unwavering official Kremlin advisor for international affairs since 2012, Nikolai Patrushev will be one of the key figures behind the scenes in discussions with the White House. "As soon as the real negotiations begin, Trump will be faced with a fait accompli by Putin. But the White House cannot give in on everything, especially on the Kremlin's ultimate goal: a friendly Ukraine with new leaders," a European diplomat in Moscow confides. "The two presidents may sign a minimal agreement, a military pause allowing the Russian army to strengthen. But the rest of the discussions may fail." Some see this as the Kremlin's tactic: Mr. Trump will have achieved an initial success, but Mr. Putin will have gained time.

title: One year after the death of Alexei Navalny, more than a thousand people gathered at the grave of the former Kremlin opponent.
lead: Charismatic anti-corruption activist and Vladimir Putin's number one political enemy, Mr. Navalny had been declared "extremist" by the Russian justice system before dying in suspicious circumstances in a prison in the Arctic on February 16, 2024.
text_eng: One year after the death in prison of the former main opponent of the Kremlin, Alexei Navalny, more than a thousand of his supporters gathered at his grave in Moscow, at the Borisovo cemetery, on Sunday, February 16, despite the risk of judicial reprisals. According to journalists from Agence France-Presse (AFP) present on site, the security presence was discreet, with mainly plainclothes police officers deployed around the cemetery. Some people wore surgical masks to avoid being recognized. Nevertheless, at least 1,500 people marched on Sunday to lay flowers at Navalny's grave in the Borisovskoye cemetery. By early afternoon, they formed a long line of several hundred people, according to AFP reporters. Other commemorations are planned outside of Russia, announced the exiled collaborators of the deceased opponent. During a brief speech at the cemetery, Alexei Navalny's mother said she wanted the "murderers" of her son to be "punished." "The whole world knows who ordered it. But we want the perpetrators to be known, those who allow this, those who committed it...," added Lyudmila Navalnaya, emotional. Several Western diplomats, including representatives from the American, French, Spanish, Norwegian embassies, and the European Union, also visited Mr. Navalny's grave. In a statement released on Sunday afternoon, the French Ministry of Foreign Affairs reiterated that Paris "holds the Russian authorities fully responsible for the death of Alexei Navalny." France "continues to demand that an independent and thorough investigation be conducted to shed light on the circumstances of his tragic death," the statement also read. A charismatic anti-corruption activist and Vladimir Putin's number one political enemy, Mr. Navalny had been declared "extremist" by the Russian justice system. Publicly mentioning the opponent or his organization, the Anti-Corruption Foundation (FBK), without specifying that they have been deemed "extremist," exposes offenders to severe sanctions. This threat remains in effect despite Alexei Navalny's death in suspicious circumstances in an Arctic prison on February 16, 2024, and despite the exile of almost all of his collaborators. The death of Mr. Navalny, at the age of 47, has still not been fully explained. Russian authorities claim it occurred while he was walking in the prison yard. For the EU, Vladimir Putin and the Russian authorities "bear ultimate responsibility" for Alexei Navalny's death. Mr. Navalny's widow, Yulia Navalnaya, who has taken over the reins of his movement, is set to participate in an event in Berlin, where many Russian opponents reside. "A Russia of the future, free, peaceful, and beautiful, the one Alexei dreamed of, is possible." "Let's do everything to make his dream come true," she called in a video released on Sunday by her team. German Chancellor Olaf Scholz is one of the first Western leaders to have honored his memory on Sunday, praising a man who died "because he fought for democracy and freedom in Russia." The European Union's foreign policy chief, Kaja Kallas, wrote in a statement that Russian President Vladimir Putin and the Russian authorities "bear the ultimate responsibility" for the death of the opposition figure, calling for the "immediate and unconditional release of Alexei Navalny's lawyers and all political prisoners." Telegram channels supportive of the Kremlin had warned supporters of the deceased opposition figure not to go to the cemetery. A message, circulated by these channels, mentions "Big Brother and his ever-watchful eye," with a photograph of a sign revealing the presence of a surveillance camera at the cemetery gates. The Russian authorities have systematically dismantled Alexei Navalny's movement, sending several of his supporters to prison. Four journalists are currently being tried in Russia for "participation in an extremist group," accused of producing footage for Alexei Navalny's team. In January, three of the lawyers defending the opposition figure were sentenced to prison terms ranging from three and a half to five years for transmitting his messages while he was in detention. In Russia, the crackdown has led to hundreds of people being imprisoned and thousands of others being sanctioned or threatened due to their opposition to the government or the conflict in Ukraine. A death with unclear circumstances Mr. Navalny was arrested in January 2021, upon his return to Russia after recovering in Germany following poisoning he had blamed on the Kremlin, which denied the accusation. In December 2023, he was transferred to a remote penal colony beyond the Arctic Circle to serve a nineteen-year prison sentence for "extremism." Repressed in Russia, the Russian opposition is trying to regroup abroad, so far without much success. Yulia Navalnaya and two other prominent opponents organized a march in Berlin in November against the Russian president and the offensive in Ukraine, gathering around 2,000 Russian exiles. The Russian opposition, decapitated by the loss of its leading figure, scattered abroad due to repression in Russia, and torn apart by internal struggles, is in an unprecedented position of weakness. Beyond slogans, the opposition struggles to propose a concrete approach that would lead to the end of the war and the departure of Vladimir Putin. Several scandals within its ranks have also weakened it and caused frustration among some of its activists.

title: "There is an ambiguity between Pope Francis' diplomacy, which is in a sense similar to that of Trump, and the more traditional diplomacy of the Vatican."
lead: In these times of geopolitical shifts, political scientist François Mabille analyzes, in an interview with "Le Monde," the ambiguous diplomacy of a Curia torn between defending international law and the positions of the pope, who chooses to engage in discussions with everyone, including aggressors.
text_eng: François Mabille is a research associate at the Institute of International and Strategic Relations (IRIS) and director of the Geopolitical Observatory of Religion. In his latest work, "The Vatican: The Papacy Facing a World in Crisis" (Eyrolles, 216 pages, 20 euros), he analyzes the role and influence of the Holy See in international relations. In these times of geopolitical upheaval against the backdrop of the war in Ukraine, Pope Francis, who is hospitalized, cannot make his voice heard... Does the Church still have a message to convey to the world? In the traditional vision of the Church, we are all part of one humanity, regardless of borders. Since World War II, the Holy See has strongly advocated for multilateralism and international organizations, including the United Nations. Despite Francis' hospitalization, the Vatican remains active on the Ukrainian issue. Archbishop Paul Gallagher, the Secretary for Relations with States [the Holy See's Minister of Foreign Affairs], recently made two strong statements: in the February 28 issue of America magazine and in a speech at the Organization for Security and Cooperation in Europe (OSCE) on February 25, he reiterated the Vatican's support for a just and lasting peace based on international law. However, this does not entirely align with what Pope Francis has said in the past. When he called for "the courage to raise the white flag" in March 2024, Francis was addressing Ukrainians and not directing his message to the Russians to stop the invasion or to international bodies. This highlights an ambiguity in the Vatican's diplomacy, where the Pope's diplomatic approach, based on media interventions and a call for peace at all costs without considering international law or condemning the aggressor, resembles that of Donald Trump, while the Curia's [Church government] diplomacy is more traditional. According to your analysis, beyond the specific case of Francis, this ambiguity has been a defining feature since the signing of the Lateran Treaty in 1929. Why is this the case? This treaty imposes neutrality on the Vatican in international affairs. But how should this neutrality be understood? Should it be seen as passivity towards the actions of states? Or should it be a strategic neutrality, aligned with those who respect the supposedly neutral rules of international law? Francis seems to lean towards the former option. His diplomacy sometimes appears to be one of complicity, as he chooses to engage in dialogue with everyone, including Vladimir Putin and Bashar al-Assad. In Catholic language, this means that one does not "essentialize" the figure of the adversary, that one always remains open to discussion and to an evolution of positions, including those of the worst dictators. However, professional diplomats, such as Archbishop Gallagher or even Pietro Parolin, the Vatican's Secretary of State, seem to favor a more positive neutrality, defending a certain universal ethics more based on the reminder of international law. The Church must certainly be neutral, but while respecting certain rules. During the Cold War, it was able to distinguish between liberal democracies and the atheistic communist Soviet Union, which it considered a threat. It will now have to question a new hierarchy of its priorities. Despite their strong opposition on nationalism, migration issues, or the environment, do Pope Francis and Donald Trump have any common ground? Absolutely. The world is in the midst of a war of values that also affects the Vatican. Some speeches by J.D. Vance [Vice President of the United States, and Catholic] on gender theory or abortion, typical of American Christian nationalism for years, are very close to the statements of the Pope. This reflects the ambiguity of Catholicism towards societal liberalism. Many Catholics remain attached to the traditional family and the protection of "life," from birth to death, thus rejecting measures such as voluntary termination of pregnancy or euthanasia. On these points, the Pope and the Trump administration are on the same page. Do the South American origins of Francis, the first non-European pope since Gregory III in the 8th century, influence his diplomacy? Yes, definitely. This is reflected in his desire to turn his gaze towards the existential and geographical "peripheries" of the Church and to call on Europeans, and even all countries of the "North," to no longer consider themselves the center of the world. But this approach also has its limits, as it raises the question of the difference between liberal democracies and authoritarian states. Francis, who was influenced by Peronism [a populist movement named after its founder, Juan Domingo Peron, president between 1946 and 1955, and then from 1973 until his death in 1974] from his youth and spent part of his career under the Argentine military dictatorship, does not always seem to naturally lean towards the former, unlike perhaps his predecessors. He also does not seem as sensitive, as a European might be, to the issues that structured the 20th century. In his autobiographies, when he mentions Nazism or World War II, it remains somewhat intellectually weak. He recounts how his family cried tears of joy when they learned of the return of peace in 1945. But there is no reflection on evil itself, on its causes and how to guard against it. I am not sure if the Pope has grasped the tragic sense of history. He has some knowledge of international relations, well-established pacifist convictions, and a constant willingness to promote dialogue. But perhaps this prevents him from understanding what is really happening in international relations, and grasping the problem posed by authoritarian regimes like those of Bashar Al-Assad or Vladimir Putin.

title: In Jerusalem, Jordan Bardella joins the reactionary international and seeks normalization.
lead: Traveling to Israel on March 26 and 27, the president of the National Rally participated in a conference on anti-Semitism where Benjamin Netanyahu and many supporters of Donald Trump were present.
text_eng: The advantage of exhaling sulfur vapors: after having, by his mere presence, caused the withdrawal of a majority of participants, Jordan Bardella became, de facto, the star of a conference on antisemitism reduced to a gathering of pillars of Trumpism, European far-right parties aspiring to power or having left it, and influencers supportive of the policies of the government of Benjamin Netanyahu. All representative organizations of the Jewish community in Europe and around the world had boycotted this meeting, as had the Israeli president, the Labor Party member Isaac Herzog, distancing himself as best he could from an initiative with which he was initially associated. But Jordan Bardella found in Jerusalem, during his visit on Wednesday, March 26 and Thursday, March 27, what he had come for: a "kosher stamp," in the words of Ariel Muzicant, president of the European Jewish Congress. Did he not shake hands with the Prime Minister of the Israeli state, like all the honored guests seated in the front row of this conference? Did he not meet, on Thursday morning, the President of the Knesset, the Israeli Parliament, or benefit from a private visit to Yad Vashem, the memorial for the victims of the Holocaust? For the president of the National Rally (RN), the matter is settled: this "historic" visit absolves the party of all its sins, which he does not like to dwell on, and exempts him from any effort to eradicate the persistent anti-Semitic stereotypes in his electorate, as shown each year by the survey of the National Consultative Commission on Human Rights. "I understand the eminent symbolic significance of this invitation (...), but also its responsibility in the face of history in the relentless war against barbarism," said Jordan Bardella in a speech devoid of any reflection on the history of his movement. Common obsessions Branded as a trophy for two days by the Minister of Diaspora Affairs Amichai Chikli, organizer of the conference, the young man believes he owes his invitation to Israel to the flattering remarks of the Klarsfeld family about the RN, to his departure from the CPAC (Conservative Political Action Conference, a forum for American conservatives) in February after a Nazi salute by former Trump advisor Steve Bannon, or to the official rapprochement between the Likud, the ruling party, and the structure led by the RN at the European level. The conference of the day mainly showed that this invitation was another step in the isolation of the Israeli government, and in the formation of a reactionary international around common enemies: Islamism and the left, which would eventually merge into one. From a panel discussion on the Muslim Brotherhood in an adjacent room, in which Marion Maréchal participates in front of about thirty people, to the closing speech of Mr. Netanyahu was warmly applauded, and the conservative sphere on both sides of the Atlantic was able to develop its common obsessions: immigration from the Muslim world, necessarily driven by imperialist intentions and inherently anti-Semitic; mainstream media, always dishonest in their presentation of the Israeli-Palestinian conflict; Supreme Courts, hindrances to governance; weakness, a flaw of the European Union which is contrasted with the power embodied by Donald Trump or Benjamin Netanyahu.
