On stage or interviewed by Le Monde, many participants compare immigrants from Arab countries to activists conquering Europe, with left-wing activists being seen as "useful idiots." "We must awaken European society," argues Spanish Vox (far-right) MEP Hermann Tertsch. "Here, people have understood: they had become a bit sleepy, Europeanized, but October 7 woke them up dramatically." Donald Trump's offensive against pro-Palestinian activists in American universities, and the cessation of their funding, is seen as a model to follow. "It's nice to light up the Eiffel Tower after attacks, but it's far from enough," attacks Mr. Chikli, praising the effectiveness of the Trump administration compared to his predecessor, Democrat Joe Biden. In 2017, Trump fulfilled a historic demand of Israeli nationalists by recognizing Jerusalem as the capital of Israel. Thus David Friedman, former U.S. ambassador, considers, in a friendly manner, that the war will only be won when "the Gazans have lost their land," which has become "uninhabitable" anyway. Or Matt Schlapp, president of CPAC and participant in a panel discussion presented by his wife, a former Trump administration communicator: "With his re-election, we have given our civilization a second chance." "Building stronger ties" In what is sometimes presented as a clash of civilizations, bridges are being built from Jerusalem to Buenos Aires, from Washington to Budapest or Rome - although the governments of Mr. Milei, Trump, Orban, or Meloni are not represented on this day. Having gravitated in these circles for three years, Jordan Bardella has become a full-fledged figure in this conservative national alliance with illiberal tendencies. "Globalists have always been more united than conservatives," believes Romanian MEP Maria Teodorescu. "Now it's up to us to build stronger ties, to ensure that the conservative world progresses consistently." Her populist party, the Alliance for the Unity of Romanians, faced a boycott from Israel, particularly due to its collaborationist roots and downplaying of the Holocaust. In 2023, initial discussions were held with the government of Benjamin Netanyahu. The rapprochement with the RN stems from the same effort to broaden alliances, with parties openly supporting Israel's colonial war and having a more authoritarian conception of power. "We will put pressure on other [European] governments to [emulate Trump]," promises Mr. Netanyahu, thanking the participants for standing alongside "the forces of civilization against the forces of barbarism." Dov Maïmon, advisor to the Israeli government on the future of French Jews, watches this diplomatic adventure with uncertainty: "Israel chooses to ally with strong leaders like Viktor Orban, Vladimir Putin, or Jordan Bardella. Jews thrive in liberal democracies, not under authoritarianism, but the interests of the State of Israel may not necessarily align with those of the diaspora. And it turns out that Jews are safer in Budapest, where state antisemitism reigns, than in Paris." Disoriented, Mr. Maïmon is ready to defer to the successor of Jean-Marie Le Pen.

title: "Between an authoritarian Russia and the United States undergoing a shift towards illiberalism, Europe must take up the mantle of the 'free world'."
lead: Europeans are caught between an authoritarian Russia and a United States undergoing an illiberal reconfiguration, notes Philippe Bernard, a columnist for "Le Monde" in his column.
text_eng: "Unprecedented since 1945..." Whether it concerns the world order, the principle of inviolability of borders, or the abandonment of Europeans by the Americans, never has the year 1945, which marks the end of the Second World War, served as such a reference to understand the historical upheavals we are experiencing. This vintage also corresponds to the time when, after the victory over Nazism, the democracies of Western Europe "rebuilt themselves through law," as jurist Aurore Gaillet expressed about Germany. The demand for "never again" inspired a whole architecture of national and international institutions aimed at rebuilding political systems and relations between states based on respect for fundamental human rights, the disregard of which had plunged the continent into catastrophe. The protection of individuals against arbitrariness, equality before the law, separation of powers, the principle of legality, in other words, the submission of the administration to laws that themselves respect the Constitution and human rights, are not exclusive to Europe. But precisely since 1945, these principles have brought an invaluable asset, with heavy exceptions such as colonial wars: peace on the continent. An expression that served as a banner for opponents of oppressive regimes in Francoist Spain, the USSR, and its Eastern European satellites has become synonymous with this legal bloc that guarantees pluralistic democracies: the "Rule of Law." If Donald Trump's reelection marks a rupture in the post-1945 era, it is because it seals a de facto alliance with Vladimir Putin not only to dismantle international cooperation bodies but also to break up a perceived hostile power: the European Union (EU). To achieve this, the American president, like his Russian counterpart, is counting on a far-right takeover within the Twenty-Seven, as evidenced by the speech of his right-hand man, J.D. Vance, at the Munich Security Conference in mid-February. Faced with the ongoing revolution aimed at replacing the rule of law with force, Europe, caught between an authoritarian Russia and the United States in full illiberal reconfiguration, must take up the mantle of the "free world." Key principle of democracy. However, it would require prevailing on the Old Continent itself the idea that there is no democracy and freedom without the rule of law. "Democracy is based on the competition of ideas which, to be exercised, itself requires respect for rules, the implementation of which requires an arbitrator, a judge," summarizes Aurélien Antoine, professor of public law at Jean-Monnet University - Saint-Etienne. This system of Russian dolls that makes law and justice essential elements of democracy means that "all individuals and all authorities of the State (...) must be bound by the laws and benefit from them," according to the formula of Tom Bingham, former British senior judge, in his book The Rule of Law. The foundation of our democracy in peril? (Aux Feuillantines, 2024), where he dissects the "universal secular religion" that is for him "the rule of law," the British ancestor of "the rule of law." However, both Donald Trump and Vladimir Putin have allies in a destabilized Europe due to the impotence of politicians, where the shocks of terrorism and the Covid-19 pandemic have multiplied the breaches to the rule of law. On the far right but also on the right, the rule of law, a key principle of democracy, is presented as an encroachment on the prerogatives of the people, or even a sign of the malevolent influence of EU supranationalism. The Minister of the Interior, Bruno Retailleau, can assert in Le Journal du Dimanche, in September 2024, that "the rule of law is not untouchable or sacred," and Alexandre Devecchio, editor-in-chief of the Debates department of Le Figaro, can rail against a "rule of law that deprives the French of their sovereignty" (Le Figaro Magazine, February 28). The legal protections surrounding immigration and expulsion processes of foreigners are the main pretext for these attacks, culminating with the promise of the National Rally to organize a "referendum on immigration" that the current Constitution does not allow. Fundamental rights violated To counter them, it would be necessary to loudly remind that the rights and freedoms protected by the rule of law are a treasure for each of us: freedom of expression, the right to challenge state decisions, or the arbitration of conflicts by an independent court. And that the legal insecurity typical of illiberal or authoritarian regimes is one of the worst forms of insecurity. The year 1945 is probably too far away for the reminder of the ignominies of Nazism or Vichy to suffice as a deterrent, even though the ongoing rupture with the post-World War II order refers to it. But it is enough to look at the countless states around the world where these fundamental rights are currently being violated to understand the price. While the rule of law, a complex and contested legal concept, struggles to be popularized, efforts in education still need to be developed. A series of eight one-minute videos produced by the International Bar Association, an influential group of lawyers, illustrates the individual benefits that result from it in our daily lives, under the slogan "Take care of the rule of law and it will take care of you." At a time when the President of the United States invokes Napoleon by proclaiming that "he who saves his country violates no law" and threatens to flout court decisions, Europe could remain a refuge for those who believe, like the English philosopher John Locke (1632-1704), that "where the law ends, tyranny begins." A fitting motto for an Old Continent in search of a new sovereignty.

title: "Ukraine: On the Trail of the Executioners" on Arte: chronicle of a horror carefully planned by Russia
lead: Ksenia Bolchakova and Manon Loizeau collected evidence of war crimes committed by the Russians in the field. A long and relentless investigation. And necessary.
text_eng: ART – TUESDAY, FEBRUARY 18 AT 9:00 PM – DOCUMENTARY Summary executions, torture, civilian abductions, deportation of children... Ksenia Bolchakova and Manon Loizeau have collected evidence of war crimes committed by the Russians on the ground, as part of the "special military operation" launched in February 2022. Years of horrors and suffering that are part of a vast project carefully planned by Vladimir Putin for about ten years, since the annexation of Crimea in 2014. A project that aims not only to "denazify" the country, as Russian propaganda claims, but also to annihilate a culture, a language. Behind the war and the atrocious fighting, there is also a vast project of forced assimilation. Even if it means deporting children, so that young Ukrainians become good little Russians. Ksenia Bolchakova, born in Moscow, co-winner of the Albert-Londres audiovisual award in 2022 for a documentary on the Wagner Group, and Manon Loizeau, a French-British journalist and filmmaker, conducted the investigation in the heart of areas that have become sadly famous: Bucha, Izyum, Kherson... Chilling testimony Several witnesses agreed to speak on camera. A former soldier recruited by the Wagner Group from a Russian prison, who is now in hiding in Russia, recounts the "cleansing" he participated in Bakhmut: "We shot 250 or 300 people that day." Nikita, another Russian soldier, witness to horrors, deserted after two months on the front lines and sought refuge in Spain. Another chilling testimony: Victoria, a young math teacher in Izyum, was abducted, tortured, and imprisoned for six months in Russia before being released following a prisoner exchange. Through interviews and revisiting places used for imprisonment and torture (such as schools and police stations in Izyum), the evidence accumulates and demonstrates that these war crimes were planned well in advance. Preparation facilitated by the presence of infiltrators in many cities and "collaborators," especially in the eastern part of the country. The evidence? Detailed lists of individuals to be arrested dating back to January 2022 and bearing the signature of the FSB, the successor of the KGB in the Russian Federation. Or the presence on the ground of "specialists" in torture methods from private companies, specially brought from Russia to extract information and break witnesses. Finally, mention these thousands of ready-to-use textbooks to instill in the minds of young Ukrainians the good principles of Russian education. From start to finish, this thorough on-the-ground investigation proves to be relentless. And necessary.

title: War in Ukraine: after the American offer of a ceasefire, the Russian government is only reiterating its demands.
lead: President Putin did not react to the ceasefire proposal. The Russian Minister of Foreign Affairs made ambiguous statements.
text_eng: In Moscow, after the American proposal on Tuesday, March 11th, for a thirty-day ceasefire between Russia and Ukraine, there is currently a silence. Neither agreement nor refusal. "We do not rule out contacts with representatives of the United States in the coming days," commented Maria Zakharova, spokesperson for the Russian Ministry of Foreign Affairs. She did not confirm the visit to Moscow of Steve Witkoff, the special envoy of Donald Trump. However, Russian agencies reported that on Tuesday, the director of the Russian Foreign Intelligence Service, Sergey Naryshkin, spoke on the phone with the CIA chief, John Ratcliffe. This is the first contact between the two officials since Donald Trump returned to the White House on January 20th - and a rare exchange between Russian and American intelligence services since the start of the Russian offensive in Ukraine in February 2022. But while Mr. Trump said he would "speak to Vladimir Putin," probably this week, the Kremlin leader remains silent. And his spokesperson made no comment on the negotiations between the American and Ukrainian delegations in Saudi Arabia. So, seemingly far from Washington's expectations: US Secretary of State Marco Rubio said he expects Russia to respond positively "as soon as possible" to the ceasefire offer. Never reacting under pressure, Mr. Putin remains faithful to his stance. In an interview with American bloggers, his loyal Foreign Minister since 2004, Sergey Lavrov, made rather ambivalent remarks: "It is obvious that the United States and Russia are states whose national interests will never be identical. They cannot fully correspond, not even by 50%. But when they coincide, in such situations, we, as responsible politicians, must do everything to develop these similarities and put them into practice." "On our terms, not on the Americans' terms." In Moscow, the message remains firm for now. Influential Senator Konstantin Kosachev emphasized: "Any agreement - understanding the need for a compromise - will be concluded on our terms, not on the Americans' terms," he warned. State media similarly convey the message by recalling recent statements by Mr. Lavrov: "We will only cease hostilities when these negotiations lead to a firm and lasting result that suits Russia," he declared on February 24th, the third anniversary of Russia's invasion of Ukraine. Behind Mr. Putin's minimal reactions to Mr. Trump's flood of statements, the Kremlin has remained steadfast for two months regarding its demands: Ukraine's surrender, its renunciation of joining NATO, and Russian control of the occupied Ukrainian territories. Moscow could certainly accept a ceasefire, because if nothing serious is planned for the day after, it will not prevent them from resuming their offensive. But the ultra-patriotic factions continue to insist that one should not trust the Americans, and that no diplomatic negotiation should distract them from a victorious march. While, in the night from Monday to Tuesday, the Moscow region suffered the largest Ukrainian drone attack in three years of conflict, resulting in at least three deaths, Russian social media networks close to the military reacted even more strongly against any ceasefire as Washington announced the resumption of its aid to Kiev in intelligence and weaponry. "You have been deceived again," warns the Dontstopwar channel on Telegram. On this messaging platform, the most popular in Russia, the Z community (symbol of support for the offensive against Ukraine) is unleashed. "Washington is resuming its military support to the Ukrainian armed forces. That is to say, it once again directly declares its participation in the war against Russia... I hope we will not accept this," implores "Jivov Z." "Should we agree to stop our offensive so that the Ukrainians can regroup, bring in reserves, so that the Yankees can provide them with weapons and intelligence, and that in a month everything resumes with renewed vigor without any initiative on the battlefield from our side?" questions "Barbe Bleue Z." "A scam," summarizes "Colonelcassad."

title: In Germany, a major and welcome turning point
lead: The likely future German chancellor, Friedrich Merz, has announced a historic plan for investments in infrastructure and defense to boost his country's growth, restore its leadership in the EU, and address the geopolitical shift from the United States under Donald Trump.
text_eng: German commentators have chosen the name of a weapon whose effectiveness was shown to be formidable during the Second World War, the bazooka, to characterize the potential impact of the plan put forward on Tuesday, March 4, by the future conservative German Chancellor, Friedrich Merz, and his Social Democratic partners in the ongoing coalition negotiations. This "double economic bazooka" indeed represents a major step forward for Berlin to regain the upper hand in the face of significant historical challenges, both domestic and international. This plan first involves a reform of the debt brake, a mechanism enshrined in the Constitution that has long blocked public investment in a country with aging infrastructure and notably underfunded defense. Such a reform of budget discipline should allow for an increase in military spending demanded by the current situation in Europe. This measure, which breaks a German political taboo, is accompanied by the creation of a special fund of 500 billion euros over ten years aimed at modernizing infrastructure (roads, trains, schools). This two-pronged policy aims to achieve two objectives: revive the German economic growth, which has been in recession for two years following the shock of the war in Ukraine, and enable Berlin to regain a leadership position in Europe that has suffered from the weakness of Friedrich Merz's predecessor, Olaf Scholz. The decision still needs to be approved by a two-thirds majority in the Bundestag on March 18. To implement this major shift, the future chancellor had to resort to a democratic acrobatics. The majority he will have in the future parliament is too narrow to hope to reach the two-thirds threshold, so he chose to submit his plan to the outgoing Bundestag, where his Social Democratic partners still have significant support. New beginning The method is bold, but the times call for boldness: this is the message that the future chancellor has been trying to convey since the elections on February 23. Despite his party, the Christian Democratic Union-Christian Social Union (CDU-CSU), achieving a disappointing score, coming in first but with only 28.5% of the votes, and despite the handicap of the transitional period with a government still being negotiated, Friedrich Merz wants to show that he is taking charge - unlike his predecessor - that he is aware of the historic moment in which Donald Trump and Vladimir Putin have plunged Europe, and that Germany will rise to the occasion. This new German start and the changes it entails, particularly in the defense sector, are potentially positive. A revival of the German economic engine will benefit the rest of Europe. In the uncertain context of the war in Ukraine, a determined chancellor in Berlin can complement the leadership of his French partner Emmanuel Macron and British counterpart Keir Starmer to defend European interests against the Russo-American offensive and push forward the efforts initiated at the extraordinary European Council meeting on March 6. There are still many obstacles on this path, including that of a German chancellor who, while bold, is known to be impulsive and lacking in governmental experience. The risk would be to see his "bazooka" misfire, as Olaf Scholz's "Zeitenwende," or "turning point," did in 2022. But, for now, the new dynamic in Berlin must be encouraged.

title: War in Ukraine: In Riyadh, Russians and Americans agree to appoint negotiators for the resolution of the conflict.
lead: Led by the US Secretary of State, Marco Rubio, and his Russian counterpart, Sergey Lavrov, the American and Russian delegations also agreed on Tuesday in Saudi Arabia to establish a "consultation mechanism" to resolve disputes between the two countries.
text_eng: Russians and Americans reached an agreement on Tuesday, February 18, in Riyadh, Saudi Arabia, to establish a "consultation mechanism" to resolve their disputes, and they will appoint negotiators to settle the war in Ukraine. Following these first talks at this level between Russians and Americans since the Russian invasion of Ukraine in February 2022, which lasted four and a half hours, U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio said he was "convinced" that Russia wanted to engage in a "serious process" to end the war. He emphasized that any agreement on Ukraine must be "acceptable" to all parties. "It must be a permanent end to the war, not a temporary one, as we have seen in the past. We know, it's just the reality, that there will have to be a discussion on territories and there will be a discussion on security guarantees," he told the press. Rubio opened the door to European participation in the discussions, as both Kiev and the Europeans were excluded from the talks, without which a unilateral lifting of economic sanctions against Russia by Washington is hardly possible. "There are other parties that have imposed sanctions [against Russia], the European Union will have to be at the table at some point because they also have sanctions," he explained. Talks "on Ukraine without Ukraine" Russian Foreign Minister Sergey Lavrov stated that he perceived "a keen interest in removing artificial obstacles to the development of mutually beneficial economic cooperation" between Russia and the United States. He believed that the Americans had started to "better understand" Moscow's position – reiterating Russia's categorical opposition to any NATO presence in Ukraine – and that Russians and Americans had not only "listened" but also "agreed." "Extraordinary opportunities [exist] for a partnership" with Russia, but "the key to achieving this is the end of this conflict," Rubio concurred. The European Union's foreign policy chief, Kaja Kallas, called on the United States on Tuesday evening to "not fall into Russian traps," stating that Moscow would try to divide Western countries. U.S. President Donald Trump, on the other hand, expressed more "confidence" in the possibility of reaching an agreement with Moscow on a potential resolution to the war in Ukraine. However, his Ukrainian counterpart, Volodymyr Zelensky, who was meeting with Turkish President Recep Tayyip Erdogan in Ankara, denounced talks "on Ukraine without Ukraine" and demanded "fair talks" that include the European Union, the United Kingdom, and Turkey. "I am very disappointed" by these remarks, Donald Trump responded. "I hear that they are upset about not having a seat [at the negotiating table], even though they had a seat for three years, and even before that," the Republican said, who believed he had "the power to end the war." Mr. Erdogan, on the other hand, described his country as "an ideal host for the likely negotiations between Russia, Ukraine, and the United States." He emphasized that "Ukraine, Europe in the broad sense - including the European Union, Turkey, and the United Kingdom - should participate in discussions and in the development of necessary security guarantees with America regarding the fate of our part of the world." The Europeans want to "team up" with the United States. Washington and Moscow have said that Kiev would participate in the talks "in due time," and the Kremlin spokesperson assured on Tuesday that Vladimir Putin was "ready to negotiate with Zelensky if necessary." The Europeans - whose main leaders, taken aback by the resumption of contacts between Donald Trump and Vladimir Putin, met urgently in Paris on Monday - demand to be able to "team up" with the United States for a "just and lasting" peace in Ukraine, said the President of the European Commission, Ursula von der Leyen, on the social network X, after meeting with President Donald Trump's special envoy on Ukraine, Keith Kellogg. Marco Rubio and Sergey Lavrov, in Riyadh, ignored these questions and decided to "appoint high-level teams to start working on a resolution to the conflict in Ukraine as soon as possible, in a sustainable, lasting, and acceptable manner for all parties," the State Department announced in a statement. "We discussed, presented our principled approaches, and agreed that separate negotiating teams on this subject would make contact in due course," confirmed the Kremlin's diplomatic advisor, Yuri Ushakov. On one point, Russia was categorical. "Today we explained that the deployment [of NATO armed forces in Ukraine], even under a different flag, under the flag of the European Union or under national flags, changes nothing. This is of course unacceptable," said Sergey Lavrov, as some European countries and Kiev put forward the idea of such forces on Ukrainian territory to ensure Ukraine's security and respect for a future agreement to end the war. Donald Trump himself declared he was "totally in favor" of the presence of European peacekeeping troops in Ukraine. Another meeting will be held in Paris on Wednesday. In an interview with French newspapers, President Emmanuel Macron assured on Tuesday that "France is not preparing to send ground troops into a conflict zone in Ukraine." He announced that he would organize a new meeting on Ukraine on Wednesday "with several European and non-European states." French President Emmanuel Macron also estimated that President Donald Trump "can restart a useful dialogue" with President Vladimir Putin. "He is creating strategic ambiguity for President Putin" by using "very firm words" and introducing "uncertainty" that "can help apply pressure." In Riyadh, Moscow, and Washington have also agreed to establish a "consultation mechanism" and "lay the groundwork for future cooperation on geopolitical issues of common interest and on the historical economic and investment opportunities that will emerge from a successful resolution to the conflict in Ukraine," the State Department declared. Prior to the talks, Russia had notably emphasized that resolving the war in Ukraine was inseparable from the reorganization of European security architecture. Russia has long demanded a withdrawal of NATO forces from Eastern Europe, as it views the Alliance as an existential threat. It had used this argument to justify its invasion of Ukraine in 2022. The administration of President Donald Trump, on the other hand, has been very critical of its European allies and less inclined to support Ukraine. The meeting on Tuesday took place after a phone conversation last week between Donald Trump and Vladimir Putin, which shattered Western unity and the strategy of isolating the Russian president. Taking advantage of American-European tensions, Russia excluded European participation in the talks because, according to Mr. Lavrov, Europe wants the continuation of the war.

title: In Nicaragua, "Russian military aid and presence play a key role in the stability of the country."
lead: In an interview with "Le Monde", political scientist Manuel Orozco believes that "political power is now concentrated in the hands of a family clan, with the complicity of the army."
text_eng: Manuel Orozco is the director of the program on migrations, remittances, and development at the Inter-American Dialogue, a think tank based in Washington, specializing in Latin America and the Caribbean. You describe Nicaragua as a "dictatorship," why? Since 2018 and the repression of the popular movement [which started on April 18 around the central university in Managua, the initial protests were against a presidential decree reforming the pension system], which resulted in 350 deaths, the regime of Nicaraguan President Daniel Ortega [in power since 2007] and Rosario Murillo [his wife and co-president of the country] has become more radical. A total system of political control has been implemented, suppressing any form of dissent, while law enforcement maintains a climate of fear. It is a cyclical repression system, operating on a nearly quarterly basis. In other words, there are waves of persecution targeting and tracking anyone the regime considers a "threat," including anyone suspected of being linked to the opposition or close regime members who are no longer deemed reliable. Additionally, political power is now concentrated in the hands of a family clan, with the complicity of the army. Today, there is no longer political pluralism [since the 2021 elections, only a few parties, characterized by their collaboration with Ortega and the Sandinista National Liberation Front, remain legal], and there are no guarantees of respect for civil rights. The private sector is tightly controlled, the public sector entirely under the influence of the ruling clan, which enriches itself by seizing strategic sectors of the national economy such as construction, energy, communications, and infrastructure. Finally, Nicaragua has deliberately isolated itself internationally in order to limit external pressures. Military cooperation with Russia [on June 9, 2022, the Congress, dominated by the Sandinistas, authorized the presence of Russian troops, warships, and military vehicles on Nicaraguan territory] ensures the loyalty of Ortega's regime's army, which receives a significant portion of the national budget. Russian military aid and presence play a key role in the country's stability. Who are the current allies of the regime? The Daniel Ortega regime has historical ties with Cuba, Venezuela, Russia, and more recently, China [in December 2021, Managua reestablished diplomatic relations with Beijing, and a significant bilateral trade agreement was announced in the spring of 2022]. With Cuba, the alliance has always been primarily ideological, with a common stance against North American imperialism. Today, although solidarity remains strong between the two countries, exchanges, especially in terms of security and intelligence, have significantly decreased. Cuba's role in training security forces in Nicaragua has largely been replaced by Russia, which has established police academies and expanded its influence. Similarly, Venezuela once had a significant relationship with Nicaragua through the PetroCaribe fund, a program launched under Hugo Chavez [Venezuelan president from 1999 to 2013] that allowed countries in the region to obtain Venezuelan oil on favorable terms. Nicaragua was among the main beneficiaries, but the deep crisis in Venezuela since 2018 has led to the collapse of this program. Today, relations between Caracas and Managua are more symbolic and tactical, forming an authoritarian trilogy with Havana. Manuel Orozco, in Washington, on February 16, 2016. REBEN BARRERA / NOTIMEX VIA AFP On the other hand, Russia has become a major strategic partner, providing arms and intelligence. Over 90% of Nicaragua's military equipment comes from Russia, which has also installed a Glonass satellite station in the country [operational since April 2017] to enhance its surveillance and espionage capabilities, benefiting the Ortega regime. Russian military advisors in Nicaragua strengthen the government's social control and repression capabilities. Apart from the military domain, bilateral trade remains limited; the total amount of trade between the two countries is less than $30 million [€29.1 million] per year, which is insufficient to reduce Nicaragua's dependence on traditional partners like the United States. How would you define Nicaragua's current relationship with the United States? Managua's dependence on Washington is extremely strong, both commercially and financially. One quarter of Nicaragua's imports come from the United States. The main imports of Nicaragua are oil and its derivatives (16% of total imports), essential for the country's economy. The rest mainly consists of machinery and industrial equipment necessary for key sectors. On the export side, Nicaragua sells half of its national production to the American market. About 70% of what the country exports to the United States comes from the maquilas' free trade zones [tax-exempt assembly plants], particularly in the textile sector, where investment is mainly American. Remittances, funds transferred by the Nicaraguan diaspora, are another fundamental pillar of the national economy: in 2023, these reached $5 billion, approximately 30% of the country's GDP. Half of households benefit from them, while since 2018, around 850,000 Nicaraguans have left the country for political or economic reasons. It is estimated that remittances finance nearly half of household consumption, demonstrating their vital role in the daily economy of Nicaraguans. The government also benefits from them, as these fund transfers contribute to about 20% of national tax revenues. Any change in the volume of remittances would therefore have significant consequences on Nicaragua's economy. This situation puts the country in a vulnerable position if Donald Trump [whose inauguration is set for Monday, January 20] decides to expel part of the Nicaraguan diaspora, as he promises to do. Is Russia's presence in Nicaragua perceived as a threat by the new leadership in the United States? The main American concern regarding Russia is related to its nuclear potential, which far exceeds regional issues. [During his Senate hearing on January 15, Secretary of State designate Marco Rubio stated that "Russia's presence in Nicaragua poses a security threat that must be countered."] The United States is much more concerned about the rise of China in Latin America. Beijing is seen as a major commercial and strategic rival. Washington is monitoring Chinese trade relations with certain countries in the region and is seeking to limit China's influence in Peru, Venezuela, Nicaragua, Honduras, as well as Brazil and Mexico. The problem facing the United States is how to find ways to compete in terms of commercial competitiveness with China, which has lower production costs. For the Ortega regime, the alliance with Beijing is pragmatic, allowing to reduce Western influence, particularly that of the United States, and to enter into partnerships in several key areas. In the past five years, Nicaragua's exports to the United States accounted for 59% of the total; they are now at 54%. The lost 5% has been distributed among other partners: 1% to China, 1% to Russia, 1% to the European Union [EU], and the remaining 2% to the rest of the world. Nicaragua has therefore not been successful in conquering new markets capable of reducing its dependence on the United States. What role does Europe, which has imposed sanctions against the regime, play? On the international stage, the European Union has adopted a more measured policy than the United States towards authoritarian regimes in Latin America. Regarding Nicaragua, the EU is coordinating its sanctions with Washington [it has adopted three sets of individual sanctions, in March 2020, July 2021, and January 2022, targeting officials of the Nicaraguan administration and public entities involved in violations of the rule of law and human rights], affecting over 40 individuals, while the United States has imposed sanctions on over 60 entities and individuals. The EU could have taken stronger measures under the association agreement with Central America, which includes a chapter on democratic governance. At the request of the European Commission, the Human Rights Council could investigate human rights violations in Nicaragua, but nothing has been done. Do other Latin American countries exert influence? Nicaragua isolated itself by leaving [in April 2022] the Organization of American States [an intergovernmental organization based in Washington, established in 1948, comprising most nations of the continent], a decision that has angered several countries, notably Brazil. Mexico, although it has historically supported populist regimes, has distanced itself from Nicaragua while remaining reluctant to openly criticize the Ortega regime. Central American countries, except for Honduras, have also distanced themselves. Guatemala, in particular, is actively working to limit Nicaraguan influence within the Central American Integration System [a political, economic, and cultural organization aimed at promoting democracy and economic development in Central America]. Brazil and Chile no longer maintain diplomatic relations with Nicaragua.

title: The difficult European unity in the face of negotiations between the United States and Russia on Ukraine
lead: While most European countries believe that Europe should participate in the negotiations, Hungary and Slovakia criticize Brussels.
text_eng: Preserving European unity and supporting a just and lasting peace in the east of the continent. Following Donald Trump's announcement of peace negotiations with Russia over Ukraine on Wednesday, February 12, the majority of European capitals sought to stand behind Kiev and demanded a seat at the negotiating table. Antonio Costa, the President of the European Council, stated on Thursday that "peace in Ukraine and the security of Europe are inseparable. (...) There will be no credible and fruitful negotiations, no lasting peace, without Ukraine and the EU." In an interview with the Financial Times on Friday, Emmanuel Macron warned against a peace that would amount to a "surrender" by Ukraine. "The only question at this stage is whether, in a sincere, lasting, sustainable manner, President Putin is ready for a ceasefire on that basis," noted the French President. Some capitals have distanced themselves from Brussels, such as Budapest and Bratislava. "The position of Brussels, which is to support the massacre for as long as necessary, is morally and politically unacceptable," denounced Viktor Orban, the Hungarian Prime Minister and supporter of both Donald Trump and Vladimir Putin. Rome, on the other hand, is questioning the path to follow: to support European unity or align with the American President. A shift that Giorgia Meloni has not yet confirmed. Faced with this initial cacophony, the Polish Prime Minister, Donald Tusk, whose country currently holds the EU presidency, held numerous discussions on Thursday with his counterparts to try to maintain unity: "The message is clear," he wrote. "Ukraine, Europe, and the United States must be fully united and engage in peace talks." So far, Donald Trump has cracked open the door to direct participation by Ukraine in the negotiations, but not by the Europeans. "Everyone understands the likely [negotiation] format: the United States, Ukraine, and Russia. But where is Europe? I am also very interested in Europe's inclusion," demanded Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky. For staunchly pro-Atlantic Poland, reaching an agreement is imperative: "The international situation is so serious that we cannot afford internal disputes about our security," Donald Tusk assured earlier on. "The aggressor remains an aggressor." German Chancellor Olaf Scholz, on his part, warned against negotiations leading to a Russian victory and a "collapse" of Ukraine, which "would not bring peace, on the contrary." While Donald Trump has already abandoned several claims on Ukrainian territory and Ukraine's participation in NATO, Kaja Kallas, the head of European diplomacy, has denounced this "appeasement strategy" already attempted in the 1930s in the face of Nazi Germany, to the detriment of Czechoslovakia. Baltic leaders have also spoken out, showing the nervousness of these early allies of Ukraine. "I assured [Volodymyr Zelensky] that nothing has changed. The aggressor remains an aggressor, and the victim - the attacked country - must be supported," said Lithuanian President Gitanas Nauseda. "A ceasefire is not a lasting peace. We must stop the aggressor now and forever." Meanwhile, Viktor Orban again criticized "useless statements" from Europeans on Thursday. "You cannot demand a seat at the negotiating table. You must earn it! Through strength, good leadership, and smart diplomacy," he said on X. Robert Fico, the Slovak Prime Minister, lamented on Facebook the "helplessness of the EU," which, in its "military madness," has only "blindly copied the administration" of Joe Biden and must now "settle for watching."

title: Andrii Sybiha, Ukrainian Minister of Foreign Affairs: "Nothing should be discussed about Ukraine without Ukraine."
lead: While in Paris, the Ukrainian foreign minister reacts to the initiatives of the new American administration regarding the conflict with Russia.
text_eng: Andrii Sybiha has been the Ukrainian Minister of Foreign Affairs since September 2024. He was in Paris on Wednesday, February 12, to attend a meeting with his counterparts from the main European states: France, Germany, Poland, Italy, Spain, and the UK. He advocates for a lasting peace agreement negotiated with the participation of his country and the Europeans, in exchange for security guarantees. Were you surprised by the phone call between Donald Trump and Vladimir Putin? Such a conversation had been announced several times by the American administration. President Zelensky spoke with President Trump after that call. He had already met with him in Paris, along with Emmanuel Macron [on December 7, for the reopening of Notre-Dame Cathedral]. He then had the opportunity to explain his vision for achieving a complete, just, and lasting peace and preventing Russia from aggressing against us again in a few years. It is in all of our interests to join efforts to end the war in 2025. We don't just want the fighting to stop. Nothing should be discussed about Ukraine without Ukraine. Nothing should be discussed about Europe without Europe. Are you concerned about a too hasty compromise? We want a comprehensive agreement. This is also in the interest of the United States. It should be the greatest diplomatic achievement since the end of World War II. Under President Trump's leadership, with strong commitment and unity among Europeans, we have a chance to inject new momentum into this process. But, to my knowledge, our American allies have not yet finalized their plan. Are you not afraid that Trump will rush into negotiations with Putin? We are interested in accelerating the process. Ukraine is the country most affected by a just peace. We suffer every day, as 20% of our territory is occupied. Ukraine managed to liberate half of the invaded territories in 2022, including the major city of Kherson, to restrict the capabilities of the Russian navy in the Black Sea to establish a grain corridor. But our economy is functioning. This demonstrates, after three years of war, the strength of our people and our army, even though we have not always received timely strong decisions. We have not received enough weapons. We have always lagged behind the most appropriate choices. Diplomacy now has a chance to play an active role in bringing us closer to the strategic goal of peace. Do you understand what Trump wants to do to reach an agreement with Russia? We are in contact with his administration, and we will be especially during the Munich Security Conference [from Friday, February 14 to Sunday, February 16]. President Zelensky is preparing to meet with President Trump soon. I hope to have contact soon with the Secretary of State, Marco Rubio. It is the best way to exchange our views and unify our efforts for a just peace. The U.S. Secretary of Defense, Pete Hegseth, stated in Brussels on Wednesday that Ukraine's accession to NATO was "not realistic." How do you react? I only comment on facts. At the last summit in Washington in July 2024, all our allies stated that Ukraine's path to NATO was irreversible. This perspective of accession is enshrined in our Constitution. It is in our strategic interest. Over 90% of our population supports this project. Before the war, support was around 50%, and society was divided. This is not about charity. We will contribute to transatlantic security with our over 110 brigades, war experience, and technologies. We are developing sophisticated production, which we immediately test on the battlefield. We have increased the size of our defense industry sixfold to be more self-sufficient. This is also part of the security guarantees we seek to obtain. We had a bad experience with the Budapest Memorandum [signed in December 1994, following the dismantling of the USSR, in exchange for Ukraine giving up its nuclear weapons]. The best security guarantee remains joining NATO. It is the cheapest way for the Alliance to ensure its own security. The transatlantic family needs our contribution. That is the reality. What is the status of the idea of deploying European soldiers in Ukraine in the event of a ceasefire? This is one of the security guarantees we are considering. It is important to know the details, scope, and timeline. Before that, of course, we must force Russia to negotiate. We rely on the support of our partners to stabilize the situation on the battlefield, which is very tough. It is about defending our country and preventing further advances by Russian troops. Is a just peace compatible with territorial concessions? There should be no compromise on Ukrainian territorial integrity and sovereignty. Putin wants to extend the post-Yalta reality, where he could divide the world into spheres of influence and create gray areas. It is important to understand that Ukrainian security and transatlantic security are indivisible. Are you willing to exchange your rare earth minerals for American support, as Trump demands? Ukraine is indeed very rich in these resources. We are willing to exploit these raw materials and use them with our allies, including the United States. They have the appropriate technologies. This would require additional investments. This is a form of security guarantee for the business. We also need to protect these resources, otherwise, Russia could take advantage of them along with North Korea and Iran. President Zelensky was able to discuss this with Trump during their meeting before the election. This will be one of the benefits that our allies can gain from our victory.

title: In Ukraine, anger and anxiety after the "humiliation" suffered by Volodymyr Zelensky at the White House.
lead: Social networks and Ukrainian media support the president, but the opposition could benefit from the failure of his trip to Washington.
text_eng: Anger, anxiety, sadness. The remarks made by the American president towards Ukraine and Volodymyr Zelensky on Friday, February 28, have caused outrage and horror in the war-torn country. A torrent of emotions that Ukrainians, whether civilians at home or soldiers on the front lines, shared massively on Telegram channels and social media throughout a night marked by numerous Russian bombings on several cities. The shock was even more significant as many, in recent days, had hoped for a rapprochement between Volodymyr Zelensky and Donald Trump, even though the latter had announced on February 12 the opening of negotiations with Russia to end the conflict, without including Kiev. The relationship between the two men seemed to be heading towards a reconciliation, after the promise of an agreement on the joint exploitation of Ukrainian natural resources, the initial version of which, very disadvantageous for Kiev, had been rejected at the risk of frustrating the American president. On the eve of this meeting with his counterpart, Donald Trump had even used kind words towards the Ukrainian president on Thursday, February 27, denying ever calling him a "dictator" - which he had done a week earlier - and expressing his "respect" for "the best president of Ukraine." And then everything changed. Suddenly, abruptly, violently, from the first handshake between Volodymyr Zelensky and Donald Trump, with the latter making an ironic comment about the Ukrainian president's black military attire worn during the three-year war. Ukrainian television channels obviously shared the astonishing images of the altercation between Donald Trump, his vice president J.D. Vance, and Volodymyr Zelensky. The scene caused a shockwave. "While we mourn our dead, we watch American leaders humiliate Ukraine through our president by treating him as a subordinate rather than an equal partner," activist Mariam Naiem lamented on Instagram. "I didn't vote for Zelensky, but I fully support him today," former deputy defense minister Vitaliy Deynega wrote. "Today, I was not ashamed of the president and the country. I'm not sure Americans can say the same." Many similar messages of support for the head of state were posted on social media. However, political scientist Igor Chalenko expects that some of the opposition will denounce Volodymyr Zelensky's "lack of diplomatic finesse" during this crucial meeting for the war-torn country. "Allies do not behave like this." The burial of an American soldier who fought with the Ukrainian army on February 28, 2025 in Kiev. EFREM LUKATSKY / AP "What do you want me to tell you?" says an officer who has been fighting since 2014 and the start of the Donbass war on the phone. Yes, it's very tough, and it's a tragedy for the whole world. But that won't stop us from continuing to fight to defend our children, our homes, and our country." "American leaders have shown disrespect to Ukrainians and their fight for freedom," says Igor Chalenko. That's how the situation is perceived in Ukraine." "Allies don't behave like that, especially not in public," writes Vitaly Sych, the editor-in-chief of Nv.ua, on Facebook. "It was a disgusting attempt to strike at the president of a country at war using all possible weaknesses. It's simply unworthy of America." "They provoked Zelensky and Zelensky couldn't not react," says political expert and teacher Oleksiy Haran. "It was a trap," says former diplomat and international relations expert at the Center for Defense Strategies, Oleksandr Khara. "Ukraine is doomed to be blamed for its 'lack of willingness' to seek peace," adds Mr. Khara, certain that this latest meeting is just confirmation of the White House's line to achieve a ceasefire in the country. In recent weeks, while sidelining Kiev, the U.S. administration has continued to increase contacts with the Kremlin, aligning with Russian talking points, even voting alongside Russia, Belarus, and North Korea against a resolution prepared by Ukraine at the UN on Monday, February 24. The Ukrainian president had gone to Washington hoping to get clarification on American aid and to discuss security guarantees. He leaves with even greater uncertainty about American support. "We will fight and seek solutions," said Vitaly Sych, editor-in-chief of Nv.ua, on Facebook. "That's what we've been doing for three years now. We will find these paths with the Americans, or we will find them with someone else."

title: Ukraine ready to sign a first agreement with the United States in hopes of winning over Donald Trump.
lead: Volodymyr Zelensky was supposed to go to Washington on Friday and question the American president about his "support."
text_eng: After two weeks of tensions and negotiations, Kiev and Washington have reached an agreement on a framework agreement regarding the revenues from the exploitation of Ukrainian natural resources. This text is just a first step towards a comprehensive agreement, but the visit of President Volodymyr Zelensky to Washington on Friday, February 28, has been confirmed. He is expected to meet with his American counterpart, Donald Trump, to sign the document and, hopefully, improve their relations. The most important thing for Ukrainians is to get closer to the American president, especially as he is making a diplomatic shift towards the Russian Federation in order to quickly end the war, even if it means sidelining Kiev. This initial agreement is a victory for Donald Trump, allowing him to present himself as a skilled negotiator to his electorate. For Volodymyr Zelensky, whose country remains highly dependent on American support, the challenge is to convince the American president not to abandon Ukraine. Kiev's refusal to sign a first version that was terribly detrimental and lacked security guarantees had angered the American president. He had accused his Ukrainian counterpart of being a "dictator without elections," questioning his legitimacy. "Zelensky better act quickly, otherwise he won't have a country left," he had stated in a sharp and misinformation-filled message on social media on February 19. Ukrainian negotiators finally managed to include a clause referring to American "security guarantees," a request repeatedly made by President Volodymyr Zelensky. However, while the project does mention the term "guarantees," it does not include any specific commitment from the United States to preserve the security of the war-torn country. "We will ask Europe to do it," stated the American president during his first cabinet meeting on Wednesday, adding that Kiev could "forget" about joining NATO. The Ukrainian president has repeatedly assured that this issue would be addressed during his meeting with Donald Trump. "My question [to Mr. Trump] will be very direct: will the United States stop their support or not? Can we buy weapons if we no longer receive aid?" declared Volodymyr Zelensky during a press conference on Wednesday. On his part, Donald Trump hinted that he primarily viewed this new agreement as a form of "repayment" for the American assistance provided since the beginning of the Russian invasion of Ukraine. "The best security guarantee" American negotiators had previously refused to mention "security guarantees," arguing that Washington's financial interests in Ukraine's natural resources would serve as protection against Russian occupation of certain territories. Thus, frustrated by Ukraine's refusal to sign a deal described as "amazing and historic," National Security Adviser Michael Waltz declared on February 20th on Fox News that an American investment in the Ukrainian economy represented for Kiev "the best security guarantee they could hope for, much more than a new range of ammunition." In the latest version of the draft agreement, some of the most controversial clauses have been removed. Initially, Donald Trump demanded that Ukraine pay 500 billion dollars into a fund held by the United States and repay twice the amount of any future American aid. The text remains extremely binding, however. It provides for the creation of a joint investment fund between the United States and Ukraine. This fund will receive 50% of Ukrainian revenues from the future exploitation of certain natural resources (minerals, hydrocarbons, oil, gas...) and from the operation of infrastructure such as methane terminals and ports. The agreement states that the United States will hold a percentage of ownership and a "maximum" level of control over the fund fueled by these revenues. Many questions remain unanswered, particularly regarding its governance structure. The text also specifies that ongoing oil and gas extraction operations, which already significantly contribute to the Ukrainian budget - and therefore to the war effort - will not be part of the agreement. This framework agreement, approved by the Ukrainian government on Wednesday afternoon under strong American pressure, remains overall quite vague. Ukrainian authorities have clarified that it is only a preliminary step. Its final version will be submitted to the Ukrainian Parliament, the Verkhovna Rada, for ratification. Extremely costly explorations The appetite of the American administration for Ukrainian minerals is part of a global competition for access to these resources considered essential for a wide range of industries, from defense to aerospace. It was the Ukrainian president who first highlighted the exploitation of these Ukrainian minerals in a "victory plan" presented to Donald Trump's teams in September, hoping to convince him of the financial benefits of supporting Ukraine. The war-torn country possesses at least twenty of the fifty minerals that the US Geological Survey considers essential for its economy, such as lithium, graphite, titanium, and rare earth elements. However, many experts point out that the extent of Ukrainian natural resources remains uncertain, considering that the last assessments date back to the Soviet era. Expert firms also ensure that an estimation of the real value of these minerals would require extremely costly explorations, as they have not been conducted by Kiev due to a lack of resources and foreign investments. Furthermore, 30% to 40% of these deposits are believed to be located in territories now occupied by Russia, while the prospect of their return to Kiev's control is becoming increasingly distant.

title: Gérard Depardieu, suspected of falsely claiming residence in Belgium, is the subject of an investigation for aggravated tax fraud and money laundering.
lead: Since February 2024, the National Financial Prosecutor's Office has been trying to determine the truth about his residency in Néchin, a border town known for hosting wealthy expatriates.
text_eng: New legal case for Gérard Depardieu. The actor, targeted by several accusations of sexual violence which he denies, is now the subject of a preliminary investigation for aggravated tax fraud and money laundering, a judicial source informed Le Monde on Monday, February 24, confirming information from Mediapart. He is suspected of having claimed fiscal residency in Belgium since 2013, while continuing to live in France without paying taxes there. Searches were carried out on February 11 in France and Belgium by officers from the Central Office for the Fight against Corruption and Financial and Tax Crimes and Belgian judicial authorities as part of a European investigation decision, the same judicial source specified, adding that the 76-year-old actor had not been questioned at this stage. The investigation opened by the National Financial Prosecutor's Office (PNF) in February 2024 aims to determine the reality of his residency in Belgium, and, if it proves to be fictitious, the amount of tax he would have evaded from France since 2013. This procedure follows several reports from the tax authorities. A "rather despicable" departure "The information from Mediapart and the PNF is once again inaccurate," replied Gérard Depardieu's lawyer, Jérémie Assous. "The tax authorities have been trying for nine years to establish fraud. Without success, they are now trying to achieve this through a criminal procedure. Unfortunately for them, and to the great despair of those who dream of being substitutes, the result will be the same. Some might be tempted to describe these legal methods as despicable, but they are legal and customary." In December 2012, criticizing the tax policy of taxing high incomes desired by the socialist president François Hollande, Gérard Depardieu announced he was leaving France to settle in Belgium, specifically in Néchin, a border town known for hosting wealthy expatriates. A heated controversy followed, with the then Prime Minister, Jean-Marc Ayrault, notably calling his departure "rather despicable," and the actor threatening to return his French passport. In January 2013, he became a Russian citizen, showing his closeness to President Vladimir Putin before distancing himself at the time of the invasion of Ukraine, nine years later. Faced with the announcement of a new judicial investigation against his client, Jérémie Assous denounces a "schedule that fools no one, exactly one month before the trial of Les Volets verts." The actor is set to be judged on March 24 and 25 by the Paris Criminal Court for sexual assaults on two women that allegedly occurred during the filming of the film Les Volets verts, by Jean Becker, in 2021. The case, which was supposed to be tried in October 2024, had indeed been postponed due to Gérard Depardieu's absence for "health reasons." The public prosecutor also requested in August 2024 his referral to the criminal court for rape against the actress Charlotte Arnould, the first woman to have filed a complaint against him in 2018. Whether or not a trial will take place now depends on the decision of the investigating judge in charge of this case. A complaint for "destruction of property" Gérard Depardieu's lawyer also denounces the methods used during the search of his private mansion in the 6th arrondissement of Paris on February 11, and announces to Le Monde that he has received a written mandate from the actor to file a complaint for "damage, theft, and destruction of property": "This concerns notably art objects, but also, and above all, books and sacred texts, including the Quran, which were intentionally trampled and torn, while there was no doubt that they had no connection, neither direct nor indirect, with the object of the search." He adds that he has "testimonies and videos attesting to the destruction of the server as well as the disappearance of the hard drives containing the video recordings made that day." Contacted, the National Financial Prosecutor's Office did not react.

title: Covid-19: five years later, time for the economic bill
lead: For nearly two years, economies around the world have been partially suspended. The explosion of debt as well as the surge in prices are direct consequences of this.
text_eng: On March 16, 2020, at 8 p.m., exactly five years ago, Emmanuel Macron announced the lockdown of the French population. Two days later, just before midnight, the European Central Bank (ECB) opened the floodgates of credit by urgently announcing an intervention plan of 750 billion euros (which would eventually rise to 1,700 billion euros). The era of free money had begun. Governments were able to borrow like never before, at interest rates around zero, to fund healthcare efforts, pay the salaries of people forced to stay at home, provide emergency loans to businesses... A large part of the world, including all of Europe and the United States, followed suit. This dual effect - massive central bank intervention and historic state indebtedness - helped keep economies afloat. Economically, it was a real-life experiment, practically unprecedented. "It was a textbook case of stimulus through monetization," explains Gilles Moëc, chief economist at Axa. It absolutely had to be done, otherwise it would have been an economic catastrophe, in addition to a health catastrophe, but all of this comes at a cost." Soaring public debt, sharp inflation spikes, fragmentation of international trade... The bill was significant and is far from being fully paid. But first, let's put it into context. Following the lockdowns, the economic rebound was very rapid. The French economy had returned to its pre-pandemic level by the end of 2021. "The global economy has fared better than expected," remind economists Isabelle Bensidoun and Thomas Grjebine in "The World Economy 2025" (La Découverte, 2024). By the end of 2023, they note, global GDP had surpassed pre-pandemic levels by 9%, and by 7% in advanced countries. The doomsday predictions at the start of the lockdown did not materialize. "Today, we take for granted that economies and institutions held up, but it wasn't a given," adds Mr. Moëc. According to calculations by Oxford Economics, global growth is currently 1% below the forecasts made in 2019, representing a marginal slowdown. Debt explosion Officially, European treaties prohibit "monetary financing." In other words, the European Central Bank is not allowed to directly "print" money to fill the coffers of states. However, in 2020 and 2021, this is de facto what happened. Of course, the process was slightly indirect to circumvent the legal question: states issued debt, which institutional investors willingly bought at zero interest rates because they knew the central bank would buy it back. The "whatever it takes" was only possible at this price. Five years later, these expenses have caused a surge in global public debt of around 10 percentage points of GDP. Public debts of countries around the world were at 84% of GDP before the pandemic; they are now at 95%. For advanced countries, the increase has been closer to 15 points. In France, debt has risen from 98% to 113%; in the United States, from 106% to 121%. But others, like Italy or Germany, have almost returned to their pre-Covid-19 debt levels. However, these figures hide several realities. First, starting in February 2022, the invasion of Ukraine by Russia caused a new geopolitical and economic shock. Soaring energy prices, when Vladimir Putin cut off Europe's gas supply, required new government interventions. The French "tariff shield" and its equivalents in neighboring countries were very costly. It is difficult to precisely separate the budgetary impact of this phenomenon from that of the pandemic. Additionally, during lockdowns, borrowing was done at extremely attractive interest rates. The low point for French 10-year bonds was... -0.3%. The French Ministry of Finance took advantage of this to refinance part of the French debt and extend the average repayment duration, now over eight years. Initially, the shock was rather positive for the state finances: in 2020, the average interest rate on French public debt reached a historically low point of 1.25%, compared to 6.6% in 1996, notes the Fipeco website. The debt was increasing, but the repayment burden was decreasing. This is no longer the case. The average interest rate on the debt had risen to 1.63% in 2023, and it is expected to be 2% this year. Now, the repayment burden is increasing. The debt burden, which had dropped to 1.2% of GDP in 2020, will rise to 2.3% of GDP this year and could reach 3.2% in 2029, according to the Court of Auditors. The effects of Covid-19 debt are therefore taking a long time to be felt. It was only when interest rates rose significantly - starting in 2022 - that the problem began to arise. Surge in inflation The second major effect of the "whatever it takes" approach was a sharp return of inflation. In total, prices have increased by 17% in France, 21% in the United States, and 22% on average across the eurozone... Such a shock had not occurred since the oil crises of the 1970s. This surge in prices has caused a significant loss of purchasing power, which households have only begun to partially regain in the past eighteen months. Before the pandemic, an economic school of thought - modern monetary theory - believed it was possible to engage in monetary financing without causing price increases. "We now know they were wrong," notes Mr. Moëc. The huge amount of money created during Covid-19 – free money – has finally been felt in the form of inflation. The situation was quite simple: households, for the most part, continued to be paid but could not consume during the lockdowns. They were sitting on "forced" savings, which were suddenly released when economies reopened. "It is never trivial to stop the economy," explains Céline Antonin, an economist at the French Observatory of Economic Conjonctures. "When it restarts, it is very disruptive to supply chains." However, it is difficult to separate the effects of the pandemic from those of the war in Ukraine. "In the United States, inflation mainly came from a demand shock [from aid payments during the pandemic]," points out Silvana Tenreyro, an economist at the London School of Economics. "In Europe, it was more of an energy shock." The surge in gas prices has nothing to do with Covid-19 and is a consequence of the war in Ukraine. Faced with this surge in prices, central banks reacted strongly. The ECB raised its interest rates from -0.5% to 4% (before lowering them to 2.5%), while the Federal Reserve in the United States went from a floor of 0% to 5.25% (before lowering it to 4.25%). Today, both institutions are congratulating themselves, believing they have successfully curbed inflation. "It has been a great success," confirms Maurice Obstfeld, former Chief Economist of the International Monetary Fund. "Inflation peaked, as was inevitable, but then it came down and there was no recession." The downside, however, is a more difficult public debt to finance due to high interest rates. Fragmentation of globalization Covid-19 also acted as a revealer: France began to experience shortages of masks, Doliprane, and certain raw materials. Its dependencies – and those of many other countries – were laid bare. "The pandemic made us realize the importance of China in global market shares," explains Ms. Antonin. "This led to questioning whether we had gone too far in globalization." She recalls that we lived "at the pace of Chinese lockdowns," suddenly lacking certain products depending on the state of openness of that country on the other side of the world. Indeed, doubts about globalization had been brewing for several years, at least since the first election of Donald Trump in 2016. But the pandemic accelerated the movement. "This is particularly true in Germany, it was the moment when the economic model began to be questioned," continues Ms. Antonin. The largest European economy was heavily dependent on exports to China. Its companies had signed 6,000 joint ventures in that country, four times more than French companies. What was once a strength suddenly appeared as a weakness. In response, the European Union (EU) began to adopt a more interventionist industrial policy. It softened its rules on state aid to facilitate support for companies deemed strategic. In France, "economic sovereignty" has become a mantra of the government. The EU also dared to take a step that was previously unimaginable: it decided to borrow collectively. Faced with the pandemic, Angela Merkel, then in her third term as Chancellor, decided to lift her veto against this idea, which had been raised many times by France. A large loan of 750 billion euros was announced. This money was then disproportionately redistributed to the countries most affected by the pandemic, notably Italy and Spain, whose economies were heavily reliant on tourism. Europe dared to take on common debt and showed solidarity in a historic gesture. However, the implementation of this plan leaves much to be desired. It took nearly a year to refine its launch. Subsequently, the disbursements were conditioned on reforms that each country had to carry out (labor market reform in Spain, pension reform in Slovenia, etc.). Identifying projects to be funded was also not simple: a minimum of 37% of the money had to go towards climate transition aid, 20% towards digital... Only after that did the money start to be distributed in successive installments. As a result, the total amount now stands at only 650 billion euros, and... only half has been disbursed so far. The program is supposed to end in 2026, but many voices in Brussels assert that all the money will not be spent in time.

title: In Russia, pro-war activists close to the Kremlin are now in favor of a ceasefire in Ukraine.
lead: A video made by the authorities, and widely circulated on Russian Telegram channels, justifies a halt to the fighting with Kiev.
text_eng: Widely circulated on Russian Telegram channels, a propaganda video discussing the prospect of ending the war in Ukraine has caused unease among pro-Kremlin military bloggers who want the invasion to continue, at least until reaching Kiev. Posted on Saturday, February 22, by the Telegram channel Pool N3, the clip shows an elderly man sitting in his kitchen, discussing with his wife the news from the front lines in Donbass, where the Russian army is slowly gaining ground at the cost of heavy losses. "It takes them a month to take each village! They are at a standstill. How long will this last? We should have dropped a nuclear bomb on them a long time ago...," the husband rants. His wife agrees. The invasion is dragging on too long. "Where is Kiev?" she laments, referring to the capture of the Ukrainian capital, previously presented by Russian President Vladimir Putin as his main war goal. To calm her furious husband, she adds: "Everyone is currently talking about a backroom deal." The "deal" in question actually refers to the ceasefire agreement that U.S. President Donald Trump wants to reach bilaterally with his Russian counterpart, Vladimir Putin. "They are now in agreement, and we will be able to go home," say two soldiers on the front lines in another scene of the clip. The possibility of a future cessation of hostilities, especially initiated by the Americans, disgusts Russian military bloggers, convinced that victory is within reach and that more drones and ballistic missiles should be fired at Ukrainian cities. Some of them were so stunned by the video that they saw it as a possible disinformation campaign by Ukrainian intelligence services, aimed at demoralizing the troops. "A departure from the initial narrative" In reality, it came straight from the Kremlin's propaganda lab. Posted by Pool N3, the Telegram channel of Dmitry Smirnov, a journalist accredited to the Russian presidency, this video is a message that the authorities are trying to convey to the warmongers to make them accept a cessation of hostilities that is far from a victory. With none of the war goals achieved, neither the capture of the capital nor the removal of the "Nazi regime in Kiev," it is difficult today to convince pro-war activists that all the sacrifices made were ultimately to invite the Americans to exploit the mineral resources of the Russian subsoil, as proposed by Vladimir Putin recently. "It's a departure from the initial narrative presented by Russian media since the beginning of the special operation," noted the Telegram channel Deux Majors (1.2 million subscribers), reposting the clip, as most Kremlin-affiliated channels have completely changed their national narrative. Another change in tone. Yes, at the beginning of the war, Margarita Simonian, the editor-in-chief of Russia Today, the flagship media of the Russian regime, inflamed the audience with her calls to crush Ukraine, but her speech is completely different since the warming of relations with Washington. "I would have liked us to take Kiev, Odessa, Kharkiv, and everything else, but in reality, what we can agree on with Trump is to keep the territories that are ours, we stop at the front line," she declared in an interview published on January 20, convinced that one must rely on "reality and not on dreams."

title: Three years of war in Ukraine: The United States, from support to abandonment
lead: Back at the White House, Donald Trump threatens to abruptly end three years of ongoing assistance to Kiev, but without a clearly defined plan.
text_eng: Three years of war, and a vital bridge collapses. For Ukraine, the transition from the Biden administration to the Trump era can be summed up in a few phrases. From mobilization to the risk of abandonment. From empathy to contempt. From tailored support to unrestrained pressure. The new president's eagerness to impose peace is fraught with threats for Ukraine, which cannot do without Washington's support. But a "pax americana" dictated primarily by Moscow, without regard for the victim, would be a decisive tragedy, adding to all the sacrifices made to defend national sovereignty and territory, already fractured by the annexation of Crimea in 2014 and the invasion of 2022. For three years, the United States stood by Ukraine. Initially surprised by its resistance and the setbacks of the Russian army, Joe Biden then worked to mobilize allies to support Volodymyr Zelensky and his people. But the veteran Democrat, intellectually shaped by the Cold War, also set limits due to a fear: that of a conflict between nuclear superpowers, extending beyond Ukrainian borders. The American effort was first preventive and unusual. Starting in November 2021, after a visit to Moscow by CIA chief William Burns, Washington decided to declassify information about Russian military mobilization on the border with Ukraine. Not believing much in the idea of military exercises, the White House denounces the Kremlin's intentions. When the war begins, allies cannot claim to be surprised. Dark predictions of a collapse of power in Kiev, a Russian tank takeover of the capital, and Zelensky's escape are not confirmed. On the Western side, the reaction is swift, with waves of coordinated economic sanctions and initial arms deliveries. "In this first phase, the United States tried to focus on what could be delivered as quickly as possible, such as assault rifles, anti-tank weapons, or artillery shells," explains Dara Massicot, an expert at the Carnegie Endowment for International Peace think tank. Artificial constraints In late May 2022, in an op-ed in The New York Times, Joe Biden outlines the logic of his support for Ukraine. Washington has finally agreed to provide multiple launch rocket systems (Himars) mounted on light armored vehicles, on the condition that they are not used to target Russia. "We do not encourage or allow Ukraine to strike beyond its borders," explains Joe Biden. "We do not want to prolong the war simply to inflict suffering on Russia." However, the war will continue. The United States will eventually, albeit too late, release the Ukrainian army from artificial constraints. "It would have been more operationally effective if the green light had been given [to strike in Russia] when the Himars were delivered in 2022, because the Russians were not yet adapted to them, they had not learned how to shoot them down or jam their communications," notes Dara Massicot. Diplomatically, Joe Biden succeeds in consolidating the Western bloc, but his narrative of a confrontation between democracies and illiberal regimes does not resonate. Countries in the South observe with some skepticism the mobilization for Ukraine. In their minds, this is a classic European conflict, not a global affair where everyone should take a moral stance. In November 2022, General Mark Milley, Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff of the United States Armed Forces, publicly acknowledges the unexpected military successes of Ukraine. Instead of formulating unrealistic dreams of a Russian rout, he says this: "You want to negotiate when you are in a position of strength, and your opponent is in a position of weakness. And it is possible, perhaps, that there could be a political solution." The coded message is clear: weapon and ammunition stocks are limited, the Russians are capable of regrouping after their initial missteps, and prolonging the conflict will not benefit Kiev. But Vladimir Putin also knows all this, and he offers no opening. Joe Biden, on the other hand, does not even seek one. Diplomatic channels are broken. Budgetary wrangling In December 2022, Volodymyr Zelensky is triumphantly received in Washington. He finally obtains a battery of Patriot missiles. His goal: "to survive this winter." Already, in the American public opinion, erosion is observed, after the empathy and shock of the first months of the conflict. Nearly half of those surveyed - compared to 39% in July 2022 - want Washington to push Kiev towards a negotiated solution with Moscow. "It would be naive to expect steps towards peace from Russia, which enjoys being a terrorist state," notes Volodymyr Zelensky before Congress.
