In February 2023, Joe Biden makes a surprise and risky visit to Kiev. Then he delivers a solemn speech in Warsaw, on the occasion of the first anniversary of the war's beginning. "Our support for Ukraine will not waver, NATO will not be divided, and we will not tire. Joe Biden assures: Ukraine "will never be a victory for Russia." However, by the end of summer 2023, anonymous criticisms appear, from the American side, against the Ukrainian strategy. Kiev may have dispersed its forces too much, wasted resources and human lives in Bakhmut, a city turned to ruins. But what can be done without air forces, without long-range strike capabilities? The Pentagon has consistently emphasized that no weapon is decisive in itself, and that the main Ukrainian problem is that of human mobilization capabilities. Volodymyr Zelensky, on the other hand, resisted American pressure, refusing to lower the conscription age to 18. In September 2023, he returned to Washington after nineteen months of war. Among the Republicans in the House, support for Kiev is no longer a given. It is relegated as a factor among others in the budgetary struggle with the Democratic administration. At the White House, there is a misplaced confidence that there still exists a bipartisan majority in favor of providing new aid. Failure of Joe Biden. The needs are urgent, and American lawmakers are in no hurry. The disadvantage in ammunition suffered by Kiev – one Ukrainian shell for every five Russian ones fired – and in anti-missile defense systems is glaring. In April 2024, after more than six months of failures and Republican blockages, the House of Representatives passed a new aid package for Ukraine, amounting to $60.8 billion (57 billion euros). Relief and bitterness are intertwined. This enormous delay has not only had operational consequences. It has reversed the momentum. Vladimir Putin is convinced that time is on his side, and Westerners will lose their breath and motivation. The constant since February 2022 has been the flexibility of American "red lines." The Biden administration, albeit belatedly, finally gave the green light to Kiev's successive requests. The M1 Abrams tanks and F-16 fighter jets, initially excluded, were eventually delivered. Ukrainian operations in Russia? There too, resistance gave way. First in annexed Crimea, in clandestine form, then in the Belgorod region, and finally in the Kursk region. Faced with the increasing Russian missile strikes on the city of Kharkiv in May 2024, the White House finally approved Ukrainian strikes in Russia itself. It then took the passing of the American presidential election for the Biden administration to authorize, on a case-by-case basis, the use by the Ukrainian army of long-range missiles to reach deep into Russia, with the Army Tactical Missile Systems (ATACMS). The official reason: the deployment of 10,000 North Korean soldiers after the Ukrainian army took territories in the Kursk region, Russia. The role of North Korea marks a failure of Joe Biden. The war is not contained. Iran, Russia, China: an authoritarian trio collaborates in the hope of witnessing the decline of American power. Joe Biden, over these three years, never outlined a possible outcome. What would a defeat of Moscow mean? What would a victory for Kiev mean? "Nothing about Ukraine without Ukraine," officials in Washington stubbornly repeat, in a respectful formula that leads to a dead end. To get out of it, Donald Trump seems to hold onto another formula: everything with Russia, and Ukraine will have to follow.

title: "After the 'happy globalization,' we could witness a strange phenomenon in Europe: the 'happy vassalization.'"
lead: Gilles Gressani, a specialist in geopolitics, analyzes in an opinion piece in "Le Monde" the consequences of Donald Trump's imperialist ambitions, who is seeking to "reshape the West" to make the United States the sole sovereign state.
text_eng: Unlike in 2017, Donald Trump has a more radical and well-defined plan. By relying on the coalition that brought him to power, uniting the disenfranchised of a broken society with the Silicon Valley oligarchs, the President of the United States is in the process of leading a profound imperial consolidation. Washington would not become the capital of an isolationist America, but the shining center of a vast space. The border is opening: from Panama to planet Mars, passing through Greenland. Geopolitics is back at the White House. In a surge of ambition that stands out from the stifled debate in our political spheres, Donald Trump evokes "manifest destiny," signs dozens of executive orders, promises the expulsion of several million undocumented immigrants, announces a staggering investment plan to win the race for artificial intelligence, and launches a cryptocurrency bearing his image that multiplies his fortune. At the Capitol, in a ceremony resembling a coronation, made unprecedented by the presence for the first time of several foreign heads of state and government, he declares: "My message to Americans today is that it is time for us to act again with the courage, vigor, and vitality of the greatest civilization in history." Taking Trumpism to Mars It is easy to be overwhelmed by the power of this staging. However, we must seek to understand what is at stake, without deference or fatalism. On the domestic front, the political matrix of the new Trumpism is based on a new form of Caesarism deeply influenced by the digital realm, its culture, infrastructure, and radically monopolistic economic models. In this new regime, technological innovation and extreme wealth concentration are linked to an expansive, militarized sovereignty and an aggressive policy of identity protection. In the intention of Donald Trump and the elites participating in the consolidation of this doctrine – which we could call "techno-Caesarism" – the aim is to accompany the transformation of a redundant and ineffective republic into an empire organized to scale America up. On the external front, this profoundly revisionist project unfolds in two phases. The first is to reshape the West so that only one entity remains sovereign: the United States. Building its power on an undisputed dominance of the military and digital domains, which are merging in an increasingly obvious manner, Donald Trump is undertaking to transform NATO into a sort of Warsaw Pact, neutralizing any residual pockets of competing sovereignty. Whether it concerns European positions on the digital public sphere or climate, to the territorial claims of even faithful allies like Denmark, he aims to prevent any real autonomy. It will be a matter of projecting this consolidated West on a global scale, finally fully aligned with the interests of the metropolis, by neutralizing China, the only power that could threaten American hegemony. In this strategy, Elon Musk plays a pivotal role. Internally, the world's richest man is the driving force behind technocesarism, which he embodies in his futuristic vision of a multiplanetary civilization carrying Trumpism to Mars. Externally, the owner of X has become the captain of a new East India Company, established within the federal government, intended to allow Washington to obtain data - the silk and spices of our time - while imposing its domination on various peoples in Asia, Africa, and especially Europe. With one goal: to replace potential adversaries with weakened and aligned allies. This imperial project changes the direction of globalization without stopping it. The circulation of people, information, and goods reaches an unprecedented level and remains, sometimes contradictorily, in the interest of the Trumpist coalition. It is the liberal phase of globalization - structured by openness, horizontality, the end of borders - that Washington seeks to definitively close. This new imperial Trumpism proposes to offer a technocesarist solution to the crises and contradictions it has generated: economic first, with inequalities that have torn apart our political systems from within. A crisis of political autonomy, then, of the effectiveness of the State entangled in impossible reforms, technocratic pushes, strategic inconsistencies, and indecision. An ideological and anthropological crisis, finally, linked to clashes of lifestyles facing a centrifugal culture incapable of recognizing and involving the masses of peripheral spaces. Obey or react After the "happy globalization," we could witness a strange phenomenon in Europe. With Le Grand Continent [a geopolitical journal founded in 2019, whose author of this text is the editor-in-chief], we have proposed to designate it by a new concept: "happy vassalization." Happy vassalization involves a particularly disturbing deal, as the exchange appears asymmetrical, transactional, and unilateral: obedience and the refusal of any autonomy in exchange for a form of protection against imperial aggression. In the vertigo of the radical transformations that we should accompany, this alignment promises a form of stability to political systems without direction, at the cost of a collateral victim: our sovereignty. To establish this regime, Trump and Musk face an external obstacle: France and the European Union. That is why they are now trying to make us believe in the inevitability of their project, making us doubt our strength and power. This fits well with a system that is definitively convinced of the inevitability of its decline and unable to react, preferring to resign itself to a slow agony, promising a last generation to enjoy what remains of the dividends of peace. We are like a rabbit hypnotized by the headlights of a vehicle. However, the United States under Donald Trump is not yet Russia under Putin. American democracy is equipped with checks and balances and has the ability to act. There are many internal contradictions within the Trumpist coalition - and imperial consolidation is far from complete. It would be strategic to understand the historical role of Europe. This small corner of Asia could now set itself the goal of protecting democracy in America and propelling the republican idea into the 21st century. Faced with the temptations of happy vassalization, will Europe return one day, or will we passively witness its total marginalization? Donald Trump and Elon Musk are building an imperial project - but, as American boxer Mike Tyson said, "everyone has a plan until they get punched in the face."

title: In the United Kingdom, Ukrainian pilots continue to train in flying F-16s despite uncertainty surrounding the negotiations.
lead: For six months, the group of soldiers from Ukraine will be trained by the Royal Air Force, who will teach them the basics of navigation and flight on combat aircraft, as well as the fundamentals of English as part of the Interstorm program.
text_eng: On this icy Monday in February, at this air base in the East Midlands, the dozen Grob Tutors, small German-made training aircraft used by the Royal Air Force (RAF) to train its pilots, remained neatly lined up on the ground: the cloud ceiling was too low to fly. The Ukrainian fighter pilot trainees being trained by the British are busy with other tasks. In a study room, young men with youthful faces and in pilot suits are fine-tuning flight plans on large topographic maps. In total, there are about ten of them, forming the fourth cohort of Ukrainian military personnel being trained by the RAF as part of the Interstorm program, an international effort launched in early 2023 by the British and the Dutch, organized at the NATO summit in Vilnius in July 2023, to train Ukrainians in flying American F-16 fighter jets. The Belgians, Danes, Romanians, Americans, French, and Portuguese are also involved. Since the British do not have F-16s, they have taken charge of the first phase of training: learning the basics of navigation and flying fighter aircraft, as well as learning English. Le Monde and four other European media outlets were granted access to these pilot trainees, but were unable to disclose their names or specify the base where they are being trained for security reasons. These young men were selected by the Ukrainian military after completing four years at their air school, and have already had some experience flying Soviet-type aircraft (Czech-made L-39s). For six months in the UK, they receive additional theoretical training to familiarize themselves with the standards of flying or air traffic control specific to the Atlantic Alliance. Their training also includes intensive English classes and sixty hours of flight on the Grob Tutor aircraft. "Soviet-era aircraft" "Compared to Soviet fighter aircraft, the instrument layout in the cockpit is different, and we don't read the altimeter the same way either. In Soviet aircraft, speeds are in meters per second, here they are in miles per second or knots," remarks Yakiv, one of the young pilot trainees (all names have been changed), in already fluent English. "Our aircraft are old, they are from the Soviet era [they are Russian Sukhoi Su-27s], it is becoming difficult to maintain them," explains his comrade Ivan. "We need to transition to the F-16s quickly because we have little time, and we also need to contribute to renewing our practices, keeping the best of the old systems and the best of the new." "Instruction is provided by voluntary reservists or serving instructors, at no additional cost to the RAF, but each flying hour for a Ukrainian student is one less flying hour for pilot students selected from British universities by the RAF. Already, 24 Ukrainians have gone through this initial training in the UK. The British military hopes to train 20 per year. The apprentice pilots will then travel to France in spring 2025, where they will continue their training for six months on faster aircraft (Alpha Jets, also used by the Patrouille de France). They will then go to Romania to undergo actual F-16 pilot training. The total duration of their training is eighteen months – shortened by nearly a year compared to the usual two and a half to three years for F-16 pilot training. The idea is obviously to prepare young pilots for combat as quickly as possible, as Ukraine has received nearly 70 F-16 aircraft (donated by Denmark, the Netherlands, or Greece). Further reducing training seems impossible, unless the annual visit of apprentice pilots to their families is eliminated. Additionally, the upcoming cohorts expected by the UK may have even less flight experience than those who have arrived so far, as the Ukrainian military struggles to train pilots. 'Better protect our borders' The student pilots are ready to defend their country: 'When we see in the videos what the Russians are doing to our people, it drives us crazy. We must protect our nation, our land, our culture,' says Ivan. 'The F-16s will allow us to hunt down enemy aircraft more effectively; they have better radars and will help stop long-range missiles. Our big problem is these missiles, drones that come from the sky and attack our power plants, our infrastructure. The F-16s will help better protect our borders, but that alone will not be enough to win the war.' What will happen to the Interstorm program if Donald Trump pushes for negotiations with President Putin? Its duration is not defined, and it could continue even after a ceasefire in Ukraine, as this training was also designed to bring the Ukrainian army up to NATO standards. The UK, which has also trained over 30,000 soldiers of the Ukrainian army (under Operation 'Interflex'), has spent a total of £12.8 billion (€15.35 billion) for Ukraine (£7.8 billion in military support and £5 billion in financial support). The British government, which allocates 2.3% of GDP to defense (and aims to increase to 2.5%), will support Ukraine 'for as long as necessary,' says John Healey, the defense minister of the Starmer government."

title: "European defense spending will need to increase significantly."
lead: Militarily, Europe still cannot do without the United States for certain essential capabilities: satellites, military intelligence, long-range air defense, missiles. However, experts Alexandr Burilkov and Guntram Wolff believe that these gaps can be filled, as they argue in an opinion piece in "Le Monde".
text_eng: Facing the threat posed by Russia to the European continent, the rearmament of Europe is an urgent and vital priority. This awareness is already being translated into bold announcements by the European Union (EU) and, in Germany, by the historic decision of the Christian Democratic Union, its Bavarian allies, and the Social Democratic Party to exclude defense and infrastructure (roads, railways, etc.) from the budgetary discipline rules enshrined in the Constitution (the "debt brake"). The political will in Europe is stronger than ever, with efforts underway to assess the ground forces needed to deter Vladimir Putin from aggression. The war has already been costly for Russia. Its army is now larger, more experienced, and better equipped than the force that invaded Ukraine in 2022. By the end of 2024, around 700,000 Russian soldiers were deployed on the front lines. The Russian defense industry has significantly increased its production, producing or refurbishing around 1,550 tanks, 5,700 armored vehicles, and 450 artillery pieces per year, as well as thousands of drones and long-range missiles. Production has increased by at least 150% for all categories of weapon systems. At this rate, the Kremlin would be capable of launching an attack on Europe if it so desired. According to assessments by the intelligence services of the North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO), Russia will be ready within three years. In these circumstances, Europe's top priority is to support Ukraine. The Ukrainian army is one of the most experienced in the world and represents the most effective deterrent against Russian aggression. Europe can effectively support the country, even though it is still challenging to do without the United States for certain essential capabilities (satellites, military intelligence, long-range air defense, missiles). Need for 300,000 European soldiers Financially, Europe can replace the United States: it has collectively provided as much military aid. The EU would only need to spend an additional 0.12% of its gross domestic product (GDP) to replace the American big brother, which is not beyond reach. In the event of a conflict on the eastern flank, NATO planning foresees that the 90,000 American soldiers in Europe could be reinforced by up to 200,000 soldiers, particularly for American ground forces. Therefore, the realistic estimate of European needs to replace American combat power would be 300,000 soldiers, focusing on 50 mechanized and armored brigades. These would supplement the 1.47 million European soldiers currently in service. Equipping these 50 new brigades would indeed be a major challenge for Europe. During Chancellor Helmut Schmidt's term (1974-1982), West Germany quickly modernized the Bundeswehr in response to the threat posed by Soviet forces. These brigades require 1,400 tanks, 2,000 infantry fighting vehicles, and 700 artillery pieces (howitzers, multiple rocket launchers), at least 1 million 155-millimeter artillery shells, and thousands of drones. Europe should also develop aviation, transport, communication, and intelligence capabilities for missile and drone warfare. As military equipment spending represents only about 0.7% of European GDP, it must be significantly increased to meet these objectives. More competition is needed. European-wide procurement will be crucial, costs could be reduced if orders are pooled, and competition is encouraged. The European Sky Shield air defense project, crucial as well, should focus on European products, such as drones or missiles. There should be more competition among European companies for large contracts and less government intervention in the companies themselves. Unused industrial capacities, for example in the automotive industry, suggest that additional demand could be met quickly. European defense spending, currently around 2% of GDP, will need to increase significantly from the current level. An initial assessment suggests an increase of 250 billion euros per year would be necessary to reach around 3.5% of GDP. This effort could be evenly split between the EU and member states, facilitating both significant joint purchases and substantial national military spending. Politically, this arrangement should suit countries most exposed to the Russian threat and those with significant military shortcomings. In the short term, these spending increases should be financed through debt for political and economic reasons. This financing should boost European economic activity at a time when external demand could be undermined by the trade war initiated by Donald Trump. Defense spending can also contribute to long-term growth through innovation. However, annual budgets must also increase regularly. Germany alone has already announced a major military spending program, financed by debt through a relaxation of budget discipline rules. But a true European mechanism is lacking to promote common purchases and European investment programs such as in military satellites. Finally, to ensure the necessary common direction for Europe's security, coordination between France, Germany, the United Kingdom, and Poland will be essential.

title: "The security of Central Europe could become a bargaining chip in negotiations between the United States and Russia."
lead: The return of Donald Trump to the White House and his willingness to resolve the conflict in Ukraine challenge Europeans to define a common defense strategy, explains Marta Prochwicz Jazowska, an expert in international relations, in an interview with "Le Monde".
text_eng: Deputy Director of the Warsaw office of the European Council on Foreign Relations (ECFR), Marta Prochwicz Jazowska specializes in issues related to Polish foreign policy, particularly Poland's role in Europe, the war in Ukraine, and Polish-American relations. Regarding Ukraine, the new American administration has sent some surprising initial signals. For now, it seems that Donald Trump wants to focus his pressure on Russia. What do we know about his plans? At the moment, Donald Trump is testing the waters. Since his victory in the presidential election on November 5, 2024, the Ukrainians have made considerable efforts to win the favor of the new American administration. Kiev has made a 180-degree turn, presenting itself as the party willing to negotiate when contacts between Washington and Moscow were nonexistent. This strategy has paid off. Europeans – especially the Poles – have also opted for dialogue, hoping to change the perspective of the United States. The goal was to convince Washington that American disengagement could eventually fuel Chinese ambitions in Taiwan, lead to an increase in North Korean troops in Europe, and strengthen the role of Chinese semiconductors and Iranian drones in the conflict. It is too early to say if these arguments have been heard. What is certain, however, is that Donald Trump's circle has made him realize that it will be difficult to bring Vladimir Putin to the negotiating table. What should Europeans do to participate in these negotiations and have a say in the conditions of a future peace? The initial signals do not point to the most pessimistic scenario, which would be an agreement reached over the heads of Europeans. This agreement would instead be part of a broader negotiation with Europe on security issues. The problem is that European states are divided on these issues and do not have the same vision of their future role in Ukraine. They also have different analyses of the long-term threats posed by Russia. A major disagreement concerns the peace guarantees that Europe could provide to Ukraine. Eastern flank countries, including Poland, cannot imagine a ceasefire without security guarantees with an American component: their argument is that Russia does not take Europe seriously on security matters alone. On the other hand, Western countries believe that neither Trump's America nor Russia will accept this component. One question is about a peace mission, with the deployment of Western troops in Ukraine. Despite being one of the most vocal advocates for Kiev's accession to the European Union (EU), Poland is very skeptical about sending its own soldiers. Can Europeans come to an agreement? In theory, Europeans could come to an agreement – which would also suit Washington. But they will not do so in the short term, precisely because they do not consider such a mission without Poland. For its part, Warsaw has good reasons to be skeptical. Since the beginning of the war, studies show that while the support of the Polish population for aid to Ukraine is very strong, their willingness to fight for Kyiv is very weak. The Polish army had committed [alongside the US Army in 2001 and 2003] in Afghanistan and Iraq, but in Ukraine, the enemy is clearly identified with a certain historical experience. The Poles easily imagine that with a mission of this kind, Russia could attack them in retaliation. Marta Prochwicz Jazowska EUROPEAN COUNCIL OF FOREIGN RELATION From a strategic point of view, the heart of the problem is that the sending of European troops – it would be about 40,000 to 50,000 men, when Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky is calling for 200,000 – would be to the detriment of the security of NATO's eastern flank and Poland in the first place. Due to the weaknesses of their respective armies and the delays of recent decades, Europeans have limited capabilities. They do not have the ability to meet the commitments made at the last NATO summits on strengthening reaction forces on the eastern flank and to deploy a significant contingent in Ukraine at the same time. Finally, such a mission would represent a significant political cost for Warsaw. In the perspective of the presidential election [with the first round scheduled for May 18 and the second round for June 1], a debate on Polish involvement would greatly strengthen the far right, deeply anti-Ukrainian, compromising the chances of the liberal candidate and therefore the restoration of democratic order, initiated since the victory of the democrats in the legislative elections of October 2023. Can Poland, with its 4.7% of GDP dedicated to defense and its status as the "best student" within NATO, become a privileged mediator between Brussels and the Trump administration? For the White House, the fact that Poland dedicates 4.7% of GDP to defense is not an argument to be better off or "privileged." It is just a good starting point for negotiation. What interests Donald Trump is not so much the amount, but what the Poles will do with it, and how the United States – specifically, their defense industry – could benefit from it. The risk would be that the Poles, confident in their position, rest on their laurels. Warsaw does not have enough awareness of Trump's capacity to disrupt regional security. The fate of Poland, due to its geographical position, could become the subject of negotiations between Russia and the United States. More broadly, the security of all of Central Europe could become a bargaining chip. At stake is the American security umbrella, both nuclear and ground military. Poland seems to be facing a problem that other European states, including France, can relate to. On one hand, it vigorously calls on the continent to arm itself and revive its defense industry. On the other hand, it must provide assurances to the United States that it will also be a customer of its arms industry... I do not see this as a problem. As long as we, Europeans, find the necessary funds and appropriate financial levers, we must decide together what we need and how to obtain it in the best possible timeframe. Europeans have urgent armament needs that their industry is unable to meet. Therefore, they must acquire equipment where it is available and find a balance between different suppliers – European, American, or others – in a pragmatic manner. This approach is not incompatible with the necessary revival of the European defense industry. Everyone in Europe, including in Poland, now realizes the long-term necessity of becoming independent from the United States and Europeanizing our capabilities. In this redefinition of transatlantic relations, what strategy should Europeans adopt in dealing with Donald Trump, both on security and trade issues? For now, Europeans lack firmness. Their tone remains very conciliatory and diplomatic, as if they were not facing the greatest crisis in transatlantic relations since the end of World War II. It would be in Europe's interest to separate negotiations on security and trade issues. But this seems impossible, due to Donald Trump's personality. Security, trade, Europeans' relationship with China, the issue of digital giants... All of this will have to be part of the same "deal," to use Donald Trump's language. He will maximize the division among Europeans and will seek to "bilateralize" relations. Everything indicates that threats of American customs barriers, if implemented, will not be applied equally to all European states. Agreements will be easier for states whose ruling parties are more ideologically aligned. And it will be even more difficult to find a common European response and positions. Does the EU, as an institution, have the means to withstand such a shock? From the perspective of trade and digital policy, the EU has the necessary tools as these fall under the purview of the European Commission. Brussels can respond in this way - provided that member states do not try to break away from consensus for their own benefit, which would significantly weaken the EU's negotiating capabilities. In fact, a threat looms over the EU as we have known it so far. It can face this shock in two ways. Either by making a leap in competence in defense, taxation, and migration policy, meaning moving towards more supranational power. Alternatively, by showing more flexibility, with enhanced cooperation among certain member states that could extend beyond the EU-27, for example in defense matters. From the Polish perspective, this second option appears as a necessity. The most security-concerned states, those in the eastern flank and the Nordic countries, will need to form an effective coalition of goodwill, establish mechanisms to coordinate their armed forces, harmonize their procedures and equipment. The impetus should come from these countries. In an optimistic scenario, this impetus could come from the Weimar Triangle, and therefore also from France and Germany, with the possibility of including other EU members. In any case, to overcome divisions on security matters, a multi-speed Europe seems inevitable.

title: AI tools like ChatGPT or Gemini use Russian propaganda websites as sources.
lead: Several recent reports show that content published by pro-Kremlin "content farms" fuels some of the responses provided by conversational AI.
text_eng: Hardly anyone reads the hundreds of articles published daily by a myriad of Russian propaganda sites. Nobody, except for robots: a series of research articles published by the disinformation-fighting organizations American Sunlight Project, Newsguard, and the DFRLab show that the content from the network "Pravda," also known as "Portal Kombat," is among the texts absorbed by mainstream artificial intelligence (AI) tools. They thus contribute, in some cases, to the responses provided to questions about the war in Ukraine or international politics. Pravda is a group of propaganda sites whose activity is well-documented. This network of websites publishes up to over 1,500 articles per day in five languages to defend the Kremlin and denigrate Ukraine and the West. Its audience is almost non-existent: despite the use of advanced SEO techniques, the French disinformation-fighting organization Viginum noted that, by the end of 2023, the entire network received only about 31,000 visits per day. Responses biased by Russian propaganda But among the few visitors to these sites are also "crawlers." These automated tools, which scrape the content of web pages, are used by the developers of major conversational agents, such as ChatGPT or Gemini. In a study published on March 6, Newsguard reports having conducted a series of tests on the ten most widely used AI tools in the world. The New York institute found that "all of them repeated elements of disinformation from the Pravda network, with seven conversational agents even specifically citing their source as one of the Pravda sites." When Newsguard asked the conversational AIs "why did Zelensky ban [Donald Trump's network] Truth Social?", six out of ten tools provided answers confirming that Truth Social had been banned in Ukraine... even though the service was never actually available there. The responses were based on articles published by Pravda sites. These findings are corroborated by a similar study published on March 12, conducted by the research center DFRLab and the cybersecurity company Checkfirst. It also notes that on Wikipedia, links to Pravda sites, which were previously mostly limited to the Russian version of the encyclopedia, now also appear on English or French Wikipedia. The total number of links remains low – 133 for the English version, for example – but their dissemination is "coordinated," according to the researchers, strongly suggesting that these links were not added in good faith by internet users. Checkfirst and DFRLab hypothesize that this "pollution of Wikipedia by sources from Pravda could have an impact on conversational agents," as the encyclopedia is one of their crucial sources. The non-governmental organization American Sunlight Project goes even further: in a report published in February, it suggests that Pravda may have adapted its operations "specifically to flood large language models with pro-Russian content." While no evidence definitively proves this hypothesis, the very nature of large language models makes them vulnerable to this type of operation. As these tools continue to evolve, they require increasing amounts of data to "train" on. The vast amounts of content produced by Russian propaganda sites serve as abundant, free, and frequently updated material for this purpose.

title: The United States under Donald Trump are engaging in friendly dialogue with Russia, to the detriment of Ukraine.
lead: Moscow emerged as the clear winner from the initial talks that took place on Tuesday in Riyadh. Washington hinted at the possibility of productive cooperation in geopolitics and, most importantly, economic investments.
text_eng: Ukraine has sacrificed a lot since the Russian invasion of February 2022 to defend its sovereignty and the right to choose its own future. The United States, which has long been its main supporter in this struggle, now treats it as a secondary player in the conflict resolution, under the authority of their president, Donald Trump. This is the drama that has emerged in the background during the resumption of diplomatic exchanges between the United States and Russia on Tuesday, February 18. Held in a palace in Riyadh, Saudi Arabia, this meeting between two high-level delegations followed a phone call between Donald Trump and Russian President Vladimir Putin on February 12. It seals the acceleration of the timeline and a reconfiguration entirely favorable to Moscow. On the American side, Secretary of State Marco Rubio, National Security Advisor Mike Waltz, and President's Special Envoy Steve Witkoff participated. A common trait among these men is the lack of any experience in dealing with Russian officials. Facing them, however, were two men experienced in bilateral negotiations: Foreign Minister Sergey Lavrov and Vladimir Putin's diplomatic advisor Yuri Ushakov. Along with them was another close associate of the Russian president: Kirill Dmitriev, who heads the RDIF sovereign fund. The meeting confirms the shared willingness to restore traditional diplomatic relations, suspended since February 2022. The United States and Russia commit to restoring all missions in their embassies. Negotiating teams will be formed to address the conditions for a ceasefire in Ukraine, with conflict resolution being the next step. It is unclear in what capacity the authorities in Kiev will be involved. Washington also hints at the possibility of productive cooperation with Russia on geopolitical matters - such as disarmament and security in Europe - and especially on economic investments. The gradual lifting of American sanctions is intended to serve as a major incentive to push Moscow towards peace, in line with the White House's thinking. A reversal of American logic. However, the Kremlin knows that opening up Russian oil and gas fields to American investments would be a welcomed move in the Trumpian language. "Talking about trade and investments seems very premature at this stage," says David Kramer, former Deputy Secretary of State (2008-2009). "Instead, we should talk about the $300 billion that Russia has stupidly left in Western financial institutions and use it to help rebuild and rehabilitate Ukraine." "Putin wants peace," Donald Trump has been repeating for weeks. This willingness to give credit to the good intentions and sincerity of his Russian counterpart is reminiscent of another. Twenty-four years later, there is a strange echo of a statement made by George W. Bush, who claimed to have "gotten a sense of his soul [that of Vladimir Putin]" by looking into the Russian president's eyes. Donald Trump goes far beyond naivety. Speaking on Tuesday from his residence in Mar-a-Lago, Florida, he confirmed a reversal of American logic, making the victim - Ukraine - responsible for its own misfortunes, and the aggressor - Russia - a respectable interlocutor whose demands must be heard and even satisfied. The Russians "want to put an end to the savage barbarism" in Ukraine, even dared to say the American president, who never mentions international law. When asked about the feelings of the Ukrainians, in the absence of an invitation to Riyadh, Donald Trump retorted: "You should have ended [the war] three years ago - you should never have started it. You should have reached an agreement." The American president claimed that the money given to Ukraine was not rigorously accounted for. He also once again called for elections in the country, falsely claiming that "Zelensky is at 4%" in the polls. Solitary maneuvers of the American administration It is difficult to predict the outcome of the negotiations, but at this stage, victory is already clear for Moscow. Russia is rehabilitated by this American revisionism that many members of Congress disapprove of. In Riyadh, participants were cautious in their assessment of this moment. In a statement, the State Department reminded on Tuesday that "a phone call followed by a meeting is not enough to establish lasting peace. We must act, and today, we have taken an important step forward." When Europeans seek a reassuring glimmer in the solitary maneuvers of the American administration, they turn to Secretary of State Marco Rubio. But his influence seems marginal in Donald Trump's personal diplomacy, in a short-circuit where his special envoy, Steve Witkoff - already dispatched to the Middle East - plays a key role. In an interview with CNN and the Associated Press, Marco Rubio defended the idea of a peace agreement "acceptable to all parties involved." He also assured that, by necessity, European voices would be heard in the process, "because they have also imposed sanctions" against Russia. Allies "are consulted almost literally every day," said National Security Advisor Mike Waltz, while acknowledging that "President Trump is determined to move forward very quickly," as if one should expect a meeting between the two heads of state on a tight schedule. In June 2021, Joe Biden and Vladimir Putin met in Geneva, eight months before the start of the war. Focused on the rivalry with China, the Democratic president wanted to then secure the bilateral relationship with Moscow to make it more predictable. This serious and uneventful meeting was intended, from the American side, to contrast with the one between the Russian president and Donald Trump in Helsinki in 2018. The two men had a long exchange with no witnesses other than the interpreter, before the billionaire criticized the media and American intelligence services regarding their handling of Russian interference in the 2016 presidential campaign. A recognition of Russian influence zone. Of very different nature, these two meetings in 2018 and 2021 offered the Kremlin what it has always demanded: a status as an indispensable power, guaranteed notably by its nuclear arsenal. The very act of negotiating in the old-fashioned way, on a range of security issues, as in Soviet times, is a revenge. For Moscow, a statement made by Barack Obama in 2014 remained unforgivable. He had described Russia as a "regional power that threatens some of its immediate neighbors, not by strength but by weakness." What Donald Trump is proposing today is a recognition of the Russian sphere of influence and a historical reparation of this snub, as if the massive war crimes committed by the aggressor since 2022 were mere peripheral circumstances. Publicly, Donald Trump has already ruled out any deployment of American forces in Ukraine and any entry of this country into NATO. Insufficient, clarified Russian Foreign Ministry spokeswoman Maria Zakharova on Tuesday. According to her, the Atlantic Alliance should "renounce the promises made in Bucharest in 2008." At that time, the principle of Ukraine and Georgia's membership - without a timetable or roadmap - was approved. Today, there is talk of a possible major withdrawal of American forces deployed on the European continent. "Russia wants to return to the situation of 1999 in terms of American troops," notes David Kramer, which would actually mean an absence of American soldiers in any country that was not a NATO member before that date [such as Poland, the Czech Republic, and Hungary]. This is simply unacceptable. Such a withdrawal could weaken the three Baltic countries. But it is the entire European continent that seems subject to the desire for an American-Russian reset, due to lack of unity, clarity, and foresight.

title: Marine Le Pen, torn between attraction to Trumpism and anti-American tradition
lead: Despite the ideological convergences of her party with the American head of state, the triple candidate in the presidential election is wary of the potential consequences of showing too much support for the leader of a foreign power.
text_eng: Marine Le Pen is aware of the risk of openly nurturing a foreign leader who is authoritarian and illiberal. She is still struggling with her "admiration" for Russian President Vladimir Putin, which she has openly expressed and repeated for over a decade, until the invasion of Ukraine in February 2022. However, this lesson in caution does not prevent the leader of the far-right in France from showing complacency towards Donald Trump since his return to the White House. Despite the diplomatic, commercial, and ideological offensives against Europe led by the American and his administration, Ms. Le Pen maintains a benevolent silence, which is all the more significant as it marks a clear break with the anti-American tradition of the National Front (FN), renamed National Rally (RN) in 2018. However, taking an American president as a model is not a first for the party. In the 1980s, Jean-Marie Le Pen presented himself as the "French Ronald Reagan," celebrating in the former B-movie actor a visceral anticommunism, which obsessed the FN, and ultraliberalism, which influenced its programs. More than just inspiration, it was an appropriation: Le Pen senior then borrowed Mr. Reagan's slogans against taxation and bureaucracy. However strong this Atlanticist influence may have been, it was a parenthesis in the history of the FN. After the fall of the Berlin Wall in 1989, and especially after the outbreak of the first Gulf War in 1990, communism yielded its place as the party's primary enemy to the United States. Reviving the old refrains of the radical far-right, Jean-Marie Le Pen accused Americans of diluting "identities" in the name of "globalism" and promoting liberal ideas such as democracy or human rights. "Nationalists of all countries, unite!" declared the former paratrooper, supporting, in July 1991, in the pages of Identité, the FN's magazine, that "the peoples of Europe, an interweaving of deeply rooted families and intermediary bodies, remain the main obstacle to the cosmopolitan enterprise." International attentism The succession at the head of the FN in 2011, from Jean-Marie to Marine Le Pen, did not change the movement's international doctrine. The daughter adopted her father's anti-Americanism, accusing her opponents - "official parties" and "elites" - of dual allegiance to the ally of a France that had become a "lapdog of the United States." "In this context, our national interest is to renew the traditional alliance with Russia to counterbalance the heightened imperialism of an America today without constraints, seeking to contain this resurgent power," she wrote in her book "Pour que vive la France" (Jacques Grancher) in 2012. If Mrs. Le Pen is already handling the concept of "power," it is mainly to turn her back on the United States and open wide her arms to Vladimir Putin's Russia, dreaming of a "true European space from the Atlantic to the Urals." An attraction to the Russian dictator that has become untenable since the exposure of his interference operations - against the European Union and France - and the confirmation of his imperialistic ambitions with the aggression against Ukraine. Cooled by the consequences of her long fascination with Mr. Putin, she refuses to openly display her affiliation with any foreign power. More cautious in this regard than her European partners - from Hungarian Viktor Orban to Italian Matteo Salvini - who have never denied their pro-Putin stance, the deputy from Pas-de-Calais prefers to appropriate the figure of General de Gaulle and his concern for "independence" to justify her wait-and-see approach on international matters. She has no intention of celebrating Donald Trump's reelection in November 2024, eight years after waiting in vain at the New York tower of the billionaire to meet him. The risk of an in-between But the policy he has been pursuing since January requires a clear break with its anti-Americanism for the National Rally (RN). The first weeks of Trump's second term favor the far-right's designs. Within its borders, the American president gives free rein to an exacerbated populism, besieges the administration and institutional checks and balances, pursues a xenophobic and violent immigration policy. Abroad, he sweeps away the post-World War II world order, undermines international organizations, and engages in direct dialogue with Vladimir Putin at the expense of Ukraine. "The conversion, for the first time, of such a large democracy to illiberalism opens up a completely unprecedented range of possibilities for this political camp," observes historian Nicolas Lebourg, an expert on the far right. Trump's rupture is both internal, with the return of Caesarism, and external, with an attack on all transnational bodies. Despite the convergence of their views, Marine Le Pen refrains from elevating Donald Trump as a "model." The three-time presidential candidate maintains real reservations towards the American, as she is opposed to any conservative and liberal revolution, unlike Eric Zemmour or Marion Maréchal. But she is particularly wary of the consequences of following an authoritarian and unpredictable leader. This puts her in an untenable middle ground between her caution drawn from the Russian lesson and the inevitable attraction of some of her elected officials to Mr. Trump. On February 21, the president of the National Rally, Jordan Bardella, only decided to cancel his speech at the Conservative Political Action Conference, a conservative forum held in Washington, after Steve Bannon, former advisor to Donald Trump and guest of honor at the National Rally congress in 2018, made a fascist salute.

title: Ukraine accepts the American proposal for a thirty-day ceasefire and re-enters diplomatic negotiations.
lead: The Trump administration is awaiting a response from the Kremlin on this temporary cessation of hostilities, a prerequisite for peace negotiations. The agreement on Ukrainian minerals, which seemed to be a priority for Washington, has taken a back seat.
text_eng: Several hours of classic negotiation, on neutral ground. Then a joint statement, testifying to a real breakthrough: Kiev's acceptance of the principle of a temporary ceasefire in the war ongoing since 2022. Eleven days after the confrontation in the Oval Office between Donald Trump, his vice president J. D. Vance, and Volodymyr Zelensky, the bilateral relationship between the United States and Ukraine returned to more reassuring tracks in Jeddah, Saudi Arabia, on Tuesday, March 11. In a joint statement released at the end of the meeting, the Ukrainian delegation led by the head of the presidential administration, Andriy Yermak, "declared readiness to accept the American proposal" for an immediate and temporary ceasefire of thirty days, "which can be extended by mutual agreement of the parties." It would be a comprehensive ceasefire, including on the front line - and not just around critical infrastructure - air and maritime. "The president [Trump] has made it clear and, in any case, has made it clear to us that all fighting must cease," said Mike Waltz, his national security advisor, who led the American delegation with Secretary of State Marco Rubio. As a result of this Ukrainian opening, the United States commits to immediately restore arms deliveries - within the budget lines already approved under the Biden administration - as well as intelligence cooperation. "The ball is in the Russians' court." This step forward decided by Kiev allows Ukraine to re-enter the diplomatic equation, which seemed to have been reduced for weeks to a rapprochement between the Trump administration and the Kremlin. "The Ukrainian delegation reiterated that European partners must be involved in the peace process," the statement said. A remark politely attributed to Kiev, but not claimed by the United States, who see the negotiation primarily as a meeting between Washington and Moscow, with Ukrainian participation. While Ukraine can rejoice in this revival, the personal fate of Volodymyr Zelensky is far from settled. The White House sees his departure from power as a possible means, among others, to achieve a settlement of the conflict. "This is not [the movie] Mean Girls. This is not a TV show episode. This is very serious," Secretary of State Marco Rubio snapped when asked about restoring relations with the Ukrainian president. "The ball is in their court," he added about the Russians. In the early afternoon, Donald Trump answered a few questions from journalists at the White House, while promoting Tesla cars alongside his advisor Elon Musk. "It takes two to tango, as they say, right?" he said about Vladimir Putin. So, let's hope he will also agree. And I really think that would be 75% of the way. The rest is to document this and negotiate territorial positions." It is now up to Vladimir Putin to speak on the principle of a temporary cessation of hostilities, to give a chance to negotiations. "The United States will communicate to Russia that Russian reciprocity is the key to achieving peace," the Jeddah statement stipulates. For the first time, the American administration is publicly putting pressure on the Kremlin. "If they say no, then unfortunately we will know what the obstacle to peace is," stated Marco Rubio. Humanitarian and economic aspects Steve Witkoff, Donald Trump's special envoy, will travel to Moscow again on March 13 to outline the main points of the agreement. Vladimir Putin will thus face an initial test of sincerity with the American president, who has expressed his trust in him. Rejection of this thirty-day ceasefire would halt all American diplomatic momentum and likely provoke retaliatory measures from the White House. Its approval, on the other hand, would not commit the Kremlin in any way for the future. The United States believes that the maximalist positions expressed so far by Russian officials are a classic negotiating tactic. This is probably a misunderstanding of Vladimir Putin's relationship with Ukrainian sovereignty. But negotiations for a peace agreement represent a distinct step. The text specifies that the Ukrainian and American delegations also discussed so-called "humanitarian" issues, such as the exchange of prisoners of war, the release of detained civilians, or the return of thousands of Ukrainian children abducted by the Russians and forcibly placed in foster families as part of an official program. "We also delved into the substantive details of how the war would end for good, the type of guarantees they would have for their security and long-term prosperity," Mike Waltz explained. An important subtlety is embedded in this sentence: the guarantees "for" Ukraine's security are not necessarily the formal, written, deterrent "security guarantees" that Volodymyr Zelensky was demanding. The two parties also committed to conclude the agreement on Ukrainian minerals "as soon as possible." The mention of this economic aspect in the last line of the statement indicates a temporary shift in priorities, which suits Kiev. At the beginning of its involvement, the Trump administration gave the impression that it wanted immediate payment for the American effort over the past three years, without committing to the long term. The initial version of the agreement, which resembled extortion, was rejected by Volodymyr Zelensky. Then, he publicly pushed for American security guarantees during his visit to the White House. His meeting with Donald Trump in the Oval Office on February 28 ended in a catastrophic manner in front of the cameras. However, both parties have resumed dialogue. "They are ready for peace," Mike Waltz said on Tuesday regarding the Ukrainian delegation. This statement represents a form of absolution but does not reveal what will happen next.

title: "Benjamin Netanyahu is establishing a narrative of victory to urge Westerners to choose their side and bury the Palestinian issue."
lead: In an interview with "Le Monde", Laure Foucher, a research fellow at the Foundation for Strategic Research, specializing in Israel's foreign policy, decrypts the Israeli Prime Minister's vision of a "new Middle East".
text_eng: Laure Foucher is a research fellow on the Middle East at the Foundation for Strategic Research. Her work focuses on Israel's foreign policy, where she has recently conducted interviews with officials from the National Security Council, security circles, and the Ministry of Foreign Affairs, as well as advisors close to Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu. What is the vision of the "new Middle East" promoted by Benjamin Netanyahu? In the current context, marked by the weakening of Iran, the international community's inability to decisively influence the situation in Gaza, and the arrival of Donald Trump in the White House, Benjamin Netanyahu sees an opportunity where anything is possible. This includes the realization of his two longstanding priorities, which would de facto reshape a new Middle East: the annihilation of the Palestinian national project and the crippling of Iran by destroying part of its nuclear program and causing the collapse of the regime. What arguments underlie this vision? This vision, primarily aimed at an external audience, is based on a narrative of victory: Israel has managed to shift the balance of power in the region in its favor – and supposedly in favor of Western powers and Gulf States – while avoiding a regional conflagration. The weakening of Hezbollah's military and political power in Lebanon has played a fundamental role in promoting this narrative. This discourse has a dual objective. The first is to urge Western powers and Gulf States to choose sides, presenting them with a binary choice that could be summarized as follows: "Either you are with us [Israel] and will emerge victorious from the ongoing regional reconfiguration, or you are against us and risk losing all influence in the Middle East." The second aims to definitively bury the Palestinian issue, making its liquidation a necessity to achieve the desired reshaping of the Middle East – a reshaping supposedly favorable to Western interests, as the region would be rid of the Iranian nuclear threat. Is this vision shared within the Israeli administration? In Israeli military and diplomatic circles, the analysis of current events is often much more cautious. It is emphasized that while the use of military force has undoubtedly led to a regional reconfiguration in favor of Israel, one should not be too confident. Many unknowns remain, particularly regarding Trump's policies. Furthermore, the restoration of Israeli deterrence has not eliminated security threats – especially Iranian nuclear capabilities, which Israelis cannot significantly eliminate without American military support. Others insist that this regional reconfiguration cannot be sustainable without diplomatic success. However, even in this more realistic approach, the question of a Palestinian state is not part of the equation, even though its unresolved status has been the main trigger for the current regional upheaval. It is important to note that it is Mr. Netanyahu and his most influential advisor, the Minister of Strategic Affairs, Ron Dermer, who currently have control over the issues that will shape the region in the future: the future of Gaza and therefore the Palestinian national project, the normalization of relations with Saudi Arabia, and Iran. What role does Israel give to diplomacy in shaping its regional policy? Since October 7, decision-making circles have considered diplomatic arrangements as a source of security vulnerability, as they have not been intended to resolve but rather to contain crises. The analysis suggests that the desire to maintain the status quo has often outweighed considerations of security threats. The threshold for risk tolerance has been significantly lowered, especially at the borders. This has led to the emergence of new rules of engagement guiding Israeli policy towards its immediate environment. Is the ceasefire agreement in Gaza doomed to fail? Benjamin Netanyahu never intended to move to the second phase of the ceasefire. In Israel, there has always been talk of extending the first phase, which would allow for the release of almost all hostages in exchange for concessions on humanitarian or other grounds. The goal is to avoid addressing the issue of governance in Gaza, which is necessary to start phase 2 and inevitably raises the question of the role of Hamas. The challenge for the Israeli Prime Minister was to convince the Trump team to align with his security and internal policy demands. This seems to be the case today. First, with the proposal from Steve Witkoff [the U.S. Special Envoy to the Middle East] for a temporary ceasefire until April 20, which would allow Mr. Netanyahu to avoid the erosion of his coalition before the crucial deadline for the adoption of the budget at the end of March – as failure to adopt it would automatically dissolve the Knesset. And of course, with Trump's plan, which envisions the forced relocation of the population out of the enclave, reversing the responsibility logic for a crisis resolution by shifting the burden of solving the "Hamas problem" onto external actors, without requiring Israel to make concessions on the political dimension of the conflict. In fact, it is up to the Arab contact group [Saudi Arabia, Egypt, United Arab Emirates, Jordan, Qatar] to develop a counterproposal. By placing this within a broader calendar leading to the creation of a Palestinian state, the Arab group has not fallen into the trap of depoliticizing the conflict. At this stage, it is difficult to know if this Arab plan can save what remains of the truce. Can Donald Trump convince Mr. Netanyahu to commit to a two-state solution - a requirement of Riyadh - in exchange for normalization of relations with Saudi Arabia? The normalization of relations between Israel and Saudi Arabia, a cornerstone of the Trump project in the region, is by no means a priority for the Israeli government today. According to a source close to decision-making circles, the way Benjamin Netanyahu and Ron Dermer are considering concessions to offer Riyadh on the Palestinian issue is nothing more than "trying to find a formulation, and not a solution, that could suit the Saudis." It is highly likely that Israel now believes that normalization with Riyadh is no longer necessary to definitively bury the Palestinian issue. In terms of security, the benefit for Israel of sealing such an agreement with Saudi Arabia must also be greatly relativized. Their discourse on the opportunity for a common defense front with the Gulf States is just a deception. Israelis are well aware that the Gulf states will not join them in case of confrontation with Iran. They do not need them in any case to carry out strikes. Their attachment [in 2021] to the US military command in the Middle East [Centcom] already provides them with the best possible military cooperation with these countries. The only question that currently matters regarding Saudi Arabia is the influence that Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman will have on the Trump team regarding Iran. Because if Israel pushes for a large-scale military operation to destroy the Iranian nuclear program and encourage a regime change in Tehran, Riyadh, like other Gulf capitals, strongly opposes this scenario, seen as an almost existential security threat. Furthermore, Israelis will do everything to ensure that the Iranian issue is brought to the negotiating table between [Vladimir] Putin and Trump on Ukraine, which is of course contrary to European interests. In Lebanon, can the ceasefire agreement lead to a diplomatic settlement? In Lebanon, the problem is considered resolved, at least in the medium term, by Israeli decision-makers - across all sectors. This assessment is based on various factors, the most important of which is the sense of freedom of action that Israel feels it has today throughout Lebanese territory, with the green light from the United States. Now that the balance of power has shifted, the debate has gradually shifted to the diplomatic arena. The Israeli state could consider returning to the negotiating table as soon as possible in the hope that an agreement will ultimately undermine the legitimacy of Hezbollah's weapons. However, Washington's apparent flexibility on maintaining Israeli troops in five points in southern Lebanon could lead Israeli decision-makers to adopt maximalist positions. Since the fall of the Al-Assad regime in Syria, Israel has bombed the country's military capabilities and occupies strategic positions in the Syrian Golan Heights. What are its objectives? Israeli diplomatic and military circles are pleased with the blow dealt to the Iranian axis by the fall of Bashar Al-Assad. A new game is also unfolding, and Israeli authorities have immediately established a balance of power with the new regime in place. Since Mr. Netanyahu's statements on February 23, demanding the "total demilitarization" of southern Syria, a political line is gradually being defined: Israel wants to seize the opportunity of the current reconfiguration in Syria to "deepen its strategic depth" – according to the terms of an Israeli diplomat. The question now is how Israeli authorities plan to proceed. Maintaining the freedom to fly over Syrian territory appears crucial for Israel, whether in the desired new balance of power with its neighbor, or in a scenario of confrontation with Iran. The Russian presence in Syria, alongside the Al-Assad regime, guaranteed them almost total freedom of action on Syrian territory. If Turkey were to extend its influence over the political and military apparatus of the new Syrian regime, it is unlikely that it would be willing to perpetuate such a mechanism. This is the main challenge of Israeli policy in Syria: knowing how to best manage relations with the regional power of Turkey.

title: In Belgrade, a historic day of anger for hundreds of thousands of Serbs who dream of "a country with functioning laws and justice."
lead: The very authoritarian Serbian president, Aleksandar Vucic, was the main target of slogans during the large-scale protest against corruption, following the deadly accident at the Novi Sad train station canopy. The movement on Saturday is reminiscent of those that led to the downfall of former dictator Slobodan Milosevic in 2000.
text_eng: Belgrade experienced a historic day of anger on Saturday, March 15. In an atmosphere made electric by the provocations of the authorities, hundreds of thousands of Serbs – 100,000 according to the police, around 300,000 according to an independent counting organization – poured into the streets of their capital to demand "justice," in the fifth month of what is the most powerful and longest anti-corruption protest movement ever seen in this Balkan country with only 6.6 million inhabitants. "We will no longer allow corruption to endanger anyone's life, as it happened in Novi Sad [North], where it caused fifteen deaths," declared a student speaker to the immense crowd to cheers, referring to the collapse of the awning at the train station in the country's second city. This incident occurred on November 1, 2024, in an infrastructure that had just been renovated, triggering a wave of anger that first spread through universities before extending to the entire society. Saturday's demonstration recalls the scale of those in the 1990s that led to the fall of the former dictator, Slobodan Milosevic, in 2000. Throughout the day, students, joined by Serbs of all ages, occupied the streets of Belgrade in an atmosphere that remained largely peaceful, despite provocations by black-clad power thugs with masked faces. The students, moreover, decided to dissolve the demonstration prematurely after the use of a mysterious sound weapon that caused a brief moment of panic during the silent tribute to the victims of Novi Sad. Nepotism The procession was largely made up of residents from the city located 80 kilometers from Belgrade. "These people who died, they could have been my children, they often went to that station," explained Stefan Katic, a 34-year-old IT entrepreneur who dreams of "a country with functioning laws and justice." The protesters focused their slogans and anger on President Aleksandar Vucic. In power since 2014, first as prime minister before being elected president, this nationalist is accused of delivering Serbia to nepotism while governing with increasingly authoritarian methods. On the eve of the demonstration, the head of state strongly attacked the movement, calling it a "color revolution" led "by foreign agencies," a rhetoric still effective in this country marked by a strong pro-Russian current. In recent weeks, the authorities have conducted police raids on several NGOs supporting the movement, accusing them of receiving money from Usaid – the American development agency, currently being dismantled by the Trump administration. "A huge amount of money has been invested from outside to destabilize Serbia," Mr. Vucic criticized in a complacent interview with Donald Trump Jr, the son of the American president, who visited Serbia on March 11 as part of a visit illustrating the new American administration's rapprochement with authoritarian powers. On Friday, several opposition activists were arrested by the police, accused of preparing violent actions. Croatian and Slovenian journalists were prevented from entering Serbian territory to cover the movement. Authorities even suspended train and bus traffic to prevent protesters from reaching Belgrade. "We arranged to come by car" with little success, judging by the size of the human tide that flooded the capital. "We knew he was going to stop all the trains so we arranged to come by car," explains Anja Pantovic, a 38-year-old mathematician who studied in France, saying she wants to "just live in a country with a justice system that does its job." She identifies as pro-European. By her side, her friend Ivana Blagojevic had pinned a badge to her jacket calling on the European Chief Prosecutor, Laura Kövesi, to intervene in Serbia "to send Vucic to prison," even though it seems unlikely as long as the country is not part of the European Union (EU). During the protest against corruption and for justice in Belgrade on March 15, 2025. MITAR MITROVIC / REUTERS Alongside these numerous protesters, mostly educated and urban, were also activists with much more nationalist convictions. Some waved flags against the EU and NATO, or even "Z" flags in honor of the "special operation" led by Vladimir Putin in Ukraine. "I am a proud nationalist, but whether I am pro-Russian or pro-EU, I also want justice," explains Milan Bogojevic, a 32-year-old accountant in jogging pants with a shaved head, whose wife is wrapped in a flag calling to "not abandon" Kosovo, a former Serbian region that declared independence in 2008. This mix of diverse geopolitical beliefs is quite convenient for Mr. Vucic, who presents himself as the best guarantor of Serbia's stability and likes to affirm to his Western interlocutors that he is the only one who can lead his country towards the EU. Carefully avoiding displaying a true leader and "not engaging in politics," the students are hesitant to escalate, both in their demands and in their methods of action. So far, they are sticking to repeating the same list of demands purely related to the Novi Sad incident and refrain from calling for the resignation of Mr. Vucic. Vucic or new elections, as the president still has a strong electoral base in rural areas and among retired voters. "Students are very careful not to be associated with the opposition, which is very fragmented and discredited in Serbia," explains Dejan Bursac, a political scientist at the University of Belgrade. They thus refuse to endorse the calls for the formation of a technical government made by several opposition figures. "It's a good strategy, but only to a certain extent," says Mr. Bursac, as no one seriously believes that Mr. Vucic could suddenly decide to establish a true rule of law under pressure from the streets. Speaking after the protest on Saturday night, Mr. Vucic did acknowledge its importance, but then called on the students to end the blockades and "resume classes." "People are tired of blockades, they want to work," he said, apparently convinced that he can politically survive this wave of anger.

title: In Ukraine, the beginning of a presidential campaign that dares not speak its name
lead: Even though the population supports the postponement of the presidential and legislative elections that were supposed to take place in 2024, Volodymyr Zelensky's popularity rating has dropped and his rivals are already positioning themselves for the future.
text_eng: Long embraces, firm handshakes, and always a kind word to relax tense faces. The scene resembles a campaign meeting. The only difference is that it takes place in Izyum, in eastern Ukraine about fifty kilometers from the front, and the audience is mainly composed of Ukrainian soldiers in camouflage. These men, members of different brigades deployed in the area, were gathered alongside piles of drones, Starlink antennas, and electric generators. State-of-the-art equipment funded by the former Ukrainian president Petro Poroshenko (2014-2019), who personally came on January 13 to deliver them to the fighters. "My goal is not only to provide them with the equipment that soldiers need, but also to give them back some energy. Despite the smiles they give me when they meet me, I see that they are exhausted," confides the former head of state, who claims to meet about ten brigades each week when he is not in Kiev, where he serves as a deputy in the Verkhovna Rada, the Ukrainian Parliament, or abroad, where he meets Western leaders and cultivates his networks. Regarding Donald Trump, whom he met in 2017 when they were both presidents, Petro Poroshenko believes that his return to the White House is a favorable opportunity for Ukraine, recalling that it was during his first term that Washington began providing lethal weapons to Kiev. "We must find the right arguments with Trump. It is also in the interest of the United States and their reputation in the world. That's why everything is possible," asserts the former Ukrainian president. Volodymyr Zelensky's popularity rating has dropped significantly on this point, he is on the same page as his successor, Volodymyr Zelensky: only open peace negotiations once security guarantees have been obtained. "Do you want to know my advice for talking to Vladimir Putin? Do not trust him, and above all, do not be afraid of him," assures the one who presided over Ukraine during negotiations with the Russian president at the beginning of the Donbass war in 2014. To maintain his influence in Ukraine, Petro Poroshenko also relies on his party, European Solidarity, the main opposition force in Parliament. Since 2023, it has been calling for a government of national unity. However, the idea is not popular among the population, for whom the priority is primarily the survival of the country, and a majority supports postponing the presidential and legislative elections that were supposed to take place in 2024 until the end of the war. No one questions the legitimacy of Volodymyr Zelensky, even though his popularity rating has dropped significantly. According to the Kiev International Institute of Sociology (KIIS), his trust rating was 52% in December 2024, compared to 77% a year earlier. However, the return of Donald Trump to the White House and the prospect - even hypothetical - of an imminent end to the fighting are accompanied by a form of "electoral excitement," according to political scientist Volodymyr Fessenko. In recent weeks, Ukrainian media have observed a resurgence of activity from several heavyweights in political life, with an increase in opinion poll orders and more assertive positions on the outcome of the war. On January 27, the influential news site Ukraïnska Pravda noted a revival of the "dormant Ukrainian political process": "Both old and new actors are gradually becoming active, seeking new voters, new communication channels, and new teams." For traditional political leaders, the challenge is to position themselves, especially since the most popular figures in the country are currently in the military world. In addition to the circles around Volodymyr Zelensky and Petro Poroshenko, whose parties have 233 and 27 deputies respectively out of the 401 in the Verkhovna Rada, the Ukraïnska Pravda investigation mentions the former candidate in the 2019 presidential election, Yulia Tymoshenko, and Dmytro Razumkov, the former Speaker of Parliament from 2019 to 2021. Unable to exert influence in Parliament, the two are trying to distinguish themselves through their sharp criticisms of the authorities. The case of Yuriy Boyko is also cited. The former leader of a pro-Russian party that is now banned had remained discreet since the February 2022 invasion, even forming an alliance with Volodymyr Zelensky's party to pass laws. However, on December 14, 2024, on the social network TikTok, the deputy criticized the "Ukrainian radicals" who prohibit Russian-speaking citizens from speaking their language and the recent ban on activities of the Orthodox Church linked to the Moscow Patriarchate. Yuriy Boyko was summoned by the Ukrainian security service and stripped of his title as "Hero of Ukraine." Valeri Zaloujny, the favorite in the polls for a presidential election, In a phone conversation, the director of KIIS, Anton Prouzhetsky, also mentions the "signals" sent by Vitali Klitschko, the mayor of Kiev. Klitschko spent New Year's on the front line with soldiers. "A way to support our armed forces, but also of course a way to showcase himself alongside the soldiers," the expert analyzes. However, the big unknown remains the former commander-in-chief of the armed forces, Valeri Zaloujny, who became the ambassador to the UK after being dismissed in February 2024. From London, this general known for his independent thinking writes columns on the conflict and has just published the first book of a trilogy, titled "My War." Although he has never shown political ambitions, polls show him as the winner in a presidential election against the current president if the election were held today. In the eyes of Petro Poroshenko, who does not hide his desire to run for future elections despite his low popularity, this campaign that dares not speak its name resembles a "suicide." "The factor of our victory does not rely solely on our armed forces or our partners. The key factor is our unity," he asserts. This does not prevent him from distancing himself from Volodymyr Zelensky, with whom relations have been terrible since the latter succeeded him in 2019. "The government and the majority talk about elections because they see that their popularity is declining and they want to act before the situation worsens," accuses Petro Poroshenko, who, in front of the soldiers, also reproaches his rival for giving 1,000 hryvnias (23 euros) to each citizen to get through the winter. "This money could be spent on electronic warfare systems or on vehicles for the army!" But this activism, powerfully amplified on social media, also raises criticism. "Let's just say we have different interests," mutters a bearded soldier who prefers not to give his name and watches with an amused look the deputy followed by a cameraman. "People say that Poroshenko comes with drones and leaves with them. But you can see that it's not true," thunders the former head of state, who often speaks of himself in the third person. Among the numerous charitable organizations delivering equipment to the army, the Poroshenko Foundation stands out for its speed of execution and its bold operations. Like when its president offered 20 Mavic night vision reconnaissance drones to the first brigade that would capture North Korean soldiers in the Russian region of Kursk, where Ukrainian forces have held positions since their offensive in August 2024. On January 13, a representative of the special forces came to receive the aid, after two soldiers from Pyongyang were taken prisoner. "We have a constant need for this aid, and we are so grateful that we have no words to express it," whispers Andri, commander of a drone unit deployed on the front, invited to participate in the ceremony. "We have so many losses..."

title: Raphaël Glucksmann wants to work with the Socialist Party for the 2027 presidential election.
