lead: The leader of Place publique expressed readiness on Sunday to "build a common political platform" with the party symbolized by the rose, which must in turn define its strategic direction for the upcoming presidential election during the June congress.
text_eng: How to help the left win in 2027? Without declaring his own candidacy, Raphaël Glucksmann took advantage of his party's congress, Place publique, which took place on the weekend of March 15th and 16th in Paris, to lay out a path forward as the left is stuck at 30% of the vote. Remaining true to his visceral opposition to Jean-Luc Mélenchon, the former Socialist Party (PS) candidate for the European elections urges other factions to "unite without LFI [La France insoumise]" and to "forge a strong project," as he explained in a lengthy interview with La Tribune Dimanche on March 16th. "We have entered a pivotal moment that demands absolute clarity," justifies the one who was noticed during the European elections for his stance against Russian President Vladimir Putin and in favor of a European defense. Thus, Place publique is focusing on crafting a "project" for June that will be "neither Mélenchonist nor Macronist." In his conquest strategy, the MEP invites the Socialists to be his first partners and says he is ready to "build a common political offer." As the PS has kicked off its congress, the essayist hopes that the party with the rose will be "in line with this strategic approach," in order to "campaign together." Raphaël Glucksmann's desire to continue collaborating with the PS should be well received, at least by half of the party. A candidate for the position of first secretary, the mayor of Rouen, Nicolas Mayer-Rossignol, shares his vision. "Who will we work with after the congress, [Raphaël] Glucksmann or [Manuel] Bompard [the coordinator of LFI]?" he seemed to ponder in recent weeks, in a purely rhetorical question. Concentric approach To create momentum and "achieve a programmatic foundation," the mayor of Rouen, who is in talks with the MEP, suggests a concentric approach. "The first circle is not the NFP [New Popular Front] new version," but a close partnership "between the PS and Place publique." "It is more intellectually coherent," he has continued to argue. The second circle involves reaching "compromises on a project" with the rest of the left, namely the "ecologists and the PC [Communist Party]." This approach aligns with the one proposed by Raphaël Glucksmann in La Tribune Dimanche, which suggests, in a second step, opening up the "political offer" concocted by the PS and Place publique to "humanists and ecologists who want to fight alongside [them]." He therefore implies that the candidate for 2027 must be social-democratic. "This platform will launch the dynamic that will allow the left to win the 2027 presidential election," he argued. "It's a step towards a federation after a new Epinay!" "Jean-Christophe Cambadélis, a member of the other minority faction of the Socialist Party, close to François Hollande, is pleased. His leader, the mayor of Vaulx-en-Velin (Rhône), Hélène Geoffroy, does not want a 'popular primary,' but wishes for the designation of a social-democratic candidate within a pole ranging from former Minister under François Hollande, Benoît Hamon, to 'disenchanted Macron supporters,' including former Prime Minister Bernard Cazeneuve and Raphaël Glucksmann. However, the method aligns much less with that proposed by the PS's first secretary, Olivier Faure, who has always aimed to bring together socialists, communists, ecologists, and former 'insubordinate' members like Clémentine Autain or François Ruffin to develop a political platform and choose a candidate. 'Once its project is adopted, even in dialogue with Place publique, the PS will have to engage with the entire left, from Ruffin to Cazeneuve. We cannot have multiple concentric circles,' justifies Luc Broussy, former president of the National Council and close to Olivier Faure, while also applauding that 'Raphaël Glucksmann's first instinct is to work with the PS.' On this thorny issue of 2027, only the deputy from Landes, Boris Vallaud, the most dangerous rival to Olivier Faure at the congress, has not yet fully revealed his hand."

title: The left is divided over aid to Ukraine.
lead: Socialists and ecologists advocate for strengthening military and financial support for Volodymyr Zelensky, while the communists and the "unbowed" reject this option.
text_eng: At the National Assembly, where the debate on Ukraine was held on Monday, March 3, the left once again presented two different worldviews on international issues. At the heart of the disagreements were the aid to Ukraine and the strengthening of European defense, following the altercation between Donald Trump and Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky at the White House, which seemed to signal the beginning of American disengagement from European soil: Mr. Trump abruptly froze military aid to Ukraine on Monday, March 3. "True pacifists know that war, always detestable, is sometimes inevitable," declared the president of the socialist group, Boris Vallaud, stating that "President Trump's America is no longer our ally." "Let's be honest and admit that we have long known about the gradual disengagement of the United States in Europe," he acknowledged. To resolve this unprecedented crisis and "fund this military support to Ukraine," the Socialist Party (PS) proposes to seize "the [210] billion euros of Russian assets frozen in our banks" and to stop allowing "its liquefied natural gas to transit through our ports, with the complicity of our companies," indirectly targeting Total Energies. It calls for a "common loan of 500 billion," the only way to "save peace and security in Europe." These are the same proposals that have already been advocated by MEP Raphaël Glucksmann, who came to listen to the debates at the Assembly from the visitors' balcony on Monday. After Marine Le Pen's speech, the former lead candidate for Place publique and the Socialist Party in the European elections went out to share his positions with the press in front of the Palais-Bourbon. He began by criticizing the leader of the National Rally, who "did not have a single word of criticism for the President of the United States" and whom he described as a "sham patriot." "The threat of war within the borders of the European Union has never been so real," he warned. New European alliances For him, as for the socialists and the ecologists, who echoed similar sentiments in the Chamber, a new chapter is unfolding. All acknowledge that NATO is now ineffective, although they do not advocate for leaving the Atlantic Alliance. "No one can believe anymore that the President of the United States would activate Article 5 [which states a collective response in case of armed attack in a European country or North America] to protect European nations," said Mr. Glucksmann, advocating for new European alliances outside the North Atlantic Treaty. Long known for their pacifist image, environmentalists are now calling for "strengthened military and financial engagement," which would involve, among other things, "providing advanced defense equipment" or "increasing the presence of European troops in the bordering countries of Ukraine," as expressed by the president of the environmentalist group, Cyrielle Chatelain. In addition to freezing Russian assets, stopping the purchase of fossil fuels or fertilizers, they want to see Vladimir Putin brought to trial before the International Criminal Court. Also very critical of Donald Trump, the "unbowed" and the communists, on the other hand, do not want to open the purse strings to rearm Ukraine or Europe. "What's the point of all this money?" questioned Aurélien Saintoul, a member of the La France insoumise (LFI) party from Hauts-de-Seine, insinuating that an increase in defense budgets would only benefit American industrialists. "What kind of deception are they trying to involve us in? In any case, these abstract discussions of numbers are absurd," he elaborated. The elected official sees in this crisis a confirmation of LFI's anti-Atlanticist stance. "It is painful to have been right for twenty years and to see our own country cornered today!" he lamented. He also asserted that "our security is not currently at risk," seemingly justifying the lack of concrete proposals or new alliances. "Where will you find this money?" echoed Jean-Paul Lecoq, the communist deputy from Seine-Maritime, who also accused Emmanuel Macron of engaging in a "warlike escalation" to "get the population to accept" these expenses. He would prefer that Emmanuel Macron negotiate directly with Vladimir Putin, even though the French president has already met with the Russian leader without success.

title: Faced with the Russian threat, the National Rally is torn between relativism and conspiracy theories.
lead: The far-right supporters criticize the head of state for "playing the war-monger" and delegating his military powers to the European Commission. A resolution proposal to strengthen aid to Ukraine is set to be examined on Wednesday in the Assembly.
text_eng: Marine Le Pen never misses an opportunity to complain about the "contempt" shown by Emmanuel Macron towards her. The one who claims to be the "main opponent" to the head of state often laments not being listened to by the executive. On February 20, however, neither she nor the president of the National Rally (RN), Jordan Bardella, responded to the invitation extended by the Elysée to political leaders in the country to discuss the war in Ukraine and the geopolitical shocks caused by the President of the United States, Donald Trump. "Emmanuel Macron decided to organize this meeting the day before for the next day, during the parliamentary holidays, and it turns out that I was absent that day," explained the far-right leader in Le Figaro two weeks later, citing the period of parliamentary work suspension - which was set from February 24 to March 2. Forced to break her silence due to the gravity of the diplomatic upheavals, the three-time presidential candidate refrains from providing any analysis of the ongoing recompositions or proposing a real alternative to the options - diplomatic, industrial, political - considered by Emmanuel Macron. The only one authorized at the RN to address international issues, the president of the group in the National Assembly simply repeats her wish to convene a "peace conference," and her concern that France maintains a "unique and independent voice" in the concert of nations. "Each fight at its own level: we are only a small political party from a third country, far below the superpowers," justifies MEP Philippe Olivier, her close advisor. "We are not going to start proposing war exit plans. We have a principle, peace, which is already good." "Fear management professor" "At its level," therefore, the RN content itself with criticizing or mocking, after caricaturing or distorting, the proposals submitted by Emmanuel Macron in his televised address on March 5. Russia has become, according to the head of state, "a threat to Europe" and its aggressiveness "seems to know no boundaries"? Marine Le Pen doubts this, considering that "if, after three years [of war], Russia is struggling to advance in Ukraine, there is little chance that it aspires to come all the way to Paris." Emmanuel Macron promotes the "European defense" and the "strategic autonomy" of the European Union? The RN accuses him of delegating his military powers to the European Commission. Emmanuel Macron wants to open "the strategic debate on the protection by our [nuclear] deterrence of our allies on the European continent"? The RN suspects him of wanting to share the nuclear button. As willing spectators of events, the far-right reproach the head of the armed forces above all for "playing the warmonger" by exaggerating the "Russian threat." "A fear management professor," according to Marine Le Pen. Difficult to assume since February 2022 and the invasion of Ukraine, her party's historical complacency towards Moscow is resurfacing now that the President of the United States, Donald Trump, has elevated the Russian President, Vladimir Putin, as a privileged interlocutor to resolve the conflict. "Today, the Russian threat is more prevalent, but I do not trust any of the major powers," Marine Le Pen asserted on Tuesday, March 11, calling for equal distrust of Russia, the United States, and China. A position that is at least more lenient in form than the one held by Jordan Bardella, who dares to speak of a "multidimensional threat" from Russia. However, Vladimir Putin's territorial ambitions, particularly towards Moldova, do not convince him to be wary and arm Europe accordingly. As it stands, the absence of an immediate threat to national borders reassures the party with the flame. European calendar In a similar dialectic to that defended in 2022, at the beginning of the war in Ukraine, the National Rally accuses Emmanuel Macron of dramatizing geopolitical issues to overshadow the domestic political crisis and its impact on Marine Le Pen's favorite areas. The latter believes that, more than Russia, "the major threat is Islamist terrorism." Some of her elected officials go even further and detect, in the sequence, the disloyalty of a head of state more concerned with advancing a supposed European calendar than a French one. "Macron dreams of being the president of a future European federation," wrote Hervé de Lépinau, RN deputy for Vaucluse, on March 2. "He is weakening France so that its integration into a federal state is the only alternative offered to the French. He is the enemy of national interests." A conspiratorial refrain, recalling a long-standing tradition of the far right insinuating that its opponents obey foreign interests, which permeates even to the top of the National Rally. "I think [Emmanuel Macron] has a very intriguing relationship with France and French history," said Jordan Bardella on March 6 on CNews. "We have the feeling that he is in France without being of France."

title: March 8th: 48,000 demonstrators for women's rights in Paris according to the police, 120,000 according to the organizers.
lead: In the capital, the procession had a political tone, with participants referring to Donald Trump. Closing the income gap between women and men also remained a top demand.
text_eng: Fight against persistent wage inequalities, against femicides, and the rise of "masculinist" discourses: around 48,000 people participated in the demonstration organized on Saturday, March 8th in Paris by feminist collectives and unions on the occasion of International Women's Day, according to information from the Police Prefecture. The gathering took place peacefully, except for some tensions between riot police and protesters at the end of the procession. Authorities reported seven arrests. The feminist strike, a collective responsible for organizing this mobilization day, claimed around 120,000 participants in Paris and a total of 250,000 across France, in the context of 150 gatherings and demonstrations. In the regions, the participation was reported to be "around 85,000 people," according to police sources. There were 9,300 demonstrators in Lyon, 7,500 in Toulouse, 6,000 in Marseille, and 5,000 in Rennes, according to the prefectures. "It's a struggle, it's not over. It's moving in the right direction. Trump, the masculinists make a lot of noise, but they are not as strong as us," said Sabine, 49, the head of a professional association, accompanied by her 7-year-old son in a very crowded Parisian procession that started from Place de la République, to Agence France-Presse. Another protester in this procession, which included many young people and families, Lucie, 18, aims to "defend the right to abortion and protest against the far right, because it is rising and it's scary." Persistent wage inequalities were also a political theme with a brief action by Femen, who, painted with American, European, or Russian flags crossed out with a swastika, made Nazi salutes while shouting "Heil Trump," "Heil Meloni," and "Heil Putin" in front of cameras. According to them, this action aims to provide a "feminist response" to a "fascist epidemic." In Paris, the presence of the identitarian collective Nemesis, announced on their social networks, was unanimously condemned by feminist and anti-racist associations. In the capital, the Eiffel Tower was supposed to display a message of support in the evening - in French, English, Farsi, and Arabic - for Afghan women. Beyond the context related to political tensions and the international situation, reducing income gaps between women and men remains at the forefront of demands. The average salary of women in the private sector was still 22.2% lower than that of men in 2023, according to INSEE. This difference, which has decreased by a third since 1995, is partly explained by a lower volume of work. With the same working hours, the average salary of women remains 14.2% lower, according to INSEE. "We're spinning our wheels, we're moving extremely slowly," which is becoming "unbearable," lamented the head of CFDT, Marylise Léon, on France Inter on Saturday. "We are on a feminist strike" to show that "when women stop, everything stops," also reminds Amy Bah, in charge of Nous Toutes Lille, who demonstrated with around a thousand people in the North. Faced with these persistent wage inequalities, the Court of Auditors estimated in a report published in January that the Ministry of Labor should "get more involved" in revaluing predominantly female jobs, as demanded by associations. Sexist and sexual violence is still "present everywhere." As a consequence of professional inequalities, women generally have lower retirement pensions. March 8th should also be an opportunity for mobilization for the repeal of the pension reform, which penalizes women more specifically, according to Sophie Binet, general secretary of the CGT. To strengthen gender equality, the government launched a five-year plan in 2023 that focused on several issues, including the fight against violence. In this context, it increased financial support for the associative helpline 3919, which experienced an unprecedented year in 2024, with over 100,000 calls handled. The government also expanded the number of women's shelters, structures that assist victims. It continues to deploy the "grave danger phone" system and anti-stalking bracelets. These measures are far from sufficient in the eyes of associations. Gathering marking International Women's Rights Day in Paris on March 8, 2025. MARTIN BUREAU/AFP Pelicot, Le Scouarnec, de Bétharram cases: examples in the news that show that sexist and sexual violence is still "present everywhere," argued Salomé Hocquard, vice-president of UNEF. "They are not just isolated incidents, but reflect an oppressive patriarchal system." A message echoed in the processions in Strasbourg - "One is not born a woman, but becomes one in death" - as in Paris: "Feminism has never killed anyone, but machismo kills every day," proclaims the sign held by Christelle, 48, an IT employee, who came to demonstrate because "threats weigh on women's rights in France and around the world."

title: "The United States' turnaround on Ukraine echoes the cynical calculation that was at the origin of the German-Soviet pact of 1939."
lead: By ending its support for Kiev, Washington recognizes a sphere of influence for Russia in Eastern Europe and puts an end to the Pax Americana, the order that has defined international politics since 1945, as noted by British historian Roger Moorhouse in an article for "Le Monde".
text_eng: History never repeats itself, say the wise, but it does sometimes rhyme and produce echoes. For those with ears to hear, many resonances have been heard in recent weeks, starting with unpleasant reminders of the German-Soviet pact. In the summer of 1939, Hitler's Germany and Stalin's Soviet Union made a strategic alignment that scandalized the world. While their pact was presented as a non-aggression treaty – like many other agreements made in the 1930s – its intention was anything but peaceful. It was accompanied by a secret protocol that delineated German and Soviet spheres of influence in Eastern Europe, leading directly to Hitler's invasion of Poland and the outbreak of the Second World War. For both signatories, realpolitik – the cold and pragmatic pursuit of national interest – prevailed: their ideological antagonism mattered less than their shared desire to abolish the order established after the First World War. Given its repercussions, it is surprising that the German-Soviet pact remains so poorly understood outside of Central and Eastern Europe, and that it is often relegated to a mere footnote in history. Yet it was a decisive event, giving the green light to the destruction of Poland and bringing vast swathes of Eastern Europe – including the Baltic States and the eastern part of the Polish Republic – under Soviet control. At the very least, it directly impacted the lives of around 50 million people. A new moment of realpolitik Furthermore, the pact marked the resurgence of the concept of spheres of influence, effectively condemning the territories between its two signatories to a harsh lesson in realpolitik. It ushered in an era where might trumped right, and where sovereignty and independence were reduced to abstract notions, subject to the whims of the neighboring great power. It appears that we are now witnessing a new moment of realpolitik, this time involving the United States and Russia regarding Ukraine. Over three decades after the fall of communist regimes in Eastern Europe, during which international institutions, backed by American power, sought to thwart any who might challenge the post-1991 consensus, Washington seems to be readjusting its strategy by withdrawing its support for Ukraine and disengaging more broadly from the European theater. This shift – sometimes presented as a manifestation of isolationism, sometimes as a strategic reorientation towards China and the Pacific – echoes the cynical calculation that underpinned the German-Soviet pact. It indicates that Washington acknowledges the existence of a Russian sphere of influence in Eastern Europe, but also that it puts an end to the Pax Americana, the order based on rules that has largely defined international politics since 1945. A pawn to be sacrificed If Putin has sought to attribute his disastrous war in Ukraine to all sorts of illusions, the question of spheres of influence is the key. Since the North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO) promised in 2008 to eventually integrate Ukraine, Moscow has done everything possible to prevent Kiev from reorienting towards the West and to reaffirm its dominance over what it has always considered its periphery. These efforts led to the invasion of Ukraine in February 2022. Over the past three years, the United States and its allies have resolutely opposed this brutal logic, refusing to validate Russia's claims and insisting on the right of Ukrainians to self-determination. However, American support has eventually weakened, and a hostile clamor has risen to demand that Ukraine cede territories in exchange for a precarious peace. Despite all their sacrifices, Ukrainians now bitterly realize that their country of nearly 40 million inhabitants is apparently little more than a pawn to be sacrificed on the chessboard of great powers. This is sadly familiar to us as well. In 1939, it was the Poles who took up arms to defend themselves against the aggressive intentions of their neighbors. Like the Ukrainians today, they received loud expressions of support from their Western allies but quickly understood how empty promises of material assistance were. Great Britain and France, both of whom had declared war on Germany in 1939 to defend Poland, only supported their ally in words, preferring to save their troops for future conflicts. Despite all the warm words, Poland was, unsurprisingly, abandoned to its tragic fate. Short-lived reprieve The German-Soviet pact lasted less than two years, becoming obsolete with Hitler's invasion of the Soviet Union in the summer of 1941. However, it caused such horrors in the countries it affected that it remains very present in the collective consciousness - and this is one of the reasons why countries on the eastern edge of Europe are so eager to join NATO. A rapprochement between the United States and Russia, as it appears regarding Ukraine, would likely be just as short-lived and no less deadly. An agreement that respects the principle of Ukraine's independence and leaves the door open to Western ambitions in Kiev would undoubtedly only mean a temporary pause in the conflict. Furthermore, without the threat of American intervention, Vladimir Putin would be encouraged to continue his expansion, while other aggressors would become emboldened. Taiwan, Moldova, the Baltic States, and Poland would find themselves on the front line. If America under Donald Trump chooses this path, if it decides to abandon its role as a leader and give up on the Pax Americana, it must understand that the world will not be more stable, quite the opposite. The echo of 1939 tells us that appeasement policy and cynical realpolitik may provide a brief respite but do not prevent catastrophe. In the end, they only fuel it. Translated from English by Juliette Kopecka.

title: The letters from Olga and Sacha: "If Ukraine is abandoned and the 'Russian world' comes to my door (nightmare), what will they do to me?"
section: M le mag means "M the magazine" in English.
lead: Since the beginning of the Russian invasion, Olga and Sasha Kurovska, two Ukrainian sisters in their thirties, one living in Paris and the other in Kiev, regularly provide updates in "M Le magazine du Monde". Through letters, they share the intimate account of their lives disrupted by the war.
text_eng: Paris, February 21, 2025 Dear readers, I am writing this letter in a very difficult time for Ukrainians. For the past few days, the President of the United States has been conducting peace negotiations with the Russians [Olga and Sasha have chosen not to capitalize "russian," "russia," and "putin"]. In my opinion, these negotiations are not legitimate because Ukraine is not involved. I was so shocked to hear Trump declare that Ukraine should not have started the war three years ago, that Zelensky is a dictator, that only 4% of Ukrainians support him... How can the President of the United States tell such lies? How can he conduct talks without Ukraine, when our president clearly stated: "No negotiations without us"? It seems that in recent years, Trump has completely ignored the reality of the war and only listened to Russian propagandists. Moreover, saying that Ukraine started the war is exactly what Putin claimed in an interview with the [very conservative] American commentator Tucker Carlson just a year ago. Personally, I stick to my initial opinion: no negotiation with a terrorist, and give us more weapons to kick him out of Ukrainian territory! Many believe that this massacre must be stopped, which is true, but if we accept the conditions of this "poutler" [a contraction of Poutine and Hitler], the Russians will take advantage to regain strength and attack again later. The war we are fighting has a purpose: the survival of the Ukrainian state and its sovereignty. The peace we desire has the same objective. The Ukrainians on the front lines know this better than anyone. My son just celebrated his first year in this upside-down world. I think about the future that awaits our children with Sasha. All Ukrainian children. Especially those who need special attention, the disabled and the war orphans. I often look at the Instagram account of the charitable organization Misto Dobra, which means "city of kindness." The cause they fight for touches me deeply. It is one of the largest shelters for children and mothers in need. It is located in Chernivtsi, a city in southwestern Ukraine, and has been in existence since 2008. I am in contact with Yuliia, who has been working there since 2011. Before that, she was a journalist. She told me that it all started with the idea of a woman: Marta Levchenko. Marta first created an association to fulfill the dreams of children in need, and then the project grew. In 2016, she began to welcome mothers who were victims of domestic violence and disabled children into rented rooms. Then, a first house was built, brick by brick. From the first days of the Great War, children from three orphanages (two in Odessa and one in Mykolaiv) were welcomed there, as the State could not take care of them. In four months, the organization built a second house. Since then, three buildings have emerged, one of which is called "the house of butterflies." It is reserved for sick children, with respiratory, intensive, and palliative care services. Misto Dobra now accommodates twice as many mothers and children as before the war. It has also become the largest center for pediatric palliative care. Nearly 80 people work there along with about twenty volunteers (a child in palliative care needs three caregivers per twenty-four hours). And then, there are all these children traumatized by the conflict. Yuliia told me about Danya, a little boy from Kherson whose mother was killed by a Russian rocket. He saw everything and remembers every detail... The organization does not receive any help from the State. It is constantly seeking donations. Yuliia calls their life "the marathon of finding solutions." One hundred percent of the funding comes from sponsors in Ukraine and abroad, from associations, and individuals. When I can, I also contribute. I have prepared several bags to send to them, with items my baby no longer uses and clothes of mine. With my mom who arrived last weekend, we started sorting. Often, people around me ask what they can do to help Ukraine, here is a very concrete way. Olga Kyiv, February 21, 2025 Dear readers, A small update in our life: we have moved and now live in a building with an elevator and a shelter in the basement, where we can protect ourselves in case of heavy bombing. In this three-room apartment, we have more space and there is a large hallway where we take refuge during air alerts, which have become daily. Our son, Marian, is 4 months old and is starting to react to the explosions. He startles in my arms and looks at me with his eyes wide open, but he doesn't cry. Every night, from the Russian borders to the north and east, hundreds of drones are launched. The attack lasts an average of six hours, and around 3 a.m., ground-to-ground ballistic missiles land as well. With Dima, we have set up a surveillance system: he stays awake until 3 a.m. while Marian and I sleep. After that, I take over. This way, the one who is awake monitors the situation and wakes the others in case of imminent danger. Thanks to chats on the Telegram app, we are well informed: what weapons, how many, where they were launched from, in which direction. In theory, we can know if they are coming towards us. Yesterday morning, I received an unexpected call from the owner of our new apartment. She asked me if we were considering fleeing the country when Putin would make Ukraine a part of the Russian Federation. But what a strange question! Three years after the start of the invasion, eleven years after the "dignity revolution" on Maidan Square, some people are imagining this terribly dark future... For the past few weeks, I keep telling myself every day that the worst has happened, but the next day, it's even worse. The Russians amaze me. Not just them, but the whole world: political classes and international media, who seem to have buried Ukraine under the chaos created by Trump. Unfortunately, the American president is no better than his buddy in Russia. Both of them seem to be living proof of the despicable side of man. And when I think that they are supported by millions of people, I feel like I'm on the verge of hysteria. While Trump is on the phone with Putin, essentially deciding what will happen in the region without even inviting the Ukrainians to the party, and accusing Zelensky of starting the war, Europe shrugs its democratic shoulders. It has just realized that it has been outmaneuvered by the Americans, it is left behind. And we, Ukrainians, are torn between two extremes: there are those who give up and think that all is lost; and those who redouble their work, volunteerism, and maintain faith in independence. Of course, there are our soldiers, who continue to fight. And then there are also all the people who hold their breath and wait for what comes next while trying to live their lives. "We are living in very dark times," my father said the other day, when he came over to play with Marian. It struck me. I keep thinking about it. This is what I told our landlord: if, as in our worst nightmares, Ukraine became a part of the Russian Federation, I wouldn't know where to flee, because the whole world would be in danger with these two power-crazed heads, Trump and Putin. She just told me to let her know a month in advance if we were leaving. I think she is very anxious. An excess of anxiety destroys everything, it is a weapon that Russia has always used. Anxiety and fear of death. I would like to quote Volodymyr Yermolenko, a contemporary Ukrainian philosopher and man of letters. He speaks of "thanatocracy" to describe the successive Russian political regimes, from the tsar to Putin, and describes this concept as follows: "Thanatocracy is the management of a country through death and fear of death. But, and this is the paradox, this system wants to live, it seeks space to thrive, space where it can carry out its favorite task: to destroy for the pleasure of destruction. And Ukraine is an obstacle in that space." In its history, Russia has never known peace or even considered living in peace. The whole world knows it and yet continues to play this hypocritical game, to try to negotiate with this killer and liar that is Putin. All because of fear, fear of death. As I write these words, I start to think: if Ukraine is abandoned by the whole world and the "Russian world" comes to my door (nightmare), what will they do to me and my family because of the words I have been writing to you here for three years? What will they do to me based on my ideas and values? As usual, with a heavy heart, I start my day. I will write to my sister, I am eager to know how my nephew's first birthday party went, he turned 1 yesterday. Our mom is in France, she went to see Olga and the little one. I make myself some coffee, I watch Marian sleep peacefully, and I try to find a glimmer of hope in all this darkness. Sasha

title: Ukraine: Europeans, shocked by the failure and violence of the Trump-Zelensky meeting, are preparing for a decisive summit.
lead: The meeting will take place on Sunday in London, with the presence of the main European leaders and the Ukrainian president. The goal is to define a strategy to continue supporting Kiev and ensure Europe's security in the face of a Russia emboldened by a Trump administration willing to make any concessions.
text_eng: And now? Beyond Ukraine, it is all of Europe that is shaken by the failure and violence of the meeting between Donald Trump and Volodymyr Zelensky on Friday, February 28, at the White House. Without the United States, how can resistance to Moscow be maintained? How, in case of American abandonment, can support for Kiev continue and, beyond that, ensure Europe's security in the face of a Russia emboldened by a Trump administration ready to make all concessions to quickly sign a cessation of hostilities? These existential questions are what Europeans should strive to answer during the summit organized by British Prime Minister Keir Starmer in London on Sunday, March 2. After the rather warm exchanges he had with Donald Trump on Thursday, February 27, the British leader hoped to convince his partners of his government's ability to serve as a "bridge" between the Old Continent and the United States. The humiliation suffered by Mr. Zelensky in front of cameras worldwide suddenly erases his efforts, as well as those of Emmanuel Macron, to advocate for the Ukrainian cause with Donald Trump. In twenty-four hours, on Friday, Europeans find themselves even more isolated than they were two weeks earlier, when at the Munich Security Conference on February 14, American Vice President J.D. Vance spectacularly downplayed Russian and Chinese threats to criticize the European Union. As acknowledged on Friday evening by the Estonian Kaja Kallas, the otherwise very Atlanticist High Representative of the European Union for Foreign Affairs, on the X network: "Today, it has become clear that the free world needs a new leader. It is up to us, Europeans, to meet this challenge." If maintained, the London summit will therefore turn into a new crisis summit. After speaking on the phone with Presidents Trump and Zelensky, the British Prime Minister promised late Friday evening his "unwavering" support for Ukraine, and stated his intention to "play his part in finding a lasting peace, based on the sovereignty and security of Ukraine." Preparation for the European peacekeeping force In addition to President Zelensky, this London summit is expected to bring together the heads of state and government of France, Germany, Poland, Italy, Denmark, and Turkey, as well as the President of the European Commission, Ursula von der Leyen, the President of the Council, Antonio Costa, and the Secretary General of NATO, Mark Rutte. Keir Starmer plans to speak earlier in the day via video link with the leaders of the Baltic countries (Latvia, Lithuania, Estonia). The meeting is intended to continue the preparatory work for the European peacekeeping force that London and Paris propose to deploy in Ukraine, as part of a genuine peace agreement, after a possible ceasefire. After the exchanges between Donald Trump and Volodymyr Zelensky on Friday, the hope of a backstop, American security support to deter Vladimir Putin from violating a future agreement, could become illusory. The question will be more about whether Keir Starmer maintains his proposal to send British soldiers to Ukraine, risking direct confrontations with the Russians without American support. And if other European states – France in particular – are willing to follow suit. The leaders of the Twenty-Seven are set to meet on Thursday, March 6, in Brussels for an extraordinary European Council. The Europeans were expected to announce new measures to support Kiev and coordinate on how to strengthen their defense efforts, in order to better "share the burden" with their "ally" the Americans, whether within NATO or outside of it. There is no doubt that Donald Trump's criticism of the Ukrainian president may force them to hasten certain decisions – without waiting for their security response to be ready. Even before these summits, solidarity reactions quickly poured in on Friday, February 28. From Emmanuel Macron to Donald Tusk, Olaf Scholz, and Brussels officials, most leaders of the Old Continent expressed their support for the Ukrainian president, who is facing hostility from the United States at a time when their military support is all the more crucial as Russian forces continue to advance on the ground. "A powerful Europe, we need it more than ever. (...) The awakening is now," tweeted Emmanuel Macron after the Ukrainian leader's hasty departure from the White House. For him, "if anyone is playing the third world war, it is Vladimir Putin," not Volodymyr Zelensky, as Donald Trump accused him during the conversation. During his visit to the White House on Monday, February 24, the French president had hoped that his Ukrainian counterpart would come to Washington as soon as possible to sign the rare earth agreement negotiated with the Republican administration – a compromise that remained pending on Friday evening. This was supposed to allow Volodymyr Zelensky, he hoped, to detail his positions to Donald Trump before the latter met Vladimir Putin, on a date that remains uncertain. As the Americans threatened to cancel the meeting, Emmanuel Macron even picked up the phone on Wednesday, February 26, to persuade the White House tenant to receive his Ukrainian counterpart. "With Ukraine, for better or for worse." At the end of a visit to Portugal on Friday, February 28, the head of state said he was ready to "open the discussion" on European nuclear deterrence, following requests in this regard from the likely future German chancellor, the Christian Democratic frontrunner Friedrich Merz. A request, also unthinkable just a few months ago. "We stand with Ukraine for better or for worse. We must never confuse the aggressor and the victim in this terrible war," the latter assured. "You will never be alone," proclaimed Ursula von der Leyen, the President of the European Commission, and Antonio Costa, the President of the European Council, in the evening. Faced with crucial questions - what transatlantic relationship? what peace in Europe? - the Europeans are divided. "What is needed is an immediate summit between the United States, European states, and allies to speak frankly about how we intend to face the major challenges of today, starting with Ukraine, which we have defended together in recent years," declared the Italian council president, Giorgia Meloni, on Friday evening, taking a middle position. While supporting Ukraine, Ms. Meloni does not intend to criticize the Trump administration, with which she is ideologically close. As for Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orban, he explicitly supported Donald Trump's approach, like many far-right parties, including the Austrian FPÖ and Matteo Salvini's League in Italy. "President Donald Trump courageously defended peace," commented the populist leader. Strong men make peace, weak men make war."

title: War in Ukraine: American ceasefire offer is seen as a "trap" in Russia
lead: As American negotiators are reportedly about to arrive in Moscow, Russian authorities claim to be awaiting the details of the proposal. However, experts and television reports repeat that the Kremlin does not want a ceasefire.
text_eng: Less than twenty-four hours after announcing his proposal for a thirty-day ceasefire between Russia and Ukraine, U.S. President Donald Trump promised that his negotiators are heading to Moscow "right away." His Secretary of State, Marco Rubio, emphasized that "the ball is now in the court" of the Russians and urged them to accept the military truce "unconditionally." However, Vladimir Putin did not respond promptly. Kremlin spokesperson Dmitry Peskov told journalists pressing for a Russian reaction, "You are jumping the gun a bit." He simply stated that contacts with the Americans are planned in the coming days and that the Kremlin is awaiting "complete information" on the ceasefire offer. He did not confirm the reported visit to Moscow by Steve Witkoff, Mr. Trump's special envoy. Amidst mistrust and skepticism, Moscow is currently hesitant to provide a clear response. "In the eyes of the authorities, the offer is seen as inadvertently anti-Russian. But they are hesitant to reject it," revealed an anonymous former Kremlin insider to Le Monde. "A thirty-day ceasefire without any guarantees is clearly advantageous for Kiev and disadvantageous for Moscow. Currently, the Ukrainian army is retreating in the face of Russian troop advances. If Mr. Putin accepts a ceasefire today without guarantees on NATO expansion and the absence of NATO troops on the ground, his current position of strength will immediately turn into a position of weakness." The Kremlin wants to avoid a scenario where, if the ceasefire fails after a month, its army resumes fighting and Moscow is blamed for failing to end the conflict despite U.S. efforts. "The West could then say: Putin deceived everyone and only intended to attack," anticipates the same source. Maintaining ambiguity While the Kremlin leader has long mocked his reputation in the West, "he does not want to be seen as an aggressor on a chain." On television and social media, experts close to the Kremlin have conveyed the message: this American offer looks like "a trap." They recall Putin's remarks on December 20, 2024: "We do not need a ceasefire but a long-term, sustainable peace supported by reliable security guarantees." Paradoxically, Russian television portrays the American offer as almost a "non-event," insignificant in the context of Russian military successes. The latest news from Kursk, the region of Russia partially occupied by the Ukrainian army since August 2024 and increasingly being retaken by Russian forces, is making headlines on television news, especially with images of the liberation of Soudja, the main city. The ceasefire proposal is being pushed to the background, portrayed as part of a Western strategy rather than a sincere attempt to resolve the conflict. Journalists from state-owned channels, who in recent weeks have been instructed by the Kremlin not to show too much enthusiasm about Mr. Trump's presidency, remain cautious: for now, Moscow is waiting for the details of the ceasefire offer, casting doubt on the sincerity of the proposal. This approach allows for maintaining ambiguity: Russia does not openly reject the ceasefire, but it also does not show interest in discussing it. "All of this looks like a trap," ponders Andrei, a teacher in Moscow, interviewed remotely by phone. "We are in a proxy war with you, Europe, and America. Accepting a ceasefire now would mean depriving ourselves of a chance to win." This sentiment is widely shared by part of the Moscow middle class, liberal in lifestyle, critical of the Kremlin in domestic politics but loyal to its hardline stance against the West. "All of this looks like a trap. In exchange for the so-called ceasefire, the Ukrainians have regained their money, intelligence, and weapons from the United States. Europe is very scared. It should come to an agreement with Putin today before the president destroys Kiev and regains all Russian territories," warns Natalia, a business executive. "We don't need a ceasefire. We want the root causes of the problem eliminated. As long as the Kiev regime remains in place in its current status, everything will stay the same," asserts Mikhail, a literary agent. "If something changes in Ukraine, then anything is possible. But we are still far from that." A way to recall the Kremlin's ultimate ambition: a change of regime in Kiev.

title: Anti-Bolloré protest by Hachette Livre employees
lead: The elected members of the CSE of the leading French publishing group express their "shame" at being led by a shareholder who gives significant weight to far-right opinions.
text_eng: It was the last straw. The elected representatives of the central social and economic committee of Hachette Livre, the leading publishing house owned by Vivendi, the group of billionaire Vincent Bolloré, sent a letter to all employees on Tuesday, March 18, denouncing the "publication [on March 5], by Fayard, of the book Bannie, by Xenia Fedorova [306 pages, 21.90 euros], a pro-Putin journalist and former president of the Russia Today France channel, banned from broadcasting in the European Union since 2022." They continue: "After deciding to publish the far-right leader, Jordan Bardella, Fayard crosses a red line with the publication of this personality designated by experts as the 'French propaganda chief of the Kremlin'." The representatives believe that the "Bolloré and Fayard media" are giving a platform to "a supposedly banned journalist who hosts a weekly column on CNews." By publishing her, they argue that the publishing house Fayard – led by Lise Boëll, former publisher of Eric Zemmour – "becomes the mouthpiece of the authoritarian Russian power that imprisons its opponents and is suspected of causing the death of some, like Alexei Navalny or Anna Politkovskaya." When asked about the issue, the management of Hachette Livre did not respond. The letter from the representatives reminds that "freedom of expression is not absolute," as constantly emphasized by the Constitutional Council and the European Court of Human Rights. "The availability, in our premises, of JDNews, an opinion journal that gives a very large platform to far-right opinions, reminds us every week who is in charge, and makes us ashamed," they write. Adding bluntly, directed at their shareholder: "The employees of Hachette Livre can no longer bear to be associated with the Bolloré group, when the founding values of Hachette are diametrically opposed to the ideas now being promoted." They mention the latest corporate social responsibility report of the leading French and third global publishing house, which mentions its societal role, "that of contributing to the world of education, culture, and democracy." While they are outraged by the "observed support of Bolloré media for the authoritarian Russian power of [Vladimir] Putin," they also deplore their support for Donald Trump and his vice-president, J.D. Vance, recalling that the U.S. president "seeks to undermine the rule of law." The Hachette employees remind that "his supporters are working to banish from libraries and schools books with humanistic ideas, tolerance, or defending women's rights." Some of these books are actually "published by [their] subsidiary in the United States," they point out. "Deep malaise" Rarely has such a violent uprising called into question the management of Hachette Livre, as elected officials have been warning "in vain" for "several months" about "the industrial and social risk weighing on [their] activities." They mention in particular "contract terminations" in the fields of distribution or sales, as well as "boycotts" by booksellers, teachers, or readers, and also "departures or non-hiring" of authors or employees, due to the "editorial line close to the far right of the Bolloré sphere [CNews, Journal du Dimanche, Europe 1, Fayard]." Following this, on Wednesday, March 19, the CFDT-Livre-Edition published a leaflet entitled "no to the Bolloréization of minds, no to the contempt for employees!" The union highlights the "brutalization of social relations, the hardening of working conditions, the increase in turnover," and sees them as "worrying signals for the entire profession." Just as worrying in its view are the "attempts at ideological influence" since "the political neutrality of publishing houses is a condition to preserve the diversity of publications." According to the CFDT-Livre-Edition, the "spectacular deterioration of social relations" reflects "the deep malaise that is spreading in the world of publishing since the takeover of the two largest groups by media conglomerates [Vivendi at Hachette Livre, and Czech Media Invest (CMI) by Czech billionaire Daniel Kretinsky at Editis]." In addition to the "piles of far-right magazines at the entrances of Hachette's offices," the union laments a "failed attempt at 'entryism'" by the ultraconservative milieu in the management of the Bayard group, as well as the successive relocations (Hatier to Hachette, CMI to Editis, and also that of Bayard) "to cram employees together disregarding their health, but also professional efficiency."

title: In Washington, Emmanuel Macron warns Donald Trump against a forced "surrender" of Ukraine.
lead: "The peace 'cannot be a ceasefire without guarantees,' warned the French president during a joint press conference on Monday at the White House, stating that 'for us Europeans, it is an existential issue'."
text_eng: Despite significant differences that persist on the core issues, French President Emmanuel Macron said on Monday, February 24th, that he was "convinced there was a way" with Donald Trump to end the war in Ukraine, while also warning his American counterpart that a peace agreement could not mean a "surrender" by Kiev. During a joint press conference with his host at the White House, the French President also emphasized the need to provide "security guarantees" to prevent Russian President Vladimir Putin from launching another attack. "We want a quick deal but not an agreement that is fragile," said Emmanuel Macron, as Europeans have been concerned for several weeks about a deal that may be too favorable to Russia after being excluded from the Russo-American rapprochement initiated by Donald Trump since his inauguration on January 20th. "This peace cannot mean the capitulation of Ukraine, this peace cannot be a ceasefire without guarantees, this peace must provide for the conditions of Ukrainian sovereignty," emphasized Emmanuel Macron, stating that "for us, Europeans, it is an existential question." Volodymyr Zelensky in Washington? The French President also suggested a ceasefire during an interview in English with Fox News after the meeting. "The plan should be as follows: negotiations between the United States and Russia and between the United States and Ukraine," explained Macron, praising Donald Trump's announcement of a possible upcoming visit by Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky to the White House. "First, a ceasefire is needed. I think it could be concluded in the coming weeks," continued the French President in English, who has recently spoken with almost all European leaders. "If it is not respected, it will be the best proof that Russia is not serious," Emmanuel Macron added. Just before his discussion with the French leader, Donald Trump, during a question-and-answer session with journalists in the Oval Office, praised his "special" relationship with Macron and claimed to be able to end hostilities in "a few weeks." The American President also stated that the signing of an agreement with Ukraine on American access to the country's minerals was "very close," and even mentioned a visit by Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky to the White House to ratify it, "this week or the next." He sees this as a way for the United States to recoup the expenses incurred in supporting Kiev militarily since the Russian invasion in 2022. The Republican, who relies primarily on his dialogue with Russian President Vladimir Putin to stop the fighting, also stated that the latter would agree to the future deployment of European troops in Ukraine, but remained vague on the security guarantees that Washington might provide for these soldiers. The Russian president, for his part, stated on Monday that Europeans could "participate" in the conflict resolution process. "Europeans are ready to go as far as sending troops" to verify that "peace is being respected," said Emmanuel Macron, also asserting that Europe was ready to "strengthen" its defense – a constant demand from his American counterpart. Gulf French President and Donald Trump resumed their diplomatic camaraderie on Monday, already well-established during the Republican's first term (2017-2021), exchanging vigorous handshakes, as well as jokes and compliments. But behind this cordial atmosphere, a real gulf has emerged between Donald Trump and the Europeans regarding Ukraine. While Emmanuel Macron spoke to Donald Trump about Russia as an "aggressor" in Ukraine, the American president continues to equate the two countries – when he is not holding Volodymyr Zelensky responsible for the conflict, as he recently did. In parallel to the meeting in Washington, the United States, joining Russia and North Korea on this occasion, voted against a resolution adopted by the UN General Assembly reaffirming its support for Ukraine and its territorial integrity. "This year should be the beginning of a real and lasting peace," said Volodymyr Zelensky, presiding over a meeting in Kyiv with a dozen Western leaders who came to express their support for Ukraine. "Deals" Three years after unsuccessfully trying to dissuade Vladimir Putin from attacking, Emmanuel Macron has embarked on an equally uncertain mission with the American president. His American counterpart, who has never hidden his fascination for authoritarian leaders and is bored by lengthy multilateral negotiations, adopts a purely transactional approach to diplomacy. On Monday, he once again hinted on his social media platform at "major" economic agreements with Russia, which has been the target of numerous American sanctions until now. "I make deals. All my life, it's about making deals. That's all I know, making deals," declared the former real estate developer. Donald Trump also claimed to have made a "clear break" with the United States' foreign policy "of the past," which, after 1945, positioned itself as a guarantor of democratic values and as a protector of Europe through NATO and its nuclear arsenal. The Russian president, echoing Donald Trump, stated that American and Russian companies were already "in contact." He also supported American investments in the occupied territories of Ukraine. During a televised interview, the Russian president also suggested that Volodymyr Zelensky, who was recently harshly criticized by Donald Trump, was on the verge of becoming a "toxic" figure.

title: The essential national debate on Ukraine
lead: Legitimately concerned about Donald Trump's international policy, the French people must know what vision their elected officials defend and on which allies they rely. It is about peace, war, and what they imply in terms of national priorities.
text_eng: After weeks of questioning, initiated by the United States under Donald Trump, of the transatlantic relationship, a debate is finally organized in France, at the National Assembly on March 3, and at the Senate the following day, on "the situation in Ukraine and security in Europe." It is hoped that the national representation will finally take hold of this issue, after having remained deaf and silent in the face of the potential upheaval of the security architecture that has been at the origin of the longest period of peace on the European continent, until the brutal invasion of Ukraine by Russia three years ago. While President Emmanuel Macron is active on this front, the elected officials from the parties represented in the National Assembly have, for the vast majority of them, preferred to take refuge in denial and avoidance of the new challenges facing the Old Continent. They have barely managed, after several months of government instability, to provide the country with a budget and a social security financing law that only marginally reduce public deficits. Divided and focused on the 2027 presidential election, they have hardly prepared their constituents for the major shift that is taking place. The absence of international issues in the debates preceding the congresses that The Republicans, The Ecologists, and the Socialist Party must hold in the coming weeks is symptomatic. A great awakening This avoidance posture is no longer sustainable today. The humiliation inflicted by Donald Trump on Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky in the Oval Office of the White House on February 28 confirms a change of era that catches Europeans off guard. Legitimately concerned, our fellow citizens must know what vision the elected officials who represent them defend and on which allies they rely. The Russian aggression of 2022 has only partially opened this debate, which has escaped the campaign for the European elections as much as the one for the early legislative elections of June 2024. French politics is still in search of its "Zeitenwende," as formulated as early as February 27, 2022 by the German Social Democratic Chancellor Olaf Scholz: for him, the invasion of Ukraine, shaking the pillars of German stability, did indeed mark a "new era," even though he subsequently proved unable to derive a doctrine from this observation. A great awakening is necessary, which must also extend to the world of business, social partners, civil society, to allow a discussion on essential questions to which the order established after the Second World War responded. These questions concern peace, war, and what they imply in terms of national priorities, including also the response to climate disruptions and the preservation of the French social model. The obligation for the European Union to ensure its defense autonomously or face disappearance will force the national representation to urgently redefine its priorities and find the necessary funding. The delicate budgetary situation in the country, the risk of economic slowdown caused by Donald Trump's protectionist policies, will not make the task easier. All the more reason to face the facts without delay or evasion.

title: The new "Star Wars" of Donald Trump
lead: The American president signed a decree on January 27 announcing the creation of a missile defense shield with the deployment of "interceptors" in space. Their function is intended to be defensive, but this project marks a turning point in the risks of weaponizing space.
text_eng:
Of all the decrees issued by American President Donald Trump in the wake of his inauguration, the one ordering, on January 27, the creation of a new "Iron Dome" for the United States promises, if implemented, a strategic shift at least as radical as that initiated by the rapprochement with Russia's Vladimir Putin. As envisioned, this future missile defense shield requires a major and unprecedented "weaponization" of space. Therefore, all Western experts and military staff, who have remained very discreet on the subject until now, are relatively cautious, even worried, awaiting the first concrete draft of this project, scheduled for February 28. The American "Iron Dome," a campaign promise of Mr. Trump, aims to be nothing less than a revival of the aborted "Star Wars" program of former American President Ronald Reagan - also known as the "Strategic Defense Initiative" (SDI) - in 1983. Named after one of the Israeli territory protection systems, it requires disruptive technological leaps and significant development of capabilities in space, a key domain for detecting missile launches. On February 28, American defense contractors are expected to meet the deadline to submit a first list of proposals to the White House in order to quickly initiate its implementation. The American military-industrial complex has immediately been under intense pressure for this colossal project with enormous budgetary implications. The Trump administration's "request for information" was published only four days after the presidential decree. The reports from the contractors must not exceed about twenty pages. The first deliveries of defense systems or demonstrations are expected to take place by December 31, 2026, followed by two-year increments until 2030. This is practically tomorrow considering the traditional development cycles of military equipment. New technological context This pressure is directly related to the need to have cards on the table for the budget debates that have begun and will accelerate in the spring as the Pentagon must make a decision on the costly modernization of its nuclear arsenal. It also corresponds to a new technological context. "For some of the sought-after capabilities, the deadlines already seem very short. However, what appears to be a revival of Mr. Reagan's SDI forty years after its formulation is not subject to the same technical constraints," says Benjamin Hautecouverture, a research fellow at the Foundation for Strategic Research. In other words, this "Iron Dome" is less unattainable today than it was yesterday. In concrete terms, the ambition of Mr. Trump's new administration is to... Trump is working to improve the missile interception capabilities of all kinds that could target the United States, while programs in this area by China, North Korea, Russia, and Iran are growing. Americans already have highly effective missile defense systems deployable in theaters of operations, whether on land or at sea, such as the Thaad, Aegis, and Patriot systems, with varying ranges. The United States also has an architecture for protecting its own territory, called Ground-Based Midcourse Defense (GMD), mainly consisting of two launcher bases located at Fort Greely in Alaska and Vandenberg in California. However, this "GMD," which has been regularly criticized, was originally designed to block limited launches of intercontinental ballistic missiles (ICBMs) from North Korea. Increasingly, missiles developed by China and Russia are said to be "hypersonic," meaning they maneuver with a trajectory that is harder to counter, despite ongoing modernizations. Recently, North Korea is reportedly on the verge of launching the mass production of an ICBM – the Hwasong-19 – with a new propulsion system that would allow it to be deployed faster than a traditional liquid propellant missile. This was revealed on February 13 by the commander of the Pentagon's continental defenses before the U.S. Senate, to justify the "Iron Dome" project. These interceptors are currently only for defensive purposes. 

At the same time, voices have been increasingly raised in recent years to draw attention to the growing vulnerability of American military installations known as "forward" in the Pacific, whether in Japan or on the island of Guam, a central point of operations in the region. The increasing range of Chinese missiles could "keep runways and traffic routes at bases closed in the critical first days – or even the first weeks – of a war between the United States and China. No combination of countermeasures – including greater dispersal of aircraft (...) and more robust missile defenses – is likely to solve the problem," according to Kelly Grieco, an assistant professor of security studies at Georgetown University, along with other experts, in a detailed study for the Stimson Center in December 2024. Hence, the Trump administration's idea of stepping up efforts, this time by placing "interceptors" directly in space. The stakes: Exceeding the limits of conventional missile defense systems, which, although supported by enormous ground radars connected to satellite resources, can currently only destroy missiles once they have already traveled a portion of their trajectory – "mid-course," in the experts' vocabulary – or only very close to their final target – in the "terminal" phase. In contrast, with "space interceptors," the ambition is to try to counter these same missiles barely after their launch, specifically in the so-called "boost" phase. These interceptors, intended solely for defense at this stage, could be reinforced by the development of "non-kinetic" means, meaning they do not directly destroy potential enemy missiles in space but can neutralize them through jamming or cyber attacks. "The United States has already demonstrated initial mastery of space sensor capabilities, which could therefore accelerate," Mr. Hautecouverture explains. "Costs, especially those of space launches, are now lower than they were in the 1990s, 2000s, and 2010s, and technologies are generally more mature." "But demonstrating such a capability by 2030 will not be easy," notes the researcher, who is very cautious about the intentions of the new American administration. A loosely binding space treaty The "Iron Dome" desired by Mr. Trump could thus involve accelerating already known programs focused on the development of networked satellites operating in medium and low orbit. This is an area where Elon Musk's company, SpaceX, now an advisor to the American president, has developed a significant portion of its activities in recent years. Initially through the Starlink network, but also with the launch, since late 2022, of a similar network called Starshield, reserved for "governmental" uses, particularly those of the Pentagon. According to the elements highlighted by SpaceX, Starshield is capable of both imaging, communications, and hosting "payloads" to "support the most demanding missions." Donald Trump has always been a staunch advocate of the militarization of space, or at least maintaining American dominance in this field. In 2019, he created the US Space Force, the sixth branch of the American military, after the Army, the US Air Force, the US Navy, the Marine Corps, and the Coast Guard. This model has been emulated by most armies with space capabilities, starting with France. However, the concept of "weaponization" has long been a taboo. Whether due to fear of reigniting a race to prohibitively expensive and uncertainly effective armaments, or out of concern for destabilizing the fragile balances of international deterrence, which partly rely on satellite tools. The Outer Space Treaty, dating back to 1967, in principle prohibits the placement in orbit of any "carrier object" of nuclear weapons or weapons of mass destruction. However, its vague wording proves to be not very binding. By mid-2024, the United States had accused Russia of crossing a threshold in "weaponization" by placing a satellite in low Earth orbit "capable of attacking other satellites in low Earth orbit." "Weaponization" can take place in all orbits. But it is low Earth orbit that is currently popular and where the majority of economic developments and over 80% of objects are concentrated," notes Paul Wohrer, a specialist in space issues at the French Institute of International Relations (IFRI). It remains to be seen whether the American "Iron Dome" could be called into question or redirected due to the rapprochement between Washington and Moscow. Until now, the two powers have been in fierce confrontation in this area. Furthermore, China is also working on the development of such defense systems, according to the Pentagon. Similarly, on January 27, the White House emphasized in the signed decree its desire for the project to "improve the missile defenses of American troops deployed forward," as well as those of "allied territories, troops, and populations." The uncertainty of peace negotiations around Ukraine at this stage risks freezing these prospects.

title: War in Ukraine: near the Russian border, "I have never been as scared as I am now"
lead: In eastern Ukraine, volunteers are evacuating civilians in danger of death in the bombarded areas by bus, risking their lives.
text_eng: Every day at 4 a.m., Denis Naoumov, 33 years old, meets with a handful of volunteers from the Ukrainian NGO Proliska at a gas station outside of Sumy. Proliska ("Snowdrop" in Ukrainian) specializes in evacuating civilians in mortal danger from bombarded areas. On Saturday, March 22, two minibuses set off towards Krasnopillia, a large village that had 7,000 inhabitants before the large-scale Russian invasion, and now has only 300. It takes about an hour to reach Krasnopillia, which has been systematically bombed by Russian aviation for a week, after Vladimir Putin ordered the creation of a "buffer zone" on the Ukrainian side of the border. The Sumy region in northeastern Ukraine faces the Russian region of Kursk, where Ukrainian armed forces had occupied a small part around the city of Soudja since August 6, 2024. Pushed back by numerically superior Russian and North Korean forces, the Ukrainians would now only control 80 square kilometers of Russian territory. "I was chased by a drone." Contrary to the norm, the military manning the roadblock at the exit of Sumy signal the minibus drivers to pass without stopping. It quickly becomes clear why. In the headlights of the vehicles, emerging from the dark night, a four-wheel drive with its rear part burnt out appears in the middle of the chicane. "There has just been a strike here. They [the military] obviously have other things to do than worry about us," notes Anatoly, a Proliska driver, stepping on the accelerator. In the area near the front line, drivers have gotten used to driving at full speed. Not without reason. "Yesterday, I was chased by an FPV drone [First Person View, equipped with a camera] near Vessele and I had to step on it to escape," recounts Anatoli, 53 years old, the third Proliska volunteer. The village of Vessele is located 15 kilometers from the Russian lines. Most FPV drones cannot exceed 60 kilometers per hour, a speed sufficient to catch up and damage, or even destroy, most armored vehicles, while lighter vehicles have a chance to escape. "The military didn't tell us anything. So, we don't really know what the security situation is on the road," grumbles Denis Naoumov, head of the Proliska branch in the Sumy region. In the two previous days, Proliska had to cancel evacuations due to the presence of numerous enemy drones. A man of small stature with a round and kind face, the humanitarian explains that he receives an average of 300 phone calls and messages per day from residents of border areas or relatives "from all over the world" reporting family members in danger in the area. It is almost always elderly people reluctant to leave their threatened homes because they are too scared to leave. Without water or electricity, "People pass on my number by word of mouth," says Denis Naoumov, who used to be a teacher in a school before the invasion. The day before, a Ukrainian soldier called him to say that he would desert if Proliska did not immediately evacuate his disabled mother who was stuck near the Russian border, after the caregiver had been evacuated. The man explained that his brother and father are also fighting on the front lines and that he had no other choice to save his mother. "We went to get her and we will transfer her to Transcarpathia, at the other end of the country, where most disabled elderly people are housed." The day has already dawned when the two minibuses arrive at their destination. Krasnopillia is one of those towns near the front lines whose appearance changes every day. Multi-story buildings, mainly apartment buildings, administrative buildings, and factories are gradually flattened by guided glide bombs (KAB, corrected aviation bombs) dropped from tens of kilometers away and at very high altitudes by the Russian aviation. The day before, on Friday, March 21, the cultural center lost a floor. This imposing Soviet-style concrete building, 100 meters long, had already been hit by a guided glide bomb on May 29, 2024. In recent days, a clinic, a bakery, and a church have been razed. The town hall building, facing the cultural center on the other side of a large central square, is waiting its turn. All the windows on the facade have already been blown out, as well as the plywood boards that were supposed to protect them. Shortly before 6 a.m., half a dozen town hall employees and local officials come with serious faces to contemplate the damage. They had stayed holed up at home since the day before due to the violence of the bombing. With the help of police officers, they clear the glass, wood, and plastic debris littering the ground floor. Some doors refuse to open because their frames have been warped by the shockwave. There is no longer electricity, gas, or water in the town. "The Russians are unleashing on us." Explosions are frequently heard in the distance, but no one pays attention. A lone man shows up for evacuation, gives his name, and settles in a minibus. Then a retired couple accompanied by a very elderly lady. The man has tears in his eyes, takes out a handkerchief, wipes his bald head. He struggles to speak, trembles like a leaf, introduces himself as Oleg Vladimirovitch, 71 years old, a retired teacher. He asks for a cigarette and a bottle of water. He inquires, looking sheepish, to find out if the minibus is free, then, not receiving an answer to his naive question, goes to sit next to his wife and mother-in-law. Other elderly people fill up a first minibus, which immediately departs for Soumy. The other minibus goes to pick up disabled people through the unpaved, empty, and bumpy streets of Krasnopillia. Having difficulty finding his way, Denis Naoumov, one hand on the steering wheel, the other on the phone, curses at a caller asking to be taken back to Krasnopillia. "You forgot your jacket, so what? I don't want to have your death on my conscience if a bomb falls on you after I bring you back here!" He turns around once more and confides, "Yuri used to help me find addresses. He was killed yesterday by one of the five KABs dropped by the Russians. Vaporized, just like his house." Yuri was an employee of the city hall. Artem, the second driver of Proliska, 22 years old, takes over the wheel from Denis to take the full minibus to Soumy. Quiet, he looks grim and appears tense. "I've never been as scared as I am now, it's getting worse," this young blond with blue eyes finally confesses. He knows that neither his helmet nor his bulletproof vest will save him from an encounter with a drone. In the back, the evacuated passengers remain silent. "It was much calmer when our people were in Soudja," says Nina Vassilievna, 72. "And I believed Trump's promise of peace within twenty-four hours. Now, the Russians are unleashing on us, and I have lost all hope."

title: After Putin's "yes, but" to a potential ceasefire in Ukraine, Trump is showing signs of impatience.
lead: The Russian president has set conditions for the ceasefire with Kiev wanted by the United States, while the American special envoy on Ukraine, Steve Witkoff, is visiting Moscow. Volodymyr Zelensky has denounced the "manipulative words" of the Kremlin leader.
text_eng: Two images, and a central subject: Ukraine. The day of Thursday, March 13 was marked by a public dialogue at a distance between Vladimir Putin and Donald Trump. Hosting his Belarusian counterpart, Alexander Lukashenko, in Moscow, the Russian president reluctantly agreed, and under conditions, to the thirty-day ceasefire proposal put forward by the White House and endorsed by Kiev. Meanwhile, in Washington, Donald Trump met with NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte. The American-Russian rapprochement and the conditional acceptance of solidarity among allies, favored by the billionaire, greatly concern the European members of the Atlantic Alliance. At the end of this day, neither peace in Ukraine nor the future of NATO are becoming clearer. Vladimir Putin's words were eagerly awaited, following Ukraine's acceptance of the ceasefire principle during discussions with the United States in Jeddah (Saudi Arabia) on March 11. "We are in favor, but there are nuances," warned the Russian president, stating that he wanted to "discuss it" with the Americans and "perhaps call President Trump." He thanked him for his efforts towards resolving the conflict, before emphasizing "important issues." Understand: his prior conditions. These were at the heart of the meeting between the Russian president and Donald Trump's special envoy, Steve Witkoff, who arrived in Moscow on Thursday. The Kremlin expressed being "cautiously optimistic" after this meeting. While the White House had announced that Mr. Witkoff's stay in Moscow would be extended until Sunday, he left for Washington early on Friday morning, taking with him, according to the Kremlin, a message from Mr. Putin to Mr. Trump. "How can we ensure that such a situation [the conflict] does not happen again? How will control be organized?" asked Mr. Putin during his press conference with Alexander Lukashenko, questioning the means to ensure a ceasefire along a 2,000-kilometer contact line. The Kremlin chief demanded that the ceasefire not serve as a temporary truce that would allow Ukraine to recruit additional soldiers and receive new Western weapons.
