However, the White House announced, after the discussions in Jeddah, that it was reinstating intelligence sharing and arms deliveries to Kiev. "Predictable and manipulative words" Mr. Putin also warned: "Depending on the situation on the ground, we will agree on the next steps to end the conflict." The message from the Russian president - who also spoke on Thursday with Mohammed bin Salman Al Saud, the Saudi crown prince willing to "facilitate dialogue" and host a Putin-Trump summit - is clear: The beginning of the ceasefire will depend on the progress of his army in the vicinity of Kursk, the Russian region partly occupied by Ukrainian troops since August 2024, but where Kremlin forces have advanced rapidly in recent days. Less than twenty-four hours before these statements, Mr. Putin was in military fatigues at a command post in the field. The commander-in-chief then called for the "complete" liberation of the region. This was a way to exert pressure in the current diplomatic contacts: the Moscow army is advancing. It was also a way to reassure the ultra-patriotic factions in Russia, who have no trust in Americans and believe that no negotiation should disrupt the country's victorious march. In a speech on Thursday evening, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky criticized the "highly predictable and manipulative words" of his Russian counterpart. "Obviously," he noted, "Putin is afraid to directly tell President Trump that he wants to continue the war and keep killing Ukrainians. That is why, in Moscow, they accompany the idea of a ceasefire with conditions that will fail or drag on as long as possible." The Kremlin remained firm on its demands. Two irreconcilable political realities emerged on Thursday. In the first, Vladimir Putin sees himself as the master of time, convinced that he can prolong the war effort for the benefit of his country. It is not in his interest to openly challenge or insult his American counterpart, but he seems hardly inclined to consider significant compromises. In the second reality, Donald Trump claims to be pushing the conflict parties, through persuasion and blackmail, to cease hostilities quickly. He views deaths on the battlefield as an anomaly, an inconvenience, and not a terrible sacrifice for a noble cause like national sovereignty, in the case of Ukraine. The American president is in a hurry. This is a vulnerability in such a complex issue. The economic sanctions he would consider against Russia if Vladimir Putin rejected his negotiation offer would add to the immense pile of Western sanctions accumulated since 2014, which Moscow has largely managed to circumvent. "We agree with proposals to end hostilities, but we believe that this ceasefire must lead to a lasting peace and address the root causes of this crisis," insisted Mr. Putin. The formula is old. On December 20, 2024, he had already warned: "We do not need a ceasefire, but a long-term, sustainable peace supported by reliable security guarantees." Since then, the Kremlin has remained firm on its demands: The de facto capitulation of Ukraine, its status as a neutral state involving a renunciation of NATO, a definitive Russian annexation of the occupied Ukrainian territories, and finally, a lifting of Western sanctions. Yuri Ushakov, the president's diplomatic advisor, specified that he had reiterated the Russian demands during a phone call with the U.S. National Security Advisor, Mike Waltz, on March 12, a few hours before Mr. Witkoff's arrival in Moscow: "We will need to work, reflect, and also take into account our position," he later emphasized on television, stating that the ceasefire proposal only considered Kiev's interests, not those of Russia. "These are hasty measures that are not conducive to a lasting settlement," he regretted. "Mr. Putin is in a position of strength." The Kremlin is willing to discuss a ceasefire, but not necessarily implement it immediately, as Donald Trump hopes. "Putin does not want to categorically refuse the Americans," a former Kremlin figure close to power, who requested anonymity, confided to Le Monde. "But if Putin agrees today to a truce without guarantees on the non-extension of NATO and the absence of NATO troops on the ground, his current position of strength will immediately turn into a position of weakness. Putin does not want to find himself in the same situation as Mikhail Gorbachev after the reunification of Germany, when the geopolitical agreement relied solely on trust and was not formally documented. The West then simply ignored that gentlemen's agreement." Amid mistrust and skepticism, Moscow maintains a strategic ambiguity. By restating its conditions even before meeting the American envoy, the Russian president seems to be buying time. "He is passing the ball back to Mr. Trump and Ukraine. The idea of a ceasefire is a direct and unconditional act. And negotiations begin afterwards. By asserting preconditions to the ceasefire, he is actually rejecting the idea, without explicitly saying 'no'," analyzes Kirill Rogov, a Russian political scientist, founder of the Re: Russia analysis platform, and associated researcher at the Institute for Human Sciences in Vienna. "Putin is in a position of strength," he adds, "not because of the situation on the battlefield or because Washington is on his side. But because Trump wants to show progress towards a peace agreement. The American president has no effective leverage to compel Putin to make concessions, whereas he does have leverage to compel Ukraine." Speaking at the White House, Donald Trump praised Vladimir Putin's "very promising statement," "but it was not complete." The American president reiterated his belief that a ceasefire would likely lead to a definitive cessation of hostilities, as neither party would want to resume fighting. The American president, who staked his credibility on trusting his Russian counterpart, also seemed to reject the idea that negotiations should start from a clean slate. "Many details of a final agreement have actually been discussed," he clarified. "We will now see if Russia steps up, and if not, it will be a very disappointing moment for the world." Donald Trump specifically mentioned topics such as "pieces of territory that would be retained and lost," with a "major power plant involved." Mark Rutte's selflessness During the lengthy exchange with the press in the Oval Office, in the presence of the NATO Secretary-General, Mark Rutte, inevitably caused cognitive dissonance among staunch defenders of the transatlantic bond. The American president was speaking without any show of respect for the allies, instead borrowing a rhetoric of unilateral strength from Vladimir Putin. While Donald Trump spoke, Mark Rutte demonstrated a flexibility bordering on selflessness. It highlighted, indirectly, the inherent weakness of the Secretary-General of the Alliance. His predecessor, Jens Stoltenberg, had already anticipated all the desires and talking points of Washington during Joe Biden's presidency. Mark Rutte, on the other hand, had nothing to say about the "artificial line" that is the border with Canada, which Donald Trump claimed made "no sense." Embarrassment was compounded regarding Greenland, on which the American president reiterated, insistently, his claims of annexation. "I hadn't thought much about it before, but I'm sitting next to a man who could be very decisive," Donald Trump said with a smile. "You know, Mark, we need it for international security." Mark Rutte weakly struggled, saying, "I don't want to involve NATO in this," while redirecting the conversation to the importance of icebreakers in the Arctic. Donald Trump praised the former Dutch Prime Minister, saying he was doing a "fantastic job." His guest returned the favor with awkward enthusiasm, praising his historical role in mobilizing Europeans in defense matters during his first term and upon his return to the White House. Mark Rutte added, regarding the war in Ukraine, "You ended the deadlock." Hearing the NATO Secretary-General congratulate the American president on the dialogue initiated with Russia – so unbalanced, so hasty – also underscored the distance that still separates Europeans from their own emancipation.

title: Françoise Thom, historian: "The list of favors done by Trump for Moscow grows longer every day."
lead: The Russia specialist explains in an interview with "Le Monde" how the Russian president manages to manipulate his American counterpart, who has aligned his speech and actions with the interests of the Kremlin.
text_eng: Historian, emeritus lecturer at Sorbonne University, Françoise Thom is the author of numerous works on Russia, including "Poutine ou l’obsession de la puissance" (Litos, 2022). She analyzes the evolution of Russo-American relations, which she believes is marked by a "Putinization" of the United States, orchestrated by the Kremlin and accepted by Trump. How do the American-Russian negotiations aimed at bringing peace to Ukraine illustrate the "Putinization" of the United States that you diagnose? We cannot truly speak of negotiations when the American side unilaterally abandoned its pressure instruments on Moscow even before diplomatic exchanges officially began: Ukraine's accession to NATO, non-recognition of Russian territorial annexations, and the gradual lifting of sanctions. Vladimir Putin's tactic is simple: pretend to negotiate, concede nothing, and wait for new concessions from his American counterparts. It is noteworthy that Putin refused to negotiate with the initial candidate chosen by the American president, Donald Trump, as a special envoy for Russia and Ukraine, General Keith Kellogg, because he knew Russia and had expressed the opinion that one should be wary of the Russians. Trump promptly acceded to the desires of the Russian president and chose Steve Witkoff, a real estate developer and his golf partner, as his interlocutor, who was approved by Putin due to his profound ignorance of the issues and propensity to swallow even the most outrageous Kremlin propaganda. The "Putinization" of the Trump administration is evident in its indifference to ethics, leading to a clouding of intelligence. Trump is incredibly easy to manipulate, and Putin demonstrates every day that he knows how to push the right buttons to get the desired reactions. What does Trump's attitude owe to his personal admiration for Putin? Trump is primarily characterized by his partisan fanaticism and vindictive temperament. Putin has convinced him that they have the same enemies: the "globalists" lurking in the "deep state": Hillary Clinton, Joe Biden... Trump feels connected to Putin by a strange solidarity since the U.S. intelligence report on Russian interference in the 2016 elections, which Trump dismisses as "fake news." Putin has linked the Ukrainian cause to the Democrats, and since Trump systematically wants to do the opposite of Biden, it is easy to manipulate him from the Kremlin by suggesting that a certain measure will enrage the Democrats. Trump admires how Putin has dealt with Russian liberals and his mastery of staying in power. In an article published on the "Desk Russie" website, you write about the public humiliation of Volodymyr Zelensky at the White House by Trump and Vice President J. D. Vance, on February 28, "has all the marks of a special operation devised within the walls of the Kremlin." Why? For a long time, the Kremlin has dreamed of having the West impose surrender on the Ukrainians. The Russians want to break the morale of the Ukrainians. To do this, they must send the message that they have been abandoned by their Western allies and that their only option is to join their Russian brothers. After the 2008 Russo-Georgian war, the main argument of pro-Russian forces in Georgia was that the West had abandoned President Mikheil Saakashvili, who had relied entirely on the support of democracies. The exemplary punishment of the latter, who has been languishing in detention for over three years, is a demonstrative revenge by Putin: "This is what happens to those who oppose me." It is this maneuver that the Kremlin wants to repeat by tasking the Americans with toppling President Zelensky. Furthermore, Zelensky's public flogging strikingly resembled Stalinist conclaves of denunciation of fallen communists where everyone was forced to demonstrate their zeal by condemning the unfortunate "enemy of the people" – an expression that is becoming common in the United States. Senator Lindsey Graham, who had assured Zelensky in the morning that he stood in solidarity with Ukraine, loudly demanded his resignation in the evening. This kind of behavior is reminiscent of Stalinism. The Trump regime more closely resembles Stalinism than fascism. How so? For me, the most telling criterion is the pervasive lying, an impudent lie that one is forced to adhere to if they want to advance in the administration. The lie is not there to persuade, but to humiliate the one repeating it, to make them feel their insignificance in the face of a dictator who can demand that they affirm that the 2020 election was stolen, that Ukraine is responsible for the war. There is a typically totalitarian mechanism at play. Just like the abrupt shifting of alliances, with the United States voting at the United Nations alongside Russia and North Korea. Another striking feature for those familiar with the Soviet system is the emulation in servility, the cult of personality, which takes grotesque forms. One can also note the systematic appeal to the basest passions, the cult of strength. Is there a connection between the Trump administration's stated desire to destroy the federal state and this relationship with Russia? It is said that Trump is sowing chaos in the United States. But there is a logic in this madness: most of the measures adopted are in Moscow's interest. The first decision of Kash Patel, the new head of the FBI, was to close the Washington office responsible for counter-espionage. Attorney General Pam Bondi closed investigations into Russian oligarchs and their assets. Tulsi Gabbard, chosen by Trump to lead the intelligence services, was criticized by Senator Mitt Romney who said that she "simply parrots Russian propaganda. Her deceitful lies could cost lives." On February 24, 2022, Tulsi Gabbard echoed verbatim in a tweet the Kremlin's justification for the Russian aggression against Ukraine: "This war and suffering could have been avoided if the Biden administration and NATO had simply taken into account Russia's legitimate concerns about a possible entry of Ukraine into NATO." Also noteworthy is the closure of Usaid, the American soft power tool that Moscow feared the most, partly due to the support provided by this organization to Russian and Belarusian dissidents, not to mention aid to Ukraine. Additionally, there is the sabotage of transatlantic relations, daily attacks on America's allies, promises to lift sanctions, and the dramatic abandonment of Ukraine... the list of favors to Moscow grows longer each day. Is the connection between Russia and the Trump administration now acknowledged? From the Russian side, it is. Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov stated that the foreign policies of Washington and Moscow have been largely aligned since Donald Trump came to power: "The new American administration is rapidly changing all the configurations of foreign policy. This largely coincides with our vision." On the American side, it is acknowledged in a different way. The Trump administration regurgitates all the clichés of Kremlin propaganda. Trump seems to have internalized Russian fabrications. His rhetoric faithfully echoes Kremlin's allegations and the shifts in the Putinist party line. How do you compare the two classes of oligarchs: the Russians and the American tech giants? Can they have common goals? The interests of Russian oligarchs converge with those of Big Tech magnates. Putin and the major oligarchs, Russian and American, believe they are above the law. Putin's contempt for the law goes so far that he questions the very notion of the state, precisely because a state is based on a legal framework and has borders, which he dislikes. Trumpism aims to replace the state with a "power vertical" akin to Putin's, in which officials are chosen for their loyalty rather than competence. The dismantling of the state is accompanied by deregulation that benefits the major oligarchs seeking to control financial flows. This is why Kremlin strategists early on understood the services that Western libertarians could provide them. Russian foreign policy is characterized by its extraordinary persistence. This new phase in Russo-American relations is sometimes referred to as a Russian revenge for the end of the Cold War... In the KGB and GRU [the Russian military intelligence service], some have not accepted the end of the communist bloc and the collapse of the USSR, which they attributed to an American conspiracy. This core group of die-hards found a theorist in Alexander Dugin. As early as the "Yeltsin years" [president from 1991 to 1999], he brought back to the forefront the irreconcilable antagonism between Russia and the United States, resurrecting it through a geopolitical lens. Dugin proclaims that there is a total opposition between "continental" land powers such as Russia and maritime merchant powers like the United States and Great Britain. The former adopt authoritarian conservative regimes, while the latter embody liberalism. Just as Lenin believed that there could be no coexistence between socialism and capitalism – with socialism inevitably prevailing – Dugin argues that there is an existential antagonism between continental powers and maritime powers. Dugin's theories have permeated the Putin regime and have inspired the policy of undermining the United States that has been implemented over the past twenty years. The goal is to symmetrically inflict on the United States the same blows that they allegedly dealt to the USSR under Gorbachev [who led the Soviet Union from 1985 to 1991]: deprive them of their allies, unilaterally disarm them, ruin their economy, push them to the brink of civil war and dismantlement. All of which the Trump administration is currently carrying out. When Deputy Andrei Lugovoi declares on the show of propagandist Vladimir Soloviev: "Today, we have achieved total revenge, erasing our setback from the end of the Cold War," he reflects the triumphalist mindset of the Kremlin elites.

title: Emmanuel Macron is trying to prepare the French public for an unprecedented war effort.
lead: The head of state gathered political leaders on Thursday at the Elysée Palace to discuss a geopolitical context disrupted by the rapprochement between Donald Trump and Russia, before flying to Washington on Sunday.
text_eng: The closed-door meeting was improvised – decided the day before for the next day – but it was nonetheless dense and lengthy on Thursday, February 20, at the Elysée Palace. Four days before the third anniversary of the invasion of Ukraine by Russia, the President, Emmanuel Macron, the Prime Minister, François Bayrou, and a dozen representatives from political parties and parliamentary groups gathered for four hours to share highly sensitive information on the geopolitical context. A context disrupted by the Russo-American rapprochement since Donald Trump's return to the White House and the repeated attacks by the President of the United States and his administration against the legitimacy of the Ukrainian President, Volodymyr Zelensky. This "sort of endorsement given by the United States to Russian aggression in Ukraine," as denounced by former Foreign Minister and Defense Minister Jean-Yves Le Drian on Wednesday, February 19, now raises fears among European allies committed to the transatlantic relationship. The executive branch wants to prepare French society for the beginnings of an unprecedented war effort since 1945. For Emmanuel Macron, the goal is to mobilize public opinion on the necessity of European security, following talks earlier in the week with European partners and the North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO). With projected maps, strategic analyses, and a serious tone, the Chief of Staff of the Armed Forces, Thierry Burkhard, and the Deputy Director General of External Security, Nicolas Cailliez, each spent three-quarters of an hour outlining the evolution of the Russian threat and the contours of the "hybrid war" waged by the Kremlin on European soil over the past decade. The day before, in regional newspapers, the President set the tone: "Russia poses an existential threat to Europeans," he said, listing numerous dangers, from maneuvers at the Polish border to interference in electoral processes. Pro-European speech Before the political parties, Emmanuel Macron reiterated his message before gauging the various forces at play. The meeting mirrored the format of the "Saint-Denis meetings," which began in the summer of 2023 when Emmanuel Macron, with only a relative majority, sought to regain momentum after the crisis related to pension reform. The last meeting was in March 2024 and already focused on supporting Ukraine. Emmanuel Macron had not ruled out sending troops on the ground, shaking up European chancelleries. In Parliament, political forces clashed in a heated debate followed by a vote. On Thursday, there was "a certain gravity around the table and a particular spirit compared to other meetings of this kind," assessed the President of the centrist group in the Senate, Hervé Marseille. "Emmanuel Macron presented this as a moment of democratic transparency, and it was perceived by all participants as a timely meeting. While each participant highlighted the President's consistent pro-European stance, opinions diverged on France's stance towards the United States and Russia, as well as on the deployment of troops on the ground. 'He suggested that we should continue to support Ukraine militarily if it wished to continue the war. This raises concerns for me,' expressed the national secretary of the French Communist Party, Fabien Roussel. The first secretary of the Socialist Party, Olivier Faure (left), and the national secretary of the Ecologists party, Marine Tondelier (right), are attending a meeting with Emmanuel Macron to discuss the situation in Ukraine at the Elysée Palace in Paris on February 20, 2025. LUDOVIC MARIN/AFP The national coordinator of La France Insoumise, Manuel Bompard, said he was 'satisfied' to see Emmanuel Macron 'taking on' the idea of 'non-alignment,' before reiterating his hostility towards NATO to ensure the security of the Old Continent. Advocating for a 'European solution,' the ecologist Marine Tondelier emphasized the ideological affinities of the French far right with Trumpism or Putinism. 'When we talk about destabilizing democracy, it is to help a party win, and that is the National Rally. I said it even if it was not picked up,' lamented the national secretary of the Ecologists. Glimmer of hope The National Rally (RN), represented by the mayor of Perpignan, Louis Aliot, remained discreet around the table. The president of the RN, Jordan Bardella, was absent: he is scheduled to speak on Friday, February 21, at the Conservative Political Action Conference, opened on Thursday morning by the American Vice President, J.D. Vance. His ally, the president of the Union of Rights for the Republic party, Eric Ciotti, was the most critical of Emmanuel Macron, accusing him of 'breaking the Gaullist tradition of diplomatic balance between the great powers.' The former president of The Republicans (LR), who does not hide his fascination with Trumpism, demanded that the President engage in a peace process, reaffirming his rejection of troops on the ground and Ukraine's accession to NATO. He criticized Emmanuel Macron's European defense project as a 'pipe dream.' The president of the Union of Rights for the Republic party, Eric Ciotti, after a meeting with Emmanuel Macron to discuss the situation in Ukraine, at the Elysée Palace in Paris on February 20, 2025. LUDOVIC MARIN/AFP 'Now is not the time for populism and escalation, now is the time for truthful discourse,' on the contrary, affirmed Cédric Perrin, the LR president of the Senate's Foreign Affairs and Defense Committee." We need a national consensus on these international issues. Because what the United States and Russia want to do is isolate Europe." To thwart this scenario, Emmanuel Macron will fly to Washington on Sunday evening to meet with Donald Trump the following day. The President is convinced that he can persuade the American president that "it is in his interest to work with the Europeans at this time." He reiterated this on Thursday evening during a live broadcast on social media, where he answered questions from internet users. "I will tell him: 'Ultimately, you cannot be weak in the face of President Putin. It's not you, it's not your trademark, it's not in your interest,'" he explained. "How can you be credible against China if you are weak against Putin?" he wondered, warning Donald Trump against a "huge strategic mistake." Emmanuel Macron's positions are reigniting a glimmer of hope among members of his party, who have been distant since the dissolution of the National Assembly on June 9, 2024. Many recognize his ability to "take control" in the field of foreign policy at a time when Europe is vulnerable. "Emmanuel Macron is becoming the privileged interlocutor in this American-Russian tango that is trying to exclude Europe from the negotiating table," believes Roland Lescure, Vice President of the Assembly and deputy (Renaissance) for French citizens in North America. "National populist wind" "Emmanuel Macron's diplomatic activism is a bit like cholesterol, there is a good and a bad side to it," nuances international relations specialist Bertrand Badie. There is the aspect that sets Emmanuel Macron apart from other European heads of state, as he tries to reconceptualize the new scenario of international relations by advocating the need to rebuild Europe. And then there is this ostentatious activism, motivated by the old postulate that French diplomacy must be at the forefront of the world and which, in the current context, also aims to counterbalance Mr. Macron's disastrous drop in popularity on the domestic scene. The President's warnings are weakened by this "national populist wind" that spares no French opinion, as elsewhere in Europe, favoring "national selfishness," continues Mr. Badie. "Today, we want to prepare public opinion urgently, but it should have been done from the beginning of the Ukrainian invasion," regrets Claude Malhuret, President of the Independent-Republic and Territories group in the Senate. This member of Edouard Philippe's Horizons party ventures into a parallel with the 1930s, "a moment when democrats sought to reassure their opinions to the end without understanding that there are serious situations where it is better to warn at the risk of causing alarm." On Wednesday, François Bayrou agreed to a parliamentary debate on the geopolitical situation, proposed by a portion of the national representation. It is scheduled to take place in early March. This is a way to continue involving the political parties, which have been somewhat apathetic in the face of recent upheavals. "On these issues, we currently have a political class that is extremely intellectually lazy," said former MoDem president of the Foreign Affairs Committee in the Assembly, Jean-Louis Bourlanges.

title: "Fen", a Chinese mercenary serving in Putin's war in Ukraine.
lead: This former truck driver saw himself as an adventurer, but, as a victim of a suicide drone, he mainly experienced Russian hospitals. Back in China, he is now a building caretaker.
text_eng: "Fen" cannot say whether it was the need for money or the desire to move that sent him to fight alongside the Russians in Ukraine. His decision was in any case not political. "It was just going somewhere," explains the 37-year-old man with short hair, sitting in a corner of a tea house in a major Chinese city, who prefers to only give the nickname the Russians gave him: "Fen." In early July 2023, he boarded a flight to Beijing, then another to Moscow, determined to join the foreign mercenaries who had joined the Russian army in its war in Ukraine. A conflict whose reasons leave him indifferent, claims the man who, since his return, works as a building caretaker. On social media, especially Douyin, the Chinese version of TikTok, Le Monde was able to identify about forty Chinese accounts claiming, with images, to have joined the Russian forces. "Fen" only met seven of his compatriots on the front, but he estimates that a few hundred may have joined the battle. All of them left on their own initiative, as tourists. But their mere presence could embarrass China, which claims to be neutral while Western countries reproach it for its diplomatic and commercial closeness with Moscow, whose arms industry depends on many Chinese components. When asked, the Chinese Ministry of Foreign Affairs said it did not have any information on the subject. Thousands of foreign fighters The Chinese mercenaries are probably fewer in number than those from countries such as Nepal (over 15,000 according to the American channel CNN) or North Korea (11,000 soldiers sent by Pyongyang, according to the Pentagon). Indians, Malaysians, and many citizens of former Soviet republics have also joined the Russian side, while Ukraine also attracts thousands of foreign fighters, either out of political conviction or for the lure of gain. The Intelligence Online newsletter claimed on January 27 that the Chinese embassy in Moscow had asked – in vain – the Russian Ministry of Defense for an exact count of its citizens engaged in Russia. The fact that the Russian army has not given up on recruiting Chinese volunteers is one of the breaches in the "limitless friendship" proclaimed between Russia and China. Perhaps because Moscow intends to remain sovereign over the choice of individuals entering its territory, or because recruitment centers are under pressure from higher authorities to maintain the necessary quotas. The idea of going to the front came to "Fen" when he discovered, on social media, Chinese people fighting in Ukraine, some of them in the ranks of the private paramilitary group Wagner. The thirty-something had just left Laos, where he was driving trucks. After managing to contact some of them, he decided to leave. After a night in the Russian capital upon landing, managing with a translation app, he boards a train to Krasnodar, where the Wagners have one of their main bases. Upon arrival, a bad surprise awaits "Fen," who doesn't really follow the news: his Chinese contacts from Wagner inform him that Evgueni Prigojine and his men rebelled, unsuccessfully, against Vladimir Putin on June 24, 2023. The time is no longer for hiring. One year of commitment and 1,900 euros per month. Back to Moscow. This time, "Fen" presents himself to the regular army. He only has to provide a few pieces of information: his age, a phone number, his parents' names, and then fill out a brief medical questionnaire where he is asked to report any serious or contagious illnesses. "It was very simple," he recalls. He immediately goes to train in a camp, 80 kilometers southwest of Moscow, and learns to use an assault rifle, fire a shell, protect himself from gunfire, and assist a wounded person. Security matters are not entirely foreign to him: he served two years in the ranks of the People's Armed Police, the Chinese paramilitary force responsible for internal political stability, counter-terrorism, and disaster response. He spends three weeks at the training camp before going to sign his contract at a Russian Ministry of Defense office on August 4, 2023. Not knowing the language does not deter him. The essential terms - one year of commitment and 200,000 rubles per month, equivalent to 1,900 euros at the time - seem acceptable to him. He is immediately sent to the Ukrainian front, near Bakhmut, where the war "consumes" the most men. He will be a mortar loader. Without speaking the language, he learns the few essential words to communicate with the one aiming. He loads the shell into the barrel. On the other side, the Ukrainians retaliate. One day, shrapnel injures his hand. His right index finger still moves with difficulty today; after pouring himself another tea, "Fen" lifts his sweater to show some scars on his chest. Despite the danger, he also remembers long days of boredom, sitting in the trench doing nothing, as well as those cans and Russian sausages with a taste so different from Chinese cuisine that he never managed to get used to. Stints on the front are long and irregular. Sometimes, "Fen" spends a week there, sometimes more. His unit had just spent ten days there when they were allowed, on the late afternoon of September 27, 2023, to leave. He can finally go take a shower and rest in the rear. On the bumpy road that takes him away from the front, the Lada in which he and three other soldiers have just boarded cannot move as fast as needed to escape the threat of suicide drones. A terrible explosion, fire, a flash... The vehicle is destroyed and the driver killed. "Fen," sitting just behind, is dazed, his face covered in blood. Another man screams in pain; the Chinese mercenary pulls him out of the vehicle, doing his best to stop the bleeding with techniques he learned near Moscow. "Fen" shows his bandaged face, the result of injuries caused by a Ukrainian suicide drone. February 21, 2025. Two hours later, a Russian team passes by and saves the three survivors, one of whom will lose both legs. "At the time, I only thought about our survival. It was only later, in the hospital, that I was overcome by fear, realizing that I could have been killed by just a few centimeters," he confides. "Fen" spends four months with his face wrapped in bandages, moving from one hospital to another, from Luhansk to Rostov, Moscow, and all the way to Khabarovsk, in the Russian Far East, almost on the Chinese border. Photos, scars on his nose and eyelids, a red spot in his eye, and hospital admission documents corroborate his account. Returning to the front "Fen" did not reach the end of his contract, though. Once recovered, he has to return to the front, still in the Bakhmut region, then in the vicinity of Marinka. "To be honest, I didn't want to go back," he admits. It will take another three months before the Russian army confirms his demobilization in November 2024. Relieved, he chooses a flight to Central Asia, then a train to the Xinjiang region in western China. To enjoy the scenery, but also because Chinese security services closely monitor men traveling alone from Russia. The deaths of some of these Chinese mercenaries add to the sensitive nature of their involvement. A reporter from the Chinese channel Phoenix TV had already reported the death of one of them, Zhao Rui, at the end of 2023. "Fen" knew another one. He went by the name Shan Lang, "Mountain Wolf," possibly a pun on the title of a famous Chinese film similar to Rambo released in 2015, "Wolf Warriors." The man, who was among the few compatriots "Fen" had met during training, died in the fall of 2023, a few weeks after visiting "Fen" in the hospital in Luhansk. "He was born in 1989 in Henan," he recalls. "He was a good guy, very cheerful." The victim's regiment of other Chinese soldiers called his family to announce his death, but "Fen" did not have the courage to do so. The conversation would have been even more painful as few of these men inform their families of their departure to war.

title: Turkey, once considered a "problematic" partner, has become essential for a weakened European Union.
lead: By arming Ukraine with one hand and negotiating with Russia with the other, without applying Western sanctions against Moscow, Ankara is playing a delicate balancing act.
text_eng: On February 17, President Emmanuel Macron gathered at the Elysée Palace seven European leaders, the heads of the North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO), and community officials to reaffirm their support for Ukraine. Several European Union (EU) officials had expressed their frustration at not being invited. However, one country was notably absent: Turkey, the second military power in NATO. It took a spectacular altercation in front of the cameras eleven days later, on February 28, at the White House between the American president, his vice president, J.D. Vance, and the Ukrainian president, Volodymyr Zelensky, for things to change. Turkey's absence from Paris, immediately labeled a "strategic miscalculation" by Middle East specialist at the American think tank Hudson Institute, Zineb Riboua, was compensated for in London two days later, on March 2, by British Prime Minister Keir Starmer. Invited to this crisis summit among about fifteen leaders, Turkish Foreign Minister Hakan Fidan did not say much after the meeting, like the other participants, but he emphasized the importance of his country's participation in the new security architecture being discussed for Europe. And that is already a lot. The day before, he had a phone call with his Russian counterpart, Sergey Lavrov. After years of distancing from Brussels, increasingly visible diplomatic tensions with Paris and Berlin, Ankara, which was an enthusiastic EU candidate just a quarter of a century ago, now embodies a country with multiple and complex diplomatic facets. Considered by its peers at best as "troublesome," at worst as "disruptive" and "unreliable" within the Atlantic Alliance, Turkey is now unavoidable, even essential, for a Europe more than ever weakened by Washington's changing stance. Displaying a cordial understanding with Putin. Why? The country led by Recep Tayyip Erdogan for twenty-two years is first and foremost one of the few regional powers capable of both cooperating with Russia and limiting its influence, as it has shown in Syria. Before the fall of Bashar al-Assad, Moscow defined the parameters and contours of Turkish influence in northern Syria. Today, it is the other way around. This seriously encourages EU countries to establish mechanisms to involve Turkish leaders more frequently. But that's not all, of course. The day after the meeting at the Elysée, US Secretary of State Marco Rubio and Sergey Lavrov met in Riyadh to start a "normalization" of their relations and discuss Ukraine. At the same time, Volodymyr Zelensky visited Ankara to meet with the Turkish head of state. By hosting the Ukrainian president, Recep Tayyip Erdogan sent a clear message that Turkey remained a key player in shaping the future of Ukraine. President Zelensky himself reinforced this idea by declaring that "the European Union, Turkey, and the United Kingdom should participate in discussions and the development of necessary security guarantees with America regarding the fate of our region." Despite the friendly relationship displayed with his "friend" Putin, Mr. Erdogan remains one of Ukraine's strongest supporters, firmly committed to defending Kiev's territorial integrity and countering Moscow's ambitions. Turkey cannot help but see Russia's aggressive expansionism in the Black Sea region as a threat to its geopolitical and energy security. The encroachment around the Black Sea, the annexation of Abkhazia in 2008, the annexation of Crimea in 2014, and the invasion of Ukraine in February 2022 are all perceived in Ankara as destabilizing the region. Not to mention the symbolic significance of Crimea, the mythical homeland of the Turkic-speaking Tatars, a former Ottoman protectorate. Strategic position It is worth noting that, unlike many European nations that hesitated to support Kiev in the early days of the Russian invasion, Ankara acted decisively. Turkey invoked the Montreux Convention, closing the Bosphorus and Dardanelles straits to Russian warships – a measure that, although presented as neutral, significantly limited Moscow's ability to strengthen its Black Sea fleet. Supporting Ukraine effectively limits Russian expansion in the region and strengthens Turkey's strategic position. From Ankara's perspective, a resilient and sovereign Ukraine is a crucial barrier to Russian dominance in the region. On the battlefield, Turkey provided Kiev with one of its most effective weapon systems: the Bayraktar TB2 drone, which had also proven decisive in Libya, where Ankara exposed Russian vulnerabilities. These drones helped Ukraine destroy enemy armored columns and ships, tipping the balance in key battles. By arming Ukraine on one hand and engaging with Russia on the other without applying Western sanctions against Moscow, Turkey is walking a fine line. This is not the first time. It is up to the Europeans to accept this Turkish moment. Recep Tayyip Erdogan has not ruled out contributing to a peacekeeping force in Ukraine – in the event of a ceasefire. The country has no shortage of manpower or technological expertise on the battlefield, whether in the air or on the ground. "Contradictory power," according to the formula of European law researcher Alexis Coskun, Turkey would have no trouble flexing its muscles in the midst of the continent's strategic awakening.

title: Faced with the rift between the European Union and the United States, Giorgia Meloni clings to the idea of unity in the "West."
lead: The President of the Italian council, aligned with Donald Trump's ideology, refuses to consider the security of her country without American protection.
text_eng: The far-right President of the Italian council, Giorgia Meloni, has significantly gained her place on the international stage. The question remains as to what she wants to do with it. Or what she can do with it. Since the beginning of the ongoing crisis in the transatlantic relationship, her stance has been to repeatedly call for preserving the unity of a "West" that is already fractured and whose conceptions diverge on both sides of the ocean. Ms. Meloni has, however, clearly expressed her ideological alignment with Washington without clearly assuming the consequences of this positioning on her role in Europe. Her shared views with Donald Trump, her "friend" Elon Musk, and J.D. Vance, whom she praised for his attack on the European democratic model in Munich, are evident. Tipping the balance towards Washington, they overlap with the reality of an Italy that fundamentally is not ready to consider its security outside of American protection, and they combine with a deep distrust of French intentions regarding European defense. In fact, Giorgia Meloni's bilateral relationship with French President Emmanuel Macron has never moved beyond the original disagreements. The urgency of the sequence that began with the start of negotiations between the Trump administration and the regime of Vladimir Putin and continues with the suspension of American aid to Kiev has not helped. It was with reluctance that the council president attended the summit on Ukraine organized in Paris on February 17, harshly criticizing the format and showing her visible and deep weariness. A sign of credibility for a former radical Eurosceptic and the cost of entry into Western diplomatic play in 2022, the Italian leader's unwavering support for Ukraine has taken on a more moderate tone. "Difficult position" There is no question of calling for a European revival. Ms. Meloni, who downplayed Donald Trump's threats regarding Greenland, calls for Western unity. She is, in fact, the only leader of the European Union (EU) to have attended the inauguration ceremony of the American president and the only one not to have clearly taken sides in the hours following the historic turning point of his altercation with Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky on Friday, February 28. However, she did meet with the latter at the London summit the day after, judging him to be "clear-headed" and "rational." The balance sought by the Italian council president may defy the laws of physics. "The moment of curiosity about Giorgia Meloni being portrayed as the new strong leader of Europe is over. She is now entering the tough realm of international politics... It is now that she will have to make the right choices," assesses a player in the Roman diplomatic scene. The problem is that this balance primarily concerns the coalition that brought it to power, between a pro-European center-right and a radical right led by its deputy prime minister, Matteo Salvini. The latter, who wore a T-shirt with Vladimir Putin's image on Red Square in 2014, never misses an opportunity to loudly echo the now converging positions of Donald Trump and the Russian president on the Ukrainian issue, while also trying to appeal to a deeply rooted pacifist sentiment in Italian public opinion. Therefore, Giorgia Meloni is opposed to the possibility of sending European troops to Ukraine. The idea of a European nuclear umbrella provided by France and the United Kingdom is equally repugnant to her. It would be a "not very intelligent solution," as she stated in London, since it would involve American disengagement. Italy would indeed be relegated to a secondary position, whereas its privileged relationship with Washington's leaders could allow it to have more influence, regardless of the nature of the future transatlantic relationship in the era of Donald Trump. So, Mrs. Meloni could finally play the role of a "bridge" between Europe and the U.S. that her camp has been advocating for her since the American presidential election. "The ongoing developments are rapid and create a lot of tensions, which makes her uncomfortable. Giorgia Meloni's position will become even more difficult if the gap widens between Europe and the United States," notes a diplomat. Commercial pitfall "For me, the priority is always to defend the Italian national interest, and I believe that it is in the Italian national interest to avoid any possible fracture within the West," said Giorgia Meloni on March 3, speaking on Italian public television, with the council president hoping to be invited to the White House in the coming weeks. While Friedrich Merz, the likely future center-right chancellor of a Germany structurally resistant to the idea of transatlantic decoupling on security matters, has stated his intention to work towards the "independence" of the EU from Washington, Giorgia Meloni has made the defense of Western unity the mantra of her current positioning. "I will do everything to keep the West united. I don't even want to consider any other scenarios," she said in London about expanding the nuclear protection of France and the United Kingdom. "Giorgia Meloni maintains the illusion for herself that the transatlantic community could still find a modus vivendi. She is not ready to accept that the Alliance is dead," analyzes Arturo Varvelli, head of the Rome office of the European Council on Foreign Relations. For her, the worst-case scenario would be having to choose between the United States and the European Union. However, the unity of the West, at least in terms of trade, is not a priority for President Donald Trump, who has clearly stated his intention to impose tariffs of up to 25% against an EU that he believes is designed to "screw over the United States," and of which Italy is a founding member, a historical pillar, and the second largest manufacturing power, fully integrated into the value chains of the continent. This could disrupt Italy's position on a matter where national interest, dear to Giorgia Meloni, aligns with that of Europe and is no longer reconcilable with that of her political allies in the White House.

title: The European far right comes to the rescue of Le Pen: "I am Marine!"
lead: The French and European far right are rallying together after the conviction of Marine Le Pen and eight other Members of the European Parliament. The left, on the other hand, is calling for respect for justice: "When you steal money from the French, you are punished."
text_eng: There is nothing like a sharp judicial decision to establish a precise map of unwavering friends, more moderate supporters, and true adversaries of those who are convicted. This is what the numerous reactions to the judgment of the Paris criminal court on Monday, March 31, offer, which found Marine Le Pen and eight former members of the National Rally (RN) guilty of embezzlement of public funds, and sentenced the leader of the RN group in the National Assembly to a five-year ban on running for office, effective immediately. Foreign far-right leaders were among the first to react. "I am with Marine!" immediately wrote Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orban, one of her allies for years, on the social network X. A similar tone was struck in Rome. "Let's not be intimidated, let's not stop: full steam ahead, my friend!" reacted Matteo Salvini, leader of the League and deputy prime minister, in a statement. According to him, this conviction was inspired by the European Union, it is a "declaration of war from Brussels." The courts, he says, would like to exclude Marine Le Pen from political life. "It's a bad movie that we also see in other countries like Romania," said Salvini. This was a reference to Calin Georgescu, the far-right candidate who came first in the annulled presidential election in Romania, and charged with "false statements" regarding the financing of his campaign. In the Netherlands, the leader of the far-right Party for Freedom, Geert Wilders, also said he was "shocked" by the "extremely severe" verdict against Marine Le Pen. He added: "I am convinced she will win her appeal and become the President of the French Republic." Russia's Vladimir Putin also expressed his support. "More and more European capitals are violating democratic norms," reacted Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov during his daily press briefing, in response to a question about the conviction of Marine Le Pen. As for Elon Musk, he also commented on the French decision with a sentence. "When the radical left cannot win through democratic voting, it abuses the judicial system to imprison its opponents," he wrote on X, the network he owns. In this case, Marine Le Pen was sentenced to four years in prison, two of which are firm and can be served under electronic monitoring. However, she announced that she will appeal, which suspends the implementation of such a measure, making it effective only once the decision becomes final. "Indignant judicial witch hunt." In France, figures from the far right also came to the defense of Jean-Marie Le Pen's daughter, on the theme of violated democracy. "Today, it is not only Marine Le Pen who is unjustly condemned: it is French democracy that is being executed," wrote the president of the RN, Jordan Bardella, who could replace her as a candidate in the 2027 presidential election. "Is France still a democracy?" similarly questioned Eric Ciotti, president of the Union of the Right for the Republic, a major ally of the RN in the National Assembly, referring to an "unworthy judicial cabal," "a power-capturing system that systematically sidelines any candidate too far to the right who could win." While acknowledging their "disagreements," Eric Zemmour (Reconquête!) also supported Marine Le Pen with a key phrase: "It is not for judges to decide who the people should vote for." Within the right-wing, comments are more nuanced, with several leaders regretting the impact of the decision without contesting its foundation. Like François-Xavier Bellamy (The Republicans), who admits that "political commitment does not place anyone above the law" but believes that the provisional execution of the sentence was not justified here. "It is not healthy for an elected official to be banned from running in an election," similarly argued Laurent Wauquiez, president of the group of Republican deputies in the National Assembly. "Political debates should be settled at the ballot box." "No excuse" Nuances are also present in parts of the left. "We reject as a matter of principle that recourse should be impossible for any litigant," commented La France insoumise (LFI) in a statement, while acknowledging the decision. It is "at the ballot box, regardless of the candidate," that LFI intends to combat the RN. "The decision to remove an elected official should be up to the people," added the party's founder, Jean-Luc Mélenchon, who himself has been under investigation by the Brussels anti-fraud office since 2017, suspected of using his European parliamentary assistants for his activities in France. The rest of the left has been more categorical. "The law is the same for everyone," declared Cyrielle Chatelain, president of the green group in the National Assembly. "There is no excuse; when one steals the money of the French, one is punished. Period. When one is elected, one has a duty to set an example." A similar tone was echoed by Jérôme Guedj, a socialist deputy from Essonne: "Justice must be the same for elected officials as for those who are not elected, for the powerful as well as the weak." This stance was also maintained by the communist Fabien Roussel: "Mrs. Le Pen is a political leader who demands more firmness from the justice system! Let us respect justice, therefore." "For the deputy of Somme François Ruffin, a former member of 'La France Insoumise' who now sits with the Greens, the conviction is 'good news' - 'But beware, the power of the far right is no longer limited to Marine Le Pen,' he warns. 'There is a plan B like Bardella, a plan R like Retailleau, a plan Z like Zemmour. Those who believe that if Marine Le Pen were to fall, the far right would collapse, do not understand the country.'"

title: After his altercation with Donald Trump, Volodymyr Zelensky is trying to preserve the dialogue.
lead: During a summit organized on Sunday in London, the Ukrainian president and around fifteen European leaders agreed on the importance of keeping the United States by their side.
text_eng: Interviewed on Sunday, March 2 in London, about being roughed up in the Oval Office by Donald Trump and J. D. Vance, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky lamented the media "format" given by the White House to his meeting. However, he stated that he remains open to discussions: "If the President of the United States of America invites me to a constructive dialogue (...) I will be there, out of respect for America, the American people, and the President." The Ukrainian President made these remarks on the sidelines of a summit where he was received alongside about fifteen European leaders. Following the summit, he praised the "clear support from Europe" and a "greater unity, a stronger willingness to cooperate."

title: War in Ukraine: Uncertainty about the fate of North Korean soldiers deployed in Kursk
lead: The 10,000 North Koreans dispatched to this Russian region to fight alongside the Russians had withdrawn from the area in mid-January after suffering heavy losses.
text_eng: Where have the 10,000 North Korean soldiers deployed in support of Russian forces in the Kursk region, Russia gone? Speculations are multiplying by the day. Kiev claimed, at the end of January, that the North Korean military had been "withdrawn" due to heavy losses. Citing a source from South Korean intelligence services, the Yonhap news agency announced on February 7th that North Korean troops had disappeared from the Kursk region since mid-January. However, Volodymyr Zelensky stated on the evening of February 7th that North Korean soldiers were once again fighting in Kursk. "There have been new assaults in the [region's] operation zones, the Russian army and North Korean soldiers were involved again," said the Ukrainian president, adding that "hundreds" of Russian and North Korean soldiers had been "eliminated." According to the 47th "Magura" brigade of the Ukrainian armed forces, "the enemy has changed tactics." "If before, they mainly attacked with armored vehicles, now they are launching infantry assaults" and "storming with quads," the brigade wrote on its Telegram channel on February 9th. Seoul has not responded to Zelensky's statements and remains cautious. Two explanations for these differing views can be put forward. South Korean intelligence services are disrupted by the political chaos in their country and are leaking contradictory information. Their Ukrainian counterparts, on the other hand, are seeking to support the presence of North Koreans alongside the Russians. "Problematic tactical coordination" The revelation on January 11th of the capture of two wounded North Korean soldiers and the international echo, orchestrated by Kiev, given to this "irrefutable evidence" of North Korean forces' engagement could explain why they are keeping a low profile. Pyongyang may be seeking to avoid such incidents from recurring and to further train its troops before sending them to the front lines. "As North Korean troops are not visible, one could indeed think of a withdrawal," says Ryo Hinata-Yamaguchi, associate professor at the Institute of International Strategy at the International University of Tokyo. "But they may have temporarily withdrawn from the front line to regroup and reorganize." The professor also does not exclude the possibility that only certain units have withdrawn. "The reasons may vary: heavy losses or inefficiency in their operations," he continues. "The problems faced by these troops are not so much about their actions themselves but about problematic tactical coordination under the command of Russian forces." The Ukrainian army seems to have taken advantage of this withdrawal to advance. According to a report from the Institute for the Study of War (ISW), based in Washington, published on February 7th, it has advanced 5 kilometers southeast of Soudja. Ukrainian military analyst Oleksandr Kovalenko believes that the withdrawal of North Koreans was only partial and linked to "colossal losses due to the fact that they were used as cannon fodder in open spaces." "It is terrible publicity for the Pyongyang regime, which was counting on success to impress its adversaries," he adds. According to Ukrainian political analyst Vitaly Portnikov, Kim Jong-un is using the Russo-Ukrainian front primarily to allow his army to gain war experience, as it has not participated in any conflict for several decades. North Korea is also taking advantage of this to improve its military-industrial technology. The precision of North Korean ballistic missiles, fired by Russian forces at Ukraine since December 2023, has significantly improved from a poor performance of 1 to 3 kilometers to 50 to 100 meters today, explained two high-ranking Ukrainian sources to Reuters. Injured Russians sent to North Korea The Ukrainian army launched its offensive in Kursk on August 6, 2024, while in a difficult position. The objective was to force the Russians to thin out their forces in the Ukrainian front in Donbass to redeploy to this area. However, Russia maintained its contingent and positions, and chose to deploy 10,000 North Korean soldiers to Kursk in October 2024. The involvement of regular troops from a third country in this war was both a first and the result of a strategic partnership agreement signed between Pyongyang and Moscow in June 2024. As of February 5, six months after the start of the Ukrainian incursion, Russian forces have recaptured at least 57% (655 square kilometers) of the territory occupied by Ukrainians, according to the ISW. Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky, the Commander-in-Chief of the Ukrainian Armed Forces Oleksandr Syrsky, and Ukrainian Defense Minister Rustem Umerov on February 8, 2025. HANDOUT / AFP It is unlikely that Kim Jong-un will permanently withdraw his troops from combat zones or reduce their numbers. By projecting his forces to the doors of Europe, the leader of North Korea strengthens his image on the global geopolitical stage. Above all, he earns foreign currency: soldiers' salaries are paid to the North Korean state, which also receives revenue from arms sales to Russia. In exchange for its involvement, the regime also benefits from Russian assistance in missile technology and oil supplies. As a sign of deepening relations between Moscow and Pyongyang, many injured Russian soldiers in Ukraine now receive care and rehabilitation in North Korea, said Russian Ambassador to North Korea Alexander Matsegora in an interview with the Russian newspaper Rossiyskaya Gazeta on February 9. According to him, they receive care for free in the best North Korean hospitals and sanatoriums. Families put in danger Volodymyr Zelensky's eagerness to provide "incontestable proof" of the presence of North Korean troops alongside the Russians has led Kiev to violate the Geneva Convention on the treatment of prisoners of war during armed conflicts, argued Human Rights Watch. By revealing the identities of the two North Korean soldiers taken prisoner - they had been ordered by their superiors to commit suicide rather than fall into enemy hands - Kiev has put their families in danger, who risk ending up in labor camps for the "treason" of their relatives. One of the prisoners, aged 19, said he was ready to defect. Kiev defends the disclosure of the identities of the two prisoners by emphasizing that it was exceptional and necessary to counter Russian disinformation. Nevertheless, it continues to release new excerpts from their interrogations. Faced with the increasing need for soldiers, Russia has relaxed the medical criteria for military service. People with schizophrenia, epilepsy, diabetes, severe obesity, or syphilis will now be considered fit for service, with exceptions. This amendment, which is expected to be quickly adopted by decree, is retroactive. All those who were exempted for these medical reasons after September 21, 2022, the date of the first wave of mobilization, could then be called back to service.

title: "Crises are Emmanuel Macron's fuel, Europe is his asset."
lead: Because since 2017 he has been developing a coherent and prophetic vision of the role and place of the Old Continent in the world, the French president can attempt a comeback to the forefront after being marginalized by his calamitous dissolution of the National Assembly, observes Françoise Fressoz, a columnist at "Le Monde," in her column.
text_eng: No one can say what Emmanuel Macron, on a visit to Washington on Monday, February 24, will ultimately manage to extract from Donald Trump, but no one can blame him for trying something. As the European Union is endangered in its identity, its future, its security, the President of the Republic has found a role that suits him. He is multiplying his efforts on the national, European, and transatlantic stage to try to loosen the American-Russian stranglehold on Ukraine, make Europe's voice heard, and avoid the vassalization of the Old Continent. Two impromptu European meetings organized at the Elysée Palace the week of February 17, followed by a meeting with French party leaders and parliamentary groups before his flight to the United States, have highlighted the return to the forefront of a president who was described as marginalized, if not finished, in the aftermath of the dissolution of the National Assembly in June. With a keen sense of the moment and the absence of strong competitors on the European stage – Germany was in the midst of an election at the time – the French president pushed his advantage as much as he could. In contrast, French political parties, so quick to bury him, were left in a bad position. Engaged in mediocre political games, they gave the impression of not grasping the significance of what had happened in Munich on February 14, when U.S. Vice President J.D. Vance had harshly attacked Western democracies, snubbed outgoing German Chancellor Olaf Scholz, and expressed strong support for the far-right Alternative for Germany party. They behaved as if they had not heard U.S. President Donald Trump treat his Ukrainian counterpart, Volodymyr Zelensky, as an enemy. The French, on the other hand, understood the seriousness of the situation very well, according to the results of an Ipsos poll for La Tribune Dimanche published on Sunday, February 23. They are very concerned about a possible disengagement of the American president from NATO or the Ukrainian issue, and refuse to see Europe give up and advocate for continued support for Kiev, even if the United States were to withdraw. On all these points, they are in line with what the French president is advocating and attempting, despite being weighed down for months by a record level of unpopularity: barely over 20% approval ratings. Weaknesses come at a high price. Crises are Emmanuel Macron's fuel, and Europe is his asset because, in this field, the French president can boast a vision, coherence, and consistency. The tragedy threatening the European Union validates the idea that he has been developing and deepening since 2017: Europe is the condition for French sovereignty, but it risks marginalization if it does not conceive and act as a power. European sovereignty, strategic autonomy, rearmament will remain the keywords of his double mandate marked by the war in Ukraine, the "existential threat" posed by Russian imperialism to Europe, and now, the possible abandonment by the United States. At each moment of tension, Emmanuel Macron drives the point home, sounds the alarm, uses expressions intended to make an impact. On June 13, 2022, four months after the invasion of Ukraine, he declared, "We have entered a war economy." On Thursday, February 20, speaking on social media, he once again speaks of a "war effort," even though in eight years the military budget has already been increased by 30%. There is now talk of raising it to 3.5%, or even 5%, of national wealth, which represents an additional commitment of 40 to 80 billion euros, in a heavily indebted France that is struggling to regain control of its public spending. This is where the limits of Macron's determination lie. The President has been paving the way for over eight years without managing to rally a national representation that appears more self-centered and fragmented than ever. The supply-side policy he defends in line with his European choice is being challenged, the pension reform is still not accepted, and his relationship with political parties remains deeply distrustful. Elected in 2017 on the ruins of the Socialist Party and the right, Emmanuel Macron has never overcome the accusation of wanting to be their gravedigger. As the initiator of a new political movement, En Marche!, which has become Renaissance, he has never cared to make it thrive. All these weaknesses came at a high cost during the dissolution in June, which resulted in a lack of majority in the National Assembly, a worsening of the democratic crisis, and the risk of halting government action: neither Michel Barnier nor François Bayrou has so far managed to address the French ills they have relentlessly inventoried. The resumption of contacts with party leaders and the announcement of a parliamentary debate without a vote at the beginning of March on the international situation constitute yet another attempt to realign national objectives with the dangers of the global stage. The likelihood of a shock appears low as the executive has lost its appeal, but the debate will at least force each party to face its responsibilities. Quick to present itself as the protector of the weakest, the National Rally is struggling this time. It is finding it very difficult to explain how its fascination with Donald Trump, its ambiguity towards Vladimir Putin, and its fight against the European defense contribute to protecting national interests and strengthening the camp of democrats.

title: The bosses are brown-nosers: "We couldn't have done this without you, President Trump."
lead: The billionaires of Silicon Valley have rallied behind the new American president as one, showering him with an abundance of high-flown compliments.
text_eng: From Bill Gates, who confides to the Wall Street Journal that he was "frankly impressed" by the interest Donald Trump showed in his concerns during a three-hour dinner, to Jeff Bezos, "very optimistic" about the prospect of this second term, even saying "I won't agree with him on everything, but I think he will be amazing for the country in many ways!" as recently tweeted by Sam Altman, the founder of OpenAI, the past few weeks have had one advantage: to remind us that Silicon Valley has disrupted everything except good old brown-nosing. Watching on television on January 20th, the lineup of billionaires who funded the new president's inauguration ceremony with $1 million each, all those who chipped in on Leetchi to buy a gift for the boss's newborn in their SME probably felt relieved to have checked the "donation amount visible" box. Brown-nosing has had a bad reputation for too long. All things considered, bosses who claim to appreciate disagreement generally prefer compliments. Learning that Elon Musk rented a cottage for $2,000 a night at Mar-a-Lago (Florida) for two months to be as close as possible to Donald Trump's residence after his election, and then seeing all those who made their fortunes in the virtual world rushing to have dinner with the new president (and making sure everyone knows about it), reminded telecommuters worldwide that nothing beats running into the boss in a hallway. And, as in any small business, it's not profits but compliments that flow. When Meta's CEO, Mark Zuckerberg, posts a photo on his Instagram platform of himself and his wife in their finest attire for Trump's inauguration party, captioned with "feeling optimistic," Laurent Solly, Meta's Vice President of Southern Europe, quickly likes the photo. Certainly, social media has contributed to normalizing insults, but it has also made sycophancy just a click away from likes and retweets; LinkedIn alone being a massive flattery machine where everyone spends their time congratulating each other on their new responsibilities. While the entire Silicon Valley seems busy wishing the new president a happy work anniversary, no one is calling out the hypocrites anymore, in a world where no one is safe from becoming one. How to recognize them: In small companies, they laugh at the boss's jokes. In large ones, they retweet his statements with a little comment ("true!" "100%"). In small companies, they went to Cap-Ferret in the summer, hoping to run into the boss on the beach. In large ones, they take their jet to Mar-a-Lago. In both small and very large companies, they adopt the boss's hobbies: golf, Village People choreography, media bashing, or wokism hunting. How they speak: "We could not have done this without you, Mr. President" (Sam Altman, upon the announcement of the Stargate plan for massive investment in artificial intelligence). "The guy is our Einstein" (Jamie Dimon, CEO of JPMorgan Chase bank, referring to Elon Musk). "Thanks to President Trump's efforts, TikTok is back in the United States!" (TikTok's home screen). "If he had been president, if victory had not been stolen from him in 2020, maybe the Ukrainian crisis of 2022 would never have occurred" (Vladimir Putin). "Monseigneur is the greatest of all the grandees of Spain" (Blaze-Yves Montand, The Mad Adventures of Rabbi Jacob, 1971). Their great truths: It's pragmatism. Donald Trump likes to make people believe that things were accomplished thanks to him. Flattery has always existed in business. The difference between a compliment and flattery is not easy to discern on social media. It's not sycophancy, it's survival. Swallowing your pride doesn't cost much, considering what it can bring in return. It doesn't matter what your real power is, as long as people attribute power to you. Their existential question: Between the flatterer and the flattered, who is using whom? Their Holy Grail: Instead of discreetly denying oneself, highlighting the evolution of one's opinion, like Sam Altman ("I've been observing the president more closely lately and it has really changed my perspective on him"). The faux pas: Those people who bring up your past statements. Those campaign videos in which Donald Trump was already saying in October how the big bosses who didn't want to take his calls before 2016, started kissing his hand after his first election.

title: "America, which practiced the rule of law and had invented the international liberal order, is over."
lead: Donald Trump is expanding the powers of the presidency beyond what any of his predecessors had ever dared, notes Alain Frachon, an editorialist at "Le Monde", in his column.
text_eng: Every day a little more, an America is disappearing, the one we had become accustomed to in Europe. Our America practiced the rule of law at home and, abroad, it had invented the "international liberal order" in 1945. It was unfaithful to it, it did not respect its own rules, but still, it had worked rather well for it and for us too. It's over. Donald Trump no longer wants it. He is demolishing, breaking, saying that the "system" no longer benefits Americans, neither at home nor abroad. Another America is emerging - tough on the weak. At home, Trump is twisting the norms and customs of American democracy. He violates the mechanics desired by the Founding Fathers, that of a presidency tempered by the checks and balances of Congress and the judiciary - together forming in the United States what is called "government." The Republican is leading the country down the path of "illiberal democracy." We know the recipe: the winner of the elections becomes the owner of the state. The judiciary, the police, the civil service, the military, the university, everything belongs to him and everything must submit. Between the leader and "his" people, there is no room for institutional mediations. Trump is expanding the powers of the presidency beyond what any of his predecessors had ever dared. He is working to dismantle the federal administration. Through intimidation and lies, the task is carried out by the richest man in the world, Elon Musk. He operates outside of any mandate, within a structure outside the administration, formed by Trump and accountable to no one else. Musk's small "hackers" have obtained the payment codes of the U.S. Treasury and have access to the personal files of millions of federal employees. To what end? Conquering presidency In this type of regime, one must rewrite or bury the past. In the Department of Justice and the FBI (the federal police), prosecutors and inspectors who investigated Trump's responsibilities in the assault on Congress on January 6, 2021 are subjected to a loyalty test! For what purpose? In the midst of the Cold War in the 1950s, Senator Joseph McCarthy hunted down suspects of communist sympathies in high administration. Today, witnesses of the political-judicial coup attempted by Trump after an uncontested election, but whose result he refused, are being intimidated. This undermining work is intended to obtain submission to the whims of the great MAGA chief [for the Trumpian slogan "Make America great again"] from all agents and experts in the administration. It is only possible with the acquiescence of both Houses of Congress, where Republicans hold a slim majority - until the midterm elections in November 2026. Abandoning all independence, the elected officials of the MAGA party have relinquished their prerogatives: the oversight of the administration's actions. To contain Donald Trump's aggressive presidentialism, only the judges remain. Four or five federal judges, mostly women, have been found to overturn, in the name of their illegality, some of the edicts from the White House. Ultimately, the ongoing political and judicial battle, which will determine the nature of American presidentialism – liberal or "illiberal" – will be decided by the Supreme Court. It is currently dominated by conservatives. Disdain towards the European Union On Friday, February 14, American Vice President J.D. Vance came to Munich, Germany, to inform Europeans that there was also a change in foreign policy. This primarily concerns the assessment of the threat to the United States' allies within NATO: there is nothing to fear from Russia or China, he said, which will reassure the Baltic states and a few others. No, what is eroding the Old Continent, as well as the United Kingdom, are "attacks on democracy" by its leaders – meaning: they want to combat misinformation on Musk's X network. A week before the elections in Germany, J.D. Vance granted an interview not to Chancellor Olaf Scholz, but to the leader of the far-right AfD party, Alice Weidel, who is pro-Russian and anti-European Union (EU). Another way, one more, to reach out to Moscow. Arrogant and spiteful, J.D. Vance's outburst expressed the contempt that a good part of the Trump team holds towards the EU – incapable of existing as a military-strategic power; unable to pay its dues to NATO; a somewhat decadent, moralizing entity wanting to regulate everything; a collective with declining demographic, economic, and technological weight. Behind this portrait – not necessarily unfounded – lies a questioning of the transatlantic pact as an alliance based on a community of values. The lesser attachment to liberal democracy observed on the domestic stage finds its echo in foreign policy. The hyper-president Trump feels closer to Vladimir Putin than to Olaf Scholz or Volodymyr Zelensky. The transatlantic alliance was one of the pillars of the "liberal international order" established by the United States in 1945. Through a set of institutions – the UN and its subsidiaries – and principles, it was the hope for a minimum of norms in state relations. Everyone has contributed to undermining the system. And today, far from reviving it, whether in terms of trade norms or respect for existing borders, Trump, Putin, and Xi Jinping intend to establish their power and territorial conquests on one principle: power dynamics. With a corollary: woe to the weak.

title: Ukraine: Chernobyl nuclear power plant hit by a Russian drone
lead: For the first time, a Russian projectile directly hits a structure housing a nuclear reactor. An incident that occurs in the context of an increase in attacks against Ukrainian nuclear facilities.
text_eng: A Russian Shahed drone armed with a highly explosive warhead struck the Chernobyl nuclear power plant on the night of Thursday, February 13 to Friday, February 14, at 1:59 a.m. local time, piercing the new containment arch protecting reactor number 4, which in 1986 was the site of the worst civilian nuclear disaster. The impact triggered a fire and compromised the integrity of the arch built in 2016 to contain the radioactivity. At the Chernobyl nuclear power plant, it is believed that the worst has been avoided. "It's a good thing we didn't start dismantling the sarcophagus [built in 1986], otherwise the consequences of the radiation would have been very different," said an employee of the site who requested anonymity to Le Monde. Known as the "New Safe Confinement" (NSC), the containment arch hit by the drone "was erected to begin dismantling the sarcophagus, many of whose structures are not repaired. There is corrosion and other risks," the same source explains. "These works are planned. As soon as the roof of the sarcophagus is removed, radiation levels under the containment arch will immediately increase. The works are now postponed indefinitely," she adds.
