The drone hit the protective shell of the NSC arch at an altitude of 87 meters, according to a statement from the nuclear power plant. "The impact occurred above the premises housing the technical equipment ensuring the remote operation of the containment. As a result, the outer and inner cladding of the NSC arch was damaged, as well as the equipment of the main crane system." A fire was detected on the outer and inner cladding of the NSC arch, covering an area of about 40 square meters. It was brought under control from Thursday to Friday night, but at 8:40 a.m., "light smoke emissions" were still coming from the NSC arch. "The incident caused no casualties. No leakage of radioactive substances beyond established limits has been recorded," adds the nuclear power plant's website. Sixty-five people and seventeen units from the Ministry of Emergency Situations, as well as fifteen members of the Chernobyl nuclear power plant staff, the Ukrainian National Guard, the National Police of Ukraine, and the Security Service of Ukraine (SBU) were dispatched to the site to deal with the consequences of the attack. "Everyone is very angry." A photograph published by the Ministry of Emergency Situations on X shows a partially burnt engine of the drone, confirming that it is a Shahed manufactured by Russia under an Iranian license. The Russian military fires hundreds of these munitions on Ukrainian territory every month, mostly during the night. The Ukrainian Air Force indicated that Russia had launched thirteen drones at targets across Ukraine during the night of February 13 to 14. Reacting to the attack on his YouTube channel, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky accused the Russians of "intentionally" hitting Chernobyl "on the first day of the Munich Security Conference [bringing together world leaders]. I think this is a very clear greeting from Moscow." The head of the Ukrainian presidential administration, Andriy Yermak, echoed on his Telegram channel, stating that "everyone is very angry about this here in Munich. Not 'worried,' as is often the case, but really angry. Because in the 1980s, the whole world helped the Kremlin to eliminate the tragedy [of Chernobyl]." Ukrainian Energy Minister German Galushchenko compared the drone attack to the "heavy weapon" assault carried out by Russian forces against another nuclear power plant, Zaporijia, on the night of March 3-4, 2022. Without formally denying this attack, Kremlin spokesperson Dmitri Peskov assured that the Russian military never targets nuclear facilities. "There can be no question of striking against this or that nuclear infrastructure (...) Russian military personnel do not do that," he stated. Attacks on Ukrainian nuclear facilities began on the first day of the large-scale Russian invasion on February 24, 2022, when the Chernobyl nuclear power plant was attacked and then occupied by Russian armed forces. In September 2022, a Russian missile exploded 300 meters from a reactor at the South Ukraine nuclear power plant. In January, the International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA) expressed alarm in a statement about "increased military activity near all Ukrainian nuclear power plants," without, however, attributing this activity to Russian bombings. "These ongoing threats continue to endanger nuclear safety and the security of nuclear power plants," noted IAEA Director General Rafael Mariano Grossi.

title: War in Ukraine: The "pause" in American intelligence towards Kiev, an unprecedented challenge for cooperation between allies
lead: This suspension is a major setback for Ukraine, which has so far relied on this data to offset the power imbalance with Moscow. However, while the Europeans are ready to take over, they cannot provide the same level of support.
text_eng: The "pause" in American intelligence support to Ukraine is now in effect. CIA Director John Ratcliffe confirmed this new development from Washington on Wednesday, March 5, signaling both maximum pressure from the Trump administration on Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky and an unprecedented shift in cooperation that had existed between allies in this highly sensitive field of intelligence. On Thursday, French Minister of Defense Sébastien Lecornu became the first European official to confirm this pause and provide details. "The assistance of American agencies in satellite observation, in everything that can be done (...), has been suspended since yesterday afternoon," he said on France Inter. "The total ban on intelligence transfers is in effect, both directly from the United States to [Ukraine], but it also prohibits NATO allies from transferring data received from the United States to us," Ukrainian military expert Ievhen Dyky told Le Monde. The ban "applies to all forms of intelligence," he added. Although the Pentagon has not disclosed any details about the nature of this suspension since Wednesday, several segments of Ukrainian operations and security appear particularly vulnerable. Starting with targeting assistance. The most complex systems, such as drones and long-range artillery, were heavily reliant on data provided by American satellites to plan their strikes. Starlink is still functioning, but for how long? "We are completely dependent on targeting coordinates provided by the Americans for the Himars [surface-to-surface missile systems]," laments military analyst Serhi Hrabsky. "We have other sources of intelligence, but the advantage of American data is their real-time precision, which is vital in modern warfare, where targets are highly mobile. It will take us time to find solutions and overcome this American freeze." "The order given was likely to stop providing intelligence for targeting purposes against the Russians," explains Jonathan Guiffard, a research associate at the Montaigne Institute and the French Institute of Geopolitics, engaged in a thesis on the technical intelligence agencies of the United States. "American agencies can receive orders not to transmit information, either in real-time or because it may be considered too precise to be used by the Ukrainians. That's the line," he details. In both cases, this can prevent Kiev from achieving a Russian objective. This type of American support has always been highly regulated in their operations abroad. "Everything always goes through the filter of an agent responsible for authorizing or not these information disclosures, called a 'foreign disclosure officer' (FDO). And many allies have experienced that, if there is no refusal, there can be dissemination delays incompatible with short decision-making cycles, such as targeting opportunities. The lifting of these constraints is largely a political issue," details Philippe Gros, a specialist in military affairs at the Foundation for Strategic Research (FRS). More uncertainty remains about the contribution of American sensors to the Ukrainian anti-missile alert system. So far, the daily attacks by drones, cruise missiles, or ballistic missiles were detected by a dense network of satellites with their 'eyes' focused on Russia. They allowed the population to be warned up to several tens of minutes in advance. "Since the announcement of this pause, there have been no missile launches," Mr. Dyky resumes. "We will wait and then know how critical the situation is." How long can the Ukrainians hold the front under these conditions, especially since American intelligence has been one of the key elements, since 2022, to compensate for the forces imbalance against Moscow? For now, the Starlink satellite communication system, essential for some communications on the ground - and manufactured by Elon Musk's SpaceX, an advisor to Donald Trump - is still operational. But for how long? A key sector: space Although the Americans maintain that it is a 'pause' - and not a halt - in intelligence support, this pressure from Washington places Kiev's allies facing the challenge of mitigating the risks. Several European countries have been providing various forms of intelligence support since 2022. "Yes, we have means [in this area], which we provide to the Ukrainians," acknowledged Mr. Lecornu on Thursday. "But at this stage, the Europeans absolutely cannot offer the same level as the Americans," considers Mr. Guiffard. Other solutions than relying on American intelligence have been anticipated in some areas, but, at this stage, the European allies of Kiev appear to be hindered in at least one key sector: space. Taking pictures of Russian bases and the front with a high degree of precision is within reach of the Europeans, especially the French, who have powerful optical reconnaissance satellites - the latest model, CSO-3, was launched on Thursday. But the United States can provide images in much greater numbers and across the entire front. For telecommunications, the Europeans signed, at the end of 2024, the launch of a constellation called 'Iris²', intended to compete with Starlink. But it will not be operational until 2030." On March 3, the option of replacing Starlink with OneWeb, the Eutelsat constellation (owned 25% by France and the United Kingdom), resurfaced after a company spokesperson revealed to Reuters that "hundreds of terminals" were being operated in Ukraine. In 2024, Kyivstar, Ukraine's largest telecommunications operator, had tested OneWeb technologies with the military. In light of the American suspension, questions arise about how British intelligence, heavily involved in supporting Ukraine but closely intertwined with the United States through the "Five Eyes" (which also includes Canada, Australia, and New Zealand), will continue its engagement. "This alliance operates on the sharing of interception capabilities, data collection, and storage facilities, including those of the American National Security Agency and the British Government Communications Headquarters. The United States may request to block these sharing infrastructures on a matter of principle, but implementation will take time," Mr. Guiffard explained. Europeans have cybersecurity expertise to offer to Kyiv Cyber cooperation, where there is the most uncertainty. While several media outlets, including The Washington Post, reported in early March that U.S. Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth had requested a pause in certain operations against Russia, an anonymous official cited by Bloomberg denied these allegations on March 3. "Cyber operations require extensive planning and are not easily paused. Despite the asymmetry in the relationship, the Americans need to maintain access to Ukrainian territory to approach the Russians," said Stéphane Taillat, a researcher at the Saint-Cyr Coëtquidan Military Academy. Regardless of what happens, Europeans also have advanced cybersecurity expertise to offer Ukraine if Washington decides to redirect some of its resources against China or South American cartels. However, over the past three years, their service offerings have largely been overlooked by Ukrainians due to American dominance over their networks, partly in the hands of private actors like Microsoft or Amazon Web Services, which host most of Kiev's state data outside its territory since the start of the war.

title: Security guarantees in Ukraine: Can Europe confront Putin without the help of the United States?
lead: Ukraine insists that any ceasefire agreement must be accompanied by "security guarantees" to ensure lasting peace. Faced with uncertainty about American support, Europe must strengthen its defense capabilities.
text_eng: "You talk to me about security, but I first have to make a deal!" This statement by the American president, made on February 28 during his meeting with Volodymyr Zelensky in the Oval Office, sums up the entire challenge of the ceasefire negotiations between Ukraine and Russia. Facing him, the Ukrainian president tries in vain to remind Donald Trump of the failures of negotiations conducted by his predecessors with the Russian president. Trump retorts: "He may have already broken his commitments with Bush, with Obama, and with Biden. But he has never broken them with me." Since his return to the White House, Donald Trump has been pressuring Ukraine to force its leaders to accept a ceasefire with Russia. However, he currently refuses to commit to a "security guarantees" mechanism that would be put in place to deter Putin from rearming and potentially attacking Ukraine, or even Europe, in the future. In this video, Sylvie Kauffmann, an editorialist at Le Monde and a specialist in international relations, and Pierre Haroche, a lecturer in international politics at the Catholic University of Lille, explain the stakes of this exchange and its impact on European defense policy.

title: Olivier Wieviorka, historian: "The current context presents some similarities with the situation that prevailed before the Second World War."
lead: History provides valuable insights to understand the current geopolitical context, according to Olivier Wieviorka in an article in "Le Monde". How might the situation evolve? It will all depend on Vladimir Putin's willingness to escalate the conflict and on Europeans' ability to demonstrate their unity.
text_eng: The sudden aggression of Russia against Ukraine has reignited fears of a world war. While this scenario seemed fanciful, it has gained some credibility since Donald Trump returned to power. A paradoxical fear, as the American president proclaims his love for peace. Europe would therefore have nothing to fear as the cannons fall silent. With two caveats: the ceasefire would offer Moscow a respite to rebuild its forces; abandoning American protection, by leaving Europe exposed, could push Vladimir Putin to raise the stakes, posing a dilemma for the countries of the Old Continent: to submit or to retaliate. In this respect, history provides valuable insights. While comparison is not reason, the current context bears some similarities to the situation before World War II. If Putin is not Hitler, he has favored aggression since 2008, on two fronts. In Georgia and Ukraine, his "artichoke" strategy aims to gradually rebuild the protective buffer of the Warsaw Pact; in Africa, he intervenes to weaken Western powers and deprive them of certain resources. Does he wish to set the planet ablaze? In 1914, the automaticity of alliances led to conflagration; in 1939, the opposite occurred. The alliance system was so loose (in both senses of the term?) that it had no deterrent effect. The Führer could therefore methodically make his moves, from the remilitarization of the Rhineland (1936) to the invasion of Czechoslovakia (March 15, 1939). He then took the risk of attacking Poland, counting on the abstention of his allies. "Our enemies are little worms. I saw that in Munich," he told his military leaders. The declarations of war from London and Paris were therefore a surprise, as the Reich was counting on a limited conflict. However, the conclusion of the German-Soviet pact offered a reassuring perspective by ruling out the specter of a two-front war: Moscow would not intervene. Double risk The current situation bears many similarities to this configuration, with the United States playing the role of the USSR. But will Putin want to go further? Striking another country represents a double risk. Economically, there is no indication that Russia has the resources to attempt such an adventure. However, in 1939, the Reich could not contemplate a prolonged war due to lack of oil, rubber, and also a sufficiently robust industry. These shortcomings did not prevent it from taking the risk of a general conflict. In other words, there is no suggestion that the state of Russia's economy deters Putin from taking action, especially as it is now on a war footing. Moreover, attacking a NATO member would expose Moscow to the wrath of the Atlantic Alliance. The world – and this is what fuels fears – would find itself in a situation comparable to that of Europe in 1914: the automaticity of alliances would trigger a generalized conflict. However, there is no guarantee that this process would be set in motion. Because Article 5 of the treaty, while obliging signatories to react, does not assume the use of cannons: the parties must only take actions deemed necessary, "including the use of armed force." In short, resorting to weapons is only an option left to the free discretion of the States. In essence, the planet is not at risk of suddenly being plunged into war by a domino effect, especially since Western countries are divided, weakening the deterrent virtue of the Alliance, and the parties have so far avoided crossing the "red lines," such as the use of tactical nuclear weapons by Russia, and the use of certain weapons by Western countries. Strategic nuclear weapons also help avoid the "escalation to extremes" dear to Carl von Clausewitz (1780-1831). Fatal error of assessment The war became global in 1941, not due to a fatal chain of events, but because the Axis powers had decided so. This factor explains why the causes of the Second World War, unlike the First, have never sparked controversies, as the responsibilities of Japan and Nazi Germany are clear. Indeed, the Reich abruptly launched its panzers into the attack on the USSR in June, and Japan struck Pearl Harbor just as suddenly in December. In both cases, the German and Japanese leaders made a fatal error of assessment. Hitler believed that the Stalinist regime would collapse at the first blow. "We'll just have to kick in the door, and this rotten edifice will collapse with a crash," he declared. The Japanese leaders, on the other hand, aimed to neutralize the US Navy fleet anchored in the Hawaiian archipelago, "a dagger pointed at the heart of Japan," according to Admiral Isoroku Yamamoto (1884-1943), and above all, to disengage the presumed isolationist Americans from their president, Franklin Delano Roosevelt (1882-1945). Soviet resistance and national unity around the American president shattered these illusions. Continuing the parallel: by pushing his pieces too far, Putin would risk provoking a reaction so violent that it would turn his gains into losses, as Japan and Germany had to acknowledge, and as the failure before Kiev in 2022 confirms. Thus, the possibility of a world war does not depend on the hypothetical risk of a mechanical process suddenly escaping the control of States. It depends, on the one hand, on the will of Vladimir Putin who, emboldened by Donald Trump, might be tempted to expand the conflagration, first by testing NATO's determination, by aggressing a Baltic State, for example. She is equally committed to the ability of Europeans to show their determination and unity, in order to create a convincing balance of power to deter Moscow from continuing its aggressions. In 1939, the credibility of France and Great Britain had been undermined by their military weakness, the policy of appeasement blindly pursued by London, and the unnatural alliance concluded in 1939 between Berlin and Moscow. This scenario has many similarities with the current situation. However, there is no suggestion of its repetition.

title: In Armenia, the Parliament adopts in the first reading a request for membership in the European Union.
lead: The text must now be studied in a second reading. But it is an illustration of the turning point in the country's history, as it moves further away from its traditional ally Russia.
text_eng: The Armenian Parliament adopted, on Wednesday, February 12, in a first reading, a bill aiming to open negotiations to join the European Union (EU), an illustration of Armenia's desire, in cold with its traditional ally Russia, to get closer to Western countries. "Expressing the will of the people of the Republic of Armenia, who have set the goal of making Armenia a safe and developed country, Armenia announces the beginning of a process of accession to the EU," states the text passed with 63 votes for and 7 votes against. The text must now be studied in a second reading. It had been submitted to the lawmakers on January 9 by the government of this former Soviet republic in the Caucasus. Prime Minister Nikol Pashinyan had then hailed a turning point in the country's history, but also warned that the process of EU accession would be complex, requiring constant political will and the support of the population. In 2024, a petition demanding the start of an accession process, supported by pro-European parties and organizations, had gathered around 60,000 signatures in this country of 2.7 million inhabitants. Distrust towards Russia For nearly a year and a half, Armenia has been showing signs of distrust towards Russia, its historical ally that has long sold it weapons and still has a military base on Armenian territory. Armenia reproaches Russia for its lack of support in the face of Azerbaijan, which forcibly retook, in the fall of 2023, the predominantly Armenian region of Karabakh, controlled for three decades by separatists. Russian peacekeeping troops deployed in Karabakh did not intervene during this Azerbaijani offensive to enforce a ceasefire agreed at the end of 2020 between Baku and Yerevan after a six-week war. The recapture of all of Karabakh by Azerbaijan forced over 100,000 Armenians to flee the territory, out of fear of reprisals. Since then, Yerevan has been distancing itself from Moscow. In January 2024, Armenia officially joined the International Criminal Court (ICC), despite Russian warnings, and is now obligated to arrest Vladimir Putin if he sets foot on Armenian territory, under an ICC arrest warrant issued against the Russian president in March 2023. In July 2024, Armenia also hosted joint military exercises with the United States. A permanent Russian military base still exists on Armenian territory, in Gyumri, and Yerevan remains a member of the Collective Security Treaty Organization, a military alliance led by Russia. In February 2024, Armenian Prime Minister Nikol Pashinyan nevertheless stated that Armenia had "practically" frozen its participation in this alliance.

title: Europeans stunned by the start of major Russo-American maneuvers
lead: Caught off guard, European foreign ministers wish to exchange with their "American allies."
text_eng: Europeans had been preparing for it for weeks. The moment finally arrived and left them stunned. On Wednesday, February 12, U.S. President Donald Trump and Russian President Vladimir Putin announced, following a ninety-minute phone conversation, their shared intention to "immediately" launch peace negotiations between Ukraine and Russia, without guaranteeing the participation of Ukrainians and Europeans in the talks. The subsequent announcement of a brief phone call between Donald Trump and his Ukrainian counterpart, Volodymyr Zelensky, did not calm tensions, and reactions were strong on the European side. "There will be no just and lasting peace in Ukraine without European participation," reminded French Minister of Foreign Affairs, Jean-Noël Barrot. "We are talking about a sovereign country with a democratically elected government. Moreover, nothing concerning European security – and Russian aggression against Ukraine directly threatens European security – can be decided without Europe," added his Spanish counterpart, José Manuel Albares. According to Annalena Baerbock, the German Foreign Minister, there can be "no decision on Ukraine without Ukraine." At the time the announcement of the conversation between Presidents Trump and Putin was made public, the foreign ministers of France, Germany, the UK, Italy, Spain, and Poland, along with the EU foreign policy chief, Kaja Kallas, were attending a new coordination meeting in Paris, focusing on the security guarantees that the Old Continent could provide to Kiev in the event of a ceasefire. "We will need to accelerate these discussions if we want to be ready in time to have an impact," confided a diplomatic source shortly before. As Europeans have realized in recent days that contacts have been intensifying between the new Trump administration and the Kremlin, without much transparency towards them, they also needed to prepare for the Munich Security Conference, during which European representatives are set to meet with U.S. officials on Friday, February 14, and Saturday, February 15, starting with Vice President J.D. Vance and Secretary of State Marco Rubio. Caught off guard, European foreign ministers, in a joint statement, expressed their desire to "discuss the way forward with our American allies." "Our common goals should be to place Ukraine in a position of strength. (...) A just and lasting peace in Ukraine is a necessary condition for strong transatlantic security," states the brief text published Wednesday evening, without any concrete announcements, while demanding "a peace that guarantees the interests of Ukraine and our own." A few hours earlier, transatlantic solidarity had already been put to the test by a speech of rare brutality delivered by Pete Hegseth, the new US Secretary of Defense, at the headquarters of the North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO) in Brussels. The Pentagon chief detailed, before his stunned allies, both the American red lines on the conflict in Ukraine and the American vision for the future of European defense: a collective defense under the operational and financial responsibility of Europeans, as the United States now has other priorities at its own borders and in the Pacific. "The United States will no longer tolerate an unbalanced relationship that encourages dependence. Instead, our relationship will prioritize empowering Europe to take responsibility for its own security," emphasized Pete Hegseth. Donald Trump's Secretary of Defense demanded that allies, like his mentor, now spend 5% of their GDP on defense. In front of his Ukrainian counterpart, he asserted his convictions regarding the conflict, as Russian troops continue their slow advance on the ground. According to Mr. Hegseth, returning to pre-2014 borders would be "an unrealistic goal," as would allowing Ukraine to join NATO, even though allies had assured, back at the Washington summit in July 2024, in the presence of Joe Biden, that the country was on an "irreversible path" towards the Atlantic Alliance, without extending the requested invitation to Kiev. Furthermore, to ensure lasting peace, "any security guarantee must be supported by competent European and non-European troops" in a non-NATO mission, not covered by the organization's Article 5 on mutual security and assistance, Mr. Hegseth affirmed. "In the context of any security guarantee, the United States will not be involved," he specified. "A moment of truth for Europeans." While the ministers present in the room were shocked by the tone of the speech, they cannot be entirely surprised by the substance. Some of these parameters, such as opposition to NATO membership, are in line with the positions taken by the Biden administration to avoid escalation with Russia. The new team had been sending messages for some months, but the way they were publicly hammered by the new US Secretary of Defense was perceived as a slap in the face. "No NATO membership, no troops on the ground? It looks like an abandonment of Ukraine," laments Gabrielius Landsbergis, the former Lithuanian Foreign Minister and a staunch supporter of Kiev. Moreover, observers are questioning Donald Trump's strategy to achieve a ceasefire and then peace in Ukraine. "With Mr. Hegseth, the United States has just deprived themselves of two important cards in the negotiation that is opening with Moscow: the question of territory and NATO security, notes Camille Grand, an expert with the European Council on Foreign Relations (ECFR). Starting a negotiation in this way, by saying that you want peace through force, is quite counterintuitive. It looks more like peace through weakness." "I have negotiated with the Russians. We never give them anything for free," confirms Michael McFaul, a former US ambassador to Russia, on the social network X. "This is now a moment of truth for the Europeans," says Camille Grand. "Are they ready to send 40,000 troops to guarantee Ukraine's security in the event of a ceasefire?" The subject will animate the upcoming meetings between European leaders, who have not yet been able to agree. To date, only a coalition of willing states, including France and the United Kingdom, is ready to deploy troops, under terms that are yet to be decided. "We will be ready if we have to be," says a NATO diplomat from a northern European country, but the uncertainty about the lack of American military support for a European ground interposition force raises questions for many states, starting with Poland. "Europeans lack a number of intelligence, troop transport, and equipment capabilities, etc. This will be a major challenge," says Mr. Grand. To impose its choices on Europeans and Ukrainians, the Trump administration wants to move quickly on all issues. However, for now, Americans are "vague" and even "evasive," as noted in European capitals. Faced with this activism, the European position is not to succumb to American pressure and to prepare, as best as possible, on the military front. Based on the Alliance's defense plans, NATO Secretary-General Mark Rutte believes that in the coming years, at least 3% of collective wealth will need to be invested, compared to just under 2% on average today. Because the Russian threat is not likely to disappear, even in the event of a still uncertain "deal" between Vladimir Putin and Donald Trump.

title: Video | Sylvie Kauffmann, editorialist at "Le Monde", answers your questions about the geopolitical situation.
lead: Video The shift in relations between global powers raises many questions. Sylvie Kauffmann, editorialist at "Le Monde" and specialist in international relations, answers the questions you have sent us on social media.
text_eng: Since the tense meeting between Donald Trump and Volodymyr Zelensky on Friday, February 28 at the White House and the American freeze on aid to Ukraine, the United States' desire to turn away from Europe is evident. This reshaping of the world order is forcing European countries to rethink their defense strategy, which is currently heavily reliant on the United States. This has made the French "legitimately worried," as emphasized by Emmanuel Macron in his televised address on March 6. This concern is reflected in the numerous questions that our readers have sent us through social media. "Is there a serious risk of a third world war?" "In the face of American withdrawal, would NATO still be effective and deterrent?" "Is Europe sufficiently militarily autonomous?" are questions that have been raised repeatedly in our comments. In this video, Sylvie Kauffmann, an editorialist at Le Monde and a specialist in international relations, directly addresses your questions about the geopolitical situation. Her analyses, enriched with diagrams and graphs, provide an insight into the future of relations between Europe and the United States.

title: Europe facing two wars
lead: Faced with the challenge of their divisions over the conflict in the Middle East, European leaders cannot afford to weaken their support for Ukraine.
text_eng: Managing a war on its borders is already a challenge for the European Union (EU), born out of the desire to consolidate peace. Facing two wars at the same time, with the second one in the Middle East carrying a powerful potential for division among its members, proves to be a challenge that the Twenty-Seven, gathered at a summit on Thursday, October 26 in Brussels, have fully realized. For the first time in almost two years, a war other than the one Russia is waging in Ukraine was at the top of the agenda. While Israeli tanks made an initial incursion into the Gaza Strip, possibly beginning the ground invasion announced after the terrorist attack carried out by Hamas in Israel on October 7, heads of state and government debated for five hours on formulating a common position on the conflict between Israel and Hamas. The outcome of these discussions is certainly not likely to disrupt the ongoing tragic dynamics: the Twenty-Seven could only agree on calling for the establishment of "humanitarian corridors" in Gaza and "pauses for humanitarian needs." The idea of calling for "a" pause for humanitarian reasons was deemed too close to a call for a ceasefire, immediately rejected by some member states, notably Germany, which emphasizes Israel's right to self-defense. This minimal formulation, accompanied by the wish for the rapid organization of an international peace conference, at least allowed the debates to be concluded with a common position after the deep divergences revealed during the hasty visit of the President of the Commission, Ursula von der Leyen, to Israel. Hamas in Moscow In addition to the risk of division over the Middle East, European leaders must also guard against the possibility of Ukraine being relegated to a secondary concern, as Russian forces have regained the initiative on the Eastern front, facing a visibly struggling Ukrainian army. The two conflicts are not entirely independent of each other: while the Twenty-Seven listened to President Volodymyr Zelensky addressing them from Kiev on Thursday in Brussels, a Hamas delegation was being received in Moscow for the third time since the start of the war in Ukraine. An Iranian Deputy Foreign Minister, Ali Bagheri Kani, was also in the Russian capital on the same day. A reorientation of Russian policy in the Middle East is evidently underway due to this new escalation of the Israeli-Palestinian conflict. President Vladimir Putin hopes to capitalize on the widespread rejection of Western positions in the Arab world and several countries in the South to overshadow his own invasion, one of an independent state that had not engaged in any provocation. On Thursday, the EU reaffirmed its support for Ukraine, without dramatizing the unglamorous game played by two Central European prime ministers, Hungarian Viktor Orban and Slovak Robert Fico. These two populist leaders have no qualms about wanting to benefit from all the advantages of EU membership, which they constantly criticize, while refusing to support Ukraine without, of course, risking a rupture with Brussels. Treating this cynicism with contempt is a good tactic. However, maintaining the course and the means for massive support to Ukraine must remain a key priority for the EU.

title: In war-torn Ukraine, the virtue of democratic debate
lead: After the sacred union of the first months, and while the soldiers are fighting, citizens are not afraid to protest to make their voices heard, as in any rule of law.
text_eng: In the midst of a conflict that promises to last and against the backdrop of a Middle East in turmoil, Ukraine quietly begins a chapter known only to democracies. After the time of unity in the first months, a public and critical debate on the decisions of the country's authorities is now open. Within public opinion, the military, and among elected officials, voices are questioning the official discourse that they seek to influence. However, underlying this, the question of the effects of such freedom is also raised. Is it a strength or a weakness as Ukraine tries to push the Russian army out of its borders? On September 27, in front of the city hall of Odessa, the major port city in southern Ukraine, dozens of mothers and wives of soldiers killed in combat are gathered, surrounded by several hundred people, including men. The city council is set to discuss tender offers for the renovation of public buildings, including the courthouse. "We are here," says Ekaterina Nozhevnikova, the organizer of the demonstration, "to denounce the mayor's intention to invest in projects unrelated to the ongoing war." A local figure, heading her Monsters Corporation foundation, she claims not to question the legitimacy of the current government, although she fears corruption. "We believe that it should focus on the war effort while it resumes its pre-war habits and manages the city as if our soldiers were not dying every day on the front lines." She adds: "We have already had some results, they have promised to allocate part of the public funds to the purchase of drones, but that is not enough; we will return here as many times as necessary, we will go to Kiev if needed." Norms of democratic societies In the crowd, Tina, 37 years old, whose husband is on the southern front, says: "I am here to share what I feel with other people, without fear of being arrested." Anna, 41 years old, carries on her shoulders the jacket of her husband killed by the Russians on September 25, 2022. "Odessa was known for its selfishness, this crowd is a first, it proves that we can make a difference." Other similar demonstrations have taken place in September in the country, including in Lviv, Kiev, Ternopil, Lutsk, Zhytomyr, Uzhhorod, and Bila Tserkva. These voices also echo the demands set by the European Union for Ukrainian governance and administration to align, even in times of war, with the norms of democratic societies, in preparation for possible accession. While soldiers fight, it is also about winning another battle, that of a rule of law and a new Ukraine. "And why not a presidential election?" a protester remarks. A perspective once supported by the American Congress for March 2024, the end of Volodymyr Zelensky's official term, but postponed since then due to lack of financial means and security. While Russian bombs fall on Ukraine every day, are such protests a sign of division within Ukrainian society or, on the contrary, do they reflect a virtue specific to open societies? That of democratic debate and its extraordinary ability to overcome obstacles. Anton, head of the mobile units of the anti-aircraft defense of Odessa and its region, has his opinion: "These tenders must be canceled, they show that governments, at the local and national levels, have not understood our needs. Debate brings up information and empowers people." "A beneficial responsiveness" Deputy (European Solidarity) and opposition member of the Odessa City Council, Oleksi Hontcharenko notes that "the gap is widening with the government and the debate on the unity of the country is open." For him, this situation is the result of questionable public decisions, as well as the slowness of the counter-offensive, hasty promises about the reconquest of Crimea, and a war fatigue that weighs on morale. "But," he adds, "this debate should not be taboo, we are talking about a need for inclusion and not defeatism. Only a small part of the population no longer wants war." Referring to the Greek historian Herodotus, the deputy asserts that "it is the strength of democracies to hold their governments accountable" and "to avoid autocratic drifts," drifts for which he accuses President Zelensky. "It is not the war of one man," Mr. Hontcharenko emphasizes, "but that of a country that must be able to debate." Hence the importance, in his view, of the role of institutions, political opposition, and a free press. "Without this democratic culture, the Russian war remains dependent on a vertical, slow, and rigid command, while the Ukrainian war, horizontal, benefits from a beneficial responsiveness." In his office as military governor of the Mykolaiv region, Vitali Kim, at the end of 2022, already embraced this argument. "The decision to invade a country generally comes from one man, while the decision to defend it is made by a large number of people. Every Ukrainian sees themselves as a component of this survival reaction. Volunteers play an essential role, and if people discuss, all the better, it makes us better." Even within the army, disagreements with the hierarchy are seen as a strength at the end of September. For Volodymyr, battalion commander of the 102nd brigade on the Zaporijia front, "it is the leaders on the ground, non-commissioned officers, or very young officers, who lead the war, not the headquarters with whom there is often friction. They are the ones who decide, which has made us very mobile and very inventive." And has allowed them, for now, to hold off a superior army in terms of numbers and equipment.

title: Burkina Faso tightens its alliance with Russia.
lead: Nine months after the end of French operations in the country, military cooperation between Ouagadougou and Moscow is accelerating and entering a "practical phase." Around twenty Russian military personnel have arrived in the capital.
text_eng: What did an Ilyushin Il-76 come to do in Ouagadougou on Friday, November 10th? While, since taking office in Burkina Faso in September 2022, Captain Ibrahim Traoré has been ambiguous about his alliances, the landing of this Russian plane, with about twenty soldiers on board, at the Thomas Sankara airport in the capital, has shed some light on the situation. Nine months after the end of French operations in Burkinabe territory, the junta has entered into a new alliance with Russia under Vladimir Putin. The Burkinabe government has been showing signs of closer ties with Moscow in various areas in recent weeks: "Humanitarian aid, energy, nuclear, culture, etc.," as stated in a government press release in October. Mid-month, the construction of a civilian nuclear power plant by the Russian agency Rosatom, "to meet the energy needs of the Burkinabe population," was announced; on Tuesday, cooperation in healthcare to combat the dengue epidemic in the country was highlighted. However, it is primarily in the military sector that Ouagadougou needs Moscow. Three days before the arrival of the Ilyushin Il-76, on November 7th, the Burkinabe Minister of Defense, Colonel Kassoum Coulibaly, was in Moscow to meet his counterpart, Sergei Shoigu. Mr. Coulibaly praised the entry into the "practical phase" of cooperation between the two countries in defense matters. The theoretical phase had taken place a few weeks earlier when a Russian delegation led by Deputy Defense Minister Yunus-bek Yevkurov was received in September by President Ibrahim Traoré. "Areas of cooperation primarily concern the military sector, including the training of Burkinabe soldiers and officers at all levels, especially pilots in Russia," stated the Burkinabe presidency. In a delicate position, the implementation of the alliance between the two countries has accelerated since the meeting between Ibrahim Traoré and Vladimir Putin at the Russia-Africa Summit in St. Petersburg at the end of July. During his only trip abroad since taking power, the junta leader stood out with his diatribe against "imperialist countries." With Russia, "we share the same history as forgotten peoples of the world," declared the 35-year-old captain, who had not removed his uniform or beret. The young head of state plays up his closeness to Thomas Sankara, the Burkinabe president who, in 1986, dared to confront François Mitterrand at a gala dinner. Feeling "like family," Ibrahim Traoré assured Vladimir Putin of the "support of the Burkinabe people and [his] government regarding the situation Russia is facing with the 'special operation' in Ukraine." This new ally could be particularly useful to him, as the Burkinabe leader is in a delicate position. Upon taking office, he based his policy and propaganda on the effectiveness of his response to jihadist groups. He recruited thousands of civilians to bolster the ranks of fighters and declared a "general mobilization" in April, signing a decree allowing him to requisition young people over 18 years old. The Volunteers for the Defense of the Fatherland (VDP), around 90,000 according to official figures, require a considerable supply of weaponry. However, on the ground, no improvement has been observed. Despite the government's promises to the population, entire regions of the country remain destabilized by jihadist attacks, around 2 million people are internally displaced, thousands are refugees in neighboring countries, and the violence - which has claimed at least 16,000 lives since 2015 according to the NGO Acled - shows no signs of decreasing. On November 5, another massacre was reported in the village of Zaongo, in the Central-North region, which left at least 70 victims, "mostly children and elderly people," according to the Burkina Faso prosecutor. The "perpetrators of the atrocities remain unknown," he admitted on Monday. Burkinabe soldiers, as well as VDP members, have also been involved in killings in recent months, without ever being brought to justice. In this context, several historical allies of Burkina Faso have refused to cooperate militarily with Ibrahim Traoré. In January, Ivory Coast had agreed to deliver a thousand Kalashnikovs, pickup trucks, and ammunition to its neighbor. "Initially, we played the card of cooperation in the face of the terrorist threat that threatens us all," acknowledged an Ivorian minister speaking anonymously, "but we will not do it again. Ibrahim Traoré is not a reliable partner." Previously, Ibrahim Traoré had turned to France, which had refused to provide him with weapons. Following this refusal, Burkina Faso had requested the departure of the French ambassador, Luc Hallade, and the French special forces from the "Sabre" operation deployed in the country. Propaganda campaigns Now, the young captain openly chooses to follow in the footsteps of Assimi Goïta, at the helm of neighboring Mali, with whom he has formed an "Alliance of Sahel States" alongside the Nigerien coup leaders, threatened by a military intervention from regional countries following their seizure of power in late July. In Mali, the 1,600 mercenaries from the Russian Wagner group deployed since December 2021 are engaged in combat alongside the Malian army, especially in recent days to retake Kidal from the rebels of the Permanent Strategic Framework for Peace, Security, and Development. This new alliance allows Russia to strengthen its influence in Africa, where it continues to be involved despite the death of Wagner's boss, Yevgeny Prigozhin, in August. Russia is conducting propaganda campaigns and attempting to build closer relationships with certain regimes or political actors in the region. Based on an exchange between security offerings and the exploitation of mineral resources, this partnership is also expected to provide Ibrahim Traoré with a valuable benefit: his personal security, similar to the protection provided by Wagner to President Faustin-Archange Touadéra in the Central African Republic. Ibrahim Traoré, who staged a coup, the second in less than a year, overthrowing Paul-Henri Sandaogo Damiba, knows that power is fragile. Facing growing trade union protests, the government claimed to have thwarted a coup attempt on September 27 and announced a restructuring of the army. "In some respects, the fight against terrorism is a cover to protect the regime. The coup plotters intend to remain in power for a very long time," said a former Burkinabe diplomat in exile. Elections to end the transition are supposed to be held in July 2024, according to the junta.

title: In Kiev, soldiers' wives are demanding their demobilization after eighteen months of war.
lead: On Maidan Square, as in the rest of Ukraine, women have been demonstrating for two weeks for the return and replacement of their loved ones who left for the front lines in the early hours of the Russian invasion.
text_eng: A small crowd has gathered in Maidan Square in Kiev, mainly women, demanding the demobilization of their husbands after eighteen months of service in the army. On Sunday, November 12, in the central square of the Ukrainian capital, a few dozen wives, some accompanied by their children, brave the wind and rain, holding signs. "Left to defend a free country and became a serf of war"; "Now it's someone else's turn." A little girl holds a sign with pink letters: "It's my turn to hug my father." "We want a time limit, because there is now this feeling that you can enter the army and never leave," explains Anastasiia Troshyna, 23, whose husband, Oleg, is fighting in the Donbass region in the east of the country, in Donetsk. "They are tired, demotivated, they want rest," insists the young woman, referring to her husband and the soldiers in his unit. "They want to come here, see their children and loved ones." Under martial law, demobilization can only occur in cases of severe injuries, urgent family reasons, or reaching the maximum mobilization age of 60. Since Oleg enlisted in March 2022, Anastasiia has only been able to see him once, when he was sent to a hospital in the capital for a leg injury. While Ukrainian law states that soldiers are entitled to thirty days of leave per year, "this law doesn't work," his wife asserts, "because there is no one to replace them." Refusal to enlist by some is another goal of these small protests that have been taking place in several cities across the country for the past two weeks: to convince those who have not yet fought to join the armed forces. "I know people around me who want to go but hesitate because they don't know how long it will last," says Anastasiia Troshyna. "Will it be for five years? For ten years? Today, the feeling is that you have to be there until the end." For Olha, who refused to give her last name, demobilization after eighteen months would provide some perspective. "No one wants to go fight, and those at home realize there is no end," she says. "We go until we die or become disabled. Maybe this will motivate others, maybe it will alleviate their fears a bit, because people are afraid, afraid to go there and die." The massive enlistment in the early months of the invasion, which had made Ukrainians proud, has given way, in recent months, to increasingly numerous criticisms against men determined to do anything to avoid the army. Some limit their movements or hardly leave their homes anymore. This is also evidenced by the popularity of Telegram channels that track the movements of recruiting officers in the cities. The refusal of a portion of the male population to enlist annoys Iryna Topynko: "We are told that there is no one to replace our men on the front lines, but we see what is happening here!" she exclaims. "We know through hearsay who paid, how they paid, who did what, who went abroad. This injustice... well, it can drive us crazy, and our loved ones too." However, this woman in her fifties wants to clarify that her husband "will not come back without his brothers in arms, as in other units where they say they will stay until the end of the war." "A little more motivated" Many videos of civilians being forcefully arrested by recruiting officers are circulating on social media. The various corruption scandals within the Ministry of Defense and regional recruitment offices that have occurred during the first two years of the war have had a certain impact. Recently, a new scandal has sparked outrage and anger. On November 3, 19 soldiers from the 128th Mountain Assault Brigade were killed, and dozens were injured, in a Russian missile attack while they were gathered for a medal ceremony, about thirty kilometers from the front line in the Zaporijia region. President Volodymyr Zelensky has addressed the event several times, offering his condolences and stating that an investigation would shed light on a "tragedy that could have been avoided." For Olya, whose husband Mykhaïlo also fights in Donbass, a demobilization after eighteen months would give hope to those on the front lines. "It will bring them a bit of strength, they will be a little more motivated, especially among those who have been there since day one," she says. "They do not want to stay there without a return date."

title: How Emmanuel Macron and Angela Merkel were deceived by Vladimir Putin: read the excerpts from "The Blinded" by Sylvie Kauffmann.
lead: In a book to be released on Wednesday, October 18th by Stock, the editorialist at "Le Monde" decrypts, through testimonies and key episodes, what prevented Europeans from perceiving the reality of Vladimir Putin's Russia. We are publishing extensive excerpts focused on the period leading up to the invasion of Ukraine.
text_eng: By deciding to invade Ukraine on February 24, 2022, Vladimir Putin shatters the European order. He also forces the French and Germans to question themselves: why did their leaders make such a serious mistake regarding the Russian head of state? To answer this question, Sylvie Kauffmann, in her investigation "The Blinded. How Berlin and Paris cleared the way for Russia" (Stock), goes back to the very beginning of Emmanuel Macron's first term in 2017. At that time, this young president with assertive diplomatic ambitions was convinced that he could "anchor Russia to Europe." Excerpt from the book.] Putin and us Vladimir Putin knows: since General de Gaulle, who equipped it with nuclear weapons, France sees itself as a "power endowed," one of the five permanent members of the UN Security Council, which can therefore negotiate on an equal footing with other powers. This is the big difference with Germany, whose power is primarily economic. With the end of the Cold War and the disappearance of the Soviet Union, the structures on which the organization of security in Europe was based are disappearing one after the other. A new era has begun. Every French president aspires to establish "a new security architecture in Europe." Emmanuel Macron cannot miss the call. To this ambition, he adds another openly declared one, that of making Europe a geopolitical actor. For him, the time when the United States and the USSR, or now Russia, managed Europe's security over the heads of Europeans is over. Europe must be at the negotiation table. "Engaging Russia" is necessary. Just three weeks after his election, the young French president receives [his Russian counterpart] Vladimir Putin at Versailles on May 29, 2017. (...) The context is delicate: there is Ukraine, of course, but also Syria, where Russian aviation bombs hospitals and other civilian targets under the guise of the fight against terrorism. The French, engaged in the fight against Daesh [the Arabic acronym for the Islamic State organization], note that the Russian forces do not at all keep their word in targeting Daesh structures. Moscow's priority, and it will be successful, is the return of Russia to the region and the consolidation of the Bashar al-Assad regime. (...) There is also the thorny issue of Russian intelligence services interfering in the French electoral campaign. Burned by the unprecedented Russian interference that disrupted the American presidential campaign in 2016, the French took preventive measures; this did not prevent the hacking of Macron's campaign headquarters' email accounts and disinformation operations about his private life. But Macron wants to reconnect and uses the exhibition on Peter the Great at the Grand Trianon in Versailles as a pretext to invite Putin. All the splendors of the Republic and the monarchy are on display. At the press conference following the meeting, Macron's intended approach with Moscow is evident: dialogue with firmness. (...) The tone is appreciated by critics of Putin. The dialogue continues in the following months, over the phone or at G20 summits. According to a witness of these exchanges, Macron "feels a connection with Putin." In 2018, he attends the St. Petersburg Economic Forum, Putin's grand economic showcase, where contracts are signed with the CAC 40 and business is booming. The French President praises what he believes is the "historical and strategic vision" that the Russian President has of the relationship with Europe. Macron is convinced that "Russia must be anchored to Europe." In reality, the desire for dialogue takes precedence over the demand for firmness. Macron cannot resist the temptation to maintain his trip to Russia for the semi-final and final of the 2018 FIFA World Cup – won by France – in July 2018, despite tensions over the Skripal case, a former double agent who was a victim of a poisoning attempt, along with his daughter, in the UK. Relations with Putin are cordial, and the two men address each other informally. Macron devises a more ambitious plan: transitioning from bilateral to "strategic" on a European scale. Is this ambition too much? Or is it a methodological error, with this solitary exercise of power that often characterizes the President's initiatives? Macron decides to launch his own reset, somewhat grandiosely named the "Confidence and Security Initiative." The kickoff is an invitation extended to Vladimir Putin for talks at Fort de Brégançon on the French Riviera on August 19, 2019, a few days before a G7 summit hosted by France in Biarritz. (...) The August sun beats down fiercely on the two delegations seated on the terrace. Everyone is sweating both literally and figuratively, and when they finally sit down for dinner at 11 p.m., they can breathe a little easier. Nothing fruitful comes out of these hours of discussions, except the idea of a new summit in the "Normandy format" on the issue of Donbass, bringing together Macron, Angela Merkel, Putin, and the newcomer in Kiev, President Volodymyr Zelensky, who has just been elected and whom the Russian President dismisses as a clown because he is an actor. Undeterred, Macron asserts his determination to "build a new architecture of security and trust between the European Union and Russia" in this "historic moment" of the "recomposition of the international order." There are two problems with this Brégançon initiative. The first is that it goes completely against the internal dynamics consistently observed in Moscow, which are repressive, expansionist, and revisionist. The Russian paramilitary group Wagner has already set up camp in the Central African Republic to push out the French; on several occasions in recent months, when Macron questions Putin about this issue, Putin responds evasively, claiming not to be aware and saying he will inquire further. The second issue is that the Elysée Palace carried out this operation alone, without involving any of its European partners, while presenting it as a European initiative. Putin suspects this, as he asks Macron, "Will you be able to convince the other Europeans?" Macron has made his personal capacity for charm and persuasion an essential tool of his diplomacy, although this tactic failed with Trump a few months earlier in Washington. Why bother with twenty-six partners when you are the best? Even Berlin was kept out of the loop. Naturally, the Germans are furious. "We were a bit surprised, even shocked," admits a senior German diplomat in a tone that hints at understatement. The phone lines between Berlin and Paris are busy in the days that follow, with "very frank and direct" conversations between people who are used to working together. On the phone, French diplomats loyally defend the Elysée's initiative, but their counterparts in Berlin sense "a certain unease." Some of these diplomats from the French Foreign Ministry had strongly suggested to the Elysée to at least involve Berlin, but they were rebuffed: "That is not what the president wants." The Germans are angry because they fear that this way of decoupling European security from American security will give Putin the opportunity to divide the Alliance. And they are angry because they suspect that the Eastern Europeans will take it very badly. In reality, Berlin shares the essence of Macron's approach to Russia, which, "whether we like it or not, will not disappear from the map," and with which a modus vivendi must be found. But the Germans find the method "so clumsy" that it risks, they say, compromising everything. They are even more offended because their partners in Central and Eastern Europe do not for a second believe that Berlin was not aware! Wary, the Poles and the Balts suspect a new Franco-German maneuver behind their backs, staged through a role-playing scenario in which Paris would pretend to take the lead while Berlin would be pulling the strings with Moscow. Macron assigns Pierre Vimont, a seasoned diplomat and specialist in delicate missions to the point that he is nicknamed the "Swiss Army knife of the French Foreign Ministry," to go heal wounds and sell his initiative in the chancelleries. The Germans welcome him kindly, but their verdict on the French reset is already in: it will not work. The Baltic and Central European countries are not showing a better disposition. "There were too many doubts about our intentions in the Franco-Russian dialogue," acknowledges a diplomat involved in this operation today. "We took our partners' trust for granted too much, and assumed that their security interests aligned with ours." In passing, Emmanuel Macron also irks another category of partners: his own diplomatic corps. The skepticism of a part of the Quai d'Orsay towards his initiative reached him. At the end of August, during the annual meeting of ambassadors, he scolds them at the Elysée like a schoolmaster reprimanding his students. He suspects a "deep state" of seeking to "undermine the presidential word" and, essentially, sabotage his project. "I know that many of you have been trained to be distrustful of Russia," he asserts. "In general, on this subject, we listen to the president and proceed as usual. I strongly advise you not to follow this path." Mistake. "I found it deeply unfair," testifies one of the diplomats present, summarizing the general feeling. "At the Quai, one can have their own ideas, but we are loyal." The accusation deepens the gap between the Elysée and the Quai d'Orsay, a structural problem of the Fifth Republic. Especially since the works on the "architecture of trust and security" are struggling to get started on the Russian side. Jean-Yves Le Drian, Minister of Foreign Affairs, and Florence Parly, Minister of the Armed Forces, will act as scouts. They fly to Moscow to restart the "2 + 2" consultations with their Russian counterparts, which had been interrupted since [the annexation, in 2014, of] Crimea. A way, they think, to test Moscow's goodwill. Their political directors accompany the ministers, and the delegation is substantial. "Look, the deep state descending from the plane," a witness quips. Things seem to be going rather well at first. After the two bilateral meetings, everyone gathers for lunch at an official guesthouse. "A Russian-style lunch," notes a French participant: vodka flows freely, toasts are made against the backdrop of imperial hunting scenes hanging on the walls. They are all there, Sergey Lavrov, the Minister of Foreign Affairs, Sergey Shoigu, the Defense Minister, Igor Kostyukov, the head of the GRU, the military intelligence... top-notch. The French present several concrete cooperation proposals – there is no shortage of "deconfliction" topics. The Russians had hinted that the two French ministers would be received by Putin in the afternoon, but the meeting does not take place: this is a first sign that alerts the French. They have dinner in the evening at the embassy with representatives of Russian civil society, journalists, historians from the Memorial association, whose courage impresses them; they return to Paris the next day. In the following weeks, they regularly follow up with their official contacts in Moscow. "But the Russians don't engage. We proposed dates, but they didn't follow up." Months go by... and nothing happens. "There was the decorum, but nothing behind it." A wasted effort. (...) Zelensky flips the table, but Macron still manages to secure Putin's agreement to come to Paris on December 10, 2019, for a "Normandy summit," as outlined in the Minsk agreements on the Donbass. It is the first time the Russian president meets the new Ukrainian president, who was elected eight months earlier – and it will be the only time. The Germans believe that this framework, with Merkel and Macron, is more suitable for this first meeting: "Knowing Putin a little bit, we thought he would eat Zelensky for breakfast if we left them alone!" The preparation for this summit costs the diplomats involved endless hours of laborious negotiations, but when the time comes, something unexpected happens at the Elysée Palace: Zelensky flips the table – figuratively. He questions the agreements that, when signed in 2015, were much more favorable to Russia than to Ukraine since the Ukrainian army was in a weak position. However, since then, it has strengthened, notably thanks to the training its soldiers received from the British and Americans. The former comedian no longer wants to be walked over. "I see the text very well," he declares, "but if we continue like this, it will take decades. And I don't have the luxury of time." The meeting is disrupted. Putin is furious, but he doesn't show it. The session leads to interesting exchanges between the Russian president and the German chancellor, who have known each other for twelve years now. "Listen, I'll tell you the truth," Putin begins. "But Vladimir, I hope you always tell the truth!" Angela Merkel interrupts, feigning innocence. "Angela," he retorts. "Everyone lies, I lie, you lie, Emmanuel lies, even Mr. Zelensky will lie, it's normal!" Sergey Lavrov, who had probably assured his president that everything was settled, becomes very nervous as he sees the negotiation falling apart. He gets up, starts running back and forth. "He wanted to negotiate amendments to the text that had been previously agreed upon with us," says a participant, amused. "In short, it was a bit chaotic." At the end of a long day, the differences between Zelensky and Putin become quite clear when the four leaders meet the press. They have at least reached an agreement on a prisoner exchange, which will take place, and promise to meet again in the same format in Berlin four months later. In the meantime, their ministers and advisors are supposed to iron out the difficulties. There will be no new summit, neither in Berlin nor elsewhere. In March 2020, Covid-19 appears in Europe and Putin isolates himself in the Kremlin, in Sochi, or in another one of his residences. The dream of the Russian president to host Trump, Macron, Merkel, and other world leaders on May 9 for the 75th anniversary of the Soviet victory in the "Great Patriotic War" collapses. The parade on Red Square is canceled. (...) In reality, this summit in December 2019 in Paris probably produced an unexpected result: Putin became aware of the political dimension of the character Zelensky. He understood that he could not "eat him for breakfast" as the Germans feared. Macron had played this point quite well. On the advice of the French ambassador to Ukraine, Isabelle Dumont, who had watched all the episodes of the TV series "Servant of the People" in which the actor Volodymyr Zelensky played the role of a teacher who became president despite himself, and then followed, fascinated, his election campaign, the French president invited the iconoclastic candidate to the Elysée in 2019, even before he was elected. A personal relationship was thus able to be established at that time between the two presidents. It will allow to counterbalance, at least a little, the Putin card that Macron will play to the full, until the disaster of February 2022. Merkel bites the dust. Some believe that it is from this Paris summit that Putin begins to conceive the large-scale invasion of Ukraine. His resentment towards the European turn of the Ukrainians and their elites is exacerbated by the isolation imposed by the pandemic, which he applies in a particularly draconian manner. What information from the field reaches him? It is impossible to know for sure: the Kremlin is becoming increasingly opaque. On her part, Angela Merkel is worried. The arrest and imprisonment of the Russian opposition figure Alexei Navalny upon his plane's descent in Moscow in January 2021, after his stay in Germany where he was treated for his Novichok poisoning, is a very bad sign that reinforces her concern. In Russia, the grip tightens further around NGOs, which are increasingly repressed. In May, three German NGOs were banned from Russia, leading to a freeze of the "St. Petersburg Dialogue," a structure formed in 2001 to promote exchanges between civil societies of the two countries. The chancellor expresses her concern in informal conversations with other leaders on the sidelines of the G7 in Carbis Bay, Cornwall, from June 11 to 13, 2021. She has a premonition. She argues that we must maintain contact with Moscow, as during the Cold War. A few days after the G7 summit, on June 16, 2021, President Joe Biden leaves his European friends behind and flies to Geneva to meet Vladimir Putin. He did not involve any of them in this meeting on European soil. From their respective capitals, Emmanuel Macron and Angela Merkel observe this summit with a hint of bitterness: when they talk to Putin, their Central European partners cry betrayal, but when the President of the United States does it, no one says anything. On June 18, Macron visits Merkel in Berlin. It is the first time in a year that the Chancellor receives him at her home, as the pandemic has prevented physical exchanges; everyone is still wearing masks. Before their meetings with the press, Merkel explains that they will prepare for the European summit on June 24 and 25, a week later; she explicitly states that Russia will be discussed and that she is in favor of contacts with Moscow, beyond the very limited ones that exist within the framework of the Minsk agreements. No one pays attention to this statement. However, the Chancellor has an idea in mind, which she submits to President Macron during a long discussion that evening, in order to turn it into a Franco-German idea. This idea is to invite Putin, for the first time since the annexation of Crimea, to a Russia-European Union summit, with the other twenty-five leaders of the member states. Paris finds the option of a 27 + 1 summit very generous for a Putin who has not moved since 2014, and believes that a summit with only the presidents of the European institutions would suffice. But Macron can only approve of the idea of reopening dialogue with Moscow: he has been trying in vain since 2019. And he will not contradict Angela so close to her departure... In the aftermath, Merkel calls Vladimir Putin and also Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky. On June 22, Putin signs a gentle article in Die Zeit, calling for the "restoration of a full partnership with Europe." Merkel just forgets to mention what has become a "Franco-German project" to the other Europeans, who discover it, furious, on the eve of the European Council. Because in all the preparatory meetings for the European Council that Charles Michel has organized, as he does before every summit, this idea has never been raised. The President of the European Council attaches great importance to this preparatory work, knowing how much the Chancellor dislikes improvisation. The European Council is the body of the Union that brings together the twenty-seven heads of state and government. The discussion is very open, everyone gives their opinion before moving on to the vote; the preparatory work carried out by the president and his team with the Member States is therefore crucial to defuse possible conflicts and ensure the smooth running of the debates. This June Council meeting was no exception; a final video conference meeting with Ursula von der Leyen [President of the European Commission], Angela Merkel, and Emmanuel Macron had helped to iron out the details. Everything seemed in order. Charles Michel was calm. However, two days before the summit, during a final video conference, Merkel surprised everyone by announcing that Macron and she would propose a summit with Russia. This was the result of the meeting on June 18 in Berlin. The Council presidency tried to defuse the situation, fearing the reaction of the Member States. "Suddenly telling the Baltic States, 'Angela and Emmanuel are proposing to invite Putin for a meeting at the European Council,' is not going to work, they are not at all prepared," European officials explained to Merkel's advisors. In vain. The Germans persisted. This was the Chancellor's last Council meeting before the federal elections in September, which would see her step down after sixteen years of not only ruling Germany but also the European Council. She wanted to push through. The day before the summit, an article in the Financial Times revealed Merkel and Macron's initiative regarding Russia. The leaders of the Twenty-Seven arrived in Brussels, some feeling tense. The meeting began, behind closed doors as usual, in a tense atmosphere. Merkel spoke first. She recounted the history of relations between Russia and the EU. She said that for sixteen years, she had been observing Putin and seeing his regime harden. He thought he could consolidate his power by relying on the economy, but he realized he knew nothing about the economy and fell back on his fundamentals: security and intelligence services. "He sees us as enemies," she said. "I have no illusions, but we must talk to him. The Americans do it. We are the target of destabilization maneuvers and we are depriving ourselves of this tool." Macron then took over with the familiar argument: we must talk to Putin, "we cannot just be passive spectators of American-Russian dialogue when we are the most affected." (...) Italian Prime Minister Mario Draghi then spoke, but much more modestly. He simply said that he supported the initiative. Dutch Prime Minister Mark Rutte said he was not opposed to such a summit but that he himself would not participate. Since the tragedy of flight MH17 and its 298 deaths, more than half of whom were Dutch, the Netherlands have been deeply affected by the relationship with Russia. Silence. Embarrassed, the officials of the Council discover that "M&M", Merkel and Macron, want to sneak in a line about this EU-Russia summit into the final statement, which was not mentioned during the prior consultations. That's when Kaja Kallas steps in. The Estonian Prime Minister, who just celebrated her 44th birthday, has only been in power for five months; this is her first in-person Council meeting, as everything was done virtually until now. She may not yet be the rising star of the new Europe, but she has the audacity; her experience as a lawyer has accustomed her to supporting her arguments, which she formulates in perfect English. Blonde, slender, she does not have the modesty in dress of the previous generation of female leaders; Kaja Kallas confidently embraces a very feminine elegance. All eyes turn to the Estonian, who confronts Merkel head-on. "A summit about what," she asks. "For what purpose?" When, in May 2023, two years later, I ask Kaja Kallas at her home in Tallinn what memories she has of the June 2021 Council, her eyes immediately light up: "Oh, I have many memories! It's a Council I will never forget!" Because she delivered a devastating critique of the Franco-German proposal. "She tore it to shreds," says a witness, "the tone was very harsh. That's a bit of her temperament." "I just said it wasn't right," she recounts, falsely modest, "and I argued." The knockout argument: "We always said we wouldn't have a summit with Putin until he returned Crimea. But the conditions are not met, so what will we look like? It will mean our word is worth nothing!" Merkel takes this challenge very poorly. Contrary to her usual demeanor, according to several witnesses, she gets angry, loses her temper, "turns all red." The discussion continues but Kaja Kallas does not back down. A participant describes "a groundswell, both in form and substance"; "it was quite painful." Viktor Orban, the Hungarian, known for his pro-Putin stance, revisits Merkel's argument that Putin knows nothing about the economy: "It's true, he's not an economist," he says. "But he perfectly understands what controlling the production of raw materials means from a strategic point of view." This dig at Germany, alluding to Berlin's dependence on Russian gas, probably does nothing to improve the chancellor's mood. Finally, Kaja Kallas recounts, Mario Draghi says, "She's right," turning to her, and the proposal is abandoned. "You must have been quite a lawyer!" he whispers to her after the meeting... However, as she leaves the room, the young Prime Minister is horrified by what she has just done: "I thought I had just shattered my country's relations with Germany!" The usual press conference of the Presidents of the Council and the Commission has been postponed, without explanation. Merkel still makes it known that there is no agreement for a direct meeting between European and Russian leaders. "Personally, I would have liked a bolder step forward," she acknowledges. But no one suspects the violence of the confrontation that has just taken place in the Council. The next day, to her great surprise, Kaja Kallas' colleagues pat her on the back to congratulate her. Her surprise grows when she later receives a phone call from Angela Merkel, who, being a great lady, wants to apologize. Kaja Kallas also apologizes for losing her temper. The two women will have lunch together to clear up any misunderstandings. Kaja Kallas is unaware, but the Chancellor also calls the other two Baltic leaders and tells them she regrets launching her initiative without consulting them. That's Merkel for you! In the heat of the moment, at the end of the second day of the Council, the Chancellor still admits to being "saddened" by the experience, which, she says, shows that the Member States "do not have enough trust in each other." A lucid and meaningful comment. In reality, the episode almost alone symbolizes the shift within the EU and the revenge of the "lands of blood." The countries of the former communist Europe had become accustomed to not raising their voices. That day, June 24, 2021, "Merkel bit the dust and Kallas defeated her," one of the participants summarizes. For her last European summit, the Chancellor of the most powerful country in the Union was thwarted by a rebellion led by the young head of government of a country of one and a half million inhabitants, on the eastern fringes of the Union. And the discord was, of course, about Russia. Doubts about the Franco-German tandem's Russian policy were growing among the countries of Central and Eastern Europe. This hastily prepared move behind their backs was the last straw. How could Merkel shoot herself in the foot like this, she who is usually so cautious and organized? Was it the prospect of her departure from the chancellery that prompted her to rush things? She had already stumbled six months earlier by pushing through a highly contested agreement on investments with China at the end of Germany's EU presidency, sealed during a video conference summit with Chinese President Xi Jinping on December 30, 2020. Bad move: relations with Beijing deteriorated so much afterwards that the agreement was suspended before even coming into effect. Perhaps the Chancellor was dreaming of leaving power on a triple geopolitical success, the Euro-Chinese summit with Xi in December 2020, a summit with Joe Biden scheduled for July 2021, and ending on a high note with a Euro-Russian summit with Putin in September. Instead of a triple success, she ultimately exits the European stage with a double failure. Sign of the times, it concerns China and Russia. (…) On August 22, the chancellor is in Kiev to bid farewell to Volodymyr Zelensky. Her heart is not in it. Ukrainians celebrate their 30th anniversary of independence two days later; she preferred to come before.
