They would have wanted her to stay to participate in an international conference on Crimea; she chooses to slip away. The exchanges are polite, but Zelensky cannot help but twist the knife in the wound of Nord Stream 2. Until the last day, the gas pipeline will have poisoned Angela Merkel's policy in the East. The horizon darkens more and more. The chancellor leaves Kiev without having succeeded in reassuring Ukraine.

title: In Rafah, the prosecutor of the International Criminal Court calls for respect for the laws of war.
lead: The British Karim Khan, who visited the only exit point from Gaza to Egypt on Sunday, warned Israel about its military operations and called on Hamas to release the hostages.
text_eng: Three weeks after the terrorist attacks carried out by Hamas in Israel on October 7, and the beginning of Israeli retaliations on Gaza, the prosecutor of the International Criminal Court (ICC) reminded Israel of its "obligation" to "comply with the laws of armed conflict." Karim Khan, who said he has been investigating the ongoing events since October 7, stated that "preventing access to humanitarian aid can constitute a crime," and that "schools, hospitals, and mosques" are protected sites. "Hostage-taking is a serious violation of the Geneva Conventions," the prosecutor asserted, before calling for the "release" of all individuals held by Hamas. On Sunday, October 29, the British prosecutor made a brief statement at the Rafah border crossing, the only point of passage from the Gaza Strip to the outside world, via Egypt, following in the footsteps of the UN Secretary-General, Antonio Guterres, who had pleaded for humanitarian access to Gaza on October 20. The ICC had opened an investigation in 2021 into the 2014 war in the enclave and the colonization of the occupied Palestinian territories, including East Jerusalem. However, the investigations have made little progress. Israel disputes the Court's jurisdiction, as it is not a member. Mr. Khan did not want to alienate Washington, Israel's main ally, despite Palestinian insistence. In December 2022, Raji Sourani, a prominent figure at the Palestinian Center for Human Rights, an organization documenting abuses for years, had criticized the prosecutor for the slow progress of the case. Since then, the activist has returned to Gaza, from where he sends out occasional tweets denouncing the ongoing "ethnic cleansing" and "genocide." "A two-tiered justice system" On October 26, the Minister of Foreign Affairs of the Palestinian Authority, Riyad Al-Maliki, met with Karim Khan in The Hague, where the ICC is based, to assure him of their cooperation, without publicly stating whether it extended to the massacre committed by Hamas on October 7. Filmed on Sunday in front of the Rafah gate, the prosecutor stated that he would have "really wanted to be able to enter Gaza and also go to Israel." Since October 7, legal experts, diplomats, NGOs, and UN experts have criticized his silence. For example, on X (formerly Twitter), Ahmed Abofoul, a jurist from the Palestinian human rights organization Al-Haq, who has lost several relatives in Gaza since October 7, called on the prosecutor to respond. In 2018, following "threats" of ICC prosecution, the Israeli government had abandoned plans to forcibly relocate the population of Khan Al-Ahmar, a Bedouin village south of Jerusalem. Many criticize the Court for "a two-tiered justice system," especially since the start of the war in Ukraine, where it took Mr. Khan only a year to issue an arrest warrant against Russian President Vladimir Putin. "Critical voices that could still be heard" at the UN General Assembly, according to a diplomat. The president of the ICC was supposed to present his annual report there on Monday, October 30th.

title: Kazakhstan's mineral resources are attracting interest.
lead: On Wednesday, November 1st and Thursday, November 2nd, President Emmanuel Macron will be visiting Kazakhstan and Uzbekistan. France hopes to strengthen its supply of critical metals in the region.
text_eng: Scandium, Lanthanum, cerium... Essential for the energy transition, rare earth elements are the oil of tomorrow. Since the West has opened its eyes to its excessive dependence on China in this matter, it has been multiplying diversification strategies. "To secure its resources, the European Union (EU) is increasingly turning to countries like Canada, Australia, and Kazakhstan," summarizes Thomas Gomart, director of the French Institute of International Relations. On Saturday, October 28, the death of at least forty-five miners at an ArcelorMittal site tragically reminded that this distant country in Central Asia is rich in underground resources. On Wednesday, November 1, and Thursday, November 2, it will receive, like its Uzbek neighbor, a visit from the French President, Emmanuel Macron. The reason: "To support these two countries in their reform efforts," emphasizes the Elysee Palace. But also, to strengthen ties "in the economic field, particularly in energy and supply." Accompanied by about fifteen business leaders, including those from EDF, Suez, and Orano (formerly Areva), as well as the Bureau of Geological and Mining Research, Emmanuel Macron promised "contract announcements." Paris is notably in the running to participate in the construction project of the first Kazakh nuclear power plant. However, access to critical metals will also be on the agenda. Difficult reserves to evaluate Since its independence in 1991, the former Soviet country boasts of having "all the minerals and elements of Mendeleev's table" in its subsoil. According to the United States Geological Survey, it is the largest producer of uranium (40% of global production), the second largest producer of chromite (13%), the fifth of cadmium (5.9%), and its mines are rich in rhenium, zinc, and manganese. However, the total state of its reserves is difficult to evaluate. "The economy is partly opaque," summarizes Agathe Demarais, from the European Council on Foreign Relations (ECFR), an independent think tank. This is because a large part of the infrastructure and mining inventories date back to the Soviet era. And because powerful clans close to the government share a portion of the resources. Western executives working in the sector readily admit, under the guise of anonymity, that corruption is not uncommon there. Nevertheless, the country is one of the most attractive in the region for foreigners, thanks in part to its reform efforts. "At its independence, it was able to exploit its hydrocarbon fields by calling on international companies with the technical expertise necessary to do so," explains Dominique Menu, former head of Central Asia for BNP Paribas, who regularly writes about the region. Its main deposits of Tengiz, Karachaganak, and Kashagan have been developed through joint ventures between a state company and Chevron, ExxonMobil, or TotalEnergies. "Oil is still the lifeblood: it accounts for nearly 80% of the country's mining production," adds Mr. Menu. And the majority of its exports. Uranium extraction is dominated by Kazatomprom, the state-owned company that operates around fifteen mines - again, often in joint ventures with foreign companies. Including Orano, present since 1996, with 1,200 employees. More than 50% of the country's uranium production is exported to China, including a significant portion from Orano. "Multivectorial" foreign policy The exploitation of critical metals is largely carried out by local actors. And upon closer inspection, Europe's charm offensive in this area began years ago. In 2012, Germany and former President Nursultan Nazarbayev concluded a partnership aimed at guaranteeing German companies the right to exploit Kazakh rare earths in exchange for investments. Ten years later, in November 2022, the EU signed a memorandum of understanding with the country, which then promised to provide it with at least sixteen of the thirty critical metals it needs. However, these agreements are not easy to implement. Notably because this country, the size of five Frances, is landlocked between Russia and China - placing it at the heart of rivalries between these two powers. "It only has access to the Caspian Sea, getting materials out is costly and sometimes complex," adds a European engineer in the sector. The case of oil illustrates this: 80% of the black gold that Kazakhstan exports passes through Russia to reach the port of Novorossiysk on the Black Sea, via the Caspian Pipeline Consortium, in which Moscow is a 31% shareholder. A dependence that Vladimir Putin's country regularly exploits. "In 2022, he suspended oil deliveries from Kazakhstan through this pipeline four times," recalls Marie Dumoulin, director of the ECFR's Expanded Europe program, in a note on the subject. A decision that was widely interpreted as a reaction to Kazakhstan distancing itself from Moscow following the invasion of Ukraine. Since then, President Kassym-Jomart Tokayev, who came to power in 2019, has continued the "multivectorial" foreign policy deployed by his predecessor. Namely, maintaining good relations with everyone: Europe, the United States, Turkey, Russia despite everything, but also China, which closely eyes its subsoil... "In this context, Kazakhstan will undoubtedly raise the stakes around access to its rare earths, foreshadowing a trend to come in many emerging countries," predicts Agathe Demarais. The latter will demand strong commitments from the candidates, such as, at a minimum, access to technologies.

title: "Without the sustained pursuit of our assistance, the Ukrainian army will not be able to prevail."
lead: As the Israeli-Palestinian conflict is on everyone's minds, Members of Parliament from Renaissance and Horizons, Benjamin Haddad and Nathalie Loiseau, call for increased military support for Ukraine in an opinion piece in "Le Monde".
text_eng: Since October 7th, our eyes have been fixed on Israel and its response, as well as on the responses of the countries in the region, Europe, and the United States to the atrocities committed by Hamas. Legitimately, the Middle East concerns us. Gradually, we are talking less about Ukraine. However, the war being waged by Russia should still hold our attention. So far, Europeans and Americans have acted together to support Ukraine, without going to war. The Ukrainian army is holding its ground, and its resistance protects us from a Russian threat that concerns us all. But without the sustained continuation of our aid, Ukraine will not be able to prevail. Yet, this Western determination is now in jeopardy. The regime of Russian President Vladimir Putin, who met with Hamas and Iran in Moscow on October 26th, could be the geopolitical winner in a scenario where we look away. The Kremlin is counting on our weariness to prevail: let's not fall into this trap. In the United States, Trump supporters are blocking the functioning of Congress, whose approval is necessary to continue funding military aid to Kiev, a preview of what a new Donald Trump presidency could look like. The former Republican American president is now in a better position in the polls to win than in 2016. Among some Democrats, voices are also rising to consider that Ukraine is not a priority. It would be wrong not to take these signals seriously. Polite silence Europe may soon have to take over. France has long been calling for strengthening the strategic autonomy of the Old Continent. Europe must heed this call if it is to continue supporting Ukraine alone or almost alone for its own security in the future. After fully realizing the return of war to their soil and providing unprecedented military aid to Kiev, Europeans are getting tired. For months, a new tranche of funding for sending weapons to Ukraine has been blocked by only one country, Hungary. The rise to power of Robert Fico in Slovakia does not bode well on this issue. At the end of August, the EU High Representative for Foreign Affairs, Josep Borrell, proposed to the 27 member states to provide more visibility and a form of security guarantee to Ukraine by committing to a ceiling of 20 billion euros in military aid over four years. To date, his proposal has received little more than a polite silence. Certainly, Europe's military support is not only through the European Peace Facility mechanism overseen by Borrell. European countries that can do so provide additional bilateral aid of nearly equivalent amounts. In 2022, France had set up a support fund for Ukraine of 200 million euros, an innovative initiative that allows Kiev to directly purchase from our industries, but its funding has not yet been renewed. During a successful trip to Ukraine in September, the Minister of Armed Forces, Sébastien Lecornu, proposed institutionalizing this mechanism to replace direct sales, which are costly for our stocks. The examination of the budget for 2024 is an opportunity to renew it and it must absolutely be seized. Filling the gaps At the same time, our arms industries struggle to produce enough to meet the needs of an unprecedented conventional war. The tools put in place by the European Union, such as joint procurement or a munitions plan, are a good start but they are still very new and the amounts allocated to them are insufficient. Strengthening European defense requires a long-promised European defense investment plan in the medium term that is still awaited. This involves financial incentives that require a rethink of the mid-term European budget, as this budget was adopted before the return of war to Europe. At the moment, Member States show little sign that they are ready to embark on this revision, yet it is essential. They also show reluctance to borrow jointly, as suggested by the Estonian Prime Minister, Kaja Kallas, during the European Council on February 9, 2023. The Twenty-Seven are divided but propose little. Furthermore, there are still gaps to be filled in the sanctions, both European and American, against Russia. Despite the arms embargo targeting Russia, Moscow continues to import essential components for its military equipment, purchased from Western companies through intermediaries and ending up in Russia. This is particularly the case with microprocessors. Europe has established a mechanism to combat sanctions evasion. However, companies are not attentive enough to the final destination of their production and should be more encouraged to be vigilant. It is urgent for us to wake up. From Jerusalem to Kiev, passing through Yerevan, the chaos of the world is at our doorstep. Let's not wait for this avalanche of crises to reach us before giving ourselves the means to protect the attacked democracies.

title: "Whether facing the Russo-Ukrainian or Israel-Hamas conflicts, the observation remains the same: there is no 'international community' capable of imposing a resolution."
lead: The United States being on the defensive, China and Russia positioning themselves as advocates of an anti-American world order, the UN Security Council finds itself paralyzed, notes Alain Frachon, editorialist at "Le Monde".
text_eng: The wars of the time are a reflection of a fragmented, divided, antagonistic international scene - and therefore largely powerless. Whether it is the Russo-Ukrainian conflict or the one between Israel and the Palestinian Hamas movement, the observation is the same: there is no "international community" capable of promoting or imposing a settlement. The massacres committed on October 7 in southern Israel should have been unanimously condemned, from south to north, from east to west. One could have expected a united front in condemnation from major powers, all of which maintain close relations with the State of Israel. At least if one accepts that the event is a trauma comparable to that caused by the attacks of September 11, 2001 in the United States. This latter interpretation was that of Western countries that condemned the Hamas operation. Elsewhere - in Asia, Africa, Latin America - the interpretation has often been different. In an atmosphere of absolute relativism, October 7th was relegated to the realm of terrorism that the Middle East is accustomed to: as everyone knows, the terrorists of some are the resistance fighters of others... Then came the Israeli response, against a backdrop of water, electricity, fuel embargo, and no way out for the Gazans. The ferocity of the bombings and the thousands of civilian casualties quickly overshadowed or downplayed the specificity of the events of October 7. But even before the Israeli intervention, China under Xi Jinping and Russia under Vladimir Putin did not explicitly denounce the Hamas attack. These two countries have very good relations with the State of Israel, but also with the entire Arab world and with Iran. "Unparalleled level of hatred" In many ways, the Chinese and Russians were well positioned to exercise a form of mediation. Their choice was different: they showed solidarity with the Palestinians and criticized the Israelis, finally denouncing US policy in the region. It was impossible to agree on a common starting point, in this case, a condemnation. The mere threat of a veto from one or the other paralyzed the UN Security Council. Why? Signatories of a declaration of "unlimited friendship," concluded on February 4, 2022 in Beijing, a few weeks before the Russian assault on Kiev, the Chinese and Russians maintain a common politico-ideological purpose: to denounce American (or Western) "hegemony" over the international system inherited from 1945. The ambition is to weaken the United States and to steer the system in a direction more favorable to the interests of Moscow and Beijing. Washington is on the defensive. By unconditionally supporting the war waged by the government of Benjamin Netanyahu in Gaza, the United States is increasingly isolated. European leaders are calling for a humanitarian pause in the fighting - a way to distinguish themselves from Washington. In Africa, Asia, and Latin America, as well as in a good part of Western opinions, the solidarity expressed after a month of bombings in Gaza goes to the Palestinians. In the Arab world, "even among the most independent minds, there is an unprecedented level of hatred" towards Israel, according to an expert from the Gulf region. China and Russia are taking advantage of this situation, positioning themselves as advocates for an anti-American global order. The goal is to rally a significant portion of the informal and vague collective known as the Global South - Africa, Asia, Latin America - against the United States. Russians and Chinese are actively working towards this. The New York Times of November 3 notes that Moscow, Tehran, and to a lesser extent, Beijing, are flooding official media and online platforms with "a deluge of propaganda in favor of Hamas and against Israel's main ally, the United States." "This is followed by hundreds of millions of people around the world," says an expert quoted by the newspaper. The Islamic Republic of Iran is a significant ally that Putin and Xi cultivate closely. This regime, which readily oppresses women and arms Hamas, is not considered a pariah, thanks to the support of Moscow and Beijing. Iran has been welcomed into the BRICS, the group of emerging economies from the South, and was chosen to preside over the annual forum of the UN Human Rights Council on November 2 and 3 (discrediting the United Nations). Tehran sells weapons to Russia for Putin's war in Ukraine and offers discounted oil to China. The limits of bloc logic In private, Joe Biden tried to moderate the Israeli offensive in Gaza. However, his apparent lack of success confirms another aspect of the international scene: the diminishing power of superpowers, as demonstrated by Israel's autonomy in its relationship with its American protector. Moscow and Beijing are gaining ground at the expense of the United States or Western countries in the so-called "Global South." Parts of Africa and the Arab world, as well as India and Brazil, have been hesitant to condemn Russia in the Ukrainian affair. Nevertheless, it would be incorrect to speak of a China-Russia-emerging countries bloc, whether in the war in Ukraine or the Middle East. India, a founding member of BRICS, supports Israel and opposes Chinese expansionism in Asia. Turkey, under Recep Tayyip Erdogan, is reluctant to leave NATO but supports Hamas in Gaza. Qatar, a financier of Palestinian Islamists, hosts one of the largest American military bases in the Middle East. The logic of blocs has its limits. A silver lining: while seeking to weaken the United States everywhere, the Chinese and Russians do not want the tragedy in Gaza to escalate into a regional conflict.

title: Russia is using the conflict in Gaza to weaken support for Ukraine.
lead: Moscow has departed from its traditional position of balance between Israel and the Arab countries by not condemning the Hamas attack. The Kremlin sees the conflict as an opportunity to turn the accusations against it regarding the violation of international law and crimes against civilians back on the "collective West."
text_eng: A "gift from heaven" for the Kremlin: the expression is repeated over and over in the comments of Western diplomats and external observers. Israeli retaliations in the Gaza Strip, in response to the terrorist attack carried out by Hamas on October 7, are a windfall for Russia in its war against Ukraine and its confrontation with the "collective West," as the prevailing term in Moscow goes. In the aftermath of these attacks, Russian media barely concealed their satisfaction, despite the risk of a widespread escalation in the Middle East, including Syria. Beyond a part of fantasy - the idea that the United States would already be unable to supply arms to both Kiev and Tel Aviv - there is indeed a reality: the resurgence of conflict in the Middle East offers a welcome diversion for Moscow and weakens the Western position on Ukraine. "The situation could play in favor of Russia," summarized the popular newspaper Moskovski Komsomolets as early as October 9. The stakes go beyond simply relegating the Ukrainian issue in the hierarchy of television news. It concerns the efforts made by Western countries since February 2022 to isolate Russia and rally countries designated as part of the global South to their cause. These efforts, met with only relative success, are on the verge of being swept away, both in public opinion and among a number of governments in the Middle East, Africa, Asia, and Latin America. Isolation threatens... the Western bloc. "A godsend for Putin" The brutality of the Israeli response and the support it receives in Western capitals weaken two pillars of the Western discourse on Ukraine: Russia's disregard for international law and the crimes committed by its army. A comparison was quickly drawn between the Russian and Israeli occupations, which Western countries refuse to equate. Similarly, the differential treatment of potential war crimes revives the discourse - old and recurrent in the Israeli-Palestinian issue - of "double standards" and Western "hypocrisy." In other words, in the current feverish atmosphere, it is difficult to avoid comparisons between Russian bombings on Ukrainian energy infrastructure and Israel's decision to cut off electricity to Gaza. "It's a godsend for Putin," acknowledges a European diplomat. "The entire discourse and efforts of the Western countries are weakened," also notes Tatiana Kastouéva-Jean, from the French Institute of International Relations. It is a windfall for Moscow, which hopes to break out of its international isolation with the opening of this new front in the Middle East." This new development was immediately understood and exploited in Moscow. In the name of the absolute priority given to the Ukrainian issue, the Russian authorities did not hesitate to sacrifice their traditional policy of balance and to go against a rather pro-Israeli public opinion. This reversal was evident from the early days through breaches of established norms, unusual for an experienced Russian diplomacy: it took Vladimir Putin more than a week to call his Israeli counterpart, Benjamin Netanyahu, on October 16, and offer his condolences "to the families of the victims." Neither during this call nor in any of the official Russian communications did Moscow condemn Hamas or refer to "terrorism," despite the death of nineteen Russian nationals in the attacks, as of the latest count. Putin even went as far as comparing the siege of Gaza to the blockade of Leningrad by the Nazis between 1941 and 1944. Mediator role The strategy, supported by a media treatment openly hostile to Israel, is clear: it aims to strengthen as much as possible a diverse anti-Western front and emphasize the internal fractures within this Western camp. This obsession was noticeable from Putin's first public comments on October 10, where he mentioned the subject only to make it "a striking example of American policy in the Middle East." A few days later, from Beijing, he warned - or rejoiced - that conflicts in the world "strengthen" the bond between Russia and China. The Kremlin takes even more liberties with Israel, a usually cherished partner, as the risk of Tel Aviv deciding to militarily assist Ukraine has become almost nil. Milan Czerny, a specialist in Russo-Israeli relations, notes that Israel was still "surprised by the Russian position given Israel's restraint on Ukraine. Russo-Iranian relations are known, but Russia was previously trying to appear more neutral." However, Israeli officials, aware of Moscow's influence on Damascus and Tehran, refrained from any protest. Moscow even continues to claim a mediator role that, if granted, would be another divine surprise. In addition to phone consultations with several Arab leaders, Russian diplomacy presented a resolution project to the United Nations Security Council on October 16 - which was rejected - condemning violence against civilians (once again, without mentioning Hamas). This move could one day put Western powers in the uncomfortable position of having to veto a text calling for de-escalation. The move is a win for Putin. The Russian stance is seen as "totally cynical" at the Elysée: "The Russians calculate that by supporting Hamas, they will align themselves with the opinion of many Southern countries and gain an advantage on the Ukrainian front." The German Chancellor Olaf Scholz also denounced the "cynicism" of Vladimir Putin calling for the protection of civilian populations. The contrast between the crimes committed by Russia and the attitude of Kiev is all the more striking. Ukraine has shown unwavering support for Israel since October 7th. Volodymyr Zelensky defended Israel's "unquestionable right" to defend itself and called Benjamin Netanyahu the next day to express his solidarity. In the eyes of many Ukrainians, the attacks and atrocities committed by Hamas resonate with those inflicted by Russia on Ukrainians since the beginning of the invasion on February 24, 2022. On social media, widely shared video montages drew parallels between the crimes committed during the attack on the rave party in the Israeli desert and those in Bucha, a suburb of Kiev. This discourse is also a way to reaffirm Ukraine's alignment with the Western camp, demonstrating that this alignment goes beyond the immediate question of the war against Russia. However, Kiev's total support for Israel could also backfire. "The Global South may wonder why support Ukraine if it takes only one side, does not condemn Israeli strikes, and does not express compassion for Palestinian civilians," notes Tatiana Kastouéva-Jean. Like other heads of state, Volodymyr Zelensky, who comes from a Jewish family, offered to visit Israel to show his support, but he was met with a refusal, as revealed by the Israeli press on October 16th. "It's not the right time," he was reportedly told, another sign of Israel's extreme caution towards Moscow. In Ukraine, this refusal has caused deep confusion.

title: "A perfect storm is brewing in the war in Ukraine: wherever you look, negative trends are converging."
lead: Militarily, diplomatically, and economically, Ukraine and its allies are facing a difficult phase that Russia is taking advantage of, analyzes Sylvie Kauffmann, an editorialist at "Le Monde," in her column.
text_eng: If the West cannot win the war in Ukraine, then what war can it win? Good question! This is posed by the Ukrainian Foreign Minister, Dmytro Kuleba, in an interview with the German newspaper Die Welt, published on Friday, November 10. The interest of the question, in fact, lies not so much in the answer it should evoke, but in the fact that it is being asked today, after twenty months of war. It implies that the hypothesis of Ukraine's defeat, despite Western support, is not only considered but plausible. Destructive and deadly, this war, launched by Russia against Ukraine on February 24, 2022, is geographically confined, but it has challenged the continent's security order; the defense of the invaded country is therefore presented as existential by European leaders. However, the conflict is currently at an impasse and clouds are gathering, to the point that what Americans call a "perfect storm" is looming: a particularly serious crisis caused by the convergence of several unfavorable phenomena. Wherever one looks, indeed, the negative trends for Ukraine and its allies converge. On the military front, the situation is stagnant. In an interview with the British magazine The Economist that made a big impact, the chief of staff of the Ukrainian army, General Valeri Zaloujny, admitted that the counter-offensive led by his forces for the past five months "will not result in a beautiful and deep breakthrough." "As in the First World War," he says, "we have reached a technological level that leads us into a deadlock." He has lost hope of pushing Russia back by inflicting massive losses on them: "That was my mistake. Russia has lost 150,000 men. In any other country, this level of losses would have ended the war." Extensive Russian recruitment However, while Russia has apparently abandoned mobilizing civilians due to its unpopularity in September 2022, it manages to regenerate its forces by recruiting massively for its professional army and focuses the bulk of human effort on the war in Ukraine. "At this moment, Norway could take Murmansk with police forces because the north of Russia is so thinly populated," quips a Scandinavian expert. The size of the Ukrainian population, which is also suffering heavy losses, does not allow for recruitment on such a scale; there are even protests in Ukraine these days from soldiers' wives who have not had leave for eighteen months on the front lines. Compensating for the lack of numbers with the power of weaponry is another challenge, both in quantity and quality. All of Russia's industrial capabilities are being called upon to contribute to the defense industry to produce ammunition twenty-four hours a day. Russia has shifted to a war economy mode. In Europe, some have talked about it, but the actual implementation is still pending. The promising commitment to deliver one million units of ammunition to Ukraine by March 2024 has only materialized so far with the production of 300,000 units. The European Union's foreign policy chief, Josep Borrell, emphasizes that the European arms industry is indeed producing, but not enough and primarily for its export clients. Unlike the Kremlin's control over the Russian defense industry, the large private arms groups in Europe are not requisitioned. As for the "technological leap" that General Zaloujny is advocating for, it requires a strategic decision: since the start of the war, Western countries, preoccupied with avoiding an escalation that would draw them too far in, carefully calibrate each level of sophistication in the aid they belatedly provide to the Ukrainian army. Embarrassment for Western leaders On the diplomatic front, the outbreak of war between Israel and Hamas has diverted the attention of the United States, which was forced to reinvest in the Middle East after being compelled to do so in Europe due to Russian aggression. Ukraine fears being in competition with Israel for American arms and ammunition supply. The global public opinion mobilization against the treatment of the civilian population in Gaza by the scale of the Israeli response to Hamas's terrorist attacks on October 7 embarrasses Western leaders. Inevitably, Russia takes advantage of this embarrassment to discredit their support for Ukraine. Finally, the inefficiency of the decision made nearly a year ago by the G7 and the EU to cap the price of Russian oil transported by sea at $60 (55 euros) to reduce Russia's revenues is now evident. The circumvention of this sanction is so extensive that in October, Russia's oil revenues increased by a quarter compared to October 2022, thanks in particular to the organization of "ghost fleets" of tankers. Russian propagandists promote a triumphant narrative. Far from collapsing, they claim their economy is perfectly resilient. They exploit the differences between President Zelensky and his chief of staff. The Prigojine affair and the challenge posed to Putin by the former head of Wagner are just a bad memory, buried in the smoking ruins of his plane: Putin will be triumphantly reelected in March 2024. Who would doubt that? All that is missing is the return of Donald Trump to the White House to unleash the "perfect storm." The time has come for Europeans, instead of wringing their hands at this thought, to plug the leaks and seriously fortify their house, as they prepare to open it to Ukraine and Moldova: a few solid additional beams will not be too much.

title: Four Ukrainian children repatriated thanks to Qatar's mediation.
lead: The emirate has managed to reach an agreement with Russia, where nearly 20,000 children are said to have been deported illegally since the beginning of the invasion.
text_eng: Four Ukrainian children are going to be reunited with their families in Ukraine. On Monday, October 16, in the morning, the Ministry of Foreign Affairs of Qatar announced that they had reached an agreement with Russia to repatriate these children who had been living in the territory of the Russian Federation since the beginning of the Russian invasion on February 24, 2022. Lolwah Al-Khater, Minister of State for Foreign Affairs, expressed gratitude to Russia and Ukraine for their commitment to the successful reunification of the Ukrainian children with their families. She stated that Qatar will continue its mediation efforts in this and other contexts, in line with its principles of conflict resolution and peace restoration. The return of these "three boys aged 6, 3, and 9, and a 17-year-old girl," according to Daria Herasymchuk, advisor on children's rights to Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky, marks the first success of Doha since it engaged as a mediator between the two enemies in this particularly sensitive issue. The first public signs of Qatar's involvement in the deported children issue date back to the summer of 2023. In June, Prime Minister Sheikh Mohammed Ben Abderrahmane Al Thani visited Moscow and met with Russian President Vladimir Putin. A month later, he traveled to Kyiv to meet with President Volodymyr Zelensky and promised the delivery of a humanitarian aid package worth $100 million. Following this visit, Ukrainian Prime Minister Denys Chmyhal thanked Qatar "for its willingness to undertake mediation efforts for the return of Ukrainian children illegally abducted by Russia and still held on its territory." The return of these four children who were in Russia or in occupied territories at the beginning of the invasion represents a diplomatic success for Qatar. Due to the complexity of these returns, only 386 children have been repatriated to Ukraine, either through Ukrainian non-governmental organizations or because families took the risk of going directly to Russia, according to the Children of War portal. The authorities in Kyiv have documented 19,546 children who were illegally deported to the Russian Federation or occupied territories. While this practice has existed since the start of the Donbass war in 2014, it has become more widespread since the beginning of the February 2022 invasion. Various methods have been documented by human rights organizations, including Russian soldiers deporting children from areas under Russian control. Ukrainian families living in these same territories were forced to entrust their children to schools or summer camps for several weeks, only to never see them again. These children ended up in orphanages, Russian foster families, or other institutions scattered across regions of the Russian Federation. Moscow has made the assimilation of Ukrainian children a true policy. As early as May 2022, Vladimir Putin signed a decree to facilitate the adoption of children arriving from the war by Russian families. In November 2022, in a report on these transfers, Amnesty International condemned this law which allows for "facilitating the absorption of children (...) into Russian society in a way that fundamentally denies their rights to choose and preserve their nationality." Adoption by Russian families further complicates the efforts of Ukrainian families and authorities to locate the children. According to Ukrainian human rights organizations, in addition to receiving a Russian passport, the children's name, date, and place of birth are deliberately changed to erase their traces. Furthermore, Kyiv accuses Russia of pursuing a policy of "Russification" of the children, aiming to erase their identity, even going so far as to "re-educate" them to make them hate their country of origin. In March, the Russian president and Moscow's children's rights commissioner, Maria Lvova-Belova, who proudly adopted a child from Mariupol, became the first actors in the war to be targeted by an arrest warrant from the International Criminal Court in The Hague.

title: Wang Yi, the Chinese Foreign Minister, will travel to the United States at the end of the week.
lead: His meeting with his American counterpart, Antony Blinken - or even with Joe Biden - could foreshadow a visit by Chinese President Xi Jinping to the United States by the end of the year, at a time when there are many points of friction between the two powers.
text_eng: The U.S. Secretary of State, Antony Blinken, will meet with his Chinese counterpart, Wang Yi, at the end of the week in Washington, as announced by senior U.S. officials on Monday, October 23. This visit, scheduled from Thursday the 26th to Saturday the 28th, comes in the wake of multiple contacts made in recent months between the Americans and the Chinese, who are striving to "manage tensions between the two great powers in a responsible manner." The Sino-Russian rapprochement, the war in Ukraine, and especially the conflict between Israel and the Palestinian Islamist movement Hamas are expected to be prominent topics of discussion, at a time when the international community is concerned about the conflict spreading in the Middle East. Beijing strongly criticized the United States' decision last week to block a United Nations resolution calling for a "humanitarian pause" between Hamas and the Israeli state. For Washington, the meeting with Wang Yi will be an opportunity to encourage China to take "a more constructive approach" in these two conflicts, and beyond, said a senior U.S. official speaking on condition of anonymity. "Maintaining open channels of communication" There are numerous points of friction. From Taiwan, which China claims as part of its territory, to Beijing's activities in the South China Sea, as evidenced by the latest incident - a collision between Chinese and Philippine ships - which Washington strongly condemned. China has also drawn closer to Russia under Vladimir Putin and is striving to expand its influence in the world, including in the Middle East. The goal is to "continue our efforts to maintain open channels of communication" with Beijing and to "manage our competition responsibly," said another senior U.S. official to journalists when announcing the visit. No details have been provided about the specific agenda of Wang Yi's visit or whether he will be received by U.S. President Joe Biden. During his visit to Beijing in June, Secretary of State Antony Blinken was received by Chinese President Xi Jinping. The visit could, in any case, help prepare for the visit of Chinese President Xi Jinping to the United States, possibly on the sidelines of the upcoming APEC (Asia-Pacific Economic Cooperation) summit in San Francisco, California, in mid-November. Joe Biden has expressed his "hope" for a meeting before the end of the year, as the last face-to-face meeting with his Chinese counterpart dates back to the G20 summit in Bali, Indonesia, in November 2022. Tensions in the relationship The relationship between Beijing and Washington is marked by tensions, as seen in the incident earlier this year when a Chinese balloon flew over U.S. territory. In June, Joe Biden upset Beijing by stating that Xi Jinping belonged to the category of "dictators." The United States is emphasizing the strengthening of their alliances in Asia, which Beijing considers to be nothing other than an intention of "containment" and "encirclement" of China. In recent months, the United States hosted Indian Prime Minister Narendra Modi for a state visit in Washington, held a trilateral summit at Camp David (Maryland) with the leaders of Japan and South Korea, strengthened defense alliances with the Philippines and Pacific islands. President Biden also made a historic visit to Vietnam and will host Australian Prime Minister Anthony Albanese in a grand ceremony at the White House this Wednesday. Joe Biden, who described America as a "beacon" of democratic aspirations in the world, is also requesting an additional $7.4 billion (7 billion euros) from the U.S. Congress to counter China militarily and economically.

title: Russia: Detention of Russian-American journalist Alsu Kurmasheva extended at least until October 23.
lead: The journalist working for the American media Radio Free Europe/Radio Liberty was arrested in the Russian city of Kazan on Wednesday. She is accused of not declaring herself as a "foreign agent," a charge for which she could face up to five years in prison.
text_eng: She has become the second American reporter detained in Russia. Arrested this week, the Russian-American journalist Alsu Kurmasheva had her temporary detention extended at least until October 23, during a hearing at the Sovetskiy Court in Kazan, in the Russian region of Tatarstan, on Friday, October 20. "By decision of the court on October 20, 2023, the detention period of A. Kurmasheva is extended by seventy-two hours," the court announced. The journalist working for the American media Radio Free Europe/Radio Liberty (RFE/RL) was arrested in the Russian city of Kazan on Wednesday. She is accused of not declaring herself as a "foreign agent." Ms. Kurmasheva was present at her hearing on Friday, in the defendant's cage, wearing an FFP2 mask. She was escorted there by hooded agents, as observed by journalists from Agence France-Presse. Up to five years in prison could be faced. Russia is accused of conducting a campaign of repression against independent media, NGOs, journalists, lawyers, and opponents since the beginning of its invasion of Ukraine in February 2022. While many activists and Russian journalists have fled the country, others - famous or unknown - have been imprisoned. Several Americans are also detained, including journalist Evan Gershkovich who is still in detention, arrested for espionage by the Russian security services (FSB) while reporting in Yekaterinburg on March 29. Russian authorities deny carrying out any form of "persecution" against US citizens. "There are American citizens who violate laws and appropriate measures are taken against them," said Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov on Friday when asked about the issue, adding that he was not following Ms. Kurmasheva's case. The Sovetskiy Court in Kazan stated that the American media journalist was being prosecuted for failing to register as a "foreign agent," while engaging in "intentional collection of information regarding military activities" that could be damaging to "the security of the Russian Federation." She faces up to five years in prison. This status of "foreign agent," reminiscent of the Soviet term "enemy of the people," imposes heavy administrative constraints and financial control on the targeted individuals or entities. It also requires that any publication, including on social media, be accompanied by this label. Some of the most respected critics of Russian President Vladimir Putin, such as Nobel Peace Prize laureate and editor-in-chief of the independent newspaper Novaya Gazeta, Dmitry Muratov, are among these "agents." Mrs. Kurmasheva's passports have been confiscated since May. She usually resides in Prague with her husband and children, but she traveled to Russia for a "family emergency" on May 20 and was unable to leave because her American and Russian passports were confiscated. According to the website Tatar Inform, she was fined on October 11 for not declaring her American citizenship to Russian authorities. The website, citing anonymous police sources, reported that she had been involved in mobilizing teachers for the Russian army's offensive in Ukraine. Alsu Kurmasheva, who joined RFE/RL in 1998, works for the service in Tatar and Bashkir languages, covering these ethnic minorities in Russia, particularly in Tatarstan and Bashkortostan, regions in the Volga and Ural areas. Based in Prague, RFE/RL is funded by the U.S. Congress and was founded during the Cold War to counter Soviet propaganda in Eastern Bloc countries. The media outlet still publishes content in multiple languages, often sensitive in countries ruled by authoritarian regimes. "I came to work for [RFE/RL] because this media outlet is important to me, its mission of providing objective information to my people, the people who speak my language, Tatar, in particular," Mrs. Kurmasheva explained in 2014. In recent years, several American citizens arrested in Russia have been released after prisoner exchanges with Washington.

title: Electricity: between France and Germany, a compromise under high tension
lead: While Paris and Berlin have finally agreed on the method of setting the price of a megawatt-hour at the Hamburg summit on October 17, the two countries continue to defend two completely contradictory energy models.
text_eng: For months, unprecedented tensions had been brewing between France and Germany over the reform of the European electricity market. For the past ten years, the two countries had been touting the virtues of their respective energy models: a strong nuclear base for Paris, and a mix of fossil and renewable energies for Berlin. This issue had become the main stumbling block in the Franco-German relationship. This was all the more unjustified considering that both nations were supposed to jointly pursue three objectives: prevent price spikes, strengthen industrial competitiveness, and decarbonize their economies. At the summit in Hamburg on October 9 and 10, Emmanuel Macron and Olaf Scholz reopened the issue without reaching a conclusion, although the French president and the German chancellor remained optimistic about the future. The final resolution came on Tuesday, October 17, in Luxembourg, where the energy ministers of the twenty-seven member states finally reached a compromise on the electricity market reform, now subject to approval by the Members of the European Parliament. The Elysée, which had been advocating for nuclear energy to be considered in the new pricing mechanism, declared victory. Germany also welcomed the outcome, as Brussels will ensure that support for EDF and the modernization of its 56 reactors do not give France a competitive advantage contrary to European competition laws. A historic conflict that Germany had feared, given its recession and the challenges faced by its industry due to the loss of cheap Russian gas, competition from American companies subsidized by the Inflation Reduction Act (IRA) to green their activities, and the threat from Chinese electric vehicle manufacturers. The immediate issue was the market configuration rather than the price of electricity for consumers, whether fossil, nuclear, or renewable, according to the negotiators. Does the Luxembourg agreement put an end to the longstanding Franco-German conflict over energy models? Berlin's transition initiated in 2001 did not fully deliver on its promises of decarbonizing electricity, despite remarkable developments in wind, solar, and hydrogen energy. While France is not exemplary in overall greenhouse gas emissions, its electricity production is seven times less "polluting" than that of its neighbor, which has had to rely on coal to compensate for the definitive shutdown of nuclear power. This is a crucial advantage in light of a projected 50% increase in electricity consumption by 2050. In February, Paris achieved a partial victory when Berlin finally agreed to include nuclear energy in the European taxonomy of decarbonized energies, making it easier to access public and private funding. The President of the European Commission, Ursula von der Leyen, could not help but qualify the success by stating that this energy is "important" for decarbonization but "not strategic." Germany will remain the spearhead of the anti-nuclear camp in Europe, led by its Vice-Chancellor and Minister of the Economy, the ecologist Robert Habeck. The crisis has its roots in history. Since 1945, France has chosen military and civilian nuclear power, driven by its armed forces, the Atomic Energy Commission, and EDF. They support an entire industrial sector and benefit from tacit public support. Nuclear power is in the national DNA, a unique situation in the world, a source of pride, despite the setbacks in the industry over the past decade. After years of uncertainty about its importance, Emmanuel Macron has revived it. He decided to extend the lifespan of nuclear power plants as long as they are deemed safe by the Nuclear Safety Authority, announced six EPR 2 reactors by 2035-2040, and passed a law to accelerate their construction. "The German obsession: destroying EDF" Nothing of the sort in Germany. Germany embraced civilian nuclear power in the 1970s, without enthusiasm, and it never exceeded 22% of electricity production (compared to 75% in France). For thirty years, some dreamed of an "Airbus of energy," symbolized by the EPR reactor from Framatome-Siemens. The Greens, on the other hand, fought against it under the banner "Atomkraft? Nein Danke!" ("nuclear power? no thanks!"), combining ecological and pacifist concerns. Coming to power in 1998, the SPD-Greens coalition (an alliance between the Social Democratic Party of Germany and the Greens) began to unravel Franco-German cooperation. Before Chancellor Gerhard Schröder chose Russian gas from his "friend" Vladimir Putin. A strategy continued by Angela Merkel that led her country into a perilous dependence. The first rupture was confirmed with Siemens' withdrawal from the EPR in 2009. It became definitive with the decision to phase out nuclear power after the Fukushima accident in 2011. France, on the other hand, has a powerful pro-nuclear lobby that cries foul of a German conspiracy, convinced that Germany refuses to have a neighboring country with a "competitive advantage like EDF," as stated by its former CEO (2009-2014), Henri Proglio: "For twenty-five or thirty years, the German obsession has been to destroy EDF. They have succeeded." There is some truth in this accusation, illustrated by the obligation to sell a portion of its nuclear electricity at a loss to alternative suppliers. But this absolves the group of its responsibility in the mismanagement of nuclear power and supports the idea that Paris has no voice in Brussels, where Berlin dictates terms. EDF must adapt to the new situation in the difficult negotiation with the state shareholder on the selling price of its nuclear production. With Brussels acting as a peacekeeper to ensure the "sincerity" of a price that should not greatly exceed the company's production costs, the Elysée admits. And Berlin as a vigilant censor of any violation of the Luxembourg compromise.

title: In Montenegro, a surprising coalition with pro-Russians "to lead the country towards the EU"
lead: Appointed as prime minister on Tuesday, Milojko Spajic has allied himself with Serbian parties that are rather hostile towards the West. The former Goldman Sachs banker is embracing this geopolitical balancing act by stating that his government's program has been accepted by all.
text_eng: He wants to transform his small country, mostly known for its formidable mafias, "into the Switzerland of the Balkans" and "the Singapore of Europe": Milojko Spajic, 37 years old, was appointed as Prime Minister of Montenegro on Tuesday, October 31, after five long months of negotiations. This former Goldman Sachs banker had come in first with 25.5% of the votes in the legislative elections on June 11 in this country of 600,000 inhabitants, which had been led for three decades by the same man, former President Milo Djukanovic. Leading the young centrist party Europe Now!, Mr. Spajic's main program is to turn the page on Djukanovic, who was defeated in April in the presidential election, and to quickly lead Montenegro towards European Union (EU) membership. Although this coastal state on the Adriatic Sea is considered the most advanced of the six Balkan countries still outside the EU (Serbia, Kosovo, Albania, Macedonia, and Bosnia), it has been stuck in the status of "candidate" for thirteen years, mainly due to its inability to establish a functional judicial system. "Montenegro has a pro-Euro-Atlantic government and a stable political majority that will help close the most important negotiation chapters and accelerate the necessary reforms," promised Mr. Spajic, who began his term by meeting with the President of the European Commission, Ursula von der Leyen, who is currently touring the Balkans. Returning to Montenegro in 2020 after starting his finance career in Asia, Mr. Spajic aims to promote press freedom and fight against endemic corruption. Developing the economy, however, is his main promise, with plans to raise the average salary to 1,000 euros, even if it means implementing ultra-liberal measures - hence Mr. Spajic's strong interest in cryptocurrencies and the comparison with Singapore. With only 24 out of 81 seats in the Parliament of Podgorica, the new Prime Minister has chosen to form a majority by forming an alliance with the various Serbian parties in the country, where a significant portion of the population still holds nostalgia for Belgrade and openly opposes the West. Known for his admiration for Russian President Vladimir Putin, his refusal to recognize Kosovo's independence, and the denial of the Srebrenica genocide during the war in Bosnia-Herzegovina in 1995, Andrija Mandic, leader of the Serbian New Democracy party, was appointed as President of the Parliament on Tuesday and is expected to join the government in a year. Mr. Spajic justified this geopolitical balancing act by stating that the pro-Serbian parties had accepted the government program, which explicitly includes "strengthening cooperation within NATO" and ensuring that "Montenegro will be the next EU member." Overcoming historical divisions. While they did indeed sign the government roadmap, the leaders of the Serbian parties have never publicly renounced their pro-Russian positions. During an interview with Le Monde in March, Mr. Spajic, however, believed that a compromise with them was justified by the need to overcome Montenegrin historical divisions and the duty to oust Milo Djukanovic, who is pro-Western but has allowed the mafia to proliferate in his country. This compromise, however, was poorly received in the United States. "The reality is that the participation in the government of groups or leaders who refuse to condemn Russian aggression, defend the rights of Ukraine, support EU sanctions against Russia, and respect the sovereignty of neighboring countries like Kosovo significantly hinders Montenegro's efforts to achieve its European aspirations," criticized the U.S. Ambassador to Podgorica, Judy Rising Reinke. The EU has been more cautious so far, with Mrs. von der Leyen stating only on Tuesday that it is "up to the Montenegrins to choose who their leaders are" and that the messages they have sent so far are "encouraging."

title: How Benjamin Netanyahu became the self-proclaimed leader of the "civilized world"
lead: The "war on terrorism," which Netanyahu has tirelessly preached for four decades, is imposing its ideological mechanisms on Western democracies.
text_eng: In the beginning was Jonathan. Benyamin Netanyahu's older brother was indeed killed in 1976 while leading an Israeli hostage rescue raid at the Ugandan airport of Entebbe. An institute named after Jonathan was founded in his memory three years later in Jerusalem by Benyamin and his father, Benzion Netanyahu, an ideologue of the Irgun (literally "The Organization," responsible for numerous attacks in Palestine from 1937 to 1948, including massacres of Arab civilians). This institute identifies terrorism, naturally Arab, as the ultimate evil and associates it with the Soviet threat, against which Netanyahu proposes "an anti-terror alliance of all Western democracies." Such ideas were adopted by Ronald Reagan and his administration from 1981 to 1989 in the name of a "new Cold War" against the "evil empire" of the USSR, to which, in this binary vision, the Palestine Liberation Organization (PLO) of Yasser Arafat would be affiliated. From Beirut to Baghdad, François Mitterrand, during the first state visit of a French president to Israel in 1982, mentioned both the "fighters" of the PLO and the prospect of a Palestinian state. Shortly after, during the Israeli invasion of Lebanon, he did everything to prevent the long siege of West Beirut from turning into street battles, which Israel would have undoubtedly won, but at an exorbitant cost. France, on the contrary, supervised the evacuation of Arafat and his fedayeen, convinced that it would preserve an interlocutor for Israel when the time came. However, it was only after the PLO recognized Israel that Arafat was received by Mitterrand at the Elysée in 1989. Four years later, Arafat signed the Oslo peace accords with Israeli Prime Minister Yitzhak Rabin, which Netanyahu, now the opposition leader, condemned as an intolerable concession to "terror." Regardless of the disappearance of the USSR, he stigmatized the PLO as the head of a terrorist hydra to be fought relentlessly. Netanyahu appeared at rallies where slogans like "Rabin, Arafat's dog" and even "Death to Rabin" were chanted. When Rabin was assassinated in 1995, albeit by a terrorist, but a Jewish and Israeli one, Netanyahu believed his political career was over. However, he quickly resumed hammering the mantra of "terror," inevitably Arab and anti-Western, managing to narrowly win the election in 1996 to head the government. During his first three years in power, he systematically undermined the Oslo accords. As a mere member of parliament again, he saw the rhetoric of the Jonathan Institute triumph with the attacks of September 11, 2001, and George W. Bush's "global war on terror." In 2002, Netanyahu mobilized in Washington to equate the PLO with Al-Qaeda while contributing to the misinformation campaign about weapons of mass destruction in Iraq. Jacques Chirac then denounces the blindness of an offensive to overthrow Saddam Hussein that would not prepare for "the day after." France thus avoids the disastrous invasion of Iraq leading to a globalized confrontation between Islam and the West. As for Netanyahu, in 2006, he prefers to celebrate the sixtieth anniversary of the Irgun bombing at the King David Hotel in Jerusalem, which killed ninety-one people, including forty-one Arabs, twenty-eight British, and seventeen Jews. The plaque placed on this occasion is probably the only one in the world to honor the perpetrators of a bombing rather than their victims. The Gaza abyss Nicolas Sarkozy organizes an international conference in Paris in 2007 to finance the Palestinian state, before stating that "Gaza cannot continue to be the largest open-air prison in the world." In 2011, during a private conversation with the American president, Barack Obama, he even calls Netanyahu a "liar," who had returned to power two years earlier. It is true that the Israeli prime minister continues to invoke the terrorist threat, not only to reinforce the blockade of Gaza but also to discredit Mahmoud Abbas, Arafat's successor, and thus continue the colonization of the West Bank. France, hit in January 2015 by a series of jihadist attacks, still refuses to make the same connections as Netanyahu. While the Israeli prime minister attends the solidarity march on January 11, 2015, François Hollande reacts by also inviting the Palestinian president. Netanyahu declares on this occasion "to all the Jews of France" that "Israel is your home," forcing Prime Minister Manuel Valls to solemnly remind that "France without the Jews of France is no longer France." Ousted from power in 2021 after four elections in two years, Netanyahu returns as prime minister in December 2022, with the support of the supremacist far-right, to whom he entrusts key ministerial positions. Still facing triple charges of corruption, fraud, and breach of trust, he launches a judicial reform, described as a "coup" by his opponents, who take to the streets in protest by the hundreds of thousands. Washington and Berlin have harsh words to denounce this attempt to undermine the judiciary's checks and balances. However, the horror of the terrorist campaign on October 7 transforms Netanyahu into a self-proclaimed leader of the "civilized world" against a Hamas equated with the Islamic State organization. As he evades his heavy responsibility in the initial collapse of Israel's security and as the families of hostages accuse him of not giving top priority to the release of their loved ones, Benjamin Netanyahu recycles his slogans from the Jonathan Institute to impose the sole goal of eradicating Hamas, without having any more vision of the "day after" in Gaza than the Bush administration had in Iraq in 2003. Western leaders who succeed each other in Israel to show active solidarity with Netanyahu should nevertheless remember that the true friends of the Israeli prime minister are, and have long been, Donald Trump, Viktor Orban, and, of course, Vladimir Putin, the clear winner at this stage of the crisis in Gaza. It is, in fact, to appease the master of the Kremlin that Netanyahu refused the solidarity visit of Volodymyr Zelensky to Israel. This obviously does not bother the leaders of the "civilized world," who are ready to fall into the trap set by Hamas in Gaza with Benjamin Netanyahu.

title: Jean Lopez, historian: "There has been nothing comparable to Ukraine since 1945, except for the war between Iran and Iraq in 1980."
lead: After a year and a half of conflict in Ukraine and far from the usual explanations about these battles, the military historian dissects, in an interview with "Le Monde", how we have reached the current situation of fierce trench warfare on a front stretching 1,000 kilometers.
text_eng: Historian and journalist, Jean Lopez is the editor-in-chief of the magazine Guerres & Histoire, which he founded in 2011. He has notably published, with Benoist Bihan, Conduire la guerre. Entretiens sur l’art opératif (Perrin, 2023), and L’Ours et le Renard, with Michel Goya (Perrin, 2023). You have published a book titled L’Ours et le Renard, on the war in Ukraine, with Michel Goya. Why this title? The bear, as one might guess, represents the Russians, who were thought to have the numbers and strength in a war; and the fox symbolizes the Ukrainians, who were assumed to rely on flexibility and agility. However, things unfolded differently. The large battalions were not on the Russian side but on the side of Kiev. Russia attacked Ukraine on February 24, 2022, with the equivalent of one hundred twenty to one hundred thirty battalions, around 130,000 soldiers from the army, while the Ukrainians had 500,000 to 600,000 soldiers engaged. On the other hand, the quantity of war material was on the Russian side, organized in small units with equipment that was not necessarily top-notch. In contrast, Ukraine fielded equipment that was often newer, more modern, and from a much more diverse range of sources, such as Turkish drones. Can we describe the Russian offensive on Kiev from Belarus as a blitzkrieg? I wouldn't use the term "blitzkrieg." I have the impression that the Russians wanted to replicate what they achieved in Afghanistan by assassinating the Afghan president on Christmas 1979, that is, a swift operation with the landing of a brigade at the Hostomel airfield near Kiev, supported by the infiltration of commandos to try to politically decapitate Ukraine. Once this task was completed, it would pave the way for mechanized forces to take control of a headless country. However, things did not unfold as planned. The Russian columns were ill-prepared, they only knew their objectives at the last moment, and many did not have maps. The Russians had successfully executed their offensive in Crimea in 2014, where, without firing a shot, they managed to seize the Autonomous Republic of Crimea with two or three brigades facing fifteen thousand astonished Ukrainian soldiers. The Ukrainian army failed in 2014. It is now known that it was heavily infiltrated by Russian intelligence services, the FSB, through Ukrainian intelligence. In 2022, the Russians envisioned a country without a backbone, infiltrated by their propaganda, assuming sympathies towards Russia in the east. None of this materialized. Even where the Russian-speaking population was the majority, the Russians received no support. In Kharkiv, it is striking: this predominantly Russian-speaking city resisted the attacker. It was undoubtedly a very unpleasant surprise. After the failure of the attack on Kiev, why did the Russians retreat in good order to the natural border of the Dnieper River, and the Donbass region which they already partially occupied? When the Russians realized they couldn't take Kiev, they couldn't even capture Chernihiv, which controls the Northeastern route, or Kharkiv. They then decided to retreat to the east in good order, and the Ukrainians did not pursue them, either due to a lack of reactivity or exhaustion. In the south, the opposite scenario unfolded: everything went as Russia had planned. Crimea is a natural fortress with few access points, an 8-kilometer-wide bottleneck, and the Russians managed to pass through. Two armies spread out in the south of Ukraine towards the east, along the coast of the Sea of Azov, advancing up to Mariupol, connecting with the Donbass region occupied since 2014, establishing territorial continuity. This is the essential victory for the Russians in this war. It is becoming clear what Vladimir Putin has mentioned several times: the return to the lands conquered by Empress Catherine - Catherine the Great - in the 18th century from the Ottomans, essentially the strip of territory along the Black Sea. The intention was clear: to cut off Ukraine from the sea, turning it into a landlocked state dependent on Russia economically. After managing to cross the Dnieper by seizing the Kherson bridge that should never have been taken intact, they crossed. They occupy Kherson and advance towards Odessa. However, there is an obstacle: Mykolaiv, the major shipyard on the Black Sea coast. They fail and retreat, securing a large bridgehead west of the Dnieper. This war is a mix of classical warfare and modern conflict with drones and sophisticated missiles... completely different from asymmetric wars where a state's armed force faces materially weak fighters... It is evident that the Russians are pursuing an imperial objective reminiscent of the 19th century. In the way they conduct the battle, it feels more like the 20th century, with the massive use of armored units and artillery. Additionally, there are elements of the 21st century with drones dominating the sky, satellites, electronic warfare to disrupt enemy communications, and the intensive use of missiles. Currently, the fighting resembles the 20th century. The Russians have entrenched themselves, mining 100,000 square kilometers. There are three lines of defense, buried bunkers. Simultaneously, the front is covered by electronic warfare, drones, and missiles capable of dispersing thousands of mines. This is nothing like asymmetric wars, such as the one in Iraq. In Ukraine, the fighting is fierce, and the level of casualties is terrible. Since 1945, there has been nothing comparable, except for the war between Iran and Iraq in 1980, which lasted eight years and resulted in a million deaths. Observers described a demoralized Russian army, lacking ammunition, on the verge of collapse. It seems that this is not the case... During the attempt to take Kiev, images showed Russian armored vehicles stopped without fuel or destroyed by Ukrainian infantry. What was the command of this army doing? The only major city the Russians have taken is Kherson. In Mariupol, it was like Stalingrad, with almost total devastation. Ukrainian propaganda depicted a Russian giant battered by blows. And in September 2022, the Ukrainians achieved a magnificent feat southeast of Kharkiv with their surprise counteroffensive, where they regained 20,000 to 30,000 square kilometers, and there we saw the retreat of an enemy brigade. Indeed, the Ukrainians advanced 20 to 30 kilometers, then everything stopped. The Russians took advantage, they rebuilt a very strong front, adapting the type of warfare they conduct to the type of soldiers they have. So far, it holds, on a front that is 1,000 kilometers long, which is huge. It has been four months since the Ukrainians have been trying to break through this defensive line. In some places, they have breached the first line, the second is already compromised, but we know that the Russians are building a fourth. This is not an indication of an army disintegrating at the moment. The Russians lack ammunition, that's true, but so do the Ukrainians. Is the privatization of war, on both the Ukrainian and Russian sides, an important factor in this conflict? In Afghanistan or Iraq, the Americans privatized part of the logistics to secure a road axis, escort trucks or personalities. It was about support, not combat. In Ukraine, it's different. Alongside the national army, there are units, among the most combative, like the Azov Battalion, for example, funded by local initiatives or oligarchs. This has been happening since 2014, even though, under Western influence, the Ukrainian army is becoming more national. It's quite similar with the Russians. Vladimir Putin allowed the development of armed forces that are like fiefdoms. Alongside the traditional army, the Wagner Group was assigned tasks in which the Kremlin did not want to be seen in Africa or the Middle East, and then in Ukraine. There are other sectors of the armed forces in Russia that depend on oligarchs: Gazprom has its brigade, the Chechens have their brigade. The Russian National Guard is under the command of the President of the Russian Federation and not the armed forces. In Russia, the sharing of the monopoly of state violence was organized by the Kremlin, but after the attempted "coup" by Yevgeny Prigozhin, the leader of Wagner, on June 24, 2023, and his elimination two months later, there is clearly a reassertion of control by the federal army. In this war, which is both conventional and technological, did foreign arms deliveries, especially American ones, have a decisive influence? Initially, no. The battle of Kiev was won through the mobilization of the Ukrainian population and soldiers, with equipment that was 90% Soviet and 10% Western. In Washington, London, or Paris, little was expected from the Ukrainians. But when Kiev's allies realized that the Russians had significant weaknesses and that the Ukrainians were eager to fight, they began to provide them with what they lacked. So they received a variety of weapons, especially those that made a difference, such as German tracked artillery, American Himars howitzers and rocket launchers, and French Caesar cannons. These weapons outperform Russian equipment thanks to their guided shells. This allowed the Ukrainians to target Russian logistics, forcing them to move their equipment landing points 500 kilometers back. This qualitative advantage helped the Ukrainians somewhat rebalance the situation starting from June 2022. Kiev also received an effective medical infrastructure, which is crucial for the morale of Ukrainian soldiers. Western allies must deliver more tanks, aircraft, and long-range missiles. Can these weapons change the course of the war? I don't think that a handful of F-16 aircraft – around sixty to seventy planes – will enable the Ukrainians to regain control of the air. The arrival of German Leopard tanks and American Abrams tanks will boost certain brigades, but how many? Four, five, six out of a hundred... All discussions revolve around a more regular supply of ammunition; Volodymyr Zelensky demands shells, more and more shells, and breaching equipment. These Cold War-era materials are capable of penetrating Russian defenses. They include bridge-laying tanks, equipment for digging trenches, and mine-clearing vehicles. Kiev's allies have very few of these sophisticated and expensive materials, and it is very difficult to deliver a large quantity of them. Ukraine's allies have made tremendous efforts, but it is not enough. (C) XEMARTIN LABORDE, VICTORIA DENYS, AND DELPHINE PAPIN

title: "Between China and Russia, relations have always been marked by deep distrust."
lead: During the Cold War, the USSR was ahead of China in the fight against the capitalist bloc, recalls diplomat Pierre Andrieu. Today, Beijing is rivaling with Washington, leaving Moscow far behind in the competition of powers, and maintaining a certain distance from Russia since its invasion of Ukraine.
text_eng: Vladimir Putin and Xi Jinping like to describe the relationship between their two countries as a "comprehensive strategic partnership for a new era, not excluding any area of cooperation." The closeness between the two leaders established through more than forty meetings, their strong ideological and political connivance, anti-American and anti-Western solidarity, as well as the good performance of economic relations could perfectly justify this designation. However, these relations have never been completely peaceful throughout their long history. Since the very first contacts established in the 17th century, periods of open or silent hostility have always been longer than periods of friendship and brotherhood. And in all cases, a deep distrust has always marked their relations. The first period of open hostility lasted more than fifty years, between 1860 and 1911, the year of the fall of the Qing dynasty. The Russian Empire had actively participated in the "break up of China," the rush towards China, along with the European colonial powers and Japan. St. Petersburg had indeed been the biggest beneficiary by seizing, under the "unequal treaties," nearly 2 million square kilometers. The memory of these spoliations, even if put aside by the current Chinese authority, is still present in the memory of the Chinese. The second period of frank hostility took place between the 1960s and 1980s, for more than twenty years, when relations between the two communist powers deteriorated to the point of bringing them to the brink of nuclear war. The main reason was Mao's claim to wrest the leadership of the international communist movement from the Soviet leaders who succeeded Stalin. On the other hand, it is possible to describe as "bittersweet" the period beginning with the Bolshevik revolution of 1917 - "the volcanic event that illuminated the consciousness of the Chinese elite," according to François Fejtö, the eminent historian of the international communist movement, and ending with the founding of the People's Republic of China (PRC) by Mao in 1949. Certainly, the Soviet Union of Lenin and Stalin had strongly supported the Chinese "anti-imperialist" movement throughout these thirty-two years. Not wanting to "put all its eggs in one basket," Moscow had then supported, either alternately or simultaneously, Mao Zedong's Chinese Communist Party and Chiang Kai-shek's Guomindang. However, its support, never disinterested, had always been in the service of Soviet interests, notably through the Comintern. Above all, the USSR, which had officially renounced the privileges acquired in China by the Romanov Empire, was careful not to return the spoliated territories. This ambivalent policy came to an end with the victory of the Communists in 1949. As for the periods of friendship and brotherhood, the first one began with the founding of the People's Republic of China in 1949 and the signing of the Treaty of Friendship, Alliance, and Mutual Assistance in February 1950, and ended with Nikita Khrushchev in the early 1960s. The new China, exhausted after several decades of Japanese invasion and civil war, then had a vital need for economic and military assistance from the "big Soviet brother" to recover from its ruins and fully adopted its development model as well as its ideological and political authority. However, the onerous conditions demanded by Stalin reinforced the old distrust existing between the two countries and leaders. Stalin distrusted Mao, suspected him of wanting to be "another Tito," and called him a "margarine communist." Mao had to swallow the dictates of the Soviet dictator and did not forget it upon his death. The troublemaker from Moscow. The second prosperous period began in the late 1980s with the rise to power in Moscow and Beijing of two pragmatic leaders, Mikhail Gorbachev and Deng Xiaoping. Their policy of normalizing bilateral relations was further amplified by their successors, especially in the 2000s by Vladimir Putin, who called for "the sails of the Russian economy to benefit from the Chinese wind," and by Xi Jinping. However, very quickly, Sino-Russian relations became very unbalanced in favor of Beijing. Currently, China's Gross National Product (GNP) is six times higher than that of Russia, which holds only a minimal share of the Chinese market. China is Russia's top trading partner, but even though significant in absolute numbers, trade between the two countries is six times less than that between China and the United States. Weakened Russia is gradually losing ground in its former Soviet "backyard" in Central Asia and the Caucasus, where Beijing already holds a dominant economic and commercial position and is preparing to do the same politically and in terms of security. In global forums like the Shanghai Cooperation Organization (SCO) or the BRICS, Russia is no longer able to maintain its position against a dominant China.
