The Russian aggression against Ukraine has only exacerbated these asymmetries. During his visit to Beijing at the beginning of February 2022 for the Olympics, Vladimir Putin apparently failed to inform his Chinese counterpart of the imminent launch of the "special military operation." This went against the principles of international law that Beijing upholds, such as territorial integrity and non-interference in internal affairs. China has nevertheless adopted a "pro-Russian neutral" attitude, with Moscow remaining essential to Beijing by guaranteeing diplomatic support in its conflict with the United States, ensuring tranquility on their common border while Beijing is preoccupied with the Taiwan and South China Sea "fronts," as well as ensuring a steady supply of raw materials and energy at low costs. This does not mean that the Chinese, who fear secondary sanctions from Washington and want to maintain American and European markets, desire a total victory for the Russian army. Beijing refrains from providing lethal weapons to Russia and learns from its setbacks on the Ukrainian front, which could be useful in a potential conquest of Taiwan. Furthermore, China's ideological support for its Russian partner is far from flawless. While it echoes most of Russia's arguments justifying the aggression, Beijing allows Chinese researchers critical of the themes promoted in Moscow regarding the defense of "conservative values" and the alleged "messianic role" of Russia to express their views. For some of these thinkers, the Ukrainian adventure has significantly tarnished Russia's international image and diminished the importance of the Sino-Russian partnership. Concerned about its relations with the West and international stability, Beijing aims to maintain a certain distance from the disruptive behavior of Moscow. Russo-Chinese relations remain complicated, ambivalent, and marked by distrust. Historical scars related to territorial disputes that China does not rule out raising one day, as well as significant imbalances, have turned Russia into a "junior partner," or even a vassal, of China. XEMARTIN LABORDE, VICTORIA DENYS, AND DELPHINE PAPIN

title: Nord Stream 1 and 2: a Ukrainian officer identified as the perpetrator of the sabotage of the gas pipelines
lead: Colonel Tchervinski is said to have led the team that blew up the two pipelines in the Baltic Sea in September 2022, according to a joint investigation by "Spiegel" and "The Washington Post." The individual denies this, which could become an embarrassing issue for Volodymyr Zelensky.
text_eng: A Ukrainian special forces officer allegedly coordinated the sabotage of the Russian gas pipelines Nord Stream 1 and 2, according to a joint investigation by Spiegel and The Washington Post published on Saturday, November 11th. His name is Roman Tchervinski. According to the German weekly and the American newspaper, this Ukrainian colonel supervised the team that blew up the two pipelines on September 26, 2022, at the bottom of the Baltic Sea. When contacted by the two newspapers, Mr. Tchervinski stated that these accusations are unfounded. "All speculations about my involvement in the attack on Nord Stream are spread by Russian propaganda," he communicated through his lawyer, while he is currently in pretrial detention in Kiev for another case. Arrested in April, Roman Tchervinski is accused of abusing his power by persuading a Russian pilot to defect in July 2022 during an operation that turned into a fiasco: instead of surrendering to the Kiev authorities as agreed, the man reportedly passed information to his superiors about the Kanatove military airport in central Ukraine, where he was supposed to land, enabling Russian forces to bomb it, killing one Ukrainian soldier and injuring seventeen others. Roman Tchervinski claims that the charges against him are political retaliation for criticizing President Volodymyr Zelensky. Zelensky was allegedly "kept out" of this operation. These criticisms are public and date back to December 3, 2021. In an interview broadcast on Ukraine 24 television that day, Roman Tchervinski accused the President's inner circle of sabotaging an operation in July 2020 to capture Russian Wagner group mercenaries in Belarus for extradition to Ukraine. According to him, associates of Volodymyr Zelensky scuttled the plan at the last minute out of "fear of provoking Russia," as Kiev and Moscow had just agreed to a ceasefire in the Donbass, a region plagued by armed clashes between Russian separatists and Ukrainian forces since 2014. At the time, Roman Tchervinski was a member of the GUR, the Ukrainian military intelligence service, which he had joined after working for several years for the SBU, the country's security service. In a statement provided to Spiegel and The Washington Post by his lawyer, he claims to have "planned and carried out" several assassinations of pro-Russian separatists in Ukraine. He also asserts that he "had a witness abducted" who could confirm Russia's involvement in the destruction of Malaysia Airlines Flight MH17 over Ukraine in July 2014, killing all 298 passengers and crew members. Volodymyr Zelensky has always denied his country's involvement in sabotaging the Nord Stream 1 and 2 gas pipelines. "Nothing like this has been done by Ukraine. I would never do such a thing," the Ukrainian president had declared to the German newspaper Bild in June, after articles in the press claimed that Western intelligence services had learned of a sabotage plan for the pipelines three months before it was carried out by a cell "reporting directly" to General Valeri Zaloujny, the commander-in-chief of the Ukrainian armed forces. In their investigation, Spiegel and The Washington Post do not question the good faith of the Ukrainian president. "The Nord Stream operation was designed in a way that Zelensky would be kept out of the loop," the American newspaper writes, specifying that this aligns with the beliefs of several states whose officials have privately expressed trust in him when he asserts that he did not approve the sabotage of Nord Stream. Dissensions at the helm of the Ukrainian state While absolving him of responsibility for the operation, the investigation published on Saturday is nonetheless embarrassing for Volodymyr Zelensky. By asserting that he probably was not informed in advance about the existence of this plan, Spiegel and The Washington Post highlight the divisions at the top of the Ukrainian state. "If the commander-in-chief of the Ukrainian army indeed took it upon himself to organize such an important operation behind Zelensky's back, it is a terrible sign of weakness for him," says Marion Van Renterghem, a former journalist at Le Monde and author of an investigation titled The Nord Stream Trap (Les Arènes, 270 pages, 22 euros). According to the journalist, the hypothesis that the Ukrainian president himself had nothing to do with the operation is credible, even though Kiev has always opposed Nord Stream, believing that the construction of this twin gas pipeline connecting Russia to Germany via the Baltic Sea would strengthen Europe's energy dependence on Moscow and thus weaken Ukraine strategically by bypassing it.

title: Visiting Beijing, Vladimir Putin denies his status as a pariah.
lead: The Russian president was welcomed with honors in China for his second trip outside of Russian territory since the International Criminal Court issued a warrant for his arrest.
text_eng: For two days, the journalists embedded with the Russian delegation invited to the "New Silk Road" Forum in Beijing, kept a close eye on the signs of attention given to their leader, Vladimir Putin. From the protocol welcome, seating arrangements during meetings, speaking opportunities, to entering alongside Chinese President Xi Jinping into the grand banquet hall on the evening of Tuesday, October 17th, the Russian president was the most pampered among the twenty-four heads of state and government received at the forum. "No matter which channel you turn on, Putin is everywhere on Chinese television," exclaimed the star host of the Rossia 1 channel, sent to Beijing. "Even with a hundred and fifty delegations, he is the main guest and the guest of honor at this summit." While the second part of the statement leaves little doubt, the hosts of the Russian-language Telegram channel "Chinese Threat" verified the first part: on the day of his arrival in China, Vladimir Putin was only shown three times on the seventeen channels of Chinese public television, for brief interventions praising Xi Jinping's role as a "leader." For the Russian president, the main focus is elsewhere. It is about refuting the pariah status in which Western states try to confine him - a status reinforced by the issuance, in March, of an international arrest warrant by the International Criminal Court against him. Since that date, Mr. Putin has only made one foreign trip, on October 12th, to Bishkek (Kyrgyzstan), as part of a CIS summit. Beijing was supposed to mark an important milestone in the international agenda of the Russian president, who has been particularly active on the Israeli-Palestinian issue in recent days, speaking on the phone with most regional leaders. The previous sequence had been mainly marked by the long visit of the North Korean leader, Kim Jong-un, to Russian territory, one of the very few states in the world to provide unconditional support to Moscow in its war in Ukraine. Speeches on Western duplicity Russian grievances against the West also take on an additional dimension in this multilateral framework. Putin's speeches on Western duplicity, even his devotion to "satanism," have become a perfectly routine exercise in Russia. In Beijing on Wednesday, his plea for "a more just multipolar world" and "the right of each state to its own development model" resonate with Chinese statements. According to him, Beijing's projects align with Russia's desire to create a "great Eurasian space." While the French delegation, led by former Prime Minister Jean-Pierre Raffarin, left the room during this speech, Putin received support in his efforts to rebuild international respectability from Viktor Orban. The Hungarian Prime Minister met with the Russian President on Tuesday, something no leader of the European Union has done since April 2022. He showed great respect, emphasizing that Budapest had "never sought confrontation with Russia, on the contrary." During his meeting with Vladimir Putin, Xi Jinping also expressed friendship towards his host, mentioning "the steadily growing mutual political trust between our two countries." However, the meeting was brief and does not erase the divergences that have accumulated in the bilateral relationship, mainly to the detriment of Moscow – whether it be the halt of drone deliveries to Russia due to fear of sanctions or Chinese hesitations regarding the Siberia 2 gas pipeline project, intended to partially compensate Moscow for the loss of the European market.

title: Presidential election in Madagascar: former judoka Siteny enters campaign and weakens opposition's boycott.
lead: The candidate held his first rally on Sunday, while a new request was filed with the High Constitutional Court to ask for a postponement of the election.
text_eng: He denies having broken the unity of the opposition. The former judoka Siteny Randrianasoloniaiko has entered the campaign to challenge the outgoing Malagasy President, Andry Rajoelina, leaving the other ten candidates of the "collective of 11" to continue their boycott of an election they consider biased and predetermined. On Sunday, October 22, candidate number 13 held his first rally in Vohémar, in the north of the island, where the collapse of the vanilla sector has ruined thousands of planters. "It was not a secret. I had warned that I would not let Andry Rajoelina go to the election alone," explained the former athlete, known to be close to the Russians, to Le Monde. "We do not trust the institutions organizing the election [the Independent National Electoral Commission (CENI) and the High Constitutional Court (HCC)], but history shows that those who do not participate always end up losing. I have no choice." "Siteny," as the Malagasy call him, nevertheless maintains his support for the demands of the "11," including the postponement of the election. The opposition argues, based on the Constitution, that the election cannot be held as long as one of the candidates is unable to participate. One of them is still recovering in Mauritius. Seriously injured in the right eye by tear gas fired by law enforcement during a march on October 2, Andry Rajoelina was evacuated and underwent surgery. Doctors have prohibited him from flying for thirty days. On Monday, his lawyers filed a new request with the HCC to recognize a situation of "force majeure" related to their client's health and to once again postpone the election. With significant financial resources, the first round scheduled for November 16 has been delayed by only one week, which "Siteny" considers a joke. But the former judoka has not always been so critical of the authorities. Although he says he feels "betrayed, like all Malagasy, by unfulfilled promises," it was under the banner of the presidential majority IRD that the deputy of Toliara, the city of his origin, was re-elected in 2019 after turning his back on the party of former President Hery Rajaonarimampianina, to which he was affiliated. In Madagascar, the imposing former judoka is primarily known for the roles he holds in national and international sports organizations. President of the Malagasy Olympic Committee, he also heads the African Judo Union and is vice-president of the International Judo Federation (FIJ). In this position, he has forged a friendship with the Austrian-born Romanian Marius Vizer, president of the FIJ and close to Vladimir Putin – himself an accomplished judoka and ambassador of the FIJ until his suspension at the beginning of the war in Ukraine. These connections have led to suspicions that the Malagasy candidate is being secretly financed by Russia. Accusations that he defends himself against, without clarifying the origin of the funds injected into his campaign. Because with Andry Rajoelina, "Siteny" is certainly the candidate with the most significant financial means, especially for traveling by helicopter through a country lacking roads. As early as January, and well before announcing his candidacy, he embarked on a "mihava tour" ("listening to the people" in Malagasy) to gather the grievances of the population and build a program that emphasizes decentralization. "I have no political ties to Russia," he asserts. "My money comes from my business, my family, and those who trust me in Madagascar and abroad." In addition to establishing the British School of Madagascar, "Siteny" has also developed activities in the fields of communication and real estate. Strike notice In the meantime, his solo departure does not seem to dampen the determination of the rest of the opposition, which includes two former heads of state, Marc Ravalomanana and Hery Rajaonarimampianina. After gathering some 50,000 supporters on Saturday at the Antsonjombe coliseum in Antananarivo, the collective resumed its peaceful marches in the capital on Monday, as it does almost every day since the official launch of the campaign on October 10. Gatherings are also organized in the provinces. "The electoral environment has not changed. The conditions are not met for a fair election," notes Hajo Andrianainarivelo, president and candidate of the MMM party. The collective demands the establishment of impartial electoral institutions, the cancellation of the HCC decision entrusting interim power to Prime Minister Christian Ntsay, and the disqualification of Andry Rajoelina's candidacy due to his French nationality acquired in 2014 through naturalization. Fearing that the situation may escalate, the Solidarity Syndicate of Madagascar platform, which brings together 86 organizations from the public, private, and informal sectors, threatens to block the country's administration if the outgoing president and the twelve candidates do not sit down together to find a political solution to the crisis. A strike notice has been filed for October 26.

title: After the outbreak of the Israel-Hamas war, "Vladimir Putin is rubbing his hands."
lead: The Russian president plans to take advantage of the crisis in the Middle East, which diverts attention from the war in Ukraine and fuels resentment against the "collective West." This is a danger that Europeans are concerned about, as noted by Sylvie Kauffmann, an editorialist at "Le Monde," in her column.
text_eng: One conflict can hide another. The war that has just broken out between Israel and Hamas, and the risks of regional escalation that it is feared to bring, has pushed another war off the media's radar, the one that Russia has been waging in Ukraine for twenty months. Yet, the two crises intersect, through the actors who can benefit from them. Foremost among them: Vladimir Putin. The most obvious reason why this new explosion in the Middle East comes at an opportune time for the Russian president is that, while Ukraine's allies turn with horror to the south, Russia is re-launching its offensive in the Donbass. These days, Russian forces relentlessly shell the Avdiivka area, a city of 30,000 inhabitants that now has less than 2,000 due to the suffering, in an attempt to dislodge the Ukrainian defenders. Since 2022, everyone in Europe knows the names of Mariupol and Bakhmut. But who, today, has heard of Avdiivka? The battle is raging there. "The enemy is suffering significant losses," reported Ukrainian General Oleksandr Syrsky on Monday, October 23, "but it constantly replenishes its forces with reserve troops, including those brought in from Russia." Not a word about Ukrainian losses; one can easily imagine them. On Saturday, October 21, a Russian missile killed six civilians in a postal sorting center in Kharkiv. Radio silence. From the start, Vladimir Putin has been betting that Western public opinion will eventually tire of the war in Ukraine. He could not have dreamed of a better scenario than a new level of horror reached in the Middle East. Fracture There are other, less immediate, reasons why this crisis benefits the Russian president. He invaded Ukraine in the name of an imperial design: the conquest of a nation whose existence as a state he denies. Operations on the ground did not go as he had planned; the "non-existent nation" resisted, supported by Western democracies. On the diplomatic front, however, Russia encountered less resistance: the war highlighted a fracture between these democracies, convinced of defending, along with Ukraine, international law, and some states from the rest of the world who see it as merely a European conflict and a new proof of Western hypocrisy, based on "double standards." The spontaneous solidarity of Western countries with Israel after the massacres of October 7 exacerbated this fracture, especially when the Israeli army imposed a blockade on Gaza, ordered the evacuation of its population from the north to the south, and began bombing the territory. On October 19, President Joe Biden linked, in a national address, the crises in Ukraine and Israel to request Congress to release $100 billion (approximately 94 billion euros) in emergency funds to help these two countries. Establishing this link was aimed at obtaining the overall agreement of the Republicans, who were reluctant to help Ukraine but very focused on Israel. However, this link is a double-edged sword. The next day, the American president received the leaders of the European Union (EU) at the White House. At the end of the meeting, Josep Borrell, who heads EU diplomacy, expressed his concern about the rise of anti-Western anger, observed by EU embassies in Southern countries since October 7 and specifically fueled by the accusation of "double standards": how, ask these critics, can one claim to rally global opinion against Russia's violations of international law in Ukraine, and then support Israel bombing Gaza? Vladimir Putin's dream is for this anger to spread to the very heart of multiethnic European societies. Overturning the "colonial narrative" It can always be argued that the invasion of Ukraine was not triggered by the massacre of 1,400 civilians or accompanied by hostage-taking. Joe Biden can always, to warn Israeli leaders, acknowledge the mistakes that the spirit of vengeance led his country to commit after 9/11. All in vain, resentment overwhelms the facts. And when the United States, alone, veto a Brazilian resolution in favor of a ceasefire in Gaza at the UN Security Council, the confusion is total: until now, the veto technique was a Russian specialty. "All of this is going to complicate our task considerably," laments a diplomat in Brussels, where commendable efforts have been made for months to "not lose the global South." In the Kremlin, Vladimir Putin is rubbing his hands together. He does so even more because the outbreak of violence between Israel and Hamas allows the "colonial narrative" that Ukraine is trying to associate with Russia, a fragmented empire seizing one of its former possessions to recolonize it, to be overturned. Because when Israeli settlers forcibly evict Palestinian Bedouins from the hills of the West Bank, when more than 90 Palestinians have been killed since October 7 in these territories, in the battle of narratives, the colonial argument shifts to the other side. Not everything is going as well for Vladimir Putin. By refusing to condemn Hamas for the massacres of October 7 – Hamas leaders he has met twice in Moscow since the beginning of the war in Ukraine – he has abandoned his balanced stance with Israel. Returning to the region since its intervention in Syria in 2015, Russia has taken advantage of American withdrawal. But if the new escalation of tensions in the Middle East leads the United States to reinvest there, Moscow will struggle to maintain its position on this front while waging war in Ukraine. Xi Jinping, the Chinese president, is more cautious. He is keeping a military distance from this explosive region, while congratulating himself that it is keeping the Americans occupied.

title: Israel-Hamas War: Differences persist in the European Union
lead: The heads of state and government have tried to put an end to a week of European cacophony regarding the situation in the Middle East. They express their support for Israel and call for respect for international law and humanitarian law in Gaza.
text_eng: In a tense atmosphere, twenty-six European heads of state and government met virtually on Tuesday, October 17th. The only one absent was Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orban, who was visiting China and meeting with Vladimir Putin. This extraordinary European Council meeting took place ten days after the brutal attack by Hamas on Israel, which resulted in around 1,400 deaths, and Israel's subsequent response, leading to the deaths of over 3,000 Palestinians under the bombs. "It was a moment of unity," explained Charles Michel, the President of the European Council. Unity was necessary after a week of confusion regarding the future of European aid to Palestine and divergent positions among the Union's leaders, making the European position inaudible in the world, especially in the Middle East. "It is of the utmost importance that the European Council, in accordance with the treaties and our values, defines our common position and establishes a clear and unified course of action that reflects the complexity of the current situation," Charles Michel emphasized before the discussions. "We strongly emphasize Israel's right to defend itself," the European leaders stated in a joint declaration, in accordance with international humanitarian law and international law, in the face of such violent and indiscriminate attacks. "We emphasize the importance of ensuring the protection of all civilians at all times, in accordance with international humanitarian law." Criticism of von der Leyen As early as October 10th, foreign ministers had agreed on this balanced position. However, Ursula von der Leyen, the President of the Commission, struggled to articulate it. During a surprise visit to Israel on October 13th, alongside Roberta Metsola, the President of the European Parliament, she did not speak on behalf of the Twenty-Seven, according to several diplomats, but rather expressed her own views. She condemned Hamas attacks and supported the Netanyahu government's policies, without any public reference to the situation of Palestinians in the Gaza Strip. Nonetheless, her team insists that the issue of civilians and respect for international law was directly addressed with Benjamin Netanyahu. At the European Council, French President Emmanuel Macron and Irish Prime Minister Leo Varadkar reiterated their thinly veiled criticisms of the Commission President's stance. "For two years, we have been trying to convince Southern countries that international law must prevail, especially in Ukraine, and now we are losing them because of these types of statements," laments a European official. "We must avoid any double standards," adds another source in Brussels. The Twenty-Seven and the leaders have pledged to better coordinate in the future and speak with one voice. At the end of the meeting, Mrs. von der Leyen assured that "there is no contradiction between solidarity with Israel and action in favor of the humanitarian needs of the Palestinians," recalling the 200% increase in humanitarian aid planned for the population of Gaza (75 million euros) and the launch of an airlift to Egypt to deliver aid as quickly as possible to the bombarded territory. "The Americans, on the other hand, are on the ground." However, the President of the Commission still appeared to be at odds with Mr. Michel. Responding to a question about the explosion at Al-Ahli hospital in Gaza, which killed several hundred people on Tuesday, the latter stated that "an attack on a civilian infrastructure is not in line with international law." Mrs. von der Leyen, "who did not have time to get informed," refused to answer the question, while Israel and the Palestinians are blaming each other for the strike. A few minutes later, Josep Borrell stated on the social network X that "the news from Al-Ahli hospital in Gaza adds horror to the tragedy unfolding before our eyes for several days. Once again, it is innocent civilians who are paying the price. The responsibility for this crime must be clearly established and the perpetrators must be held accountable." The leaders emphasized the importance of continuing their consular cooperation to evacuate the thousand European citizens still living in Gaza. They also stated that they are working on the issue of Hamas hostages, "several dozen" of whom, according to a European diplomat, have EU nationality. Finally, according to the Elysée, "the leaders agreed to work together with regional actors to end the violence and work towards creating the conditions for lasting peace." However, there is still a lingering unease on this matter in Brussels. Ten days after the start of this new war, "we are still trying to get on the same page internally," laments a diplomat in Brussels. "The Americans, on the other hand, are on the ground trying to make progress, especially for civilians."

title: Paris 2024: Russian President Vladimir Putin accuses the IOC of "ethnic discrimination"
lead: If the Olympic committee has not yet decided on the presence of Russian athletes at the sports event, it has conditioned this possibility on a series of neutrality criteria.
text_eng: The debate on whether Russian and Belarusian athletes should participate in the 2024 Olympic Games in Paris has been stirring the sports world for several months. Relatively quiet on this issue until now, Vladimir Putin strongly criticized the International Olympic Committee (IOC) on Thursday, October 19, accusing it of "ethnic discrimination" during a speech on sports in Perm, in the Ural region. While the IOC, based in Lausanne, Switzerland, has not yet decided on the presence of Russian athletes at the summer event, it has already conditioned this possibility on a series of criteria: individual participation only, with teams excluded; competing under a neutral flag, meaning no anthem or flag; provided that the individuals involved have not "actively supported the war in Ukraine" and are not under contract with the military or security services. Their participation would therefore be limited, much to the dismay of the President of the Russian Federation. "Thanks to certain leaders of the current International Olympic Committee, we have learned that (...) the Games themselves can be used as a political pressure tool against people who have nothing to do with politics and, in fact, [as a tool] of crude racist and ethnic discrimination," Vladimir Putin criticized. For the IOC, "an invitation to the Games is not an unconditional right for the best athletes, but a kind of privilege," the Kremlin's strongman rebuked. Suspension of the Russian National Olympic Committee Russian athletes, along with their Belarusian counterparts, were banned from all international competitions after the start of the offensive in Ukraine on February 24, 2022. In March 2023, the IOC recommended their reinstatement in international competitions outside of the Games, provided once again that they adhere to the neutrality model set by the organization. The IOC has deferred its final decision on the Olympic event "at the appropriate time." Meanwhile, Kiev has threatened to boycott the Paris Games if Russians and Belarusians are allowed to participate. Several European countries have already expressed their support for the Ukrainians. The International Paralympic Committee (IPC) has already voted in favor of the participation of Russians and Belarusians under a neutral flag and strict neutrality conditions at the Paris Paralympic Games. Another point of contention between the Russian authorities and the IOC is the suspension, on October 12, of the Russian National Olympic Committee for placing several sports organizations in the Ukrainian regions occupied by the Kremlin's army under its authority.

title: Russian oligarch Alexei Kuzmichev indicted in France for money laundering and tax fraud.
lead: The justice system is investigating the assets of this close associate of Vladimir Putin, who has remained in French territory despite being placed under European sanctions in March 2022.
text_eng: Troubles continue for Alexei Kouzmitchev. After two days in police custody, the Russian oligarch was brought before an investigating judge at the financial division of the Paris judicial court on Wednesday, November 1st, and indicted on charges of "aggravated money laundering, presumption of money laundering, and organized concealed labor" according to the National Financial Prosecutor's Office (PNF). Despite the prosecution's request for detention, he left the court freely under judicial supervision, with the obligation to pay a bail of 8 million euros. He is also prohibited from leaving France. Police conducted searches on Monday morning at his villa in Saint-Tropez (Var) and his mansion in Paris. These operations involved forty elite police officers from the Raid and BRI units, tasked with securing the locations. According to our sources, the PNF investigation into money laundering related to tax fraud began in February 2023. "The suspected fraud is linked to the ownership of real estate in France and undeclared income," said a judicial source to Le Monde. This lead was prompted by a report from Tracfin, the financial intelligence unit of the French Ministry of Finance, during the summer of 2022. The Kremlin's support The justice system is also investigating whether Mr. Kouzmitchev violated the European sanctions imposed on him on March 15, 2022, in response to the Russian invasion of Ukraine. While the mere presence of the businessman in France could constitute a serious offense under customs law, "the potential characterization of these offenses requires additional investigative actions," said the judicial source. The businessman's entourage denies any wrongdoing and explains that he was already in French territory when the sanctions were imposed. The case, entrusted to officers from the National Brigade for the Repression of Tax Offenses (BNRDF), is now proceeding as a judicial investigation under the supervision of an investigating magistrate. Alexei Kouzmitchev, a co-shareholder of the Alfa Group consortium, which owns Alfa Bank, is considered by European authorities as "one of the most influential people in Russia," with "well-established ties to the President of the Russian Federation, Vladimir Putin." The Kremlin stated on November 1st that it was ready to "protect the rights" of the Russian businessman following his arrest. "We must be informed of the detention of a citizen of the Russian Federation through our diplomatic mission," said the spokesman for the Russian presidency, Dmitry Peskov, as reported by AFP. "Upon receiving the information, and if the detainee wishes, we will of course assist in protecting his rights as a Russian citizen." Do you have any information to share with us? Feel free to securely contact our investigations team, who is at your disposal to exchange with you if you have information or documents of public interest.

title: "Lula has an anachronistic view of international politics."
lead: Long hailed for his global popularity, the Brazilian president is currently struggling to maintain a balanced position in the conflicts in Ukraine and the Middle East, according to international relations specialist Guilherme Casaroes. At the United Nations, his resolution proposal for "humanitarian pauses in Gaza" was rejected on October 18th.
text_eng: Guilherme Casaroes is a professor at the Getulio Vargas Foundation and a specialist in international relations. He analyzes the foreign policy of the left-wing president, Luis Inacio Lula da Silva, who has proclaimed the "return of Brazil" to the global geopolitical stage. Can Lula have a role in the war between Israel and Hamas? He has several cards in hand. Brazil has a long tradition of mediating conflicts and currently presides over the G20 [which is set to host the next summit in Rio in November 2024] as well as the United Nations Security Council. Lula, who was in power from 2003 to 2011, personally knows the actors in this conflict and has a certain mastery of the situation. His statements have been balanced: he has called the Hamas attacks "terrorist," expressed solidarity with Israeli victims, while also calling for international humanitarian intervention to assist civilians in Gaza. Lula wants the UN to take concrete measures and for more countries to get involved in resolving this conflict. When Lula tried to mediate in the war in Ukraine, his statements, seen as favorable to Russia, angered Western countries. How can his position on this conflict be explained? First, there was a form of improvisation. In the months following [the Russian invasion of Ukraine on February 24, 2022], Lula was preoccupied in Brazil with the presidential campaign. Faced with this war, the extent and duration of which he had not anticipated, he did not have time to structure his thoughts. In haste, he simply repeated the viewpoint expressed by the historical leaders of his party, the Workers' Party (PT), which is a very anti-imperialist vision highly critical of the United States, stemming from Washington's past support for dictatorships in Latin America. According to this perspective, Vladimir Putin was merely responding to expansionist threats from NATO. This paradigm served as the basis for Lula's analysis, leading him to have very harsh words for Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky, particularly in an interview with Time magazine in May 2022 where he stated, "This guy is as responsible [for the war] as Putin." There is another dimension to consider, related to Brazilian diplomacy. By proposing [in February] the idea of a "peace group," bringing together countries interested in finding "a peaceful solution," Lula positions Brazil as a natural mediator in global crises. This is a historical vocation of Itamaraty, the Brazilian Ministry of Foreign Affairs. Its diplomats have always seen themselves as peacemakers, intermediaries, and mediators in territorial disputes, particularly in Latin America (such as between Peru and Ecuador). By adopting neutrality, Lula is simply following in this tradition. Additionally, there is a more personal factor that could explain his position on Ukraine. Lula sees himself as a born negotiator, capable of bringing together adversaries with opposing views. He has successfully used this power of conciliation in Brazil, where he was able to unite business leaders and labor unions, members of the right and left, part of the elite and the poorest... He is convinced that he can facilitate the resolution of conflicts such as the war in Ukraine or even between Israel and Hamas. This explains his desire to stay at a distance from each side. In 2010, Lula intervened in the issue of Iranian nuclear power. He was able to sign an agreement with Iran through Turkey... before being disavowed by the United States. What role did this episode play in his approach to the Ukrainian conflict? During his first two terms [from 2003 to 2011], despite his extraordinary popularity abroad, Lula ultimately achieved very few concrete international successes. Interesting initiatives, such as the creation [in 2010, in Rio] of the Community of Latin American and Caribbean States or the South America-Arab Countries summits [the first in Brasilia, in 2005] and Africa-Latin America [inaugurated in November 2006 in Abuja], were not sustained. The Brazilian command of the United Nations Stabilization Mission in Haiti (MINUSTAH) did not solve the country's problems, despite a presence of thirteen years [from 2004 to 2017]. And Lula never secured a permanent seat for Brazil on the UN Security Council. The Iranian episode is emblematic. Brazil had never been so close to becoming a global geopolitical player... Lula emerged from this failure with a real sense of frustration. This probably explains his desire to return to the stage with Ukraine. Furthermore, Lula and his close associates had a hard time with the "abandonment" by the United States, which contributed to fueling anti-American sentiment within the Brazilian left. Especially since Washington was later accused by the PT of being behind the "Lava Jato" anti-corruption operation, which led to the downfall of President Dilma Rousseff [also from the PT] in 2016 and the imprisonment of Lula from 2018 to 2019. What is Lula's view of the European Union, shaken by the war in Ukraine? Lula has a very positive view of the European Union. This stems first from his intellectual and political formation since the 1980s, closely linked to the European left and labor movement. Lula has referred to the EU as a "democratic heritage" of humanity. Not only does he believe that European integration contributes to the emergence of a multipolar world, but he also sees this union as a triumph of social democracy: A continent integrated around peace and prosperity. A model to follow for Latin America. But Lula's thinking is also marked by the Cold War. While he greatly respects the political and civil project of the Europeans, he also considers them to be completely dependent and subordinate to the United States, through NATO – which is not entirely false. In the context of the war in Ukraine, he believes that Europeans are victims of Russian imperialism, but also of American imperialism. This explains his difficulties in aligning with EU positions. The BRICS (Brazil, Russia, India, China, and South Africa) will have six new members in January 2024: Saudi Arabia, Argentina, Egypt, United Arab Emirates, Ethiopia, and Iran. Why does Lula attach such importance to this entity? Firstly, because he is one of the founding fathers of this club – undoubtedly his most enduring diplomatic success. For a decade now, the BRICS have been asserting themselves on the international stage and have managed to achieve some political advances, such as the – albeit partial – reform of the International Monetary Fund. It is now an essential element of Brazilian diplomacy. The other reason for this attachment is less brilliant, linked to the rather anachronistic aspect of Lula and the PT's foreign policy. Lula sincerely believes that the BRICS can help build a multipolar world, in which Brazil would play a leading role. However, the emerging multipolar world is not based on soft power and diplomacy, but on military hard power. The armed forces of China, Russia, India, and the United States are facing each other in a very hostile manner. In this evolving world, Brazil's weight is very weak. This is one of Lula's major analytical errors. Can Lula still be a bridge between Western countries and China or Russia? He would like to be, and does everything he can. But, once again, his vision is anachronistic. In 2003 [the year of his first election], it was still relatively easy to maintain a balanced position between the Western countries and China or Russia. Since the 2000s, there has been a hardening of Beijing and Moscow on human rights issues, coupled with aggressive expansionist policies. The signs of friendship that Lula continues to send them are very poorly received by Western countries. And he faces the greatest difficulties in embodying a point of balance in an increasingly polarized world. Instead of risking involvement in the Ukrainian situation, Lula should focus his efforts on areas where his talent is globally recognized, namely the environment and the fight against poverty. On these subjects, he achieved significant successes in the 2000s, and some of his policies still serve as a reference worldwide. On the Amazon and the eradication of hunger, his word is eagerly awaited. Brazilian diplomacy could also prove useful in terms of regional integration. This is a question that Lula masters. He understands the historical importance of deepening ties between South American countries, and the predominant weight of Brazil in this dynamic. According to him, Brasilia could play a role similar to that of Berlin in European construction, with, in his view, a significant demographic and territorial advantage. The enormous obstacle to this integration remains the humanitarian and political crisis in Venezuela. Many countries in the region have distanced themselves from Caracas and have recognized opposition leader Juan Guaido as the legitimate Venezuelan president. Brazil is one of the few states to have kept communication channels open with Venezuela. Lula knows Nicolas Maduro well [whose re-election in the 2018 presidential election is contested] and can exert his influence. But, once again, he was imprudent. His remarks in May, reducing the "authoritarianism" [of Mr. Maduro] to a mere "fictional narrative" were very poorly received in several capitals of the region. The Venezuelan elections of 2024 will be a real test for Lula's diplomacy. Find all of our geopolitical files.

title: Israel-Hamas: the anger of the Global South
lead: The speed and intensity with which the protest against Western democracies has spread since the new escalation of the Israeli-Palestinian conflict are indicative of a political shift, under the dual pressure of the Russian war in Ukraine and the Israel-Hamas conflict.
text_eng: Anger is rising, it comes from the South and is directed against the North. This is one of the global effects of the new war between Israel and Hamas. The violence of the Israeli army's response in Gaza to the barbarity of the terrorist operation carried out on October 7 by Hamas against Israeli civilians has triggered a powerful wave of protest, tinged with a spirit of revenge, against Western countries, accused of hypocrisy in choosing which victims to support. The accusation is not new. It mainly revolves around the principle of "double standards" reproached to Western democracies depending on whether the target of an attack is part of their camp or not. But the speed and intensity with which this protest has spread over the past ten days are indicative of a political shift, under the dual pressure of the Russian war in Ukraine and the Israel-Hamas conflict. American and European leaders were surprised in 2022 by the reluctance of major Southern countries to join them in their unconditional condemnation of the Russian army's invasion of Ukraine. The renewed outbreak of the Israeli-Palestinian conflict and its exorbitant cost for civilian populations, while the Russian war in Ukraine continues to rage, have reignited the debate. Helplessness The chronology of events on the ground fueled controversy: after the massacres of October 7, several Western leaders visited Tel Aviv to show their support for the Israeli ally at a time when it was carrying out massive bombings on Gaza and its population. The deadly explosion that struck Al-Ahli hospital in Gaza - for which there is currently no evidence of an Israeli bombing - while the American president was flying to Israel, accentuated the perception of imbalance. The Arab part of his trip had to be canceled due to a lack of interlocutors, as they withdrew in the face of the outrage in Arab public opinion. While Joe Biden was on this difficult mission in Israel, Chinese President was gathering representatives of the global South in Beijing to celebrate his grand project, the "new silk roads." The star guest was Russian President Vladimir Putin, a pariah in the West but who, in Beijing, received special honors and a three-hour bilateral meeting. Neither China nor Russia condemned the Hamas attack on October 7; they reserved their criticism for Israel and the United States. Western governments are now paying the price for their inability to find, or even seek, a solution to the Palestinian issue. They are also paying for their inability to adapt international governance, which they dominated, to the new realities of global balances. In the current climate of tension, their support for Israel, perceived as exclusive in the rest of the world, risks jeopardizing their efforts to convince Southern countries that international security is at stake in Ukraine. None of the countries that challenge "American hegemony," including China, have proposed an alternative plan to resolve the Israeli-Palestinian conflict. If Western leaders want to maintain some room for maneuver, they will have to, on one hand, more vigorously and visibly accompany their support for Israel with the demand for respect for humanitarian law, and on the other hand, ensure the involvement of leaders from Southern countries in mediation efforts and the search for solutions in the Middle East.

title: Ursula von der Leyen, the missteps of the hyper-president
lead: The President of the European Commission has exasperated some member states by displaying a more pro-Israeli stance than that of the Twenty-Seven in Israel following the Hamas attack. On other occasions, she has already overstepped her role, particularly in the relationship with the United States.
text_eng: When she sits at the table of European heads of state and government, who are set to meet in Brussels on October 26 and 27, Ursula von der Leyen will undoubtedly be greeted very courteously. True to herself, the President of the Commission will also show herself to be perfectly friendly. In this exclusive club of men and women who shape the European Union (EU), one must maintain a good image, even when facing criticism. Lately, Ursula von der Leyen has been severely criticized by the Twenty-Seven on multiple occasions. She is reproached for overstepping her duties by taking initiatives, sometimes unfortunate ones as in the Israeli-Palestinian issue, in foreign policy matters. It is objected that she makes certain decisions without consulting the Member States, such as signing the EU-Tunisia agreement on migration. Concern is raised about her efforts to please Washington, even when European and American interests are not always aligned. As for her perpetual "war" with Charles Michel, the President of the Council, it is extremely exasperating. Since taking office at the end of 2019, Ursula von der Leyen has repeatedly encroached into the realm of European foreign policy, which falls under the Member States, Charles Michel, and the High Representative, Josep Borrell. "The difficulties of the Franco-German couple leave her an opening that she exploits," analyzes a European diplomat. On Ukraine, the Twenty-Seven, who were relatively united on the issue, did not make it a casus belli. Paris and Berlin may have found that she went too far in the promises she made to Volodymyr Zelensky to open the doors of the EU to him or to seize Russian assets in Europe. But, as the Social Democratic MEP Raphaël Glucksmann says, "she has managed to embody Europe in a time of war, and that is no small feat." On the Israeli-Palestinian conflict, things are quite different as the Europeans are deeply divided. Every statement at the level of the EU institutions must be carefully measured, which Ursula von der Leyen evidently forgot during her visit to Israel on October 13, which she did not inform anyone about. When she asserts Israel's right to defend itself after Hamas' terrorist attacks, without reminding that Israel must respect international law and protect civilian populations in Gaza, the Twenty-Seven are outraged. In doing so, she does not convey the position that the European foreign ministers agreed upon on October 10. Worse, she gives the impression to those who do not always grasp the subtleties of Brussels that Europe's voice is pro-Israeli. And she sends a disastrous message to the Southern countries that refuse to condemn the Russian invasion in Ukraine: Israel could exempt itself from this international law to which Western countries want to subject Moscow. Spain, Portugal, Ireland, Belgium, and Luxembourg, sensitive to the Palestinian cause, strongly criticize it. His remarks in Israel were "thoughtless and even unconscious," ruled Irish President Michael D. Higgins on October 16. Other member states, including Germany, which claims its duty to stand by Israel, are exasperated by this "mistake" which forces them to display their divisions and muddles their message. "We are all trying, and it's difficult, to remain as united as possible. And we are witnessing a pathetic parade in the Middle East of political leaders from institutions that no one knows," exclaims a European diplomat. An ego story "I don't understand what the President of the Commission has to do with Europe's foreign policy, which is not her responsibility," the Macronist MEP Nathalie Loiseau ranted on X (formerly Twitter) on October 14. "It's a blunder. She let herself be carried away by emotion. She is German, she was Angela Merkel's defense minister, she has often been to Israel in that capacity," argues a source. For a less benevolent senior European official, who highlights her management style as solitary and vertical, "she also lacks instinct, from being locked in her windowless apartment on the 13th floor of the Commission, where she had a studio set up for herself." It's also an ego story. Learning that Roberta Metsola, the President of the European Parliament, is invited by the Knesset, Ursula von der Leyen invites herself to accompany the Maltese, who had already been the first to visit Kyiv after the Russian aggression. The former minister of Angela Merkel does not like to let others take the spotlight. Charles Michel, equally concerned about his image, and with whom she maintains terrible relations, knows this well. On October 11, in the photo that Ursula von der Leyen posted on social media after the minute of silence organized in front of the European Parliament, attended by the presidents of the three EU institutions side by side, she cropped out the Belgian President of the Council. "In this institutional war, Michel and von der Leyen have gone crazy," judges a close associate of Emmanuel Macron. As unfortunate as it may be, this sequence should not prevent Ursula von der Leyen from seeking a second term after the European elections in June 2024. Since then, she has corrected her course, and her record - European post-Covid-19 recovery plan, joint vaccine purchases, financial and military support to Ukraine, Green Deal, digital regulation, etc. - remains her best asset. But "she must be careful. The member states [who must appoint her] have sent her signals," warns a European diplomat. At this stage, "we don't have a better candidate, but we are considering alternatives," adds one of his colleagues. The message is clear. Transatlantic seduction company More than the Israeli-Palestinian issue, it is Ursula von der Leyen's strong transatlanticism that worries Europeans. France, of course, which has cultivated its difference since Charles de Gaulle. But not only. Italy and even Germany, despite their strong ties with Washington, fear that she may not defend their economic interests well against the United States. "With Biden, one might have the impression that the Americans are benevolent. In reality, it's still 'America first' and the American agenda on industry, technology, or trade remains very harsh," notes a European diplomat. Joe Biden has an obsession: to involve Europe in his fight against Beijing. With Ursula von der Leyen, he has found an ally who has seemed ready, on several occasions, to sacrifice the interests of the continental industry on the altar of this fight. Just recently, the Twenty-Seven had to restrain her so that an unbalanced agreement in their favor was not signed during the United States - Europe summit held in Washington on October 20. "Von der Leyen was ready to do anything to get an agreement," says a source angrily. Especially since Washington is not rushing to meet the demands of the Europeans to amend the Inflation Reduction Act, which provides massive subsidies for the American green industry and makes them fear a wave of offshoring across the Atlantic. On March 10, the President of the Commission had already visited the White House to plead the case of the Old Continent. Not only did she not get anything, but she also said she was in favor - once again without consulting the capitals - of setting up "export controls" and "investments" for European companies, which everyone understood would concern movements towards China. Despite the recurring reproaches of the Twenty-Seven, Ursula von der Leyen persisted in her transatlantic seduction company, fueling doubts among those who, despite her denials, imagine her wanting to take the lead of NATO at the end of the year. On March 30, she delivered a particularly critical speech towards Beijing, which was appreciated in Washington, but much less in Berlin, Paris, and Rome. "Without any mandate, she defined the EU's foreign policy towards Beijing," still chokes a European diplomat today. In June, she presented a strategy to strengthen European economic security in which she tried, with no more success, to revive the projects outlined in Washington three months earlier. In July, the Commission hired American economist Fiona Scott-Morton for a strategic position before backing down under pressure from the Elysée, among others. In September, she announced the opening of an investigation into subsidies for Chinese electric vehicles, which certainly addressed a longstanding French request but was also seen as a gesture to Washington and greatly displeased Germany. "There are twenty-seven European leaders, she is invited to the Council table, but she is not at their level," a European diplomat quips.

title: Visiting Kiev, Ursula von der Leyen brings a breath of fresh air to Ukraine.
lead: While the war between Israel and Hamas monopolizes the world's attention, the President of the European Commission stated on Saturday that Kiev had made "excellent progress" in the reforms deemed necessary before opening potential negotiations for Ukraine's accession to the EU.
text_eng: It was a visit that the Ukrainian authorities needed. The President of the European Commission, Ursula von der Leyen, was in Kiev on Saturday, November 4th, for a day of meetings and speeches aimed at reaffirming the "unwavering" support of the European Union (EU) for Ukraine. Beyond the effects of the conflict between Israel and Hamas that have caused "attention to shift away from Ukraine," as acknowledged by President Volodymyr Zelensky during a press conference, the country is going through a critical period, between the supposed "fatigue" of its allies and the difficulties faced by the Ukrainian army on the front line. Ursula von der Leyen's sixth visit since the beginning of the Russian invasion took place a few days before the expected publication on Wednesday, November 8th, of a crucial report by the Commission on the progress made by Kiev in the seven reforms that were submitted to it at the same time as its candidate status for the EU in June 2022. The implementation of these seven recommendations, which touch on justice, corruption, national minorities, and media, is considered necessary before any potential accession negotiations for Ukraine to join the EU can begin. "You have made excellent progress, it is impressive to see, we will testify to this next week when the Commission presents its enlargement report," assured Ursula von der Leyen during a press conference. "Ukraine is ready and will continue the reforms," responded the Ukrainian President. "Ukraine has completed all the tasks [expected of it] within the framework of the seven recommendations from the EU Commission," stated Olga Stefanishyna, Deputy Prime Minister responsible for European and Euro-Atlantic integration on X (formerly Twitter). "Our progress is recognized. That being said, we must not forget that these are only the first steps towards full integration into the EU. Reforms are ongoing, and more are to come." Aligning with EU legislation The European Commission's report, to be published on November 8th, is expected to have a decisive impact on the Twenty-Seven, who will be tasked, at a summit in Brussels in mid-December, with voting for or against the opening of accession negotiations with Ukraine. In the case of a positive response, Kiev will then begin a lengthy process to complete its reforms and align with EU legislation in over thirty areas, including the rule of law and the economy. In June, during an initial assessment, the European Commission had stated that two of the seven recommendations had been fulfilled by Kiev. Ursula von der Leyen also mentioned the EU's efforts to deliver a €50 billion aid program to finance Ukraine for the next four years, for which she hopes to reach an agreement "by the end of the year." She also stated that a twelfth package of sanctions against the Russian Federation will be announced "next week" to the Member States. This package will include a list of sanctioned individuals, "new import and export bans," as well as "measures to strengthen the cap on oil prices" and "to continue the fight against sanctions evasion," she specified on X. The President of the Commission also committed to publishing a proposal before the end of the year to use the proceeds from Russian assets frozen in the EU since the beginning of the invasion. This initiative, coordinated with other countries and strongly desired by Kiev, would unlock more funds to help the country. "It would be a blow to the authoritarian Russian regime if, with the frozen assets, we started financing [European] integration of Ukraine," tweeted Olga Stefanishyna during a conference on October 21. Fatigue of the population after twenty months of war Ursula von der Leyen's visit brought some light a few days after the publication by The Economist of an article by the commander-in-chief of the Ukrainian armed forces, Valeri Zaloujny, in which he meticulously describes the technological leap necessary for his army to break out of a very unfavorable positional war for Ukraine. His remarks on the counteroffensive launched in early June - in a "deadlock" - as well as the mention of a "defeat" in case of delays in arms deliveries had an alarming echo in the country. Asked about this article on Saturday, President Volodymyr Zelensky began by mentioning the fatigue of the Ukrainian population after twenty months of war: "It is understandable. There is nothing wrong with that. But it is not a deadlock," he asserted, openly contradicting the commander-in-chief of the armed forces. Earlier in the day, Ihor Jovkva, deputy head of the president's office, was even more incisive in a comment on Ukrainian television, stating that a military leader should not make such statements, "because then we make the aggressor's job easier." On Sunday evening, a segment from an interview of the head of state by the American channel NBC News particularly circulated on social media. "War takes the best of us," says Volodymyr Zelensky, visibly moved, as a journalist asks him if he ever feels defeated. "But we are not ready to give our freedom to that damn terrorist Putin. That's it. That's why we fight."

title: "The War of the Worlds": welcome to the "warm war" between autocracies and the "collective West"
lead: As conflicts multiply, political scientist Bruno Tertrais describes the dynamics of a new geopolitical era marked by the rise of authoritarian neo-empires willing to challenge Western dominance over the world order.
text_eng: Book. From Ukraine to the Middle East through the Caucasus, conflicts are escalating on the edge of the European Union. Potential clashes are just as evident in Asia, particularly in the South China Sea, as Beijing increases pressure on Taiwan. The warning signs of this return to war have been very real for a few years now, even though Europe – starting with Paris and Berlin – had preferred not to see them for too long. This world in chaos is marked by the rise of authoritarian and revanchist neo-empires such as Russia, China, Turkey, or Iran, determined to challenge Western dominance in the international system. Awakened from a long slumber, these regimes want to reclaim, in the name of their past grandeur, what they believe is their place on the world stage. How to characterize this new situation? "We are both in the 1910s – the era of empire competition –, in the 1930s – the era of the threat of fascist states –, but even more so in the 1950s – the era of the nascent Cold War," writes Bruno Tertrais, who adds: "Welcome to the warm war, the third clash in a hundred years between the world of autocracies and that of democracies, in which revisionist states, dissatisfied with the status quo, will be responsible for indirect conflicts and major crises." The Deputy Director of the Foundation for Strategic Research (FRS), a passionate historian and political scientist, takes on the provocative title of H.G. Wells' famous science fiction novel "The War of the Worlds" to force us to confront this reality. While new blocs are forming, they are not as rigid as during the Cold War. Bruno Tertrais prefers to speak of "families" where different actors play their own cards according to their current interests. A middle ground that is neither the bipolar world of the Cold War nor the multipolar world before 1945. In this new global great game, Moscow and Beijing seek to attract countries from the global South towards them. "The strengthening of the Sino-Russian couple and the consolidation of the West give new vigor to the old distinction between tellurocracies [domination by land] and thalassocracies [by sea], the former embodied by Russia's Eurasian project and Chinese 'silk roads', and the latter by the Euro-Atlantic ensemble now accompanied by the states of the Indo-Pacific who wish the latter to remain free and open," he notes. At the same time, the political scientist paints a picture of a confrontation that is played out on all terrains including under the oceans, in outer space, or in cyberspace. Not just pessimistic, the illusions of the post-Cold War era are well and truly dead, as is the hope of reaping the "peace dividends," which was long the hope of Europeans. The German model is in crisis. It was based on low-cost energy, significant exports of high value-added industrial products to China, and defense ensured by NATO, with the United States being the largest contributor. Also, a certain French diplomacy model. France wanted to strengthen what remains of its position in the international arena by posing as a balancing power engaging in dialogue with Moscow as well as Beijing and the South. All of this shattered with the Russian aggression against Ukraine. A shock that was foreseeable, even if not inevitable, provided a policy of firmness, without any complacency, towards Putin and other authoritarian leaders was pursued. In the opening of his book, the deputy director of FRS mentions the Kosovo war, the bombings in Belgrade, including the one on the Chinese embassy by NATO, and Russia's failure, within the peace plan framework, to take control of a part of the province. An humiliation that neither Moscow nor Beijing have ever forgotten. Kosovo and its Western-backed independence will serve, in the Kremlin's propaganda narrative, to justify territorial occupations at the expense of Georgia in 2008 and Ukraine since 2014. The confrontation between Russia, led by Putin for almost a quarter of a century, and what he calls the "collective West" has only intensified on all crisis fronts, as lucidly demonstrated by Bruno Tertrais throughout this work. But his diagnosis, however harsh, is not entirely pessimistic about the chances of a "West that still has some strengths." In his conclusions, he mentions eight lessons to be drawn from these years of confrontations to strengthen democracies. Among them, the lesson of being willing to face a show of strength when necessary: "Accepting it means giving up the illusion of pretending to know our adversaries' interests better than they know them themselves and hoping to 'bring them to reason' solely through the virtue of our diplomacy."

title: War in Ukraine: Russian arms industry is growing in power
lead: The Ukrainian military headquarters admits the failure of its counter-offensive and alerts its Western allies about entering a phase of attrition warfare that benefits its adversary. The production of combat tanks in Russia is expected to reach 600 units in December.
text_eng: Concern is rising among Ukrainian leaders in the face of a new Russian offensive, especially with the growing power of the invader's arms industry. "Russia should not be underestimated. They have suffered heavy losses and expended a lot of ammunition. But they will maintain superiority in weapons, equipment, missiles, and ammunition for a long time," wrote Valeri Zaloujny, the Chief Commander of the Ukrainian Armed Forces, in an article published on November 1st in the British weekly The Economist. The general, whose popularity in Ukraine rivals, if not exceeds, that of President Volodymyr Zelensky, paints an alarming picture of the military outlook. According to him, the conflict with Russia has entered a "new phase," one of prolonged attrition with few maneuvers, but rather armies entrenched in static positions. "Currently, the conflict is gradually evolving into a war of positions, which history has taught us is always difficult to break out of, both for armed forces and a state," writes Mr. Zaloujny. He adds that this shift "plays into Russia's favor, allowing it to rebuild its military power." The senior Ukrainian officer sees a potential advantage in the technological edge that Western arms deliveries could provide, listing them in detail. "A war of positions is a long war with enormous risks for the Ukrainian armed forces and the state. New and innovative approaches can help shift from a war of positions back to a war of maneuvers," he believes. Roaming munitions But the Russian army also demonstrates a strong capacity for adaptation and innovation, notes the military officer, citing in particular the roaming Lancet munitions, the Orlan and Zala drones, which are "very difficult to counter." He reveals that Russian forces have found a way to counter American guided shells like the Excalibur through highly advanced electronic warfare and jamming systems. Equally concerning for Ukraine is General Zaloujny's admission of "loopholes in the legislation allowing evasion of mobilization," which "reduce citizens' motivation to serve in the army," facing human resources "almost three times superior" on the Russian side. Material-wise, the Kremlin is indeed stepping up by increasing its military budget by 68% in 2024. The defense industry is rapidly gearing up to supply the front. An investigation by the Ukrainian media Skhemy, based on satellite observations, shows the construction or expansion in recent months of several key factories in the Russian military-industrial complex. In the aerospace sector, there are the Gorbunov factory in Kazan (producing the strategic bombers Tu-16, Tu-22, and Tu-160), the Irkutsk aviation factory (Su-30 fighters), and the Yekaterinburg factory in the Urals (engines and gearboxes for military helicopters, the Mi-24 and Ka-52). Other factories, specializing in mechanical construction in Dubna (Kh-22, Kh-55, and Kh-101 missiles), in Kronstadt (military drones Orion, Helios), as well as the Kalashnikov company (Zala, Lancet, and Italmas loitering munitions), have also expanded their industrial facilities. The production of combat tanks from the Uralvagonzavod and Omsktransmash factories is expected to reach 600 units by 2023, only hindered by the manufacturing of cannons, while electronic component issues have been resolved. This is nearly equivalent to the number of tanks supplied to Kyiv during the same period by its Western allies.

title: Exhibition "Destinies of Verdun" at the Verdun Memorial, the war told from a human perspective.
lead: The exhibition traces the journey of twenty men and women whose lives were transformed by World War I. Many guided tours are organized at the memorial sites this Saturday, November 11th.
text_eng: Two million. This is the staggering number of soldiers who participated in the Battle of Verdun, the devastating fights that the French and Germans engaged in during the First World War. The exhibition "Destins de Verdun," open since May 24 at the Verdun Memorial, aims to explore the lives of some of them, often unknown, through a series of portraits. Twenty of these destinies have been selected by the two curators of the exhibition, Florence Guionneau-Joie and Nicolas Czubak. French or German, women or men, elevator operator or doctor: they form a kaleidoscope of society at that time. The building hosting the exhibition is located on the front line of the Battle of Verdun, transformed a few years after the 1914-1918 war into a memorial forest. Under the trees, the ground is still littered with bones, inactive shells, and gas masks. Here, more than 300,000 lives were lost, 400,000 men were wounded, and thousands of souls remained haunted by the memory of the "hell of Verdun." While military advances were minimal. This museum was erected to honor the memory of these men, whom the soldiers sought to keep alive after the end of the Second World War, as the memory of the "war to end all wars" began to fade. Mobilized on the front lines in 1914, the writer Maurice Genevoix became the voice of these veterans eager to pass on their story. Known for his accounts of the Great War, compiled in "Ceux de 14" (Flammarion, 1949, last reissued in 2013), he chaired the National Committee for the Memory of Verdun, which works for the creation of the Verdun Memorial. Inaugurated in 1967, the building embodies a unifying vision of the memory of Verdun, devoid of its patriotic component. The handshake between President François Mitterrand and German Chancellor Helmut Kohl on September 22, 1984, in front of the Douaumont ossuary, became the symbol of this Franco-German reconciliation. Pedagogical and immersive setup Thirty-two years later, François Hollande and Angela Merkel continued this gesture by commemorating together the centenary of the battle on May 29, 2016. On this occasion, the memorial had been renovated: the building had been renovated and expanded after three years of work. The establishment also established a scientific committee, led by historian Antoine Prost, who modernized the museum and allowed the telling of the Battle of Verdun on site through a pedagogical and sometimes immersive setup. The two permanent spaces, inaugurated in 2016, now allow us to delve into the lives of those who experienced it. In a maze of objects and archives, we discover the evocative power of the "hell of Verdun." Period photos and reconstruction films, where former soldiers often play their own roles, are shown on a large screen placed at the center of the tour. A fake muddy ground, cannons, and vintage trucks plunge into the somber landscape of the trenches. Every week, French fighters cross paths with those who come to replace them on the "sacred way," a road that connects the rear to the front. And discover the horror of war on the haggard faces of the survivors. The emotions experienced by the fighters are then conveyed through multiple testimonies. A text by Romain Darchy, a soldier stuck for several hours in the mud, describes a fear shared by many fighters: the fear of dying anonymously. Gas masks embody the horror of the battlefield, where it is so difficult to breathe. A four-leaf clover and a soldier's amulet express the hope of surviving. The portraits of pilot Eugène Bullard and boxer Eugène Criqui, exhibited at the Verdun Memorial in 2021. VERDUN MEMORIAL/BATTLEFIELD The exhibition "Destinies of Verdun" is located one floor above. Striking faces are revealed in a life-size photographic frieze. Their lives are unfolded in the form of audio narratives, available on mediation terminals. French actors have lent their voices to present these unique journeys, where heroism often encounters misfortune. For example, the story of Eugène Criqui, a Parisian boxer and war-wounded soldier, is narrated by Mathieu Amalric. On the front lines, a German bullet severs his tongue and shatters his jaw. Despite these severe injuries, he returns to boxing. An iron device welds his jaw back together, forcing him to transform his playing style. He finds his way back to victory and becomes the world featherweight champion in 1923. Increase in attendance The journey continues, outside the walls, on the battlefield. Other destinies are presented around the forts of Douaumont and Vaux, connected to the memorial. Omar Sy, the exhibition's sponsor, tells the story of Moussa Dansako, a Senegalese tirailleur who took part in the offensive at Fort Douaumont. On the night of October 25-26, 1916, he saves four wounded brothers-in-arms. His forgotten destiny reminds us of the significant mobilization of colonial troops from Africa. Nicolas Barret, who has been at the helm of the memorial since 2021, is pleased that the site's attendance is experiencing a new surge since the Covid-19 crisis. "The establishment recorded 300,000 entries in 2023, a 30% increase compared to 2022," he explains. According to him, international events, which have brought back the specter of Verdun, may have played a role in this increase in attendance. During a visit to Paris on March 23, 2022, Volodymyr Zelensky referenced it in his speech to the French Parliament: "The damage in Mariupol and other Ukrainian cities recalls the ruins of Verdun." Memorial tourism also attracts many curious visitors interested in the history of their fighting ancestors. The last part of the exhibition is dedicated to this family aspect of memory. Three testimonies, sometimes moving, are heard from descendants of soldiers who followed in their ancestors' footsteps. On Tuesday, October 31, one of these voices is unexpectedly encountered. Peter Sauter, 71 years old, is the son of a German soldier wounded at Verdun. For thirty years, he has been trying to reconstruct his father's story. On this rainy day, he has just combed through the forest of Verdun in the company of his grandchildren, nephews, and nieces, who are involved in his quest. To encourage this process of transmission, of which Peter Sauter is the embodiment, the exhibition closes with a call for testimonies: "You too may have a Verdun destiny in your family." The trenches of Verdun's memory have not finished being dug.

title: In Russia, the return from the Ukrainian front of murderers pardoned by Putin
lead: At least seventeen convicted murderers have been granted a presidential pardon after fighting in Ukraine, according to available data. And at least three of them have already killed again.
text_eng: The case is still fresh, the murder is less than four years old. A particularly barbaric femicide: a rejected boyfriend who proclaims in advance his intention to kill; a victim, Vera Pekhteleva, a 23-year-old student, fell into a trap as she came to retrieve her belongings; a rape; one hundred eleven wounds on the young woman's body, inflicted with punches and a knife; and finally death by strangulation, with the cord of a clothes iron. The police's reaction, or rather their lack of reaction, to this sordid incident that occurred in 2020 in Kemerovo, Siberia, was shocking. During the three and a half hours of torture and then loud agony of Vera Pekhteleva, neighbors called the security forces at least seven times, with no response: not enough patrols, officers on break, and a profound disinterest in domestic violence. Since then, two police officers have been sentenced to short suspended prison terms for "negligence," without suspension. The murderer, Vladislav Kanious, on the other hand, received a seventeen-year prison sentence. This was in July 2022. Today, he is back in Kemerovo, free. Recruiting in prison The victim's parents learned this in early November, after turning to the justice system. Vladislav Kanious was granted a presidential pardon in April, probably at the time he was joining the Russian army to fight in Ukraine. At that time, the Ministry of Defense had already regained the ability to recruit in prison, previously reserved for the Wagner mercenary group. According to the common saying, after a few months on the front lines, he "redeemed his guilt through spilled blood." The parents are devastated, also worried, but powerless. The judicial case was still to be examined by the Supreme Court, at the request of the civil parties, when the murderer was released. Kanious even stopped paying the monthly compensations he owes them. "We are hundreds in the same situation," says Oksana Pekhteleva, Vera's mother. Their number is unknown, but the independent and exiled media outlet Agentstvo made a sensation on November 9 by compiling seventeen cases of murderers returning from Ukraine after being pardoned by Vladimir Putin. With no public list existing, the number is a low estimate: these are only stories shocking enough, or known enough, for local sites to report on them. Agentstvo's compilation has the feel of a chamber of horrors.
