It includes stories of young girls raped and then killed or elderly ladies murdered for a meager loot (the vast majority of victims are women), of hardened psychopaths or alcoholics in decline, of dismembered, burned, decapitated bodies thrown into the river... Many of the murderers are repeat offenders and, in most cases, the crime that led them to prison is relatively recent. 2018, 2019, 2020... Buried as "heroes," all are now free, often back in their hometown or city, burdened by the trauma - or the thrill - of their war experience, protected from any criticism by the law that prohibits "discrediting the participants of the special military operation," the official name for the war in Ukraine. Other cases are reported by local websites: the return of coffins of former murderers who died in combat, the number of which is equally difficult to estimate. These individuals are entitled to hero burials, which sometimes causes tensions at the local level. Another difficult fact to accept is that those who return alive only fought for six months (most of the time in the ranks of the Wagner Group, in units with significant losses), while fathers mobilized in September 2022 are still at the front, with no prospect of returning soon. State media are instructed to remain silent on the subject, but Kremlin spokesperson Dmitri Peskov was still questioned on November 10. He reiterated that there are two procedures to obtain a presidential pardon: one, complex and lengthy, exceptional; the other, simplified, "under bullets and shells, through bloodshed." Increase in domestic violence The issue is even more sensitive as it recalls the difficulties posed by the return of fighters. If they are pampered with significant salaries or reserved university placements, the question of violence inevitably arises. By the end of 2022, official statistics already showed a general increase in crime in Russia, particularly an unprecedented rise in murders, attempted murders, and domestic violence in the past twenty years. Since then, no figures have been published. Of the seventeen pardoned murderers identified by Agentstvo, three have already resumed killing since their return from Ukraine. In general, around thirty murders have been committed in the past year by former combatants - and about half by Wagner veterans, who heavily recruited inmates convicted of various crimes or offenses, not limited to homicide, from prisons. These cases only represent those reported in local media where the alleged perpetrator is named. They also share extreme brutality. In just the first few days of October, three murders were committed by suspected Wagner members: in the Krasnoyarsk region (Siberia), one set fire to the neighbors' apartment, killing two people; in Nizhny Novgorod region (northeast of Moscow), another burned his sister alive; and in Lipetsk (about 510 kilometers south of the capital), a third killed his partner's 4-year-old daughter. Furthermore, on Tuesday, November 14, the Telegram channel Baza reported that the organizer (and not the sponsor, never identified) of the murder of journalist Anna Politkovskaya in 2006 had been released from prison due to the war in Ukraine. Sergei Khadjikurbanov, a former police officer who was supposed to be released in 2034, has reportedly been in the "special military operation" zone since the end of 2022.

title: A month after the storm that ravaged the east of Libya, the dangerous game of the status quo supporters
lead: Instead of prompting introspection on the governance failures between the authorities in the west and east of the country, the cyclone Daniel that hit Derna on September 10 fueled security reflexes amidst rivalries over the reconstruction.
text_eng: There was no "Derna effect" on the Libyan political scene. Neither a citizen revolution, nor authorities' mea culpa, nor an electroshock unlocking the institutional deadlock. One month after the dramatic floods caused by the storm Daniel in the city of Cyrenaica (eastern Libya) - with 4,000 deaths according to a provisional official toll - a heavy silence descends on the traumatized population, especially in the east of the country living under the relentless rule of Marshal Khalifa Haftar. The disaster in Derna leaves Libya doubly wounded: both in mourning and locked down. The protest movement that seemed to emerge on September 11 in the coastal city, amidst the ruins, initiated by residents demanding accountability from failing local authorities, was quickly quashed by Marshal Haftar's forces affiliated with the Libyan National Army controlling all of Cyrenaica. The swift crackdown immediately neutralized any risk of a citizen movement similar to the one that had emerged in late August 2020. For nearly a week, both Tripolitania and Cyrenaica had been the scene of gatherings where the population expressed their frustration with the deteriorating socio-economic situation. Unprecedented since the 2011 revolution, the wave seemed to herald the awakening of a civil society rebelling against the country's control by predatory militias. Nothing of the sort happened in post-cyclone Libya, due to repression. The public stage was rather monopolized by militias from the East (such as Saddam Haftar's Tariq Ibn Ziyad brigade, the son of Marshal Haftar) and the West (the Tripoli 444 brigade) who, although militarily opposed until 2020, "staged their fraternization" around the aid provided to Derna, notes Wolfram Lacher, a researcher at the German Institute for International and Security Affairs based in Berlin. If these sudden reunions between East and West, whose fracture has shaped the politico-military landscape in Libya since the summer of 2014, had been without ulterior motives, there would be cause for rejoicing. However, they were "exploited by armed groups to prevent accountability for the disaster in Derna," explains Mr. Lacher. Stalemate prevails in Libya. Since the ceasefire in October 2020, which marked Marshal Haftar's failure to seize the capital, Tripolitania and Cyrenaica have reached a form of mutual non-aggression pact. Each side has its own executive authority: the "Government of National Unity" of Abdel Hamid Dbeibah in Tripoli (recognized by the international community) and the "Government of National Stability" of Osama Hammad linked to Marshal Haftar in the East. Their formal rivalry is actually accompanied by fruitful backstage arrangements, particularly concerning the redistribution of oil wealth. And each one is perfectly fine with it. It is as if the two political-security apparatuses, both in the West and the East, are primarily seeking to survive. And to maintain their rents carved out of predatory networks while pretending, to the international community, to be discussing an illusory reunification. Not only did the tragedy of Derna not disrupt this amicable partition - a "sharing of the cake," as many Libyans denounce - but paradoxically it has reinforced it. As the reconstruction plans for Derna begin to take shape, each side is trying to preempt future funding. An intense struggle for influence is thus unfolding around the control of international funds to rebuild Derna. "Everyone is looking for international approval to win the race for funding," summarizes Mr. Lacher. The risk of paralysis, or at least of stagnation, is evident. The reinforcement of the status quo takes on another form: an unprecedented authoritarian tightening. The repression in Derna illustrates an obvious anxiety on Haftar's part in the face of popular demands for accountability. "There is currently anxiety and nervousness in him," emphasizes Oussama Assed, director of the Libya Center for Strategic and Future Studies, based in Tripoli. The director of the Benghazi branch of this think tank, Siraj Daghman, was actually arrested on October 1 following a meeting on the responsibilities for the disaster in Derna. Tribal Clashes Another manifestation of Haftar's "nervousness" was the order given on October 6 to his forces to capture Mahdi Al-Barghathi, former defense minister of the former government of Fayez Al-Sarraj (in power from 2016 to 2021) in Tripoli and a known opponent of the marshal, whom he had fought militarily. After a long exile in Tripolitania, Al-Barghati had returned to his city of Benghazi after apparently obtaining guarantees regarding his personal safety. These guarantees apparently were not enough as his return led to a mobilization of Haftar's forces aiming to capture him. This resulted in tribal clashes, as Al-Barghati's clan is affiliated with the large Awagir tribe, one of the most important in Benghazi. Tensions in Benghazi between the Awagir, historically rooted in the city, and the Ferjani, Haftar's tribe originating from Tripolitania, are not new in the Eastern metropolis. But their reappearance in the post-Derna context is a worrying challenge for Haftar, especially since his rapprochement with Vladimir Putin, which he sees as an insurance policy, is not universally supported in his regional stronghold. Behind the apparent deadlock, the lines are shifting silently.

title: "In China, the 'new silk roads' are adopting a less spectacular but perhaps more sustainable pace."
lead: Beijing will inject ten times less money than in its previous cooperation program, but these investments will be made in less expensive and more "green" projects, notes Frédéric Lemaître in his column.
text_eng: What do the Tchaikovsky Moscow Conservatory of Music, the National Bank of Serbia, the road network around Dakar, the port of Chancay in Peru, a high-tech food preservation research center in South Africa, the Mongolian forest, and a future global women's chess tournament have in common? They will all soon receive support from China as part of its "Belt and Road Initiative" investment program. These are just seven examples out of a total of 369 practical cooperation agreements that China has signed with around 150 countries and 30 international organizations during the third "Belt and Road" forum, officially known as the "Belt and Road Initiative" (BRI), presided over by Chinese leader Xi Jinping in Beijing on October 17 and 18. While much attention was given to the red carpet rolled out for Russian President Vladimir Putin and numerous speeches that did not mention Ukraine or the Middle East, the essential information from these two days is that China is continuing its global investment program while reorganizing it. Xi Jinping did not express any reservations about the past ten years, but a close examination of the signed agreements shows that China has still taken some criticisms into account. While large projects are not entirely out of fashion, priority will be given to "small but beautiful" investments, as described by the Chinese president. Additionally, there are more "green projects," from solar power plants in Poland and Romania to the establishment of a research center on combating drought and desertification in Saudi Arabia. Furthermore, the fight against corruption is becoming more systematic. Promoting "an honest BRI with an evaluation of the integrity of the companies involved" is one of the eight priorities announced by Xi Jinping. Budgetary realism seems to be prevailing. It appears that after an impressive start, the "Belt and Road Initiative" is settling into a more sustainable pace. In 2013, China was both overly optimistic and proud. The goal was to establish the stature of the new emperor at the center of the world, which has been achieved. Now, realism, especially in terms of budget, is key. For the coming years, Xi Jinping has announced lines of credit totaling around 100 billion euros, a significant amount but ten times less than the commitments of the past decade (around 1 trillion euros). Should we speak of failure? Yes and no. Who can deny that China plays a more significant role on the international stage today than before? The "Belt and Road Initiative" has played a major part in this. While China is strong, it is often more feared than loved, although this is not true everywhere. "Africans have eyes only for her," notes a European diplomat who has traveled extensively in over twenty African countries. But the closer one gets geographically to China, the more intimidating it becomes. In Beijing, the most pro-Chinese speeches were given by the Argentine president, Alberto Fernandez, and by the Brazilian Dilma Rousseff, president of the New Development Bank of the BRICS. In contrast, Asian speakers showed more restraint. A sign of the times: unlike the two previous forums, China did not offer foreign leaders - who were also fewer in number than before - to sign a joint declaration. As for the Europeans, apart from the Hungarian Prime Minister, Viktor Orban, and the Serbian President, Aleksandar Vucic, they were reportedly not unhappy that the presence of the Russian president in Beijing provided them with an excellent excuse not to make the trip. If Putin did not get what he hoped for - the signing of an agreement for a new gas pipeline connecting Russia and China via Mongolia - he wholeheartedly supported the "new Silk Roads," connecting them directly to the Eurasian Economic Union that he is trying to build. Even if he has the will, Putin no longer has the means to compete with Beijing.

title: Vladimir Putin announces Russia's withdrawal from the treaty banning nuclear tests.
lead: The Comprehensive Nuclear-Test-Ban Treaty was signed in 1996 but was never ratified due to a lack of sufficient states coming together. The United States has never ratified it.
text_eng: Russian President Vladimir Putin signed on Thursday, November 2, the law on the withdrawal of the ratification of the Comprehensive Nuclear-Test-Ban Treaty (CTBT), against the backdrop of the conflict in Ukraine and tensions with Western countries. The CTBT was opened for signature in 1996 but never entered into force because it was not ratified – a necessary step for its entry into force – by a sufficient number of states among the forty-four countries that possessed nuclear facilities at the time of its creation. In early October, Mr. Putin had announced that Russia could withdraw its ratification of the CTBT in response to the United States' failure to ratify it. "I am not ready to say whether we should resume testing or not," added the Russian president, while touting the development of new powerful missiles capable of carrying nuclear warheads. "Odious attitude of the United States" Since the start of the conflict in Ukraine in February 2022, the Russian president has sent mixed signals regarding the use of nuclear weapons, deploying tactical nuclear weapons in Belarus, its closest ally, during the summer of 2023. The law on the withdrawal of the CTBT ratification was first adopted by the State Duma, the lower house of the Russian Parliament, before being unanimously approved by the upper house at the end of October. The President of the State Duma, Vyacheslav Volodin, described this vote as a "response to the odious attitude of the United States towards their obligations on maintaining global security." In late October, Russia also conducted test launches of ballistic missiles to prepare its forces for a "massive nuclear strike" in retaliation. Russian nuclear doctrine envisions a "strictly defensive" use of nuclear weapons in response to an attack on Russia with weapons of mass destruction or in the event of aggression with conventional weapons "threatening the very existence of the state." In February, Russia also suspended its participation in the New START nuclear disarmament treaty signed with the United States in 2010, the last bilateral agreement between Russians and Americans.

title: Europe uncovers Russian spies operating under diplomatic cover.
lead: The Twenty-Seven are sharing information and have strengthened their controls. Since spring 2022, according to information from "Le Monde," around 200 visa applications made by Russian diplomats have been refused.
text_eng: In parallel to the war it has been waging in Ukraine since February 24, 2022, Russia is secretly multiplying its attempts to rebuild its spy networks at the heart of the Western camp. Moscow is indeed seeking to compensate for the expulsion, worldwide, of 452 Russian intelligence officers operating under diplomatic cover, as a retaliation for the aggression against the Ukrainian neighbor. Since March 2022, France has declared 41 members of Russian diplomatic personnel persona non grata, forcing them to leave its territory. According to information from Le Monde, obtained from French diplomatic sources, since the spring of 2022, around 200 visa applications made by Russian diplomats to be assigned within the European Union (EU) have been rejected by the countries concerned. The twenty-seven member states have strengthened controls to prevent Russian civilian and military intelligence services from sending their agents back to Europe by posing as diplomats and benefiting from consular protection. This vigilance is based on procedures already in place within the Schengen area, which regulates the conditions of access to European territory. Each visa application is examined by intelligence services, the Ministry of Foreign Affairs, and the Ministry of the Interior of the country where the diplomats have submitted their documents. "The landscape of Russian espionage remains very concerning," says a senior French official with responsibilities within the intelligence community. "But now, we believe that the shield is as strong as the sword." Many intelligence officers find themselves stranded in Russia, some without assignments, as they are considered suspicious by the EU. Others are deemed "burned," having had their diplomatic cover exposed after investigation. In both cases, they can no longer join a diplomatic mission in the Schengen area. The increased vigilance of EU countries has forced Russia to try to bypass the visa barrier. Remote recruitment According to French authorities, the Russian security services, the FSB, have stepped out of their usual domestic scope to address these deficiencies. They have been asked, in particular, to recruit and manage sources remotely, from Russia. This recruitment work is carried out solely through technological tools, via social networks and other encrypted messaging groups. This method is more random than field recruitment and targets "proxies" present in Europe, especially in France, sympathizers of the Russian cause, and exiles. Another lesson learned by European intelligence services regarding the activity of their Russian counterparts since spring 2022 concerns the significant mobilization of their technical and human resources in Ukraine and the hunt for traitors within Russia itself. "A large part of their intelligence tools are focused on supporting the military effort currently on Ukrainian soil," notes the same senior French official. Visa applications from Russian diplomats are not the only ones closely scrutinized by EU members. For example, the General Directorate for Internal Security (DGSI) also provides opinions on applications submitted by ordinary Russian citizens who may, on occasion, conceal profiles hostile to French interests. This was the case following the visa application of Ioulia Chifmanovitch in the summer of 2022. Initially, the French consular services did not suspect anything. The war in Ukraine had led many Russians to want to leave temporarily to escape the sanctions imposed on Moscow, the "partial" mobilization, or simply to travel. And the most dangerous spies of Vladimir Putin, the killers and saboteurs, are rarely women. However, exercising caution, they waited to receive the intelligence services' opinion before giving their response. Ioulia Chifmanovitch was not unknown to the DGSI. She was denied a visa due to her proximity to her partner, Alexandre Koulaguine, a member of unit 29155 of the 161st Special Training Center of the Russian military intelligence services, a key player in Russian interference in Europe for nearly a decade. Her presence had even been traced in France alongside her partner in 2017. This unit is held responsible, notably, for the 2018 assassination attempt in Salisbury, southern England, using Novichok on Sergei Skripal, a defector who had sought refuge in the UK. Ioulia Chifmanovitch's visa application for France in 2022, against the backdrop of the war in Ukraine, demonstrated that unit 29155 was still operational and was now opting for new profiles, such as women or very young agents, to evade Western surveillance and European arrest warrants issued after the Skripal case. The details of her application were shared with allied services to enrich the list of Russian agents, whether itinerant or acting under diplomatic cover.

title: In the Netherlands, the justice system is convicting companies that have circumvented the embargo on Russia.
lead: Neither the names of the companies nor those of the individuals convicted for helping Moscow build the Crimean bridge have been revealed. The companies involved supplied equipment and provided technical assistance to the designers of the structure, despite the European sanctions imposed after the annexation of the Ukrainian peninsula.
text_eng: The investigations were delayed and conducted in a particularly discreet manner. The first convictions, announced on Friday, October 13, were only made known through a press release from the national prosecutor's office: the Dutch justice system has penalized four companies and eight individuals for their involvement in the construction of the Crimea Bridge, or "Putin Bridge," a 19-kilometer-long structure inaugurated in May 2018 and built to connect annexed Crimea to Russia. It plays an important role in the war that Moscow is waging in southern Ukraine. The names of these four companies, fined a total of 160,000 euros and, for one of them, ordered to repay 71,000 euros in profits? Unknown. Just like the identity and exact role of the eight individuals who will have to perform work sentences ranging from twenty to sixty hours. The press release that made these essentially symbolic decisions public only specified that the firms in question intentionally supplied materials and provided technical assistance to the bridge designers, in violation of the European and American sanctions imposed in March 2014 after the annexation of Crimea by Russia. 75 ongoing cases These revelations are all the more shocking in the Netherlands because in July 2014, 196 Dutch citizens perished in the crash of Malaysia Airlines flight MH17, which was supposed to connect Amsterdam to Kuala Lumpur. The plane was shot down over Ukrainian territory by a Russian-made missile. The disaster claimed a total of 298 victims. In November 2022, the Dutch justice system sentenced two Russians and a Ukrainian separatist to life imprisonment in absentia for their involvement in the plane's destruction. The press played a crucial role in uncovering the collaboration between companies and Russia. In September 2017, a regional newspaper in the province of Gelderland, in the central-eastern part of the kingdom, mentioned the involvement of two small companies in supplying drilling equipment and providing technical assistance to the bridge designers. Eight months later, the national prosecutor's office initiated an investigation involving five other companies. The names of the chemical and paint giant AkzoNobel and Mammoet, specialized in heavy object transport, were mentioned, but their potential involvement was not proven. In total, 75 cases are currently open, and an unknown number of suspects are in detention awaiting trial. Secrecy also surrounds these proceedings. Vague nature of the rules Investigations conducted by journalists based on documents obtained under public institutions' transparency have, in any case, demonstrated the unclear nature of the rules for embargo control. In March 2023, as reported by the NRC newspaper on Saturday, officials from the Ministry of Justice admitted their ignorance regarding the information to be collected to verify the correct application of European rules. During a meeting at the level of the Twenty-Seven, the Dutch senior official present concluded: "My feeling so far is that most Member States do not really know what they should do. Not all of them have legal provisions regarding sanctions." The Dutch government itself reportedly only became truly concerned about the application of restrictive measures towards Russia in April 2022. Hence the apparent indifference of the companies involved in circumventing the embargo. Some had established subsidiaries in Russia to evade it, and all had also signed contracts with their clients containing clauses stating that European sanctions could not be used as "excuses" for the non-delivery of the products and services in question.

title: From Russia, Hamas rules out any release of hostages without a ceasefire.
lead: An official delegation from the Palestinian Islamist movement was received on Thursday by the Russian Deputy Minister of Foreign Affairs. Since October 7th, Moscow has clearly taken a hostile stance towards Israel in hopes of weakening the Western powers.
text_eng: This is a very rare move at a time when Hamas leaders' contacts with the outside world are mostly limited to discreet consultations in Arab countries that host them. On Thursday, October 26, Russia officially hosted a delegation from the Palestinian Islamist movement led by one of its members of the political bureau, Moussa Abou Marzouk. First announced by the official news agency RIA Novosti, this visit was confirmed by the spokesperson of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs, Maria Zakharova. It was later revealed that this delegation was received by Mikhail Bogdanov, who is both the Deputy Minister of Foreign Affairs and Vladimir Putin's representative for the Middle East. On the same day, Mr. Bogdanov also met with the Iranian Deputy Minister of Foreign Affairs, Ali Bagheri Kani, as reported by Russian diplomacy. After the meeting with Hamas, Russian diplomatic sources anonymously assured news agencies that the focus was on the issue of hostages held in Gaza since the terrorist attack on October 7 in Israel. Moscow reportedly insisted on the "immediate release of foreign hostages," according to these sources, and discussed "issues related to the evacuation of Russian citizens and other foreign citizens from the Palestinian enclave." New positioning According to the latest figures provided by the authorities, Russia has twenty nationals among the victims of the October 7 attack, and three others are reportedly being held hostage in Gaza. Mr. Abou Marzouk did not respond to questions from Le Monde, but another member of the delegation, identified as "Abou Hamid," told the Russian newspaper Kommersant that the release of "civilian prisoners" was impossible without a ceasefire, essential to "locate" the people who have been captured by "various Palestinian groups" in the Gaza Strip. According to this representative, fifty hostages have been killed so far "due to Israeli bombings." In addition to Moscow's unusual concern for its nationals held hostage, the visit of such a delegation, which is not the first for a group that Russia does not designate as a terrorist organization, also corresponds to the new positioning taken by Moscow in this matter, for very political purposes. Russia maintains significant leverage in the region by engaging with almost all actors, from the Middle East to the Arab-Persian Gulf. Playing a role in any negotiation would be a way for the Kremlin to refute international isolation and the pariah role that the West would like to see it confined to. Comparison with Leningrad Since October 7, Moscow has notably taken a decidedly anti-Israeli turn, as evidenced by its initiatives at the UN as well as official reactions after the Hamas attack. Russian diplomacy did not condemn the group or even use the word "terrorism." Vladimir Putin himself waited more than a week to call Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu, theoretically a close partner, and compared the siege of Gaza to the blockade of Leningrad, which resulted in approximately 800,000 deaths during World War II. This Russian shift is mainly explained by the desire to weaken the Western front, to press on its internal fractures and divergences with the "Global South," which Moscow hopes to align with its cause. In its very first reaction after October 7, the Kremlin leader had only words for the "American failure" in the region. The main target is the support for Ukraine that Russian diplomacy aims to weaken. On Thursday, the Hamas delegation made it known that they appreciated this stance. "The Russian Federation is a friendly country to the Palestinian people and supports all representatives of this people," "Abu Hamid" praised to Kommersant. The Israeli side called the visit of Hamas members to Moscow a "shameful move," but the situation forces them to maintain a cautious stance towards initiatives and comments coming from Russia.

title: The European Commission recommends the opening of accession negotiations with Ukraine and Moldova.
lead: The Commission's requests, including the granting of candidate status to Georgia under certain conditions, will be submitted to the European Council in December, which must make a unanimous decision.
text_eng: In front of the European Parliament, which was preparing to vote on his nomination to head the Commission in July 2014, Jean-Claude Juncker had stated that there would be no further enlargement of the European Union (EU) during his term. "The EU needs to take a break in its enlargement process in order to consolidate what has been done with the Twenty-Eight," he explained, noting that thirteen states had joined since 2004. He did not imagine at the time that the United Kingdom would leave the European community. Nor did he imagine that, nearly ten years later, the Twenty-Seven would be outlining the contours of a EU with more than thirty member states. "Enlargement is a call from history, it is the natural horizon of our EU," said Ursula von der Leyen, who succeeded Jean-Claude Juncker at the end of 2019. In line with this logic, the Commission proposed opening accession negotiations with Ukraine and Moldova, as well as granting candidate status, subject to conditions, to Georgia. The EU executive also announced measures aimed at providing reassurance to the six Western Balkan countries that have been waiting in the EU's waiting room for years (Albania, Montenegro, Serbia, North Macedonia, Bosnia and Herzegovina, and Kosovo) and may fear being overtaken by Kiev and Chisinau. After being put on hold, "enlargement is back," according to a senior European official. The invasion of Ukraine by Russia on February 24, 2022, has radically changed the situation, and even the member states most resistant to any further enlargement, notably France, have been forced to change their stance. In this new geopolitical and security context, "our neighbors must choose" between "democracy" and "authoritarian rule," between the EU and Russia, Ursula von der Leyen assured. Ukraine and Moldova have made their choice. Since being granted candidate status in June 2022, despite the war for one and the strong tensions at the Russian border for the other, they have multiplied reforms so that negotiations can begin. According to the European Commission, Kiev and Chisinau have completed "more than 90%" of what was asked of them in terms of fighting corruption, respecting minority rights, judicial independence, and measures to reduce the influence of oligarchs on power. "Not historic," according to Volodymyr Zelensky. Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky praised the "historic step" taken by the Commission. "Our state must be in the EU. Ukrainians deserve it," he emphasized, as the Ukrainian counteroffensive stalls, the Israeli-Palestinian conflict diverts the attention of its allies, and the question of the sustainability of American support arises. For her part, Maia Sandu, the Moldovan president, stated that her country "is firmly committed to the path of EU accession and [will] continue to work tirelessly to achieve this goal." The announcements on Wednesday mark an important step towards the accession of Kiev and Chisinau to the EU, but they do not guarantee the outcome of a process fraught with obstacles. It will already be necessary for the Twenty-Seven, whose heads of state and government are set to meet on December 14 and 15 in Brussels, to unanimously adopt the Commission's proposal. While the vast majority of them are expected to support it, Hungary, which is using all means of pressure at its disposal to obtain the EU funds that the Commission has blocked in the name of the rule of law, could voice dissent. The government of Viktor Orban has already indicated that it may oppose the opening of negotiations if Ukraine does not provide enough guarantees to the Hungarian minority living in the western part of its territory. "Once the Council gives its green light, we can start negotiations immediately," asserts Ursula von der Leyen. "As of the evening of December 15, the Commission will send teams to Kiev and Chisinau," adds a senior official, to begin assessing the tasks ahead and prepare for what comes next. However, to move forward and formally open accession negotiations, Ukraine and Moldova will need to complete the reforms requested of them in June 2022. The Commission will review progress in March 2024, but it is "fully confident" in the ability of both countries to make progress. Legislation to be thoroughly reviewed Assuming this step is also successfully completed, Volodymyr Zelensky and Maia Sandu will need to undertake a profound overhaul of their legislation to align it with EU practices, not only in terms of the rule of law, but also in areas such as free movement, energy market, social regulation, competition, combating climate change, economic environment, financial services, and agriculture. In total, thirty-five "chapters" will need to be opened and closed for Ukraine and Moldova to acquire the necessary "acquis communautaire" for their EU accession. The experience – Serbia has been negotiating since 2013, Montenegro since 2012... – shows that the matter is not simple. One thing is certain: even though the political context is more favorable today, the war will not make Ukraine's task easier. Especially since Kiev cannot join the Twenty-Seven as long as the conflict persists: the treaties would require the EU to go to war alongside Ukraine under the mutual assistance clause. Compared to the Western Balkans, "Moldova and Ukraine are moving at full speed," according to a senior European official, who assures that the Commission, for its part, is determined to move as quickly as possible. "The preliminary phase, which allows for an assessment of the projects to be carried out, usually takes one to two years. We intend to complete it in six months," this source illustrates. On Wednesday, Ursula von der Leyen delivered a passionate plea for enlargement. "Past enlargements have shown the enormous benefits that both candidate countries and the EU gain. We all win," she stated. Not all member states see things the same way, as the entry of Ukraine and its 40 million inhabitants into the EU would deeply alter the financial, economic, and political balances of the European integration. Some countries, including France and Germany, believe that prior reforms are needed in both the functioning of the EU and its policies, should it absorb a country as large and as poor as Ukraine. This is also expected to be a challenging task.

title: Ukraine is preparing to face a new winter of war
lead: Traumatized by the massive bombing campaign on the country's energy infrastructure during the winter of 2022-2023, the population is anticipating and equipping themselves with gas cylinders and generators. Faced with a general decline in morale, President Zelensky is calling for unity.
text_eng: Winter is gradually setting in, and Ukrainian authorities expect Russia to once again target its energy infrastructure to paralyze the country. Several nighttime attacks have already caused power outages since mid-September. However, so far, relatively few Russian missiles have targeted power plants and heating systems compared to the winter of 2022-2023, and Kiev fears that Moscow may be waiting for subzero temperatures, while preparing supplies, to strike these sites massively. "As winter approaches, Russian terrorists will try to cause more damage," warned Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky on the X platform (formerly Twitter) after a night of Russian bombings in ten regions of the country on November 3. The previous year, hundreds of Russian missiles and Shahed kamikaze drones had hit critical infrastructure. "Nearly 60%" of Ukraine's electricity production capacity had been damaged, according to Mariia Tsaturian, communications director of the public operator Ukrenergo, and "all major thermal and hydroelectric power plants were attacked." This campaign of strikes, which took place between October 2022 and March, also caused damage to over 43% of the entire high-voltage network, according to the company's spokesperson. In a report on the consequences of the winter of 2022-2023 published in April, the United Nations Development Programme estimated the cost of the destruction at "10 billion dollars" (9.3 billion euros), leaving "12 million people" without access to energy, or with limited access. These massive strikes traumatized the population, forced to adapt overnight to blackouts and power cuts lasting several hours organized by the authorities to relieve pressure on the grid. "Last year was very tough for us because everything happened suddenly, we were not prepared," recalls Nina Antochtchenko, co-manager of an Indian restaurant in downtown Kiev. Living with four hours of power a day, Anna Lebedeva, a mother of two living in a neighborhood on the left bank of the Ukrainian capital, particularly affected, remembers how she and her husband had to organize their lives with four hours of power a day, during which they had to cook, do laundry, charge their phones, endure the cold the rest of the time, and use candles. The family got through the winter with the help of a couple of friends: "When they had electricity, we went to their place, and vice versa." At the generator section in the Epicenter supermarket in Kiev on October 12, 2023. GUILLAUME HERBAUT FOR "LE MONDE" This year, Ukrainian authorities started preparing the population for the possibility of another winter under Russian strikes as early as the end of summer. Energy operators have been working on damaged infrastructure to repair 90% of the network, according to Ms. Tsaturian. However, this does not mean that the installations have been restored to the level they were before the massive bombing campaign. "This network," the official continues, "was built over decades, and it is impossible to completely restore it in eight months." In order to better protect its strategic infrastructure, Kiev has installed concrete walls around them. Compared to the first winter of the war, Ukraine now has more anti-aircraft defense systems. President Zelensky and members of his government have been requesting such systems from allies for months to protect the cities. While the equipment is now more numerous, it does not cover the entire territory larger than France. Indeed, while residents of Kiev remain relatively confident after witnessing the effectiveness of the Patriot surface-to-air missile batteries provided by the West, other cities have proven to be much more vulnerable. Beyond Ukrainian energy operators, the entire population is mobilizing to save its economy and prepare for the cold. Department stores in the outskirts of Kiev have stocked up on various types of generators. Informed by experience, residents have identified shopping malls and places where they can go to access electricity and internet in case of power outages. "Encouraging people to revolt" Ms. Lebedeva explains that she has purchased gas canisters for cooking and fluorescent stickers that she and her children will wear at night to be visible to drivers if the street lights go out. "We have warm clothes, strong spirits, we are ready," she asserts with a triumphant laugh. "I spoke with the girls at the bar, and asked them how they were preparing for winter," recounts Nina Antochtchenko. "We agreed that the first thing to do is to pray for our anti-aircraft defense..." A generator in downtown Kiev, November 5, 2023. ADRIEN VAUTIER / LE PICTORIUM FOR "LE MONDE" According to Ms. Tsaturian, the objective of the Russian strike campaigns, in addition to "paralyzing the economy," is also to "create discontent and push people to revolt against the authorities." However, while the country's unity held during the last winter, the situation in which Ukraine finds itself today is very different. The Ukrainians, who were then celebrating the military successes achieved by their army, with the reconquest of the Kharkiv region and part of the Kherson region in the winter of 2022-2023, have little reason to rejoice now. The front has hardly moved since then, and Ukrainian losses have accumulated. In May, Russian forces seized what remains of the city of Bakhmut after months of intense fighting. In June, the Ukrainian counteroffensive launched in the south and east of the country, followed optimistically on Ukrainian television for months, gave way to a sense of powerlessness. The commander-in-chief of the armed forces, Valeri Zaluzhny, recently admitted in The Economist that it probably would not lead to a "deep and magnificent breakthrough." "Avoid getting caught up in quarrels" The impact of such a statement, which President Zelensky tried to soften during an interview with the American channel CBS News, is difficult to measure. But the atmosphere in recent months, between news from the front, doubts about continued support from the American ally in front of a divided Congress, and the conflict between Israel and Hamas that diverted international attention, is dampening the morale of the population. A survey conducted from September 30 to October 13 by the International Institute of Sociology in Kyiv reveals that while "the majority of Ukrainians are optimistic about the country's future," the number of those who believe that "things are moving in the right direction" has decreased from 68% to 60% compared to the same study conducted in May. The proportion of those who believe that things are "definitely going in the right direction" has dropped from 32% to 17%, and the percentage of those who think the situation is "moving in the wrong direction" has increased from 16% to 29%. On the evening of Monday, November 6, in his daily address to the nation, the president announced that there will be no presidential election in March 2024 due to the mobilization of thousands of soldiers on the front and the situation of millions of displaced Ukrainians within the country or beyond its borders. Volodymyr Zelensky mainly called for unity: "Now, everyone must think about defending our country. We must pull ourselves together, avoid relaxing and getting caught up in quarrels or other priorities. The situation is the same today as it was before: without victory, there will be no country."

title: Bombardments in Gaza, storms Ciaran and Domingos, New York marathon... The five key pieces of news from the weekend
lead: If you haven't been following the news, here's what you need to know from the last forty-eight hours.
text_eng: Have you fallen a bit behind on the news these past two days? We summarize the main information from Saturday, November 4, and Sunday, November 5. • Israel-Hamas: refugee camp bombed, calls for a "humanitarian truce," and demonstrations Forty-five people, mostly children and women, were killed and around a hundred others were injured in an Israeli bombing on Saturday evening against the Al-Maghazi refugee camp in the center of the Gaza Strip, according to the Ministry of Health of the Gaza Strip, administered by Hamas. Earlier on Saturday, a bombing hit a UN school where displaced people are taking shelter in a refugee camp in the northern Gaza Strip, causing several casualties, according to the UN agency for Palestinian refugees, UNRWA. The Ministry of Health of the Gaza Strip stated that fifteen people were killed in this bombing, which it also attributes to the Israeli Defense Forces (IDF). The French Minister of Foreign Affairs, Catherine Colonna, called on Sunday for an "immediate, sustainable, and supported humanitarian truce[, which] is absolutely necessary and [which] must lead to a ceasefire," following a meeting in Doha with her Qatari counterpart, Mohammed Ben Abderrahmane Al Thani. She emphasized that France is working to have a resolution to this effect adopted by the UN Security Council, which has been deeply divided on the issue since the start of the conflict. US President Joe Biden reported progress on Saturday in obtaining a humanitarian pause, an option currently rejected by Israel, which dismisses this possibility without the release of hostages held by Hamas. Palestinian President Mahmoud Abbas linked the return of the Palestinian Authority to Gaza after the war between Israel and Hamas, as discussed by Washington, to a "political settlement" that also includes the occupied West Bank and East Jerusalem. Several peace demonstrations took place in France and around the world over the weekend. On Sunday in Marseille, 2,700 people marched in support of the Palestinian people, calling for a ceasefire in Gaza, according to the police prefecture. On Saturday in Paris, a procession of 19,000 people, according to the police, marched to demand an end to the bombings in Gaza. About forty other gatherings were announced throughout France, with 5,000 people in Lyon and 1,500 in Strasbourg. Demonstrations also took place in Washington, London, Berlin, and Tehran. • Jews in Europe "are living in fear again," warns the European Commission. Stars of David were spray-painted on buildings in several districts of Paris. MICHEL EULER/AP Tags, assaults, protests, desecrations... The European Commission condemned on Sunday the "resurgence" of anti-Semitic acts since the outbreak of the conflict between Hamas and Israel, and expressed concern that "Jews in Europe are once again living in fear." "The increase in anti-Semitic incidents across Europe in recent days has reached exceptionally high levels, reminiscent of some of the darkest periods in history," denounced the European executive. "In these difficult times, the EU stands by its Jewish communities. We condemn these despicable acts in the strongest terms. They go against everything that Europe represents, our fundamental values, our way of life." These acts are contrary to the "European model of society based on equality, inclusion, and full respect for human rights," emphasized the European executive. The prefect of police in Paris, Laurent Nuñez, announced that there had been 257 anti-Semitic acts in the Paris metropolitan area and 90 arrests, out of the 857 recorded nationwide, since October 7, the start of the conflict between Hamas and Israel, which is "more than in a whole year." • After storm Ciaran, depression Domingos A Gujan-Mestras, in Gironde, Sunday, November 5, 2023, after the passage of depression Domingos. THIBAUD MORITZ / AFP Succession of storms in France, with a growing toll: an Enedis employee mobilized after storm Ciaran was electrocuted and died on Saturday evening in Brittany, while another depression, named Domingos, caused new damage on the Atlantic coast. This storm caused significant damage to the railway network in Nouvelle-Aquitaine and led to delays for two trains on the Paris-Orléans-Limoges-Toulouse (POLT) line, whose passengers had to spend the night at Brive station. "The signaling installations between Bordeaux and Hendaye were also severely damaged," according to SNCF. The electrocution death of a 46-year-old agent brings the death toll to at least three victims in France after the passage of Ciaran between Wednesday and Thursday, which also left forty-seven injured. Domingos, described as "less severe" but accompanied by very strong winds reaching up to 152 km/h in Gironde or 144 km/h in Charente, caused five injuries. • For Zelensky, the war with Russia is not at a "standstill" Volodymyr Zelensky, in Kiev, Ukraine, on November 4, 2023. ANATOLII STEPANOV / AFP Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky challenged on Saturday that the war between his country and Russia is at a "standstill," also denying any pressure from Western countries to start negotiations with Russia. His comments come after the Chief of Staff of the Ukrainian army, Valeri Zaloujny, stated this week that the Russian and Ukrainian armies were trapped in a war of attrition and positions. "Just like in the First World War, we have reached a technological level where we are at an impasse," he told the British weekly. "There probably won't be a magnificent and deep breakthrough," he added, dampening hopes raised by the Ukrainian counteroffensive launched in early June. "No, the conflict is not at an impasse," Kremlin spokesperson Dmitry Peskov had already responded to journalists, stating that "Russia [was] relentlessly pursuing its special military operation" and that "all set objectives [had] to be achieved." • Ethiopian Tamirat Tola wins the New York Marathon in less than 2 hours and 5 minutes; Kenyan Hellen Obiri in 2 hours and 27 minutes From left to right, Kenyan Albert Korir, second place, winner, Ethiopian Tamirat Tola, and third place, his compatriot Shura Kitata. CRAIG RUTTLE / AP Ethiopian Tamirat Tola won the New York Marathon on Sunday in 2 hours, 4 minutes, and 58 seconds, setting a new record for the American race. Starting with the best time among the participants after the withdrawals of Kenyan favorites Evans Chebet and Geoffrey Kamworor, he took the lead to run the second half of the race alone at the front. He finished ahead of Kenyan Albert Korir (by 1 minute and 59 seconds), the winner in New York in 2021, and Ethiopian Shura Kitata (by 2 minutes and 13 seconds). Tola also breaks the record of the most famous marathon in the world, which had stood since 2011. On the women's side, Kenyan Hellen Obiri won the last major marathon of the year in 2 hours, 27 minutes, and 23 seconds. The women started at a very slow pace before accelerating abruptly at the 35th kilometer, and it was Obiri who sprinted to victory ahead of Ethiopian Letesenbet Gidey (by 6 seconds) and Kenyan Sharon Lokedi (by 10 seconds), who had won in New York last year. At 33 years old, Obiri secures her second marathon victory this year, one year after her debut at the distance and seven months after her win at the Boston Marathon. Also: New earthquake in Nepal Novak Djokovic crowned for the seventh time in Paris, the fortieth time in Masters 1000, by defeating Grigor Dimitrov Russia claims to have successfully tested an intercontinental ballistic missile Police thwart the abduction of a girl by her father who had entered the tarmac of Hamburg airport Socialist Party leader Olivier Faure calls on "all political forces" to rally against anti-Semitism Death of Charles Piaget, emblematic figure of Lip Budget of Social Security: rejection of the no-confidence motion by LFI, the text adopted in the first reading

title: Ukraine's charm offensive towards Africa goes through Pretoria.
lead: Visiting South Africa for the first time, Dmytro Kuleba, the Ukrainian Minister of Foreign Affairs, aims to open a new chapter in Kiev's relations with Pretoria and the rest of the continent.
text_eng: The last time a Ukrainian Minister of Foreign Affairs visited Pretoria, Nelson Mandela was still president. It was in 1998, which feels like an eternity ago. "We should commit to never waiting so long again," wished Dmytro Kuleba, the Ukrainian Minister of Foreign Affairs, during a joint press conference with Naledi Pandor, his South African counterpart, on Monday, November 6. For comparison, Sergei Lavrov, the Russian Minister of Foreign Affairs, has already traveled to South Africa three times since the beginning of the conflict in Ukraine in February 2022. Mr. Kuleba's visit once again highlighted the gap between Kiev and Pretoria, as South Africa has never condemned the Russian invasion of Ukraine. Its government has even been suspected of supplying weapons to Russia. The closeness between Moscow and Pretoria stems from a common history of fighting against the apartheid regime. However, history sometimes forgets that it was the Soviet Union as a whole, including Ukraine, that helped the African National Congress (ANC), the ruling party, in its struggle against the racist white minority government. Harmonizing their views With his visit, Dmytro Kuleba aims to adjust the narrative. He reminded that the Soviet Republic of Ukraine chaired the UN subcommittee against apartheid. Naledi Pandor acknowledged "the support that Ukraine, as a member of the Soviet Union, provided to the fight for freedom." After harmonizing their views, South Africa and Ukraine could, if Mr. Kuleba is heard, open "a new chapter," initiated by the meeting between Presidents Cyril Ramaphosa and Volodymyr Zelensky in Kiev in June. Mr. Ramaphosa then led a delegation of African leaders, supported by seven African countries and composed of the presidents of Senegal, Comoros, Zambia, and South Africa. They visited Kiev before meeting Vladimir Putin in Saint Petersburg. The Africans then proposed a ten-point plan that was only marginally considered. "We never considered the African peace plan as a plan," corrected Dmytro Kuleba, while considering that this visit by African heads of state was "crucial," as it allowed South Africa to join the think tank on the Ukrainian peace plan and send a message "to others." The others are mainly the African countries with which Kiev is trying to reconnect. "Drowned in its internal problems," Ukraine forgot about this continent in the 1990s and 2000s. "We had to ensure the transition from the Soviet empire to democracy, shift from a state economy to a market economy, and reclaim our place in Europe." "I regret it, but unfortunately, during this period, we did not allocate enough resources to maintain our relationships with African countries," justified Mr. Kuleba in an effort to educate. A new network of embassies Ukraine, well aware of Russia's growing influence on the African continent, wants to offer a response based on development aid and become a welcoming land for African students. It also proposes to share its expertise on digitizing administration and to leverage its pharmaceutical industry to sell and manufacture drugs in Africa. Furthermore, it aims not only to export its cereals but also to develop better agricultural practices on the continent. "The more Africans know about Ukraine, the less they will turn to Russia," hopes Mr. Kuleba. This ambition is supported by a new network of embassies. At least five are in the works: in Côte d'Ivoire, Ghana, Rwanda, Botswana, and Mozambique. "It is a significant investment for us, as we are in cost-saving mode, but President Zelensky has made the political choice to allocate the necessary resources to strengthen our presence in Africa," emphasizes the Ukrainian foreign minister. The minister's numerous trips to the continent testify to this offensive. South Africa is the twelfth African country visited in a year. But this time, Pretoria was not just a stop on a tour; it was a separate trip, stresses Dmytro Kuleba. Is Ukraine seeking attention at a time when the world is focused on the war between Israel and Hamas? This visit "has nothing to do with what is making the headlines, it is a strategy, not related to a current need," defended the minister. The atmosphere remained cool in Pretoria. However, the conflict in the Middle East quickly entered the conversation. A staunch supporter of the Palestinian cause, South Africa, through its Minister of International Relations, pointed out that this visit took place in the context of a "serious escalation of the conflict between Israel and Palestine." While on October 27, Ukraine abstained from voting on a resolution calling for a humanitarian ceasefire and the protection of civilians, Naledi Pandor, whose diplomats were recalled from Israel for "consultations" on Monday, called for the unequivocal condemnation of the ongoing "carnage" and respect for UN resolutions. Prodded by a South African journalist about the reason for this behavior, the Ukrainian Minister of Foreign Affairs threw the ball back: "Abstaining is not the same as voting against a resolution. And South Africa knows this better than anyone because it has abstained from voting on each of the resolutions concerning Russia's massive invasion of Ukraine." "Despite efforts to improve relations, the atmosphere remained cool in Pretoria between the two parties. 'We must respect each other's positions while ensuring that no one will stab us in the back,' summarized Dmytro Kuleba, aware that South Africa's diplomatic weight gives it the status of a 'gateway to the continent.' Ukraine took a long time to cross this threshold, and its Minister of Foreign Affairs acknowledged the distance it still needs to cover before being received as a friend. Mr. Kuleba wanted to meet with President Cyril Ramaphosa. A meeting was scheduled, 'but I was told this morning that he had gone somewhere.' In January, during the visit of Sergey Lavrov, the South African head of state, on the other hand, was indeed present."

title: "Do the Russians want war?": an ideological gap separates the youth from the government
lead: Political scientist Vera Grantseva, who grew up under Vladimir Putin, questions the apparent denial of reality by the Russian people in the face of the atrocities committed in Ukraine and considers the prospect of a political awakening within the country.
text_eng: Book. In 1961, the idea came to the poet Yevgeny Yevtushenko (1932-2017) to write a song titled "Do the Russians Want War?" with pacifist undertones, intended to pay tribute to the meeting of American and Soviet forces on the banks of the Elbe at the end of the Second World War. Popularized by the Red Army Choir, it resurfaces today in the title of Vera Grantseva's essay (Le Cerf, 186 pages, 19 euros). Armed with this literary key, the author, a former associate professor at the Higher School of Economics in Russia who emigrated to France a few years before the invasion of Ukraine, attempts to answer this other, more pressing question in the West: what do the Russians want? No, she immediately addresses, the Russians are not more warmongers – men did not rush to enlist, on the contrary, many fled – than "servile" beings. While the system, built by Vladimir Putin on corruption and loyalties inherited from the KGB, remains "neo-feudal," "the past century (...) taught them that radical political changes could only worsen their situation, however unbearable it may be. It is therefore not a supposed slave consciousness but rather the painful experience of history that dictates the current apathy towards recent events." Generational fracture "Putin's war against Ukraine, Vera Grantseva insists, is above all a confrontation between two opposing worldviews concerning the individual and their place in politics." And for the Kremlin leader, the Ukrainian model, drawn to Europe, capable of debating and taking to the streets to express discontent, "was a repellent." Then, starting from the annexation of Crimea in 2014, "Russian television was tasked with focusing on Ukraine, its domestic politics, and, in particular, the situation in the Donbass. In fact, since 2014, the government has systematically fueled Russians' hatred towards a neighboring country, preparing them for a future war." Despite this indoctrination, there have been signs of resistance, but they have been systematically crushed. Fear has permeated throughout society. And yet... The author highlights a fundamental element: a generational fracture has occurred between the youth and their elders. The ideological gap of the former with the power, she writes, has continued to widen: "many have felt that Putin has buried their future" and, "often, they do not want to bear the moral responsibility for the crimes committed in Ukraine." Their mobilization, mostly underground, demonstrates a "horizontal solidarity," difficult to grasp but very real, within a society that self-organizes away from parties and the political scene. The heavy condemnations targeting 25-35 year olds, whether for a post on a social network, a petition, a clandestine action, or an informal support network, bear witness to the fact that "resistance has a young face." Now, Putin is afraid of the youth in his country.

title: A German journalist paid under the table by a Russian oligarch for his pro-Putin books.
lead: Confidential documents from the "Cyprus Confidential" investigation reveal that Hubert Seipel, known for his pro-Kremlin stance, received 600,000 euros from an offshore company linked to Russian billionaire Alexei Mordachov.
text_eng: In June 2021, German journalist Hubert Seipel was invited to a radio show to present his latest book, "Putins Macht. Warum Europa Russland braucht" ("Putin's Power. Why Europe Needs Russia," Hoffmann und Campe, untranslated). The man was friendly, with a courteous tone. However, after about fifteen minutes, he suddenly became tense. Out of the blue, the host asked him if he "receives fees from Russia." "How dare you?" exclaimed the essayist, taken aback. The question was not unreasonable. Hubert Seipel, known for being a specialist in Russian geopolitics, boasts of being "the only Western journalist in direct contact with Putin," whom he met in 2010 to prepare his documentary "Me, Putin. A Portrait." This closeness has led him to be frequently criticized for his pro-Kremlin stances. His previous book, published in 2015, had already been widely criticized in the German press for its "lack of the most basic journalistic standards." The "Cyprus Confidential" investigation raises the possibility that the Kremlin may have secretly tried to influence public debate in Germany. It also sheds new light on the journalist's stances: confidential financial data from the Cypriot firm Cypcodirect Corporate Services reveal that his two books were partly funded by offshore companies linked to a Kremlin-connected oligarch, in contradiction with the most fundamental rules of journalistic ethics. An oligarch behind the frontman A "sponsorship contract" was signed on March 16, 2018, between Mr. Seipel and a company from the British Virgin Islands, De Vere Worldwide Corporation, for "writing a book on the political environment in the Russian Federation." This confidential contract specifies that "the sponsor wishes to support the development" of the book by paying the author 600,000 euros and providing "adequate logistical and organizational support" - while stating that "the author has no obligations regarding the content or composition of the book." According to leaked documents, this sum was paid to the journalist in two installments from an account held by the Russian bank Sovcombank, in 2018 and 2019. Another internal document, initialed by the Cypriot branch of the audit firm PwC, also reveals that a "similar contract" was signed in 2013 for "Putin's biography" - the subject of Hubert Seipel's first book, published in 2015. The advantage of using opaque offshore companies for this type of contract lies in the fact that the owner, as well as the money movements, often remains unknown. However, the documents from "Cyprus Confidential" reveal many clues about the true client's identity, suggesting that it is Alexeï Mordachov, the main shareholder of Russia's largest steel company, Severstal. It was this Russian group that registered De Vere Worldwide in 2006, before handing it over to Igor Voskresensky, a discreet but central figure in the oligarch's financial galaxy. Everything indicates that this Russian man in his fifties acts as a frontman: he is the official manager of dozens of companies linked to Alexeï Mordachov, both in Russia and in tax havens. In March 2022, when Western sanctions hit his boss after the invasion of Ukraine, he suddenly became the majority shareholder of the mining group Nordgold - previously owned 90% by the Mordachov family. All the articles in this investigation from 14/11/2023: In Cyprus, dirty money and Russian fortunes continue to defy Europe; A German journalist secretly paid by a Russian oligarch for his pro-Putin books; Secret transfers from Abramovich fuel suspicion of a slush fund at Chelsea; Cyprus, a high-tech hub for the porn industry; In Cyprus, the life of the "start-up island" disrupted by the war in Ukraine; In Cyprus, a conglomerate of cyber surveillance between legal opacity and tax optimization. "You sympathize with your hero." Contacted by our German colleagues from Paper Trail Media, Mr. Voskresensky stated that he had never entered into a "contract with a German journalist," and even explained that he "did not know of any company called [De Vere Worldwide]" or "have been the director of such a company," before abruptly hanging up. However, shared addresses, bridge loans from companies consolidated in the Mordachov group to pay Hubert Seipel, or signatories who work for Severstal, are all clues suggesting that Alexeï Mordachov is at the heart of this sponsorship contract. Contacted, Hubert Seipel feels accused of "guilt by association" without denying his relationship with Alexeï Mordachov. He also states that the "support [from Mordachov] concerns exclusively book projects," explaining that the Russian payment corresponds to work "on the books for over eight years, a period that is not always calculated by publishers." The day before the presentation of his first book in Moscow in 2015, in the presence of Vladimir Putin, the Kremlin news agency, RIA Novosti, asked him the following question: "Your documentary (...) gives a very clear impression that you sympathize with your hero. Is that true?" The journalist had answered in the affirmative. It is now known that the entourage of the Russian president did indeed reciprocate. Do you have information to share with us? Do not hesitate to contact our investigations team securely, who are at your disposal to exchange with you if you have information or documents of public interest. Cyprus Confidential Tax advantages, judicial tolerance, golden visas: the leak of 3.6 million confidential documents from six Cypriot financial services firms shows how the island remains an opaque financial center, a haven for Russian money, and a gray area of the cyber economy. To be published on 11/14 In Cyprus, dirty money and Russian fortunes continue to defy Europe To be published on 11/14 A German journalist paid under the table by a Russian oligarch for his pro-Putin books To be published on 11/15 Secret transfers from Abramovich fuel suspicion of a black fund at Chelsea To be published on 11/15 Cyprus, a high-tech hub of the porn industry To be published on 11/16 In Cyprus, the life of the "start-up island" disrupted by the war in Ukraine To be published on 11/16 In Cyprus, a conglomerate of cyber surveillance between legal opacity and tax optimization

title: Ukraine fears being forgotten amid the conflict in the Middle East.
lead: Kiev is concerned about the future of support from the United States and its Western allies, which may be shifting towards Israel. In an interview with the British weekly "The Economist," the commander-in-chief of the Ukrainian forces, Valeri Zaloujny, paints a worrying picture of the situation.
text_eng: Winter in Ukraine is shaping up to be dark and cruel. Just under a month after the start of the war between Israel and Hamas, Ukrainians fear that their war may be forgotten. The authorities in Kiev are also concerned about the future of U.S. support, as their American ally struggles to secure international aid for Ukraine amidst a divided American Congress. On Thursday, November 2, the House of Representatives approved a military aid package of 14.3 billion dollars (13.4 billion euros) for Israel, while President Joe Biden is calling for a combined package of 105 billion dollars for Israel, Ukraine, and partners in Asia to counter Republican lawmakers reluctant to maintain American support for Kiev. Beyond the supposed "fatigue" of Western countries, twenty months after the start of the Russian invasion, the situation around and in the Gaza Strip seems to have momentarily overshadowed Ukraine in Western priorities. "This is not an online game." "The attention of the whole world has turned to what is happening in the Middle East," laments Olexiy Haran, a professor of political science at the prestigious Mohyla University in Kiev. "We must act pragmatically. Because with this new conflict, and all its consequences, we are facing many new challenges," alarms Solomiia Bobrovska, a member of the Verkhovna Rada, the Ukrainian Parliament, and the commission for national security and defense. "I understand that it is difficult to continue to follow, that people are getting tired," she continues. "But this is not an online game. It is about the world in which people, families live... What we are experiencing is not normal, it is abnormal." On Saturday, November 4, aware of Kiev's doubts, the President of the European Commission, Ursula von der Leyen, was to visit the Ukrainian capital to reassure authorities about European financial support and the accession process to the European community. In recent days, as media coverage of Ukraine has shrunk worldwide to focus on the Middle East, two articles published by the international press have had a strong impact in the country, serving as an additional warning signal. The first, published by the American magazine Time on October 30, describes a terribly defeatist atmosphere within the Ukrainian presidency, between anger and disillusionment at the declining support of allied countries. "The scariest thing is that part of the world has gotten used to the war in Ukraine," says Volodymyr Zelensky, quoted in the article. "War-induced exhaustion is spreading like a wave. We see it in the United States, in Europe," continues the Ukrainian leader. The article, written by a journalist who had raised Mr. Zelensky to power, underscores the need for continued international attention and support for Ukraine. Zelensky on the cover of Time a little less than a year earlier as "person of the year 2022," focuses this time on the obstacles facing the Ukrainian presidency: a stalemate on the front, difficulties in mobilizing the army, deep-seated corruption... The text, widely circulated and translated in Ukrainian media, was praised for its clarity by some of the country's opinion, and criticized by others. Some reproach the journalist for using quotes from anonymous advisers, like Oleksi Danilov, secretary of the national security and defense council, who denounced an article as "completely false," biased by ill-intentioned individuals. A "deadlock" Two days later, the British weekly The Economist, also picked up by the country's media, also made a sensation without, however, being criticized, as the person speaking in it is unanimously supported by the population. Yet, the assessment that the commander-in-chief of the armed forces, Valeri Zaloujny, gives of the situation on the front, in this opinion piece published on November 1, is particularly alarming. Regarding the Ukrainian counter-offensive launched at the beginning of June to reclaim territories occupied by Russia, the general acknowledges a "failure" and depicts it as blocked, in a "deadlock." The senior officer, driven, no doubt, by the desire to draw international attention to the war that continues to ravage his country, does not mince words. He mentions the transformation of the front into a war of positions, highly unfavorable to Ukraine, and which, in his view, will not be able to sustain in terms of human resources against Russia. If Ukraine does not find the technological means to achieve a "quick victory," then "sooner or later, we will realize that we simply do not have enough people to fight," he asserts. The general even goes as far as to mention a "defeat" in case of delays in the delivery of arms, outdated methods, and equipment. "I think this underscores how important it is that we continue to support Ukraine," said John Kirby, spokesperson for the U.S. National Security Council, referring to Valeri Zaloujny's remarks at a press conference on Thursday. The White House and Congress have until November 17 to agree on a budget to maintain funding for regular government operations. On September 30, Congress narrowly avoided a shutdown, skipping aid to Ukraine and triggering the removal of the then House Speaker, Kevin McCarthy. "Defend Ukraine or defeat Russia?" For Solomiia Bobrovska, the consequences of a separate allocation between aid to Israel and Ukraine could be catastrophic. "If this happens, I think the main focus will definitely be on Israel, and that means that no one will pay attention to what Russia is doing and all its violations and terrorist attacks against Ukraine," she emphasizes. The deputy from the opposition party Golos ("Voice") also believes that a U.S. retreat on this issue could have consequences for European support. "America and Europe seem undecided about the ultimate political goal of the war. Is it about defending Ukraine or defeating Russia?" bitterly questions Mick Ryan, a retired major general of the Australian army and military strategist. It is also in light of this dependence on the American ally that Ukraine's position on Israel is played out, aligned with the United States at the risk of making enemies among the so-called "Global South" countries. "At present, Ukraine is largely dependent on Western support, primarily American support," acknowledges Ihor Semyvolos, director of the Center for Middle East Studies based in Kiev. According to the researcher, the position of the Ukrainian authorities explains why Kiev seeks to establish good relations with Israel, even though Israel has always refused to provide military aid due, in part, to its relations with Russia. Shortly after the Hamas attack in Israel, Volodymyr Zelensky was one of the first leaders to offer to show his solidarity with the State of Israel on the ground, in vain, while the Israeli Prime Minister, unlike Western leaders, has never visited Kiev since the Russian invasion in February 2022. Participating via videoconference in the NATO Parliamentary Assembly, held in early October, the Ukrainian official also drew a parallel between the Russian aggression and that of Hamas. "The only difference," he declared, "is that a terrorist organization attacked Israel and here, it is a terrorist state [Russia] that attacked Ukraine." However, this positioning could worsen Kiev's already complicated relations with the Global South, which Ukraine and its Western allies have been trying to persuade since the beginning of the Russian invasion to stand with them against Moscow. In recent weeks, countries like Turkey, Saudi Arabia, and Qatar, supporters of Ukraine to varying degrees, have accused the West of practicing "double standards" in Gaza, comparing the unanimous condemnation of Russia's war crimes with the silence surrounding Israeli bombings in the Palestinian enclave. According to Iliya Kusa, an international relations analyst at the Institute for the Future based in Kiev, "it is inevitable that the more Ukraine aligns with the West, which is the only strategy at the moment, the more it risks suffering from the negative impact of certain things, such as bombings in Gaza or any other incidents occurring in other regions."

title: What is the Siberian Battalion, the Russian unit integrated into the Ukrainian army?
lead: Around fifty Russians, opposed to Vladimir Putin, have decided to take up arms against their country, in the ranks of the Ukrainian army.
text_eng: In the suburbs of Kiev, Russian soldiers are training for combat. Soon, they will be on the front lines alongside the Ukrainian army. These men belong to a new unit, made up of about fifty Russians and called the Siberian Battalion, integrated within the Ukrainian army. "I believe it is necessary to eliminate Putin's dictatorship. And for that, we need a victory for Ukraine," explains a volunteer.

title: Raw materials: "Palladium has left the podium"
lead: The price of metal has fallen by nearly 40% since January 1st. The slowdown of the Chinese economy is weighing heavily, notes Laurence Girard, an economic journalist at "Le Monde".
text_eng: Inflated with helium, palladium has long soared from one record to another, regularly clinching the title of the commodity with the highest price jump over a year. In March 2022, the peak of its stratospheric rise, the metal shattered its record, reaching $3,442 (€3,233) per ounce. Palladium at its peak. Since then, the tide has turned, and the bubble has begun to inexorably deflate. By the end of 2022, the price dropped below $2,000 per ounce. Today, it is approaching $1,130. This represents a decline of nearly 40% since January 1st. Palladium has stepped down from the podium. It had snatched the title of the most precious metal from gold during its irresistible ascent, but the yellow metal has regained its crown. It reclaimed it in early January, during the Epiphany celebrations. Since then, it has not let go, and it seems firmly in place for now. The price of gold even flirted with the $2,000 per ounce mark at the end of October. Tensions in the Middle East have heated up the demand for gold. In March 2022, it was also geopolitics that propelled palladium to its historic high. The invasion of Ukraine by Russian troops led by Vladimir Putin had ignited the markets. Russia is, in fact, the largest supplier, accounting for nearly 40% of global volumes of this metal that has made Vladimir Potanin, the head of the world's largest palladium producer MMC Norilsk Nickel PJSC, a fortune. The fear of supply disruptions due to sanctions had fueled speculation. Ultimately, the fever subsided, commercial flows were not disrupted, and the metal continued to circulate. "The markets have changed their perspective" Today, it is the state of the global economy that dictates the ups and downs. "The markets have changed their perspective by focusing on the macroeconomic situation, and palladium shares a common story with industrial metals. It is highly dependent on corporate demand," says Yves Jégourel, co-director of the CyclOpe report and a specialist in raw materials. The slowdown of the Chinese economy weighs heavily on prices. Palladium is not immune to this slowdown. Palladium also faces competition from its counterpart, platinum. Both vie for the favor of the automotive industry. A pinch of these materials is used in catalytic converters to filter out pollutants from gasoline engines. Although the tightening of environmental regulations has made these components strategic, manufacturers balked when the bill soared. Platinum, being less expensive, competed with palladium.
