Another question raised by analysts: will sales of gasoline or hybrid cars slow down in the face of the rapid spread of electric vehicles? The future of gasoline engines is under pressure. This casts a shadow over the bright future of the gray metal. Palladium has faded...

title: Johann Soufi, a voice for Gaza
lead: The lawyer, who worked for the United Nations agency responsible for Palestinian refugees in the Middle East, is concerned about the fate of the people in Gaza and the thousands of displaced individuals in the enclave.
text_eng: Suddenly, tears welled up in his eyes. "A child dies every fifteen minutes in Gaza. We have been talking for two hours: eight children have already died." Since the tragic day of October 7, with over 1,400 people killed by Hamas in Israel, and the outbreak of war, Johann Soufi, 41, has become a voice, that of Gaza. A specialist in international law, he emphasizes, "I do not want to become a media figure, the embodiment of the defender of the people of Gaza. But I want to be able to give them a voice." On September 21, he wrote in an opinion piece published in Le Monde, "In the absence of any political solution, only the International Criminal Court (ICC) is now capable of bringing hope of justice to Palestinian victims." Now, as the war rages on, he explains, "Justice knows how to be patient. The urgency is the humanitarian disaster in Gaza, the protection of civilians, and the survival conditions of the thousands of displaced people towards the southern part of the territory." From September 2020 to March 2023, he led the legal activities of the UNRWA office, the United Nations agency for Palestinian refugees in the Middle East. Over 13,000 employees, 1.6 million beneficiaries at the time. "UNRWA manages schools, hospitals, social services, food distribution, labor law, international law...," explains the man who was not destined for such a mission. Son of a Breton mother and a Kabyle father (he holds French and Algerian nationalities), both lawyers, Johann Soufi grew up and studied in Cergy, in the Val-d'Oise region. He joined a criminal law firm in Pontoise, but did not thrive there. "I wanted to confront more important issues. I experience justice on a larger scale." East Timor, Ivory Coast, Mali... At 25, he wrote to the approximately 300 lawyers who collaborate with the ICC. Only one, Arthur Vercken, gave him a chance. He joined his team to participate in the defense of Callixte Kalimanzira, a former senior official of the Rwandan Ministry of the Interior tried for his involvement in massacres of Tutsis. He flew to Kigali, then to Tanzania, where the International Criminal Tribunal for Rwanda is located. He stayed there for four years. "It was wonderful. That's where I became addicted to international justice and told myself I would do this until I die!" Followed by missions in East Timor, Ivory Coast, Mali, and the Central African Republic. In 2012, he was recruited by the Special Court for Sierra Leone, which tried Charles Taylor, the former president of Liberia. He then moved on to the court for Lebanon during the trial of the suspected assassins of former Prime Minister Rafic Hariri. Since March, he has been providing legal and strategic advice to the Attorney General of Ukraine and working on building cases against the main perpetrators of crimes committed since the outbreak of the war. Canadian Marie-Pier Barbeau, a jurist at the ICC, says of him: "Johann has the ability to quickly see things in their global context, and this holistic approach allows him to resolve complicated situations effectively and pragmatically. When faced with an issue, he can quickly identify the central questions that need to be addressed." "A crime against humanity" He arrived in Gaza in the midst of the Covid-19 pandemic in September 2020. Three years that would leave a lasting mark on him. "When I heard people in France complaining about lockdowns... In Gaza, ever since the blockade imposed by Israel, it has been going on for so long! Being born in Gaza is a curse. There is no hope, not even the possibility of escaping and attempting exile, because if you take to the sea, the Israeli navy will intercept you. I have rarely encountered such humanity, such generosity among the inhabitants. But I also understood the pain of living there. You cannot grasp it if you have never been there. This feeling of claustrophobia, confinement, abandonment by the rest of the world... And living under the drones that you hear, monitoring you and capable of killing you at any moment." On social media, Johann Soufi, who officially left the UN in March, is regularly insulted. He says he doesn't care, continuing to denounce the double standards of international justice between Ukraine and Gaza, also not hesitating to describe Israel's policy towards the tiny Palestinian territory as "hostage-taking." At the same time, while he emphasizes that terrorism is not universally defined in international law, he claims not to be interested in the political debate on semantics that particularly concerns La France insoumise: "I clearly state that Hamas committed not only a war crime on October 7, 2023, but also a crime against humanity." How does he see the future? He refuses to comment. "I am a jurist, it is not my role to engage in political analysis or predict the future." But he will fight for international criminal justice to judge the atrocities committed on both sides. An utopia? "I sat a few meters away from a man as powerful as Charles Taylor, in a court that sentenced him to fifty years in prison. I was in Kiev when Vladimir Putin was issued an arrest warrant by the ICC. I believe that nothing is unrealistic in matters of law and justice." As for his future, it may involve joining Human Rights Watch, Amnesty International, the ICC, or returning to the United Nations. Even though he believes that this perspective is blocked for the coming years by his positions, Marie-Pier Barbeau sees it as an opportunity: "I think that this freedom of speech is one of Johann's great strengths."

title: Myroslav Marynovytch, a Ukrainian figure of Soviet dissidence, said: "I expect organized resistance from the Russians against the war in Ukraine."
lead: In ten or twenty years, cultural relations could resume with Russia, but not before, asserts the intellectual who was sentenced to seven years in a gulag in 1977, in an interview with "Le Monde". According to him, the primary duty of Russian opponents to the war is to act to change the situation in their country.
text_eng: Rector of the Ukrainian Catholic University in Lviv, Myroslav Marynovytch, 74 years old, is one of the last living Ukrainian Soviet dissidents. His commitment to defending human rights led to a seven-year sentence in the gulag in 1977. He served this sentence in the infamous Perm-36 camp, 1,500 kilometers from Moscow in the Ural Mountains, followed by five years of exile in Kazakhstan. Today, he is one of the most respected intellectual figures in Ukraine. Like any country facing an existential threat, Ukraine, since the Russian invasion, needs unity. However, this war is prolonged. Do you think it is desirable and possible to criticize the political power in such a situation? I am a democrat, and therefore, I believe that citizens of any country should have the right to criticize. But, at the same time, while observing what has happened in Ukraine, I can only be pleased that the intensity of political controversies is decreasing. This is not due to political persecution or censorship. Our nation has shown a reflex of unity, which reduces political debate. Nevertheless, freedom of speech remains, as does press freedom. There are television channels in the hands of the opposition where relatively critical views of our president [Volodymyr Zelensky] can be heard. I am not worried about freedom of expression in Ukraine. Do you think it is necessary to hold the presidential election as planned in 2024? This topic surprises me a lot. How can one consider an election in times of war? When so many of our fellow citizens are abroad and so many others are under Russian occupation! An American friend argued that it is crucial for Westerners to hold such an election. But I want a fair and honest election. I do not want my country to appear as an autocracy. Is there a public debate on this issue in Ukraine today? Absolutely, the debate exists, on social media as well as in the media. The Ukrainian Parliament is currently considering a law to ban the Ukrainian Orthodox clergy affiliated with Moscow. Is this a wise decision? Are all priests of this Orthodox Church agents of enemy influence? Undoubtedly, some of the clergy are sincere in their faith. There was a statement from the clergy of the Ukrainian Orthodox Church of the Moscow Patriarchate [UOC-MP] at the end of 2022 to address the issue of their independence. This includes the right not to refer to the Patriarch of Russia, Kirill, during prayers. This move did not sit well with the higher clergy, who began to dismiss the one hundred and fifty signatories of the statement. At the same time, Ukrainian society itself has repeatedly demanded an end to this affiliation. The Church should have cleaned up its act by excluding collaborators, stopping the flow of parishioners' money to separatists, ceasing to hide weapons in crypts, and refraining from passing information about our army to the enemy. The reasons why Ukraine is holding the UOC-MP accountable are numerous and relevant in the context of the Russian invasion. National security imperatives take precedence above all else. The UOC-MP enjoyed complete freedom for thirty years after Ukraine's independence. It abused this freedom following the Russian invasion. The Ukrainian political system has long been under Russian influence. How do you explain that Viktor Medvedchuk, a close associate of Russian President Vladimir Putin who participated in the repression of dissidents during the Soviet era, was able to become a billionaire and hold official positions until 2020? This is a question that continues to puzzle me. Over the past thirty years, Ukraine has embraced freedom imperfectly, failing to complete its transition from a state of national subjugation [to Russia] to a democracy. At the time of the declaration of independence in 1991, there was an interesting configuration because the idea was supported by democrats, the ruling elite, and miners from the Donbass region. The former Soviet elite, still in power, began to suffer from a sense of inferiority and thus took Moscow as a political model. Medvedchuk is, to me, the epitome of this elite corrupted by Russian money. He is among those who early on helped Moscow and promised that when the Russian army would occupy Ukrainian territory, they would hand over the country. This is one of the reasons why Putin was convinced he could subjugate Ukraine in less than eight days. If I were in Putin's shoes today, I would either condemn Medvedchuk to prison or confiscate his money. Ukraine's past continues to spark heated debates both inside and outside the country. Streets are being renamed in honor of controversial figures like the ultranationalist Stepan Bandera (1909-1959), accused of collaborating with the Nazis, or Simon Petliura (1879-1926), suspected of allowing anti-Jewish pogroms to take place when he was the leader of the Ukrainian People's Army from 1918 to 1920. Where do you stand in this debate? I hope that Ukrainian civil society will form its own opinion on these two figures, preferably in a peaceful time and with complete freedom. CAIMI & PICCINNI Which historical figures embody your ideals? My heroes are my fellow dissidents. Ievhen Sverstiouk [essayist, philosopher, and poet], Vassyl Stous [poet and journalist, who died in the Gulag under Gorbachev in 1985], Viatchelsav Tchornovil, and Mykhaïlo Horyn [human rights defenders]. I could mention many others. None of them ever claimed any glory. They died with the certainty of having done their duty. Are there common reference figures for the whole country today? The Ukrainian heroes of tomorrow are those who fall on the battlefield defending the homeland. They will be part of the Ukrainian pantheon. The efforts made by the authorities to remove symbols of Russian culture, such as renaming Alexander Pushkin streets, are not understood in the West... If you are referring to "cancel culture," it is a real phenomenon in the United States, but not in Ukraine. Those who attack George Washington because he owned a black slave do so in a free country, while we are at war. The difference is huge. The United States is not facing an existential threat. Ask an elderly French person how they felt hearing a Wagner opera during the German occupation. I remember my mother's stories of how she shuddered when she heard German being spoken. It's the same with "Pushkin Street." Personally, I understand why Ukrainians do not want to have Russian cultural names in front of them every day. At the end of the war, in ten or twenty years, cultural exchanges will naturally resume with Russia. It should also be noted that during the Soviet era, Ukraine was subjected to a policy of forced Russification. Many in Ukraine also reject any dialogue with the Russian opposition, which is actually against this war. Why? I continue to have remote conversations with a number of old Russian acquaintances. I know very well that not only are they opposed to this war, but they also recognize the imperialistic nature of Russia. But I must also say that in March 2022, I declined a friend's proposal to organize an evening dedicated to reading Vassyl Stous' poetry in Russian and Ukrainian. It is not the time, in the midst of war, to recite poetry together. Instead, I expect organized resistance and concrete actions against the war from my Russian contacts. During the Soviet era, there were very organized opposition movements in the socialist republics. They proved that a part of the population wanted the regime to fall. I am grateful to the Russian opposition for the sympathy they show towards Ukraine in these tragic times. But their first duty is to find a way to change the situation in Russia. You argue that the war will stop when there is a change within Russian society. What feeds this hope? My point of view is not widely shared in Ukraine. Many are inclined to think that there will never be a change in Russia. This reasoning is also based on a long history. The Russian elite considers it their duty to subdue Ukraine. Moscow believes itself to be the heir of Kievan Rus [or Kievan Russia, a political entity founded in 980 and encompassing territories across Belarus, Ukraine, and Russia]. Since the Christian baptism of Prince Vladimir of Kiev is part of Russian history, the Moscow elite believes that Kiev must, at all costs, be part of the Russian state. Russian society also thinks so. This war, today, can be a source of salvation for Russia, as the surrender at the end of the Second World War was for Germany in 1945. The defeat of Russia is important in a moral sense, as it will give the Russian people the opportunity to rethink their history.

title: "The frozen Russian assets should be used to rebuild Ukraine."
lead: Kiev wishes to recover around 300 billion euros of frozen Russian assets as part of the sanctions against Moscow following the start of the invasion of Ukraine as reparations, notes Julien Bouissou, a journalist at "Le Monde", in his column.
text_eng: The 2024 budget of the Ukrainian government, voted on in Parliament on Thursday, November 9, foresees a huge deficit of around 44 billion dollars (41.22 billion euros), mainly to finance the national war effort. The country relies on foreign aid, which has already reached 34 billion dollars since the beginning of the year, and 31 billion in 2022. While its needs for 2024 will be at least as significant, the support from its two main donors is showing signs of weakness. The promise by the European Union (EU) to provide 50 billion euros over three years until 2027 is facing opposition from Hungary. As for the 61.4 billion dollars pledged by U.S. President Joe Biden, it is stalled in Congress. In the long term, Ukraine fears wearing out its supporters. Not only does the war in the Middle East divert attention from the international community, but its counteroffensive is not achieving the expected success. The country is therefore eyeing another source of income, much less dependent on the vagaries of its allies' domestic politics. In early November, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky said he wants to "take the money" from Russian assets frozen by Western countries since February 2022. The amounts at stake are significant, as 300 billion dollars have reportedly been frozen, with nearly 200 billion euros in the EU alone. The Russian Central Bank had placed a portion of its foreign currency reserves there. It can no longer access them since Brussels imposed sanctions following the invasion of Ukraine. These assets must now be used for its reconstruction. As of September 1, 2023, the destruction was estimated at 151 billion dollars according to the Kyiv School of Economics, and the country will need at least double that to rebuild. Supporters of confiscation cite the precedent of 1992, when, after the invasion of Kuwait by Iraq, the United States froze 32 billion of Iraqi assets before transferring them to a fund managed by the United Nations to compensate the victims. Loss of Western influence After the deadly and destructive invasion of Ukraine, can we imagine that the frozen assets will one day be returned to Russia? Moscow obviously demands it... invoking international law, which is quite ironic. As explained by Laurence Tribe, a professor of constitutional law at Harvard University (Massachusetts), in an article in The New York Times published in September: "There is simply no basis [in international law] to say that Russia can violate Ukraine's sovereignty while invoking its own sovereignty as an inviolable shield." However, the confiscation of Russian assets is far from unanimous. It could be perceived by many countries as an additional sign of Western hegemony, which may lead them to consider placing some of their reserves in safe locations such as China or India rather than in Europe or the United States, where they risk being confiscated. This could result in a "de-dollarization" of the global economy and a loss of influence for the West. The other option is to redistribute to Ukraine the interest collected on the hundreds of billions of frozen assets. These assets have become a significant windfall due to the global rise in interest rates. Belgium, which hosts the headquarters of the international securities depository Euroclear, is one of the EU countries with the highest amount (164 billion euros). In the first nine months of the year, they generated nearly 3 billion euros in interest. Belgian Prime Minister Alexander De Croo explained that his country was "working with the European Union" on a legal framework to tap into these reserves. In the meantime, he has promised to transfer 1.7 billion euros to Kiev in 2024 by taxing the interest generated by these assets.

title: Russian Caucasus plagued by multiple anti-Semitic incidents
lead: In Dagestan, the airport in Makhachkala, the capital of this Russian republic with a Muslim majority, was closed after being stormed following the announcement of the arrival of a plane from Israel.
text_eng: The airport of Makhachkala, the capital of the Russian republic of Dagestan, was the scene of riots on Sunday, October 29th - a word that in Russian can be translated as "pogrom". For several hours, a crowd stormed the airport in search of either "Israelis" or "Jews". The events were filmed by dozens of phones. After breaking through the doors of the main terminal shouting "Allah Akbar", hundreds of people, some carrying Palestinian flags, stormed the airport. The arrival of a flight from Tel Aviv had been announced beforehand, inflaming already heated tensions in this republic of nearly 3 million inhabitants, mostly Muslim, where Salafist influence has greatly increased in recent years. In the videos, groups of enraged men searched every corner in search of their prey. Others checked vehicles and pedestrians around the airport, demanding to see passports and berating people with "suspicious" appearances - like the Uzbek doctor who was targeted by the angry crowd. In other images, a man points to a bus whispering, "They are there, the Jews, they are all there." Police passivity After tearing down security barriers, the protesters invaded the runways. Several planes were besieged, with passengers and crews barricaded inside. Faced with police passivity, airport employees tried to negotiate by offering the crowd to designate representatives who would be allowed to board to ensure, camera in hand, the absence of Israeli nationals. The intervention of riot police only took place later in the evening. Monday morning, authorities announced sixty arrests and the opening of a criminal investigation for public disorder. "My Jewish friends are afraid, they wonder if people will show up at their homes to 'check their passports'", testifies an activist from Makhachkala who has long been committed to peaceful coexistence between communities. These events were all the more shocking as they occurred as the culmination of a weekend marked by multiple anti-Semitic incidents throughout the Russian Caucasus. In Khassaviourt, also in Dagestan, dozens of men besieged a hotel after rumors identified it as hosting "Israeli refugees". In Nalchik, the capital of the Republic of Kabardino-Balkaria, a community center was burned, and graffiti saying "death to Jews" was written on the building. Anti-Semitic rhetoric In the neighboring region of Karachay-Cherkessia, also predominantly Muslim, protesters demanded the "expulsion of Jews" from the territory. At the same time, addresses of synagogues and rabbis across southern Russia were circulated on Telegram channels calling for riots. An interview on the Podem website with the rabbi of Derbent, a city near the border with Azerbaijan with a millennia-old Jewish history, has left a lasting impression. Ovadia Iissakov stated that he is considering the "evacuation" of the 300 to 400 families that make up his community. When reached by phone, the rabbi claimed it was a "fake" before abruptly hanging up. These incidents in Dagestan and neighboring republics are a rebuke and a threat to the federal authorities. Russian President Vladimir Putin continues to praise the "multinational" character of the Russian Federation and the good relations between communities. Opposing the West Meanwhile, the Kremlin's shift in the Middle East dossier in favor of Palestinian interests and even Hamas, whose delegation was received in Moscow on October 26, has only served to inflame tensions. This choice, primarily driven by the desire to oppose the West, has been accompanied by strong anti-Israeli propaganda on television and a rise in anti-Semitic rhetoric. This is primarily the work of ultranationalist supporters of the war in Ukraine and the most extreme propagandists of the regime, but Mr. Putin himself has been distinguishing between "good Jews" and "bad Jews" for months, with Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky being singled out. In September, he referred to Anatoly Chubais, a key figure in the regime who defected, as "Moche Izrailevich," a caricatured and contemptuous way of referring to a Jew. Regarding Gaza, the Russian president compared the siege of the Palestinian territory by the Israeli army to the blockade of Leningrad, which resulted in 800,000 deaths during World War II. He also waited a week before calling Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu, without condemning Hamas attacks. These recent developments are unfolding in the fertile ground of the Caucasus, where religious radicals are tolerated as long as they show loyalty to Moscow and local authorities, who turn a blind eye to honor crimes or the oppression of women. A sign of the turmoil is that no high-level official has commented on the incidents in Makhachkala on Sunday evening, and official news agencies waited until Monday morning to report on them. Television stations also ignored the events. Local officials were left on the front lines, with the mufti of Dagestan calling for calm. Authorities in Chechnya, a territory where radical Islamism is promoted by the Kadyrov clan, also played a pacifying role. The prevailing line on Monday was to attribute these various incidents over the weekend to actions "orchestrated by enemies of Russia," in the words of Dagestan's president, Sergei Melikov. These suspicions, regarding the incidents at Makhachkala airport, target a Telegram channel linked to former deputy Ilia Ponomarev, who is now fighting the Kremlin from Kiev. This channel is said to have contributed, among other channels, to inflaming tensions and organizing the protest. On Monday, Kremlin spokesperson Dmitry Peskov also echoed the rhetoric attributing these disturbances to "attempts by the West to destabilize Russian society" and announced a "major government meeting" on the subject in the evening, with the participation of Vladimir Putin.

title: The counterattack of Anatoliy Tymoshchuk, the renegade of Ukrainian football
lead: In 2022, the most capped player in the history of Ukraine was stripped of his titles for not condemning the Russian invasion. As an assistant coach of the Saint Petersburg team, close to Russian oligarchs, he filed a request for annulment before the sports court at the end of September.
text_eng: On February 24, 2022, Anatoliy Tymoshchuk went from being an idol of all Ukraine to a traitor reviled by his country. That day, the former player and captain of the national football team refused to condemn the Russian invasion. Living in Saint Petersburg, he, who had become the assistant coach of Zenit, the club sponsored by Gazprom and favored by Vladimir Putin, remained silent, a silence that his compatriots could only judge as complicit. Only on March 9 did he post a bland message on Instagram, while Russian tanks were still at the gates of Kyiv: "We need peace. For the sake of our children and our parents, for the sake of life on earth." There was no reference to the aggressor country and its president. A meager and rather weak reaction from someone who had worn the colors of Ukraine for fifteen years in all European stadiums. While soldiers filmed themselves on the front lines trampling on his number 4 jersey, official sanctions were not long in coming. In March 2022, the Ukrainian Football Federation removed the former star from its hall of fame. They erased his one hundred and forty-four national team appearances, the national record. They stripped him of all his titles won on Ukrainian fields and revoked his coaching diplomas. The man is banned from football on national soil. Far from repenting, the player is now counterattacking before the sports court. In late September, he filed a request for annulment with the Court of Arbitration for Sport (CAS) in Lausanne, Switzerland. When contacted by Le Monde magazine, his lawyer, the Bulgarian Georgi Gradev, refused to comment: "My client does not want to participate in media propaganda." The hearing for the case "CAS 2023/A/9443 Anatoliy Tymoshchuk v. Ukrainian Association of Football (UAF)" has not been scheduled yet. Tymoshchuk vs. Ukraine... How did this unthinkable divorce come about? In the early 2000s, the midfielder, along with striker Andriy Shevchenko, was one of the stars of the young national team, nicknamed the Sbirna. The team had reached the quarterfinals of the 2006 World Cup. A source of pride in a country where football reigns, Anatoliy Tymoshchuk was courted by top clubs. His long blonde hair was a symbol of Ukrainian sport. He won the Champions League in 2013 with Bayern Munich. After each victory, the player always appeared draped in the national colors. He spoke Ukrainian to journalists. In an interview with Le Monde in November 2013, when he had inherited the captain's armband left by Andriy Shevchenko who had retired, the player spoke of his pride in representing Ukraine. He praised the Sbirna, "[its] team spirit, [its] fighting spirit, [its] dedication to the national team, and [its] commitment." Anatoliy Tymoshchuk in the jersey of Zenit Saint Petersburg (Russia), in Dortmund (Germany), on October 28, 2020. ALEX GOTTSCHALK / DEFODI IMAGES VIA GETTY IMAGES However, in 2014, when Russia annexed Crimea, the player who was with Zenit Saint Petersburg did not react to the Kremlin's power grab. In April 2015, he did visit wounded Ukrainian soldiers on the Donbass front alongside other members of the national team. In 2017, Maksym Demtchychyn, his wife's half-brother, was killed by separatists. After retiring as a player in 2017, Anatoliy Tymoshchuk returned to Saint Petersburg as an assistant coach for Zenit. His friends and teammates from the national team noticed a change in attitude, with him displaying pro-Russian positions. At that time, other players from the national team like Andriy Voronin at Dynamo Moscow, or Yaroslav Rakitskiy at Zenit as well, maintained a similar ambiguity. Rakitskiy often angered the Ukrainian public by refusing to sing the national anthem during team presentations. After the invasion in 2022, both players left Russia in solidarity with their people who were suffering from the bombings. The former stars of the national team formed a united front behind their army. Defender Vladyslav Vashchuk (sixty-three caps), who had played for Spartak Moscow, even enlisted. Ballon d'Or winner Andriy Shevchenko (one hundred eleven caps), despite his past ties with Russian oligarch Roman Abramovich, for whom he played when the billionaire owned Chelsea, also clearly supported his country. He was appointed as an external advisor to President Volodymyr Zelensky at the end of September, while also receiving the title of national hero. A more mercantile than political motivation Anatoliy Tymoshchuk's patriotic betrayal was particularly disconcerting given that he hails from Lutsk, a nationalist stronghold in the west of the country. Even though he played in Donetsk for a long time, a region in the Donbass invaded by the Russians in 2014, he is not from the Russian-speaking East, known to be more susceptible to Kremlin propaganda. His motivation appears to be more financial than political. Close to the oligarchic circles of Saint Petersburg, Anatoliy Tymoshchuk has opened several restaurants in the city. He refuses to give up his lucrative sporting and non-sporting activities. His former national team colleagues are not sympathetic to the renegade. "Anatoliy is probably the last subject I would want to discuss right now," replied Oleksandr Shovkovskiy, the former goalkeeper with ninety-two national team caps who lives in Ukraine. "What is happening to him is the result of his choices. And he will have to live with that." His family is no more forgiving. "I'm not going to talk to you about Anatoliy," Oleksandr Tymoshchuk said before hanging up. His father, who joined a territorial regiment. His now ex-wife, who moved to Germany with their children, poetically referred to him as a "Russian ass-kisser" in the Ukrainian press. Her former husband stopped paying alimony in 2014, citing difficulties in transferring money due to economic sanctions against Russia following the annexation of Crimea... Stripped of the title of honorary citizen of Lutsk, sanctions have continued to rain down on the former star. By a decree on January 7, 2023, Volodymyr Zelensky banned him from any financial, administrative, or real estate operations in Ukraine for a period of ten years. Symbolic humiliation: Lutsk, his hometown, which had made him an honorary citizen in 2008, stripped him of this title this year. Ukrainian media also echoed accusations from the player's former personal secretary. Displaying documents with unverifiable sources and accuracy, Vitaliy Yurchenko claimed in March 2022 that the player had been an agent of the FSB, the Russian secret services, since 2012, while he was still a pillar of the national team. Perhaps too good to be true evidence, but it further tarnished the former player's reputation. Faced with all these accusations, the man remains silent, preferring to rely on a court to regain, if not his honor, at least his caps and diplomas.

title: Jake Sullivan, National Security Advisor to Joe Biden: "Protecting civilians in Gaza is a moral and strategic imperative."
lead: In an interview with "Le Monde", Jake Sullivan, the National Security Advisor of the White House, describes the American vision of the war between Israel and Hamas and refutes Washington's isolation on this conflict.
text_eng: Jake Sullivan was unable to enjoy the festive atmosphere at the White House on Monday, October 30, for Halloween. The national security advisor to President Biden has been absorbed by the Israeli-Palestinian conflict since the Hamas attack on October 7. In an interview with Le Monde, just before meeting with Prince Khaled Ben Salman Al Saud, the Minister of Defense of Saudi Arabia, 46-year-old Jake Sullivan outlined the American strategy and vision in this crisis. The White House has shown impressive empathy and support for Israel after the Hamas attack. Since then, you have had to repeatedly readjust your position, first by focusing on humanitarian aid for Gaza, then by discussing the laws of war. How do you explain American isolation in the Middle East? In his first major speech just days after the crisis began, President Biden mentioned the laws of war and the fact that Hamas does not represent the Palestinian people. So I refute the idea that we only introduced this later. The president then unequivocally stated that Israel has the right and even the duty to defend itself against terrorist attacks, that Hamas presents exceptional challenges by hiding among the population, using civilians as human shields. This represents an additional burden for the Israeli army. But it does not diminish its responsibility in distinguishing between terrorists and innocent civilians. You speak of isolation, but the United States gathered enough voices to pass a resolution at the Security Council condemning Hamas, which addresses these humanitarian issues. The only reason it did not pass is the veto of Russia and China. But in reality, we have obtained the support of a significant number of countries around the world, in Latin America, Asia, and Europe. So we will continue to work in New York. We will continue to work with Arab states. On Sunday, the president spoke with the Egyptian president, Al-Sissi. He will speak with other Arab leaders this week. We will continue to uphold our principles and offer our best advice to Israel. Did you give Israel full approval for its military operations in Gaza? I think that is a mistaken way of presenting it. Israel is a sovereign nation. They do not come to us to ask if we have given the green light or not, if the fire is green or red. We ask them difficult questions. We advise them as friends. We tell them that we have been in a similar situation, although I do not want to make a full comparison, after September 11, 2001. We were determined to pursue the terrorists, and nothing was going to stop us. But we also made mistakes and learned from them. What are they? First, there was an exaggerated military engagement in the broader Middle East following 9/11, and the way we conducted ourselves in these conflicts. If we could turn back time, we would do things differently. President Biden has spoken eloquently about this regarding Afghanistan for over ten years. But I will add this. When the president speaks with Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu, when I talk to my counterparts, when Secretary of State Antony Blinken, Secretary of Defense Lloyd Austin, and General Charles Brown [Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff] speak with their counterparts, we convey our deep conviction: protecting civilians and facilitating the delivery of vital aid to them is both a moral and strategic necessity. This is a central point in the public and private statements we make, and it will remain so. Whenever U.S. officials are asked about the disproportionate use of force by the Israeli military in Gaza, the response is: "We do not discuss specific strikes." But isn't this a matter of principle, in light of the past three weeks and past wars in Gaza? In reality, I have repeatedly mentioned, as has the president, the principle of protecting civilians and the sacredness of every innocent civilian life: Palestinian, Israeli, or otherwise. And we will continue to invoke this principle. What we do not want is to react to specific reports, to specific qualifications. We will not be drawn into a role of judge and jury in the midst of all this. Is it possible to destroy Hamas? It is estimated to consist of 20,000 to 25,000 armed men, and each new cycle of confrontation brings forth a new generation... From my perspective, it is possible to ensure a future where Gaza cannot be used as a terrorist base to threaten Israel or anyone else. When the president rules out returning to October 6 [the situation before the attack], he means two things. First, Hamas must not be able to preserve the capabilities to continue to pose a strategic threat to Israel. Second, there must be a political horizon for the Palestinian people, anchored in the two-state solution. This is so that the future in Gaza and the West Bank is one of peace, not violence. I believe this outcome is possible. Of course, it is difficult to achieve. It has eluded everyone for decades, but I think it is attainable. And I believe that the events of the past month only reinforce the president's conviction that we must work towards this. This requires working with Israelis and Palestinians, but also in a broader vision of regional integration with key Arab countries. But have you told the Israelis that with an organization like Hamas, when you cut off one head, another one grows back? Our own experience indicates that dealing with terrorist groups requires a multifaceted approach. There is a military aspect, but not only that. There is an effort in terms of financing, political, social, and economic conditions. An effort to seek diplomatic and political responses to these conditions, in order to forge a more sustainable solution over time. We have been clear with the Israelis. As we have stated publicly, we believe that ultimately the solution does not lie solely in military action, even though military action against terrorists is a part of the solution. It also lies in the political horizon that we have described. Obviously, not everyone in the Israeli government shares this perspective. Many do not support the two-state solution. President Biden firmly and unequivocally supports it. He has made this clear in his response to this crisis. Several Israeli officials have suggested that Gaza should be reduced in size after the war, with the creation of a demilitarized zone. Would this restore Israel's security? I do not want to comment on specific opinions. From a general point of view, I do not believe that the territory of Gaza should be reduced. I prefer to leave it to others to consider these questions, the operational aspects, how to ensure that attacks cannot be launched from Gaza towards Israel. I do not think it is sensible or acceptable to forcibly relocate the inhabitants of Gaza. And as the president has said, a reoccupation of Gaza by Israel would make no sense. On the issue of buffer zones or others, I would defer to a consultation with a wide circle of involved parties, including the Palestinian people. When President Biden met with the President of the Palestinian Authority, Mahmoud Abbas, in July 2022, he reaffirmed his commitment to the two-state solution. But he also said, "The ground is not currently fertile for restarting negotiations." Did you make a mistake by following in Donald Trump's footsteps, by prioritizing the normalization process between Israel and Arab countries before any attempt to resolve the conflict? Do you think the ground was fertile? If we assume that the ground was not fertile for direct negotiations, the question is: how do we achieve a two-state solution, or at least preserve this possibility in the future, without it completely disappearing from our field of vision? We had an answer. It lay in seeking a normalization process between Israel and Saudi Arabia that included a substantial Palestinian component. He offered political and economic benefits to the Palestinian people. I haven't heard a better solution or approach than that. I think the effort to bring both parties together in a room to agree was not going to get us anywhere. So we believed that this alternative would actually help progress and create the possibility of significant outcomes for the Palestinians, which could ultimately preserve the two-state solution. That's how we saw things, and how we continue to see them. The Biden administration has deployed significant military forces in the Eastern Mediterranean. How do you assess the threat posed by Hezbollah? What are the differences compared to Hamas? There are obvious differences between them, both in terms of capabilities, religious affiliation (Sunni for Hamas, Shiite for Hezbollah), as well as in terms of the relationship between Hezbollah and the Lebanese government. But there are also similarities. Both receive a huge amount of funding, training, and sponsorship from Iran, and both represent a significant threat to Israel. I would describe the risk of a further escalation on Israel's northern border as very real. The president was attentive to this from the beginning when he said that no nation or group should try to take advantage of this crisis. We have moved forces, including two naval groups, to send a deterrent message and warn: don't even think about it. The United States has been clear about its intentions. Regarding Iran, we have been clear as well: if you attack American forces, we will respond. We have done so before. And if these attacks continue (in Syria and Iraq), we will respond again. The Biden administration is asking for over $105 billion from Congress as part of a security package, mainly for Ukraine and Israel. Could your foreign policy in these two major crises be hindered by a minority of Trump-supporting lawmakers? I still believe that ultimately, even if the path is not completely straight and short, there will be strong bipartisan support, both in the House of Representatives and the Senate, for aid to Israel and Ukraine. I cannot predict exactly how things will turn out. But we have put forward a set of proposals because we believe that all these pieces fit together and must be adopted together. When asked about the purpose of American support for Ukraine, you say, "A Ukrainian victory based on territorial integrity and sovereignty." And when asked to define this victory, you say it is up to President Zelensky to do so. Shouldn't the American administration have its own strategy and war objectives? We have a strategy. Our strategy is to maintain a coalition of 50 nations to provide Ukraine with the necessary support so that they can make as much progress as possible on the battlefield, which can then be translated into maximum leverage at the negotiating table. However, it is up to the Ukrainians to determine their destiny. They are a sovereign nation. It is not for America to do it for them. We are not here to tell the Ukrainians how their war should end, in which they are fighting and dying every day. They define that. We define ways to assist them in achieving their goals. In these two crises, it is easier to say what the United States does not want – a regional conflict in the Middle East, a war against Russia beyond Ukrainian borders – than what they do want. Is this new form of containment the essence of your foreign policy? First and foremost, I would describe it as common sense, as a way to safeguard the fundamental interests of the American people. A third world war would not be good for Americans, nor would a massive regional war in the Middle East. Do not lose sight of the fact that, in many respects, national security, defending the interests and values of a nation and its people, allies, and partners, involves defending against threats and preventing bad things from happening. That is a big part of what we are trying to do. But we are also strongly in favor of something in these two cases. In the midst of the crisis, President Biden spoke from the White House to develop a vision for a future between Israelis and Palestinians, and the region at large, rooted in something we strongly believe in, not something we oppose. As for Ukraine, the President visited Kiev in February, and I had the privilege of accompanying him. He then spoke about how Ukraine had already achieved a significant part of the vision they are fighting for. Kiev stands. A sovereign and democratic Ukraine stands, increasingly oriented towards a Euro-Atlantic direction. This is a positive vision, a vision that few would have believed possible when Russian tanks crossed the border in February 2022. Why is Joe Biden opposed to Ukraine's accession to the North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO)? Is it a definitive no, or is it as long as the war continues? First, the United States and every other NATO ally signed the declaration in Vilnius [July 2023] stating that Ukraine's legitimate place is in the Alliance. And every year, since the Bucharest summit [2008], we have renewed the commitment that Ukraine will become a NATO member. President Biden raised two questions. First, how do we ensure that Ukraine is implementing all the necessary reforms to become a full member of NATO? And then, how do we create the necessary conditions for its entry? Because currently, with the ongoing war, if Ukraine were to join NATO tomorrow, NATO would be at war with Russia. The United States would be at war with Russia. And that is not something the president is willing to support. So, these are the two things driving his thinking: the question of Ukraine's membership and the conditions he envisions for moving that forward.

title: Ukraine surprised by Russian counterattack in Avdiivka.
lead: The Moscow forces have reportedly managed to conquer several square kilometers of territory at the cost of heavy losses. The city, with a population of 35,000, which had been occupied by pro-Russian militias for a few months in 2014, is "heavily fortified."
text_eng: The maneuver has surprised the Ukrainians and their Western allies. While it was believed to be in retreat along the entire 1,200 kilometers of the front line, the Russian army has been conducting a significant counterattack in Avdiivka, an industrial city in the Donbass region located 15 kilometers north of Donetsk in the eastern part of the country. "Our troops are improving their position in almost the entire area, a fairly large area," said Russian President Vladimir Putin in a televised interview broadcast on Sunday, October 15, even though the progress of his forces appears to be slow. According to the Institute for the Study of War (ISW), an American think tank that closely monitors the conflict's developments, this new offensive by Moscow began on October 10 and saw "Russian forces simultaneously attacking to the northwest, west, and south of Avdiivka using armored assault groups, helicopters, and artillery," an inter-arms maneuver that has been rarely used by the Russians due to poor mastery. "This is probably the most significant offensive operation undertaken by Russia since at least January," confirmed the British Ministry of Defense in a note published on X (formerly Twitter) on Tuesday, October 17. To carry out this operation, Russia is said to have deployed over a thousand men, as well as a large amount of equipment, including some of the most modern in its arsenal - such as the Terminator armored vehicle and the TOS-1 rocket launcher. This mass deployment intrigues analysts, as Moscow had appeared to focus its efforts on defending the front in southern Ukraine, where Kiev's troops have been engaged in a large counteroffensive operation since June. "This attack shows that the Russians have managed to rebuild some of their offensive reserves," said Leo Peria-Peigne, a researcher at the French Institute of International Relations. For Moscow, a political objective According to analysts, this maneuver aims to allow Russia to regain the initiative, as the Ukrainian counteroffensive is faltering and the conflict has been ongoing for over six hundred days. It could also have a political purpose: to show the Russian population that the conquest of the Donbass, the main objective declared by Moscow since the end of the battle of Kiev, is still possible. Avdiivka, which had a population of 35,000 before the war, has been contested for nearly a decade. In 2014, the pro-Russian militias of the "People's Republic of Donetsk" even occupied it for a few months. "It is a heavily fortified position and will be difficult to take," said Mr. Peria-Peigne. According to images circulated on social media and geolocated by analysts, Russian forces have reportedly managed to conquer several square kilometers of territory around Avdiivka since October 10. Some Russian military bloggers even claim that a breakthrough of 10 kilometers has been achieved, a data that cannot be verified. However, this advance would have been made in the early days of the offensive and has since slowed down, a sign that it may have already reached its peak. "It is unlikely that Russian forces will achieve significant breakthroughs or cut off the presence of Ukrainian forces in these territories in the short term," according to the ISW. Moreover, this advance would come at the cost of heavy losses for Russian forces. A spokesperson for the Ukrainian army stated that 3,000 Moscow soldiers have been killed or injured and that over 300 armored vehicles and artillery pieces have already been lost in the offensive. These numbers are impossible to verify but partially confirmed by videos circulated on social media, showing Russian armored columns under enemy artillery fire. "The Russian army appears to be using a tactic of wave attacks by throwing masses of poorly trained soldiers directly onto the battlefield without proper equipment," emphasized John Kirby, spokesperson for the White House National Security Council, on October 13. Ukrainians are using long-range missiles In response to this counterattack, the Kiev army continues its slow erosion of Moscow's forces. During the night from Monday, October 16 to Tuesday, October 17, several strikes hit the airfields of Luhansk and Berdiansk, located in the east and southeast of Ukraine. Nine helicopters and significant ammunition stocks were destroyed or damaged, according to the Ukrainian army. More surprisingly, these bombings were carried out using ATACMS missiles, with a range of about 165 kilometers, according to images of debris taken on site. Until now, the United States had always refused to supply these long-range projectiles to the Ukrainians, believing that they would make it too easy to strike Russian territory. In his daily address on social media, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky confirmed on Tuesday evening their arrival in the country's arsenals. "The agreements we have reached with President [Joe] Biden are being implemented. They are being implemented very precisely: the ATACMS have proven themselves," he stated.

title: Israeli-Palestinian conflict: "Why this disengagement from the major powers, this attitude of indifference for so many years?"
lead: The passivity of the United States, the European Union, Russia, and China has paved the way for Benjamin Netanyahu and marginalized the Palestinian issue, analyzes Alain Frachon, editorialist at "Le Monde" in his column.
text_eng: "You have abandoned us." This "you" is addressed to the United States and to Europeans, but also to other major powers. It is an Israeli speaking, a peace activist, a proponent of the so-called "two-state solution"; it could be a Palestinian, at least one who is willing to recognize Israel. This "you" is a cry of distress and protest. It calls out to Joe Biden as the American president landed in Tel Aviv. In the bitterness and tragedies of the moment, this "you" is a way of asking a fundamental question that will determine the future of the Israeli-Palestinian conflict: why has the mediation of the powerful of the time, and especially that of America, the de facto guardian of the Middle East after the end of the Cold War, stopped? In Washington, Moscow, Beijing, Brussels, the directive seems to be the same: do not touch, anymore, the Israeli-Palestinian conflict – or only in a purely rhetorical way. Why do Israelis and Palestinians no longer have an international framework to compel them to dialogue? Why this disengagement of the Great Powers, this withdrawal, this indifference for so many years? Behind the spiral of violence and atrocities that once again marks the conflict between the two peoples fighting over the same land, there is this accusation. It is justified for a reason that the history of contemporary Middle East – twenty years of uninterrupted wars since 2001 – prohibits us from ignoring: there, emptiness calls for violence, always. America's abandoned mission The collapse of the USSR in the early 1990s gave Washington a predominant role in the region – an interlocutor and protector of many Arab regimes; a friend and strategic ally of Israel. America had a mission, that of being an honest mediator. Over the years, and particularly since the beginning of the 21st century, it has abandoned this mission, allowing free rein to those in Israel and among the Palestinians who did not want negotiations. In 1993, sealing mutual recognition between Israel and the Palestine Liberation Organization (PLO), the Oslo process, incomplete and unbalanced (to the detriment of the Palestinians), institutionalized, under the auspices of the United States, a dialogue between the two parties. The Israeli right did not want it: they intended to colonize the entire West Bank. A rival of Fatah, the central group in the PLO, Hamas, an offshoot of the Muslim Brotherhood created in 1987, also did not want it: they had the ambition to "liberate" all of Palestine. The fragile "Oslo process" would slowly die, a victim of its intrinsic weaknesses and the blows of history, both local and regional, internal developments among the protagonists, and finally the transformation of the Republican Party in the United States. Under the increasing weight of its fundamentalist Christian bloc, the party of the elephant has converted to the creed of the Israeli right. With Donald Trump, it even went as far as to defend and promote colonization in the West Bank. John Kerry, Secretary of State during the second term of Democrat Barack Obama, had tried to restart a final Israeli-Palestinian dialogue. In vain. It was not a priority for a White House susceptible to isolationist tendencies. In a region shaped by Washington's politics, there has long been no "honest American mediator." But Russia under Vladimir Putin, which has cultivated strong relations with Benjamin Netanyahu, and China under Xi Jinping, a significant investor in Israeli high-tech, have not been any more proactive on the Israeli-Palestinian issue. While the European Union generously funds the Palestinian Authority in the West Bank, it has never dared to exert any pressure, political or economic, on the conflicting parties. The Palestinians have played their part. The division of the national movement into two enemy branches (Fatah and Hamas) is an obstacle to negotiation. In 2007, in control of the Gaza Strip, Hamas could have decided to turn it into a model of a proto-Palestinian state. Instead, it dedicated its resources to assembling rockets and firing them at southern Israel – without creating a network of shelters for the civilian defense of Gazans. In doing so, it helped bolster the argument of the Israeli right: peace in exchange for territories does not work. The passivity, a form of complicity, of the Great Powers paved the way for the leader of the Israeli right, Benjamin Netanyahu, who has been in power for nearly fifteen years. From Washington to Brussels, they all turned a blind eye to the colonization of the occupied territory of the West Bank – disregarding UN resolutions. The Palestinian Authority of Mahmoud Abbas was humiliated and belittled when, in its competition with Hamas, it should have been strengthened day by day. But the major powers tacitly accepted what the Israeli government asked of them: leave us alone to deal with the Palestinians, and we will manage. The Netanyahu doctrine was taking shape. The Arab world was going to normalize its relations with Israel without demanding anything in return on the Palestinian issue. The same applied to the development of ties between the Israeli state and Washington, Moscow, Beijing: they were not dependent on stopping settlements in the West Bank or resuming Israeli-Palestinian dialogue. The Palestinian issue was marginalized, a matter of law enforcement. Understanding in three minutes What is the Gaza Strip? Understanding in three minutes Videos and photos of atrocities are pouring unfiltered on social networks Gaza, seventy-five years of history in turmoil A century of conflict in maps and key dates False images and videos circulating since October 7 Find the latest news in our section on the Israel-Hamas war.

title: In Antwerp, the impossible sanctions against Russian diamonds
section: I don't understand the phrase "M le mag." Can you please provide more context or clarify?
lead: As the G7 is on the verge of banning the sale of Russian gemstones on the territory of its member countries, diamond traders in the Belgian port are worried and arguing. However, in this opaque market, it is easy to conceal the exact origin of the gemstone.
text_eng: The three narrow streets near Antwerp's train station in Belgium, where approximately $200 million (189 million euros) worth of diamonds transit each day, protect themselves from the rest of the world. On the morning of October 10th, in a "diamond district" undergoing renovations, rare groups of tourists cross paths with representatives of major Indian firms or Orthodox Jewish families who have been working in these grayish facades for generations. In the Beurs Voor Diamanthandel, one of the four diamond exchanges in the Flemish city, a visitor badge is required to pass through any gate. On the 6th floor are the offices of several companies, including Alrosa, the Russian leader responsible for a third of the world's diamond production. While many Russian companies are under sanctions since the invasion of Ukraine, Alrosa employees go to the office "every day," several employees in the neighborhood assure. This situation could soon change: the G7 (France, Germany, Canada, United States, Italy, Japan, United Kingdom) is expected to announce the ban on the trade of Russian diamonds any day now. This would add to a market price decline and the competition from synthetic diamonds, which are cheaper and very popular. Within this Antwerp industry that has existed since the late 19th century and represents 70% of the world's production with its 1,700 specialized companies, many diamond traders met complain: "This is the worst crisis in our history." Close to the Kremlin The Belgian port and Alrosa have been long-time allies. Established in Antwerp since its creation in 1992 and becoming influential in the mid-2000s, "the Russian company considers the city a reliable entry point to avoid finding 'blood diamonds' on the market, which fund armed groups around the world," explains Hans Merket, a researcher at the International Peace Information Service (IPIS), an independent human rights research institute. Alrosa holds 90% of the Russian market through its mines in Siberia. The remaining 10% is operated by a second Russian company, Grib Diamonds. Sanctioned by the United States since October, this company is suspected of money laundering by Belgian authorities. After the invasion of Ukraine in February 2022, none of the eleven series of sanctions developed by the European Union against Russia targeted diamond traders. Alrosa continued to do business in Europe: in 2022, European companies bought 1.4 billion euros worth of Russian diamonds. However, this company of around 3,500 employees, partly owned by the Kremlin (33% of shares), supports the invasion of Ukraine by financing the multiple renovations of the Russian military submarine B-871 Alrosa. The family of its CEO, Sergei Ivanov, is known to be close to President Vladimir Putin. "Many of our office neighbors, of all nationalities, saw the war as an opportunity and boasted about the good deals to come," regrets Rafael Papismedov, co-founder of the start-up HB Antwerp, which mainly works with mines in Botswana. "In this industry, the only shame is not making money." Stable sales figures On March 31, 2022, in front of the Belgian Parliament, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky pointed out this profit-driven race: "We are fighting against tyranny that wants to destroy Europe, that wants to destroy those for whom freedom is very important. But there are those for whom Russian diamonds, sometimes sold in Antwerp, are more important." Despite pressure, Belgium has opposed European sanctions in this area, fearing they would benefit Dubai, the new diamond hub. In April 2022, the United States, the world's largest importer, disconnected Alrosa from their banking system and banned the sale of raw stones originating from Russia. But these sanctions are ineffective: hardly any Russian gems were directly imported into the U.S. territory. In fact, a rough diamond takes on the nationality of the country where it is polished, usually in India. At the same time, several luxury groups, including LVMH and Cartier, publicly stated that, sanctions or not, they will no longer use Russian diamonds. According to figures from the Antwerp World Diamond Centre (AWDC), one of the federations representing diamond traders, local purchases from Alrosa in the first half of 2023 dropped by 94% year-on-year. Problem solved? Only on the surface. Sales figures have actually remained stable since 2022, notes an independent consultant and industry expert (who wishes to remain anonymous), met at his home in Antwerp, complete with a pool and a robotic lawnmower. "Mixed origin" stones In the first half of 2023, Alrosa totaled $1.9 billion (1.8 billion euros) in revenue, a 0.2% increase year-on-year. It is quite easy to disguise the origin of the stones: if Russian diamonds are mixed with others of different nationalities, the whole batch will then be exported as a "mixed origin" product, without further specification. "These luxury companies rely on third parties for their imports and therefore cannot really ensure that they are not selling any Russian gemstones," says the expert. When contacted, LVMH states that no Russian diamond "can be sold to [its] houses, regardless of the country where it was manufactured and polished." Russian diamonds continue to embarrass the European political class. According to an advisor within the Belgian government, the "reputational risks" are becoming "enormous" as Volodymyr Zelensky regularly discusses the issue with Belgian Prime Minister Alexander De Croo. In May 2023, in an unexpected turn of events, Alexander De Croo finally announced the upcoming vote on a total ban on Russian diamonds for all G7 countries. Through the European Commission, in association with the G7, the Belgian government aims for all rough diamonds traded among the seven member countries to first pass through Antwerp, where their origin will be certified, before being able to be exported to the rest of the world for cutting and resale. This would also allow the Flemish city to withstand the growing influence of Dubai, while imposing unprecedented transparency in the industry. The AWDC welcomes this "revolutionary" choice. However, their position is contested. In mid-September, 16 influential companies sent a confidential letter to the AWDC, as seen by M Le magazine du Monde, accusing them of a lack of "communication, information, and collaboration" in the current "turmoil." The director of the AWDC, David Gotlib, had to resign a week later. A commitment without binding measures Another representative of diamond traders, the World Diamond Council (WDC), opposes the Belgian version of sanctions against Russia, supported by the G7 countries. This federation advocates for a simple formal commitment from the entire supply chain not to use Russian diamonds, without any binding measures. "They mainly want to maintain the status quo," regrets a French diamond trader in Antwerp, who wishes to remain anonymous. The WDC has also found new allies... in France. The French Union of Jewelry, Jewelery, Silversmithing, Stones, and Pearls (UFBJOP) confirms in an email that they prefer to rely on "supplier declarations on invoices." According to an internal document from LVMH, obtained by M Le magazine du Monde, the luxury group also emphasizes the relevance of the "scenario developed by the WDC" and its "necessary verifications" at all levels of the supply chain. "The French luxury group has contacted the Elysée to discuss the issue," says the advisor within the Belgian government. When contacted, LVMH did not respond on this point. After a vote in the coming days, the G7 plans to implement the sanctions in early 2024. In his Antwerp property, the consultant shrugs: "Whatever happens, Russian diamonds will end up on the market, everywhere in the world." In Antwerp as elsewhere.

title: A trip by Volodymyr Zelensky to Israel called into question after a leak in the media.
lead: The Ukrainian president, who has a pro-Israel stance, has been trying to rally the Israeli state to his cause since the beginning of the invasion of his country by Russia.
text_eng: The rumor of an imminent visit by the Ukrainian President, Volodymyr Zelensky, had spread like wildfire in the Israeli media. On Saturday, November 4, citing an anonymous Ukrainian diplomat, the Times of Israel finally announced that the trip was being called into question due to a leak revealing the planned visit to a national news channel. According to the diplomat, the Ukrainian president "wanted the trip to be made public when he set foot on Israeli soil." "He is very disappointed," the diplomat added. Ukrainian authorities have not commented on this decision reversal, just as they had not made any announcements beforehand. It is difficult to know whether the visit will be maintained, postponed, or canceled. This leak in the press nonetheless remains a weak sign of change since Ukraine positioned itself in support of the Israeli state and Moscow took a hostile turn towards Israel at the beginning of the conflict with Hamas, a month earlier. At the end of October, the Israeli ambassador to Ukraine, Michael Brodsky, explained to local media that he supported the idea of a visit by the president of one of the most pro-Israeli countries in Europe, in his words. Shortly after the Hamas massacres on October 7, the Ukrainian president had been one of the first leaders to offer to visit the region in solidarity with the Israeli state. This initial request had been rejected through the press. According to Iliya Kusa, an analyst based in Kiev, this public support can be explained in part by Ukraine's ambition to "capitalize" on the deteriorating relations between Russia and Israel. "Since Russia has seemingly taken a pro-Palestine stance [since the beginning of the war], many hoped that this would lead to a rupture in the usual relations between the two countries and that it would help Ukraine to sway Israel to our side," he explains. Israel's dependence on Russia Kiev has been trying to rally Israel to its cause since the beginning of the invasion, hoping to receive military support, but without much success. Unlike Western leaders, the Israeli Prime Minister has not visited Ukraine since February 24, 2022, due in particular to Israel's strategic dependence on Russia. Moscow, which has had a presence in Syria since 2015, allows the Israeli air force to carry out precise strikes against Iranian interests in the country. However, this new pro-Israeli positioning, inevitable due to Kiev's relationship with the United States, is not without risks. "Ukraine is torn between the need to keep Israel as a valuable partner and the interest in improving relations with non-Western countries, especially in the Middle East, as we want the Middle East to remain neutral, at least in the war between Ukraine and Russia," Mr. Kusa explains. Since the beginning of the conflict between Israel and Hamas, some countries playing strategic mediation roles between Ukraine and Russia have accused the West of using double standards, between the general condemnation of Russian war crimes and the silence surrounding civilian victims in Gaza.

title: A Parisian investigating judge takes over the investigation into the Russian oligarch Souleïman Kerimov.
lead: The legal procedure opened in Nice on suspicions of money laundering related to the acquisition of villas in Cap d'Antibes (Alpes-Maritimes) was heading towards dismissal. A report from Tracfin in June 2022 has revived the investigation.
text_eng: The resounding Kerimov case was left in the midst of negotiations in the summer of 2022 between the courts of appeal in Aix-en-Provence and Paris, awaiting to know who would inherit this emblematic case of the French justice system's inability to apprehend certain Russian assets. According to information from Le Monde, a decision was made this summer to transfer the case to a specialized magistrate at the Paris judicial court. "A judge in Nice relinquished the case to a judge from the national jurisdiction for combating organized crime," the Paris prosecutor's office stated. The procedure concerns the offense of organized money laundering from the direct or indirect proceeds of a crime, including corruption, embezzlement of public funds, and aggravated tax fraud. This latest development, far from being an epilogue, is part of a legal saga that began in 2014 when the Nice judiciary started investigating strange real estate transactions in Cap d'Antibes. During wiretaps in an unrelated case, investigators discovered that the purchase of a villa named "Hier" allegedly involved the payment of tens of millions of euros in under-the-table money by the presumed buyer, Souleïman Kerimov, a Russian oligarch close to President Vladimir Putin. While the official price of the villa was 35 million euros, the sellers actually pocketed an additional 92 million euros undeclared. On the sellers' side, the investigation led to the indictment of thirteen individuals and two companies. Their referral to the criminal court is currently under appeal. On the buyers' side, the investigation quickly expanded to three other villas suspected of also belonging to Souleïman Kerimov. The judicial police are particularly interested in the role played by the houses' steward, Cédric F., and their official owner, Alexander Studhalter, suspected of acting as a front man. However, due to the investigating judge's misinterpretation of the facts, the money laundering charges against the three men were overturned by the Aix-en-Provence Court of Appeal in June 2018. The investigation was revived with the war in Ukraine. After re-indicting Mr. Kerimov's entourage in September 2019 for money laundering of tax fraud allegedly committed by the oligarch, the investigating judge in Nice decided to split the sprawling case in December 2019 to extract the "Kerimov" component into a separate procedure. The goal is to investigate the origin of the money used to purchase and maintain the villas, and to understand who the true owner is. The investigation uncovered a dual financing network based on offshore companies and the use of large amounts of cash to operate the villas, suspected to come from tax fraud in Russia. More quickly, this new procedure also quickly falls through, unable to demonstrate the existence of the original offense. The investigation is finally closed in October 2020, with no investigative actions taken, apart from the investigating judge's decision in July 2020 to reverse the indictment of Suleiman Kerimov's entourage. A general dismissal seems inevitable, but the invasion of Ukraine by Russia changes the situation. It becomes difficult for judicial authorities to close the investigation against the oligarch as he appears alongside Vladimir Putin in the hours following the start of the Russian offensive and is listed on the European sanctions list. Especially since Mr. Kerimov finds himself in the media spotlight. The investigative unit of Radio France reveals in March 2022 that the declared beneficiary of the villas targeted in the investigation is now Gulnara Kerimova, the oligarch's daughter, while he has always denied being the owner of the properties. When questioned by journalists about this strange coincidence, Alexander Studhalter simply explains that the villas were sold to the "highest bidder" at the end of 2019 after "receiving several offers." He mentions being accompanied by the audit firm EY, which ensured the regularity of the sale price. This discovery piques the interest of Tracfin, the French financial intelligence unit, which initiates verifications to understand how Ms. Kerimova came to head the companies owning the villas. The financial transactions are deemed suspicious enough to warrant a report to the public prosecutor's office in Nice in June 2022. Tracfin details several transactions that allowed Gulnara Kerimova to replace Alexander Studhalter at the helm of the Swiss holding company Swiru at the end of 2019, and then to repurchase the villas for herself to house them in a brand new Swiss company, Sunset Properties AG, in 2021. According to a source close to the investigation, these operations followed negotiations between the Kerimov family and the tax authorities to clarify the ownership chain of the properties and designate a true beneficiary. A dossier seen as a "legal burden" Tracfin nevertheless estimates that the 268 million euros used to repurchase the villas reached Gulnara Kerimova "under particularly opaque conditions." "The financial operation was made possible by significant money transfers made, under the guise of donations in Russia, by Mr. Said Kerimov, brother of Ms. Gulnara Kerimova," notes the intelligence service, concluding that "the financing circuit (...) is particularly opaque and devoid of apparent economic rationality." These elements could constitute a money laundering offense through concealing its origin. This report triggers a dual reaction. Initially addressed to the public prosecutor's office in Nice in June 2022, it caught the attention of the financial section of the national jurisdiction for combating organized crime (Junalco) at the public prosecutor's office in Paris, which invoked its right of referral for cases of "very high complexity" to open a preliminary investigation into these new facts in the fall of 2022. The work of Tracfin also enables the public prosecutor's office in Nice to revive the investigations in the initial aspect of the case, which was on the verge of being dropped. The magistrates in Nice take this opportunity to request the transfer of the procedure, perceived as a "legal burden," to a financial investigating judge in Paris from Junalco. After lengthy debates between jurisdictions, the judicial hierarchy ultimately arbitrates in favor of this decision last summer. In addition to the legal proceedings in France, Souleïman Kerimov is also the subject of an investigation in the United States, where the U.S. Department of Justice announced on October 23 that it had initiated proceedings to confiscate the yacht Amadea, estimated at 300 million dollars (284 million euros). According to U.S. authorities, Mr. Kerimov is the true owner and has violated sanctions measures against him by using the U.S. financial system to maintain and improve the boat. The Russian oligarch denies being the owner.

title: Russian oligarch Alexei Kuzmichev placed in police custody by the French justice system in a money laundering case.
lead: The billionaire, who owns yachts and real estate in France, has been subject to sanctions since the beginning of the war in Ukraine. According to information from "Le Monde," his villa in Saint-Tropez was searched on Tuesday morning.
text_eng: It is one of the biggest blows dealt by the French justice to a Russian oligarch since the invasion of Ukraine. According to information from Le Monde, Alexei Kouzmitchev was placed in police custody on the morning of Monday, October 30. The National Financial Prosecutor's Office (PNF) confirms the information and specifies that "different places were searched," including "the oligarch's residence in Paris," who owns a mansion located in the 7th arrondissement of the capital, between the National Assembly and the Orsay Museum. According to our information, the oligarch's villa in Saint-Tropez (Var) was also searched in the morning. Mr. Kouzmitchev, a co-shareholder of the Alfa Group consortium, which owns Alfa Bank, is considered by European authorities as "one of the most influential people in Russia," with "well-established links to the President of the Russian Federation, Vladimir Putin." European sanctions theoretically prohibit the presence of individuals on European soil, but a source close to the oligarch told Le Monde that Mr. Kouzmitchev was already in France at the time of his addition to the sanctions list, and that he also has family ties in the country. The searches involved sixty people, including police officers from the Central Office for Combating Corruption and Financial and Tax Crimes (OCLCIFF), in charge of the investigation, and around forty elite police officers from RAID and BRI who came to secure the vast property in Var, which extends over more than two hectares. According to our information, bundles of cash were found on site. Three investigations by the National Financial Prosecutor's Office The investigation concerns suspicions of money laundering, tax fraud, and other offenses, "including sanctions violations," details the PNF. This is the third legal procedure opened by the PNF against oligarchs following the invasion of Ukraine by Russia. Meanwhile, the Paris prosecutor's office has launched about fifteen procedures targeting Russian personalities for money laundering related to the acquisition of real estate in Paris, on the French Riviera, and in the Alps. In three of these investigations, the Paris prosecutor's office has already seized nearly 100 million euros in assets, mainly real estate. The fate of Alexei Kouzmitchev's properties in Paris and Var following the searches remains unknown for now. Upon the oligarch's inclusion on the European sanctions list on March 15, 2022, his two yachts, La Petite Ourse and La Petite Ourse II, moored in Antibes and Cannes (Alpes-Maritimes), were immobilized by customs. Alexei Kouzmitchev had these measures canceled by the court in October and December 2022.
