The decisions, consulted by Le Monde, reproach the agents of the customs coast guard service and the national customs intelligence and investigation directorate (DNRED) for having circumvented the law to board ships and notify captains of the immobilization of yachts. The Paris Court of Appeal mentions "serious errors" and "a lack of rigor on the part of customs agents." In July 2023, the Court of Cassation finally reinstated the customs immobilization of one of the yachts. Both boats, however, are still subject to a freeze that prohibits any sale or rental. In May 2022, the billionaire also contested, before the Council of State, the publication of the list of real estate assets frozen by the Ministry of Economy. He argued that this publication constituted "a serious and immediate violation of his privacy" and put "his life and that of his family in danger." His request was rejected by the administrative court. Do you have information to share with us? Feel free to contact our investigations team securely, which is available to exchange with you if you have information or documents of public interest.

title: The race against time to repatriate Bogdan Ermokhin, an orphan from Mariupol deported to Russia.
lead: The young Ukrainian will celebrate his 18th birthday on November 19th. His cousin and his lawyer are fighting to secure his return, fearing that he may be forcibly conscripted by the Russian army once he reaches the age of majority.
text_eng:
A race against time is underway to repatriate Bogdan Ermokhin to Ukraine. The teenager, an orphan deported from Mariupol (Donbass) occupied by Russia, will turn 18 in a few days, on November 19. He has already received a summons from the Russian military recruitment office, which has scheduled a meeting for him on December 19. His cousin, Valeria Poparcha, a resident of Odessa and now his legal guardian, and his Ukrainian lawyer, Kateryna Bobrovskaya, fear losing contact with him during his military service and that he may disappear within the Russian army, or even be forcibly mobilized to fight on the Ukrainian front. Bogdan Ermokhin is not a stranger. He is part of a group of children known as "the 31 of Mariupol," who were transferred from an orphanage in the city captured by the Russian army in May 2022 to the Polyani center in the Moscow region. These 31 Ukrainian orphans were showcased on television for propaganda purposes by Moscow's children's rights commissioner, Maria Lvova-Belova, who herself adopted one of them, Filip Golovnya. Bogdan Ermokhin was then placed in a Russian foster family. Moscow claims to have "welcomed" 744,000 Ukrainian children, accompanied or not, on Russian soil. Kiev, on the other hand, claims to have identified 19,546 children as cases of "deportation" and "forced population transfer," in violation of the Geneva Conventions. This Russian policy of deporting and Russifying Ukrainian children, under the guise of humanitarian evacuations, led Russian President Vladimir Putin and Maria Lvova-Belova to be targeted on March 17 by an arrest warrant from the International Criminal Court in The Hague, tasked with judging individuals suspected of "genocide," "crimes against humanity," and "war crimes." To date, only 386 deported children in Russia have been repatriated to Ukraine, by their families or through humanitarian organizations. The case of Bogdan Ermokhin is sensitive. While Filip Golovnya's case became famous because he was adopted by the architect of this policy of transferring Ukrainian children to Russia, Bogdan Ermokhin's case is emblematic from another perspective. He has not only expressed his desire to return to Ukraine, where he has a legal guardian and proper documentation, but the principle of his return was agreed upon on October 24 during a video call organized by his lawyer, by both the Russian Human Rights Ombudsman, Tatiana Moskalkova, and his Russian foster mother, Irina Rudnitskaya. However, since that meeting, nothing has happened, communication with him has become nearly impossible, and his legal age is approaching. "Tatiana Moskalkova promised that he would return to Ukraine, and I am ready to go get him in Russia if necessary," says Valeria Poparcha during a phone interview from Turkey, where she is currently staying. "But since that meeting, I haven't been able to reach Bogdan. His host family just sends me 'smile' emojis, as if everything is fine, even though he has received his military summons. He had previously told me that he was under intense psychological pressure. I believe that Russia wants to keep him there." His lawyer, Kateryna Bobrovskaya, laments that, apart from the notable exception of the ombudsmen of both countries in Bogdan's case, the only communication channel remains between the families, without any help from international organizations such as the UN or the International Red Cross.

The case of Bogdan Ermokhin is sensitive. Since being taken to Russia, the teenager has attempted to escape the country at least once before being arrested at the Belarusian border. He has posted messages and songs on social media praising Ukraine and expressing his pride in being Ukrainian. Shortly after, he recorded a video in which he claimed to be happy in Russia, but he later explained to his cousin and his lawyer that he had to make the video under pressure from Russian security services.

The case is also complicated by his friendship with Filip Golovnya. The two teenagers, friends since the Mariupol orphanage, have stayed in touch. Their conversations make Bogdan Ermokhin a direct witness not only of the system of deporting Ukrainian children in which he was engulfed but also of how Maria Lvova-Belova personally got involved. There is little doubt that neither the children's rights commissioner nor her protectors in the Kremlin and security services want repatriated children in Ukraine to testify in the media and perhaps one day before international justice about their involvement.

"At their majority, Ukrainian children are sent to military institutions, and their repatriation becomes impossible," denounces Kateryna Rashevska, a lawyer from the Regional Center for Human Rights NGO specializing in children's rights. Of the 386 deported children who have been able to return to Ukraine, none were of legal age, she points out. Rashevska believes that the deportation of Ukrainian children was "planned" by Russia. She despairs at the silence of the international community. "We need a resolution from the UN General Assembly, we need mediating countries, we need international organizations to get involved, otherwise it will be too late! These children will be Russian, their brains will have been washed, and some will have become soldiers," she declares. For Bogdan Ermokhin, there are only a few days left to regain his freedom, before a birthday that could once again change the course of his life.

title: In Lyon, "We are no longer..." mourns a lost Russia.
lead: At the Théâtre des Célestins, the Russian actors from the KnAM company, exiled in France since the beginning of the war in Ukraine, are staging their homeland under the control of Vladimir Putin.
text_eng: THEATER Six Russian actors dressed in white and armed with flashlights enter the stage of the Théâtre des Célestins in Lyon. Cautious spelunkers, they take the measure of the space before presenting themselves to the audience in still hesitant French. Programmed as part of the Sens interdits festival, they offer a mourning spectacle titled "We are no longer...", which is conjugated in the present to better express the imperfect, irremediable, and heartbreaking nature of the story being told. Tatiana Frolova and the actors of the KnAM Theater had been working (this verb is now conjugated in the past) for thirty-seven years in Komsomolsk-on-Amur, a city created by Joseph Stalin in the 1930s, in the heart of the taiga. Far from Moscow, but not from the persecutions carried out by the Russian president, Vladimir Putin, against any form of dissent. On February 24, 2022, Tatiana Frolova and her troupe, creators of a theater too critical for the political authorities of their country, decided to flee. Russia had just begun its invasion of Ukraine. It was "the last straw," comments the Frenchwoman Bleuenn Isambard. A companion of the KnAM since 2011, she translates the actors' words live during a show whose subject is this lost Russia, in more ways than one. Because the actors will not return there. Because it is "irradiated," they say, by the specter of the defunct USSR and the grip on the people of the current Putin regime. "Today, it is impossible to live in Russia and not want to die there," is heard throughout a plural narrative, enriched by the experiences of the protagonists and oscillating between documentary and metaphorical writings. No longer afraid Shortly after February 24, 2022, preferring uprooting to "imprisonment that would have turned her into an animal," Tatiana Frolova packed her bags. Twenty-three kilos. Not a gram more was allowed on the plane. The luggage transited through Kyrgyzstan before reaching Lyon. "Five members of the KnAM arrived in March 2022, the three others four months later," testifies Pierre-Yves Lenoir, director of the Célestins. They applied for political asylum, obtained it quickly, and are now awaiting their residency permits. The actors were financially supported in their efforts by the Célestins, the Sens interdits festival, the National Dramatic Center of Besançon, and the Théâtre populaire romand de La Chaux-de-Fonds (Switzerland): "We are not alone around the cradle. The Ministry of Culture, through the regional direction of cultural affairs, also supports the KnAM company with a project grant," confides Pierre-Yves Lenoir, who has appointed Tatania Frolova as an associated artist of his theater for "a minimum of three years." A natural support from a municipal house more than ever concerned with international creation, for whom France is a paradise. At 62, the author and director from Russia, adopted by the Auvergne-Rhône-Alpes region, is learning not to be afraid when she walks in the street. "I knew I was being watched in Komsomolsk-on-Amur," says Tatiana Frolova. "When I arrived here, I stayed indoors for six months. I was paralyzed, unable to do anything. Gradually, I thawed out." Like her comrades, she left behind family and friends. What belongings to take when one goes into exile for good? A book of songs for one, an embroidered tablecloth for another. A third chose the teddy bear with which he played puppetry as a child. These remnants of a bygone life are held up by the actors, who unravel, both literally and figuratively, the strings of their bundles. Fleeting music The stage is makeshift. A window, a mirror, a few tables, paper costumes, scissors, carnival masks, a camera zooming in on details of faces or hands, small or large piles of earth, videos and their news images: one of a Russian soldier, a prisoner in Ukraine, whose mother claims he must die for the homeland. Another of Vladimir Putin asserting that Russia has no borders. In the background, on the wall, adhesive tape outlines its territory, 17,095,000 square kilometers, with figures delivered as is by the actors: "Fertile land represents 13%, but only a fifth is developed, which is 2.5% of the total territory. The remaining four fifths are beyond the capabilities of the Russian man. But for a reason I do not know, the Russian needs to appropriate the lands of others," exclaims one of the performers. It is this gaping heart where blood no longer flows that is the matrix of the show. A void where the human is absent, but which Vladimir Putin fantasizes about expanding infinitely, in a desire for unrestrained contamination that Tatiana Frolova and her troupe stage by pointing out, through their words, the slow and insidious poisoning of Russian mentalities: "The serf is still inside people," laments the director. What to do then, except to leave, to escape the irradiation? When the actors evoke "The Cherry Orchard" (1904), a play by Anton Chekhov (1860-1904), the abandonment of the beloved house by its ruined inhabitants and the closing doors on what was and will be no more, emotion surges through the audience. This discreet and impressionistic theatrical work unfolds in small touches. In a continuum of short narratives, fleeting music, ephemeral gestures, it gives shape to intimate fractures, moral disintegration, and the ontological void of a Russia whose cartography, drawn on the wall by a precarious adhesive tape, ends up in tatters.

title: "The Frankfurt Book Fair claims to make Israeli voices 'audible' while reducing the space given to Palestinian voices."
lead: More than six hundred writers - including three Nobel Prize in Literature laureates, Annie Ernaux, Abdulrazak Gurnah, and Olga Tokarczuk - publishers, translators protest, in a column in "Le Monde", against the cancellation of the public award ceremony of the LiBeraturpreis 2023 to the Palestinian writer Adania Shibli, for her book "A Minor Detail", scheduled for October 20, during the Frankfurt Book Fair.
text_eng: The tragic and upsetting events that began on October 7th and continue today have repercussions worldwide, even in the publishing industry. Palestinian writer Adania Shibli, a recipient of several awards and a finalist for the 2020 National Book Award for her book "Minor Detail" (translated by Stéphanie Dujols and published by Actes Sud/Sindbad), was supposed to receive the LiBeraturpreis 2023 for the same work, published in German as "Eine Nebensache" (translated by Günther Orth and published by Berenberg Verlag) during the 2023 Frankfurt Book Fair, held from October 18th to 22nd. On October 13th, the literary society LitProm, organizer of the award partially funded by the German government and the Frankfurt Book Fair, announced that the award would not be presented to the writer at the Fair. A public event with Adania Shibli and her translator, Günther Orth, scheduled to take place at the Fair was also canceled. LitProm stated in their announcement that the decision was made in agreement with the writer. This claim was later repeated without verification in an article by The New York Times. However, this is false: Adania Shibli stated that the decision was not made in consultation with her but was imposed on her. A missed opportunity, she said, as she would have used the ceremony to offer reflections on the role literature can play in such difficult and painful times. (LitProm and The Times have since corrected their texts.) Adania Shibli's American publisher, Barbara Epler of New Directions, sent a letter to The New York Times, in which she wrote: "At a time of unimaginable pain on all sides, spreading lies serves no purpose, especially when they concern the author of a novel about the Nakba, whose historical accuracy is exemplary. Cancelling the ceremony and thus attempting to silence Adania Shibli's voice because of the war in Israel is a cowardly act. But to claim that Adania Shibli had agreed (even as Gaza is going through such a trial) is far worse." The Book Fair states its intention to make Israeli voices "particularly audible" while diminishing the space given to Palestinian voices. Although "Minor Detail" has been accused of antisemitism by two journalists and literary critics, more serious reviews have strongly refuted this accusation in the German press and elsewhere. The responsibility of the Frankfurt Book Fair The book refers to well-documented events related to the rape of a young Bedouin woman in 1949 by an Israeli army unit. Adania Shibli's British publisher, Jacques Testard of Fitzcarraldo Editions, wrote: "One of the main objectives of literature is to promote understanding and dialogue between cultures. In these times of violence and terrible suffering, the world's largest book fair must promote the literary voices of Palestine and Israel. We wish to express our solidarity with Adania Shibli and Berenberg Verlag, her German publishing house." It is in this same spirit that we, who belong to the world of writing, translation, and publishing, affirm loudly and clearly that the cancellation of cultural events is not a solution. We remember that the Frankfurt Book Fair supported Turkish publishers and that in 2022, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky addressed the Fair through a prerecorded video. The Frankfurt Book Fair has a responsibility, as a major international book fair, to create spaces that allow Palestinian writers to share their reflections on literature in these grave and terrible times, rather than silencing them. We need to find new ways to write, speak, think, and approach this dark period. For this, more than ever, we need writers, including Palestinian writers.

title: Humanitarian aid in Gaza, protest against the A69, poster for the Rugby World Cup final... The five key news from the weekend.
lead: If you haven't been following the news, here's what you need to know from the last forty-eight hours.
text_eng: Have you fallen a bit behind on the news these past two days? We summarize the main information from Saturday, October 21, and Sunday, October 22. Israel-Hamas War: First humanitarian convoys entered Gaza After fifteen days of war between Israel and Hamas, humanitarian convoys entered Gaza on Saturday and Sunday, under Israeli blockade. These trucks crossed the Rafah terminal, a border town with Egypt, which was opened exceptionally for this humanitarian operation. A first convoy of around twenty trucks arrived in Gaza on Saturday, but according to the UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs (OCHA), at least a hundred trucks per day are needed. On Sunday, a second convoy of seventeen trucks passed through Rafah. Additionally, six tanker trucks delivered fuel on Sunday, according to a journalist from Agence France-Presse. Rocket fire from Palestinian groups targeting Israel continues in response to Israeli strikes on the Palestinian enclave, where, according to the United Nations, one million people have fled the bombings in the North to gather in the South, near the border with Egypt. The situation is "catastrophic," five UN agencies stated on Saturday. Meanwhile, Cairo was the scene of a "peace summit" that failed to forge an "international consensus" to call for a ceasefire in the Gaza war and respect for international law. The summit, attended by heads of state, ministers, and diplomats, mainly from Europe and the Arab world, ended without a joint final statement. The Israeli military also announced on Saturday that it would intensify its strikes on Gaza as part of preparations for the next phase of the "Operation Iron Swords," which aims to invade the Palestinian territory, controlled by Hamas, in order to "annihilate" the Islamist movement. Tensions also remain high at the border between Israel and Lebanon. Residents are fleeing border areas as exchanges of fire increase between the Israeli army and the pro-Iranian Hezbollah, an ally of Hamas. A69 Toulouse-Castres: a demonstration brings together several thousand people, an opponents' ZAD evacuated Demonstrators in Saix on October 21, 2023. CHARLY TRIBALLEAU / AFP Thousands of opponents of the A69 highway project between Toulouse and Castres demonstrated on Saturday near Castres. The mobilization ended on Sunday after law enforcement officers evicted opponents from the construction site at La Crémade, east of Castres, where they had set up a ZAD (zone to defend). Nine people were arrested, six law enforcement officers were slightly injured, and the organizers reported around thirty minor injuries, without the need for the fire department or emergency medical services. The day before, the demonstration had gathered more than 10,000 people, according to the organizers. The prefecture reported 2,400 protesters in the main procession, as well as "2,500 violent individuals" who targeted two companies, the Carayon cement plant and a construction company, Bardou et fils, both involved in the A69 construction site, according to the concessionaire Atosca. During a press conference at the end of the day on Sunday, the prefect of Tarn mentioned around 150 people still present near the evacuated site, in a neighboring field. However, "the majority of them are packing up their tents and dismantling the marquees," according to the prefect. "There is no longer any form of aggression towards the gendarmerie forces," he assured. In Ukraine, a Russian strike on a postal depot kills six in the Kharkiv region. At least six people were killed and seventeen were injured on Saturday by Russian missile strikes on a postal depot in the outskirts of Kharkiv, in northeastern Ukraine, as reported by local officials. Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky shared a video on social media showing what appears to be a severely damaged warehouse next to debris and a container bearing the logo of the Ukrainian private postal operator Nova Pochta. The six people killed and the injured were "employees of the company who were inside the Nova Pochta terminal," said the governor of the Kharkiv region, Oleh Synehoubov. "The victims, aged 19 to 42, were injured by shrapnel and the blast [from the explosions]," he added. Bobby Charlton, World Cup winner with England and Manchester United star, has passed away. English football legend Bobby Charlton, a World Cup winner in 1966 and former Manchester United star, has died at the age of 86, as announced by the English club and his family on Saturday. "Manchester United is in mourning following the death of Sir Bobby Charlton, one of the greatest and most beloved players in the club's history," Manchester United stated in a release. Born in Ashington, a working-class town in the northeast of England on October 11, 1937, Bobby Charlton joined United at the age of 15. Known for his powerful strikes, he also became a symbol of elegance and sportsmanship throughout his career. Rugby World Cup: South Africa advances to final to face the All Blacks. Defending champions South Africa will defend their world title against New Zealand in a week, after breaking through a tightly contested semi-final against England (16-15) on Saturday. "We are in the final, that's all that matters," declared the colossal second-row Eben Etzebeth at the end of a match where the Springboks were trailing for seventy-eight minutes. In the final, it will be a rematch of the 1995 final between the top two teams in the World Rugby rankings: the Springboks against the All Blacks. Back then, South Africa clinched victory in a hard-fought match (15-12, ap) thanks to the kicking game of their fly-half Joel Stransky. Twenty-eight years after their first title at home, the South Africans have the opportunity to become the first team to sew a fourth star on their jersey (after 1995, 2007, and 2019), a quest also pursued by the New Zealanders (1987, 2011, and 2015). Also: Israel-Hamas. Hamas releases two American hostages. Hurricane Tammy. After hours of violet alert, "diminished danger" in Guadeloupe and end of the lockdown. Niger. Overthrown president Mohamed Bazoum is with his family and "doing well." International. France confirms the suspension of adoptions of children from Madagascar. Switzerland. Populist right-wing party leads by a wide margin in legislative elections with 29.2% of the votes.

title: The letters from Olga and Sasha: "Will our era be the most inhumane of all, even worse than the wars of the 20th century?"
lead: Olga and Sasha Kurovska, two Ukrainian sisters, one living in Paris and the other in Kiev, kept a journal for a year in "M Le magazine du Monde." The two thirty-year-olds shared an intimate account of their lives disrupted by the war. Now, they provide updates on their daily lives through regular correspondence.
text_eng: Paris, October 31, 2023 Dear readers, I am putting these words on paper in a very difficult moment: Yanis's father, my partner, is in the process of leaving us after a surgery that went wrong. We have been in limbo for a week, between life and death. This feeling is not unfamiliar to me: I felt it in my own flesh at the very beginning of the great war, starting on February 24, 2022. I have not lost anyone, thank God, but the fear of losing a loved one is the worst feeling imaginable. How many people are feeling it at this very moment as I write this letter? With the war in Ukraine, the conflict between Israel and Palestine, terrorist acts, the war between Armenia and Azerbaijan... In the corridors of this Parisian hospital, I think about this and my stomach churns. I feel nauseous. When did humanity go astray and start wanting to "divide and conquer"? Did you know that this saying is attributed to Philip II of Macedonia? I did not. Why is this violent way of handling disputes at the core of humanity? You may find me naive, but in the midst of all this sorrow, I feel so wounded and full of empathy. A few days ago, it was my birthday, I turned 36. We celebrated it very simply, with Yanis. I thought a lot about last year. Sasha had come from Ukraine to celebrate with me, I had a surprise party. I miss my sister and my whole family so much right now. Our travels between the two countries are becoming less frequent, they are so physically and mentally exhausting, we are getting more and more tired. The ongoing war that the world forgets scares me. It is said that in Russia [Olga and Sasha have chosen not to capitalize "Putin," "Russian," and "Russia"], the "rachists" [a contraction of "Russians" and "fascists"] are finishing building three weapons factories. Can you imagine? If we allow them to arm themselves more and more, in a few years we will all have to swim across the English Channel. Even a child could foresee it. This year, I did not participate in the "national unity dictation," the dictation that Ukrainians do on October 27 to celebrate our mother tongue! It was a text by the contemporary Ukrainian poet Kateryna Kalytko. It moves me to see on Insta the number of friends who did it, how beautiful it is! I read in an article that the fighters of the 128th Transcarpathian Assault Brigade did it on the battlefield... when the connection allowed. To give you an idea, here are a few lines: "Spring is at our doors in the destroyed villages/The scorched grains have been incubating all winter/Tenacious shoots are emerging everywhere from the ruined granary/The destroyed bridges lie flat on their bellies in their rivers and the rivers embrace them/The dust and the blue evening light cover the harvested fields/The roads of Ukraine are tangled threads of love: cut in some places but knotted again to be even stronger." Last year, I remember that day very well, we were on the train between Poland and Ukraine with my Sasha, we were trying to write but we couldn't catch a good signal. It's strange to feel nostalgic about those difficult moments. Is this what it means to get used to war? At the end of September, I went to a rare and ancient book fair at the temporary Grand Palais. The booth of a Berlin bookstore was offering a book by a Lenin illustrator... and many other Russian books. I don't understand. I will say it again and again: at this moment, the Russians are invading a neighboring country and killing Ukrainians, it is not the time to highlight the "great Russian culture." Despair... and hope, because I discovered that the Louvre Museum was behind a cultural initiative to support Ukrainian museums! Since December 2022, the Louvre has been collaborating with the National Museum of Arts Bohdan and Varvara Khanenko in Kyiv. Since the beginning of the invasion, hundreds of cultural sites have been destroyed or damaged. What will be left of our past? Secretly, museum teams have arranged to bring sixteen major works that were in Ukrainian national collections to France. Four of them are icons and are part of the exhibition "In the Origins of the Sacred Image," which is still ongoing at the Louvre for a few more days. I finish the letter, I am still in the hallway of this hospital with terracotta walls. This color makes me think of the cycle of life. P.S. - I just learned that Mom and Sasha have bought tickets to France. I am happy amidst an ocean of sadness... It is truly very courageous of them. They are coming to support us with Yanis, to be with his family, our family. Kyiv, October 31, 2023 Dear readers, Since my last letter on September 21, Kyiv has not been bombed. The period is calm, we are spoiled, we sleep at night, we are not stressed at every moment. Last year, October had been one of the toughest months, the rachists were bombing the energy infrastructure, winter blackouts were starting, the whole of Ukraine was in the dark. But now... life is in full swing in Kyiv. People work, go out, children are everywhere. This "reallusion" (a mix of reality and illusion) gives the impression that the war is no longer there. However, I constantly see messages in Telegram chats about the bombings in the regions of Kherson, Zaporizhia, Odessa, Sumy, and cities in the East and South. My friend Dmytro's hometown - Kryvyi Rih - is constantly being bombed. I feel safe here, but we always wish for a "quiet night" rather than a "good night." The front line is filled with fierce battles, and our army's counteroffensive is intense. Fighters and volunteers organize daily fundraisers for various needs. Donations have become an almost professional activity for those who organize them, those who spread the word, and of course, those who contribute. Every day, I send 5 euros to each fundraiser that comes across my path (secure links from people I know or friends know and vouch for their credibility). For a while now, I have noticed a fatigue or rather a kind of desensitization in foreign media regarding everything related to Ukraine, and the same goes for Ukrainian media. In early October, Olga participated in a program on France Culture alongside Andrei Kurkov, a well-known Ukrainian writer in France, and Tetyana Ogarkova, a Ukrainian philosopher and journalist. I have known Tetyana by name since my time at the French Institute about ten years ago. This woman represents the intellectual elite of our country. Along with her husband, Volodymyr Yermolenko, they are involved in several cultural projects and have a podcast on philosophy and literature that I listen to regularly. Since the start of the war, they have been going to the South and East for humanitarian missions. Tetyana also hosts a podcast in French titled "Ukraine, facing the war." A few days ago, she invited us, Olga, Elisa, and me, to share our experience of journal writing. I bring up this program on France Culture because I want to quote Tetyana Ogarkova. She expressed an idea that seems as dreadful as it is clear in expressing this fatigue; she said: "War becomes banal." Everyone is getting used to the existence of war in Ukraine. The idea is simple, terrible, and true. Especially now that the whole world has been shaken by the acts of terror by Hamas in Israel and the subsequent attack on Gaza. Where can one be completely safe? Will our era be the most inhumane of all, even worse than the wars of the 20th century? If Kyiv is not being bombed, mobilization is still very much present. Stories abound of men being called up overnight, taken from their homes or even off the streets, and sent to the front lines without any preparation. Zelensky has done some things to eliminate corruption, but even today, in the chaos of our system, it seems to me that if you don't know influential people, you are much more at risk of experiencing tragedies and injustices. This damn war is so long. The question of mandatory mobilization of women is still pending, without a clear answer. Personally, I am considering first learning the basics of tactical medicine to treat war wounded, but also other disciplines that I find essential for my future, perhaps shooting, for example. I would like to live in the illusion that someone else will fight the war for my freedom, but reality is stronger. How can I think differently? The day after tomorrow, my mom and I are taking the train and heading to Paris. We made this decision very quickly. We want to say goodbye to Yanis's father and join Olga and our extended family in grief. I am finishing this letter... and there is precisely an attack. I am taking refuge in the hallway.

title: Ukraine and Moldova's accession to the European Union: The Commission recommends the opening of negotiations.
lead: The European Commission's favorable opinion must now be approved or not by the Twenty-Seven at their next summit on December 14 and 15 in Brussels. Ursula von der Leyen recommends granting candidate status to Georgia.
text_eng: Ukraine has taken a further step towards joining the European Union (EU). Through its president, Ursula von der Leyen, the European Commission officially recommended the opening of negotiations in this regard on Wednesday, November 8, with Ukraine and also with Moldova. "Today is a historic day," said Mrs. von der Leyen. In June 2022, the EU granted Ukraine candidate status, in a highly symbolic gesture a few months after the start of the Russian invasion, as well as to neighboring Moldova. The opening of formal negotiations is the next step before a potential membership. The Commission's favorable opinion must now be approved or rejected by the twenty-seven member countries at their next summit on December 14 and 15 in Brussels. "Ukraine continues to face enormous difficulties and the tragedy caused by Russia's aggression," said Ursula von der Leyen, emphasizing that despite these difficulties, Ukraine has continued to reform "deeply" in preparation for its EU membership. Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky welcomed the European Commission's "good" decision on Wednesday. "Our state must be in the EU. Ukrainians deserve it," said Mr. Zelensky in a video posted on Telegram. However, this recommendation is still far from marking the end of the road for Ukraine and other candidate countries covered by this Commission report on EU enlargement, released on Wednesday. "Geopolitical response to Russia's aggression" If the Twenty-Seven endorse the Commission's opinion in December, Ukraine will join other European countries that have entered into negotiations with the EU, some of which, like Turkey, have been waiting for many years. Negotiations between the Union and Ankara were opened in 2005 but have been frozen since June 2018. Nevertheless, enlargement to the East is considered by several countries as a fundamental element of security in Europe in the face of Russian ambitions. "A stronger, larger, and more united European Union is the geopolitical response to Russia's aggression," said German Foreign Minister Annalena Baerbock. In contrast, Hungary, which maintains strong ties with Moscow, has been critical. "We believe that it is not appropriate to take further steps in the accession negotiations with Ukraine," said Hungarian Foreign Minister Peter Szijjarto, adding that Kiev cannot "join the European Union until there is peace." Furthermore, a possible entry of Ukraine, a country of over 40 million inhabitants and an agricultural power, into the Union will not be without difficulty. The FNSEA, the largest French agricultural union, even stated that it would be a "catastrophe for European agriculture." Gestures for Georgia and Bosnia The situation is different for Moldova, a small country among the poorest in Europe. Chisinau regularly denounces destabilization attempts by Moscow. The country has undertaken "significant reforms" in view of the accession process, praised the President of the European Commission. Ms. von der Leyen also announced that she had recommended granting Georgia, a country partly occupied by Russia since 2008, an official candidate status for EU membership. "Today is a day of celebration in Georgia," said Ms. von der Leyen, who nevertheless emphasized that this country still needs to undertake "significant reforms" corresponding to the "aspirations of the overwhelming majority of its citizens to join the EU." An announcement that was immediately "welcomed" by the pro-Western Georgian President, Salome Zourabichvili. Finally, the European Commission also made a gesture towards Bosnia, a candidate country for accession, by showing support for the opening of accession negotiations with this deeply divided Balkan country once certain progress has been made. "We have opened the door wide," but results are now expected, emphasized Ms. von der Leyen.

title: In the de Gaulle family, succession and betrayals
lead: The succession of Philippe de Gaulle, the last living child of the General, aged 101, is a concern for the faithful followers of the man of June 18th. After the one who has long been seen as the guardian of the values of the father of the Fifth Republic, who will take up the torch? The question divides the family, especially since one of its members, Pierre, openly expresses his support for Vladimir Putin's Russia, claiming loudly to be following in his grandfather's footsteps.
text_eng: Philippe de Gaulle dedicated his life to managing an inheritance. The thoughts, military achievements, and legacy of his father, Charles de Gaulle, have occupied his daily life for fifty-three years since the death of the General on November 9, 1970. For over half a century, the admiral has stood guard with his tall silhouette, from memoirs to political meetings, from official ceremonies to television appearances. The image of the great man follows him even in the mirror, where every morning a thin mustache under a cliff-like nose – that of his father – appears. As the years have passed, Philippe de Gaulle could have hoped for some respite, but there is always something to accomplish, even at 101 years old. One winter morning in 2022, bent over his walker, the heir leaves his room at the military hospital of Les Invalides, where he resides, to visit the Museum of the Order of the Liberation just a few corridors away. A room there is dedicated to Charles de Gaulle. He has deposited most of the objects: the mess kit carried by the General when he left for London on June 17, 1940; his khaki uniform as the leader of Free France, with tunic, pants, and kepi; the Grand Cross collar of the Royal Order of Victoria offered by Queen Elizabeth II of England in 1960 to the man who was then President of the Republic. Philippe de Gaulle now wants to entrust some military decorations of his father. "A deposit without a time limit," specifies the centenarian to the national delegate of the Order of the Liberation, General Christian Baptiste. He knows his time is running out. Everyone around him has already left. His wife, Henriette, has been waiting for him since 2014 under a tombstone in the cemetery of Colombey-les-Deux-Eglises (Haute-Marne). Gray, simple, like the one that houses, on the other side of the alley, Charles de Gaulle and his wife, Yvonne, as well as Philippe's sister, Anne, who had Down syndrome and died at 20 in 1948. His other sister, Elisabeth, was buried to the right of her parents in 2013, alongside her husband, the Grand Chancellor of the Legion of Honor and hero of Free France, Alain de Boissieu. The most famous surname in France. Who will take up the mantle of the legacy when Philippe joins them? In terms of politics, the question does not arise: there is always someone ready to claim the title of "the most illustrious of the French," as René Coty, the General's predecessor at the Elysée, put it. The anniversary of his passing is an occasion, every year, for a parade of personalities in Colombey-les-Deux-Eglises, even if no prominent figures are expected to attend this year. In 2021, the five candidates for the Republican Party primary stood together in front of his grave for a photo. Emmanuel Macron has made the pilgrimage twice as President. The founder of the Fifth Republic has become a figure of consensus. Even Marine Le Pen claims his legacy, like Jean-Luc Mélenchon, who cites him to justify equating Israel and Hamas since the massacre committed by the Palestinian terrorist movement on October 7. The question of inheritance arises mainly from the family side. There is only one clan to bear the name de Gaulle, to carry the most famous surname of contemporary France, known worldwide. All its representatives are descendants of Henri, the father of the General. Anyone who displays this name on their ID card therefore has a connection to his son Charles - or one of his four other children. This can open many doors, but also close them as success can be irritating. A de Gaulle lives "thanks to or despite" his name, says Yves de Gaulle, 72 years old, one of the five grandchildren of the General, son of Philippe, who was bullied at school for this reason. Any promotion seems suspicious to outsiders, while failure becomes synonymous with disgrace. In the late 1960s, the teenager questioned his grandfather about the very usefulness of his existence, inevitably dull in comparison to the legacy left by his ancestor in history: "Our life, my life can only be in decline. Does it have any interest?" Since one must live, it is better to try to seize the family flag. The succession of Philippe de Gaulle has become a matter of concern for his relatives as well as for all those who dedicate their lives to preserving the memory of the man of June 18. It worries even the Elysée, where Emmanuel Macron tasked his entourage in 2020 to look into the matter. The future of La Boisserie, the General's residence in Colombey-les-Deux-Eglises, now a memorial site open to the public, is particularly concerned. Also, the moral heritage of the great man. A clan that did not want, or could not, establish itself as a dynasty. Who will be authorized to speak for the family? Who will defend the Gaullist memory when it is besieged? There is no shortage of candidates. Philippe de Gaulle had four sons: Charles, Yves, Jean, and Pierre. His sister, Elisabeth, had a daughter: Anne. There are also a myriad of cousins, nieces, and nephews. All unknown, or almost, to the French. Unlike the Kennedys, the de Gaulles did not want - or could not - establish themselves as a dynasty. "A de Gaulle does not do just anything," they say in the family to justify this discretion. That's the theory. In practice, not everyone follows this precept. Take Pierre, for example. The youngest of Philippe, 59 years old, barely knew his grandfather, who died when he was 7. This does not prevent him from loudly claiming his legacy. Especially since he has shown his support for the Russian president, Vladimir Putin, during the invasion of Ukraine. On March 14, Pierre de Gaulle is at the Pushkin Museum in Moscow to celebrate the launch of a new association: the International Russophile Movement. An organization that must demonstrate the extent of Russia's friendships in the world and fight against the regime of sanctions imposed by Western countries since the outbreak of the war in February 2022. Russian Foreign Minister Sergey Lavrov is present, as is the oligarch Konstantin Malofeev, a key figure in Kremlin propaganda abroad. American actor Steven Seagal, converted to Putinism, is also in attendance. Friends of Russia have traveled from Africa, Lebanon, Serbia, Bulgaria, and Hungary. Elie Hatem and Fabrice Sorlin, shadowy figures of the French far right, with a traditionalist Catholic tendency, are among the driving forces of the movement, "of course under the auspices of the Russian government and the blessing of President Putin, who has conveyed a message," assures the former. The latter, a resident of Moscow, is a valued intermediary between France and Russia, especially within the National Rally (RN) party. Promoter of a French-Russian rapprochement Pierre de Gaulle has the honors of the platform. With a badge around his neck, he steps up to the lectern to deliver a monotonous speech. He criticizes the United States as the "greatest destroyer of peace in the world," denounces the "greed" of Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky, who is "enslaved to the Americans and NATO," and castigates "the submission of the European continent." The audience applauds. Excerpts of his speech are broadcast on social media. A de Gaulle as a moral authority, what a windfall! Since the beginning of the conflict, this independent financial advisor based in Geneva has been making the rounds in the media, from the Russian channel RT to French far-right groups, calling for a French-Russian rapprochement, "in line with General de Gaulle's thinking." In his view, Russia embodies "a world for the defense of values of faith and spirituality," while the West is only dedicated to "the promotion of the ego." His activism catches the eye of Emmanuel Leroy, former advisor to Marine Le Pen and fervent supporter of the Kremlin, who approaches him in the corridors of the Pushkin Museum. The two men stay in touch. This radical activist, heir to the nationalist-revolutionary trend, helps him structure his ideas. He is behind the recent launch of the Pierre de Gaulle Foundation for peace and prosperity among peoples. For now, it seems to be an empty shell, primarily serving as a business card for its founder. So far, no one in the de Gaulle family knew about Pierre's interest in geopolitics, or politics in general. Certainly, the youngest had made a name for himself in recent years for his disruptive presence at the lunches organized on November 9 in Colombey. In 2021, he had almost succeeded in making the placid Jean Castex explode with anger, who had come to the ceremonies as prime minister and was overwhelmed by de Gaulle's grandson with all the country's problems. The following year, Pierre de Gaulle boasted, in the presence of the two (RN) deputies from Haute-Marne, Christophe Bentz and Laurence Robert-Dehault, of having voted for Marine Le Pen in the presidential election - something he refuses to confirm. Many among the de Gaulle family wonder if his sudden pro-Russian epiphany might be motivated by financial considerations, aimed at supporting his lifestyle. "It's absurd," the person in question responds in a video conference interview, between two trips. "I am absolutely not paid, neither by the Russians nor by the Americans. The people who support my movement are all volunteers, no one has ever paid me." Pierre de Gaulle then ends the interview after informing us with a smile that he has recorded us without our knowledge. A common method on the far right. A painful memory His antics shake his family, who have been lost for nearly a year and a half in conjectures about how to react. Should they respond publicly to him? Object to the usurpation of political heritage? The image of the clan is at stake, as well as the respect due to General de Gaulle's thoughts. Pierre's older brother, Yves, an ENA graduate and State Councilor, broke his silence once in January to assure that the troublemaker only speaks for himself. But Pierre persists in wanting to step into his ancestor's very large shoes. His older brother, he judges, is "neither the family's legal representative" nor "the executor of General de Gaulle's thoughts." Therefore, he has no intention of keeping quiet. Resigned, his relatives now only respond to his outbursts with sovereign contempt. "Pierre's positions are completely inaccurate," Yves de Gaulle is once again annoyed, reached by phone. "For him to consider them as those of the family and General de Gaulle is false and foolish. One only needs to look at what our grandfather said about the independence of peoples." The conflict has stirred up a painful memory for the de Gaulle family. It dates back to the end of the last century, in the spring of 1999. Jacques Chirac is then residing at the Elysée, Lionel Jospin at Matignon. The European elections are approaching. Strange blue-background posters appear on walls all over France: "Jean-Marie Le Pen - Charles de Gaulle, for a free France let's change Europe." Charles, the eldest son of Philippe de Gaulle, named after his grandfather in accordance with an old tradition, has just committed the unforgivable act: allying with the president of the National Front. A party founded by nostalgics of the Vichy regime and French Algeria. France discovers the rosy cheeks and boyish face of this 51-year-old international lawyer, shy. The first grandson of General de Gaulle, born in 1948, was cherished by his very tender grandfather. He had been quietly serving in the European Parliament under the banner of Philippe de Villiers. Jean-Marie Le Pen offered him the second spot on his list. The family was outraged. The disturbance created in public opinion allowed the old leader to momentarily divert attention from the turmoil caused by the split in the National Front initiated by Bruno Mégret. The capture was only for his name, nothing more. Charles de Gaulle was not a speaker. "A sincere patriot and sovereigntist," Bruno Gollnisch barely agrees today, who was then the party's number two. Ready, above all, for any contortions. On the sidelines of a meeting in the Bouches-du-Rhône in June 1999, the General's grandson found himself face to face with Jean-Jacques Susini, the departmental secretary of the National Front and co-founder of the OAS (a clandestine terrorist organization opposed to Algerian independence), who plotted the – failed – assassination attempt against his grandfather on Mount Faron, in Toulon, in 1964... Instead of defending the family honor, Charles de Gaulle declared: "There can be no personal vendetta with people who rightly considered that Algeria was France." Against all likelihood, the heir believes that "the National Front defends the ideas and concepts of General de Gaulle." His family is outraged. Geneviève de Gaulle-Anthonioz (1920-2002), the General's niece, a resistance fighter deported to Ravensbrück and founder of ATD Fourth World, responded in Libération on May 19, 1999: "We, the General's family, do not navigate in these troubled waters." On the same day, in Le Monde, fifty-three grandchildren, grand-nephews, and grand-nieces published a column to say "no!" "NO, Mr. Charles de Gaulle, National Front candidate in the European elections, the name you bear does not belong to you. (...) We protest against the insult thus made to the life and memory of our grandfather and great-uncle, and thereby to the fight of our parents for France." All branches of the family are mobilized: de Gaulle, Tardif, Cailliau, Gutmann. Even Charles's three brothers signed the text. Pierre included, despite his attempt to convince us, a quarter of a century later, that he had nothing to do with it. "I was a teenager," claims the one who was then... 36 years old. Charles de Gaulle left the European Parliament at the end of his term in 2004. The force of the blow inflicted by his relatives as well as the media exposure made him avoid family gatherings and journalists since then. The man, 75 years old, did not respond to our requests. He retreats into oblivion, not attending any ceremonies related to the memory of his grandfather. Even when he was younger, he got married in London without informing anyone, not even his parents. A way to assert his independence, his parents wanted to believe. The mayor of Colombey-les-Deux-Eglises, Pascal Babouot, with twenty-two years of experience on the municipal council, has only seen him once in the village, at the funeral of his mother, Henriette. Aurore Jacquinot, head guide at the residence of La Boisserie, still owned by the de Gaulle family, has never met him in twenty years of service; yet she knows every little great-nephew as if they were her own family members. It is hard to imagine the burden that such a famous name carries. Can one measure, for a 20-year-old law student, the violence of being named Charles de Gaulle in the midst of May 1968? The descendants of the General, "famous by association," as humorously put by one of his great-nephews, Rémi de Gaulle, have often been tossed about by grand history. Those who grew up in the 1960s, for example, had to endure taunts from classmates whose parents supported French Algeria. "With de Gaulle, in three years, France will be red!" Hélène de Gaulle, wife of Macé de Lépinay, a great-niece, heard among her classmates at Notre-Dame-de-Sion, in the heart of Saint-Germain-des-Prés. "I didn't know what it meant, but it made me cry," she recalls, who was later elected for three decades to the Paris City Council. Anne de Laroullière, daughter of Elisabeth de Gaulle and Alain de Boissieu, remembers on her side that the mother superior of her school in the 7th arrondissement of Paris had expressed concern to her parents because the Bastien-Thiry family, whose son Jean organized the Petit-Clamart assassination attempt against General de Gaulle in 1962, intended to enroll one of their daughters there. "When my grandfather died, my teachers came to see me crying. I didn't understand," she whispers. The young girl was 11 years old at the time. She remembers the crowd of anonymous people in Colombey-les-Deux-Eglises, seen through the family car window, silently shedding tears as they watched the funeral procession of the great man. The sixty-year-old now oversees the Anne de Gaulle Foundation, established by her grandparents to assist individuals with mental disabilities. She also serves as the general secretary of the families of the companions of the Liberation. Most of the time, she is the one who organizes family gatherings among the de Gaulles. The most memorable one brought together nearly two hundred people in 1999, alongside the performance given by Robert Hossein, "De Gaulle, the man who said no." Her cousins Charles, Yves, Jean, and Pierre did not attend. In any case, they rarely participate in these gatherings. "I have rarely crossed paths with my cousins in my life; they were overprotected because they were the grandsons of the General," notes Rémi de Gaulle, 71 years old, a long-time lawyer in Aix-en-Provence, now a novelist. One cannot imagine the various threats, nor the pressure from the press, always on the lookout for scandals. Or the appetite of politicians, eager to associate a famous name with their electoral campaign. In the family, the belief is that the surname de Gaulle offers, roughly speaking, an additional 5 to 10 percentage points of votes compared to an average candidate. A coveted family home Jean de Gaulle, the third of the siblings, would not disagree, having had a long - and discreet - career as a member of parliament between 1986 and 2007. A position handed to him on a silver platter by Jacques Chirac, who nevertheless forced him to leave his quiet constituency in Deux-Sèvres in 1993 to be elected in Paris, in the crocodile-infested waters of the local right-wing. His biggest regret. Jean de Gaulle left public life through the back door in 2011, sentenced to three months of suspended prison in the case of fictitious jobs at the Paris City Hall. Now 70 years old, he also did not respond to our requests. Like his brother Yves, the man enjoys spending weekends occasionally at the family residence of La Boisserie in Colombey-les-Deux-Eglises. The mini-golf and tennis court installed by their grandfather are no longer usable, but the place, surrounded by a 2.5-hectare park, remains pleasant. In autumn, the Virginia creeper on the facade displays a bright orange-red color. Nearly forty thousand people visit the few rooms open on the ground floor each year, whose layout has not changed since the death of Yvonne de Gaulle in 1978. It is here that the man of June 18 prepared his return to power in 1958. He also wrote his Memoirs here and staged the Franco-German reconciliation by receiving Chancellor Konrad Adenauer in September 1958. The preservation of his books and photographs, displayed in sunlight, does not meet current standards, historians sigh. Especially in the corner office. One does not manage a family home like a museum. The staircase leading to the upper floor is blocked by a small sign: "Private rooms, still used by the descendants of General de Gaulle." An arrangement that Philippe de Gaulle insists on, as he did not want to part with the place and remains formally the owner. His wife, Henriette, enjoyed sitting on the park benches and chatting with visitors. "La Boisserie will remain in the family." In 2020, after a visit to the house, Emmanuel Macron tasked the prefect of Haute-Marne, his former communication advisor Joseph Zimet, to look into the future of La Boisserie. This historian by training laments that the site in Colombey-les-Deux-Eglises is under-visited. Its management is fragmented. The Haute-Marne Department Council has a strong influence over the Charles de Gaulle Memorial, while the Charles de Gaulle Foundation, owner of the monumental Cross of Lorraine, is responsible for managing La Boisserie, under the vigilant - some say suspicious - eye of the family. Yves de Gaulle, in particular, suffers from having been kept away from the foundation's board of directors, chaired by former minister Hervé Gaymard. This was the wish of his grandfather, who did not want to see his descendants sitting on the institution's leadership. The senior civil servant is the only one in his siblings who has maintained his distance from politics, even though he does not hide his preference for Emmanuel Macron, whom he sees as "the most Gaullist, in spirit, of the Presidents of the Fifth Republic." Yves de Gaulle has emerged as the more or less official representative of the family, even being invited to the dinner in honor of King Charles III at Versailles in September. The heir always carries a letter from his father authorizing him to manage the future of La Boisserie. "He will gather his brothers when I am no longer here," Philippe de Gaulle once confided to an old companion. It is in this capacity that Joseph Zimet received Yves de Gaulle, with his wife, at the Haute-Marne prefecture, and tried to convince him to sell the residence, when the time comes, to the Department Council. The idea appeals to the General's descendant, but faces opposition from his brothers. Especially from Pierre. "La Boisserie will remain in the family," he asserts. "La Boisserie is not a family home, but a historic home, which belongs to all French people," Yves argues. As long as their father is alive, the status quo prevails. A thin power held in the hands of Philippe de Gaulle, a man long discredited. The centenarian lived, as he recounts in his Memoirs (Bouquins, 2022), "with the impression that we are never, my family and I, more than pawns among others in my father's game, and without anything to say." Charles de Gaulle, fearing accusations of nepotism, always refused to induct his son into the Order of the Liberation despite his acts of bravery demonstrated during World War II. He would be its last representative today.
